 Definitions of Operations -4 STAGE REFINISH- In addition to the base and mid coat applications of a 3-Stage process, the 4-Stage refinish process, depending on paint manufacturer, may possibly require the application of an additional ground coat. Some paint manufacturers may use a tinted clear coat in addition to the final non-tinted clear coat. These additional steps are not included and should be considered when developing an estimate using an on-the-spot evaluation. 25% full refinish time of each panel/part -Adhesion Promoter (per I-car )- Adhesion promoter may not be required for primed parts. Many replacement bumper covers are supplied primed from the maker. A solvent test should be performed to ensure the primer does not dissolve when solvent is applied. An adhesion promoter may be required for parts that are not supplied primed. In addition, adhesion promoter may be required on bare areas of repaired parts. Adhesion promoter should not be applied to adjacent areas where the original coatings are left intact. -Add for Inside- Refinish the backside of panels. Aperture, Fender, Floor, Quarter, Rear body. (half the base refinish time of the panel) -Caulking Materials- Similar to Seam Sealer - Charging Battery / Maintain Battery Voltage - Certain repair situations may require the on-board vehicle battery to be connected to a battery charger/ battery maintainer during repairs. - Example of this situation is during the scanning process. The OEM may state the vehicle must maintain a certain voltage to ensure all modules, computers, and electrical consumer equipment are being scanned at the proper voltage level. Battery charging/ Battery maintenance is NOT INCLUDED in developed labor times and would be an on the spot evaluation. -Clean and Retape (double sided tape)- 10$ and 0.2 body. For parts that are double sided tape and will be reused. Don’t forget Adhesive cleanup also, that’ll be 0.2 body. - Collision Damage Acce weld stud ss - Cut open a panel so you can see additional damage -Color Tint- The paint being mixed and then sprayed out to see if it is the correct color -Corrosion Protection Rust Proof- Another coating before the base coat/clear coat stage only when a panel is repaired. Replace it does not need. *Etched primer? -Abrasion Corrosion – rubbing or hitting of one material by another -Corrosion Protection – material applied to deter corrosion (oxidation) -Crevice Corrosion – oxidation when two metals are joined -Electrolytic Corrosion – electrical action taking place between two materials in the presence of an electrolyte (liquid) -Fogging – applying material in a mist form -Fretting – deterioration of metal at contact surfaces due to motion and corrosive elements -Galvanic Corrosion – electrical action (electrolysis) between two dissimilar metals in the presence of electrolyte (liquid) -Hot Spot – an unprotected area subject to corrosion -Pitting Corrosion – corrosion on a surface that results in small “specs” or “pinholes” -Stress of Fatigue, Cracking Corrosion – cracking due to stress and atmospheric elements -Desired Characteristics of Corrosion Protection Material 1) Corrosion Prevention Material – the material must displace water to prevent corrosion. This can be tested by spraying water on an open panel on the floor, then spraying the corrosion preventative material over the watered panel and observing if the material displaces the water 2) Creepage of Material – to insure thorough and complete protection coverage, the material should have a “creep” capability, approximately ¼ inch per minute while drying. This assures protective penetration of pinch welds, cracks, etc. 3) Safe Material – material should be non-combustible when dried and when wet unable to support a fire after ignition 4) Clean Up – the material should be of a viscosity which inhibits runs or drips. Overspray on a vehicle’s painted surface should wipe off easily without solvent when wet, with solvent when dry. The materials should also dry clean off clothing 5) Guarantee/Warranty – the corrosion protection has to be done to maintain factory corrosion warranty. Manufacturer’s recommendations must be followed -Flex Additive (Clear Coat)- $15T for plastic paintable items that need to flex such as Bumpers -Flex Mixing Time- 0.3 Refinish, time to make the Flex Additive (Clear Coat) -Mask Jams- Prevents overspray. Different from Cover Car for Overspray. (0.4 refinish smaller parts such as Fender, A-pillar, 0.5 refinish for larger parts such as Liftgate, Quarter, Door, Hood) -Matrix Wand – definitions Sway Straightening Width – sway is a term commonly used to identify a part or area that has been pushed to one side by collision forces, causing a width alignment. Sway may be corrected by making a forward pull that is slightly slanted toward the direction of impact. Placing a block near bends in a part helps straighten the part and keeps it from hinging further back in the vehicle, creating additional damage. If there is a buckle or deformation in one side of the rail, pull on that side of the rail, as it will likely be short and this may help remove the damage Sag Straightening Height – correcting height misalignment commonly involves pulling the part forward in addition to upward or downward. This is because there is often some degree of mash from the collision forces. The forward pull also helps prevent buckling the part during the pull. Straightening height may also require holding adjacent areas. Blocking may be used during a downward pull to prevent adjacent areas from being moved out of specification. Sag is a term commonly used to identify a part or area that is too low Mash Straightening Length – a change in length, also called mash, collapse or short rail, is a condition where any section of a part is shorter than factory specification. Mash may be corrected by pulling the part in the opposite direction of the impact. Some mash damage may be repairable, however, depending on the extent of the damage, partial or complete replacement of the part may be necessary -Prime &amp; Block – prime &amp; block (high build/primer-filler) is a required procedure that restores a repaired panel surface, including the joined areas of replaced welded panels, from 150-grit finish to new undamaged condition. -Sealers and Sound Deadeners – -Terminology -Full Cure Time – time when a sealer has reached full strength -Handling Time – the time when a part can be safely transporter and sealer can no longer be tooled or repositioned -Paintable Time – established time when refinish materials can safely be applied to a sealer -Set Time – time when there is no longer product transfer -Work Time – the length of time a sealer can continue to be applied or tooled effectively -Sealers -Brushable - single component sealer applied with a brush -Flowable – sealer with low viscosity and self-leveling characteristics -Pumpable – a two component or one component sealer that seals interior and exterior joints and voids -Resistance Weld-Through – sealer/adhesive that can be used in conjunction with resistance spot welding -Sealer Tape – preformed sealer -Sprayable – sealer applied with a pneumatic dispenser to duplicate original textured appearance -Thumb Grade – heavy bodied sealer for sealing large gaps and filling voids. Should remain soft and pliable -Sound Deadeners -Mastic Pads – sound deadener pad that is preformed to fit a specific area -Non-Structural Flexible Acoustical – flexible foam with sealing and sound deadening properties -Non-Structural Ridged Acoustical – ridged foam with sealing and sound deadening properties -Identifying Sealers – several types of sealers and sound deadeners are used during assembly. Therefore, specific applications may not be identified in this publication. General applications and the various types of products for repair will be featured to identify and replace OEM sealers and sound deadeners. -Helpful Sealer Tips -Check self-life or expiration date prior to beginning sealer applications -Be sure “work time” is appropriate for sealer application -Temperature, humidity and thickness of sealer will affect the work, set and paintable times -Test fit replacement panels prior to installation to be certain of a tight fit and proper seal -Equalize 2K productions according to adhesive manufacturer’s recommendations -Always follow manufacturer’s recommendations for storage, usage and application to achieve bet performance of the product -Seam Sealer- For new panels and welded on panels. Seam sealer goes around the edges/gaps -Types and Use of Sealer - sealers can be categorized according to their ingredients, applications, hardening types, functions or effectiveness. There are three kinds of sealers (adhesive) used for car manufacturing. One type is a structural use, used for adhering structural components together that need a strong adhesion. The second type is a non-structural use, used for adhering interior upholsteries, and the third type is a quasi-structural use, used to fill-in clearances between the body component parts. For quasi-structural use sealer, there is a mastic sealer that is used for improving panel rigidity, preventing vibration and noise, and a body sealer that is effective for preventing entry of water, dust and wind. -Body Sealer (Paint Sealer) – applied to part contact surfaces to prevent entry of water and dust. It also has a rust-prevention effect for the edges of steel sheets and is used to improve exterior appearances -Foam Filler Material – significantly reduces the amount of passenger compartment road noise when applied to close off body cavities -Glass Sealer (Urethane-based Sealer) – for glasses and other external parts (including the side protector, a urethane-based sealer that is hardened at room temperature is used. The automobile window glasses are attached with a method called direct glazing, which directly adheres laminated glasses onto the painted body. In this method, one-component moisture-curing polyurethane type adhesive is used. The adhesion surfaces are a ceramic coated glass and painted steel panel. To improve the adhesion, primer is applied on both surfaces -Mastic Sealer – used to adhere the hood, trunk, door and roof with outer panels. It not only joins them together but also contributes to increased rigidity of the panels, helping to prevent vibration and noise from the outer panels -Separator – separators are installed in between the panels of mass produced vehicles. Foam is generated around the separators by heat during paint baking process fill up body cavities. Some newer models use expandable foam applied through the inner panel holes for this same purpose -Spot Sealer (aka Weld-Through Primer) – used to prevent rust on the mating surfaces of spot welds -Undercoat- Paintable product which is different from Gravel Guard. Most cars have this, this is usually with a car whose rocker molding is touching the quarter panel. This is sprayed and then gets painted over. Blend Time Study SCRS Blend Study Report: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS BETWEEN FULL REFINISH AND BLEND OPERATIONS www.scrs.com info@scrs.com 877.841.0660 Audatex, CCC/MOTOR and Mitchell each identify a unique refinish time for individual parts within their estimating systems. Each IP also provides a formula to calculate time to blend adjacent panels as 50% of the blend panels base refinish time, when using a two stage refinish. CCC/MOTOR and Mitchell both specify a calculation of 70% of the blend panels base refinish time, when utilizing a three-stage color. Audatex adds 35% to each panel for three-stage, but utilizes the same 50% formula. The conclusions of this study indicated that blending, on average, took 31.59% more time than full refinish, rather than 50% less. -Example – Tesla Model Y Hood refinish is 2.8, blend is 1.4. Take 1.4 x .82 = 2.5. Change the blend time and put “Blend Time Study”. No clear coat Tips on being Efficient Work Smart Time management is key in this business. Here are ways you can be more efficient and really streamline the process Write everything down and document it with photographs Create an R Drive or Shared Location and be organized Save your OEM position statements to an R Drive or Shared Location for quick reference and usage -Answers – get all your answers while you have the vehicle in-front of you in whatever stage the vehicle is in and your estimate / blueprint is in . Keep chipping away as much as you can. -Accident – - How did the accident occur? -How many people were in the vehicle? -What is related and what is not related? -Check for ADAS on vehicle, does it have the following: -Blind Spot Radar -Front Radar -Surround View Camera -Windshield Camera -Etc -Do you have New parts already and can do a trial fit on the parts to ensure multiple things -1) The mounting points on the vehicle are in tolerance -If not, does it require alignment or replacement? -2) The New part is not defective -Inspect the damage, good rule of thumb is to compare the opposite/undamaged side to the damaged side. Most vehicles are symmetrical - Measurement - w as the vehicle measured in one way or another -Digital Angle guide -Frame measurement -Gap dimensions -Pre wheel alignment -Determine if suspension parts are needed or one time use hardware is needed for adjustment. Also do the wheel alignment at this time to ensure it can be adjusted into spec or not. -Tram Gauge measurement -Etc -Paint – did you or a painter check the color – to know if you have to blend, refinish, etc -Photos – did you capture all photos of the vehicle, did you review with a technician and do a walk around -Damaged parts – take a photo of the part number on it as well -R&amp;I parts -Vehicle and its condition -Etc -Updating the vehicle owner, get all your answers before you give them a call. -Get an ETA on the parts -Status update with insurance, it was submitted, they came out, etc - Pressure - If removing a part from the vehicle such as an impact bar and you notice there was pressure upon release, there may be more damage to that vehicle that you may not see. Inertia travels. Check online for that year make and model vehicles inertia travel diagram to get a better feel for where the damage could have traveled -Upsell Opportunities - Check with the vehicle owner and see if they are interested in anything additional to be done to their vehicle. They may want new tires, they may want that dent repaired, they may want ceramic coat. -Bookmark – the websites you need on a daily basis -Create a Check List - this will help ensure you have captured each part of the repair plan. I use a check list every day and it consists of multiple stages of the repair to help ensure I have captured all sectors of the estimate / blueprint. Examples are as follows: -Was the vehicle scanned? Was the scan report reviewed? Were DTC procedures researched -Does the vehicle require ADAS Calibrations? Do you have all ADAS Calibration Procedures? -Was the Paint Code documented? -Is the vehicle repair requiring any specific material? Do you have OEM documentation to back that up? Did you create the invoice relating to the material? -These are some of the things I have on my own checklist and by the time I am done with my blueprint, it is nearly filled out completely. And I say nearly because not every repair will require every category on my checklist. -Clear Mind – personally I prefer being one of the first to arrive at the shop. This gives me plenty of time to work on the estimates / blueprints before the staff arrives, before the noise begins, before the emails and phone calls come through, before the insurance adjuster comes in, before the customers come in. Less traffic early in the morning also. I prefer early morning than late afternoon. -Consistent -be consistent across all estimates / blueprints, this means good organization, easy to follow line operations , consistent labor units. What you do not want is an estimate that is scattered and you are having to scroll up and down to follow along. -Dealer -when working with a dealer on getting / finding correct parts and sending photos of the part over, label the part with part number. This makes it easier for the dealer to find the correct part -Diagrams – print part diagrams from your Estimating Database for writing Estimate / Blueprint, its an easy to follow process for you and your technician. -Filter RT / LT Parts – in your estimating database, to ensure accuracy and efficiency, filter your options to the side you are working on. -Line Notes - if you are adding a specific operation to your estimate such as part needs Loctite 242, put in the line note the procedure name for why. Do not list specifically why it has to be done, list the procedure in the line note. Example: -See Procedure: Front Bumper Removal and Installation -Longevity - is key in this industry. This industry will age you fast. Here some ways to protect yourself -Disassembly Table - use a table to document parts on instead of bending down and working on the ground -Floor Mat - use a cushioned floor mat to stand on to protect your joints -Totes or Parts Carts - Store damaged parts or R&amp;I parts as you go through them -Mobile Work Station - utilize a work station that can be wheeled over to a vehicle, having your work station mobile allows you to hold all the necessary things you need to write an estimate / blueprint -Camera phone -Computer -Dent board -Markers -Notepad -Pens -Pointer stick - for photo usage to show in images - blend, repair, replace, refinish, etc -Printer -Multiple Monitors – allows you to run multiple applications at the same time -Email -Estimating database -Images -Ultimate Guide to Estimating / Blueprinting -Procedures -Part Code Table - add operations to your part code table or copy and paste the line operations in vs typing it all out. -Parts Ordering – use a parts ordering system to quicken your time of ordering -Process of Elimination – older files take priority over newer files. Get as much done as you can on one file before you move on to the next. All procedures, part prices / part numbers, Invoices, Scans, Calibrations, etc. regardless if the vehicle is completely done in the estimate / blueprint phase. Say you are waiting on a measurement and your tech is out, you can still complete everything else on that estimate / blueprint. -40/60/80 % -Operations - list the operations for a part directly underneath it for an itemized breakdown . Example -R&amp;I / R&amp;R - Bumper -DE-NIB - Bumper -Buff - Bumper -Order – Create an order that works for you, such as work front to back or back to the front and while working on the parts in the estimating system, go through each part and the corresponding image. It is the same for researching OEM Repair Procedures. -Standard Operating Procedures – for your team, for yourself, for your estimates / blue prints, what are processes you should follow -Two Step Verification – have two means of documenting a repair. For me, it is documenting the repair via notepad and also taking photographs of the vehicle. Before I complete any estimate or blueprint, I go through my notes on the vehicle and then go through my photos. At times I will notice that I may have not written something down but I did photograph it (or vice versa). This helps ensure accuracy. Descriptions and Explanations -ADAS Diagnostic Report- ADAS PRECAUTIONS G33 ADAS PRECAUTIONS On vehicles that use advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS), OEM repair information must be consulted to ensure repairs will not negatively impact ADAS functionality. -Battery Support - Required to maintain constant voltage during scan process. Per Motors GTE G9: Many OEMs recommend a pre- and post-repair diagnostic scan on all vehicles involved in a collision that could reveal pre-accident or accident-related damage. Please refer to OEM position statement and repair procedures for more information. Due to the variety of scan tools used, both OEM and aftermarket, as well as the variation from vehicle to vehicle and repair to repair, MOTOR is unable to develop a time that accounts for these different scenarios. Therefore, MOTOR recommends an on-the-spot evaluation to determine an appropriate estimated time to perform diagnostic scans for each repair. -Bring Vehicle to Operating Temperatures – NOTE? -Charge and Maintain Battery – Charge and maintain battery during the repair and calibration process - Check and Adjust Tire Pressure – - Need to adjust tire pressures so that the tires travel at the same speed -To aim lamps -Clean for Delivery – Found on P-page G9. SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. If an estimated work time is not available for the items below, an on-the-spot evaluation is recommended to determine an appropriate estimated time. Clean up or detailing of vehicle prior to delivery. -Color Tint- Found on P-page G33. Refinish times do not include time which may be required to match color tints or defective finish textures on interior or exterior surfaces. -Corrosion Protection- Found On P-page G6-G9-G34 Corrosion Protection: A vehicle's ability to resist corrosion may be reduced if improper repairs are made and the manufacturer's corrosion protection is not restored. This may lead to future safety and/or resale value problems. DOES NOT INCLUDE: Anti-corrosion material application SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. If an estimated work time is not available for the items below, an on-the-spot evaluation is recommended to determine an appropriate estimated time. Anticorrosion material restoration/application -Cover and Protect Electrical Connections – Protect electrical connections from contaminant, debris &amp; moisture. Sealing open electrical plugs. When a plug/wire/connector or computer module is left open to the environment, especially in adverse weather…it can potentially allow moisture and debris into electrical system and can cause issues with vehicle. These issues may not show right away, the vehicle may take some time to recognize there is an issue in system -Cover Interior &amp; protection during repairs process. - Protection during repairs, cutting, welding, bonding, riveting, dust, dirt etc. protection during repair process. -D E-NIB - Found on P-page G38 SPECIAL NOTATION: Refinished panels may or may not require a varying amount of de-nibbing, a process used to remove small particles in final finish surface. Each panel requiring de-nibbing (refinish or blend) HOOD, ROOF, TRUNK LID, SPOILER First panel add up to 20% of full base refinish time, each additional panel add up to 10% FENDER, DOOR, QUARTER PANEL, BUMPER COVER First panel add up to 10% of full base refinish time, each additional panel add up to 5% - Drive Cycle Operational Verification - Non included operation, per CCC/Motor Guide Page G9 - Test drive vehicle prior to disassembly due to vehicle collision and being immediately towed. Vehicle needs to have a drive cycle performed to have DTC’s occur prior to final road test after repairs have been complete. -Dynamic Systems Verification – Found on P-page G11 G14 G16 G17 Test Drive to confirm all operations are functioning properly. DOES NOT INCLUDE: G11 Adjusting tire pressure Checking levelness of the ground Creating targets Filling the gas tank R&amp;I of parts for alignment Removing/replacing customer valuables from vehicle Repair of any mounting surfaces Road test dynamic aiming Road test vehicle G14 Road test vehicle G16 Road test vehicle G17 -Electronic Reset – Non included operation, per Motors GTE G9: LABOR TIME DOES NOT INCLUDE: SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. • Reset electronic memory functions after battery disconnect -Feather Edge &amp; Block Sand – Found on P-page G34-G35-G38 Prime &amp; block (high build/primer-filler) is a required procedure that restores a repaired panel surface, including the joined areas of replaced welded panels, from 150-grit finish to NEW UNDAMAGED condition. It is MOTOR’s position that prime and block is a refinishing process best reserved for the judgment of an estimator/appraiser following a thorough on-the-spot evaluation of the specific vehicle and damage in question. DOES NOT INCLUDE: Anti-corrosion material application, Filling, blocking, featheredging repaired panels. Prime &amp; block (high build/primer-filler -Flex Mixing Time – Found on P-page G34. DOES NOT INCLUDE: Anti-corrosion material application Filling, blocking, feather edging repaired panels. Flex additive mixing time Flex prep application Material costs -Glass Cleaner- Any unclear glass, sensors, cameras, etc. can throw off calibrations by up to 1.5 degrees. -Mask and Protect Removed Components – Wrap, store items removed &amp; protect from collateral damage, during the course of repairs. -Mask for Buffing – Protection during de-nib, wet/dry sand and compound, protection from residue in jambs &amp; moldings etc. -Mobile Kart for EV- Mobile kart for EV platform is designed for high voltage safety as a top priority. A high voltage safety system is a set of measures and components designed to protect people and equipment from dangers associated with high voltage electrical systems. To ensure handling safely, proper precautions are taken for systems to be handled safely. Ensuring proper precautions are taken on high voltage vehicles, protecting both vehicle and people from harm. Personal protective equipment (PPE) such as high voltage gloves, protective coated tools, barriers, safety hook, EV fire blankets, class D fire extinguisher, dedicated voltage meter, eye protection, isolated caps to prevent people from encountering high voltage electrocution. In compliance with EV manufacturers. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and Emergency Equipment for particular hazards and applications. Selection and application of PPE always depends on an adequate pre-task risk assessment and must be in compliance with local regulations, norms, and standards. For safe and effective us, PPE must be: Suitable for the purpose for which they are used. Used properly in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions. Well maintained in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions Safely stored in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions. -OEM Research – Found on P-page G6. OEM COMPONENT REPLACEMENT - GENERAL The collision repair facility must have the equipment, training, and experience using the latest factory/industry information and procedures. Ultimately, the safety and quality of any repair will depend not only on the equipment that is used and the procedure that is followed, but also on the skill and knowledge of the repair technicians, the steps they take to control repair quality and how well they check the details of the repair. Some repair operations reported in this guide may be beyond the capability of the repair shop in terms of equipment, skill and knowledge, etc. In this case the repair shop should not attempt the repair. Extreme care and caution should always be used when performing collision repairs on any vehicle. All repairs must be of the highest quality using the latest factory recommended repair procedures with the following concerns kept in mind. Vehicle can not be repaired properly without OEM procedures or research; this is a non-included operation &amp; a on the spot evaluation. -Parts Disposal Fee- Found on P-page G9 G34. Access to repair information/subscription cost -Wet/Dry Sand, Rub-out &amp; Buff Found on P-page G39 WET/DRY SAND, RUB-OUT &amp; BUFF SPECIAL NOTATION: Refinished panels may or may not require a varying amount of wet sanding, compound rub-out or buffing operations in order to match original finish texture Each panel requiring wet sand, rub-out and/or buff (refinish or blend) – Add 30% of full base refinish time Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) Operation Category – is what should be on every repair plan, I say should as this is for your average vehicle repair. An average vehicle repair would be a vehicle that requires calibrations, paint work, part replacement, etc. Operations from each department essentially. -ADAS Diagnostic Report - $25.00 -Automatic Emergency Braking Calibration– as of September 2022 – all vehicles will have emergency braking and will require calibration -Battery Support – 0.2 mechanical -Check and Adjust Tire Pressure – 0.2 mechanical -add to estimate when there is not a wheel alignment on an estimate. Matt Duzel write up for 29007 Front Radar replacement, Program to Vehicle and Calibrate Put the vehicle on battery support. Health check vehicle, report attached. Relearn idle air control valve Initialized driver window and sunroof modules. Initialized Automatic Drive Positioner System Reset/Initialized Heater &amp; Air Conditioning Control System Reset steering angle sensor Checked tire pressures and reset TPMS module. Initialized and configured the front radar ACC module to the vehicle due to replacement. Established communication with the front radar ACC Module. Removed all personal items out of the vehicle. Verified coolant and oil level are at recommended levels. Received vehicle with a 1/4 fuel level. Added weight to make up for the missing fuel. Pulled the required aiming procedures. Created a centerline Measured and allocated the target placement location. Set up the target system and level in the proper location. Static aimed the ACC module due to radar replacement. Cleared all codes generated during the calibration. Test drove vehicle to verify that all modules are operational. Rescanned system to confirm no other dtcs have been generated. Aftermarket and Used Parts Operation Category When ordering / receiving Aftermarket Parts, here are some things to look for: -Lamps - Lumen tester -Condenser / Radiator / Intercooler / etc - -Count the number of fins -Count the number of lines (lines in between fins, what is name?) -Measure the size, measure the gaps, measure the hose attachment size, measure the AC line attachment size, etc -Glass -ADAS Camera functions as normal? -Defrost functions as normal? -Rain sensor functions as normal? -Metal / Aluminum Part / Panel - did you do a hardness test? will the vehicle react the same way in an accident as an OEM part? Is the part the same thickness? Is the part the same material strength (HSS, UHSS, etc) If you are using Used parts, here are some things to add: -Clean and Recondition-1.0 -Repair needed? Body work? Primer? -Overhaul – 2 nd overhaul required if you order, for example, a door that came assembled Blend Operation Category There are many reasons to blend, first, always check with your painter as paint manufacturers and painters may vary. There are multiple things to take into account, such as the following: -If you are replacing a part or panel (Fender, door, quarter, liftgate, (Bumper occasionally, depending on the color, some of the time the bumper doesn’t match neighboring panels to begin with)). Does the replacement part or panel mate to other surfaces? Such as, if you replace a fender, does it sit flush to the door? Does it sit flush to the hood? If so, typically you blend. -If you are replacing a fender and are wondering about blending the roof rail, check with the paint department on the color prior to disassembling everything up (roof rail) and back (quarter) for refinish -If you are repairing a panel, where does your bondo end? Where does your primer end? -Mating Part/Panel – if you are replacing a part/panel, what is the mating surface like to the neighboring / adjacent part/panel? Is it a large or small area? Is it the same angle or a different angle? -Repaired Part/Panel – if a part/panel has been repaired, is the repair area / bondo / primer where you will be adding color to, near the mating / neighboring part/panel? -What kind of paint is it? -Two stage -Three stage -Four stage -Metallic paint When do you choose to Refinish a panel vs a Blend? -If you are painting a fender and a rear door and the front door is inbetween both panels. You wont be extending the clear coat / blend over. You will be refinishing Body Operation Category When it comes to inspection body parts for damage, there are multiple things at play. Take the vehicle color into account when deciding between a repair and a replace, damage on darker colored vehicles are easier to see. Another thing to take into account is that just because the damage can be repaired, doesn’t always mean it should be repaired. Take into account the damage and the repair method, would that part be repaired properly. A good example of this is if the door is damaged on the edge and the framing of the door is buckled. On the exterior, yes the door can be repaired and looking good but on the inside where the outer door skin and the inner door frame meet, can that area be fixed properly back to pre loss condition? Will that framing be worked and out of spec? if the foundation of a house is compromised and you put a coat of paint over it, is it now a good foundation? Crumble zones? Work Hardened part? Color of vehicles – darker easier to see? If you can see the inner panel frame poking through the outer panel damage, what is the proper way to repair that? pictures If the vehicle has damage/dents on the door edge to quarter panel edge, how do you gauge it? How do you know how to hammer the quarter panel / door dmg out? Good indicator is to replace the door, use the new door gaps / body line to gauge how far you need to repair the quarter panel. pictures When it comes to repairing or replacing parts and panels on a vehicle, here are some operations to consider and get started: -A-Pillar – -Land Rover Defender – dash reinforcement is mounted in the way of the rivet / gun head and needs to be removed. -Add for Second Color – Inner Panels – 0.5 refinish -And add color tint and spray out cards (see - Paint Operation Category – for more) -Align - the door took enough of an impact to need replacement, which means the mounting components of door to body have been moved and needed tweaking to ensure all body lines and gaps are in spec -Body Panels - 1.0 body -Hinges - 0.3 body -Striker - 0.3 body -Aluminum facts -99% of dents on aluminum are microcracked. Need to do an attempted repair -Very hard to cold pull aluminum (for stud weld puller and repairs) -Aluminum is one of the fastest oxidation around. You have a few minutes after brushing (to apply studs, or stud weld puller). Need to brush where stud is being installed -Cannot hammer and dolly aluminum as it becomes work hardened -Backing Plates – when listed in OEM repair procedure -Fabricating Backing Plates and Installing – 1 -2 body -Ordering OEM backing plates when listed – use database install labor unit or 0.5 – 1.0 body -Aluminum Room Clean &amp; Setup - ___ -Body Plugs – Add to R&amp;I Body Plugs – 0.1 -Carpet and Insulation – 0.5 body or is this included? -Cavity Wax Injection – 0.3 body per panel -3 mils or 3 passes to be effective -Any repaired panel needs to have body wax added, 5 grams per body labor hour or 1 can per every 100 hours or 1 can a week. -Chip Guard - bill out material. There are a few different ways to write this -Chip Guard cleanup, mask, test, replicate OEM appearance – body labor unit __ -Replacing a part – Replicate chip guard OEM appearance – body labor unit __ -Some vehicles are equipped with this, such as: -Ford Pickup Trucks -Tesla Model 3 -Toyota’s -Clean, prep, re-install seals -1.0 body – need to reapply material so it adheres / sticks -Audi has the foam seals, 2023 volvo has the foam seal. Time is to remove, clean, prep, reinstall. Add line for bolted body component to, such as when the door or hood or liftgate cannot be opened and needs to be cut out -Collision Damage Access – 1.0 body – -Occurs when the outer panel is damaged beyond repair and the inner panel needs to be inspected. Prior to cutting the outer panel, document the damage with all photos, add the product used to make the access hole -Color - take the vehicle color into consideration when considering a repair or replace. Darker colored vehicles tend to show more damage -Corrosion Protection / Rust Proof – each metal / steel part being repaired and the panel being replaced -High Point / Plymouth Rock - $12.00 and 0.2 body - one line at bottom of estimate -Non-DRP – 0.1 refinish – small part -Non-DRP – 0.3 refinish- large part - Various repair procedures may require additional protection to the interior of the vehicle to prevent welding/grinding/ sanding sparks, dust going past the repair zone. Additional consideration for labor and/or materials to protect the vehicle interior is NOT INCLUDED. To reuse cut off the end with pliers. Use a body file to remove the existing tab on the panel -Don’t’s- do not pull it as that will remove material and warp the panel. do not use rolox as that uses a lot of heat -Can bill out Dent Puller or Stud Puller and Studs as 2 separate lines. -Destructive Test Weld –1.0 each weld body – time is not associated with replacement panel. A 2K product will have the proper corrosion protection -0.2 refinish - Add for masking off front side of panel (ex: Covering the front side of the panel when spraying the inside) -Relieve Pressure – 1.0 – 2.0 - if you can not pull the vehicle due to location / vehicle construction / vehicle manufacturer guidelines, you can hammer the part out to restore dimensions and relieve pressure prior to removing and replacing the panel -Remove and Cleanup Debris from Drilling / Grinding – 0.2 body - As per DEG inquiry this is a non-included operation -Repair Backside of repair panel – Time will differ &amp; include Refinish backside of panel (see note above) -Sacrificial Panel - refer to OEM procedures. A sacrificial panel is one that must be removed prior to replacing the original panel you were going with. An example could be if I have to replace a quarter panel on an Audi, the rear body panel has to be removed first. Which will then create additional paint, operations, repairs, etc. - Seam sealer – put labor units in for the department that will be doing the work. Body department – body labor unit. For more information see notes down below – Reliable Automotive Class 4/6/2023 -Stud/Nutsert gun setup- $15 &amp; 0.3 body – includes gas, oil, lubrication, fitment/cylinder/nozzle/head -Install studs/nutserts – 1.0 body -Destructive Stud Test – 0.2 body -Bad Stud – falls off completely. Has a half bump/half moon on the back of the panel -Good Stud – the stud breaks in half, the head stays in the panel. Full bump/full circle on the back of the panel -Clean with solvent and degrease prior to brushing -Stainless Steel Brush – bill out per job – needs to be white so you can see pure aluminum. Brush only where the stud is going -Surge Protector – 0.2 mechanical – protects battery during body, may not apply if you disconnect battery -Tesla Cybertruck – exoskeleton is made from Ultra Hard 30x series Cold-Rolled stainless steel structural skin and Tesla armor glass. -Stainless steel may be refinished according to the specific guidelines in the Collision Repair manual in Part Repairability Guidelines &gt; Exterior Stainless Steel Panel Refinishing -All panels containing the same plane as the repair area must be refinished. So if you are repairing the front door, you have refinish the entire side of the vehicle. - Test structural adhesive prior to applying – 0.1 body -Touch up painted bolts – -Non-DRP - 0.5 refinish -Plymouth rock - 0.3 refinish -Trial Fit - use full R&amp;I labor unit of a part, example - if a door takes 1.5 body units, it will be Trial Fit 1.5 body unit. For a welded part / panel, 0.5-2.0+-. Repairs could even require the same part to be trial fit multiple times. That same door that takes 1.5 body units, could turn into 3.0 body units -Explanation of this: Trial-Fit of a component to verify dimensional accuracy of adjoining parts. Can clamp into place/vice grip, can use self tapping screws, can tack weld Examples are -State Farm does not pay for trial fit, build it into the estimate -Quarter panel – trial fit using vice grips / tack welds prior to installing -Trial fit multiple parts – ex: tail lights, weatherstrip, lock, striker, bumper, reinf - Trial fit parts pre repair, during repair, post repair -Trial fit of parts starts from the furthest point from the damage. With replacement front door, rear door, and quarter panel. Trial fit front door to fender, rear door to front door, quarter panel to rear door. -Time is to put replacement hood and hinges on vehicle to ensure gaps, dimensions, vehicle body is in spec and that the mounting points for the hinges and/or other locations are not damaged. -29813 note: Trial fit bumper with replacement radiator support. After removing damaged radiator support and installing new radiator support, adjustments will be lost and have to be aligned to ensure proper gap dimensions. -Ultra-High Strength Steel – cannot be repaired I-car says (ex: Panel Removal and Preparation for Installation pg 11/22). See OEM procedures. -Zinc and Magnesium can also not be repaired. -Wash and Tack after Blocking - $5.00 and 0.3 body -Wash and Tack after Repairs - $5.00 and 0.3 body -Weld Blankets – 0.3 body -Welding Plates / Welding Studs - $25.00 and 0.5 body -When you are repairing a panel and the damage needs to be pulled out but there is not an actual location to clamp down to (such as the middle of a quarter panel), Welding plates are used to weld to a panel to gain clamping access so that the damage can be pulled -Weld Through Primer – $10.00 -FCA US LLC (Dodge, Chrysler, etc.) does not recommend the use of any type of “weld-thru” primer during repairs. Weld-bonding with corrosion protecting adhesives or sealers, along with final application of inner panel corrosion protection is the proper method. -VW has their own – Refer to procedures. Procedure from job # 28798 – Long-Term Body Protection – D00750004 -Copper weld through primer is no good -Zinc weld through primer is good -Zinc weld through primer must be stored upside down or sideways to prevent it from clogging or having all the zinc stuck at the bottom -Welder Setup Time, Power up, Power down - $25 &amp; 0.5 body per welder -Spot Weld -Plug Weld / Mig / Mag -Welding Shield – 0.2 body -A welding shield is used to prevent sparks from flying into the neighboring vehicle, tools, equipment, etc. It is a barrier. -Welding Supply Consumables -$25 -does this include -Coatings required by OEM for welding -Cost of OEM specific welding coupons -Labor for protecting the vehicle from welding and sparks -Shielding gas -Spark deflection material -Welding tips -Welding wire (Bohler Union x96, Brazin wire, Er70s-6, OEM specific Alu wires, etc.) -Weld Zone Repair – also known as WZR, occurs when you replace a welded body panel and have to perform a repair to the mating panels. Mating panels are what touches the replacement panel, so it can consist of the visual exterior panel, the inner panel, -Small Panel - 0.5 body repair -Large Panel or two mating surfaces- 1.0 body repair -If the vehicle is welded from factory and the replacement part is bonded and riveted, you still have to perform a weld zone repair as the vehicle was once welded -If the panel is also damaged, add repair units to do the repair and repair units for the weld zone repair as it is two separate operations. Collision Damage Access is cutting an access hole so the area can be accessed and opened. Material used to make that access hole should be billed out as it is a not included operation / material . Bill out product used – saw blade, cutting wheel, etc. -Door Check -Loctite – is OEM Procedure stating to use Loctite? -Grease – Refer to OEM Procedure -Door handle -Window regulator -Hinges – -If aligning – 0.3 body -If replacing – stage and secure hinge(s) for refinish -Keyless Entry -Labels -check for labels -Latch and Striker– its best to replace the latch and striker when the impact has occurred in that location or if the body part itself is destroyed and the latch and striker integrity is questionable / compromised. Some latches are now electric -Latch and Striker is best replaced together -We have had Latchs working when we disassembled the vehicle and then fail upon reassembly. -Lift Cylinder – Hood / Liftgate – need to be R&amp;I’d? Need lubrication? -Lock Cylinder – is the lock cylinder damaged and needing to be replaced? Does it require a new key to be cut and coded? Do you need the vehicle owners permission per the OEM? Clear DTCs, and determine which ones reset after battery voltage is stabilized -Here are four possible 12 volt battery conditions via ALLDATA for 2020 honda accord sedan, 12 Volt Battery Removal, Installation, Test -Good battery- the 12 volt battery has at least 60 percent of its charge and required no action -GR8 diagnostics needed-the battery voltage is below 60 percent of its state-of-charge, and the condition of the 12 volt battery is unknown. Use the GR8 to charge the 12 volt battery and properly diagnose it -Replace Battery-the 12 volt battery condition is poor. Replace it -Bad cell-there is an internal problem with the 12 volt battery. There are multiple ways to measure a vehicle depending on the extent of the damage. Before doing any type of measuring, check and remove any parts for access. Listed below are operations regarding Measurements: Car Bench usage tip – minus on the jigs have to be facing in, positive facing out -Clamp Mark Repair (Frame Bench) – 0.4 body and 0.4 refinish -Mask Underside for Clamp Mark Repair Remove the following or re-word it?? I don’t believe its my writing, maybe from a class or someone else -Clamp Marking -clamps used for securing unitized and full frame vehicles to equipment for measuring and repair purposes leave contact witness marks that typically require multiple operations to correct -Pinch Weld Clamping – rocker pinch weld clamps on unitized vehicles deform, disrupt, and pierce paint and chip-resistance coatings. Removal of coatings at contact location that may build up on clamps and reduce their gripping ability is highly recommended. E-coat, chip resistance PVC and primer coatings and topcoats need to be restored to provide the same level of appearance and corrosion protection as the original coatings. To replicate factory e-coat, apply a two-part epoxy primer before applying chip-resistant coatings and topcoats. -Frame clamping – frame clamping also causes witness marks that need to be restored to provide the same level of appearance and corrosion protection as the original coatings. Frame clamp marks should be smoothed then coated with two-part epoxy primer and a single-stage topcoat to match -Clamp mark repair/refinish -different for every job – 1.0 repair/refinish (add clearcoat) (also corrosion protection and feather edge n block sand) -Clamp marking – clamps used for securing unitized and full frame vehicles to equipment for measuring and repair purposes leave contact witness marks that typically require multiple operations to correct -Frame Clamps (Body On Frame Vehicles) – clamp to frame instead of pinch welds – 0.2 R&amp;I -Full Jig Setup – 8.0 Frame -Things to check on, add, review with technician. -Check Approved Jig Build Sheet, some may have with or without suspension in -Remove Front Bumper -Remove Rear Bumper -Remove all Under shields -Remove all 4 Wheels -Jig Arm Attachment, setup, position, takedown – 3.0 fram e -Maserati – Car Bench -Measure and Mark Out Gap Dimensions – 0.4+- -Can be written out in multiple ways -Pre disassembly -Pre and post part replacement -Measure Underside Pre , In Process, Post Repairs /Replacements – 3.0 frame -Add 3D arm attachment for measuring B-Pillar, Door Opening Panel, Engine Compartment, etc – 0.5 frame -Porsche – Car-O-Liner -Pinch Welds – 0.4 body and 0.4 refinish -Remove any chip guard, seam sealer, undercoat prior to clamping vehicle -Remove any parts in the way for access -Rocker moldings -Under shields - Pull Damage and restore dimensions - if allowed by OEM and labor unit differs per damage -B-Pillars on Vehicle – B-Pillars are designed to absorb an impact and are not designed to collapse like other parts of a vehicle. Pulling a B-Pillar will require more labor unit and may not restore the dimensions as pre loss condition -Depending on the extent of the damage, the damage must be pulled out to restore as much of the dimensions as possible and to relieve as much pressure as possible. -Bonded and Riveted vehicles cannot be pulled as it will compromise the bond, the rivets, the structural integrity -Requires vehicle to be secured and clamped down to pull -Setup and Takedown Pull Tower – 0.5 frame -Weld Plates $25 and 1.0 to create an area to pull -Setup – 2.5 frame -Tram Gauge – 0.4 +- body -Truck clamps (body-on-frame vehicle)-0.2 F Paint Operation Category Paint Code formula should be documented on every repair plan, showing that the paint code is 2 / 3 / 4 stage -Add for Second Color – (example) – Inner Panels -0.5 refinish -Add for Second Color Clear – Inner Panels – 0.5 refinish -Add for Third Color – (example) Gloss Black -0.5 – 1.0 refinish - Add for Matte color, Matte clear $$, Extra labor unit due to not being able to de-nib or buff -Booth Maintenance - As per DEG inquiry this is a non-included operation -Bumper – Mask backside of Bumper for refinish – 0.2 refinish -Mask backside of Bumper for ACC Radar / Blind Spot Radar prior to painting to prevent paint residue and extra paint thickness -Mask backside of Bumper for Blind Spot Radar prior to painting to prevent paint residue and extra paint thickness -Bentley Re-Clear – re-clear on the panels/parts -Bentley re clear Paint vehicle, use regular clear, then sand down, then repaint with matte clear. Matte clear may not have UV protection so you paint with regular clear and then repaint it with matte clear 1 - e coat 2 - primer 3- base coat 4- regular clear Sand down 5- matte clear -Color Tint – -Two Stage 0.5 (Base Clear) -Three Stage 1.0 (Three Stage) -Four Stage 1.5 (Four Stage) refinish -EV Vehicles – have a paint booth temperature and time limit, see graph at bottom of section -Flex Additive – -$9.00 DRP – High Point / Plymouth Rock -$15.00 Non-DRP -$10.00 additional part – Non-DRP -Flex Mixing Time –0.3 refinish -Labor unit is to mix Flex Additive into the paint that is used on the Bumper cover (or other flexible plastic part) so that the bumper can flex with paint and not be damaged in the process -Spray Out Cards – 0.5 (Base Clear ) 1.0 ( Three Stage) / 1.5 (Four Stage) refinish -Color Tint and Spray Out cards can be written multiple times per vehicle for operations such as -Color Tint/Spray Out Cards – Body Color -Color Tint/Spray Out Cards – Bumper paint code R for Radar -Color Tint/Spray Out Cards – Inner Panels -Color Tint/Spray Out Cards – Second Color (example - Gloss black, metallic, etc) -Color Tint/Spray Out Cards – Third Color (same as above) -Four Stage Refinish – this is a manual entered line due to Estimating Databases not having a Four Stage Option. Calculation on adding Four Stage Refinish is to take all the paint units for the parts that will be buffed and then x 25% = the number. Example -Bumper – 3.0 -Door (Full paint unit is 3.0 but refinishing the door / buffing, only will be based off of 2.0 refinish unit) – 2.0 -Hood – 3.0 =8 x 25% = 2.0 refinish unit -Matte Clear - $$ -Monitor Flash and Cure Time – 0.2 refinish - As per DEG inquiry this is a non-included operation - Monitor flash times during bake cycles --- Flash times need to be monitored based on ambient air temperature, bake temperature, and drying times as per the paint manufacturer -Prep Unprimed Bumper -Quarter Panel Lock Pillar – lock pillar is the jam/recessed area located behind the door and the “edges and pockets” is the area located under or behind the bumper, trunk lid and/or liftgate when closed INCLUDEPICTURE \d "https://content.app-us1.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=650,dpr=2,fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect/4PRq1/2025/02/10/9a5dfbf6-b920-426d-8017-23288f8a6593.png?r=2116154898" \y \* MERGEFORMATINET \x -Raw plastic prep/unprimed plastic components – Front and Rear bumpers usually have this in additional operations at the bottom but other parts such as rockers, mirror caps or other plastic parts that need to be painted do not. -CCC – 25% of the components base refinish time (maximum time allocation: 1.0 hours. Included removal of mold-release agents as outlined by manufacture, masking (if required), Application of adhesion promoter) – Not included: Correction of pre-existent surface imperfections, material cost -Mitchell – allow .2 per refinish hour (20%) for plastic components that come from the manufacturer/supplier in a raw/un-primed state. Included operations: Detergent wash, Alcohol/plastic cleaner wash, Additional solvent wash, Application of specialized adhesion promoter, clean equipment. -Audatex – the Audatex formula for preparation of a raw, unprimed bumper cover or plastic part is 20% of the base refinish labor -Find this in – Reference Information –&gt; Additional Operations –&gt; Refinish –&gt; Raw Substrate Prep and “Allow 0.2 per refinish hour (20%) for plastic components that come from the manufacturer/supplier in a raw/un-primed state” -Refinish backside of panel – 100% of the exterior refinish labor unit. - Example – quarter -2.6 exterior refinish labor unit = 2.6 refinish labor unit for backside -Use a 2K product (2K product has a hardener). Were all the ADAS calibrated as recommended by the OEM? -Cavity Wax - was cavity wax sprayed / injected? -DE-NIB - was the vehicle de-nibbed? -Emblems / Nameplates - was it installed correctly? is it aligned? is it peeling? -Gaps - Are the gaps in spec? -Hardware - is it the correct hardware? Is it the same hardware or mixed and matched -Leaks - is the vehicle leaking anywhere? -Measure – is the vehicle measurements in spec? -Paint Millimeter - is the paint the correct millimeter? Is it the same throughout? -Procedures - were procedures researched, applied, documented, and followed? -OEM Parts - were OEM Parts used? -Overspray - was the vehicle covered for overspray? Does overspray exist on the vehicle -Rust -Scan - was the vehicle scanned? did it leave with codes cleared / no DTC’s? -Scope the Damage out - inspect in between parts / panels and see if foam, cavity wax, burn marks, etc, -Swirl Marks -Tape Lines -Welds - Was the part/vehicle properly welded? Was it welded in the correct location? Are there burn marks? Was the welds destructively tested? -Wheel Alignment - was the vehicle aligned? Is it in spec? -Wheel Torque- Was the wheel torqued to the correct Nm / Lb-ft? Sheet Molded Compound (SMC)/Carbon Fiber/Fiber Glass Operation Category When it comes to repairing this type of material, please note that it does require time for it to dry properly. Small physical damage could actually take a lot longer due to the drying cycle. Here are a few steps and product when it comes to repairing: -1) Resin – Fibre Glast Epoxy Resin &amp; Epoxy Cure -2) Fiber glass – Fiberglass Mat (in a white bag, red letters) -3) Gorilla hair – Evercoat Fibertech 100633 – Reinforced Repair Compound Filler -4) Filler – Dyantron Fiberglass Filler -5) Primer (25% repair unit– as refinish unit) And treat this part the same as you would on other parts. -Stage and Secure Carbon Fiber Molding for Refinish -DE-NIB -Wet/Dry Sand, Rub-Out and Buff SRS Inspections Required After A Collision Remove people names, remove OEM names. Relocate to your own notes? Keep this general? Cleanup the next SRS lines -Audi – find on General Information Body Repair, Body Collision Repair -&gt; Damage, Evaluating -When repairing vehicles involved in collisions, damage to the body or suspension is not discovered. In some cases, this may cause serious subsequent damage later. In accidents that indicate a heavy load on the vehicle, the following components must be checked – regardless of whether or not the axles were already checked: -Check steering and steering linkages using steering wheel angle for problem-free function, visual inspection for bends or cracks -Check suspension, all suspension parts such as control arm, suspension struts, steering knuckle, stabilizer bar, subframe, axle beam and their fasteners for bends or cracks -Check rims and tires for damage, run-out and imbalance. Check tires for cuts in the tread and the sides and check the tire pressure -Check mounts for engine, transmission, axles and exhaust system for damage -Finally, a proper test drive after repairs ensures that the vehicle is safe to drive and can be handed over to the customer -Overall steering column measure – 1.0 mechanical -Need a compressor to measure -Need to measure new steering column – do it, don’t write for it -Safety inspections – 4.0 mechanical (average time amongst alpha shops) -Honda – Ed May from Honda – Only inspect if deployed, has code, or is damaged -Nissan – send photos thru I-Car for safety inspections -Porsche – If passengers felt an impact, replace steering column. -Subaru – If damage is beyond outer body cosmetic distortion, thus requires the inspection locations after a collision to be performed -State Farm does not pay for this, build it into the estimate. -Toyota – if structural damage or airbags / seatbelt / SRS deployment -Rear seatback inspection SRS Operation Category Check OEM Guidelines / Procedures in regards to SRS Inspections required after a collision and check OEM Guidelines / Procedures if SRS (seatbelt, airbag, etc) deployed to know what else to replace, inspect, program, check for. Cleanup description -Access to Airbags/Seatbelts/Sensors– R&amp;I parts for access -Airbag Residue Cleanup – 1-2 body -+ --Notes from 28434 – 2008 Jeep Wrangler Alldata procedure 11/19/2021 -Following a Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) component deployment, the vehicle interior will contain a powdery residue. This residue consists primarily of harmless particulate by-products of the small pyrotechnic charge that initiates the propellant used to deploy a SRS component. However, this residue may also contain traces of sodium hydroxide powder, a chemical by-product of the propellant material that is used to generate the inert gas that inflates the airbag. Since sodium hydroxide powder can irritate the skin, eyes, nose, or throat, be certain to wear safety glasses, rubber gloves, and a long-sleeved shirt during cleanup. 1. Begin the cleanup by using a vacuum cleaner to remove any residual powder from the vehicle interior. Clean from outside the vehicle and work your way inside, so that you avoid kneeling or sitting on a non-cleaned area. 2. Be certain to vacuum the heater and air conditioning outlets as well. Run the heater and air conditioner blower on the lowest speed setting and vacuum any powder expelled from the outlets. CAUTION: Deployed front airbags having two initiators (squibs) in the airbag inflator may or may not have live pyrotechnic material within the inflator. Do not dispose of these airbags unless you are certain of complete deployment. Refer to AIRBAG SQUIB STATUS. All damaged, ineffective, or non-deployed Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) components which are replaced on vehicles are to be handled and disposed of properly. If an airbag or seat belt tensioner unit is ineffective or damaged and non-deployed, refer to the Hazardous Substance Control System for information regarding the potentially hazardous properties of the subject component and the proper safe handling procedures. Then dispose of all non-deployed and deployed airbags and seat belt tensioners in a manner consistent with state, provincial, local and federal regulations. 3. Next, remove the deployed SRS components from the vehicle. 4. You need to vacuum the interior of the vehicle a second time to recover all of the powder. -Body Harness Wire and Connector to Airbag and Seatbelt – check to see if it melted due to deployment -Cut seatbelts to remove – 0.1 body -Diagnostic Unit – R&amp;I parts for access, R&amp;I customer belongings for console removal?, Replacement, Program / Calibrate -Impact sensor – always R&amp;I - Removed to prevent accident SRS deployment while vehicle is being worked on / transported -Lock Mode – 0.1 mechanical -31534 – 2022 Honda CRV with airbag deployment -Nissan – “recommends that all seat belt assemblies in use during a collision be replaced, unless the collision was minor and he belts show no damage and continue to operate properly” -Pyro Fuse – if SRS is deployed and vehicle is equipped with Pyrotechnic Fuse, the vehicle will become disabled. -Rivian – 2025+ is in the HV battery pack and will require to go to a service center if it doesn’t total out. Shops cannot do that -One Time Use Parts – check OEM procedures, some hardware is thread locked -Seatbelt Inspection – 1.0 M -2027 new seatbelt for rear -Volkswagen – requires 6 different -Distance and stretch -Thump Test of Seat Belt Retractors – -Seat Belt Operational Locking Check – -Static Pendulum Test of all belts – hold the seat belt in hand, tilt it and try to pull it. If not, it will have to be added and billed Stolen Recovery Vehicle Category Stolen Recovery Vehicles can consist of many things, ranging from scratches to dents to missing parts, it is best to review the vehicle with the vehicle owner and the insurance adjuster due to a Stolen Recovery Vehicle could have everything wrong with the vehicle as included. Stolen Recovery Vehicles take priority as it can be pretty emotional to have your vehicle stolen and invasive, some vehicle owners may have had their house broken into and keys stolen or may have been held up for their key. Vehicle Owners may also have not had their vehicle for some time due to it being stolen, then recovered, then transported, then inspected and documented, then the vehicle arrives at your facility, meanwhile the customer may have been paying for a rental during this time. The goal is to make it as smooth as possible for the vehicle owner. Here are some things to look for when reviewing and writing a vehicle that was stolen and recovered: -Biohazard Clean – 1.0 body -Check brakes – 0.3 m -Collision damage inspection– 2 hour + -Curb rash on wheels -Fingerprint powder cleanup -Floor mats – stolen? -Garage Door Opener -Gear Selector Small Cover - small part -GPS Antenna (OEM) -GPS Tracker (Theft Device) -Impound Invoice -Interior Detail - $250 -Keys stolen? – order battery, key, transmitter, recode to vehicle - recode to vehicle -Overhead Console – usually pulled down -Owner’s manual -Missing -Hardware -Parts -Panel Alignment and Gap Dimensions -Pre-Wheel Alignment -Pry Marks -Radio -Remote - for radio -Scratches -Inside Trim Panels and Seats -Outside Panels -Seatbelt Inspection -Smoking - -Ashes -Burn marks -Clean fee - for smoke and ashes -Smoking smell - $125 -Stains – included with interior detail -Test Drive – 0.5 m -Tow Bill -Underside scrapes -Wheel Lock and Wheel Lock Key -Wires - any wires ripped out / cut -Hotwired - Underneath steering wheel / dash Suspension Operation Category When it comes to suspension damage on a vehicle, it can be a bit tricky. There are many variants of suspension setups, with two main suspensions, Solid Axle or Independent Suspension. Any time a vehicle has wheel and/or tire damage, it is recommended to have a pre wheel alignment done on a vehicle. A pre wheel alignment is checking the specs of a vehicle to see if it is or is not in spec. If it is not in spec, can it be adjusted into spec? If it cannot be adjusted into spec, what parts does it need to have that vehicle back into spec? If you do not have access to a wheel alignment measurement, what should you look for when inspecting suspension damage. How are the bushings? Are the bushings torn, are the bushings tilted/angled/misaligned? How are the bolts, are the bolts misaligned? Can you see see an alignment mark from where it was originally? Does the wheel look pushed back/forward/in/out? Did the wheel in question get measured and compared to the opposite side? Another thing to take into account is if you need to remove any parts to gain access to the suspension such as Exhaust, Undershield, etc. Here are some operations to add and look for when working on suspension: -ABS Sensor – -ABS Sensor may need to be programmed to vehicle as per a 2020 Jeep Gladiator -Aftermarket Suspension- -Does customer have specific specs needed / requested? -Have customer sign a waiver stating they requested X specifications due to aftermarket suspension -Will the vehicle be calibrated properly with the aftermarket setup? -Alignments- There are four different types of alignments -Maintenance – struts, tires, worn parts -Symptom/Corrective – steering wheel off, pulls -Performance – Lowering, Raising, Customizing -Collision – damage parts, structural, calibrations, ride height -Activate Park Brake Release – 0.1 mechanical -Rivian– turns off after 15 minutes -For four wheel alignment -Add Weight for Wheel Alignment – 0.2 body -Some vehicles require weight to be placed in the vehicle while you perform the wheel alignment, such as: -Tesla Model 3 and Y - as stating in the Four Wheel Alignment procedure and on the Hunter Alignment System -Air Suspension Leveling – 0.3 mechanical -Tesla Model X -Air Suspension Depressurize / Pressurize – One Air Spring – 0.6 mechanical -Audi – Vehicle Lift Mode -Rivian 1.0 mechanical -Rivian – go to website, Tools-&gt; Diagnostic. Search Compressor and depressurize/pressurize pops up. -Rivian – use RiDE and search Compressor. Procedure code is 185420010. Discharge procedure and charge procedure. -Brakes -Bleed Brake System -Brake Fluid -Bleed the ABS pump - __ - bleeding the brakes does not include bleeding the ABS pump. The estimated work time of 0.5 hour applied to Bleed Brake System is for those situations when the Brake Hydraulic Line is open at one or all four Suspension points (caliper/wheel cylinder). Bleeding the ABS Unit was not considered in the estimated time allowance for Bleed Brake System. -Bushings - are bushings listed separately? Do they require labor unit for removal and installation? -Caliper – Occasionally, if working on the suspension, you can remove the caliper and hang it up and not have to disconnect the brake line and not have to replace brake fluid -Rivian Kinetic Suspension Evac and Fill Cart -1.0 mechanical – functions like an AC machine -Keep bumper harness plugged in otherwise ride height cant be adjusted Regarding the use of the Kinetic Suspension evac and fill cart, the current state of the Service Manual and the RiDE procedure should have all the documentation that the shop needs. Jacking compensation lifts the vehicle into the air . Vehicle is not sitting on its wheels/tires. Used if vehicle has a flat or damaged wheel -R&amp;I 4x wheel covers for wheel alignment – -Release/Engage Parking Brake – 0.3 Mechanical -Ride Height - -Measure and Calibrate Ride Height - 1.0 mechanical -Measure Ride Height - 0.5 mechanical -Measure Ride Height Pre and Post Part Replacement - 1.0 mechanical -Ride Height Adjustment - 0.5 mechanical -Ride Height Calibration - 0.5 mechanical -Ride Height Mode - 0.1 mechanical -Rivian -Road Test Vehicle – also referred to as Dynamic Systems Verification for 1.0 mechanical -Shock Disposal / Strut Disposal - $5.00 and 0.2 mechanical -Shock/Strut must have the gas discharged. Methods can be the following: -Drill a hole -Use a hacksaw and make a hole -31340 – 2024 Lexus RX 350 – place the front shock absorber horizontally with the piston rod extended, and using a hacksaw, make a hole between (A) and (B) shown in the illustration to discharge the gas inside -31333 – 2022 Chevy Camaro – make an indentation 10mm (0.4 inch) from the bottom of the tube using a centerpunch. Clamp. Drill a hole in the shock absorber at the centerpunch using a 5mm ( 3/16 in) drill bit. Gas or a gas/oil mixture will exhaust when the drill bit penetrates the shock absorber. Drill a second hole, etc, etc. -Spring Compressor – to remove sprin g from vehicle – 0.5 mechanical (per spring) -Steering Angle Sensor – 1.0 unit mechanical, -Refer to OEM Procedure for calibrating the steering angle sensor during a wheel alignment -Steering Gear – check procedures, and such -Refer to OEM Position Statement on Steering Gear Damage and Inspection. -Replace steering gear after any damage to the front suspension per OEM guidelines / Position statements. Typically Electronic Steering Gear must be replaced if Front Suspension is damaged. -Does the Steering Gear need to be programmed? -Steering Column bolt to Steering gear – replace -Steering Gear Force Gauge – 0.2 M - Measure / inspect / test -Steering Wheel Lock – 0.1 M (not included with four wheel alignment) -Use a digital inclinometer tool or leveler -Subframe Fixture – 0.2 mechanical - Used to lower and raise subframe from vehicle -Suspension Check – Retorque – 0.3M -Suspension replacement – mark the suspension with a marker once you tighten everything so you could see if it was moved. -Tie Rod - when outer tie rod nut -Tip on replacing tie rods -Check the measurement of the original tie rod and use that measurement to help install the replacement tie rod. Then when you do the wheel alignment, you may only need to adjust it slightly as you are using pre loss specs -Tire Change Mode – 0.1 mechanical -Rivian – need to put vehicle into Tire Change Mode as per online class ‘Collision/Service: Rivian R1T Wheel Alignment -Tooling - is the vehicle requiring any specific tooling that you do not have at your facility? Perhaps it is requiring a specific socket for that particular vehicle -Tow Mode -Replacing Rear Knuckle on Tesla Model Y -Wheel Alignment Mode – 0.1 mechanical -EV Hummer Total Loss Charges Category When a vehicle becomes a Total Loss, that does not mean the vehicle has become a loss at your facility. For a vehicle to become a total loss at your facility, there are operations you as a shop have done for the vehicle status to get to that point. Here are some operations you can bill out (as long as they apply to the work done): Before you send over any Total Loss Charges to the insurance company, the customer has to accept the offer from insurance. If customer is unhappy with the offer from insurance, the customer can reach out to a company that will help get them a different valuation such as Vehicle Value Experts -ADAS Diagnostic Report - $25.00 -Administration Fee - $300 – To create the work file for CCC, Shopman, check the car in, receive information, etc -Assist customer with belongings – 1.0 hour body. -Bags and Totes $$ -If bags and totes were given to vehicle owner as assistance and good faith, charge them out -Blueprint Fee – 10% of overall estimate -Blueprint Fee is if the vehicle was completely disassembled for repair and a completed estimate / supplement was written and then after the vehicle was deemed a total loss -Collision Damage Inspection – 1.0 body unit -Collision Wrapped -Color Check -Cover and Protect Electrical Connections - $5.00 and 0.3 body unit -Cover Steering Wheel, Floor Mat, Seat Cover – $ 5.00 &amp; 0.2 body unit -Seat cover – 0.1 – non-disposable -Delete Customer Information – 1.0 –2.0 body unit -customer address, saved locations, phone numbers, etc -Every vehicle is different and we have to learn what options and which systems this vehicle has and then see what the customer has programmed in and learn to clear it. -Disassembly Labor Units - how many units does your technician have into it? If the technician has 10.00 into the vehicle, that is 10.00 units away from another vehicle -Estimate Documentation - $50.00 -EV Battery - https://www.instagram.com/reel/CwX7seaICpa/?igshid=MzRlODBiNWFlZA%3D%3D Interesting how they do this. -Glass cleanup - 1-2 body units -Hazardous Waste Removal - $7.50 -Such as leaking fluids or material that was removed from the vehicle that won't be going back with the vehicle as a total loss -Parts Handling Fee –$20.00 &amp; 2.0 body unit +/- - - Includes wrapping, storing, labeling, organizing, retrieving and delivering -Damaged Parts -R&amp;I Parts -Parts Restocking Fee - $___ different for every job -Pullout Fee – 1.0-hour body – Pulling vehicle out, removing all of your facilities information (estimate, window tag, work order, etc.) and taking the vehicle to the tow truck -Reinstall Parts - -Depending on the condition, parts can be reinstalled for a variety of reasons, reasons can be: -To make it easier for the vehicle to be picked up and transported to a yard -If the customer is buying the vehicle back or removing it from your facility to go else where -Have vehicle owner sign a waiver -The Labor unit will vary on reinstalling parts due to: -Condition of the damages. -Does the vehicle owner have the original receipt for the modification part and the original receipt for the install? -Accessory Switch -Aftermarket Research - $50+- and 1.0+- body -Bags - suspension -Bug Deflector -Bull Bar -Bumper Guard -Ceramic Coating -Removal – 1.0 body per panel -Application - $200 per panel -Cross Bars on Roof -Dash Cam - front, front and rear -Door Edge Guard -OEM – usually metal -Aftermarket – usually a flexible/bendy part -$15.00 and 0.3 body -$ and Labor unit -Adhesive cleanup -Exhaust System -Handle Sill Protector -Hitch -Does the vehicle require a cutout on the bumper for the hitch -Hitch accessory -License Plate Bracket -Light Bar -PPF / Clear Bra -Once lamps (foglamps, headlamps, taillamps) are PPF’d, they cannot be undone as it risks delaminating -PPF Removal and Adhesive Residue Cleanup-1.0 - 2.0 body -PPF Application – Price varies -Pinstripe Painted or Taped – -Cleanup Taped Pinstripe / Removal– 0.3 body per -Painted –1 hour refinish each panel -Prep for panel/vehicle for second color (time to mask, time to color tint, spray out card, time to swap out for second paint) -Taped –$25 and 0.3 body each panel test -For dual pinstripes – double the $amount and labor unit -Radar Detector -Roll Bar -Sill Protectors -Step Pad Accessory -Stereo System -Suspension -Specific specifications as per customer / suspension? This means this tire was made in the 25 th week of 2014. Most tires expire in 6-10 years. This number is only on one side of the tire. https://www.instagram.com/reel/Cy6yqQcvXvS/?igsh=MWVhbW1xdHl4aHhqaQ== -Bring Tire(s) to Mounting Temperature – 0.1 m -Audi Position Statement / Procedure – 5.6 Tires, Bringing to Mounting Temperature -Center Cap Emblem – does the center cap emblem point to the TPMS / Valve stem? -Porsche - https://www.instagram.com/reel/C0FodZgPZXo/?igshid=NTYzOWQzNmJjMA== -Jack stand– 0.1 body -Lug nut covers – 0.1 body -Machined Style Wheel – Must replace -Two toned, machined wheel. -Deep scratches through machined portion. -Scratches through painted black portion. -Cannot repair machined style wheels. -Repairs to machined wheels consist of shaving the surface down to rid the damage. -Example of a machined wheel listed below at the bottom of this category -Pre-Balance and Dismount – 0.3 mechanical -add more note to this? Are you re-balancing it and re-mounting it? - Pre-balance and Dismount to determine if wheel can be used with new tire.Found wheel is bent and unable to balance. -Check Balance – balancing wheel/tire as is – 0.3 mechanical -Add notes – found wheel to be balanced or wheel cannot be balanced. -Remove locking lug nut – 0.2 mechanical -Spare Wheel -If the vehicle has a spare tire mounted on the backside of the vehicle (not underside) and the vehicle was involved in a rear end hit, Check and see if the spare wheel is balanced, or if its damaged. -If the vehicle was driven on a spare wheel, does the wheel state how long it can be driven on? Example if the wheel states it can only be driven for 25 miles and the vehicle owner drove 50 miles, that will require replacement -Tire Disposal - $5.00 (non-taxed) sublet – per tire -Tire Mount and Balance – -$20 High Point / Plymouth Rock -0.5 mechanical for Non-DRP - Tire Mounting Paste - $2.00 - total for all wheels/tires -Tire Repair Kit - $ and labor unit -Tire Replacement – use DEG link that you saved and look up the owner’s manual regarding when to replace all four or two tire’s to match the opposite side on the same axle https://degweb.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/01/Manufacture-owners-support-weblinks.pdf -Tire Tax - $1.50 (non-taxed) sublet – per tire -Tire Valve Stem - __ different for every vehicle -Torque Wheel(s) ( __ Nm / __ Lbs-ft) – 0.2 mechanical per wheel -TPMS Sensor / Valve Stem – does this part require replacement -TPMS Sensor Disposal - $5.00 and 0.2 mechanical -Remove battery, remove urethane, cut battery terminal -31340 – 2024 Lexus RX 350 - dispose of tire pressure warning valve and transmitter. Remove the urethane that protects the lithium battery and the circuit board. Cut the 2 terminals to remove the lithium battery from the tire pressure warning valve and transmitter -TPMS Sensor Recalibration - $75 or 1.0 mechanical – refer to OEM procedure -Tesla – use own calibration labor unit -31410 – 2017 Toyota Rav4 – same thing as above -31430 – 2018 Nissan Altima – remove battery, remove urethane, cut battery terminal, -Universal spare wheel – 0.2 mechanical -If the vehicle does not have a spare wheel, a universal spare wheel will be installed if the vehicle has a flat tire or if the wheel is being sent out for repair and you want to keep the vehicle mobile -Wheel Repairs - refer to OEM Position Statement on what is allowed for a wheel repair. Some manufacturers state you can not remove or add material and you can only repair a cosmetic scratch through the clear coat. -Does the wheel have a label on it that can be replaced or re-created? Some vehicle manufacturers do not sell the label, therefore, you cannot correct repair that wheel to pre-loss condition. Example of a label on a wheel listed below at the bottom of this category Wheel Weights – $5.00 - per wheel Label on a wheel Audi (wheel is also machined) Rivian Machined wheel of a Hummer EV “ Estimating Tip: CCC, Mitchell and Audatex – TPMS Sensors (Tire Pressure Monitoring System) When wheels need to be replaced, it is important to follow the OEM Repair procedure and protocol when dealing with valve stems and TPMS Sensors. Many OEM may require the TPMS Sensor be rebuilt using a “parts kit” Anytime the sensor nut is loosened or the tire is replaced. Rebuilding TPMS Sensors is NOT INCLUDED as well as the cost of the repair kit. CCC/Motors GTE Guide States that TPMS Sensor labor is INCLUDED when replacing a wheel. Rebuilding a TPMS Sensor is NOT INCLUDED. Refer to inquiry 12423 Mitchell: CEG Guide under wheels: TPMS Sensor R/I or R/R is NOT INCLUDED in the wheel replacement labor time Audatex: Labor report may include the TPMS Sensor labor as INCLUDED when replacing a wheel. Rebuilding a TPMS Sensor is NOT INCLUDED unless specifically stated in the labor report. Additionally: Electronic reset or programming of the TPMS System is NOT INCLUDED and will require an on the spot evaluation. SCRS also offers a TPMS Relearn Chart to serve as a quick reference for technicians working on TPMS equipped vehicles. The chart can be found at https://scrs.com/product/tpms-relearn-chart ” Training Classes – written out ADAS Operation and Service (1/5) Lighting and Vision Assist -Adaptive Lighting Systems – -Adaptive lighting is designed to create better visibility during night driving by automatically optimizing the light on the road ahead.
