Features of an adaptive lighting system (ALS) may include the ability of the headlamps to: -Turn in the direction of travel -Automatically level -Automatically switch between high and low beam -Dim or turn off select high beam lights -Vehicle makers have different names for ALS such as adaptive driving beam, adaptive front lighting system, or intelligent light system -Adaptive Lighting System Operation and Parts -The ALS works by receiving input from various sensors and modules. Input data may be processed directly by the ALS control module or the data may first go to other control modules such as the : -Engine control module -Transmission control module -Electronic brake control module -Body control module -Adaptive front lighting parts include: -Swiveling and leveling actuators -Control modules -Sensors, such as the steering wheel angle sensor, vehicle speed sensor, and vehicle ride height sensors -Adaptive Driving Beam Headlamps – adaptive headlamps turn to follow curves in the road and to better illuminate the roadway ahead for the driver. These headlamps can have incandescent halogen, high-intensity discharge (HID), or light-emitting diode (LED) bulbs. They have fail-safe features that return the headlamps to the straight-ahead and low-beam positions in the event of a communication error with system inputs. Adaptive front lighting systems generally do not operate with the transmission in reverse or at speeds less than 2 mph or (3.2 km/h). Movement of the headlamps may be limited at low vehicle speeds, and full movement of the lamps may not be allowed until a certain speed is attained. The steering angle sensor is the primary input for adaptive headlamps -Auto-Leveling Headlamps and Leveling Sensors – the ride height sensors are the primary input for the auto-leveling headlamps. Vehicle speed, acceleration, and braking data is also used to determine the correct level for the headlamps. This allows the headlamps to retain at the optimal position as the vehicle ride height changes due to braking, acceleration, or heavy loads. There can be up to four ride height sensors on a vehicle. Each system is unique. Some use a single ride height sensor mounted in the rear while others may use two or more located in the front and rear. The sensors are typically located on suspension points near the wheel. Calibration is typically required if a leveling sensor is removed or replaced. -High Beam Assist- the high beam-assist system turns the high beams on and off based on information from a front facing camera that detects color, intensity, and movement of light. This system ensures the best headlamp setting to illuminate the road ahead regardless of whether you are driving on a highway, a country road, or in the city. The high beam-assist system monitors approaching vehicle headlamps to turn off the high beams. It also monitors other light sources, like tail lamps of another vehicle, to determine if the high beams should be turned off. -Front Facing Camera – the front facing camera is typically found near the rearview mirror. It detects ambient light and light from vehicles ahead to determine the activation and deactivation of the high beams. Like other cameras, the lens needs to be kept clean to function properly. It is also important the windshield is clean and defect-free for the camera to work properly. Calibration may be required if the front facing camera or the windshield are removed or replaced. -Steering Wheel Sensor – used for various advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), the steering angle sensor (SAS) helps determine the angle of the headlamps when the steering wheel is turned. The SAS may be part of the steering column clock-spring assembly, located along the steering shaft or use data from the electronic power assist steering (EPAS) motor position sensor to determine how many degrees the steering wheel is turned. The SAS may require calibration if it is removed or replace, if a wheel alignment is performed, or a diagnostic trouble code (DTC) indicates calibration is required -Night Vision – -Night vision systems extend the perception of the driver beyond the reach of the headlamps. Night vision works by using a thermal imaging camera to detect infrared (IR) light. It is typically located low in the front bumper or grille. This makes the camera and its mounting brackets particularly susceptible to front-end collision damage. night vision assist systems are broken into two types: -Active, which uses near-IR imaging -Passive, which uses thermal or far-IR imaging -Most systems use passive night vision, although some systems may use both -Active Night Vision and Infrared Illumination – active night vision systems detect near-IR, which falls outside the visible spectrum of light. Because near-IR light does not contain any heat, an active night vision system must use IR flood lamps on the vehicle to illuminate the darkness for the camera. An IR camera can see this light and displays the image on screen. The visible range for active night vision is about 600’ (183m) but may vary depending on the vehicle system configuration. Active systems provide a sharper image than passive night vision systems but are not as effective in rainy or foggy conditions -Passive Night Vision – passive night vision systems do not use an IR light source to illuminate the road but instead capture the thermal radiation given off by objects. All objects emit thermal, or far-IR light waves, which is father from the visible light spectrum than near-IR. Because IR light is emitted from the source, the range for passive IR is greater than that of active IR, typically about 1,000’ (300m). Rain and fog have much less impact on a passive system. These systems don’t produce as sharp of an image as active systems -Night Vision Display Options- -Depending on the specific vehicle, the night vision display may be on the: -Head-up display -Infotainment screen -Driver information center -In some systems, the image can be viewed on multiple displays at the same time, such as a dedicated screen and the head-up display. Although some systems may add color to highlight objects for greater visibility, most images are displayed in black and white. Some systems provide additional alerts if a pedestrian is detected ADAS Operation and Service (2/5) Braking, Steering, and Collision Assist -Advanced Brake Systems- -Advanced brake systems use control modules and sophisticated software to provide braking features beyond anti-lock braking systems (ABS). many of these systems rely on the ABS for operation and include: -Hill start and hill descent control -Automatic brake hold -Some advanced brake systems work in harmony with ADAS systems to provide a safer driving experience. Advanced braking systems that are partnered with ADAS includes emergency braking assist and automatic emergency braking. -Hybrid and electric vehicles (EV) also have advanced braking unique to them, including: -Regenerative braking -One-pedal driving -Regenerative Braking – hybrid and EVs use regenerative braking to help recharge the batteries during driving. Electric motors are used to slow the vehicle and generate electricity to charge the batteries simultaneously. To maximize efficiency, regenerative braking is used as much as possible, but friction braking is still needed for faster stopping as well as ABS, traction control, stability control, and other emergency braking systems -One-Pedal Driving – one-pedal driving allows drivers of hybrid and EVs the ability to accelerate and stop their vehicle by using only one pedal. This is made possible by regenerative braking. The magnetic resistance of the electric motor can slow the vehicle to a full stop. When one-pedal driving is active, the brake lights function just as if you were pressing the brake pedal. One-pedal driving is a way to maximize EV range by regeneration the most electricity possible while driving. It also reduces wear on the conventional brake system parts. One-pedal driving is not good for emergency stops, which still require a hydraulic brake system -Hill Start – like one-pedal driving, hill start ensures a vehicle stays stopped until the accelerator is engaged. Hill start systems use data from sensors to determine if the vehicle is on an include while stopped. The ABS holds the vehicle in position long enough for the driver to take their foot off the brake and move it to the accelerator pedal. This is especially helpful on a manual transmission vehicle to prevent rolling backwards before the clutch engages -Hill Descent Control – hill descent control regulates vehicle speed to maintain a slow, safe, controlled descent when driving downhill. When the system is active, it works by using the ABS to maintain a speed set by the driver. The speed at which the system works varies by make and model. This information can usually be found in the service information under the ABS description and operation -Automatic Brake Hold – the automatic brake hold system holds the vehicle in position using the ABS until the accelerator is depressed. This allows the driver to maintain their position in stopped traffic without having to keep their foot on the brake. This is especially helpful in stop-and-go traffic because it helps to avoid fatigue. Some systems will automatically set the electronic parking brake if automatic brake hold is active for longer than a set length of time -Advanced Brake System Parts – advanced brake system parts may include: -The ABS -Wheel speed sensors -Inclination sensors, accelerometers, and other sensors that may be part of a module such as the restraints control module -Electric drive motors on hybrids and EVs -Additional control modules, electric park brake actuators, and sensors -Lane Departure Warning System – the lane departure warning system helps the driver keep the vehicle in a chosen lane. The system uses audio, visual, or steering wheel vibrations to warn the driver that the vehicle is drifting out of the lane, allowing the driver to correct the position of the vehicle. Lane departure warning assist systems typically operate at speeds of 35 mph (56 km/h) or above, depending on the vehicle maker -Lane Keep Assist System – lane keep assist systems monitor the position of the vehicle in the lane and take corrective action to help keep the vehicle there. This type of system can apply steering torque using the EPAS. Both lane keep assist and lane departure warning systems use the front facing camera or cameras mounted to the windshield to watch the lane markings on the road. Good lane markings are required for the system to operate. Many systems have a display on the instrument panel which shows the lane markings visible to the camera and if the vehicle is drifting out of the lane. The system is temporarily deactivated if the turn signal is used to allow a lane change without prompting an alert -Traffic Sign Recognition – traffic sign recognition systems use a front facing camera to locate road signs and display them on a screen. The camera used for sign recognition may be dedicated to the system, or it may use a camera from another ADAS system. This system is intended to assist the driver in following posted road sign instructions and regulations. For example, a vehicle may use a vibration or audible warning if it is not following the posted speed limit. The system may also save recognized signs to more easily identify them in the future. Always look at service information to verify the system parts and servicing requirements -Auto Speed Limit – auto speed limit, also called intelligent speed assist, helps the vehicle maintain a safe, legal speed. If the speed limit is exceed, the driver is alerted so they can reduce the speed. Systems may have user settings that allow the vehicle to automatically change the speed to match the posted limit. This feature uses traffic sign recognition features and may use GPS system to calculate legal speed limits. It is tied into the adaptive cruise control system to help regulate vehicle speed. -Lane Departure Warning and Lane Keep Assist System parts – the primary parts of the lane departure warning and lane keep assist may include a: -Camera, typically located behind the windshield near the rearview mirror -Control module -Lane departure warning ON/OFF switch -Vibrator motor, if equipped -Drive information center -Audio system for audible warning notification -EPAS -Steering torque sensor -Collision Avoidance Systems – collision avoidance systems are designed to predict a collision by using the front facing camera as well as the front radar module, if equipped. Some systems may function using only radar. The system monitors traffic to help prevent or minimize the severity of a collision. The system determines the distance between vehicles and how fast that distance is closing. It will forewarn the driver through audio or visual signals. The warning prompts the driver to either slow down or take evasive maneuvers. If a warning is given and the actions taken by the driver are not evasive enough, these systems can step in and help by applying braking and steering -Collision Avoidance Brake Assist Systems -Collision avoidance can engage the brakes without driver input to prevent a crash or lessen the impact of a crash. It includes: -Emergency braking assist -Automatic emergency braking (AEB) -Many modules and communication networks work together with these advanced systems, but they still rely on the base ABS to work. If the sensors or cameras used in collision avoidance systems are unbolted or moved, the corresponding system will most likely require calibration. Always check vehicle-maker service information to verify if calibration is required while servicing a vehicle. -Emergency Braking Assist – emergency braking assist can apply the brakes at full force if needed. A brake pedal position sensor is used to calculate how forcefully a driver is applying the brakes. It can send a command to the ABS to apply full stopping force move quickly, and with more pressure, than the driver might be able to generate on their own -Automatic Emergency Braking – AEB automatically applies the brake without driver intervention to avoid collisions. AEB sensors may include radar, laser, or camera systems, or a combination of technologies, to monitor activity in front of or behind the vehicle. Ultrasonic-based parking sensors in the bumper covers may also be used for low-speed AEB -Evasive Steering Assist – some vehicles offer evasive steering assist, which works with the emergency braking assist system. By using radar sensors and cameras, the system can detect a slow moving or stationary obstacle. It can help steer around these to avoid a collision. Typically, an audible warning or a visual display warning alerts the driver. If no action is taken, the emergency brake assist system will activate. If a driver then chooses to use evasive driving to avoid a collision, evasive steering assist provides the amount of steering torque needed -Collision Avoidance System Parts -Depending on the system options, the collision avoidance system uses: -Radar sensors -Front facing cameras -Control modules -A front millimeter-wave radar sensor is used to detect vehicles and obstacles on the road ahead. Some vehicles use additional medium-range radar modules to expand the field of view of the collision avoidance system. These are typically located behind the front bumper cover at the front corners of the vehicle -Attention Alert Systems – attention alert systems use many sensor inputs, such as the steering angle sensor and the steering torque sensor. These are used to monitor driver behavior patterns which are assessed in real-time. If the system detects abnormal behavior, it will alert the driver and suggest a pause from driving. These systems may also monitor: -Continuous drive time -If a driver’s hands are on the wheel -The driver’s concentration level or drowsiness -Some vehicles will slow to an automatic emergency stop if the alert fail to correct the driver’s behavior -Attention Alert Parts – attention alert system parts may include the: -Driver-facing cameras -Steering torque sensor -Steering angle sensor -Drive time monitor -Driver information center -Speakers for audible alerts ADAS Operation and Service (3/5) Parking Assist -Parking Assist – parking assist has progressed to the point where most vehicle makers offer the system as an option or standard equipment on their new models. There are two types of parking assist. These include: -Assist only, where the system provides visual and audio feedback to help determine the distance to obstacles -Hands-free or active parking assist -Parking Assist Operation – with a parking assist system, the driver maintains complete control of the vehicle. This system works at low speeds, typically around 11 mph (18 km/h) or less. These systems typically use cameras to provide the driver with a real-time view of the bumper and nearby objects. Distance sensors provide audible signals as the vehicle approaches an object. Some models provide braking assist to help avoid a collision. Some systems work both in REVERSE and in DRIVE. Many systems engage when they detect an object about 8’ (2.4m) from the vehicle. This feature can usually be switched ON or OFF on the instrument panel. -Active Parking Assist – some systems have been designed to automatically parallel or perpendicular park the vehicle into an open parking space. These systems may also be able to pull the car out of the parking space. Once a parking space is confirmed, the driver may only need to apply the brakes and shift the transmission. The vehicle performs the steering, using the parking sensors for guidance. Other parking assist systems can drive the vehicle into or out of a parking stall using a function on the remote, without a driver inside the vehicle. This is helpful for tight parking situations where it may be difficult to open a door to get in or out -Ultrasonic Sensors – parking assist sensors are designed to calculate the distance to an object. These are typically ultrasonic sensors, which emit sound waves to measure the distance from an object. These sensors are effective for short-distance object detection and are mounted across the front and rear bumpers and occasionally on the sides of the vehicle. In addition to audible alerts, most vehicles have a display that shows where objects are being detected and how far away they area. There is a quick check that can be done if a sensor is suspected of not working. You can hear a working sensor ticking if you use a mechanic’s stethoscope, or a piece of tubing between the sensor and your ear. -Parking Assist Cameras – rearview cameras are mounted on the deck lid, rear gate, or bumper of the vehicle to provide a view of the rear bumper area. These may be built into the emblem or the rear gate handle. Front-view cameras are often mounted in the grille to provide a view of the front bumper area. Images are displayed on the infotainment screen and may have active guidelines that move with the steering to show the projected path of the vehicle -Surround-View Cameras - -Surround-view cameras, also called 360 degree view cameras, use a system of cameras to view the entire area immediately around the vehicle. These cameras may also be shared with parking assist and other ADAS features. While the location of these cameras vary, they generally are located: -In the front bumper or grille -On the door mirrors -On the rear gate, deck lid, or rear bumper -More advanced surround-view camera systems may include a camera on each end of the front bumper cover for cross-traffic view, or multiple cameras for the rear view ADAS Operation and Service (4/5) Radar-Based Systems -Adaptive Cruise Control – the adaptive cruise control (ACC) is an extension of conventional cruise control systems. The ACC system automatically maintains a set distance from a vehicle ahead. However, it is not a fully autonomous driving system. Parts of the ACC system may be integrated into the collision avoidance systems. The ACC also enhances driver convenience by accelerating and braking without driver intervention. As with conventional cruise control systems, manual inputs from the driver, both to the accelerator and brake, take priority over the ACC system -ACC Operation – the radar-based ACC distance sensor uses a radar module located on the front of the vehicle that detects the presence of a vehicle ahead as well as the distance from that vehicle. Radar sensors may be exposed or behind the front bumper or grille. There are some systems that combine their radar and forward-facing camera to improve the accuracy of ACC. Some vehicles use only their front facing cameras for ACC&gt; these systems typically use dual cameras using two images to calculate the distance to the leading vehicle. These types of ACC systems usually do not have a front radar module -ACC LiDAR Systems – some vehicles are using LiDAR instead of a radar module to detect and calculate the distance of an object or vehicle ahead. Instead of using reflected radio waves like a radar, LiDAR uses the reflected light of an invisible laser to calculate the distance to the vehicle ahead. LiDAR sensors are less common and may be exposed on the bumper, grille, or behind the windshield. like radar ACC, some systems may use LiDAR combined with input from a front facing camera to improve system accuracy. -ACC Control Modules – the control modules include the ACC module in addition to the ABS and powertrain control module (PCM). The primary function of the ACC module is to process the radar information and determine if a forward vehicle is present. When the ACC system is engaged, the system sends information to the PCM and ABS module to control the distance between the ACC vehicle and the target vehicle. It accomplishes this by varying the throttle position and applying the brakes if necessary. -Hands-Free Driving – while fully autonomous vehicles are not yet available, manufacturers continue to add features that get a littler closer each year. Some vehicle makers offer hands-free steering, but this feature only works on roads that meet certain requirements to operate safely. Drivers are still required to keep their eyes on the road which is verified by driver-facing cameras -Blind Spot Detection – the blind spot detection system calculates if a warning should be issued when an approaching vehicle enters the blind spot area. The indicator will illuminate sooner if the approaching vehicle is traveling at a high rate of speed. Another advanced system incorporates collision-avoidance with the blind spot system. In addition to other blind spot alerts, these systems apply braking force if a vehicle is detected in the blind spot while the driver attempts to change lanes. Some vehicle systems apply corrective steering torque using their EPAS. -Blind Spot Detection Radar Sensors and Parts – most vehicles use radar sensors for blind spot detection. Some vehicles use both cameras and radar for their blind spot systems. The cameras, usually mounted in the door mirrors, may be used by multiple driver-assist systems in addition to blind spot detection. Blind spot detection system parts include radar sensors, which are generally located behind the rear bumper cover or taillamps, on each side. A control module calculates speed, object position, and vehicle tracking. On systems with cameras, when the turn signal is switched on, the feed from the blind spot camera will be displayed for the driver -Warning Indicators – along with a visual alert on the door mirror, there may be an audible warning and a message in the instrument panel to indicate an approaching vehicle. In some cases, the indicator lamp will glow continuously when a vehicle is in the blind spot sensor field of view. It may flash if the turn signal is activated. On some systems, the steering wheel vibrates when vehicles are detected in the blind spot -Cross-Traffic Alert – cross-traffic alert (CTA) systems assist the driver with oncoming traffic from the sides. The blind spot radar is used to detect approaching vehicles in the rear. Front CTA systems use radar modules or cameras located at the outer front corners of the vehicle. When the system detects an approaching vehicle, it warns the driver with an audible and visual alert. Visual alerts may be found on the door mirrors, the driver infotainment center, or infotainment screen. Some vehicles may also indicate the direction of the oncoming traffic. Some systems also use AEB along with CTA to automatically apply the brakes if the system determines an impact is imminent ADAS Operation and Service (4/5) Service Considerations for ADAS -Control Modules – ADAS features require many systems to work together. DTCs, along with service information, will guide you in starting the diagnosis. Be sure to reference the description and operation of the system to understand which modules may be affected. A DTC in one module may set multiple DTCs in other systems. If a module requires replacement, it will likely need to be programmed or initialized for the specific vehicle. Additionally, information may need to be downloaded from the original module to use in the new one -Calibrations – calibrations are not only required when a part is replaced or removed. There are other operations which may require calibration, such as removing or replacing an adjacent part. Its critical to reference the most current vehicle-maker service information for any system being serviced. ADAS parts and systems that may require calibrations include, but are not limited to the: -ACC -Lane departure system -Surround view system -Blind spot alert -Night vision system -Cameras -Radar sensors -Headlamps -Steering angle sensor -Ride height sensors -Active guidelines -Parking Assist Sensor Service – parking assist and active parking assist rely on the correct operation of the ultrasonic sensors. Sensors must be clean; mud, snow, ice, or dirt can impar sensor function. These sensors must also be fully seated in their mounts and pointed in the correct direction. Service information may require monitoring sensor parameter identification (PID) data while the vehicle is in DRIVE or REVERse to watch for abnormal data. Another check may also be performed by placing pylons in front of the sensors and comparing the PID data to pylon placement specifications. This may be referred to as an azimuth check in the service information -Parking Assist Camera Service -Parking assist cameras are only helpful if they accurately show the driver the surrounding environment. Many operations may require these cameras to be aimed or calibrated including, but not limited to: -Camera replacement -Camera removal -Removal of a part or panel that houses a camera -Calibration procedures are explained in the vehicle service information and may require: -A scan tool -Vehicle-specific targets -A grid constructed on the floor -Calibrating the parking assist cameras ensures that their images are accurately displaying the area surrounding the vehicle -ACC Calibrations - manually adjusting a front radar module may be required before calibration. There is a certain amount of adjustment that a module can make electronically, but if the sensor is too far out of position, it may end with a calibration failure. Static calibration requires setting up targets before initiating the calibration with a scan tool. Both radar modules and cameras may use targets for calibration. Dynamic calibration is done by initiating it with the scan tool and then driving until complete. Some vehicles require both a static and dynamic calibration. With either, the vehicle should be test driven afterward to verify proper operation of the system -I-CAR Repairability Technical Support – the I-CAR Repairability Technical Support (RTS) website proves valuable information about proper repair methods and where to locate procedures. Through this website, you can access information about ADAS, calibration requirements, hybrid and electric vehicle (EV) information, and much more. -Service Information – when repairing, replacing, or diagnosing ADAS, having access to the most recent vehicle-specific service information is crucial. Don’t assume to know a procedure based on past experience. Service information is regularly updated and may have changed. In addition to current calibration procedure information, the description and operation sections help technicians understand how all the systems work together. Follow diagnostic flow charts when there is a problem with a system. Service information may also include special repair procedures. Sometimes a module has been superseded by a newer version. This may require programming updates to other modules. When they work together, like blind spot sensors, both the damaged and undamaged modules may require replacement -Blind Spot System Calibrations – sensor alignment is crucial to the proper operation of the system. This is especially true for vehicles that don’t require calibrations for blind spot sensors. Measurements may be available to repair sensor-mounted locations to as-built specifications. Programming and calibrations may be required if a sensor is replaced or reinstalled. This may require a scan tool, targets, and a large work area. Some vehicles may only require a dynamic calibration. Calibrating the sensors ensure accurate data is sent to the control module to inform the driver when a vehicle enters their blind spot. A test drive should be performed as a final check to ensure the system is functioning properly -Test Drive – while test-driving a vehicle, make sure t understand how the system is designed to function. It’s a good idea to identify a nearby road that has good lane markings for use when testing lane keep functions. ACC and blind spot alert require driving in traffic above certain speeds, so a highway may be a good choice. Check to see if vehicle-maker service information specifies road requirements for dynamic calibrations. If may be possible to monitor data with a scan tool during a test drive. For example, if the ACC is not functioning properly, it may be helpful to see exactly how many degrees off-center the radar module is reading -Blind Spot Considerations – because most blind spot radar sensors must send and receive their signal through a bumper cover, the composition and thickness of that cover must be within its designed specifications. If it is too thick, or has anything obstructing it such as dirt or stickers, the signal will be affected and the system may not work correctly. Excessive paint film thickness in the sensor areas can also obstruct the signal most vehicle makers have position statements regarding bumper repairs on vehicles with blind spot detection -Camera and Radar Issues- -Some conditions that may affect ADAS camera operation include: -A damaged or misaligned camera -An obstructed or damaged windshield -Poor weather conditions -Radar sensors may operate erratically, or may not operate at all, if there is an obstruction in front of them. This could include: -A service part sticker on the backside of a new part -An incorrect grille emblem not designed for use with a radar module -A bent license plate that is located near the radar sensor -If the vehicle does not track straight ahead due to an excessive thrust angle, some systems will not work properly. Forward radars and cameras may not pick up the vehicle ahead or may pick up a vehicle in an adjacent lane - Advanced High-Strength Steel (1/2) Types of Steel and Strength identification Steel Strength -High-Strength Steel -Steel types have been designed that allow: -Parts to be made stronger without the addition of reinforcements -Parts to be made thinner and lighter, but still have the same strength -For added control of collision energy management. Mechanical properties of new steels allow the collision energy management of a part to be fine-tuned for added energy absorption or transfer as needed -Yield and Tensile Strength – steel types can be classified by strength, mechanical properties, and metallurgical designation or the name of the steel. Steel strength may be listed as tensile strength, yield strength, or both. Mechanical properties include those things that determine the workability of the metal. The metallurgical designation is determined by the build process and alloy of the steel. Throughout this course, yield strength and tensile strength are used as common measurements -Yield and Tensile Strength – yield strength is the minimum force required to achieve permanent deformation of the steel. Before the yield point, the steel will deform elastically and return to its original shape when the stress is removed. However, once the yield point is passed, the steel plastically deforms and the damage remains. Tensile strength (or ultimate tensile strength) is defined as the measurement of the amount of force necessary to tear a piece of steel apart. Tensile and yield strength are also related to the ability to repair a steel part. As steel is deformed, either through collision forces or straightening, the yield strength rises, making the steel harder, and thus more brittle. Harder steels are prone to cracking and breaking. -Strength Measurement – the amount of strength is typically measured in megapascals (MPa) or kilopound per square inch (ksi). A pascal is a unit of measurement equivalent to one Newton per square meter. One megapascal is one million pascals. One megapascal is also equal to 145 psi or 0.145 ksi -Total Elongation – steels are also classified by a property known as “total elongation”. Total elongation is how much a material can stretch before breaking. The percentage value is determined by how long the material stretches compared to the original length. A material that can be stretched to double the original size without breaking would have a total elongation value of 100%. Total elongation occurs after the material reaches the maximum tensile strength. The total force exerted on the material will then go down and the actual fracture point will occur at a lower force than the maximum tensile strength of the material -Steel Strength Classifications – automotive steels fall into basic strength categories -Mild Steel – -Is typically any steel that has less than 270 MPa tensile strength and 210 MPa yield strength -Is easily formed and is the most repairable of the steel types used in vehicle structures -Can typically be heated during repairs as long as the limits for maximum temperature and time are observed. -Note Mild steel usage for structural parts is limited on late model vehicles -High-Strength Steel – -Typically has a tensile strength between 270 and 700 MPa and a yield strength between 210 and 550 MPa -Is made in numerous types, with each type having unique mechanical properties and strength characteristics -Is commonly used for structural parts because it can be thinner, lighter, and stronger than the same part made from a mild steel -Is heat-sensitive and is typically cold-straightened -Note: Heat can dramatically affect the strength and properties of HSS; therefore, it is not recommended -Note 2: A steel frame may be made from different strengths of steel -Ultra-High-Strength Steel – -Is very hard and strong -Has a tensile strength over 700 MPa and a yield strength over 550 MPa -Is typically used to reinforce areas and limit passenger compartment deformation in a collision -Has its strength destroyed by heat -Is typically not straightened at all due to its strength and the high degree of brittleness that it has -Increasing Steel Strength – - increasing the steel strength can be done by: -Altering the manufacturing process -Alloying the steel -The amount and rate of heating and cooling of the steel, and the pressures used as it is manufactured, can have a drastic effect on the strength of the finished steel. Alloying is mixing two or more metals or elements. For example, the percentage of carbon mixed into the iron that steel is made from is one of the main contributing factors to its final strength. Other metals are also added in small amounts to alter the properties and strength of the steel -Alloying Elements – alloying elements that are added to steel when it is made are added in very small percentages. The percentage depends on the element added and the desired properties of the steel, but are typically in the 0.1 to 5.0 percent range. Alloying elements also affect the microstructure and grain size of the finished steel, and affect the temperatures where different grain structures form during manufacture -As Steel Strength Increases – -As the strength of steel increases, it typically becomes: -Harder -More brittle -More heat sensitive -The Rockwell hardness of steel typically increases as the tensile strength is increased. This makes the steel less workable and harder to form or straighten. The Rockwell hardness scale uses a testing method based on indention testing that measures the steel’s resistance to deformation. The harder and stronger a steel is, the more prone it becomes to cracking when it is worked. The properties of HSS and UHSS are changed by heat much more than mild steels -Mechanical Properties – -Mechanical properties of steel include: -Total elongation, which is the percentage that the metal will stretch before it fractures or breaks -The work hardening exponent, which is the rate that the metal work hardens -The toughness of the metal, which is a combination of strength and ductility -For a metal to be considered very tough, it has to have high strength along with a high degree of ductility. Many very strong metals are not considered tough because they are very brittle, while many of the very ductile metals are not tough because they lack strength -Work Hardening – work hardening describes the tendency of steel to increase in strength as it is worked. Work hardening affects both the yield and tensile strength of steel, but tends to affect yield strength at a higher rate than tensile strength. This leads to an increase in the brittleness of the steel as it is work hardened. Work hardening rate differs by steel type. Higher strength steels generally work harden faster than lower strength steels. Note: Metal is work hardened when formed during manufacturing, again when deformed, and further when straightening Identifying Steel Types and Reinforcements -Identifying Steel Types – there are two ways to identify what type of steel a structural part is made of. The first method is to look up the name or metallurgical designation of the steel in vehicle maker service information. Some collision repair manuals may include information on the types of steel used to make the structure and body panels of a mild vehicle. The second method is to conduct hardness testing to categorize as mild, HSS, or UHSS. Typically, hardness testing will give numbers that can be compared to that of a known steel. This will allow the steel to be placed into a basic strength category based on the comparative data -Steel Testing – there are a variety of methods used for testing steel -Hardness Testing – -May be done with fixed or bench-mounted equipment that requires a small piece to be removed from the part -May be done with portable equipment, which allows testing without having to cut sections out of the vehicle parts -For comparison readings, hardness testing should be done with the same equipment. Do not compare results from one piece of equipment to those taken with a different piece of equipment -Drill Bit Strength – the drill bit strength is important because if the material is harder than the drill bit, it will not drill out. A standard drill bit has a strength range of around 950-1,070 MPa, which is far below some of the strengths used on todays vehicles. Drill bits made for HSS are in the strength range of 1,750-2,100 MPa. Drill bits made for UHSS are in the strength range of 2,750-3,050 MPa. If a standard drill bit is used on a 1,300 MPa part, it will never penetrate the steel. To perform the drill bit test, drill a spot weld with a standard spot weld drill bit at high speed, above 1000 rpm. If the metal burns the bit on one spot weld, it is most likely UHSS. If the drill bit drills three to five spot welds, it may still be UHSS, but at the mid to lower strength range of UHSS -Cutting/Saw Test – when performing the cutting test, if the damaged part cannot be cut with a standard air saw blade, it may be UHSS. Caution should be used with this test because some saw blades are designed for higher strength steel. If teeth are removed from the saw blade after a short cutting time, the part is most likely made of UHSS -Best Practices: Steel Identification - as a best practice for steel identification, check the vehicle maker service information, which is the most accurate method to identify the steel strength. Vehicle maker recommendations may also include repair procedures and special precautions for those parts. If no vehicle maker information can be found, a hardness tester is used to give the approximate steel strength. Note: If there is no access to a hardness tester, the drill/cut tests are used as a last resort because these tests only give a very broad idea if the steel is near HSS and are not definite -Reinforcements – inner reinforcements are used to add strength to a part. They are typically placed where the part is intended to transition from energy absorption to energy transfer. They may be in multiple locations on the frame rails of modern vehicles, and may be found spaced along the entire length of the rail. They also may be used to help design crush zones into the part. Inner reinforcements are typically made from HSS or UHSS -Inner Reinforcement Considerations – inner reinforcement considerations include limits to the amount of straightening that can be done. Areas that have HSS or UHSS reinforcements inside will be so stiff and strong that it is often not practical to straighten visible damage in these areas. Straightening of rails or pillars in reinforced areas is limited to minor movement of the part that does not show visible deformation. Limits to sectioning locations for the part are also a consideration. Heat input from welding is a consideration, because inner reinforcements are typically made from higher strength steels. There are also corrosion protection considerations because of limited access to parts that are buried deep within an assembly. -Multiple Reinforcements – structural parts may have multiple reinforcements inside them. Reinforcements may be staggered through the part to help create collapse zones. This is especially true of frame rails. The areas that are reinforced will be very strong and transfer collision energy, while the areas between reinforcements will be weaker and will collapse to absorb energy. They may also be layered on top of each other in a part. This is done to add strength to the part and to keep it from collapsing. Layered reinforcements are typically found in A-, B-, and C-pillars Metallurgical Designation -Usage – each type of steel has some common automotive usages -Typical Mild Steel Usage – mild steel is typically used for: -Outer body panels -Center floor pans, trunk floors, and cowl panels -Some outer unisides and pillars -HSS Usage – HSS is typically found on: -Some outer body panels, allowing them to be made thinner and lighter -Major structural parts -Reinforcements for structural parts -Other Types of Steel – -Dual-phase (dp) and complex-phase steel: -Is a type of steel that comes in many different types, depending on the desired strength and mechanical properties -Use is becoming common on front frame rails and other structural parts of unitized because it offers both good strength and energy absorption, making it a tough steel -Transformation Induced Plasticity (TRIP) steel: -Has tensile strengths in the 500-1050 MPa range -Has similar mechanical properties as DP steels, but with slightly more ductility -Martensitic (MART) steels: -Are very strong and hard, with tensile strengths up to 1700 MPa -Typically have low ductility, low total elongation percentages, and high yield strength-to-tensile strength ratios. This make them hard to form and very difficult to straighten when damaged -Hot Stamped Parts – hot stamped parts are made by stamping the sheet steel while it is very hot. Parts are then quenched to cool while still in the dies. This id done to add increased formability with higher strength steels, and the heating and quenching process can actually increase the steel strength. They are typically made from boron-alloyed steels. The strength of a boron-alloyed steel part can increase as much as 250% during the hot stamping process. In addition, hot stamped parts are typically not galvanized because the high heat used during stamping would destroy the galvanized coating -Boron-Alloyed Steel- -Boron-alloyed steel: -Is very strong and hard, with tensile strengths up to 1600 MPa -Typically has low ductility, low total elongation percentages, and high yield strength-to-tensile strength ratios -Should not be straightened -Locations on unitized structures where boron-alloyed steel parts may be found include: -Door intrusion beams -Bumper reinforcements -Roof structures -Pillar inner panels and reinforcements -Rocker panel reinforcements -Rear body panels -Note: Boron-alloyed steels may be called by different names Advanced High-Strength Steel ( 2 /2) Steel Repair Considerations Repair Considerations for Structural Steel Parts -Steel Strength and Repair Considerations – the increased strength of the HSS and UHSS used in the parts of a unitized structure has various effects on the collision repair process -Steel Strength and Collision Damage – the inclusion of higher strength steels in vehicle structures has led to changes in the way vehicles are damaged during collisions. These changes include less passenger compartment deformation and fewer visible damage indicators, along with more collision energy transfer into the vehicle structure. Damage may radiate deeper into the structure and cause deformation of the softer parts of the structure, well away from the area of the primary damage -HSS and UHSS Straightening Considerations – HSS and UHSS structural parts add some considerations to the structural straightening process. These considerations include that increased force is required to straighten a damaged HSS part. Most UHSS parts that are damaged should not be straightened and will require replacement. Kink vs bend may not apply to damaged parts made of higher strength steels. The kink vs bend rule was made when most structural parts were made of mild steel or conventional high-strength steel that falls into the lower strength range of the group. With newer, advanced HSS and UHSS, even minor visible damage may be hard to repair -Steel Strength and Repair Considerations – the increased strength of the HSS and UHSS used in the parts of a unitized structure has various effects on the collision repair process -Steel Strength and Collision Damage – the inclusion of higher strength steels in vehicle structures has led to changes in the way vehicles are damaged during collisions. These changes include less passenger compartment deformation and fewer visible damage indicators, along with more collision energy transfer into the vehicle structure. Damage may radiate deeper into the structure and cause deformation of the softer parts of the structure, well away from the area of the primary damage -HSS and UHSS Straightening Considerations – HSDS and UHSS structural parts add some considerations to the structural straightening process. These considerations include that increased force is required to straighten a damaged HSS part. Most UHSS parts that are damaged should not be straightened and will require replacement. Kink vs bend may not apply to damaged parts made of higher strength steels. The kink vs bend rule was made when most structural parts were made of mild steel or conventional high-strength steel that falls into the lower strength range of the group, with newer, advanced HSS and UHSS, even minor visible damage may be hard to repair -Heat Sensitivity – parts made from higher strength steel should not be heated unless vehicle maker documentation allows it or the part is going to be replaced. The heat-affect zone (HAZ) when welding should also be considered. Use only enough heat to make a sound weld. Make practice welds that are destructively tested to verify the quality of the weld -Yield Strength-to-Tensile Strength – the yield strength-to-tensile strength ratio indicates the ductility of the steel. A lower number indicates a higher ductility of steel. “1” indicates no ductility at all. It affects the formability and repairability of the steel. If a steel has a yield strength that is very close to the tensile strength, it is considered unrepairable. This is because when there is enough force put on the steel to move it plastically, it will typically crack. For example, a frame rail that cracks when pulled back into position is likely made from a steel with a high yield-to-tensile ratio -Work Hardening Rate – steel parts used in unitized structures gain a good deal of their final strength through work hardening when parts are formed. Collision damage further work hardens the part and the straightening process adds to it again. This limits the amount of straightening that should be done, because even if the part is restored to its correct shape, the mechanical properties and strength of the part may be significantly different than intended. Work hardening also tends to make a part more brittle and prone to cracking when straightened -Structural Straightening Considerations – straightening high-strength steel structural parts may require increased pulling force and additional anchoring to avoid collateral damage to other parts of the vehicle -Complete Part Replacement – complete part replacement is more common with the new steels and construction technologies being used today. Straightening and sectioning issues with HSS and UHSS, and multiple reinforcements, are leading to more instances where complete replacement may be required Heat Effects on Steel -Heat Effects on Steel – the strength and mechanical properties of steel may be changed by the application of heat -Heat Effects on Steel – the effect of heat on steel varies depending on the type of steel and other variables. It also alters the strength of the part. Heat tends to strengthen mild steels, weaken high-strengths steels, and completely destroy the strength of most ultra-high-strength steels -Mechanical Properties – the effect of heat on steel also changes the mechanical properties. The amount of heat and the rate at which it is applied, as well as the cooling rate, can have a dramatic effect on the mechanical properties of the steel. Mild steels tend to become more brittle when heated, while some of the higher strength steels may become softer and more formable. This may lead to a part that was intended to transfer collision energy actually absorbing it instead, and a part that should absorb energy may either crack or be hard enough to transfer the energy instead -Video: Heat Effects on Steel – using a tensile strength tester and strips of metal, you can see how heat affects both the strength and mechanical properties of different types of steel -Steel Heating Considerations – heating considerations for steel include: -UHSS parts should not be straightened unless vehicle maker documentation states that it can be straightened -Steel parts should be straightened cold unless vehicle maker documentation states that heat can be used -Observe the limits for both maximum temperature and the length of time can be heated. If a vehicle maker has heating recommendations, they typically include a maximum temperature and amount of time that the part can be subjected to heat -Observe the limits for both maximum temperature and the length of time a part can be heated. If a vehicle maker has heating recommendations, they typically include a maximum temperature and amount of time that the part can be subjected to heat -Use heat on parts that are going to be replaced during initial straightening. This may help to reduce collateral damage to adjacent parts -Note: Using heat on parts that are going to be replaced during initial straightening allows the entire structure to be returned to proper dimensions without having to apply excessive force to remove damage to HSS and UHSS parts that are going to be replaced -HSS and UHSS Welding Considerations -Heat-Affect Zone – considerations for welding HSS and UHSS include the heat-affect zone that welding creates. The strength and mechanical properties of steel can be greatly changed by the extreme heat in the heat-affect zone. Heat-affect zone softening typically increases as the steel strength increases. Because of this, when welding on HSS and UHSS, the amount of heat input into the metal should be held to the minimum necessary to make a quality weld. Methods that can help control the heat input include making stitch welds where long continuous welds are needed, and using welding equipment that utilizes the pulse-spray transfer method -STRSW – another consideration for welding HSS and UHSS includes that squeeze-type resistance spot welding (STRSW) may be the preferred welding method to use on HSS and UHSS. STRSW equipment may have separate HSS and UHSS settings that also help to reduce the HAZ of the weld. These settings may work by pulsing the current input into the weld Acura NSX Aluminum Repair and Refinishing -Course Overview – the Acura NSX Aluminum Repair and Refinishing training course is designed to provide a basic understanding of the aluminum repairs and refinishing allowed on the second-generation Acura NSX. This includes aluminum panel repair, surface preparation, and refinishing considerations. -Learning Objectives – learning objectives for this module include: -Explaining Acura NSX aluminum repair requirements -Explaining aluminum panel straightening -Explaining galvanic corrosion concerns -Explaining Acura NSX-specific repair considerations -Repair Process Criteria – -Non-structural repairs allowed at NSX Non-Structural Repair Network shops -Repairs include -Bolt-on parts -Paint damage -Glass replacement -Bonded parts -Dedicated Aluminum Work Area -Typically a separate room -Aluminum dust extraction system -Explosion proof -Dedicated hand tools -Protects from cross contamination and potential for galvanic corrosion -Minimum recommended size -26.2 ft long x 16.4 ft wide (8 m x 5 m) -Safety Consideration -Maintain a safe work environment -Proper personal protective and safety equipment -Correct use of tools and equipment -When is Straightening Allowed – -Outer aluminum panels only -No structural straightening allowed -Replace outer panels if the inner panel / support is damaged -Note: Straightening damaged aluminum is allowed on NSX outer panels only. -Hammer and Dolly Techniques – -Hammer-off-dolly preferred for aluminum -Hamer-on-dolly primarily used for steel -Spoon / slapper may also be used -Note: For aluminum damage repairs, the hammer-off-dolly technique (left) is preferred over the hammer-on-dolly technique (right) -Heating Temperatures – -Aluminum does not change color when heated to melting point -Aluminum oxide melts at about 3,700 degrees Fahrenheit (2,038 degrees Celsius) -Temperate is more critical than time -Heat spreads faster in aluminum than steel -Aluminum outer panels may be heated -Do not exceed 392 degrees Fahrenheit (200 degrees Celsius) -Monitor temperature -Weld-On Dent Removal Tools – -Tools designed specifically for aluminum -Disconnect battery and isolate cables before using -Used when there is limited access to the panel backside -Tools for aluminum require more electrical current than tools for steel -Note: Weld-on dent removal is used for pulling out low spots on a panel -Removal Sequence – -1) Identify the area of the dent -2) Remove the finish using P80 grit or finer sandpaper -3) Attach the ground clamp -4) Install the weld-on stud -5) Start on the area with the least amount of damage -Note: Be sure to follow the appropriate sequence when using a weld-on dent removal tool. -Paintless Dent Repair (PDR) / Glue-On Tabs – -Requires access to panel backside -Do not drill holes for access -Glue-on dent removal when there is no backside access -Note: With PDR, picks are used to raise a low area from the backside of a panel. -Note: A glue-on dent removal system can be used when there is no backside access. -Hand Tools and Abrasives - -Separate tools used for steel from tools used from aluminum -Have dedicated abrasives -Label or color-code tools and abrasive -Note: These are dedicated tools labeled for aluminum repairs -Hand Tool Considerations – -Polished steel face with edges rounded off -Aluminum-faced -Plastic-faced / rubber -Wood with leather face -Pouches of lead shot -Picks -Note: The edges of this hammer are rounded to help avoid damaging aluminum. -Note: The steel face of this hammer is being polished for working on aluminum. -Note: Using a rubber hammer is an option for working on aluminum. -Note: Using an aluminum hammer is an option when repairing aluminum. -Note: Serrated-faced hammers should not be used to repair aluminum. -Aluminum and Steel Applications – -Seal out moisture -Separate steel and aluminum with a non-conductive insulator -Used coated fasteners -Note: The aluminum is turning white as the aluminum corrodes. -Note : Fasteners have coatings applied to protect against corrosion. -Note: When unprotected fasteners are used, the result is galvanic corrosion. -Aluminum Dust Particles and Cautions – -Grind or sand at low speeds using light pressure -Helps reduce chance of overheating aluminum, clogging abrasive, embedding contaminants -Dust extraction system and sparkless vacuum -Particles are nontoxic, lightweight, irritant -Note: Use a sparkless vacuum to collect aluminum dust particles -No Straightening (Structural) – -No straightening is allowed on frame parts -Damaged parts must be replaced -Note: No straightening is allowed on NSX space frame parts -Crack Inspection – -Use dye penetrant to check for cracks -Three steps -Remover -Penetrant -Developer -Note: Dye penetrant can be used to check damaged areas and welds for cracks and flaws. -Outer Panel Repairs – -No serrated or pick-type hammers -No sharp-edged tools for straightening -Work slowly and evenly -No excessive sanding / grinding -Too much heat will distort panel -Note: This shows how aluminum repair processes compare to similar repairs on steel. -Removal – -Attached with urethane adhesive and bolts -Stay close to roof panel when cutting the adhesive -To prevent damage to the body -Note: During removal, stay as close to the roof panel as possible when cutting the adhesive. -Installation – -Refer to service information for surface preparations and adhesive locations -Refer to adhesive product maker for use conditions and drying time, -Note: Adhesive is located around the perimeter as well as various spots in the middle of the roof panel. -Aluminum – -Also called rear floor -Attached with structural adhesive and rivets. -Note: The middle floor pans are stamped aluminum -Non-Load Bearing – -Rear floor pans are non-load bearing -If seats are removed, use a support across the extrusion crossmembers. -Note: Do not place a load on the rear floor pans -Module Summary – Topics discussed in the module included: -Acura NSX aluminum repair requirement -Aluminum panel straightening -Galvanic corrosion concerns -Acura NSX-specific repair considerations -Learning Objectives – learning objectives for this module include: -Explaining aluminum surface preparation -Explaining aluminum refinishing considerations -Surface Preparation – -Sand the surface and remove the dust -Apply body filler as soon as possible after sanding -Note: Clean the surface with a wax and grease remover -Body Filler – -Polyester body fillers consist of a polyester resin, a catalyst, and talc -Body filler may also contain aluminum powder -Note: Ensure body filler is mixed properly. -Primer – -Prepare the surface by cleaning, sanding, masking, applying primer, sanding cured primer -Refinishing is similar to refinishing steel -Note: Apply primer following the product maker’s recommendations. -Refinishing Considerations – -Epoxy primer – -Applicable for aluminum -After drying apply putty, primer-surface, and topcoat directly -Wash primer - -Applicable for aluminum -Heating device is needed -Polyester putty, epoxy primer, and topcoat cannot be applied directly -Note: There are specific considerations regarding the primer layer. -Module Summary – topics discussed in this module included: -Aluminum surface preparation -Aluminum refinishing considerations ADAS and Convenience Systems Overview and Service (1/2) Course Overview -Course Overview – in this course, we will review some of the comfort and convenience systems, as well as some of the advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), found on modern vehicles. To gain a competitive advantage, vehicle makers add new comfort and convenience systems to their vehicles frequently. While these systems used to be found primarily in luxury vehicles, many vehicle makers have made these systems standard or optional features on their vehicles. This course provides you an overview of these systems, their pairs, and common repair procedures. -Common Diagnostics and Troubleshooting – when performing diagnostic and troubleshooting procedures, most inspections will begin with a physical inspection for physical damage, such as collision impact or damaged wiring, sensors, lines or hoses, and malfunction indicator lamps (MILs). In most cases, the physical inspection will need to be followed by a vehicle scan to retrieve any diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) stored in memory. In many cases, accessing vehicle maker service information will provide the best DTC identification tables and troubleshooting aids such as system diagrams and diagnostic flowcharts. However, in some cases, a quick internet search may allow you to identify the DTCs retrieved during the vehicle scan. Note: Always reference vehicle maker service procedures to identify proper repair procedures, mandatory replacement items, service precautions, and required calibrations or initializations following system service. -Electronic Diagnostics – when troubleshooting power accessory failures with no vehicle-specific troubleshooting flowchart, follow the I-CAR Five Step Troubleshooting Flowchart. The steps in this flowchart require: -Defining the problem -Knowing the system -Finding the cause -Making the repair -Testing the system -Note: With electronic systems, diagnosis starts with checking for power, ground, and shorts, conducting typical tests, checking network communications, and troubleshooting. Power Locks, Keyless Entry, Theft-Deterrent, and Remote Start Systems -Power Locks – power lock systems typically include door lock assemblies, lock rod/linkages, sensors, actuators, control switches, and control modules that process commands to lock or unlock the vehicle. On newer vehicles, power locks may be locked or unlocked using many different ways including interior door switches, key fobs, and smartphone applications. -Power Lock System – most modern vehicles have the lock/unlock feature programmed into the control module. When the vehicle reaches a specific speed or when the transmission is shifted into DRIVE, the locks automatically engage. -Part Replacement Considerations – when servicing power locks, it is important to remember that adjustment, calibration, or initialization may need to be performed after parts are removed and installed. An example service procedure for replacing the door lock actuator may include. -1) Turn the ignition switch OFF and wait the prescribed time before proceeding -2) Disconnect the cable from the negative battery terminal -3) Remove the required trim and assembly to gain access to the front door lock with motor assembly -4) Disconnect the electrical connector -5) Remove the screws and slide the front door lock with motor assembly downward to disconnect the front door lock open rod -6) Remove the front door lock with motor assembly and cables as a unit -Note: To install the replacement actuator, reverse the removal steps -Keyless Entry Operation – a keyless entry system controls access to the vehicle without using a traditional mechanical key. These systems typically use an electronic control module to activate motors that control the vehicle locks. Keyless entry may be divided into two categories, active and passive. -Active keyless entry is the traditional keyless entry where a key fob is pressed to unlock the doors or trunk, or to start the engine -Passive keyless entry allows vehicle entry without pushing buttons on a key fob. Simply carrying a pass key will unlock the door and start the engine with a push-button. This system may also be part of the vehicle push-button starting system. -System Parts – common parts of a keyless entry system include: -Keyless entry transmitters -Antennas (located throughout the interior to cover the entire vehicle) -An exterior antenna -Control modules -Exterior lighting (if equipped) -Theft-Deterrent System – the typical theft-deterrent system is designed to sound an alarm and/or flash the exterior lights during forced entry. These theft-deterrent systems have been improved over the years to include more advanced electronics to detect intrusion and limit theft. -Types – there are three common types of theft-deterrent systems: -Telematics subscription service -Listening devices -Engine immobilizers -Note: The telematics subscription service that has a Stolen Vehicle Slowdown feature allows the service provider to slow down, stop, and recover stolen vehicles that might be involved in a high-speed chase. This feature is in addition to features that use global positioning system (GPS) technology to locate lost or stolen vehicles. Listening devices detect the sound of glass breaking and trip an alarm if the sound is heard. Engine immobilizers activate when anything other than the proper key is used to start the vehicle. -System Parts – parts that make up a theft-deterrent system include: -Door lock control system parts -A control module -A security horn, which may be powered independent of the battery -A security indicator lamp -Door, hood, and trunk ajar switches -A glass breakage sensor and a glass breakage sensor electronic control unit (ECU), if equipped. -Operation – the theft-deterrent system operates when the doors are locked, either manually or remotely. The system monitors movement, noise, latches, and/or vehicle movement, and it sets off an alarm when specific criteria are met. On some systems, the switches are checked three times per second to make sure the switches are closed. These systems may also have a movement sensor that detects movement inside the vehicle. This particular system requires the windows to be closed for proper operation. Some systems have a heartbeat sensor. The heartbeat sensor is used to detect the presence of someone inside the vehicle, with an indicator on the key fob. -Service Considerations – most theft-deterrent system parts are integrated with other systems, so looking for related DTCs when performing a vehicle scan may aid in the troubleshooting process. Common system faults may include the: -System not arming -Alarm not sounding and/or the proper lights not flashing -System arming when the doors/lids are open -Note: The horns are most susceptible to damage in a front-end collision -Remote Start – remote starters are designed to start a vehicle engine before the driver enters the vehicle. There are a variety of vehicle maker and aftermarket designs. Remote start systems may use one-way or two-way communication between the key fob and the vehicle. One-way systems use radio control signals that communicate from the key fob to the vehicle. Two-way systems communicate back to the key fob and may illuminate a key fob indicator to confirm the engine has started. Newer vehicles commonly feature remote starts that can be operated via a smart phone app. Glass and Power Mirror System -Power Mirrors – in addition to power adjustment of the mirror itself, power side mirrors on modern vehicles may be integrated with a number of systems including: -Lane departure warning/lane keep assist -360-degree surround view -Self-park -Note: on some vehicles, the mirrors may angle downward when the vehicle is in REVERSE and may automatically fold in when the ignition is OFF and the vehicle is locked. -Power Mirror Operation – power mirrors are typically controlled by a switch that directs power to the appropriate mirror control circuit when the directional button is pushed. On older vehicles, pushing the button may have provided power directly to the mirror motor; on modern vehicles, pushing the button may send a signal to a control module that then sends the appropriate command signal to the mirror motor to move. System parts commonly associated with a power mirror assembly include: -Control switches -A fuse or relay -Position sensors, or potentiometers, for the memory option -Mirror motors or drive mechanism -Options – optional equipment that may be included with a power mirror assembly includes: -Heated mirrors. A heating element is used on the backside of the glass to defrost a mirror. -Electrochromatic mirrors. This feature is used to reduce headlamp glare. -Memory systems. These systems are used to readjust mirrors to a previous setting. -An automatic day/night feature of the driver outside rearview mirror that is controlled by the inside rearview mirror. This inside rearview mirror supplies a signal and a low reference to the driver outside rearview mirror. -Directional signals in the mirror. -Power fold-in mirrors. -Telescoping mirrors. -Passenger side park-assist mirrors. -Lighting. -Service Considerations – an example side mirror replacement procedure may include -1) Removing the door trim panel to expose the mirror bolts. -2) Removing insulators, hole plugs, etc. -3) Disconnecting the connector. -4) Removing the nuts securing the mirror to the door. -Note: Reverse the steps to install the new assembly. Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify which systems the vehicle you are servicing may be equipped with and perform all necessary calibrations and initializations following system service. Note: Breakaway mirror assemblies are typically friction-fitted and may require special tools for removal. -Power Window Operation – a power window system: -Uses a two-directional motor. -May use a cable and a pulley system. -May use two gear arms between the window and drive source. -May use a spring-assist device to reduce the amount of current required to lower and raise a window. -Note: To operate the system, the wiring harness transmits a signal from the switch to the relay. A relay is used because the switches and computers cannot handle the amount of current necessary to power the window. The relay sends current to the motor that powers the window. A relay may also be used to prevent the operation of the windows when the ignition is OFF. -Related System Parts – system parts commonly associated with a power window assembly include a: -Motor -Switch -Relay -Regulator -Wiring Harness -Control Module -Fuse -Removing and Installing Window Regulators – when removing and installing a window regulator: -1) Remove the trim panel to access the inner door area. -2) Remove the moisture barrier and the door glass. -3) Identify the regulator-to-door attachment method. Regulators can be attached with bolts or rivets. Rivets can be a special design called a “t” or peel-type and must be replaced with the same type rivet. -4) Remove the regulator-to-door shell fasteners. -5) Inspect the replacement regulator to make sure it is undamaged and is the correct part. -6) Position the regulator in the door shell and install the regulator-to-door shell fasteners. -7) Install the door glass. -8) Check for proper operation of the window through its full range of movement. -9) Install the moisture barrier and door trim panel. -Sunroofs – a power sunroof assembly opens and closes using either a cable and pully system and a reversible, or bi-directional, motor or a worm gear system. When the motor is not operating, both the sunroof motor open circuit and sunroof motor close circuit are grounded. Some modules are equipped with a timer circuit, which stops the operation of the sunroof motor after 8 seconds. Power sunroof systems may have an express open feature or a vent feature, and either slide between the roof and the headliner or pop up over the roof panel. -Mechanical Inspection – when inspecting the operation of power sunroof assemblies: -Look for the full range of travel. -Verify the proper operation of the express feature, if applicate. -Verify proper operation of the vent open feature. -Note: If glass panel replacement is necessary, cycle the sunroof through its full range of travel, looking for interference that may be the cause of the glass breakage. Any damage to the housing or tracks can then be repaired before replacing the glass, avoiding the possibility of breaking the replacement glass panel when opening and closing the sunroof. -Sunroof Timing – the timing of the drive motor and cables that move the glass panel in a sliding sunroof assembly is critical to the proper operation of the unit. The timing may be set or adjusted to a variety of methods. Procedures used to set or adjust cable timing are typically vehicle-specific. Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify proper service and adjustment procedures and any calibration or initialization requirements. Seat Systems -Seat Options – there are many options available in seating systems for modern vehicles including heaters and coolers, inflatable back support, and seat position memory systems. -Seat Heaters/Coolers – advancements in seat technology are, for the most part, designed to increase comfort and reduce driver fatigue. One such technology is climate-controlled seats. Climate-controlled seats provide heat through electronically controlled heating elements and ventilation through perforated seat covers, which may be integrated with the air conditioning (A/C) system. These seats cannot heat and cool at the same time. -Climate-Controlled Seating System Parts – the serviceable climate-controlled seat system parts may include: -An air filter. -Cushion and backrest manifolds. -Cushion and backrest foam pads and trim covers. -A heat/cool seat switch. -Fuses. -Ventilation ducts. -Blowers. -Lumbar Support – some seat systems use an air pump to inflate a bladder located inside the seat. An example of this is found in power lumber systems. The lumbar switch directly controls the lumbar air pump, which is used to inflate and deflate the lumbar bladder. Some seats utilize a manual adjustment knob that moves supports built into the seat back to adjust lumbar support. -Power Seats and Memory Systems – power seats utilize switches, relays, sensors, and motors similar to other power accessories. There are many different power configurations for seats that determine the number of directions the seat can be moved and tilted. The switch position controls voltage and polarity to operate the motors and move the seat in the intended direction. Some vehicle makers feature memory systems that allow drivers to store and quickly recall their desired seat positions with the push of a single button. In some vehicles, this memory feature may also store pedal location preference. -Seat Removal Considerations – seats are mounted to the floor pan through the carpet. To locate the seat mount locations, adjustable seats may need to be in the complete forward or rearward position to access the mounting fasteners. Refer to the vehicle maker service information for proper removal procedures. Seats with a manual adjuster may require them to be placed in the full forward position to remove the rear attaching nuts. If the seat is not in the forward position when removing the nuts, the seat track can spring forward, causing personal injury. Never sit on the seat when the seat and the track assembly are not installed in the vehicle. -Folding Seats – some vehicles, such as minivans and SUVs, may have folding seats designed to be quickly folded down and stowed or removed from the vehicle to create additional cargo space. In some cases, these seats may be power-operated and will utilize switches, sensors, and motors much like standard power seats. Note: Always reference vehicle maker service information as these seating systems typically require calibration or initialization procedures to ensure safe and proper operation. Power Sliding Door Systems -Power Sliding Door Operations – a power sliding door operates when the OPEN/CLOSE switch is pressed and the ON/OFF switch is on. The power sliding door latch switch helps determine the position of the door and sends the information to the power sliding door module. When one of the OPEN switches is pressed, the power sliding door module sends a signal to the latch release actuator. The latch release actuator releases the door latch. The door operation requires the vehicle to be in PARK before it will open. Once the door is released by the latch actuator, the drive assembly actuator slides the door. An optical position sensor may be located on the driver assembly actuator and is used by the control module to determine the position of the door. -Related System Pats – system parts commonly associated with a power sliding door assembly include -A drive assembly actuator. -A latch release actuator. -A position sensor. -A door switch plate plungers and pads. -A power sliding door latch switch. -An overhead console. -An ON/OFF switch for both doors. -Motion Sensor Switches – the hands-free access system only operates when the key fob is within range and only under specific conditions. In order to prevent the door from opening while the vehicle is in motion, or when animals, balls, etc. pass near the sensor, the motion sensors are programmed to respond to a specific sequence of movement. In general, this is the user approaching the vehicle, and the foot kicking in and then out. As an additional safety feature, the key fob must be outside the vehicle. Once triggered, the sensors alert the control module, which sends a command to the electric motor to either open or close. -Part Replacement Considerations – if a malfunction is detected in the power sliding door sensor assembly, you should investigate these areas by checking for applicable DTCs. Also reference vehicle service information to identify diagnostic procedures such as checking resistance measurements for: -Power slide door sensor assembly. -Touch sensor. -Rear door wire. -Wire harness or connector. -Side door motor unit. -Power slide door electronic control unit (ECU). -Calibrations – if the ECU for the power sliding door is replaced, the system may require initialization or calibration. An example service procedure for reinitializing the power sliding door may include the following steps: -1) Turn the main power door switch on the dash to ‘OFF’. -2) Close the door manually. -3) remove the negative battery cable for 1 minute and then reconnect it. -4) Turn the key to ‘ON’. -5) Push the main door switch on the dash to ‘ON’. -6) Push the power door switch to open the door. -7) Wait 3 seconds. -8) Close the door with the switch. -9) Wait 3 seconds. -10) Repeat steps 6 through 9.
