Windshield Wiper Systems -Windshield Wiper System Features – windshield wiper systems may include variable speeds with intermittent wipers. Some systems include a wipe-after-wash feature. The wipe-after-wash function turns the wipers on for a set period of time, usually between six and ten seconds. Other systems use “Depressed Park”. This feature engages the wiper motor so that it drops the wipers below the windshield trim when they are not in use. -Wiper Operation – when the wiper switch is activated: -A current is sent to a relay box. A relay may be used to reduce the size of the wires leading into the steering column. -The relay sends power to the wiper motor. -The activated wiper motor rotates a spindle that is connected to the wiper linkages. -Note: Wiper systems are normally open and only operate when the ignition switch is in the ON or ACC position. -Rain-Sensing Wipers – contrary to the name, it is mot the wipers that sense rain on the windshield, but rather it is an infrared sensor module. The sensor module is mounted on the interior side of the windshield, in a housing either next to, or part of, the base of the rearview mirror. The module connector is also usually in the same housing. Rain-sensing wipers are designed to detect moisture on the windshield and auto-start the wipers when on. When rain is present, the light is deflected and less light makes it back to the photo diode. A signal is then sent to the wiper motor module to automatically start the wipers. Parts included in the rain-sensing wiper assembly include the: -Wiper assembly (blades, linkage, etc.) -Wiper motor. -Rain sensor. -Junction boxes. -Wiper motor module. -Rain-Sensing Wiper Operation – in general, the rain sensing system operates like this: -With rain sensing wiper systems, the user sets the system to the desired sensitivity. -The wiper switch is in one of the ON settings, or on an automatic setting. -The Rain sensor sends an infrared beam of light toward the cabin side of the windshield and the light reflects back into a photo diode sensor. -When rain is present, the light is deflected and less light makes it back to the photo diode. -When the sensor module detects reduced light, a signal is sent to the wiper motor module to automatically start the wipers. -Note: Always check the vehicle maker’s service information to the specific system operation. Heated Glass Systems -Heated Glass – nearly all modern vehicles are equipped with heated glass systems. One example, of a heated glass system is the rear defrosters, which typically uses a thin electrical element to warm the glass. When the switch is pressed, power is sent to the system timer switch. This switch energizes the relay. When the relay contacts close, voltage is sent to the heater grid. Resistance in the grid creates heat to warm the glass. When the time cycle is complete, the relay de-energizes to open the circuit. A timer circuit is used to prevent battery drain and charging system failure. Most have a 10-minute cycle. System parts associated with a rear defroster system may include: -Switch. -Time relay. -Fuse. -Heating grid. -Heated Windshield – some vehicle makers provide heated windshields, which are made by laying a nearly invisibly web of tungsten wires within the windshield glass. Heated windshields may also feature a heated wiper park that resembles and works in conjunction with the back glass defroster. When the rear defogger switch is pressed, typically the heated mirror system also engages (if equipped). The defogger relay energizes the heated mirror grid. This allows the computer-controlled timer to control both the heated mirror and defogger. -Heated Glass Service Considerations – tests used to determine electrical problems with heated glass may include voltage or continuity. Voltage tests are typically done at the defroster grid terminals to confirm the appropriate voltage is reaching the defroster grid, and continuity checks are done to confirm there are no breaks in the defroster grid lines. Repairs to the defroster grid system may include repairing: -Loose terminals with a conductive epoxy or solder. -Damaged grid lines with a conductive paint. Navigation/Infotainment Systems -Navigation/Infotainment Systems – navigation/infotainment systems are available on most vehicles as standard equipment or as an option. Some infotainment system features may include: -GPS navigation. -Satellite radio. -Wi-Fi hotspot connectivity. -DVD video for rear seat passengers. -Multi-zone audio/video control, which allows passengers in different areas of the vehicle to listen to different audio/video programming. -Apple Carplay, Android Auto, and/or Amazon Alex integration. -Vehicle performance information. -Navigation System Operation – navigation/infotainment systems typically receive signals from satellites to identify the vehicle’s position and direction by combing received data with information obtained from an onboard computer and various sensors. The GPS transmits geolocation and time information, which is received by a GPS receiver located in the vehicle’s navigation/infotainment system. Navigation/infotainment systems: -Process GPS signals to detect the vehicle’s location, -Detect the direction of travel. -Detect the travel distance. -Display maps on the navigation system screen. -Check GPS and sensor information against a map database. -Navigation System Parts – navigation/infotainment parts commonly include: -AM/FM antenna. -GPS antenna. -GPS receiver. -Direction sensor. -Speed sensor. -Display. -Navigation computer. -Antenna Service Considerations – when servicing a navigation/infotainment system antenna, removal may require accessing the backside of the panel to which it is mounted. Antennas located on the roof typically require removing or repositioning the headliner to access the antenna mounting fastener and antenna wiring. Specifically designed tools may be necessary to remove fender-mounted antennas. Following system service, always reference vehicle maker service information to identify required calibration procedures for the navigation/infotainment system. Occasionally, the system may not immediately recognize the correct location of the vehicle and may require repositioning the vehicle away from power lines or other sources of interference. ADAS and Convenience Systems Overview and Service (2/2) High Beam Assist, Adaptive Lighting, and Night Vision Systems -High Beam Assist – the high beam assist system uses a forward-facing camera that detects light color, intensity, and movement to determine when the high beams should be turned on and off. This system ensures the best headlamp setting to illuminate the road ahead regardless of whether you are driving on a highway, a country road, or in the city. The high beam assist system will monitor things such as: -Approaching vehicle headlamps and turn off high beams. -Preceding vehicle’s tail lamps and turn off high beams. -Outside light and turn the high beams on if necessary to ensure maximum visibility or off it is bright enough that the high beam headlamps are not required. -Adaptive Lighting – the adaptive front lighting system (AFS) generally does not operate with the transmission in reverse or at vehicle speeds less than 2 mph (3.2 km/h). This may vary by vehicle maker. Movement of the headlamps may be restricted at low vehicle speeds, and full movement of the lamps may not be allowed until vehicle speed is greater than about 30 mph (48 km/h). -System Parts – adaptive front lighting parts include swiveling and leveling actuators, control modules, and sensors such as the: -Steering wheel angle sensor -Vehicle speed sensor -Vehicle height sensor -Night Vision – night vision assist systems extend the perception of the driver beyond the reach of the headlamps through the use of thermographic cameras, infrared lights, head-up display, and other technologies. - courtesy of BMW -Night Vision System Parts – night view assist systems may use near-infrared technology. Night vision enhancement systems that use this technology require special infrared “flood lamps” to project infrared light into the surrounding area. The projected light is similar to what is used for many household remote controls. The night view assist system is typically activated when the headlamps are on and may detect objects a set distance ahead in darkness. Some systems can detect objects nearly 500 feet (152 m) in front of the vehicle. When it is activated, the system displays the images on a black-and-white screen typically found in the instrument panel or console. -Thermal Imaging – thermal imaging systems use far-infrared technology without active infrared illumination. It essentially converts thermal radiation into electronic signals. The sensor elements alter the resistance in proportion to the temperature. The higher the temperature, the higher the signal, creating a whiter pixel. This system also: -Shows objects ahead in varying degrees of brightness depending on the temperature of these objects. Provides a warning in the head-up display and the central information display. Has a heater element on the inside of the protective window to prevent the camera lens from misting over or freezing up. Blind Spot Detection, Cross-Traffic Alert, and Parking Assist Systems -Blind Spot Detection – many vehicles are now equipped with blind spot detection systems that detect vehicles approaching from behind in an adjacent lane. On some vehicles, the blind spot detection system has an indicator lamp in the door mirror. The blind spot detection system: -Engages when the turn signal is activated and signals the driver when a vehicle is approaching. -Generally uses radar to gauge the distance of approaching vehicles. -May use a camera in the door mirror to watch for approaching vehicles. -Is designed to ignore stationary objects. -System Parts – primary blind spot detection system parts include: - Sensors , which are generally located behind the rear bumper cover on each side. - Cameras, which are typically mounted beneath door mirrors to aid in detecting approaching traffic. - A steering wheel , which on some systems vibrates when vehicles are detected in the blind spot. - A control module , which calculates speed, object position, and vehicle tracking. Control modules cannot be adjusted or repaired. -Service Considerations – always reference vehicle maker service information to identify required calibration procedures following service on any of the blind spot detection system parts. These calibrations may require using a vehicle-specific diagnostic and targeting equipment. Calibration may also be required after rear bumper cover removal or replacement. In some cases, vehicle makers may not allow the repair of bumper covers in or around the location of sensors. Measurements taken from these sensors determine if a signal should be sent to indicate an approaching vehicle and repairs in these areas could hinder sensor performance. Note: Sensors for this system can be sensitive to contamination from items such as dirt, mud, snow, and other debris. The build-up of contaminants over these sensors can result in system malfunction. -Cross-Traffic Alert – some vehicles have cross-traffic alert system that notifies the driver of oncoming traffic when backing out of a parking space. System warnings may include an audible alert from the parking aid speaker, a visual alert on the right or left door mirror, and a message on the instrument panel cluster or infotainment screen. -Cross-Traffic Alert System Parts – the cross-traffic alert system: -Uses many of the same parts as a blind spot detection system, such as sensors located behind the rear bumper cover. -Uses radar that detects an approaching vehicle. -Has sensors that work at a 90-degree angle. -red – Cross- Traffic Alert -yellow – Rear View and Side View Mirror -green – Reverse Sensing System -Parking Assist – parking assist has progressed to the point where most vehicle makers offer the system as an option or standard equipment on their new models. There are two types of parking assist. These include: -Assist only, where the system provides visual and/or audio feedback to help determine the distance to obstacles. -Hands-free parallel parking assist. -Parking Assist – Assist Only – the parking assist system is designed to: -Calculate the distance to an object from the rear of the vehicle. Some systems engage when they detect an object 8 feet (2.4 m) behind the vehicle. -Provide audible and/or visual signals to the driver while parking and maneuvering the vehicle in REVERSE. -Work at low speeds, typically less than 5-11 mph (8-18 km/h). -Note: Some systems may also calculate how close an object is to the front of the vehicle when the vehicle is in DRIVE. Additionally, some systems do not detect objects below the bumper, such as people or pets and can be turned ON/OFF manually using a switch on the instrument panel. -Hands-Free Parallel Parking Assist – some systems have been designed to automatically parallel park the vehicle into an open parking space. The driver only needs to confirm the desired parking space and apply the brakes when required to control speed. The vehicle handles the proper steering. -System Parts – parts that make up the parking assist feature include: -Parking aid sensors, which are located in the bumper covers and used to judge the distance to other parked vehicles. -Parking aid modules-locations vary. -A parking aid speaker that, when the vehicle is in REVERSE, emits an audible beep sound to alert the driver of any obstacles behind the rear of the vehicle. -Cameras that, on some systems, are located in the rear or front to provide a visual of the area around the vehicle. -Hands-Free Parallel Parking Assist Parts – in addition to the parking assist system parts already discussed, there are additional cameras and sensors that are integral parts of the hands-free parallel parking assist system including: -Cameras -Parking assist ECU -Multi-display -Steering angle sensor -Skid control ECU -PARK/NEUTRAL position switch -Display and Navigation -Note: The hands-free parallel parking assist system may utilize cameras mounted in various locations on the vehicle, including the deck lid, tailgate, side mirrors, bumpers, and grille. Identifying damages, other than physical damages, will typically require the use of a scan tool. -Service-Considerations – check parking assist sensors for damage, even after a minor collision. The sensors on the bumpers can typically be refinished. However, excessive coats of refinish can hinder sensor performance. Some vehicle makers do not recommend refinishing sensors with minor finish damage because excessive paint thickness may adversely affect the operation of the parking assist system. Always reference vehicle maker service information to review information for repair or replacement recommendations for parking sensors. Lane Departure Warning, Attention Assist, Adaptive Cruise Control, and Collision Avoidance Systems -Lane Departure Warning – the lane departure warning assist system helps the driver keep the vehicle in the chosen lane. The system uses audio, visual, or steering wheel vibrations to warn the driver that the vehicle is drifting out of the lane. Some systems use the brakes to provide counter steering force to help keep the vehicle in the lane, but other systems may only provide a warning. The lane departure warning assist system typically operates at a speed of 35 mph (56km/h) or above, depending on the vehicle maker, and requires lane markers. -Lane Departure vs. Lane Keeping Assist – lane departure warning system is not activated if the turn signal is engaged before changing lanes. If the system is activated, the warning ends when the driver steers back into the lane, a lane change is completed, the vehicle drives on the line for longer than 2.5 seconds, or if the brake pedal is pressed. Active lane keeping systems alert the driver who is drifting out of a lane and nudge the vehicle back into the lane if the driver does not react. On some models, an icon that shows a vehicle drifting out of its lane indicates if the active lane keeping system is ON or OFF in the vehicle. -System Parts – primary parts of the lane departure warning system may include: -A camera, typically located behind the windshield near the rearview mirror. -A control module. -A lane departure warning ON/OFF switch. -A vibrator motor (if equipped). -The instrument panel cluster. -The radio for warning notification. -Service Considerations – when performing diagnostics and troubleshooting on these systems, it is important to remember road construction or poorly marked roads will prevent proper operation of the lane departure warning system. The lane departure warning system may not accurately detect: -Temporary markings along construction sites that would confuse the system. -Worn or faded road markings. -Road markings covered by snow or ice. -Road markings on roads with sharp bends. -Road markings under very poor weather conditions. -Note: These non-mechanical issues can cause the lane departure warning system to appear not to be operating properly. -Attention Assist – the attention assist system monitors the drivers’ attentiveness and may also be called “drowsy driver” or “driver alert control”. The system uses sensor technology to create an overall driver profile at the beginning of a trip and continuously compares the behavior pattern with real-time data from the sensors throughout the journey. Many of the same parts used for the lane departure warning system are used for attention assist system. These include: -A forward facing camera. -Driver information control module. -Forward sensing control module. -An infotainment module (silences the sound system to provide an audible warning). -A driver information center. -Service Considerations – the attention assist system uses the same camera as the lane departure warning system and may experience similar problems, which may occur from: -Weather and road conditions. -Physical damage to the windshield or camera housing. -Note: For issues other than physical damage, use a scan tool to diagnose the attention assist system. Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify calibration requirements following service of the attention assist system. -Adaptive Cruise Control – the adaptive cruise control (ACC) is an extension of conventional cruise control systems. The ACC system automatically maintains a set distance from a vehicle ahead. The system is not a collision warning or avoidance system; however, parts of the ACC system may be integrated into the collision avoidance systems. The ACC ONLY assists the driver and is not a fully independent driving system. The ACC does enhance driver convenience, since accelerating and braking operations occur without driver intervention. -System Parts – in addition to anti-lock brake system (ABS) and engine control modules, ACC systems use an ACC module to process radar information and determine if a forward vehicle is present. When the ACC system is in “time gap control,” the system sends information to the engine control and brake control modules to control the clearance between the ACC vehicle and the target vehicle. Wheel speed sensors read the speed of the vehicle’s wheel rotation and send the information to the ABS module. The distance sensor is located on the front of the vehicle to detect the presence of a vehicle ahead and the clearance between the ACC vehicle and the forward vehicle. Some distance sensors can be found under or behind the front bumper or behind the grille. -Collision Avoidance – collision avoidance systems detect situations in which a collision may be imminent. The collision avoidance system: -Is designed to predict a collision. -Helps prevent the collision from happening or minimizes the severity. -Can be used to detect front and rear collisions. -Uses forward-object detection with additional information, such as vehicle speeds, to predict an imminent collision. -Note: A properly operating system determines if the distance between vehicles is at a dangerous level and if a collision is about to happen. The system will forewarn the driver (through audible, visual, and/or physical alerts) of a possible collision to prompt the driver to take preventative action. -System Parts – the collision avoidance system: -Is dependent on system options. -Uses radar sensors to detect vehicles in front and behind the vehicle. -Uses a control module that calculates speed, object position, and vehicle tracking. -Note: On some vehicles, detecting vehicles and obstacles in front of the vehicle is determined from information provided by the front millimeter wave radar sensor and an object recognition camera. The millimeter wave radar sensor is also used by the adaptive cruise control system. The object recognition camera consists of two side-by-side cameras typically located in the center front area of the headliner. -Service Considerations – following a collision, always reference vehicle maker service information to identify mandatory system calibrations as it may be necessary to calibrate systems such as the collision avoidance system even if no damage was found to system parts. It is also important to note that servicing system parts such as the radar sensor may require special tools for adjusting and calibrating the radar sensor beam axis. Correct alignment of sensors is necessary to ensure the proper operation of the system. ADAS Diagnostics – When the Calibration Fails Module 1 – Diagnostic Problem Solving for ADAS -Awareness Goal – this course is designed to raise the awareness of technicians and those in the collision repair industry who are impacted by advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS). It is no surprise that today’s vehicles are equipped with sophisticated technologies leading to a future of self-driving vehicles. Understanding the systems, sensors, and how they operate can lead toward more successful diagnostics when you encounter problems. This course introduces you to the complicated topic of diagnostics. It is not designed as a deep dive in the vast ocean of diagnostics. We off you a high-level awareness as a start in your further education. -Diagnostic Technician – the role of a technician has evolved in recent time to include diagnostics. Following a repair, many procedures require sensors to be initialized and/or calibrated. Sensors and modules that have been replaced typically require programming or coding before any calibration. Also, there can be no diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) related to the system’s operation to perform a calibration, apart from a calibration-related code. If a calibration fails or is incomplete, it is helpful to approach the situation with a problem-solving mindset, that of a diagnostic technician. -Problem-Solving Approach – diagnostic technicians need to have a plan. There are variety of different problem-solving approaches that vehicle makers and diagnostic technicians may use. Although the number of steps and their names may differ, the goal is similar – to have a systematic approach to resolving the problem. This course follows the I-CAR five-step process. -Define the problem -Know the system -Find the cause -Make the repair -Test the system -Define the Problem – start with defining the problem. Lets use an example that a calibration has failed. A description of the problem is that you performed a calibration but received the message that the calibration is not complete. In diagnostics, take a systematic approach. Verify that the problem currently exists. Discover when the problem started. Find out what conditions are present. There is no detail that is too small. Uncover if there are other problems that may connect with the calibration failure. Vehicle makers may have interview sheets to help collect information. -Know the System – a key aspect of knowing a system is research. Review the description and operational information for the system. Part location diagrams, schematics (wiring diagrams), network communication maps, and message charts offer more information. DTC explanations and symptom charts are additional resources you need to be familiar with as you continue with system diagnostics. You should also consider technical service bulletins (TSBs) or other similar resources when you encounter problems. RTS has Collision Repair Diagnostics Definitions and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) Collision Repair Diagnostics Process for your reference as well. Do the research early in the diagnostic process. -ADAS Sensors Overview – having an understanding about each sensor that informs the ADAS in the repair is invaluable. Sensors may be ultrasonic, radar, cameras, or LiDAR. Lets learn more about the sensor types. Video …… -Other Vehicle Sensors – there are many additional sensors on the vehicle that are required for the operation of other systems. Sensors such as steering angle sensors (SAS), wheel speed sensors, yaw rate sensors, and many others are connected to a variety of vehicle electronic systems. These sensors gather data for the systems to which they are connected. That data may also be shared with the ADAS. Any inaccuracy or problems with this sensor data can adversely affect the primary system the sensor reports to as well as ADAS and other systems. For example, something as simple as an SAS that is out of calibration can have a domino effect on a variety of systems. -Invisible ADAS Communication – lets continue in our problem-solving process to know the system. ADAS communication is not something we can see. We should deepen our understanding of the communication process among the various sensors and systems in the ADAS and vehicle communication networks. -Parts of the ADAS Network – some ADAS features may be relatively independent of other vehicle systems; however, most are not. The word “network” is important to consider. The parts of the ADAS are interconnected to a larger vehicle communications or system. What happens to individual systems may impact what happens within the other systems on the network as well. The parts that comprise the ADAS network include the sensors, computers, and the vehicle parts, or actuators, that act on the directions. We have already learned about some of the ADAS sensors. They gather data about the surroundings and vehicle operational conditions or inputs. The inputs stream to computer software that processes the data. -Computer Modules and Actuators – system modules and certain parts have data transceivers which receive and transmit information over the communication network, connecting the vehicle systems. A node is a module, sensor, or other device connected to the controller area network (CAN) bus. The commanded outputs from an ADAS may be sent to other systems. This communication may activate warning devices for some systems or request actions like increase or decrease powertrain torque, apply brakes, and steer left or right. -ADAS Network – that ADAS network involves a chain of communication that is interdependent. Each part influences the operation of a system. Many systems work together sharing data to make decisions affecting the actions taken by the different systems. Inaccurate data from one sensor may not only affect the system, but it may affect many systems. The ADAS commonly interact with: -Powertrain control module (PCM) -Anti-lock braking system (ABS) -Supplemental restraint system (SRS) -Electronic power steering (EPS) -Body control module (BCM) -Note: Depending on the vehicle and its features, there may be interactions with additional systems. -Sensor Function – each sensor that contributes inputs to ADAS has its strengths but also weaknesses. Sensor fusion is the concept of bringing together the information from more than one sensor and/or system and using that combined data to better inform the vehicle systems. Sensor fusion builds a stronger data set, enabling increased system performance or capabilities. -Sensor Fusion Example – sensor fusion provides awareness of the situation. For example, a forward-facing camera may be used to fill the gap by radar and ultrasonic sensors, so lines, signs, and other objects can be identified. Sensor fusion combines the data to attempt an accurate reading of the circumstances surrounding the vehicle. Radar may detect an object. The camera also sees the object. Object-recognition image processing may be able to determine what it is, distinguishing between a person and a bicycle. -Complicated Processing – on some ADAS, the data collected by a sensor may be processed within that unit. That unit issues commands on the CAN bus for programmed warnings or actions. The relevant modules on that network receive the messages and act accordingly. In other cases, the data from one ADAS sensor/module may be sent to a different ADAS sensor/module for analysis and processing. This module uses decision-making algorithms which are programmed rules for processing data to validate objects and threat level. Based on the system programming and information collected, the system sends out warnings or action requests on the communication network as determined appropriate. -Dedicated ADAS Control Module – some system designs use a dedicated ADAS control module. The information collected by the sensors is sent to the dedicated ADAS control module which is responsible for analyzing and processing the inputs and issuing output commands. -Crucial Knowledge – this is only a glimpse into the ADAS communication network. You will need to fully understand the systems for the vehicle on which you are working by researching the vehicle maker service information. By knowing the system, performing the research, and completing more diagnostics, you will grow in your understanding and expertise. -What Could Go Wrong in Communication – armed with the knowledge about ADAS and vehicle communication, you can begin to find the cause in the problem-solving process. As you diagnose and problem-solve, consider the power, ground, and data communications. Ask yourself, “are communications happening? Is there something hindering communications?”. Typically, troubleshooting is enhanced with flowcharts that guide you through a process of inspecting, testing, and eliminating the possible variables. Decision trees walk you down a diagnostic path. -Communication Busses – technicians do not write the code or messages, but they must flow for the system to operate. Message transfer issues may include. -Shorts between CAN bus lines or other wiring -Short-to-power or ground -Opens or a break in the wiring/connectors -Issues with a module or part on the CAN bus that is corrupting or interfering with communications. -Note: As other sensors or modules are introduced in vehicle design, there may be additional communication busses used to support the data transfer between sensors, modules, and systems. In some cases, these are private, dedicated, CAN busses. -Other Diagnostic Tools – a scan tool and vehicle diagnostic software may have the capability to provide a graphic representation of the communication network topology. There may be a color-coding system for module status, identifying which nodes are communicating as designed or if there are problems. This process assists in finding what is corrupt and viewing what is present. It works as an electronic roll call checking in to see who is present and working. As a diagnostic technician, you may need to use a lab scope to analyze the signals on the CAN bus. This is to identify possible problems during diagnostics where no or intermittent communication is happening. -System Data – when conducting ADAS diagnostics, it can be beneficial to look at system data which may identify values associated with certain parameters or the state of inputs and outputs. This may be referred to as live data or parameter identification (PIDs) that request data from a module or system. This is done using a scan tool connected to the vehicle. When following a diagnostic flowchart, the technician may be guided to view certain data within a system and compare that to known, normal values or identified current conditions. There may also be further testing or adjustments based on the results. -Finding the Cause – as a diagnostic technician, you need to gather information about the vehicle and the circumstances that brought it before you. Start with the basics and assume nothing. This process involves questions that uncover facts to help find the cause. -What is the repair history? -Were there repairs in the vicinity of a sensor? -Did the vehicle have suspension, alignment, or structural work? -Have you personally done a visual inspection? -Were all preconditions met before the calibration was attempted? -Were all calibration procedures carried out properly? -Are there DTCs, or is there some other indication of a problem? -Are there any non-OEM accessories installed? These could be body, chassis, or communication network-related modifications. -Visual Inspection – do a visual inspection. What do you see? Are the wires and connectors in good condition? Wire and connector issues will likely set DTCs. Is something interfering with the way the sensor sees the world of impeding its ability to see the world clearly and correctly? If the radar mounts behind the emblem on the grille, was the correct emblem installed? Was the windshield replaced? Did the glass installer use OEM-approved glass? Look at the sensor mounting locations. Are they properly oriented with the correct side of the sensor facing out? A sensor or camera can only gather information based on how it is mounted. Is there a hidden repair that may not be immediately visible such as on a bumper cover? -Diagnostics with DTCs – if there are DTCs, you need to understand what they mean and how they affect the system. Research the DTCs. Service information has flowchart-type information and diagnostic decision trees to guide you through the inspection and testing process to arrive at the root cause. If there are network communication error codes, correct them first. Some DTCs may point to an aiming or calibration issue or to some electronic system or circuit issue. Where codes appear in multiple systems, you need to understand how the systems communicate with one another. You also need to know how this affects the operation of the different systems in order to prioritize your diagnostic approach. Diagnostic tools and/or service information may provide additional resources to help you prioritize how you move forward. -DTC Trends and Patterns – when diagnosing multiple DTCs, as you review the list of codes, look for any trends or patterns that may help identify a common root cause. For example, if the anti-lock brake controller was not communicating, there would likely be a number of other modules reporting a “no communication” code. The pattern that the ABS controller is not reporting is a trend worth investigating. -Diagnostics with No DTCs – if there are no DTCs, your approach may involve using symptom charts. Some steps in a symptom chart require visual inspection of the vehicle, system, and/or parts. It may require some disassembly for complete inspection. As you work to find the cause, the symptom chart may require testing for power, ground, communications, and specific pinpoint tests. In the process, additional testing of other systems or parts may be identified, including the use of the diagnostic scan tool, DVOM, and other test equipment. Typical elements of symptom charts may be: -Symptom condition descriptions -A list of possible causes -Next steps such as inspections, test, or corrective actions. -Unobstructed View – lets consider a blind spot radar on the quarter panel, hidden by the bumper cover. The radar must be able to transmit and receive through the cover without any distortion or loss of signal strength. In situations where the radar will not calibrate, visually inspect the sensor. Then, consider calibrating without the cover. Did it calibrate? What was the difference? You are not done yet. This just eliminated a possible cause. Was the cover repaired or refinished? The final calibration must be done with a properly refinished bumper in place. Finding the cause is a repetitive process in which you ask questions and then confirm or deny the information. Continue through this cycle until you find the root cause. -Make the Repair – the next step is to make the repair. This is probably the shortest duration in the entire process. A solution may be to replace a bent bracket or correct the sensor mounting orientation. Another solution may be to correct an obstruction in the view of the sensor. When carrying out repairs, always follow the OEM repair instructions. Documentation is important for every step. -Wiring and Electronics Solutions – if the problem was with communications, it may be with the wiring, a connector, or a module that needs to be corrected. If any parts have been replaced, proper coding, programming, and/or initializations must be done. Part of the solution could be replacing a faulty module. Typically, this is only done after you have eliminated all other possible causes, or there is a specific code indicating internal module failure. Other wire or connector damage identified on any part of the system or supporting systems needs to be corrected. -Test the System – as a diagnostic technician, you must test the system as part of the process. After the repairs are complete and the systems are properly calibrated, make a thorough test drive part of your routine. You must evaluate the performance of each of the ADAS features, as well as all other aspects of the vehicle, to ensure it will perform as designed. Your customers have entrusted their vehicle to you for complete and safe repairs. Always aim for 100% accuracy. -Knowledge Investment – diagnosing and repairing the system problems can range from relatively simple sensor alignment corrections to complex network diagnostics and testing, depending on the true root cause. With the right tools, equipment, information resources, and knowledge, you will gain proficiency over time. The world of diagnostics is an ever-changing landscape. It is no different than the evolution of the vehicle and its future. Investing the time to further advance your knowledge and skills is an investment in your professional future. Invest wisely. Adhesive Bonding (1/2) -Adhesives for the Collision Repair Industry – Adhesives have been used in the collision repair industry for years, and their use is expanding. Vehicle makers are using adhesives in a variety of wars, from something as small as a sensor to something as large as structural components. Many performance vehicles are manufactured using lightweight composite materials to reduce vehicle weight. These composite materials cannot be welded together and mechanical fasteners would add weight. The use of adhesives allows these materials to be joined together easily. Another advantage to using adhesives is they help absorb vibration and common road impact forces. -Who Repairs with Adhesives – vehicle makers commonly use adhesives in many different ways including -Panel Bonding -Rivet Bonding -Structural Bonding -Weld Bonding -* It is important to always reference vehicle maker repair information to identify repair requirements and procedures. It’s not uncommon for a vehicle to be manufactured using one joining process and repaired using a different joining process. We can’t assume that using the same method a vehicle maker uses to assemble a vehicle is the correct and recommended method to repair collision damage. -General Characteristics – characteristics of adhesive include the ability to cure relatively quickly at room temperature, strength, impact resistance, and substrate preparation requirements that are different than typically required when welding, riveting, or bolts parts together. You can find the specific characteristics of the adhesive on the technical data sheet. -Types of Adhesives – depending on the adhesive maker, material may be available in volumes of 6 oz to 13 oz. One-part adhesives are packaged as a single tube. Two-part adhesives are packaged as two tubes and are dispensed together through a single mixing tip. These adhesives are available from the vehicle maker or from aftermarket product supplies -Urethane-Based – urethanes are typically one-part materials. One-part urethanes cure with the presence of moisture. Two-part urethanes chemically cure and can be accelerated with heat. -Acrylic-Based – Acrylic-based adhesives are typically two-part materials that cure by chemical reaction. Acrylics cure faster than urethanes and epoxy-based materials. * Some adhesive makers may refer to their acrylic-based products as methacrylate -Epoxy-Based – the image shows an epoxy-based adhesive. Epoxies are two-part materials that cure by a chemical reaction and can have their cure rate accelerated with heat. -Combination – this image shows a combination, or hybrid, product using urethane and epoxy bases. Some adhesive makers have products that combine bases like urethane and epoxy to meet specific applications. -Mixing Tips – mixing tips are product- and material-specific and should not be switched between brands or material types. These materials are chemically unique and may be mixed at different rates. If not properly mixed, an inadequate repair may occur from an incorrect cure or mixing rate. Some mixing tips use different length augers. For example, some companies use mixing tips for expandable two-part foams that will fit on their two-part adhesive materials, and the only different between the mixing tip is the length of the auger. In that case, mark the mixing tips to indicate the appropriate product. -Directions for Using Adhesives – when working with adhesives always reference vehicle maker service information to identify product and application requirements. If the required adhesive is labeled from the vehicle maker, follow the instructions provided by the vehicle maker. The vehicle maker may recommend following the product maker instructions for part preparation and some application considerations. In this situation, make a note in the repair file that vehicle maker procedures were used. -Non-OEM Label – if the required adhesive is not vehicle maker labeled, follow the adhesive maker’s recommendations. -File Documentation – you should record the name, part, and lot number of the materials used for the repair. This can be done by writing the information on the repair order, or by removing the label from the used cartridge, attaching it to a sheet of paper, and placing it in the customer’s file. Retaining this type of information may prove helpful if there is a product recall or legal action regarding the repair. -Panel Bonding Methods – when using adhesive during the repair process, vehicle makers will typically require a specific bonding procedure. -Adhesive-Only Repairs – an adhesive-only repair does not use welds, rivets, or other mechanical fasteners. Anchor welds may be required in certain areas for adhesive-only repairs. These typically include roof skins and quarter panels. Anchor welds are welds that are typically located at the corners of a part and help keep a part from being peeled off the vehicle. When performing an adhesive-only repair, some benefits may include -Corrosion resistance -No heat being introduced into the repair area -The ability to reposition a part after attachment and clamping -No mechanical fasteners, such as bolts and rivets -* Always refer to vehicle maker service information to identify approved repair methods, adhesive materials, and required bonding techniques. -Repairs with Welds and Adhesives – some vehicle makers require using welds and adhesive. Using adhesives and welds is called weld bonding. Depending on who supplies the reattachment procedure, the part may be attached using bonding and either GMA plug weld or spot welds. -Repairs with Rivets and Adhesives – some vehicle makers require using rivets and adhesive. Using adhesives and rivets is called rivet bonding. Many aluminum-intensive vehicles use rivet bonding on both structural and non-structural locations on the vehicle. -Adhesive vs Cohesive Failure – not using the adhesive in a manner instructed by the adhesive maker can cause the adhesive to fail prematurely. There are two types of failure: adhesive and cohesive. Adhesive failure on the left. Cohesive failure on the right -Adhesive Failure – adhesive failure is when the adhesive does not hold to substrates such as bare metal, primer, or topcoats. To help prevent adhesive failure, always work with clean surfaces that are prepared following the adhesive maker’s instructions. If a substrate is not abraded properly, or if the bonding surface is not free of contaminants, adhesive failure can result. -Cohesive Failure – cohesive failure is when the adhesive fails to remain intact with itself. An example of cohesive failure would be two parts separating with the adhesive remaining attached to both substrates. Causes of cohesive failure include: -The adhesive has expired -The recommended work time is exceeded -The cure time has been accelerated too aggressively -Properly Storing Adhesives – it is important to store and dispose of adhesives properly in order to ensure good repairs and comply with regulations -Storage Guidelines – to ensure maximum shelf life, store adhesives in a clean, dry area at 65-85 degrees F and out of direct sunlight. Both one- and two-part adhesives should be stored in relatively low humidity. Acrylic and urethane adhesives are moisture-sensitive and must be protected from relatively high humidity. To protect adhesives from humidity, do not open the product package until the product will be used. Store partially used adhesives with the mixing tip attached to the cartridge. Some adhesive makers recommend removing the mixing tip and replacing it with the original packaging plug. Store any partially used cartridges in an airtight plastic bag. -Shelf Life – adhesives are usually guaranteed for 6-12 months from the date of purchase depending on the adhesive maker. This is known as shelf life. After a product has been opened, the shelf life will be reduced. To ensure quality, keep a minimum inventory of opened products. If an adhesive maker recommends that the cartridge be stored with the nozzle end down, it may be to keep air from entering the cartridges. If an adhesive maker recommends that the cartridges be stored upright, it may be to keep any air in the cartridges at the top so it can be purged easily. -Disposal – if an adhesive shelf life has expired, discard the material. If a product is being discarded, non-dispensed products are considered hazardous waste. To dispose of the material so it is considered nonhazardous waste, dispense the product and let it cure. When the material changes state to a solid, it is typically not considered a hazardous material. * When disposing of adhesive, always refer to the Safety Data Sheet (SDS), and follow local, state, and national environmental control methods. Cured adhesives may be disposed of as non-hazardous waste. -Adhesive-Only Process – when performing an adhesive-only repair, lap joints are used. A lap joint is one substrate resting parallel and in contact with another substrate. With this type of repair the adhesive is the only material holding the parts together. An exception to this would be a hemmed flange, such as a door skin or quarter panel, that also helps to lock the panel in place. The arrow is the overlap area. Replacement part on the top, original part on the bottom -Adhesive-Only Considerations – some benefits of doing adhesive-only bonding include corrosion resistance, no heat being introduced to the repair zone, the ability to reposition a part after attachment and clamping compared to mechanical fastening methods, and no mechanical fasteners such as rivets. Before performing this type of repair, always reference vehicle maker repair information to ensure this type of repair is approved by the vehicle maker. Most vehicle makers have an approved procedure for this type of repair when replacing door skins, but there is limited acceptance of this type of repair with structural parts. -Vehicle Application Examples – some examples of parts where vehicle makers may approve the use of adhesive-only include -Roof panels -Quarter panels -Door skins -Rear body panels -Stationary glass -Trim -Floor pans -* Not all vehicle makers approve the use of adhesives. Even with those that do, if adhesive is recommended for a specific part, those guidelines should not necessarily be applied to the same part on a different model. -Wet or Work Time – wet or work time is the time between when the adhesive is applied to when the parts must be attached and clamped in place. A weak bond may result if the panel is installed, repositioned, or clamped to the substrate after the wet time has expired. For most adhesives, wet time is specified at 50% relative humidity and 70 degrees F. This time is halved for every 20 degrees F increase and doubled for every 20 degrees F decrease. If the bond is not made before the wet time expires, the adhesive must be removed and reapplied, because the adhesive may begin to dry and not adequately “bite” into, or adhere to, the substrate. * Some adhesive makers say that the wet time is starting to expire when the adhesive changes color as it cures. -Clamp or Handling Time – clamp or handling time is the required time that parts must remain joined with pressure applied. This time is not part of wet time and is commonly less than cure time. There may be situations when an adhesive maker lists an unclamp time. Unclamp time is when the clamps can be removed from the part, but the part should not be placed into service. Unclamp time is before the cure time has been reached. * The part should not be placed in service when clamp time expires. This includes installing a part on a vehicle and then raising the vehicle on a hoist before the cure time has been reached. -Cure Time – cure time is the specific time required for an adhesive to dry or set at a specific temperature (typically 70 degrees F or 21 degrees Celsius) and humidity. When an adhesive cures, the material changes state. Only after the material cures should the part be placed in service. Two-part adhesives cure by a chemical reaction that occurs when mixing the two materials: -A resin -A hardener, or catalyst -Forced Curing – some adhesive makers allow force curing two-part adhesives to decrease the cure time. Force curing with heat is not allowed on most two-part acrylic adhesives because the heat breaks down the chemical structure of the adhesive. When allowed, force curing can be done using heat from an infrared heat lamp, heat gun, temperature-controlled spraybooth, etc. when using heat to shorten cure time, follow the product maker’s instructions. Do not heat bonded parts with an open flame. -Corrosion Protection of the Flange Area – adhesives that do not require a primer to the bond area are commonly available for bonding metal parts. These adhesives are designed to be applied to bare metal areas, and protect against corrosion. These adhesives must cover all of the bare metal to provide protection. Failure to cover all bare metal surfaces will result in corrosion that could creep under the adhesive and cause an adhesive failure. When working with an adhesive that requires a primer, clean the mating surfaces and apply a primer before applying the adhesive. -Adhesives Not Requiring a Primer – to apply adhesives with corrosion-resistance properties to bare-metal mating surface -1) apply a ¼ to 3/8 inch bead of adhesive to the bond areas of all parts -2) smooth the adhesive bead along the entire area to be bonded using a plastic spreader. Make sure all bare metal bonding areas are covered to ensure proper corrosion protection -3) if necessary, apply an additional adhesive bead to the inside edge of the replacement panel, or to the flange on the vehicle structure. The additional bead ensures adequate bond-line thickness, and reduces the possibilities of air bubbles in the adhesive bead -4) install the part -Adhesives Requiring a Primer – when working with an adhesive that requires primed mating surfaces -1) begin with coated flanges as recommended by the adhesive maker -2) apply a ¼ to 3/8 inch bead of adhesive to either the replacement panel flange or the vehicle flange -3) do not smooth the adhesive bead. Allow the adhesive to cover the joint from the pressure applied at the repair joint -4) assemble and clamp the parts together. When applying clamping pressure, glass beads inside the adhesive help control over-clamping -Applying Adhesives – when applying adhesive to a part, the adhesives should be applied in a continuous non-breaking bead overlapping at the start and stop locations of the bead. Be sure the bead is of uniform size around the panel on the bonding area and is applied around the holes on a part to provide proper bonding strength -Bond-Line Thickness – bond-line thickness is the thickness of the cured adhesive in the joint. If the bond line is too large, some areas of the adhesive may cure quicker than others because of the heat generated by the adhesive. To ensure proper bond-line thickness, some adhesives contain glass beads to prevent a bond line that is too thin. Adhesives used for plastics, and a few products used for steel, do not contain glass beads. When working with these types of materials, shims may be required to create a proper bond-line thickness. When unsure about the adhesive being used, refer to the adhesive maker’s recommendations for maintaining a consistent and specific bond-line thickness Adhesive Bonding Considerations -Cleaning the Flange Area – for bonding procedures that use epoxy or acrylic adhesives, remove all of the original adhesive and clean the mating flange area using: -A plastic-coated abrasive wheel to remove any remaining adhesive, corrosion, E-coat, or other substances. -The recommended grit sandpaper to remove the weld nuggets and galvanizing -* Do not use grinding tools that may reduce metal thickness. Some adhesive makers recommend applying metal adhesives to properly prepare bare metal. * After panel removal and cleaning, straighten the mating flanges to create a flat bonding surface. -Applying Primer as Required – during the panel removal process, the mating surfaces may have been scratched. If called for by the adhesive maker, cover scratches with a corrosion-resistance primer. Depending on the adhesive, the adhesive maker may recommend applying a corrosion-resistant primer to the bonding area of the panels. Regardless of the bonding process, always refer to the technical data sheet and follow the specific steps provided. When choosing a corrosion-resistance primer, refer to the adhesive maker for the recommended type of product to use. -Part Attachment – when attaching adhesively bonded parts with two-part adhesive -1) assemble the parts and clamp them into position so they do not move -2) if necessary, reposition the parts by sliding. If a part is lifted, air bubbles can become trapped inside the adhesive. If the parts are lifted, the adhesive must be removed and reapplied. 3) secure the mating parts as soon as possible. Some tools that can be used for securing panels include clamps, locking pliers, and fasteners, such as clecos, rivets, or threaded fasteners. -Steps Following Assembly – after attaching the panel -Clean any excess adhesive by scraping, grinding with a plastic-coated abrasive wheel, or by using a towel dampened with adhesive remover. Do not apply adhesive remover directly to the bonded part, as doing this can weaken the strength of the bond. -Apply body filler, if necessary, following the required clamping or handling time. -Begin the preparation steps for refinishing. A/C Systems Operation and Damage Analysis Basic A/C Systems Operation -Basic A/C Systems Operation and Refrigerant Flow – to understand air conditioning (A/C) systems, it is important to know how refrigerant removes heat from the vehicle’s passenger cabin. Air conditioning systems are considered closed loop systems that leverage the properties of refrigerant when it changes state. By controlling refrigerant pressure in the system, the state can be changed from a gaseous state to a liquid state and from a liquid state back to a gaseous state. As the state of refrigerant changes, heat is absorbed when it changes from a liquid state to a gaseous state, and heat is released when it changes from a gaseous state to a liquid state. Most A/C Systems in modern vehicles have electric controls for user demand requests. On-board vehicle communications networking is used to send the request to turn on the compressor and fans, as well as monitor a variety of sensors for system pressures, temperatures, and other parameters. If the A/C system is inoperative, a scan tool will be required to retrieve DTCs and perform diagnostics. This course will focus on refrigerant flow and related A/C system parts. There are two common system types that will be discussed. -Accumulator A/C – -Receiver/Drier System Accumulator A/C System Operation and Damage Analysis -PCM and Other Computer Modules – the PCM or some other computer module may be used to monitor the high and low side pressure sensors as well as other system sensors, and is used to command clutch engagement and compressor operation as well as cooling fan operation, usually through relays. The heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) control head or multi-function control panel sends the request for A/C to the PCM and may monitor the outside air temperature. -A/C Systems Inspection and Testing – when inspecting an A/C system, a visual inspection of the system should be done to identify any obvious breach in the system parts. If no obvious issues are found, turn the system on to verify the system is cooling properly and the blower motor and cooling fans are working. This may include measuring the air temperature at the outlet vents on the dash, and comparing the readings to information found in the service manual. If the engine cannot be started, manifold gauges can be used to identify whether or not the system has a charge. Regardless of whether or not the system is operating properly, it should be used to identify whether or not the system has a charge. Dye may be present or added to the system and monitored to identify slow leaks. Fittings and joints are the most common areas for leaks to occur. When inspecting for leaks, inspect these points first. Refrigerant is heavier than air, so be sure to use a sniff tester to “sniff” the lower areas of the part being tested. Modern systems have smaller refrigerant charges compared to older systems and are more sensitive to refrigerant charge issues. It is important that the correct amount of clean refrigerant is in the system for proper operation. -Compressor Operation and Damage Analysis – the compressor is used to create flow, and change the pressure of the refrigerant in the A/C system. -Compressor Operation – refrigerant enters the compressor as a low-pressure gas and exits as a high-pressure gas. On most vehicles, the compressor uses a belt and pulley with a clutch to externally drive the compressor, allowing visual verification of operation. Some belt-driven compressors are variable displacement and may not use a clutch. The compressor requires oil for lubrication. The oil travels through the A/C system with the refrigerant. Electric vehicles and some hybrid-electric vehicles use an electrically driven compressor. -Compressor Damage Analysis – depending on the design of the system, the A/C compressor may require removal to allow access for inspection. Electric compressors should be carefully inspected for damage to the case and wiring. Do not touch any of the high voltage wiring, connectors, or parts unless the high voltage system has been disarmed and it has been verified there is no shock hazard present. When inspecting an A/C compressor for damage or to determine repair ability: -Inspect the clutch and pulley. These parts may be replaced individually or as a unit. If the clutch or pulley is damaged, inspect the shaft and seal for damage or signs of leakage -Inspect whether the compressor engages and disengages when the system is activated, and as the system pressures increase and decrease -Inspect the attachment points of the refrigerant lines -Inspect the pressure relief valve, switches, and wiring for broken insulation or shorted wires -Inspect the compressor case for signs of impact. Impact could cause internal damage, leaks, or other problems. -Inspect the brackets that attach the compressor to the engine -Determine what oil is required by the vehicle maker before doing repairs. Using the wrong oil will lead to inadequate protection and premature part failure -Note: If the compressor pully is in close proximity to a frame rail or other parts, inspect it for possible impact marks -High-Pressure Relief Valve – -High-pressure relief valves can be located in many areas on the high side. Common locations are on the: -High-side line -Receiver/drier -Back of the compressor -High-pressure relief valves vent refrigerant if excessively high pressures are sensed to prevent compressor damage. When a high-pressure relief valve has released pressure from the system, there is normally a visual indicator, such as a piece of tape being blown off the valve, indicating that high pressures were reached at some time. Some systems may contain an electrical control that prevents the high-pressure relief valve from venting refrigerant into the atmosphere. These systems may use a: -Single contact switch that disengages the clutch and shuts down the compressor -Dual contact switch that turns the cooling fan to high in an attempt to increase the airflow and heat transfer at the condenser -Compressor shutdown switch that disengages the clutch and prevents internal damage to the compressor -Condenser – Refrigerant enters the condenser as a high-pressure gas and exits as a high-pressure liquid. The condenser: -Changes the state of the refrigerant through condensation and heat exchange. -Releases the heat that was taken from the passenger compartment into the atmosphere -Condenser Characteristics – some A/C systems may have a secondary condenser, or sub-condenser, to provide additional heat transfer. Many R-134a systems use a parallel flow condenser as shown here -Condenser Damage Analysis – when inspecting a condenser for damage: -Depending on the severity of the damage, straightening may be possible -Bent fins may be straightened by combing. If the condenser tubes are damaged, there are typically no repair options besides complete part replacement -Determine if there is any refrigerant in the system. To do this, attach manifold gauges to the A/C system service ports. Note: If this is being done, a certified A/C technician is required to work on systems containing A/C refrigerant -Do not automatically anticipate loss of refrigerant when a vehicle has been in a collision. Depending on the damage and type of impact, an A/C system may retain its charge -Evaporator – evaporators absorb the passenger compartment heat and leave behind cool, dehumidified air to be circulated in the passing er compartment. The refrigerant enters the evaporator as a low-pressure mist and exits as a low-pressure gas. The evaporator: -Uses the evaporation process to absorb heat from the passenger compartment -Removes humidity as the warm passenger compartment air contacts the cooler evaporator -Feels cold to the touch while the heat exchange is taking place -Evaporator Designs – most evaporator designs include: -Tube-and-fin (single, round tubes zig-zagging back and forth through a pattern of fins) -Serpentine (flat tubes with fins winding between them) -Plate-and-fin (a series of plates with various paths) -Note: Refrigerant flow through tube-and-fin and serpentine evaporators can be seen by inspecting how the tubing is routed. There is typically only one path for the refrigerant to flow, unlike the plate-and-fin design, which has multiple paths. -Evaporator Damage Analysis – when inspecting an evaporator, look for dye or oil leaking from the condensate drain on the passenger’s side cowl/under body, indicating a leak. Due to their location in the dash panel, fin or tube damage as well as collision damage is not common, but may be caused by stress placed on A/C lines or indirect through the body to the evaporator housing. Evaporators are not easily accessible and are usually inspected last. Dirt and debris can accumulate on the evaporator and lead to corrosion and leaks in certain situations. Note: If engine coolant is leaking from the condensate drain, the heater core is likely bad. -Orifice Tubes – orifice tubes are found on A/C systems equipped with accumulators. -Refrigerant Changes – a change in refrigerant pressure and state occurs as the refrigerant passes through the orifice tube. The refrigerant enters the orifice tube as a high-pressure liquid, passes through the orifice tube, and exits as a low-pressure mist -Damage Analysis – orifice tubes may be located at the condenser outlet or in the line leading to the evaporator inlet and may be damaged if the encapsulating part is damaged or if there has been a part failure in the system or if other contaminants caused the orifice tube filter screen to clog. When replacing orifice tubes, it is important to order the correct size and that it is installed with the arrow in the direction of refrigerant flow. Orifice tubes have a variety of inlet end designs that may require specialized removal tools or a tool attachment -Accumulator – a system with an accumulator has a fixed orifice tube. Refrigerant enters and exits the accumulator as a low-pressure gas. The accumulator is not used to change the state or the pressure of the refrigerant in an A/C system -Accumulator Characteristics – -Are located on the low side of the air conditioning system, right before the refrigerant returns to the compressor, typically at the evaporator outlet. -Are found on systems with fixed metering, such as an orifice tube. Since orifice tubes are in-line part, an accumulator may act as an indication that an orifice tube location should be identified during problem-solving -Use an internal desiccant material to remove moisture from the refrigerant as it passes through -Act as a storage area for excess liquid refrigerant -Prevent liquid from entering the compressor and damaging it. Liquid cannot be compressed -May contain an external pressure cycling switch. This switch monitors pressures and cycles the compressor on and off as necessary -Accumulator or Receiver/Drier Damage Analysis – accumulator or receiver/drier replacement recommendations vary. Accumulators and receiver/driers typically require replacement following collision repairs when the system has been open for an extended period of time. Additional evacuation time may be needed for R-134a systems. Moisture is a contaminant and can cause corrosion and insufficient cooling problems. The refrigerant oil in R-134a systems absorbs moisture and extra evacuation time will help remove the moisture. Note: Check vehicle maker service information for specific replacement requirements -Pressure Switches – there are three different cutoff switches used in an A/C system -High-Pressure Cutoff Switch – high-pressure cutoff switches disengage the compressor clutch when excessively high pressures are sensed. High-pressure cutoff switches can be located throughout the A/C system. The most common locations are on: -The high-side line -The compressor discharge muffler -The back of the compressor -Low-Pressure Cutoff Switch – low-pressure cutoff switches cycle the compressor clutch when low pressures are sensed and will not allow the compressor clutch to engage, preventing damage to the A/C system. Low-pressure cutoff switches can be located throughout the A/C system. The most common locations are on the low-side line, accumulator, H-valve, and back of the compressor -High/Low-Pressure Cutoff Switch – a high/low-pressure cutoff switch is a combination of a high-pressure and a low-pressure cutoff switch. The dual function switch prevents compressor operation at both high and low pressures. It may also control fan operation on some systems. The high/low pressure cutoff switch: -Is commonly located on the high-side or low-side line -Controls the compressor operation to prevent compressor and system damage from excessively high or low pressures -Disengages the clutch at specific minimum and maximum pressures that are set from the factory for that system. The vehicle maker specifications should be checked for exact measurements -Switches and Sensors Damage Analysis – the pressure switches and sensors on the high and low side of the A/C system: -Should be inspected for physical damage to the switch, sensors, connectors, or wires -May require evacuating the refrigerant in the system before replacement -Have replaceable O-rings -Should be inspected for physical damage to the switch or wire damage that could cause the system to operate incorrectly or be damaged -Lines and Hoses Damage Analysis – when inspecting lines and hoses: -Kinks in aluminum or rubber lines may affect system performance -Any damaged lines should not be repaired, they should be replaced. Typically, A/C service ports are located on the high- and low-side pressure lines -Fans – airflow moving through the condenser is required to remove heat from refrigerant and to allow for A/C system peak performance. The engine and radiator cooling fans are typically the primary airflow source when stationary or moving at low speeds. Additional airflow may be created with fans located in front of the condenser pushing air through it. Electric fans are most common and typically controlled by the PCM as needed. When fans are positioned in front of the condenser, the fans push air through the condenser. Pushing fans may be used when sufficient airflow cannot be obtained with air deflectors or airflow through the grille. When fans are positioned behind the condenser, the fans pull the air through. Fans in an A/C system: -Can be mechanical or electric -Are used to direct airflow through the condenser. This assists the heat transfer in the condenser -Provide airflow. Airflow is needed for the condenser to remove the heat absorberd from the passenger compartment from the refrigerant and release it into the atmosphere -May be single or dual -Function differently depending how they are positioned around the condenser -Directing Airflow – aerodynamic vehicle designs have increased the need for sufficient airflow through the condenser. Sufficient airflow is needed for the condenser to release the heat removed from the passenger compartment. It is important to repair and/or replace all air ducting and seals to maintain sufficient airflow if they are damaged or removed. Reduction in grille size has increased the need for assisting the fan with: -Foam seals -Air dams -Shrouds -Note: Active grille shutters may also affect A/C system performance if they are not opening at the appropriate times -In-Line Filters – in-line filters are not typically installed by the vehicle maker and may indicate service work has been performed. They are designed to trap small particles that may be flowing through the system and prevent particle build-up in the orifice tube and other parts. They may contain an orifice tube and additional filters and are usually found on the high pressure side of the system. In-line filters are located in the high-side, liquid line. The refrigerant is filtered through and contaminants are trapped. These filters may be installed after a part failure, such as a compressor, to trap and contaminants that may be remaining in the system Receiver/Drier A/C Systems Operation and Damage Analysis -Receiver/Drier A/C System Parts Function -Parts that are found on a receiver/drier-based system include: -A receiver/drier -A variable metering valve -Standard A/C system parts include: -The compressor -The evaporator -The condenser -Receiver/Drier – a receiver/drier works the same way as an accumulator, the difference being their locations. Refrigerant enters and exits as a high-pressure liquid. Receiver/driers: -Are located on the high-side (liquid) line after the condenser -Are found on systems with variable metering. Variable metering includes expansion valves and H-valve systems -Utilize desiccant material to remove moisture from the system. Moisture can damage the system and cause insufficient cooling -May store extra refrigerant like accumulators -May have a sight glass on top -Note: The receiver dryer may be separate or built into the condenser -Metering Valves: Expansion Valve and H-Valve – a metering valve changes the high-pressure liquid into low-pressure mist. Two types of metering valves include expansion valves and H-valves. The two types are used on different systems but serve the same purpose -Additional Parts – the accumulator and receiver/drier systems utilize many similar parts, which typically include: -A clutch -A high-pressure relief valve -A high/low-pressure cutoff -A PCM -An A/C relay -A fan relay -Relays – relays are used in the A/C system to control the compressor clutch and the condenser fan(s). the relays are typically controlled by the PCM. The fan relay is usually energized when the air conditioning is engaged and when engine coolant temperature gets high enough. Relays are commonly located in power distribution boxes under the hood, but may be in a different location. When performing damage analysis, inspect for physical damage as well as electrical function, damaged relays should be replaced. It may be possible to monitor or command relay operation with a scan tool to check clutch or fan operation Refrigerant and Oils -Refrigerant Qualities – refrigerant requires qualities that allow it to be used effectively in an A/C system. Refrigerants must be non-destructive, must not harm system lines and parts, and must not absorb contaminants. Refrigerants must absorb and release large amounts of heat easily. Heat transfer is a major consideration in the efficiency of A/C systems. A refrigerant must be able to be easily controlled by pressure. A good temperature and pressure relationship is used in an A/C system for evaporation and condensation. -Types of Refrigerant – there are different types of refrigerants used in vehicles. Refrigerant R-134a has been used in automotive A/C systems since the mid-1990s. some vehicle makers are using R-1234yf refrigerant instead of R-134a in some models. Vehicles manufactured before 1994 contain R-12 refrigerant. It is important to use the correct service equipment for each refrigerant to recover and recharge the system -R-134a Refrigerant – properties of R-134a include: -It is a htdrofluorocarbon (HFC) -It is colorless. A leak cannot be detected through visual observation of a color change -It is odorless. A leak cannot be detected through an odor expelled from the system -Its state is easily controlled by pressure -Note: The type of refrigerant will be clearly labeled under the hood of the vehicle. Labels may be found on A/C system parts or on painted surfaces under the hood -R-1234yf Refrigerant – the development and use of R-1234yf refrigerant became necessary in order to comply with environmental impact standards. R-1234yf: -Requires specifically designed service equipment -Evaporator leak testing should be done when charging the system -Systems cannot be retrofitted from R-134a. the system must be installed by the vehicle maker when the vehicle is built -Is used in A/C systems similar in design to those using R-134a. it should not be used in R-134a systems, as these refrigerants are not compatible. -Note: Several vehicle makers are installing R-1234yf in certain vehicle models, but they may not be using it in all models, so it is important to verify the specific refrigerant type on each vehicle. R1234yf is flammable, however it has been determined to be safe for automotive use. -R-12 Refrigerant – R-12 refrigerant is not environmentally friendly and was phased out of use many years ago. If you are working on a classic vehicle with R-12, it may be difficult to source and the vehicle owner may want to consider having the system retrofitted to a different refrigerant. -R-134a Refrigerant Oil – polyalkylene glycol (PAG) is used in R-134a systems that do not have an electric compressor. The oil mixes with the refrigerant to lubricate the compressor. Because PAG oil absorbs moisture, an open system must be protected from the air to prevent corrosion and additional system problems. PAG oil capacities may be found: -Listed under the hood -In service manuals -On a list provided by the Mobile Air Conditioning Society -Note: As is the case with any type of A/C system, using the correct oil level is important. Overcharging a system with oil can reduce system efficiency, and the excess oil can cause accelerated compressor wear or failure. -Used PAG Oil – used refrigerant oil is considered a hazardous waste and should be handled accordingly. Do not mix refrigerant oils with motor oil waste. Adding refrigerant oil to engine waste oil may result in fines. -R-134a Lubricant Cautions – use caution when handling R-134a lubricant. R-134a lubricant can: -Damage vehicle finishes -Damage plastic parts -Damage engine drive belts and coolant hoses -Note: If a spill occurs, lush the area with water -Electric Compressor Oils – polyester (POE) oil is used in electric compressors. Vehicle makers may require a specific viscosity of oil be used that is compatible with the refrigerant and pump type. Introducing the wrong type of oil in an electric compressor system can cause a high-voltage system fault and may require replacement of all system parts. -R-1234yf Refrigerant Oil – the refrigerant oil and refrigerant travel through the system together and return to the compressor to lubricate it and prevent excessive friction and compressor failure. R-1234yf systems use the same PAG oil as R-134a systems. PAG oil absorbs moisture. This makes it important to prevent moisture from entering the system to prevent corrosion and additional system problems. As is true with all refrigerant types, adherence to R-1234yf system refrigerant oil capacities is important for proper system operation. Aluminum Panel Repair (1/2) Exterior Aluminum Panel Overview Aluminum Panel Characteristics -Aluminum use on a Vehicle – the use of aluminum in vehicle construction continues to increase with each model year. Some vehicles are constructed almost entirely out of aluminum, while others may only have certain parts and panels made of aluminum, such as the hood, fenders, doors, and trunk lid. Aluminum is also commonly used in mechanical parts. When aluminum is used for exterior panels, the benefit is less weight with strength similar to steel. Typically, aluminum panels will be thicker than a steel panel and still weigh less. Aluminum panels may be used in conjunction with aluminum, magnesium, steel, or composite materials -Alloying – alloying is the process of mixing two or more metallic elements with aluminum, such as magnesium, silicone, copper, tin, zinc, and manganese. Alloying is done to change the properties and strength of aluminum. There are two categories of aluminum alloys. One is heat-treatable.
