These alloys are heated in a controlled environment at some stage during part manufacture to strengthen the aluminum and create the temper, designated by the letter T, such as 6061-T0 (soft) and 6061-T6 (rigid). The softer heat-treatable alloys can be scratched more easily than the harder alloys. Some of the harder alloys may be comparable to steel in dent and scratch resistance. The other type of aluminum alloy is non-heat-treatable alloy, which are alloys that do not benefit from heating during manufacture and only gain strength through the stamping and forming process. These alloys are designated by the letter H, such as 5052-H0 (soft) and 5052-H32 (rigid). During repairs, heating can assist with repair efforts on both categories of aluminum, but should only be done following vehicle maker service specifications -Work Hardening Characteristics – work hardening of aluminum occurs during the forming process, collision damage, and straightening. Work hardening will cause an increase in strength at the location of bends and working of a panel. this can be beneficial to the design of a panel during manufacture, but may be detrimental to repairs due to the propensity of aluminum to crack if over work-hardened. Aluminum has less memory than steel, and if deformed, will want to stay in its new shape. Aluminum will resist efforts to return to its original shape. While aluminum repairs use many similar straightening techniques as steel, it is necessary to understand the differences in the application of these techniques and to use the correct tools specific to aluminum repair in order to be successful. Proper techniques and car must be taken in order to reduce the chance the part will crack Repair Considerations -Repair Considerations – considerations when analyzing damage include the: -Finish condition -Backside access -Damage in highly formed areas -Severity of damage -Note: The decision to repair versus replace a panel may be based on the vehicle maker’s recommendations. This information may be found in the body repair manual. -Finish – the condition of the vehicle finish will be one of the first considerations in determining the repair method. This includes the age, mil thickness, and damage. The film thickness is checked using a mil thickness gauge set for nonferrous. If the finish is cracked or broken, paintless dent repair (PDR) will not be an option -Backside Access – some dent removal options, when there is limited or no access to the back of the panel, may include using heat, glue-on pulling tabs, or dedicated aluminum weld-on dent removal equipment (weld-on studs). Use caution to avoid flammable materials behind the panel. when there is access to both sides, dent removal tools may include picks (PDR or traditional), hammer and dolly, spoons, slappers, and other metal working tools. Note: Always use caution when using heat or weld-on dent removal equipment on exterior panels. There may be flammable materials behind some panels. -Damage in Highly Formed Areas – damage to panel edges, body features, and contour lines should be considered when making repair or replace decisions. These areas are typically repaired first. Also consider the surrounding damage using the rule, last in, first out. There are some exceptions to this rule such as the edges of the panels and body feature lines. Damage in highly formed areas is prone to cracking during repairs -Damage Analysis for Cracks – damaged and repaired areas should be inspected for cracks. A visual inspection may or may not reveal a crack in the panel; therefore, a dye penetrant kit can be used. The kit consists of cleaner, dye, and developer. The cleaner prepares the surface for the dye penetrant. The dye is designed to penetrate and then stay in the damaged are after it is wiped off. The dye must be removed before any welding operations because the dye will contaminate the welding area. To remove dye penetrant and developer, a solvent, such as the cleaner or a wax and grease remover, may be used. The developer makes the defects visible. Aluminum Panel Repair (2/2) Exterior Aluminum Panel Repair Galvanic Corrosion Protection -Tools and Abrasives – corrosion results when different metals contact each other in the presence of oxygen and an electrolyte. An example of an electrolyte is water that is not pure. Galvanic corrosion can happen when aluminum and steel contact each other. During the repair process, you should make every effort to avoid cross-contamination so there are no future galvanic corrosion issues due to collision damage or the repair process. Cross-contamination can be prevented by having tools and abrasives that are dedicated for use on steel and tools and abrasives that are dedicated for use on aluminum -Hand Tool Considerations – hammers or dollies used for repairing aluminum should not have nicks or gouges. Aluminum is softer than steel and may be damaged by the sharp edges of the tool. Some options for tools used on aluminum include: -Polished, steel-faced, with edges rounded off -Aluminum-faced -Plastic-faced/rubber -Wood with leather face -Pouches of lead shot -Picks -Note: Picks can be used to lift low areas when there is limited access to the panel backside. Picks should have polished, rounded tips so that they do not damage the panel. a plastic cover may also be placed over the tip to prevent scratches and damage to the inner panel. shrinking or serrated-faced hammers and dollies should not be used because they may damage the aluminum -Power Tool Considerations – as a best practice, power tools should be dedicated for steel or aluminum. If it is necessary to use the same power tool for steel and aluminum, you should: -Clean the housing with an appropriate cleaner and a clean cloth -Separate the blades, bits, or abrasives used on aluminum from steel accessories -Aluminum and Steel Applications – there are numerous applications where steel and aluminum are intersecting, connecting, or touching in some ways. Protecting parts is critical to keeping galvanic corrosion from forming. Some galvanic corrosion prevention methods include: -Sealing the area to keep out any electrolytes, such as water or moisture -Using coated fasteners -Separating the two metals with a non-conductive insulator, such as adhesive or non-conductive spacers -Aluminum Part Assembles – some vehicles have vehicle maker-installed threaded inserts, or if aluminum threads are damaged, a replacement stainless steel thread may be available. A magnet may be used to identify aluminum versus steel parts. Service information is also a good resource. Some vehicles use both steel and aluminum parts to make an assembly. When dissimilar types of materials are joined together, proper coating and insulation (with paint or adhesives) must be considered. -Vehicle Maker Recommendations – vehicle makes may have specific recommendations to help prevent galvanic corrosion. For example, non-conductive urethane may be recommended by the vehicle maker for bonding stationary glass to an aluminum pinch weld. Damage resulting from corrosion due to the use of improper fasteners, rubber or plastic parts, or adhesives may not be covered under the warranty. -Aluminum Dust Particles and Cautions- grind or sand at slow speeds using light pressure to avoid damage or thinning the part. Doing this will help to reduce he chance of: -overheating the aluminum -Embedding contaminants in the aluminum, which could lead to corrosion -Clogging the abrasive with aluminum. Some sanding and grinding discs are designed to be used only on aluminum. These types of discs are typically identified by the letters “ALU” on the label or the backside of the disc. -Aluminum dust particles are nontoxic, but they are lightweight and float in the air, and are lung and eye irritants. The dust particles are collected using a dust extraction system and a sparkless vacuum. Dedicated aluminum work areas and curtain walls can help reduce cross-contaminant issues. Do not blow the dust using compressed air, as doing this will spread the aluminum dust around the work facility and cause contamination issues where it lands. -Note: high concentrations of fine aluminum dust can present a fire and explosion hazard Aluminum Panel Straightening -Paintless Dent Repair (PDC) Glue-On Pull Tabs – when choosing repair methods, it is recommended to use the least invasive method necessary to remove the damage. Consider using glue-on pull tbs if the finish is in good condition. Glue-on pull tabs are commonly used when there is no access to the panel backside, but can be used even if there is backside access. When removing damage, it is common to combine different techniques depending on access. Glue-on pull tabs require the panel surface to be cleaned. Special hot-melt glue is used to adhere pulling attachments to the panel. a hand-pulling tool, pulling bridge, or slide hammer is used to pull on the attachment. Stress-relieving is done around the area. A cleaner is used to remove adhesive residue -Removing Dents with Heat- heat can be effective to remove dents. Always refer to the vehicle maker’s recommendations when working with heat. Heating in a circular motion around the dent and slowly moving the heat source toward the center of the dent can remove or greatly reduce the size of the dent. Thermal expansion will cause the metal to expand and relieve internal stresses, this causes the metal to rise and push toward the center. As the aluminum cools, it contracts, causing the metal to return closer to its original shape. Heat must be monitored to avoid reaching an annealing temperature. Once annealed, the aluminum is permanently softened. Corrosion protection is a consideration in affected areas -Heat for Repairs- when repairing aluminum, heat will help soften the metal slightly to aid in straightening efforts and reduce the chance the panel will crack. When heating aluminum, consider the following points: aluminum does not change color when heated to its melting point. The approximate melting point of aluminum is 1200 degrees Fahrenheit (649 degrees Celsius). An aluminum part can be melted easily with some heat sources, such as an induction heater and open flame sources. Aluminum naturally forms an oxide coating when exposed to air; this oxide melts at about 3,700 degrees Fahrenheit (2,038 degrees Celsius). Heat spreads faster in aluminum than steel. Temperature is more critical than time. If the repair heating temperature is exceeded, the panel may become permanently softened, also referred to as annealing. 425 degrees Fahrenheit (218 degrees Celsius) is a typical repair heat recommendation. Heat as low as 250 degrees Fahrenheit (121 degrees Celsius) will damage some adhesive and require removal of the affected panels. Avoid heating joints that will remain in service. When heating aluminum, always follow the vehicle maker heating recommendations Degree chart -3,500 degrees Fahrenheit (1,927 degrees Celsius) – Melting point of Aluminum Oxide -2,700 degrees Fahrenheit (1,482 degrees Celsius) – Melting point of Steel -1,200 degrees Fahrenheit (649 degrees Celsius) – Aluminum melts -755 degrees Fahrenheit (402 degrees Celsius) – Annealing – heat-Treatable Alloys -650 degrees Fahrenheit (343 degrees Celsius) – Annealing – Nonheat-Treatable Alloys -400 to 570 degrees Fahrenheit (204 to 299 degrees Celsius) – Repair Window according to the Aluminum Association -350 degrees Fahrenheit (177 degrees Celsius) – Paint Bake Temperature -68 degrees Fahrenheit (20 degrees Celsius) – Room Temperature -Hammer and Dolly Techniques – when repairing aluminum, every effort should be made to avoid overworking the metal to minimize additional work hardening, and reduce the chance the panel will crack -Hammer and Dolly – when using a hammer and dolly, the hammer-off-dolly technique is the preferred method of repairing damage to aluminum. The dolly is positioned to raise low areas on the backside of the panel, while the hammer or slapper is used on the front to tap down high areas. The hammer strikes the panel slightly to one side of where the dolly is help. The positioning of the dolly in relation to the hammer makes this effective because the high areas are being lowered and the low areas are being raised at the same time. The hammer-on-dolly technique should not be the primary technique for aluminum. This technique can be used to remove pick marks and small high spots, and to reshape localized damage, but is not the preferred method on aluminum because it can be easily thin and stretch the panel -Spoon/Slapper – when removing damage with a hammer and dolly, a spoon/slapper may also be used. A spoon generally has a larger striking surface than a hammer, and may be used to work larger damage or high crown lines around dents -Pry Picks- pry picks may be used in conjunction with a hammer and dolly to methodically remove the damage. Picks should have rounded ends to minimize panel damage. If using pry picks, gently work the panel and avoid raising high points in the panel. the use of a metal file in conjunction with a pick and hammy/dolly should be done cautiously to avoid thinning the panel -Weld-On Dent Removal- there are weld-on dent removal tools designed specifically for the removal of dents in aluminum panels. This equipment welds a stud onto the panel that can be used to pull damage out of the panel. -Weld-On Dent Removal Tools- before using this equipment on a vehicle, the battery should be disconnected and the cables isolated to protect the electronic systems on the vehicle. Before attaching a weld-on stud, make sure there is no chance of fire from foam or other material on or touching the backside of the panel. once the stud is welded on, a pulling attachment is then installed on the stud. The attachment is used for pulling out the low areas. A variety of pulling devices are available including hand pulling tools, slide hammers, and bridge/gantry devices to pull on multiple studs at the same time. Weld-on dent removal tools can be used when there is limited access to the backside of a panel. aluminum weld-on dent equipment operates on very short, high voltage spikes to weld the studs, so the burning of backside coatings is usually minimal. After repairs are complete, corrosion protection on the panel backside should be addressed. -Removal Sequence – before working on a damage part, test the equipment on a similar aluminum sheet to determine the power settings. This will reduce the chance of burning through the panel. it will also ensure that there is enough heat to attach the pulling stud to the panel. if too much power is used, a hole may burn into the panel, requiring part replacement. The sequence for removing a dent is: -1. Identify the area of the dent -2. Remove the finish using P80 grit or finer sandpaper -3. Attach the ground clamp(s) -4. Install the weld-on stud -5. Start on the area with the least amount of damage, and work to the center of damage, while using caution not to over-pull the damage, creating a stretched condition. -When identifying a dent, sanding block, file, or DA with guide coat may be used. Once the high or low spots are identified, the repair plan can be revised and the damage removed. Not all damage is the same. Analyze the damage and determine the appropriate repair plan Making a Hem Flange -Working with Hem Flanges on Aluminum Panels – hem flanges on aluminum exterior panels may include: -hoods -Rear closure panels -Door skins -The quarter panel wheelhouse flange -Removal – when removing a panel that was bonded using adhesive along a hem flange area, grind the outer edge of the panel with P50 grit. Separate the panels by applying heat to soften the adhesive. A panel separation tool may be inserted along the ground edge to separate the panels. When separating panels, use care to not damage the mating flanges. Special tool bits for an air hammer are available to open the hem flange for panel removal. In some situations, there may be rivets or other attachments that require removal -Preparation – straighten and prepare the flange on the inner panel to ensure the new panel fits properly and a uniform bond line of adhesive is applied. Steps may include: -Removing the remaining adhesive and other coatings -Cleaning the bonding area -Applying primers if required by the vehicle or adhesive maker -Note: the replacement panel may require removing E-coat in the areas adhesive will be applied, or the E-coat may only require cleaning and/or scuffing. Keep in mind some panel installations may be to a different type of metal, so precautions should be followed to avoid cross-contamination and possible galvanic corrosion issues -Panel installation – when installing an aluminum panel with a hem flange: -Apply adhesive to the panel where the hem flange will be made. Adhesives should be applied to both sides of the flange to seal out moisture and contaminants -Ensure all bare metal areas are coated -Set the panel in place and slide to adjust the position, do not lift -Form a rope hem flange around the panel perimeter. Use care not to flatten the flange, as this may cause the panel edge to crack -Remove excessive adhesive from the panel while it is wet -Rope Hem Flange and Forming Process – a rope hem flange is a special type of hem used on aluminum panels. A rope hem flange has a larger radius than a standard hem flange and is used on aluminum panels. Heat-treated aluminum does not form easily, so a gradual radius keeps the hemmed edge from cracking while being formed. Some replacement parts will require a hem. Parts such as door panels and quarter panels will have their flanges pre-bent to 90 degrees. After the panel has been properly fit-up pliers, hammer and dolly, or a special hem flange forming tool may be used to begin bending the flange, creating a larger radius. A hammer and dolly are typically used to complete the rope hem flange. The dolly is placed on the edge and face of the replacement panel while the flange is being hammered down. Work the hem flange in increments, do not attempt to completely hem all at once. Hammering is kept away from the bend area to avoid flattening it. Working too close to the bend area may cause the aluminum to crack Welding and Surface Prep -Welding a Tear – if a panel has a tear or crack from collision damage or due to repairs, welding may be an option. Considerations for welding a crack or tear include vehicle maker recommendations. Check the vehicle maker’s service information to identify if panel replacement is required in the event of a crack or tear. The location of the damage may preclude welding due to adhesives or lack of backside access to properly clean the panel. the extent of the damage is a factor. Welding a crack or tear is similar to welding an open butt joint and requires a high degree of skill to complete successfully on an aluminum panel. welding is typically done using a GMA welder with pulse and synergic capabilities. Tungsten inert gas (TIG) welding may be an option, but part removal from the vehicle is required to protect the vehicles electronic systems from damage -Welding Aluminum – when welding aluminum, it is recommended to use a machine that operates on 208-230 volt power (or higher) and is either single-or three-phase. Pulse spray-arc transfer with synergic controls is also recommended. The most common shielding gas is 100 percent argon. Use the plush technique when welding aluminum. Aluminum welds are easily contaminated, so it is important that both sides of the panel are completely clean down to the bare metal surrounding the weld area. Welding cracks or tears across panel edges may not be possible due to the lack of access to clean the backside and the possible use of adhesive by the vehicle maker -Electrode Wire Considerations – to ensure a durable repair, it is important to select the correct electrode wire. Most vehicle makers will specify the electrode wire alloy and diameter that should be used on a particular application. ER5356, ER5554, ER4047, and ER4043 electrode wires are commonly used -Surface Preparation – after straightening work and welding, the panel should be finished and prepared for paint. prepare the surface by: -1. Sanding the surface to the recommended grit that is identified by the vehicle or product makers, as they may have specific grit requirements for body filler or refinishing -2. Remove dust using an explosion-proof (sparkless) vacuum. A shop vacuum is not an explosion-proof vacuum. Do not use compressed air for removing dust from aluminum panels. -Note: Body filler should be applied as soon as possible after sanding to limit oxidation. Before applying the body filler, determine if the vehicle or product maker requires an epoxy primer between the bare metal and the body filler. -Body filler – when selecting a body filler, verify that it is designed for application to an aluminum panel. the vehicle may specify a particular product to use. Polyester body fillers typically consist of polyester resin, talc, and a catalyst. Talc can absorber moisture, which may reach the panel it is applied to. Some body fillers are made with aluminum powder. The panel should be sanded with P80 grit in preparation for body filler application. Ensure all oxide has been removed and apply body filler immediately. When cured, sand to shape, finishing in P180 grit or finer as required by your paint maker. If bare metal is exposed during the sanding process, a direct-to-metal primer will be required before applying any additional body filler. When working on aluminum panels, P80 grit or finer should be used to reduce thinning of the panel. an exception may be where specific weld dressing or hem flange removal requires a coarser grit. Note: Some vehicle makers require the use of an aluminum-based body filler for use on their aluminum vehicles -Primer – when applying primer, prepare the surface by: -1. Cleaning and preparing according to the product maker’s recommendations -2. Sanding with the recommended grit sandpaper and featheredging the repair area -3. Masking the vehicle to prevent overspray -4. Applying primer following the product maker’s recommendations. -5. Sanding the cured primer following the product marker’s recommendations -Note: after the primer has been applied, the refinishing process is similar to refinishing steel parts Aluminum Vehicle Overview (1/3) Module 1 – Aluminum Overview -Overview – modern vehicles are built to meet certain fuel economy requirements, emission regulations, and safety standards, while providing the performance, comfort, and features consumers want. To meet this, many vehicle makers use aluminum in the outer body panels and structure. Repairing damaged aluminum requires an understanding of: -Various forms -Attachment methods -Limits of repairability -Work characteristics -Other considerations -Three Manufacturing Methods- there are three manufacturing methods commonly used in vehicle body construction. Depending on vehicle maker requirements, aluminum parts may be made from: -Extrusions – these parts are generally used as structural parts -Castings – these parts are almost always used as attachment points for structural parts -Stampings – these parts may be structural or cosmetic -Note: forging is another method used to make parts, but this is less commonly used on body part construction -Developing a Repair Plan – when developing a repair plan, reference the vehicle maker service information to determine if the vehicle parts and structure can be straightened, or if replacement is the only option. Additionally, it is important to identify the required repair attachment method, which may differ from the original factory method. Sectioning may be allowed on some parts, while others require replacement at factory seams. Additional actions to take when developing a repair plan include: -Verifying parts and fasteners are correct -Identifying any special tools required -Checking heating recommendations for removing bonded parts and straightening aluminum parts -Note: A vehicle maker may specify a certain adhesive, body filler, and primer to be used on their aluminum vehicles and have specific refinish requirements -Aluminum Stampings and Locations – -A stamping is created from a rolled sheet of aluminum that passes through a die. Stamped parts will generally be identified by: -Relatively uniform thickness -Flanges for attachment -Stretch marks -Areas that seem to have gathered from the forming process -An aluminum stamping may be used for exterior panels and other body parts such as: -Fenders -Hoods -Deck lids -Outer body side panels -Rails -Pillars -Stamping Repair Considerations – cold straightening is the process of straightening bent metal without using heat; for example, removing damage from a panel with a hammer and dolly or pulling a bent rail. Generally, very minor damage can be repaired without heat depending on the thickness of the part and the extent of the damage. Straightening a thick part without heat will often cause the part to crack. Heat may be applied to assist moving the molecules to help reduce the chance the part will crack. Follow vehicle maker temperature recommendations -Extrusions – extrusions are made by pressing heated, solid aluminum billets through heated dies. Sometimes, if the part must be straight, an extrusion is pulled as it exits the die. A simply example of extruding is pushing adhesive out of a tube or cartridge. The adhesive tip serves as the die. Unique characteristics of extruded parts include no pinch weld flange. However, there may be an attachment flange on the part. This looks like a pinch weld and is designed to have parts riveted or welded to it -Extrusion Identification – extrusions can be complex designs and shapes with seams that are not visible and may have a variable thickness cross section. Extrusions are made from a continuous form and can be either straight or curved. A rocker panel reinforcement is an example of a straight extrusion. If an extrusion is curved, this is typically done by hydroforming the extrusion. Extrusion can be either single-cell or multi-cell. A single-cell extrusion could be a piece of tubing. A multi-cell extrusion will have webbing inside the main extrusion, and the internal webbing design may be a variety of different shapes -Extruded Structural Applications – extruded parts work well for structural applications, and some vehicles are built primarily from extrusions. Examples of extruded structural parts may include: -Rocker panels -Roof rails -Pillars -Frame rails -Tie bars -Extrusion Repair Considerations – when considering what repairs may be possible for an extrusion, keep in mind extrusions may crack when damaged or during the repair process. This occurs because of the work hardening of the aluminum. Many extrusions have internal webbing that strengthens the part. When straightening is attempted, the extra support from the webbing may cause the part to crack -Limited Repair – repair options may be limited due to the design of an extrusion. Some vehicle makers may not allow any straightening to their aluminum structures. If straightening is allowed, cold straightening may cause the part to crack. Heat should only be used if allowed by the vehicle maker. Do not exceed the vehicle maker’s heating recommendations. As a general rule, temperatures should not exceed 450 degrees Fahrenheit (230 degrees Celsius). Keep in mind that heat spreads quickly in aluminum and can cause adhesives in nearby joints to fail -Sectioning or Partial Replacement – damaged extrusions may require replacement at factory seams. There may be procedures for sectioning, partial replacement t factory seams, or replacement of only the damaged cell. Procedures may use welded joints, bonded joints, or rivet-bonded joints -Aluminum Castings – aluminum castings are created by pouring or injecting molten aluminum into a mold. The aluminum is allowed to cool and harden, and then the cast part is removed from the mold. Castings can be made into nearly any shape or thickness, with variations in cross section and wall thickness. Castings are typically machined to a smooth surface at the attachment areas. Parts are generally attached to other parts with gas metal arc (GMA) fillet welds, bolts, rivet bonding, or a combination of methods. -Use of Castings – -Due to their light weight and strength, aluminum castings are used on some vehicles for: -Wheels -Rear-end housings -Transmission cases -Engine blocks -Steering and suspension parts -They may also be used in door frames and for inner structures. Cast aluminum parts may be used for joints where multiple parts come together, as well as: -Rail sections -Strut towers -Pillars -Suspension mounting locations -Casting Identification – one identifying characteristic of an aluminum casting is a rough, textured surface. Sine addition indicators include parts being solid, irregularly shaped, and a machined surface with tapped holes -Casting Repair Considerations – castings are usually replaced if damaged. When replacing a casting, it may be reattached using adhesive, bolts, or rivets. GMA welds, or a combination of these. If threads in a casting are damaged, the vehicle maker may specify to re-tap, or use a thread repair insert. When installing a thread repair insert, it is required to use a stainless steel insert. If a carbon steel insert is installed, corrosion may occur -Vehicle Design – aluminum-intensive vehicles may use a space frame or unibody design. They may also have a hybrid structure that combines steel and aluminum -Galvanic Corrosion – galvanic corrosion occurs when dissimilar metals come in contact with each other in the presence of an electrolyte. Some ways to avoid galvanic corrosion include applying coatings to dissimilar materials that contact each other or using coated fasteners. Corrosion can come from using conductive materials like plastic or rubber plugs, hoses, and body grommets that contain carbon. Carbon is an electrically conductive material. It becomes electrically active with aluminum and causes corrosion between the aluminum and the part containing carbon. When installing stationary glass, a non-conductive urethane adhesive may be recommended since it does not contain carbon Aluminum Vehicle Overview (2/3) Module 2 – Aluminum Repair Considerations -Working Safely with Aluminum – aluminum dust is explosive in the correct concentration and can be more volatile if mixed with iron particles and other substances. Aluminum dust is a non-hazardous irritant to the eyes, nose, throat, and lungs. Personal protective equipment should be used. It is important to have adequate ventilation to remove the aluminum particles from the air in the work area. A dedicated vacuum may be used to collect aluminum dust. It must be an explosion-proof and sparkless motor design. Using a regular shop vacuum may present an explosion risk -Separate Work Area – vehicle makers typically require aluminum repair work to be done in a designed aluminum repair area. They may require their vehicles be repaired in an area that is clean and isolated from steel parts and vehicles. This reduces cross-contamination and the possibility of galvanic corrosion -Dedicated Tools – it is recommended to use separate tools when working on aluminum. This reduces the chances of contaminating the aluminum with steel particles. If dedicated tools are not used, extra care must be taken to thoroughly clean tools to remove and aluminum or steel debris -Compressed Air – do not use compressed air to remove or move dust that may contain aluminum particles. Aluminum dust remains suspended in the air longer than steel and will settle throughout the repair facility -Cutting Tools and Abrasives – dedicated cutting tools and abrasives for aluminum must also be used. Using the same saw blade for steel and aluminum parts is a near certain method of cross-contamination. Some abrasive materials, like cutting and grinding discs, are designed for aluminum only. The grinding discs designated for aluminum, usually marked ALU, do not clog like grinding discs that are made for steel -Workability of Aluminum – working with aluminum is different from working with mild steel. For instance, aluminum has less memory than a mild steel part. Once deformed, aluminum cannot be easily returned to its previous shape. Keep in mind thicker material is more susceptible to cracking when straightening. Heat may be an option for straightening aluminum as long as the parts are not heated above 450 degrees Fahrenheit (230 degrees Celsius) or vehicle maker limits. It is also important to know the workability of aluminum varies with alloys. Some alloys are easier to repair than others -Methods of Applying Heat – when applying heat to aluminum, there are multiple methods that can be used. One method is using a propane, map gas, or oxyacetylene torch. Exercise caution when using an open flame to avoid excess heat input. An induction heater may be used, but heating times will typically be longer compared to steel because aluminum dissipates heat much quicker than steel. Additionally the nonferrous properties of aluminum may reduce the effectiveness of some induction heaters. Another method is using a heat gun. A heat gun may be a better tool for applying heat to aluminum because of the lower heat output and better control. Note: Temperature-controlled heat guns can help manage heat applications better -Heating Considerations – when heating aluminum, it does not change color before reaching its melting point. Aluminum has a melting point of approximately 1,220 degrees Fahrenheit (660 degrees Celsius) and a very high rate of heat transfer. Compared to steel, aluminum will transfer. Compared to steel, aluminum will transfer heat across the panel about three times faster. Aluminum also has a much greater rate of thermal expansion compared to steel. Bare aluminum forms an oxide layer when exposed to air. The oxide layer melts at a much higher temperature than the aluminum. Heat should only be used if allowed by the vehicle maker -Temperature Limits – when considering the use of heat, verify vehicle maker temperature limits. Heat indicators such as crayons, paint, noncontact thermometers, and adhesive thermometers can be used to ensure the heating window is not exceeded. Compared to steel, aluminum does not have cumulative heating times that need to be monitored. The spread of heat may compromise the adhesives in nearby joints and areas. This requires the removal of affected parts and new adhesive applied. This may also require some parts replacement -Annealing – if aluminum is heated above a certain temperature, the area being heated will become annealed. When a part becomes annealed, it is permanently softened and cannot be returned to its original hardness. The specific annealing temperature varies by aluminum alloy and may be as low as 650 degrees Fahrenheit (343 degrees Celsius). This is why the repair temperature window is typically limited to 450 degrees Fahrenheit (232 degrees Celsius). Vehicle makers may have different limits. -Emissivity – if a noncontact thermometer is being used, it may not provide accurate temperature readings. This is because of the reflectiveness of bare aluminum. The emissivity, which is the temperature radiated from the reflective surface, causes an inaccurate temperature reading. When a noncontact thermometer is used to measure temperature, reflective parts cause the sensor to read inaccurately. To compensate for this, measure the temperature in a nearby painted area. A dull, high-temperature paint can also be applied to the part so the measured temperature is more accurate -Anchoring an Aluminum Vehicle – when conducting structural repairs, the vehicle requires measuring and anchoring. Structural straightening and stress relieving should only be done if allowed by the vehicle maker. During anchoring, the vehicle maker may specify universal fixture or dedicated fixture or may allow bench-style frame machines with specialized adapters and additional anchoring equipment -Straightening Considerations – there are considerations that must be made when straightening a vehicle part. For instance, when straightening aluminum, it is common practice to pull on parts that are non-repairable and going to be replaced. Using undamaged parts for pulling will likely cause additional damage -What May Be Repairable – when aluminum is bent or kinked, returning it to its undamaged position is not easily done, and the part may crack or tear. Kink versus bend does not apply with aluminum like it does with steels. Straightening aluminum that is kinked or that has sharp bends may crack when straightened -Dye Penetrant – after straightening, dye penetrant should be used to inspect for cracks in the part. Weld beads can also be checked for defects. Dye penetrant can be a valuable tool for locating damage that may not be visible otherwise -Crack Repair – check the vehicle maker service information or position statement before repairing a cracked part. Vehicle makers typically do not allow repairs of cracked structural parts. This requires partial, if allowed, or full part replacement. Cast parts that are cracked should be replaced. Some vehicle makers allow cracked welds to be ground out and re-welded, but there may be length limits -Straightening Aluminum – aluminum does not easily return to its original shape. Due to spring-back, a part may need to be overpulled slightly and stress-relieved to realign the structure. This can be done with a hammer and wooden blocks or wooden slappers. Heating during this process reduces the chances of cracking. Monitor for progress and damage at the location of the pull and other areas on the vehicle where damage could occur from pulling forces. Monitor anchor locations to ensure no damage is incurred. Additional anchoring may be required depending on how the structure is responding. Collision damage and repair operations may compromise rivet-bonded joints -Adhesive Bond Removal – when removing aluminum parts, adhesive may have to be removed. Adhesives can be softened using heat. This can be done using a heat gun or an infrared lamp. Adhesive begins to break down at about 400 degrees Fahrenheit (205 degrees Celsius), some at lower temperatures. Note: Using sharp tools will help reduce heat and clogging of the cutting teeth -Cutting, Drilling, and Grinding – there are some general best practices that can be applied when working with aluminum. When drilling or cutting, lubricant helps prolong the life and minimize clogging of the cutting tool. Lubricants could be a solid bar or a liquid. Solid lubricant is rubbed across the cutting surface. Grind or sand with reduced speed and light pressure. Tools operated at too high of a speed and with too much pressure creates heat and can cause the aluminum to smear. Work with properly maintained tools and cutters that are sharp. Sharp cutting tools reduce heat in the part and fatigue of the operator. Note: Rotary carbide burrs made for steel will clog quickly. Use burrs designed for aluminum to avoid this. Aluminum Vehicle Overview (1/3) Aluminum Overview Aluminum Fabrications -Overview – modern vehicles are built to meet certain fuel economy requirements, emission regulations, and safety standards, while providing the performance, comfort, and features consumers want. To mee this, many vehicle makers use aluminum in the outer body panels and structure. Repairing damaged aluminum requires an understanding of various forms, attachment methods, limits of repairability, work characteristics, as well as other considerations. There are three manufacturing methods commonly used in vehicle body construction. Depending on vehicle maker requirements, aluminum parts may be made from: -Extrusion – these parts are generally used as structural parts -Castings – these parts are almost always used as attachment points for structural parts -Stampings – these parts may be structural or cosmetic -Note: Forging is another method used to make parts, but this is not typically used on body part construction -Developing a Repair Plan – when developing a repair plan, reference the vehicle maker service information to determine if the vehicle parts and structure can be straightened, or if replacement is the only option. Additionally, it is important to identify the required repair attachment method, which may differ from the original factory method. Sectioning may be allowed on some parts, while others require replacement at factory seams. Additional actions to take when developing a repair plan include: -Verifying that the parts and fasteners are correct -Identifying any special tools required – examples include structural repair equipment, riveting tools, and welders -Checking heating recommendations for removing bonded parts and straightening aluminum parts -Note: A vehicle maker may specify certain adhesives, body filler, and primers to be used on their aluminum vehicles and have specific refinish requirements -Aluminum Stampings and Locations – a stamping is created from a rolled sheet of aluminum that passes through a die. Stamped parts will generally be identified by relatively uniform thickness, flanges for attachment, stretch marks, and/or areas that seem to have gathered from the forming process. An aluminum stamping may be used for exterior panels and other body parts including, but not limited to: fenders, hoods, deck lids, outer body side panels, as well as rails, pillars, reinforcements, crossmembers, floors, and other parts of the vehicle body or frame. -Stamping Repair Considerations – cold straightening is the process of straightening bent metal without using heat; for example, removing damage from a panel with a hammer and dolly or pulling a bent rail without heat. Generally, minor damage can be repaired without heat, depending on the thickness of the part and the extent of the damage. Straightening a thick part without heat will often cause the part to crack. Sometimes when cold straightening has been done as much as possible, limited heat may be applied to assist moving the molecules to help reduce the chance the part will crack -Extrusions – extrusions are made by pressing heat, solid aluminum billets through heated dies. Sometimes, if the part must be straight, an extrusion is pulled as it exits the die. A simple example of extruding is pushing adhesive out of a tube or cartridge. The adhesive tip serves as the die. Unique characteristics of extruded parts include no pinch weld flange. However, there may be an attachment flange on the part which looks like a pinch weld designed to have parts riveted or welded to it -Extrusion Identification – extrusions can be complex designs and shapes with seams that are not visible and may have a variable thickness cross section. Extrusions are made from a continuous form and can be either straight or curved. A rocker panel is an example of a straight extrusion. If an extrusion is curved, this is typically done by hydro forming the extrusion. Extrusions can be either single-cell or multi-cell. A single-cell extrusion could be a piece of tubing. A multi-cell extrusion will have webbing inside the main extrusion, and the internal webbing design may be a variety of different shapes -Extruded Structural Applications – extruded parts work well for structural applications, and some vehicles are built primarily from extrusions. Examples of extruded structural parts may include: -Rocker panels -Roof rails -Pillars -Frame rails -Tie bars -Extrusion Repair Considerations – there are a number of things to consider when repairing an extruded part -May Crack When Damaged – when considering what repairs may be possible for an extrusion, keep in mind extrusions may crack when damaged. This occurs because many extrusions have internal webbing that strengthens the part. When straightening is attempted, the extra support from the webbing may cause the part to crack -Limited Repair – because of the design of an extrusion, repair options may be limited. Some vehicle makers may not allow any straightening to their aluminum structures. If straightening is allowed, cold straightening may cause the part to crack. Heat should only be used if allowed by the vehicle maker, do not exceed the vehicle makers heating recommendations. As a general rule, temperatures should not exceed 450 degrees Fahrenheit. Keep in mind that heat spreads quickly in aluminum and can cause adhesives in nearby joints to fail -Sectioning or Partial Replacement – damaged extrusions may require replacement at factory seams. There may be recommendations for sectioning, partial replacement at factory seams, or replacement of only the damaged cell. Procedures may use welded joints or bonded joints, usually with rivets in the joint area -Aluminum Castings – aluminum castings are created by pouring or injecting molten aluminum into a mold, usually made of sand or metal, under pressure. The aluminum is allowed to cool and harden, and then the cast part is removed from the mold. Castings can be made into nearly any shape or thickness variations in cross section and length and are generally machined to a smooth surface or attachment. Additionally, aluminum castings are often made with variable wall thicknesses throughout the part and are generally attached to other parts with gas metal arc or GMA (MIG) fillet welds. They can also be attached to other parts using bolts or rivet bonding -Use of Castings – due to their light weight and strength, aluminum castings are used on some vehicles for wheels, rear-end housings, transmission cases, engine blocks, and steering suspension parts. They may also be used in door frames and for inner structures. Cast aluminum parts may be used for joints where multiple parts come together, as well as rail sections, strut towers, pillars, and suspension mounting locations -Casting Identification – one identifying characteristic of an aluminum casting is a rough, textured surface that is the result of molten metal contacting the mold surface during the casting process. Some additional indicators include parts being sold, parts that have irregular shapes, and machined surfaces on parts that may have tapped holes -Casting Repair Considerations – castings are replaced if damaged. When replacing a casting, it may be reattached using adhesives, bolts, or rivets, or GMA (MIG) welding. If threads in a casting are damaged, the vehicle maker may specify to re-tap, or use a thread repair insert. When installing a thread repair insert, it is required to use a stainless steel insert. If a carbon steel insert is installed, corrosion may occur -Vehicle Design – aluminum-intensive vehicles may use a space frame or unibody design. They may have a hybrid structure, where steel and aluminum are used together to create the vehicle body -Galvanic Corrosion – galvanic corrosion can occur from different sources. Some of these include dissimilar metals coming in contact with each other in the presence of an electrolyte and using conductive materials. Some ways to avoid galvanic corrosion include applying coatings to dissimilar materials that contact each other or using coated fasteners. Another source of corrosion can come from using plastic or rubber plugs, hoses, and body grommets that contain carbon. Carbon is an electrically conductive material that can become electrically active with aluminum and cause corrosion between the aluminum and the part containing carbon. When installing stationary glass, a non-conductive urethane adhesive is recommended since it does not contain carbon Aluminum Vehicle Overview (2/3) Aluminum Repair Considerations Working with Aluminum -Working Safely with Aluminum – when working on or around aluminum vehicles, some special safety considerations should be observed -Dust Ventilation – aluminum dust is explosive in the correct concentration. Aluminum dust is a non-hazardous irritant to the eyes, nose, throat, and lungs. Personal protective equipment should be utilized. It is important to have adequate ventilation to remove the aluminum particles from the air in the work area. If a vacuum is used to clean up aluminum dust, it must be an explosion proof/sparkless motor. Using a general vacuum can cause an explosion risk -Separate Work Area – vehicle makers may require aluminum repair work to be done in a designated repair area or they may require that their vehicles be repaired in an area that is clean and isolated from steel vehicles -Dedicated Tools – some vehicle makers require separate tools for working on aluminum. This is to reduce the chance of contaminating the aluminum with steel particles. If dedicated tools are not being used, extra care must be taken to thoroughly clean tools to remove any aluminum or steel debris, ensuring no contamination will occur -Compressed Air – do not use compressed air to remove or move dust that may contain aluminum particles. Aluminum dust will remain suspended in the air longer than steel and will settle throughout the repair facility -Cutting Tools and Abrasives – dedicated cutting tools and abrasives for aluminum must also be used. Using a saw blade interchangeably between a steel and aluminum part is a near certain method of cross contamination. When cutting aluminum, there are lubricants that can be used to reduce heat in the area being cut, and also increase cutting efficiency. Some abrasive materials, like cutting and grinding discs, are marked for aluminum only. The grinding discs that are designed for aluminum do not clog for grinding discs that are made for steel and used on aluminum. -Work Hardening – work hardening occurs when a metal part is formed or bent. Sheet aluminum is work hardened when it is stamped into a specific shape, causing the molecules in the metal to be strained. Work hardening can happen multiple times to a part: first when the part is made, second during a collision, and third during the repair of the damaged part. A part may crack or tear due to damage or may occur during the straightening process. Parts that crack typically require replacement. Check the vehicle maker service information or position statements before repairing a cracked part -Workability of Aluminum -working with aluminum is different from working with mild steel. For instance, aluminum has less memory than a mild steel part. Once moved, aluminum cannot be easily moved back to its previous shape. Keep in mind that thicker material is more susceptible to crack when straightened. Heat may be an option for straightening aluminum as long as the parts are not heated above 450 degrees Fahrenheit. It is also important to know that the workability of aluminum varies with alloys. Some alloys are easier to repair than others. Heating and Straightening Considerations -Methods of Applying Heat – when applying heat to aluminum, there are multiple methods that can be used. One method is using a propane, map gas, or oxyacetylene torch. An induction heater may be used, but heating times will be longer compared to steel because aluminum dissipates heat much quicker than steel. Another method is using a heat gun. A heat gun may be a better tool to use for applying heat to aluminum because of the lower heat output and better control. Note: Temperature controlled heat guns can help manage heat application better -Heating Considerations – there are a number of things to consider when heating an aluminum part. When heating aluminum, note that it does not change color before reaching its melting point. Aluminum has a melting point of approximately 1,220 degrees Fahrenheit and a very high rate of heat transfer. Compared to steel, aluminum will transfer heat across the panel about three times faster. Bare aluminum forms an oxide layer when exposed to air. The oxide later melts at a much higher temperature than the aluminum. Heat should only be used if allowed by the vehicle maker. -Temperature Window – Heat Indicators – Cumulative Times – when considering the use of heat, verify vehicle maker temperature limits. Heat indicators such as crayons, paint, noncontact, thermometer, and adhesive thermometers can be used to ensure the heating window is not exceeded. Compared to steel, aluminum does not have a cumulative heating times that need to be monitored. The spread of heat may compromise adhesives in nearby joints and areas requiring the removal of affected parts -Annealing – if aluminum is heated above a certain temperature, the area being heated will become annealed. When a part becomes annealed, it is permanently softened and cannot be returned to its original hardness. The specific annealing temperature varies by aluminum alloy and may be as low as 650 degrees Fahrenheit. This is why the repair temperature window is typically limited to 450 degrees Fahrenheit. Vehicle makers may have different temperature limits -Emissivity – if a noncontact thermometer is being used, it may not provide accurate temperature readings. This is because of the reflectiveness of bare aluminum. The emissivity, which is the temperature radiated from the reflective surface, will cause an inaccurate temperature reading. When a noncontact thermometer is used to measure temperature, reflective parts will cause the sensor to read inaccurately. To compensate for this, measure the temperature in a nearby painted area of dull paint can be applied to the part so the measured temperature is more accurate -Anchoring an Aluminum Vehicle – when conducting structural repairs, the vehicle will require measuring and anchoring. Structural straightening/pulling and stress relieving should only be done if allowed by the vehicle maker. During anchoring, the vehicle maker may specify universal fixture or dedicated fixture or may allow bench style frame machines with specialized adapters and additional anchoring apparatus. -Straightening Considerations – there are considerations that must be made when straightening a vehicle part. For instance, when straightening aluminum, it is a common practice to pull on parts that are non-repairable and going to be replaced. Using undamaged parts for pulling can create additional damage -What May Be Repairable – when evaluating what may or may not be repairable, questions need to be asked: does the vehicle maker allow repair of the part(s)? is the part a stamping, casting, or extrusion? Depending on the answer to that question, part thickness needs to be considered. The thicker the part, the harder it is to straighten. Location of damage may also affect accessibility to repair damage -Kink vs Bend, Dye Penetrant – when aluminum is bent or kicked, returning to its undamaged position is not easily done without some cracking occurring. Kink versus bend does not apply with aluminum like it does with steels. Straightening aluminum that is kinked or that has sharp bends tend to crack when straightened. After straightening has been done, dye penetrant should be used to inspect for cracks in the panels or the weld beads. Dye penetrant can be a valuable tool for locating damage -Crack damage – cast parts that are cracked should be replaced. Crack repair on other types of parts is dependent on the vehicle maker and may be permitted. Some vehicle makers allow cracked welds to be ground out and re-weld -Straightening Aluminum – aluminum does not easily return to its original shape. Due to spring-back, a part may need to be over-pulled slightly and stress relieved to realign the structure. This can be done with a hammer and wooden blocks, or wooden slappers. Heating during this process will reduce the chance parts will crack. Monitor for progress and damage at the location of the pull and other areas on the vehicle where indirect damage could occur from straightening. Monitor anchor locations to ensure no damage is incurred. Additional anchoring may be required depending on how the structure is responding -Part Removal and Preparation – when removing aluminum parts, rivets, welds, or other fasteners will require removal as well, and adhesive can be softened using heat. This can be done using a heat gun or an infrared lamp. Adhesive begins to break down at about 400 degrees Fahrenheit, some at lower temperatures. Keep in mind, heat can damage adhesive in adjacent and underlying flanges as well, which may require additional part removal. When separating the panels, use a thin separating tool, as using large chisels and pry bars can cause the flanges to stretch and become damaged. Note: Using sharp tools will help reduce heat and clogging of the cutting teeth -Cutting, Drilling, and Grinding – when cutting, drilling, or grinding on aluminum, there are some general best practices that can be applied to make the process easier. Using lubricant helps the drilling or cutting processes and prolongs the life of the cutting tools. These lubricants could be bar or solid lubricants or a cutting oil. When sanding, grinding, cutting, or drilling, a solid lubricant block is rubbed across the cutting surface. Grind or sand with light pressure and reduced tool speeds. Tools operated at too high of an RPM and with too much pressure create heat, clogs, abrasion, and smears in the aluminum. Work with tools that are in good condition and cutters that are sharp. Sharp cutting tools reduce heat in the part and fatigue of the operator. Note: Rotary carbide burrs made for steel will clog quickly, use burrs designed for aluminum to avoid this Aluminum Vehicle Overview (3/3) Module 3 – Attachment Methods and Processes -Mechanical Fastening – vehicle makers use many different methods for attaching parts to the vehicle. There are a variety of mechanical fasteners that may be used to attach parts. These fasteners include bolts, flow drill screws, rivets, clinches, and hemming of panels. Adhesives are commonly used with mechanical fasteners -Adhesives – adhesives are commonly used on aluminum-intensive vehicles for joining both structural and cosmetic parts. Adhesive locations usually have mechanical fasteners, such as rivets. The color of adhesive used by the vehicle maker is generally not available for aftermarket repair. Regardless of what color adhesive the vehicle maker uses during vehicle assembly, always use the specified repair adhesive. Vehicle makers may have unique flange preparation and treatment steps identified in their service information -Welding – GMA welding, laser welding, and resistance spot welding may be used in aluminum vehicle construction -Working with Clinches – clinches are another method vehicle makers use where panels are pressed together to make their own fastening systems. A die is used to imprint onto a panel, and no hole is made. Clinches are not used in structural applications. Inspection of a clinch is fairly simple. Either it is loose or it is not. If a clinch comes loose, a hole is typically drilled through the clinch, then adhesives and a rivet are installed to secure the panels. Vehicle makers may specify GMA plug welds to reattach a panel at some clinch locations -Repair Fastener Methods – during repairs, refer to the OEM service information to determine the repair attachment method. Note that the vehicle maker may identify more than one method that can be used in a particular location and that more than one method may be required on the same panel at different locations -Mechanical Fastening – -Many of the fasteners used for collision repair are similar to what is used by the vehicle maker. These may include: -Bolts -Flow drill screws -Rivets -Hems -Some factory panel attachment methods cannot be duplicated during collision repairs. These include clinches and the installation of flow drill screws into panels without an existing hole. When parts are joined by a hem, such as a door skin, some vehicle makers may only service those parts as assemblies or require a special type of hem flange -Adhesives and Rivet Bonding – adhesives are commonly used on aluminum-intensive vehicles during repairs, usually with other attachment processes, such as rivets or a hemmed flange. Adhesives and primers should not be used where the vehicle maker does not require them. Therefore, specific preparation guidelines should be referred to when rivet bonding flange preparation is done to an area that will receive adhesives. Vehicle makers may recommend applying adhesive to bare aluminum only or lightly scuffing the E-coat of a new part before applying adhesive. Some additional actions when rivet bonding include: -Applying adhesive to cover all bare metal surfaces -Clamping the panels together and installing the rivets -Working within rated times of the adhesive -Allowing for a full cure of the adhesive -Welding- when welding, pulsed-spray-arc-capable equipment is required. The vehicle maker may specify a particular welder with programmed settings. Argon shielding gas is usually specified along with a specific electrode wire alloy and diameter. Welding aluminum can be challenging as it is not as forgiving as steel. Extensive practice may be required to master aluminum welding, particularly when welding overhead and in tight contours like a rocker panel. test welds should be performed on similar materials and position before welding on the vehicle. Aluminum must be very clean before welding with all oxide and other coatings removed from all joint surfaces. Do not use weld-through primer. Note: Resistance spot welding is not commonly done during repairs due to the high current requirements -Aluminum Frames – vehicles with aluminum construction may require rivet bonding or welding when sectioning or replacing structural parts at factory seams. Vehicle makers may have special expanding bonded inserts for some sectioning joints -Aluminum Frame Sectioning – sectioning of frame rails and other structural parts may be permitted by some vehicle makers. For example, the 6 th generation Corvette ZR1 frames are made of aluminum and have one piece hydroformed side rails that run the length of the vehicle. There are a number of sectioning procedures for the aluminum frame rails that use a butt joint with backing -Replacement at Factory Seams – some parts may only be replaced at the factory joining locations. For example, replacement frame parts for the C7 and C8 Corvette are installed at the factory seams. Sectioning or partial replacement is not supported by the vehicle maker unless specifically documented in a service bulletin or repair procedure -Hemmed Flanges – aluminum hem flanges are made at the vehicle maker before the 6000-series aluminum is bake-hardened. By doing this, the aluminum is formed while it is softer. A rope hem flange is used for installing replacement parts that are already heat-treated. A rope hem flange allows for a larger radius to be made as the replacement panel is installed on the part. This reduces the chance the radius edge will crack -Bolts – steel bolts used for attaching aluminum panels should have a special anti-corrosion coating some of these coatings may have a light green or dull grey color. This type of bolt coating cannot be applied by the repair technician. Depending on the recommendations by the vehicle maker, these fasteners may be one-time use. This is because after the fastener is installed, the coatings may be become damaged. Some vehicle makers may allow coated bolts to be reused following proper inspection. If there is any corrosion on the fastener or if the coating is damaged, it should be replaced -Bolt-On Aluminum Parts – when replacing bolt-on parts, inspect the condition of the attachment points. Ensure any required materials or coatings are in place and torque fasteners to specification. If bolts are one-time use and the part being installed requires adjustment where the bolts may be loosened and retightened, it may be suggested to test fit the part using the old bolts -Flow Drill Screws – -Flow drill screws are: -Used to attach cosmetic and structural parts -Made of steel -Installed by the vehicle maker -Typically available as a service part -May require replacement with rivets during repair -A flow drill screw is not a self-tapping screw, but rather a screw that is spun at a fast speed and pressed against the aluminum. When the aluminum gets hot and soft, the flow drill screw is pushed through the aluminum. A flow drill screw may have a Torx-head or a special socket-head design. When joining dissimilar metals, such as steel and aluminum, vehicle makers may use these. Whenever dissimilar metals are attached to each other, adhesives may be recommended to help insulate against galvanic corrosion -Video: Installation of a Flow Drill Screw – EJOTs, otherwise known as flow drill screws, are installed at the factory in panels where there is a hole in the upper panel and none in the bottom panel. to install a flow drill screw, the fastener is spun very fast. The flow drill screw is pressed against the lower panel to create friction and heat in the lower panel. as the lower aluminum panel gets warm and soft, the flow drill screw is pushed into the panel. when the screw passes all the way through the panel, it stops spinning and the aluminum cools and threads form around the screw. If repairs are necessary in parts that use flow drill screws, they can be removed and reinstalled, as long as the threads in the lower panels are not damaged. Some vehicle makers requirement replacement, either with a new flow drill screw or with a different type of fastener such as a bolt or blind rivet -Working with Rivets- -Aluminum-intensive vehicles generally use rivets for both new vehicle assembly and during repairs. Rivets are used for both structural and cosmetic parts. The types of rivets used may include: -Solid -Blind -Self-piercing rivet (SPR) -Flow form -Solid punch, these are only installed by the vehicle maker -Rivets are one-time use fasteners. When ordering rivets, make sure to use what is recommended by the vehicle maker. Keep rivets organized by type in separate packaging or divided storage container until needed to reduce contamination issues and mixing of sizes. Note: Special tools may also be required to install some rivets. Although some of the tools can be bought from different suppliers, some of them are only available from the vehicle manufacturer -Solid Rivet Installation Scenarios - vehicle makers may identify aluminum or steel solid rivets for some locations during repairs. When installing solid rivets, adhesives must be used. Protruding-head rivets are used in some applications. Flush-mount rivets may be identified for some cosmetic and structural part attachment on some vehicles -Working with Blind Rivets – blind rivets require holes through all panels being joined and may be used in new vehicle construction and during vehicle repairs. Types of blind rivets used in repair part installation may include hemlock or monobolt rivets. These types of rivets may be universal (protruding-head) or flush-mount. Blind rivets may require a pneumatic or hydraulic rivet gun and a special nose piece to install. Hemlock and monobolt rivets should not be interchanged unless the vehicle maker permits this -Service Part Attachment Scenarios – the chart on this page shows some of the different combinations of attachment methods vehicle makers may specify. Part joining methods are subject to change; therefore, it is always recommended to access the most up-to-date service information. When selecting rivets, be sure to use the rivet size and part number specified by the vehicle maker. Fasteners, including rivets sourced outside the vehicle maker supply chain, may not meet required specifications. -OEM Attachment-Repair Attachment -Weld-&gt; weld, blind rivet, SPR -Clinch-&gt; blind rivet, SPR -Blind rivet-&gt; Blind rivet, weld -SPR-. SPR, blind rivet, solid rivet, weld -Working with SPRs – SPRs are made of high-strength, coated steel. SPRs are installed by the vehicle maker and may be specified during repairs to duplicate the original attachment process. SPRs can be installed with special electric – or pneumatic-hydraulic pressing tools. SPRs can be removed by pressing out with special dies, grinding the backside, or drilling, which can be difficult Analyzing Damage from Natural Causes (1/2) Hail Damage Dent Removal -Temporary Repairs – in some cases, especially where there was a large hailstorm that damaged many vehicles, repair time may be at a premium, requiring the customer to wait several weeks before their vehicle can be repaired. In these cases, temporary repairs may be a good option to make the vehicle drivable while waiting for scheduled repairs. Types of repairs may include replacing damaged headlamps or tail lamps for drivability. In some cases, the windshield or back glass maybe replaced to protect the interior from further water damage -Conventional Dent Removal – hail dents can be removed through conventional dent removal techniques using: -A hammer and dolly -Heat shrinking, which requires heating the panel and quick cooling to contract the metal and reduce the size of the dent -Stud welding, which requires welding small pins, or studs, to the panel and gently pulling on the pins systematically to remove the dent -Body filler following initial straightening -Panel Finish Considerations – pre-existing panel condition should be taken into consideration when determining PDR options. For example, PDR is typically not an advantage when the finish is scratched, chalked, or dull. However, it may be useful with finishes that are difficult to color match. If there is body filler beneath the finish, the part cannot be repaired using PDR. Body filler will crack from the PDR process. If the vehicle was restored and older materials were used, it is possible the finish will crack during the dent removal process. Note: Check the film thickness to identify previous repairs or additional paint coatings. Custom finishes should also be taken into consideration, as these coatings may be more brittle and crack from the PDR process -Additional Estimate Items for PDR – most vehicle makers allow PDR, but do not have a formulalized position statement. You may need to add these items to the PDR estimate: -Parts that must be removed to access the backside of the dents -Airbags located along the roof rail, may limit access to the roof panel or roof rail and may need to be deactivated -Sheet metal designs make access to the backside of hail dents difficult and increase the required repair time -Corrosion protection for the panel backside -Damage that exceeds a half-dollar size -Roof repairs for certain vehicles, such as minivans, SUVs, and extended cab pickup trucks -Common PDR Access Points – to access the backside of panels, there are a number of holes in the vehicle structure that can be used. Some of these include the headlamp mounting area and leading edge of the front door. Both of these are used to access the backside of the fender. The tail lamp mounting area may be used to access the backside of the quarter panel. Also, any factory plug holes can be used to access the panel backside. You can also access the under hood area, but this requires you to remove the insulation. Accessing the backside of the door skin may require you to remove interior trim and window regulators. Because there are airbag sensors contained in some doors, restraint system deactivation may be required before performing PDR work. -Roof Panel PDR Access Points – the roof panel may require adjacent parts to be removed to access the panel backside. This may include removal of the hatch, luggage rack, sunroof, and headliner. If doing PDR on a roof rail, note that some vehicle designs have side curtain airbags in this area. Some vehicle makers may require disabling the restraint system before removing the headliner assembly -PDR Limitations – when performing PDR repairs, technicians should not drill access holes. If holes must be cut to access the panel backside, PDR is not an acceptable option. Adding holes to a structural part can alter the structural integrity of the vehicle, creating collapse zones where none were intended. Foam should not be removed when performing PDR. It would be difficult to reinstall foams in many situations. PDR should not damage other parts during the dent removal process. An example of this may include elongating an access hole by placing pressure on it during the dent removal process. Be aware that the finish may be damaged during dent removal -PDR on Aluminum Panels – the PDR process is slightly different for aluminum panels compared to steel. Dents in aluminum are shallower than steel dents, as aluminum resists impacts better than steel. So it is possible that on the same vehicle, an aluminum hood will have less damage compared to the steel roof. Also, aluminum has less metal memory than steel. In some cases, when working with aluminum panels, heat may be used to make the metal more malleable. If heating, consider what is behind the panel to avoid damaging adhesives or foam and to avoid overheating the panel, as this may result in damage to the paint, eliminating the benefit of PDR. Be aware that more time may be required to perform PDR on aluminum if the spot becomes work-hardened. Note: It is important to remember that aluminum work hardens quicker than steel. The more the dent has to be worked with the PDR tools, the less likely the dent will be completely removed. -Post PDR Inspection – during the PDR process, it is possible that the corrosion protection materials on the backside of the panel may be removed from the repeated scratching of the dent tool tips, even if the tips may be protected with plastic. Therefore, corrosion protection must be restored to ensure the durability of the repair. Because it is difficult to see if the corrosion protection was removed, it is safest to assume that the finish has been scratched and apply anti-corrosion compound to the backside of the panel. Following PDR repairs, check for microcracking. Any damage to the coating will require the area to be refinished -Combination Repair – some types of damage may allow a combination repair, which is where PDR is used along with conventional repair methods. For example, on one panel the bigger dents may be repaired conventionally and the smaller dents may be repaired with PDR. This type of repair may allow blending to stay within a panel, depending on the blend location Hail Damage -Weather-Related Damage – types of weather-related vehicle damage include hail, wind, and lightning. Floods are also a very common type of weather-related damage. However, flood damage is covered in separate I-CAR course -Hail Damage Identification – hail damage looks similar to door dings, but is generally found above the crown of the panel, unless there are high winds driving hail into all areas of a panel. Damage is generally only on one side of the vehicle, unless the vehicle is being driven during the storm. Hail generally does not chip paint. It will create a smooth dent with no breaks in the finish. If the hail halls on the panel edges, such as the front of the roof that meets the windshield, the paint film may crack. Depending on the thickness, refinishing panels may not handle the hail as well as the original finish. A high film thickness may result in possible chipping of the paint in a hail storm. Note: When analyzing hail damage, note that panels may delay the appearance of hail damage until they are heated by the sun. as the metal expands, hail damage may become more pronounced. -Repair/Replace Hail-Damaged Panel – when making the decision to repair or replace panels, consider the amount of damage to the panel, the cost to repair vs replace, availability of parts, repairability, and the ease of replacement -Percent of Damage – consider the percentage of damage to the panel. If a panel has hundreds of hail dents, panel repair may simply not be feasible, as the damage is too extensive. There may also be concern about the durability of the repair. -Cost – consider the cost of repair versus replacing the panel. Depending on the vehicle make and model, even a panel with considerable damage may be more cost effective to repair rather than replace -Parts – take the ease of obtaining the part into consideration. If a panel is back-ordered or rare, repair may be the better option. Since parts are ordered following the estimate, this may not be immediately evident, requiring a change to be made to the estimate -Repairability – consider the repairability of the part. Some parts, such as trim pieces, are difficult to repair, or to repair cost effectively -Ease of Replacement – consider the ease of panel replacement. For example, replacing a hood or deck lid, which are bolt-on parts, is much easier than replacing a welded or bonded roof panel. Replacing a roof generally requires removing and installing the windshield and back glass. Depending on the flange configuration, it may also require drilling out the top portion of the B-pillar or sail panel. It is recommended to be as least invasive as possible during the repair process -Half Damage Inspection Tips – there are several techniques that will make evaluating hail damage easier -Systematic Approach – follow a systematic order to ensure all panels are inspected and an accurate dent count has been made. Use water-soluble markers to highlight hail dents on the vehicle. Do not use grease pencils, as they may not be washed off adequately. It is also possible that grease pencils may bake into the finish, damaging the clearcoat -Multiple Angles – it is helpful to view each panel from multiple directions. Different viewing angles provide different lighting that may better identify hail damage. Reflection is key, especially for taking digital images. Using a striped or checked board can help highlight depressions in the panel surface. This only works on a gloss finish -PDR Matrix – several paintless dent repair (PDR) professionals and insurance companies have created charts to help assist in determining the cost. The PDR matrix is broken into seven different categories that range from “very light” to the “limit” which is the maximum number of dents to be addressed. The matrix assists in determining the cost of PDR relative to damage location, quantity, and size -Reference Tool – a reference tool may be used that is specially designed to determine hail dent size for damage analysis purposes. This tool includes various sizes of discs used to determine dent size when compared to a specific reference chart or cost matrix. These discs are commonly the size of a U.S. dime, nickel, quarter, and half dollar. While not used every time to estimate damage, these discs may be used when starting out to identify damage size. Note: PDR damage estimates are typically based on the size and quantity of hail dents in conjunction with a hail damage PDR matrix -Inspecting Hail Damage – factors to consider when inspecting hail damage include lighting, cleanliness, colors, and water. Lighting angles can cast shadows into panel depressions. Indoor inspections typically provide better light for identifying dents. Direct sunlight can often hide smaller dents, so shade is necessary to get an accurate dent count. A clean vehicle is helpful when inspecting hail damage. Even a light layer of dust can hide smaller dents, causing an inaccurate count. Dark colors show dents better than light colors. You can use a squeegee over a wetted panel to help identify depressions in the panel, as they will remain filled with water. Note: Look for the smallest dents first. Do not let obvious size dents direct your perception. This may lead to missing a large percentage of damage -Glass Damage from Hail – when analyzing glass damage, keep in mind that hail size is directly related to damage.
