Hail storm damage, unless severe, generally does not damage laminated glass. Large hail typically results in a smash-type damage that is not repairable. Hail does not create repairable damage, such as a bullseye, starbreak, or crack. Pit marks are often caused by everyday driving, not hail -Convertible Top – convertible tops are also susceptible to hail damage. Depending on the size of the hail stones, damage may include rips, tears, and punctures. Possible convertible top repair options include: -Sewing seams -Replacing fabric -Repairing or replacing the frame -Replacing the back window -Note: The older the top, the more damage that may occur. This is due to degradation of the fabric that can occur over time from weather and UV exposure Hail Damage Fraud Indicators -Hail Damage Fraud Indicators – when inspecting hail damage claims, some common fraud indicators may include: -Rounder dents, common with a golf ball or ball peen hammer. Hail dents are typically steeper, with no creases and do not have any paint transfer or scratches -Damage on all sides of the vehicle. Often, hail damage is directional, meaning it is on the upper panels and one side of the vehicle, unless the vehicle was being driven during the hail storm -Even dent pattern. Hail falls on random points of the vehicle with no specific pattern -Dents on panels, but no damage on metal trim such as roof and belt moldings. Fraudulent hail damage claims can be detected by inspecting the shape, location, and pattern of damage -Insurance Service Office (ISO) – Fraud may be difficult to prove, even when suspected. One tool used to help determine if there have been previous claims on a vehicle includes checking Insurance Service Office (ISO) reports. Prior damage is not covered under new polices. The ISO is a data collector that specifically services the insurance industry and has a portion of the business devoted to auto claims information. The ISO will need information about the claim and the zip code and/or vehicle identification number (VIN) to retrieve the required data in order to find the size, cause, and amount of a previous loss Analyzing Damage from Natural Causes (2/2) Wind, Lightning, and Infestations Wind Damage -Causes of Wind-Related Damage – high winds can cause vehicle damage. While the wind does not cause the damage itself, it commonly occurs when falling objects, such as: -Tree limbs -Home roofing materials -Miscellaneous flying debris, essentially anything that can fall or become a projectile from high winds -Note: Wind damage is often directional, similar to hail damage -Wind and Sand – another type of damage that is more common in the desert regions is wind-blown sand. Blowing sand can create a sandblasted-type of damage on a vehicle that can affect the: -Trim -Chrome -Headlamps -Wheels -Finish. Sand can etch or scratch the clearcoat, if not deeper -Note: Some storms have been known to damage the windshield. Sand storm damage can cause sand to blow through the crankcase breather tube into the oil crankcase. Generally, damage cannot simply be repaired by applying clearcoat. Most times, the finish must be sanded and reapplied -Wind Damage Repairs – common methods of repair from wind-related damage include: -Refinishing -PDR or conventional dent removal techniques for dents and dings in the panels -Suspension repair due to damage that may occur from heavy objects falling on a vehicle -Structural repairs, depending on the extent of damage. For example, a tree falling on a vehicle can damage the exterior panels, structure, and/or suspension Lightning Damage -Lightning Strikes – lightning is essentially a large spark of electricity that can reach over five miles (eight kilometers) in length, raise the temperature of the air by as much as 50,000 degrees Fahrenheit and contains 100 million electrical volts. These strikes, many of which go cloud to ground, occur about 22 million times per year in the United States. The odds of a lightning bolt striking a vehicle or striking near a vehicle are good, but a rarity to witness -Lightning Damage – lightning causes damage in one of two methods, including a direct strike or electromagnetic induction -Direct Damage – a direct lightning strike to a vehicle is difficult to determine. Generally, there are no large areas of damage that occur to the vehicle structure. Often the damage shows up as a small puncture in the metal. For direct strikes to the vehicle, note that lightning: -Strikes the highest points on a vehicle. This is generally the roof or antenna -May cause the antenna to burn, melt, and/or bend -May cause the antenna mounting location to show signs of burning or melting -Electromagnetic Induction – a lightning strike near a vehicle may cause damage in the form of electromagnetic induction. Electromagnetic induction can travel hundreds of feet. In these cases, there is no apparent physical damage to the vehicle, but the vehicle electronics may show damage. Low voltage systems are most prone to damage from electromagnetic induction -Lightning Damage Analysis – when analyzing lightning damage to the vehicle: -Verify a thunderstorm occurred by checking recent local weather reports -Verify damage to electrical or mechanical parts by turning the vehicle ON and checking for malfunction indicator lamps. In some cases, the vehicle may not run due to extensive damage to the electronic systems -Check for evidence of burning, or charring on circuit boards, wire connectors, or the fuse box -Inspect fuses to determine if several fuses have been damaged at once -Lightning strikes tend to affect more than one vehicle. Check with the insurance companies to determine if multiple claims were made for vehicles parked nearby -Note: It can be difficult to determine the full extent of the damage through visual inspection alone. It may be impossible to know the full extent of damage until the diagnostic process is complete -Lightning Damage Repair – technicians may start with testing main system modules, such as powertrain control module (PCM) or engine control module (ECM), just to get the vehicle running, then work down to smaller sub-systems. They may also need to talk to a vehicle maker representative to determine what should be repaired first after a lightning strike. It is also important to note that just because a vehicle has been repaired and operates properly does not ensure a long-lasting repair. Parts can be partially damaged from a lightning strike, and fail at a later time, even after repairs have been made and the vehicle delivered to the customer Infestation Damage -Types of Infestations – infestation can cause serious damage to a vehicle. Common intruders include mice, squirrels, and rabbits. Common infestation problems include electrical issues with various systems, commonly caused by chewing through wiring harness insulation, and nesting in fuse boxes and the engine compartment. Nesting in heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) ducts can block ducts, leading to heating and cooling problems. Infestation is more common in vehicles that have not been used for a period of time. Common types include RVs or collector vehicles that have been in storage. It is also more common in rural areas compared to more urban areas -Infestation Inspection – when inspecting a vehicle for infestation, all accessible cavities should be checked, including the engine compartment, hoses, wiring, insulation, interior, and trunk. Rodents will look to gain entry at any access point, including areas filled with rubber grommets and seals. If necessary, a borescope may be used to see inside panels or inside HVAC ducts without disassembly. If nests or remnants are found, the parts can be disassembled for proper cleaning -Infestation Cleanup – cleaning up infestation debris can be as simple as removing a nest from an engine compartment or as involved as calling in a specialist who deals specifically with cleaning biohazardous materials. Determine when specialists are required. Often, if the infestation includes the interior or ductwork of the vehicle, professional cleaners are recommended. With mice, one of the larger risks includes exposure to the hantavirus. While not common, consider this health risk when treating infestation. Common removal techniques include removing the nest and vacuuming up the residue. Do not blow the material out of the affected area. Use a recommended cleaning solution to wipe down the area. It is important to wear an air-purifying respirator, safety goggles, and gloves to protect against contact with potential infectious waste Analyzing Fire Damage (1/2) Fire Damage Identification The Cause of Fire -Vehicle Fire Statistics – some reports say U.S. fire departments respond to approximately 152,300 automobile fires per year. According to this report, common causes of vehicle fires include: -Mechanical failures (49%) -Electrical failure (23%) -Arson (8%) -Exposure to other sources of fire (5%) -Collisions and rollovers (3%) -Note: 64% of mechanical fires began in the engine compartment, running gear, or wheel area, while only 2% of the vehicle fires originated in the fuel line or fuel tank area -Determining the Cause of Fire - -Vehicle fires are commonly started: -By accident -Intentionally (arson) -By mechanical or electrical failure due to poor maintenance, a design flaw, etc -Before beginning the estimate, one of the primary items to look for is cause. Auto physical damage appraisers and estimators need to ask themselves the following questions: -What started the fire? -Where did it start? -Was the fire initiated in the vehicle, or was it caused by an external source, such as a nearby house fire or garage fire -Note: Determine if the fire burned from the engine compartment into the passenger compartment, or burned from the passenger compartment into the engine compartment, or started from an outside -Determination Aids – when determining cause, check the vehicle maker or national database, such as the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), for potential recalls. The NHTSA website, www.nhtsa.gov , provides a searchable database of all recalled vehicles. Also, look for vehicle modifications that may have caused shorting or overheating -Common Modifications – common modifications that can lead to fires: -High performance stereo equipment -Aftermarket electronics, such as GPS, alarms, phone charges, and video systems, that have been hard-wired into an existing vehicle circuit -Note: These types of fires generally have an electrical ignition point. Many events are not obvious. In these instances, some insurers may choose to use professional fire inspectors to determine the cause and origin of a fire. These individuals are trained to determine where the fire started in the vehicle, and the source of the fire -Vehicle Owner – when determining the cause of the fire, asking the customer the right questions can be almost as valuable as examining the fire damage itself. Some questions that can provide clues about how the vehicle started include: -Was the vehicle running smoothly? -Were there any prior problems? -Was the vehicle running when the fire started? If so, how long was it running? If not, how long was it since the vehicle was turned off? -Was there any recent repair work performed? -What color was the smoke? Different materials create different colors of smoke -How was the vehicle being used at the time of the fire? Was it normal operation? -Was any aftermarket equipment installed? -What did the vehicle owner see at the time, or just before the fire began? -Note: For example, understanding what the conditions were at the time of the fire can help pinpoint the system that malfunctioned -Fraud – an intentional vehicle fire is either an act of vandalism (joy rides or for use in thefts) or a cause of fraud, where the vehicle owners are looking to collect compensation for the damage. -Potential Indicators – potential indicators of fraud include: -Keys left in the ignition. If the vehicle was stolen, the ignition system should have an indication that it was defeated -All windows were rolled down. This is done to ensure there is adequate ventilation to fuel the fire. -Fires starting in the back seat, front seat, or floor of the vehicle -Remnants of the accelerant -Varying accounts between the adjuster interview of the customer and police report -Note: If the vehicle burned completely, the odds are considerably less likely that this material can be found -Oil Sample – another potential fraud indicator can be found in an oil sample. Having the oil tested for contaminants can provide a motive behind arson. For example, if an oil test reveals that the oil contains high amounts of metals such as tin, lead, iron, or aluminum, it is a good indication that the engine has broken down. The presence of metals such as those in the oil is not caused by fire and would lead an investigator to examine the service history of the vehicle -Suspicion – if there is a suspicion of fraud, it is important that estimators and auto physical damage appraisers not disturb any evidence. Fire investigators need to examine the vehicle to pinpoint the exact cause. Timeliness is key to fire investigation. If it will be some time before an investigator can inspect the vehicle, it will be helpful to take pictures Point of Origin -Determining Point of Origin – determining cause and origin is important to: -Help identify trends in potential electrical/mechanical fires -Determine if fraud (arson) is a possibility -Note: When determining the point of origin, burn patterns are examined. Some may think the first thing you look for is the hottest point of the fire. However, often times the hottest spot of a vehicle fire is not the point of origin. For example, a wiring harness that starts a fire will not burn as hot as power steering fluid -Fire Burn Pattern – vehicles often have a burn pattern that gives the fire inspector an idea of the general location of where the fire started. Two common locations where fires start are the instrument panel and the engine compartment -Instrument Panel – if the fire starts beneath the instrument panel, it will consume the flammable materials and work its way up to the headliner. The heat from the fire can potentially break the tempered door glass, allowing in more oxygen and intensifying the fire. The heat of the fire rises, so the floor will be the last to burn. The fire will move forward to the engine compartment and rearward to the trunk, burning in a V-shaped pattern, eventually consuming the entire vehicle -Engine Compartment – for fires that start in the engine compartment, it is important to note that how engines burn faster than cold engines. As the fire burns, the fluids in the area start to feed the fire. Eventually, the fire burns through the cowl at the point of the heater fan or A/C inlet, as these areas are generally made of plastic. Once the fire enters beneath the instrument panel, a similar burn will occur as described for the instrument panel -Mechanical Fire – mechanical fires can start through a variety of methods, including: -Fluids beneath the hood spraying onto a hot source, such as gasoline, transmission fluid, motor oil, power steering fluid, brake fluid, and coolant -Tires, from being overheated -Brakes not releasing properly, resulting in a brake drag situation that overheats the brakes -A heat shield being removed or falling off the vehicle, resulting in the floor getting hot enough to start the interior carpeting on fire -Power steering fluid and brake fluid rarely start fires, but once heated to a very hot temperature in a fire, they will burn very hot -Coolants that contain ethylene glycol. These has a flash point of around 230 degrees Fahrenheit and an auto ignition temperature around 770 degrees Fahrenheit. If there is a leak that causes the coolant to fall on a hot surface, the water can boil away, leaving just the ethylene glycol, which could ignite. -Note: Each of these fluids, if not the actual ignition point of the fire, will certainly fuel the fire and help it spread throughout the vehicle -Accidental Fire – the majority of accidental fires start in the engine compartment. It helps fire inspectors if they have a general knowledge of how parts under the hood are positioned and their proximity to each other. This in turn helps the inspector understand how the fire spread. It is also important to remember that due to the extensive heat, most engine fires are not repairable unless they are extinguished quickly -Electrical Fire – it has been mentioned that approximately 23% of all vehicle fires are started by a faulty electrical system. Electrical fires can occur from -Shorts -Faulty switches -Faulty motors -A wire that is too small for a power requirement that is too large -Note: When a smaller wire shorts, the melting will only go back to the larger wire. With a large wiring harness, a fire can be much larger. The burning wire harness now acts like a fuse as it burns toward more combustible material, such as plastics or vehicle fluids. It is helpful to know where electrical items are routed when tracing the potential source of a fire. Items to look for when suspecting an electrical fire include blown fuss, which may help identify which systems they protect, and unusual wiring attachments -Evidence Locker – vehicle fires are not normal, for this reason insurance companies and repair facilities are interested in identifying the cause of the fire to determine if it was due to poor design, worn parts, or was intentionally set. Knowing this will help to determine liability and how potential evidence is handled -Liability and Faulty Parts – no repairs should be made until the source and cause of the fire is identified. This is a liability issue for the technician and repair facility. By restoring a vehicle exactly as it was before the fire, the technician may inadvertently set up a situation where another fire will take place. For example, if the cause is a bad circuit design by the vehicle maker, and the vehicle is repaired using replacement parts of the same design, the technician has essentially ‘lit the fuse’ for another potential fire. If the cause is determined and a faulty part is identified, the faulty part that caused the fire should be saved by the collision repair facility. This part can then be viewed by any party who has an interest in the cause and origin of the vehicle fire, and may be used as evidence in any subsequent legal proceedings. Additionally, any evidence, whether it is part of the original vehicle design, aftermarket, or related to possible arson, should not be altered, destroyed, or lost. This is known as ‘spoilation of evidence’ and should be avoided to prevent possible litigation problems -Chain of Custody – additionally, photos may be required of the suspected damaged part. Maintaining and documenting this evidence and nothing who has had access to it, in legal terms, is known as ‘chain of custody’. Chain of custody is the movement and location of physical evidence from the time it is obtained until the time it is presented in court. An example of when chain of custody would be applied is if the gas tank straps were breaking on one vehicle, causing the tank to fail. In situations like this, the collision repair facility should store, record, track, and protect the faulty part from being tampered with, in the event the part needs to be inspected during a subsequent investigation. If there are any indications of potential product liability, perhaps the repair should not be completed quickly, as there may be pending investigations Determine Temperatures -Determining Temperatures – different materials burn at different degrees, and different degrees of heat have varying effects on steel -Determining Temperature – fire investigators are able to determine specific temperatures by examining oxidation of metals, such as steel and copper. Thus, if an investigation is going to be performed on a vehicle, it is recommended to keep the vehicle covered. Exposure to the elements, such as rain and sun, can expedite the corrosion process, making it more difficult to pinpoint the ignition point. Additionally, if the vehicle can be repaired, proper covering prevents damage to the interior if windows are open or broken. Investigators also check the melting of plastics, taking into consideration that some plastics melt at higher temperatures. Note: A bumper cover will melt and burn at a much lower temperature than a plastic intake manifold -Solids – this chart identifies the ignition temperatures and melting points of various materials. Knowing these temperatures helps identify the temperature of a fire. For example, acrylic fibers have an ignition temperature of 1040 degrees Fahrenheit, and a melting point of 122 degrees Fahrenheit -Liquids – this chart helps identify fire temperature by showing the flash point and ignition temperature of liquids. For example, the chart shows that brake fluid has a flash point between 240-355 degrees Fahrenheit, and an ignition temperature between 536-644 degrees Fahrenheit. Analyzing Fire Damage (2/2) Fire Damage Repair Considerations Effects of Heat on Vehicle Parts -Smoke and Heat Damage – aside from creating stains fabrics, smoke or heat damage also has a bearing on whether a repair or replacement will be necessary -Repair vs. Replace – if there was minor damage to the vehicle, there will always be the question, “What is repairable, and what must be replaced?’ As a general rule, smoke damage may be repairable, whereas heat damage generally requires part replacement. For example, if plastic trim has melted, the seat has burned, or the headliner is scorched, replacement is required -Smoke and Soot – smoke and soot enter all areas of the vehicle, such as under seats, into air vents, and behind door panels. It permeates all fabrics, such as: -Headliners -Carpeting -Seats -Note: The problem is being able to remove all the smoke residue and completely neutralize the smoke odor. This task is not easily accomplished and requires special procedures and products. Even if the smoke odor is removed, often the customer still has memory of it and may claim they can still smell the odor, even if a neutral third party could not -Fire Damage Repair Considerations – often, fires leave a heavy amount of fire extinguisher retardant, soot, and water that can make damage appear worse than it is. To begin, the vehicle interior is generally removed. This requires removal of the seats, carpet, headliner, door panels, and plastic trim, which allows the repair facility to assess what is truly damaged -Fire Extinguisher Retardant – if a fire extinguisher retardant was used, it may be helpful to know what type in order to effectively clean the vehicle. For example, extinguishers that use water, carbon dioxide, potassium bicarbonate, or sodium bicarbonate can be mopped or vacuumed out of the vehicle interior or removed with a mild solution of vinegar and water. Monoammonium phosphate and foams require slightly more aggressive methods, hand scrubbing and water dilution -Soot – with soot, it is important to begin the cleaning process as soon as possible. Soot residue is acidic and can cause damage to metal, plastics, etc. if left to sit for a period of time. Soot can turn parts a yellowish color if not removed quickly from the surface. Quick removal will limit the amount of etching into the surface of vehicle parts. Note: Soot is a fine particulate that can work its way into just about any area of a vehicle. For this reason, the entire vehicle must be thoroughly wiped down -Cleaning Process – one type of cleaning process includes the following: -The fabric parts are vacuumed to remove soot, ash, and any chemicals used to extinguish the fire. These are easier to remove when dry -All the seats and carpeting are shampooed and pressure-washed -The vehicle interior is pressure-washed -All parts that have been exposed to water are considered for repair or replacement -Note: Some items, such as chrome trim on door handles or console, can etch or tarnish pretty quickly, so if the soot is not removed promptly, new parts will need to be installed -Odor Removal – to remove the fire odor that has permeated the vehicle, cleaning companies may use specific processes. All areas must be cleaned, including air ducts -Traditional Cleaning Chemicals – traditional cleaning chemicals, such as soap and water, are generally used at first to remove the physical residue of the fire. This residue is the primary source of the odor, so removal of this layer is a good start -Special Chemicals – some chemicals are specifically formulated to neutralize the smell of burnt plastic. They are used on all fabric and on paintwork, which absorb the fire residue that causes odors. The chemicals used on plastics themselves are designed to block the smell chemically. The odor is difficult to remove, so the only option is to find a solution that permanently blocks it. Smells are often trapped in plastics, as the heat of a fire allows the pores in the plastic to open and trap contaminants -Fog Solution – a fog solution can be used on the vehicle interior. Fog is the only material that can go where smoke goes. Special machines with a heated element are designed to turn a liquid deodorant into a fog. -Ozone Machines – odors have characteristics similar to a frequency. If you use another odor with the opposite ‘frequency’, the smoke odor can be cancelled out, or neutralized. Ozone machines attack and break apart smoke molecules that causes the odor. -Smoke/Heat Damage on Glass – the type of heat damage to glass depends on the type of glass. For example, tempered glass may hold up to higher temperatures, but can be etched by the acidic smoke from the fire. Laminated glass can also be etched from smoke, but heat can create air bubbles in the laminate, essentially destroying a windshield or laminated door glass -Heat (Fire) Effect on Steel – the effect of heat on steel: -Alters the strength of the part -Changes the mechanical properties. Heat tends to strengthen mild steels, weaken high-strength steels, and completely destroy the strength of most ultra-high strength steels. -Note: The amount of heat and rate at which it is applied, as well as the cooling rate, can have a dramatic effect on the mechanical properties of the steel. Mild steels tend to become more brittle when heated, while some of the higher strength steels may become softer and more formable. This may lead to a part that was intended to transfer collision energy actually absorbing it instead, and a part that should absorb energy being hard enough to either crack or transfer the energy instead -Structural Integrity – high-strength steel (HSS) and ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS) are altered by the application of heat. Heat makes HSS and UHSS softer, more malleable, or weaker, essentially losing their high tensile strength. Most vehicle makers recommend against the use of heat on their structural parts. Heat is generally not recommended unless the part is made from mild steel, a type of steel where heat application will not affect the integrity of the steel. Mild steel is used on many non-structural parts, such as exterior body panels -Heat Exposure – more intense fires will weaken the steel, while lower heat will leave the steel properties as originally designed. Knowing which is the case, of course, would require determining how much heat the part was exposed to. If the fire was quickly extinguished and the paint on the part is mostly intact, it is likely there was not enough heat to affect the structural strength of the steel. However, if most of the engine compartment was consumed and portions of the paint have been burned off, it may be assumed that temperatures were above what is required for the steel to maintain its structural strength. Note: It is also important to verify the type of steel that has been exposed to heat. Some HSS parts can be heated, while most UHSS cannot -Heat Indicators – paint on a part can become a heat indicator. Knowing at what temperatures paint starts to burn or melt can provide indications of the maximum fire temperature. Clearcoats and topcoats can start to burn at about 450 degrees Fahrenheit, while zinc coatings start to burn at approximately 750 degrees Fahrenheit. So if all the coatings have been burned, it could be a good estimate that the heat was at least 750 degrees Fahrenheit. It may be helpful to look at the opposite side of the part. Looking on the inside of a rail can provide an indication that heat temperatures rose even higher than the 750 degree fehrenheit at which zinc melts, since the inside of the part was away from the area of direct heat. To perform this type of inspection, a borescope may be used to visually inspect inside a rail -Heat Effect on Foams/Adhesives – heat is one of the primary sources used to loosen adhesive and separate a bonded part. After an adhesive is heated, it loses its bonding properties. For example, in an engine fire, heat can cause the exterior hood panel to separate from the inner structure. Foams are built into inner structures, such as B-pillars, rocker panels, and quarter panels. When exposed to heat, these can also separate from the vehicle structure. For example, if a vehicle is parked next to a fire that exposes it to high heat, the temperature could cause the flutter foam that is used to attach the door skin to the intrusion beam to detach. Note: In higher temperature fires, foams, and adhesives can ignite, essentially burning the vehicle from inside the tubular structures -Heat Effect on Electronics – electronics are very sensitive to heat and smoke. A typical vehicle fire has smoke (soot), humidity from burning fluids, and of course heat. All three of these are problematic for sensitive electronic parts that are used throughout most late model vehicles. Soot residue has the potential to short-circuit parts by bridging the circuits within the part. Additionally, soot can create a layer over the heat-producing parts of an electronic component, providing almost an insulating effect – resulting in overheating of the part. Note: Soot leaves limited repair options for control modules and other vehicle electronics. Some companies offer services to repair these particular pars, and it becomes a business decision to determine whether or not the repair is cost-effective Analyzing Flood Damage (1/2) Flood Damage Identification and Health Concerns Flood Level Indicators -Determining Flood Level – determining the height that the floodwater reached on a vehicle is one of the key factors in determining whether a vehicle is repairable. For this reason, it is also one of the first things an estimator or auto physical damage appraiser (APDA) will do. Flood levels are often found as dirty water lines on outside panels of the vehicle -Flood Level Indicators – not all vehicles have exterior panel lines deposited by the flood, especially if the water is clean or if subsequent rains wash the flood lines off the outside panels. In this situation other moisture indicators must be examined. These generally include: -Wet paper or materials in the glove box -Water lines on interior trim or seats -Evidence of debris in the radiator or air conditioning condenser – the fins tend to trap residue, creating a clear water level line -Water in the trunk or spare tire tub -Interior Moisture Indicators – moisture can wick upward on water-saturated cushions, so even if the tops of the seat cushions feel dry, look beneath the seats for evidence of moisture. Some rear seats can be removed easily to check the foam underneath for saturation. Check for evidence of mildew or mold – this type of damage depends on how long the vehicle sat without cleaning. Similar to seat cushions, floodwaters that soak into the jute behind the carpeting will allow the water to wick up farther than the actual floodwater level, especially in the cowl area. For this reason, it is important to look at all the indicators to determine flood level -Additional Moisture Indicators – look for fresh corrosion or silt inside the exhaust tailpipe, pockets on door trim panels filled with water, and interior cup holders filled with water. Uncoated corroded fasteners on the inside of the vehicle are a good indication that they were once under water. A completely corroded rotor would be an indicator that flood water rose above that part of the vehicle, provided the vehicle has not sat too long. Partially corroded rotors can provide floodwater levels, provided the vehicle was not driven. Also note that all rotors corrode eventually, so if the vehicle has been sitting for a couple weeks, corrosion will start to form. -Length of Floodwater Exposure – the length of exposure to floodwaters reduces the likelihood of repairs. For example, a flash flood may offer more repair options than floods where vehicles remained submerged for several days. Other items that may contribute to the repairability considerations include potential body damage if a vehicle is pushed by floodwaters, contacting stationary objects or other vehicles as it flows with the current Total Loss Indicators -Total Loss indicators – floodwater up to the instrument panel, regardless of the cleanliness of the water is one indicator that a vehicle would be a total loss. Other indicators include saltwater or brackish water over the rocker panels, and water over the breather hose in the transmission. Note: Water that has risen up to the instrument panel generally totals the vehicle -Total Loss Threshold – older model vehicles that are not equipped with as many electronics may not be subjected to the extent of damage experienced by late model vehicles. One of the biggest issues with flood-damaged vehicles is that it is almost impossible to determine how many systems were affected by floodwaters. Systems that initially appear to be undamaged often fail several months later -Total Loss Guidelines – flood damage laws are state and local, not federal. Some states, depending on their laws, require the vehicle to have a flood title if water reaches the rocker panels. If not decided by state law, each insurance company may have different flood guidelines based on flood lines, options, number of computers, sensor locations, air intake/vent locations, etc. Flood Fraud Indicators -I-CAR Position Statement – this I-CAR training course has been designed to identify potential indicators of fraud and the issues related to damage analysis that may warrant additional investigation. I-CAR is not in the position to recommend how situations that might involve an element of fraud should be handled. The investigation and handling of these situations is a highly sensitive matter, and one which must respect the relationship, rights, and obligations that exist between an insurer and its customers. All situations that involve potential indicators of fraud and related damages should be handled according to the policies, practices, and procedures of the insurance company involved. While it is not recommended to go into every claim suspecting fraud, it is important to be able to identify the common signs of fraud. Some of these signs include: -Vague or inconsistent details regarding the claim -Something out of character -Physical indications that are not consistent with the claim -Fraud Indicators -Fraud is an unfortunate reality of the claims industry. There is a small percentage of vehicle owners that intentionally vandalize their vehicle in order to receive insurance compensation. Common reasons fraud may occur include that the customer is looking to total the vehicle, increase repair amounts, or receive cash to cover deductibles. Common types of flood fraud include: -Using a garden hose to flood the interior -Intentionally driving into an ocean, lake, or river -Intentionally driving into dep water during heavy rains -Common flood fraud indicators include: -Freshwater damage when the floodwater was supposed to be saltwater -The vehicle interior is water damaged, but there is no moisture in the trunk or engine compartment Flood Damage Health Concerns -Personal Protective Equipment – depending on how long the vehicle has been sitting before inspection, molds and bacteria can start to grow. The greenhouse effect on the vehicle interior turns into a virtual petri dish. Therefore, proper personal protection, such as respirators, nitrile gloves, and safety glasses, are necessary when examining some flood-damaged vehicles. The protection used depends on the cleanliness of the water. In some instances, depending on the biological contaminants, the vehicle may require demolition. For example, many of the vehicles that were flooded by Hurricane Katrina were destroyed due to the length of exposure and the type of water exposure. Note: Ungloved hands or other body parts exposed to damp vehicle parts should be thoroughly washed after inspection -Health Concerns – one of the most noticeable items when inspecting a flood-damaged vehicle is the odor of the interior. Mildew, mold, or decaying organic material, especially under the heated conditions that occur inside a vehicle, can create a pungent smell. Vehicles that have been exposed to floodwaters, particularly brackish water, saltwater, or river water, may have mold growth. Mold can start to grow beneath the carpeting, seats, floor mats, filters, and other areas that trap moisture. Floodwater may contain E.coli and other harmful bacteria. Vehicles that are not properly and promptly cleaned, dried, and disinfected will experience some sort of bacterial growth that can cause respiratory problems for vehicle drivers and/or repair technicians. Drying out the inside as quickly as possible is critical to limiting the amount of potential contamination -Cabin Filter and HVAC Inspection – cabin filters are often beneath the dash, which can be exposed to low-level floodwaters. Similar to the air intake filter, these can start to create mold once they have been saturated. Heating and air conditioning (A/C) vents are often placed at floor level. These fill with floodwater, even in a low-level flood. If left untreated, these areas can be a breeding ground for mold, mildew, and bacteria. The evaporator case for the A/C system can also fill with floodwater. This piece, if left untreated, can create an odor problem in the vehicle when operating the A/C Analyzing Flood Damage (2/2) Effects of Water on Vehicle Parts Flood Damage Inspection Considerations -Part Design and Floodwater – not all parts are damaged by water. Some parts of a vehicle are designed to withstand repeated exposure to the outside environment. Therefore, one of the considerations during damage analysis is to determine if a part is designed to get wet. Some parts can withstand moisture, such as road splash or heavy rains, but not complete submersion. For example, an exhaust system can take quite a lot of moisture, but complete submersion will put water into the engine. However, brake system pads and rotors can take submersion for a short period of time -Flood-Damaged Electric Vehicles – vehicle makers have various warnings and recommendations for dealing with submerged hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) and electric vehicles (EVs). However, from a safety standpoint, there is no electrocution danger to an occupant or rescue personnel from a vehicle that is submerged. Batteries that have submerged will short-circuit internally and lose their charge. Flood-damaged hybrid batteries must be replaced. Drain the water from the vehicle, if possible. Manually disable the HV system using vehicle maker procedures Engine Compartment Inspection -Air Cleaner Housing – the air intake provides a good indicator when determining the height of the floodwater or whether or not water may have entered the engine. Removing the air cleaner cap and checking air filter for dampness will determine if the floodwaters reached that height -Air Cleaner Housing, Continued – in some instances, depending on how long it has been since the flood occurred, the air filter will have mold and other contaminants starting to grow on the filter. If moisture is not evident on the air filter, it is still a good idea to remove the air filter and check the housing for moisture beneath the filter. The presence of water is a good indication that there may be water inside the engine -Mass Airflow Sensor – if the air intake was flooded, it is likely the mass airflow sensor was also exposed to floodwater. Exposure to water may be indicated by white oxidation on the connector prongs. The mass airflow sensor is located inside the intake air duct. This sensor is used to measure the amount of air entering the engine. Water damage to the sensor can cause stalling, misfiring, poor acceleration, and the malfunction indicator lamp (MIL) to illuminate -Engine Oil – another method of checking for water in the engine is to check oil levels. Levels that are above the full line on the dipstick or signs of moisture on the dipstick generally indicate that water has seeped into the system. If the engine was running when exposed to floodwater, the oil will have an appearance similar to that of chocolate milk, a light brown color. Any signs of contamination require, at a minimum, the oil to be drained from the engine -Engine Contamination – with no obvious signs of water in the engine, it may be helpful for the estimator or APDA to ask the customer if the engine was running when flood conditions occurred. If the engine was not running, repairs may be a possibility. However, if water entered the engine, corrosion can start immediately on the cylinder walls and piston rings. The pitting from corrosion can cause the engine to burn oil and run unevenly when started again. -Hydrolock – if the vehicle was running when it entered deep water, followed by the engine shutting down, it is more likely that serious damage has occurred. Often, a condition known as ‘hydrolock’ can occur. Hydrolock is when water enters the engine and the floods the cylinder. Water, unlike air, does not compress. When the pistons try to compress water on the upstroke, a variety of problems can occur. These include: -Bearing problems -Broken seals -Bent or broken connecting rods – even a minor bend can scar the cylinder wall, and this damage may be evident by a knocking sound when the vehicle is started -Fractured head or block -Crankcase damage -Air Intake Locations – water is often brought into the engine through the air intake. Intake locations vary, but are generally higher in the engine compartment. The air intake location in the engine compartment determines the water level required to flood the engine. For example, an air intake that is lower on the vehicle will flood the engine at a much lower level compared to an air intake that is higher up -Engine Damage and Speed Considerations – if the engine locked up while the vehicle was being driven, the odds of mechanical damage are fairly high. However, if the engine was idling or being driven at very low speeds, water is slowly introduced into the system. This means that severe mechanical damage is less likely. It is important to ask the vehicle owner how the engine came to a stop. Was the owner trying to move through the water quickly, or was the vehicle at a standstill -Engine Flood Repair Options – the engine should never be started if there is evidence that moisture has entered the engine. This may hydrolock the engine. Engines exposed to clean water may be repairable. Generally this requires disconnecting the battery cables. Remove the spark plugs and turn the engine over by hand to ensure that it turns over normally – using the starter to turn the engine can cause additional damage if there are problems. Remove all moisture from the cylinders. Disassemble and clean the engine – any parts that show corrosion should be replaced -Transmission – water in the transmission can sometimes be evident by water droplets on the dipstick. Some systems do not have a dipstick. In this situation, if contamination is suspected, it is recommended to drain a small amount of fluid and have it tested for moisture contamination. If the vehicle was running, the transmission fluid will have an appearance similar to a pink milkshake. -Water Entering the Transmission – although the transmission seems like a completely sealed system, water can enter either through the dipstick, if equipped, or through a vent. All transmissions have this vent to maintain equal barometric pressure inside of the transmission. When the vehicle is exposed to flood conditions, and the water level is at or above the vent, the water will cool the unit, lowering the internal temperature and drawing water inside the transmission -Water Exposure Considerations – water that enters the transmission is difficult to simply ‘flush out’. There is some equipment available that can be used to extract the water from the system. Exposure to water may cause internal damage to the transmission. Water can dissolve the water-based glues that vehicle makers commonly use to bond friction material to the transmission clutch plates. Additionally, depending on the length of time exposed to water, the internal parts can start to corrode. Following a flood, this internal damage may require transmission to be overhauled -Other Fluid Inspection – when inspecting underhood, the levels in the power steering reservoir, brake fluid reservoir, and coolant reservoir should be checked. Excessive levels may mean that water has entered the system. Brake fluid absorbs water and does not separate. Therefore, testers are available for determining the amount of moisture in the brake fluid. However, water and power steering fluid do not mix, so water in the system will be evident. -Checking for Moisture – moisture can seep into drivetrain and suspension parts submerged in water for an extended period of time. Damaged parts may include: -CV joints -Tie rod ends -Wheel bearings -Hub assemblies -Gasoline tanks -Crankshaft and transmission seals -Front and/or rear differentials -Note: Front and/or rear differential damage is also common on vehicles that submerge the back end when launching watercraft. All contaminated fluids or grease should be changed out to prevent any further damage to the systems and the damaged parts replaced -Checking Electronic Systems – other areas of the brake system that can be affected by flood damage include the electronic systems that control the vehicle braking. These systems include the anti-lock braking system (ABS), traction control, and electronic stability control. These systems have sensors and control modules that monitor and engage the systems when necessary. Note: If brake system electronics are flood-damaged, they may not work as designed and could present a safety hazard Vehicle Interior Inspection -Trunk and Storage Tub Inspection – water often pools in lower areas of the vehicle. This includes the spare tire tub in the trunk and interior storage tubs commonly found in minivans. It is important to note that with the spare tire tub, levels may not always be as they appear. When vehicles are towed, the front end may be lifted and the water may drain to the rear of the vehicle, creating a higher water line than the rest of the vehicle. Look for consistencies throughout the vehicle when estimating the water level. Note: The truck inspection includes checking the spare tire cover. These may become waterlogged and the board material can warp or split from exposure to moisture -Doors – the height of the floodwater may be determined by examining the door trim panel for water lines in addition to any stains that may have occurred from the flooding process. However, what is difficult to examine are the parts behind the door trim panel. This includes: -The window regulator (power and manual) -Power door locks -Switches for door locks and power windows -Door lamps -Power mirror switches -Note: In some cases, there are even pressure switches for side airbag deployment. Depending on the length of exposure, some switches and window regulators may be able to be disassembled and cleaned. This will require dismantling the door and removing the interior parts to check and clean the individual parts -Seats – seats can be basic or contain multiple electronic systems that can be affected by floodwater. When inspecting the seat, items that can be damaged from flood include the seat fabric (leather or cloth) and the seat foam. If either is not properly dried, there will be a mold problem. Even if the seat looks and feels dry, the foam padding absorbs moisture. This moisture can be felt on the underside of the seat, and may even leak out of the seat cushion when it is compressed by hand -Seat Tracks – the seat track and the seat adjustments often start to rust immediately. After several weeks, if not properly repaired or replace, these items will seize up from corrosion. Other items to consider when analyzing seats include the seat position sensor and occupant classification sensor (OCS), seat heaters, airbag modules, and electronic seat adjustment motors. Any restraint system parts should be replaced if exposed to floodwater -Removing Stains from Flood-Damaged Fabrics – stains from flood damage are common interior trim pieces used on doors, B-pillars, A-pillars, and so on. Stains may also be found on carpeting and seat fabric. With the proper removal techniques and materials, stains can be removed. Common stains in carpets and seats are mostly acidic, or below 7 on the pH scale. These are generally protein-based stains and can be neutralized with an alkaline solution, such as all-purpose cleaner. Alkaline stains, such as fresh eggs or water spots, can be neutralized with a mild acid solution, such as vinegar and water. Common stains enter in a liquid, which then evaporates leaving only the pigments. The stain must be made into a liquid again so it can be easily removed. -Cleaning and Odor Removal – with the seats and carpeting removed for drying, clean out any silt or organic material from the floor and floor-mounted vents. Special cleaners, some formulated with enzymes, may be used. This type of cleaning solution can be used on fabric, plastic, metal, etc. Pressure washers with heated water can also be used to clean the removed carpeting. Once cleaned, the carpeting is left to air dry. Carpeting with a jute backing may not be salvageable. -Fogging – a fogger is a type of cleaner used to remove odor and contamination from flood vehicles. This process turns special chemicals into airborne particles that can get into all interior areas of the vehicle Vehicle Electrical Inspection -Wiring Harnesses and Connectors – while the wiring harnesses themselves are protected from water, because such wire is encased in insulation, the problems arise from floodwater contamination of the harness connector. Connectors will corrode. Even the slightest amount of corrosion can cause problems, since corrosion will increase the resistance in a circuit. Since many of the systems are low voltage/low amperage, meaning they run on millivolts and milliamps, small changes may cause the system to fail, or illuminate a MIL. If the system was energized when the flood occurred, corrosion beings immediately. Note: To avoid further corrosion, it is beneficial to disconnect the battery as soon as possible when a flooded vehicle is brought in for repair -Cleaning Connectors – the connectors can be cleaned. This requires removing water with compressed air, cleaning with a solvent, and brushing to remove any surface corrosion. Some cleaning materials are designed to dissolve the corrosion on contact. After the contacts are cleaned, they may be treated with dielectric grease to prevent any future corrosion. It is recommended to avoid using mechanical methods to clean connectors. Using an abrasive-type material to remove corrosion can scar or gouge the surface of the metal, leaving it open to further corrosion. While repairs to wiring harnesses and connectors may be possible, they will likely be limited by the duration of exposure and type of water. Note: Too much corrosion, or corrosion that has pitted the surface of the metal, may inhibit the effectiveness of any repairs -Vehicle Lamps – headlamps that were submerged often have sediment and moisture on the interior of the lens. If the lamps were on when the vehicle encountered flood conditions, it is possible the quick cool of the water may have destroyed the bulb. Additionally, the system could have short-circuited during the submersion. Similarly, this can occur with turn signals or tail lamps. Depending on the type of headlamp assembly, additional problems can arise. For example, vehicles with adaptive front lighting (headlamps that swivel or tilt), may have issues with the motors not functioning properly, problems with the wiring connectors, or problems with the sensors. -Control Modules and Computers – when writing an estimate on a vehicle, it is helpful to have knowledge of where the control modules and related system sensors are located. If the flood only reached the floor pan, that could still cause potential problems, since most of the control modules and sensors are mounted lower on the vehicle. If a control module is submerged in water for a period of time, as a general rule, it should be replaced. Vehicle computers are no different than any other computer; if water seeps into the housing they may work for a short bit after being dried out, but long-term effects of the water may lead to a malfunctioning part. -Restraint Systems – seat belt mechanisms exposed to floodwater must be replaced since they corrode over a short period of time. While the webbing may be cleaned based on the type of water, retractor mechanisms will corrode, causing a lock-up when trying to extend the seat belt. Many vehicles are equipped with seat belt pre-tensioners. When exposed to floodwater, these devices cannot be dried out, or disassembled and cleaned. Cleaning the connectors and assembly will not ensure a proper deployment in a collision. If submerged in floodwater, pre-tensioners must be replaced. -Airbag Replacements – replace any airbags if they were submerged in floodwater. There is no repair or reconditioning procedure. Generally, if the water reaches the airbags on a vehicle, the vehicle is totalled since the waterhas risen at least to the instrument panel. However, as with all restraint system parts, do not recycle or use these parts on collision-damaged vehicles -Airbag Deployment – in some instances, airbag deployment may occur in a flooded vehicle due to a short circuit in the system. This can occur from corroding contacts and a battery that is still connected. The longer the vehicle sits without being dried out, the greater the possibility for an airbag deployment. For this reason, if entering a vehicle for inspection, checking mileage, or seeing if the engine turns over, be aware that this is a possibility. If the ignition must be turned on, do not reach through the steering wheel to turn the key -Cleaning Electronics – vehicle electronic parts may include stereo equipment, LCD monitors, alarm systems, navigation systems, back-up cameras, and a number of other convenience, communication, and safety systems. There are some companies that will work to save vehicle electronics, which generally requires disassembling and drying the parts and circuits, and then applying a product that will remove any corrosion from the metal parts. However, permanent damage may occur, especially if there was an electrical current flowing through the part as it was submerged. If a part was submerged in fresh, clean water, and only for a short period of time, the likelihood of saving the part increases. Note: Cleaning flood-damaged electronics does not guarantee they will work for an extended period of time. Analyzing Theft Damage (1/2) Common Stolen Items -Two Types of Vandalism – vandalism commonly occurs in two ways. The first is when someone is intent on damaging the vehicle, either targeting a specific owner and vehicle through an act of vengeance, or through random vandalism. The second type of vandalism occurs from someone who damages the exterior when looking to gain access to the interior for the purpose of theft. This type of damage generally includes broken glass and damaged door lock assemblies. This type of damage can also be in the interior of the vehicle after access has been made -Common Stolen Items – thieves are aware of market demands. For this reason, some vehicles may be stolen for specific parts and taken to a location where the parts are removed. The vehicle is then towed or driven to a remote area and abandoned. Examples of commonly stolen items include the following: -Catalytic converters – which are often sold to scrap yards for their precious metals -Air bags – sone studies concluded there are over 75,000 airbags stolen every year -Wheels/Tires – even wheels with locking lugs can be stolen. Tools are easily available to those with the knowledge of how to remove them, and the proper contacts for accessing the tools -Note: Trucks and SUVs are often targeted for catalytic converters, as it is easy to get underneath the vehicle for removal -Smash and Grab Stolen Items – one of the more common types of theft is the smash and grab, where windows are broken to quickly take items inside. These items include: -ipods/MP3 players -Computers -Portable navigation systems -DVD players -Phones -Purses -Radar detectors -Note: Generally, items that are stolen are easy to remove and/or have a high dollar value OEM and Aftermarket Electronics -Automotive Electronics – Aftermarket – aftermarket stereo equipment, such as radio units, amplifiers, speakers, and TVs, are much easier to steal compared to factory parts, and typically do not require special codes to reactive. During damage analysis, there are steps that can be taken to verify that the vehicle was indeed equipped with certain electronics. These include: -Checking for a wiring harness that would match the description of the stolen part -Asking the vehicle owner for receipts, owner’s manual, or original packaging -Verifying dealership records for dealer-installed equipment -An inline fusible link -A permanent fixture that holds the stereo -Wiring to an amplifier -Larger gauge speaker wire -Part-specific mounting brackets, such as those used for a subwoofer -Note: When dealing with OEM equipment, it is also recommended to check the vehicle build sheet, if available, to see what options were included from the factory -Factory Stereos – in-dash items, such as factory-made stereos, are not as popular to steal as they once were and are rarely stolen. Factory-made stereos are difficult to remove from the dash, and often require specific codes from the vehicle maker to re-active once removed -Instrument Panel Damage – when analyzing theft recovery, check for damage to the instrument panel. This includes: -Damage from aftermarket radio removal -Damage from vandalism, such as cuts, tears, stains, etc. -Broken plastic on the instrument cluster -Fingerprint powder residue, which may be located throughout the vehicle, and will need to be removed Analyzing Theft Damage (2/2) Total Vehicle Theft Initial Inspection of a Recovered Stolen Vehicle -Initial Inspection – when performing an initial inspection following a break-in or theft recovery, it is important to: -Look for damage beyond the obvious area of damage -Determine how the vehicle was entered and/or started -If equipped with an alarm, try to determine how it was defeated -Verify the license plates and registration stickers on the vehicle as still correct -Check fluid levels for the vehicle, such as coolant, brakes, or transmission -Inspect the underbody to identify any damage to the drivetrain and suspension components from rough driving -Damage from Accessing the Interior – common methods thieves use to gain entry to a vehicle include accessing door lock cylinders, or working windows down or to the side to access the vehicle lock mechanism. Therefore, items commonly damaged include: -Door handles and the area around them that become scratched from pry tools, such as screwdrivers -Broken door lock cylinders and ignition cylinders -Weatherstripping and belt moldings -Door frames, which can be bent to access the door lock -Broken window regulators, which will occur when thieves push the window down hard and snap the retainer clips to the glass -Interior trim panels and seats, which are damaged from broken glass -Note: Generally, thieves prefer to keep the window intact so they do not have to sit on broken glass while driving. They may even punch of the vent window, which tends to fall on the floor -Stolen Vehicles – instead of breaking in and stealing parts of the vehicle, some vehicles are stolen and driven somewhere to be disassembled for parts. Vehicles can also be taken for joy rides, where thieves take the vehicle for a short period of time and then abandon it. These types of thefts are typically recovered within 30 days. In some instances, the vehicle may be stolen by towing the vehicle away. In this instance, its necessary to check for damage at jack points or towing points. Thieves rarely take the time to ensure the vehicle is properly attached before hauling it away -VIN Compromising – for recovered theft, it is important to check the vehicle identification number (VIN) plates to make sure that they were not altered or removed. To verify the vehicle has the proper VIN, check the multiple VIN locations on the vehicle, such as the door jamb or trunk floor. Make sure all VIN labels have the same number. To help with VIN tracking, the government developed the Motor Vehicle Theft Law Enforcement Act. This act requires makers of designated high-theft passenger vehicle lines to put the VIN on the engine, the transmission, and 12 major body parts such as fenders, doors, and hoods -Ignition Damage – steering column damage occurs from thieves trying to remove the lock cylinder. Lock cylinders are removed from the housing by: -Chiseling out the cylinder -Using a special tool to pull out the lock cylinder -Note: After the ignition lock cylinder has been removed, a screwdriver, pliers, or other custom tools can then be used to turn the ignition over and start the vehicle. This type of damage typically requires the steering column to be replaced or rebuilt -Theft Versus Prior Damage – as with traditional collision damage, it is important to differentiate between pre-existing damage and damage related to the claim. This requires looking for clues to previous damage. Check for any prior claims on the vehicle. Look for signs of age to the previous damage, such as corrosion. Also examine the consistency to the damage. For example, if there is minor damage to a panel that is not adjacent to where the majority of damage is, it is possible that it is not related to the claim. However, it is difficult in most cases to say for sure, and asking the vehicle owner is generally the only way to verify -The I-CAR Position on Fraud – though this course has focused on best practices and techniques for analyzing theft damage, it also serves to help identify potential indicators of fraud. I-CAR is not in the position to recommend how situations that might involve an element of fraud should be handled. The investigation and handling of these situations is a highly sensitive matter that must respect the relationship, rights, and obligations that exist between an insurer and its customers. All situations that involve potential indicators of fraud and related damages should be handled according to the polices, practices, and procedures of the insurance company involved -Theft and Fraud Inspection Items – there are inspection items that may raise red flags with regard to fraud. One red flag may be a clean vehicle, as thieves typically are not worried about cleanliness. Unplugged wires may also be a red flag, since thieves will usually cut wires to save time. Items that are unscrewed from their mounting may be an indication of fraud, as most items are torn out with little regard for appropriate removal methods, or damage to adjacent parts. It is important to note that these are items to consider. Fraud is a delicate issue that must be handled following specific company policy -Possible Indicators of Fraud with Vehicle Theft – indications of possible fraud regarding vehicle theft include: -Multiple accidents with no repair, or the owner has multiple deductibles -Signs of previous damage -One method that may be used to determine possible fraud is to look at digital images captured from a previous estimate and compare those images with the current claim. Other indications of possible fraud include no signs of forced entry. Typically there is a broken ignition, broken lock cylinder, and damaged door handle, door frame, or door glass. However, vehicle jacking always remains a possibility. With that type of crime, there are no signs of forced entry. For this reason, it is important to verify the vehicle condition with the claimant testimony and police report Analyzing Vandalism Damage (1/2) Analyzing Finish, Interior, Theft Recovery, and Mechanical Damage Finish Damage -Two Types of Vandalism – vandalism commonly occurs in two ways. The first is when someone is intent on damaging the vehicle, either targeting a specific owner and vehicle through an act of vengeance, or through random vandalism. The second type of vandalism occurs from someone who damages the exterior when looking to gain access to the interior for the purpose of theft. This type of damage generally includes broken glass and damaged door lock assemblies. This type of damage can also include damage to the interior of the vehicle after access has been made. -Finish Damage – one of the more common types of vandalism damage is to the vehicle finish. Depending on what was used, damage may be just to the clearcoat. In this instance, detailing or refinishing may be required depending on the depth of damage. Finish damage may also be through either the topcoats or all the finish layers. This will always require the panel to be refinished -Eggs – eggs are commonly used to vandalize vehicles. While eggs seem fairly harmless, they can etch clearcoat if left on the panel for an extended period of time or simply allowed to dry into the finish. Eggs are slightly alkaline, with a 7.6-7.8 on the pH scale, so even this small amount of alkalinity will damage the clearcoat. The impact of egg shells may chip or crack the finish, depending on how hard they are thrown at the vehicle. Damage not related to the impact can generally be avoided with immediate removal of the egg yolks and egg whites -Key Scratches – key scratches are generally deep scratches into the vehicle finish. When analyzing repair options for key scratches, the depth of the scratch determines repair options. If only the upper layer of the clearcoat is scratched, and a fingernail does not catch on the scratch, buffing may be a repair option. Deeper scratches, where a fingernail catches on the scratch, require refinishing. This is generally the more common type of key scratch. Note: Vehicle makers may provide a recommendation regarding the amount of clearcoat that can be removed from a panel. Limits are put in place to prevent excessive amounts of clearcoat from being removed. If too much clearcoat is removed, paint failure may occur. Most vehicle makers recommend not removing more than 0.5 mil of clearcoat. This will require monitoring the finish thickness during the buffing process -Spray Paint – spray-painted graffiti is a very deliberate form of vandalism. Spray paint removal products are available to remove graffiti from a finish. Spray paint, or graffiti, on a vehicle surface may require a variety of repair techniques based on the spray paint chemistry. Common types of spray paint are made from: -Acrylic -Epoxy -Latex -Note: In addition to vandalism, other types of paint-related damage may include house paint, road paint, or other types of industrial overspray -Spray Paint Removal – graffiti removal may require using detailing clay. Detailing clay used with a water-based lubricant can be used to remove some forms of overspray and paint on non-porous (smooth) surfaces, such as glass and metal. In some instances, a pressure washer may be used to remove latex-based paint. Another option for removing graffiti spray paint, permanent marker, or other staining products include specifically designed products such as graffiti remover. These are either petroleum-based or water-based. It is important to note that before using this type of product, it should be verified that it is compatible with automotive finishes. When using chemical cleaners, always test the effects of the cleaner on an inconspicuous area to determine that the surface will not become damaged from the cleaner. -Miscellaneous Fluids – other types of fluid that may be used to vandalize a vehicle include: -Battery acid -Paint thinner -Brake fluid -Note: These should be removed as soon as possible to prevent etching the clearcoat or even deeper into the finish. Generally, materials such as this will require neutralizing the fluid, followed by refinishing the panel -What Custom Finish Repairs may Require – if the clearcoat scratches cannot be repaired with buffing, repair to custom finishes may require determining the refinishing process, and finding documentation about the finish from the company that painted the vehicle. Custom finishes may also require stripping an entire panel. Unlike a traditional finish, custom finishes are difficult to blend, such as in situations where the color is a custom mix or has a very detailed air brush design that cannot be duplicated without a complete refinish Interior Damage -Common Damage to Interior Items – vandals that gain access to the vehicle interior can do considerable damage. Common types of damage include: -Instrument panel, trim, and wiring (when the radio is stolen) -Cut/scratched door panels from the broken glass -Slashed seats/upholstery -Slashed airbag covers. There are no repair options for this type of damage. The slashed airbag covers require replacement of the airbag module -Stains on seats -Stained or cut carpeting -Contamination from various material -Note: International water damage is also a common type of vandalism. This may require replacement of carpeting, as oftentimes it is difficult to completely dry the matting that is attached to the carpeting. This can result in mold problems. Also, the floor of many vehicles now contains control modules and wiring harnesses that can be damaged from excessive moisture -Torn Seat Cover Damage Analysis – seats that are slashed may require replacement of the: -Fabric, if it is sold separately from the seat -Seat cushion -Seat cushion and fabric, if they are sold together -Seat cushion heaters -Side impact airbag module, if it is integrated into the seat back -Note: When replacing the fabric or seat cushion, some vehicle makers recommend disabling the passive restraint system if the side airbag is integrated in with the seat. Many passenger seats are equipped with an occupant detection system that includes a bladder that can be damaged if punctured. This would require replacement of the occupant detection parts and recalibration of the system. Which parts are replaced is dependent on the vehicle maker. Reference the vehicle service information for a specific list. Even if the detection system is not damaged, some vehicle makers recommend replacing the sensor if replacing the cushion. Note: Stains may also require removal. The method used depends on the material that caused the stain -Damage from Accessing the Interior – damaged door handles or ignition cylinders are indications that the vehicle was forcefully accessed and driven -Gaining Vehicle Access – gaining vehicle access is one of the first steps in stealing a vehicle. Common methods include accessing a door lock cylinder, or working with windows down or pushing them to the side to access the lock mechanism. Therefore, items commonly damaged during vehicle access include: -Door handles and surrounding areas, which can become scratched from pry tools -Door lock cylinders -Broken ignition cylinders -Weatherstripping and belt moldings -Door frames, which can be bent from being pulled out to access the door locks -Broken window regulators. Sometimes thieves will push the window down hard and snap the retainer clips to the glass -Other Access Methods – in some instances, the vehicle may be stolen by towing the vehicle away. In this instance, its necessary to check for damage at jack points or towing points. Thieves rarely take the time to ensure the vehicle is properly attached before hauling it away. When stealing vehicles, generally, the thieves prefer to keep the window intact so they do not have to sit on the glass while driving. Some may punch of the vent window, as that would tend to fall on the floor; however, this missing piece of glass may be an indication to police that the vehicle was stolen Theft Recovery and Mechanical Damage -Initial Inspection – when doing an initial inspection following a theft recovery, it is important to look for vehicle damage beyond the obvious areas of damage. Determine how the vehicle was entered and started. If equipped with an alarm system, determine how the system was bypassed. Check for cut battery cables, as this could be an indication of how the alarm was bypassed. This is also recommended for break-in thefts. Look for missing items from the interior. Verify that the license plates are still on the vehicle. Make sure the vehicle registration stickers are still on the license plates. These can be taken off and reattached to another plate. The entire vehicle should be inspected for damage, including checking the fluid levels to confirm that all fluids are full and have not been contaminated, and checking the underbody, including the exhaust and suspension, looking for signs of damage from impact, hard driving, or going off road -Under Hood Damage – common items that may be damaged under hood include wire harnesses and radiator hoses, which both can be cut. It is rare to find under hood items that are vandalized in this manner, as more vehicle designs enclose the engine compartment. This generally requires access to the vehicle interior so that the hood latch can be disengaged. With any vandalism damage, it is important to perform a complete inspection inside the vehicle, outside the vehicle, under hood, and underbody. Covering all areas ensures that any hidden damage is found and analyzed -Tire and Wheel Damage – if one tire is damaged, there may be damage to others, thus all four tires should be checked. When analyzing theft recovery, check the tires and wheels for excessive tire wear, tire wear spots, gouges, scuffed, dented, or gouged wheels, and damaged or missing hubcaps. Also, check the spare tire to make sure it is still in place. Slashed tires are tires that have been intentionally punctured through various methods, such as knives and ice picks, or cut valve stems. Tire repairability depends on the damage location. For example, the tread area is sometimes repairable, but sidewalls are not repairable due to safety concerns. Also, for vehicles equipped with tire pressure monitoring systems, the system sensor on the wheel should be checked to make sure it was not damaged. To verify that the tire pressure monitoring system is still functioning, turn the vehicle on and make sure the tire pressure monitoring system warning light is off Analyzing Vandalism Damage (2/2) Fuel Contamination Damage Fuel Contamination – contaminating the fuel tank has been a popular form of vandalism for some time. Contaminants that have been used by vandals include: -Sand -Sugar -Dirt -Water -Fire extinguisher residue -Soda water -Varnish and paint -Note: most solid contaminants do not dissolve in gasoline or diesel fuel, and will clog the fuel filter and fuel injectors. Some may even damage the fuel pump, but rarely do larger, granular-type contaminants make it to the engine. However, some contaminants, such as soda water, can make it to the engine and disable it. -Obtaining a Fuel Sample – to determine if there is fuel contamination, take a fuel sample from a fuel line leading out of the fuel tank into the engine. When extracting the sample: -Fill a clean glass container with fuel -Operate the fuel pump to gather 0.5 L (one pint) of fluid -Allow the sample to sit for 5 minutes or longer to allow sediment to gather, if it exists -Note: When servicing fuel systems, if any fuel spills on your skin, immediately wash the affected area with soap and water. If your clothing becomes saturated with fuel, remove the clothing immediately. Always wear safety glasses, ventilate the area to control fuel vapor buildup, and plug or cap all fuel system openings when removing or disconnecting fuel system parts. It is also recommended to clean up spilled fuel immediately, and to avoid sparks and other sources of ignition. -Determining Fuel Contamination – fuel contaminants are just about anything that can make it down a fuel filler neck. When determining fuel contamination, check the fuel filter for large amounts of solid contaminants. Gasoline is a preservative and items like sugar will not dissolve. It may be necessary to have the fuel analyzed by an independent laboratory. There are generally three levels of testing available. More basic, or level one tests, simply determine that the fuel is contaminated. More extensive testing, or a level three test, will indicate the type of contaminant. The extent of testing varies depending on the desired results. Note: If doing outside testing, it is recommended to use the container provided by the testing facility to ensure the container is free of contaminants that may taint the results. When shipping fuel samples to a testing laboratory, technicians must follow the proper shipping regulations regarding transportation of hazardous fluids -Wrong Fuel – in some instances, fuel contamination is not deliberate. People may inadvertently mix the wrong fuels at a gas station. For example, some may mix E85 and E10. Unless the vehicle is specifically equipped for E85 fuel, using E85 can cause drivability conditions and deterioration of some fuel system parts. People may also inadvertently mix gasoline and diesel, and water and gasoline. While the vehicle owner generally does not add water to the tank, some service stations can have leaky storage tanks that allow water to flow into the supply. Some vehicle makers may have specific test kits that allow technicians to check for the presence of excess alcohol or water in the fuel system. Some vehicle makers may also have specific recommendations and tools for purging the fuel system of E85 and other contaminants. Note: Many vehicle owners mistakenly add the Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF) to the fuel tank vs the DEF tank which causes a contamination issues with the fuel, and often leads to replacing a number of fuel system components. -Engine Oil Contamination – another type of vandalism that may occur is adding contaminants directly into the crankcase to lock up the engine. This requires access to the engine compartment, so this type of damage is less common. Common contaminants added to the crankcase are sand or dirt. Some indications that there may have been foul play may be residual sand or dirt around the oil cap. To verify whether contamination actually occurred, engine oil may be analyzed for contaminants, similar to how the fuel is analyzed.
