If water is in the crankcase, it should be verified that it was intentional. This can be done by checking for a cracked block, damaged head gasket, or other areas that would allow moisture into the engine. As with fuel, not all contamination is deliberate. Some cases are accidental, for example adding the wrong fluid to a reservoir (power steering fluid added to the brake fluid reservoir, windshield wiper fluid added to the power steering fluid reservoir). Improper fluid can cause damage. For example, the wrong brake fluid can disintegrate the seals on some systems Auto Physical Damage Appraiser (APDA) Administration APDA Administrative Duties Assignments/Planning Routes -Introduction to the Job Duties of the APDA – in addition to estimating responsibilities, an auto physical damage appraiser (APDA) is commonly assigned administrative responsibilities. These duties include: -Downloading assignments -Planning routes -Creating and maintaining estimating profiles -Addressing customer concerns -Maintaining equipment -Download and Review Assignments – assignments are typically received electronically through the estimating system, but can come in other forms, including fax, email, and phone. In some cases, APDA daily routes may be pre-planned and customer appointments already made. However, in some situations it may be necessary to contact the customers to schedule appointments and plan the route for the day. It is important to review assignments for the next business day upon receipt to identify the location of each vehicle, the type of inspection, and any urgent assignments that may need to be treated as a priority. Next, plan your route for the day to maximize efficiency, taking any urgent assignments into consideration Creating and Maintaining Estimating Profiles -Estimating Systems and Profiles – estimating systems utilize profiles to store and manage information for insurance companies, insurance claim offices, and repair shops. Depending on company policy, areas of the system, such as insurance company and insurance claim office profiles, may not be accessible to an APDA. For example: if the APDA’s company manages an insurance company’s claim office profiles at a corporate level, the APDA will be able to create and edit shop profiles, but may not be able to edit the insurance company’s claim office profiles -What is a Shop Profile? – a shop profile allows an ADPA to load specific shop information into the estimating system that can be recalled when writing an estimate. This avoids the need to re-enter shop information each time that shop is used. Information housed in the shop profile includes: -Shop name -Address -Phone/fax numbers -Website/email address -Shop tax ID/business license -Shop labor and tax rates profile -Other shop-specific fields -What is an Insurance/Claims Office Profile? – an insurance/claim office profile is similar to a shop profile, but allows for creation of profiles for each insurance company and, if necessary, individual claim offices for each insurance company. insurance/claim office profiles typically include: -The insurance company name -The claim office ID -Address/contact information -A labor and tax rate profile -A part code table -Note: Part code tables typically include items found on collision repair estimates that may not be included in the estimating system databases. As with the shop profile, all profile information will be available in the estimate when the proper insurance/claim profile is selected Addressing Customer Concerns -Addressing Common Customer Complaints – an APDA will occasionally be called upon to assist with resolution of customer concerns. Typically, these concerns will be related to repair quality or to provide a second opinion on claim-related damages -Repair Quality – a vehicle owner may have a concern about color match, finish quality, or panel fit. In these situations, an APDA may be called upon to meet with the vehicle owner and/or repair shop to inspect the vehicle and determine appropriate actions necessary to resolve the customers concern -Related vs Unrelated Prior Damage – an APDA may also be called upon to assist with identifying claim-related vs unrelated damages. It can sometimes be difficult to differentiate between related and unrelated damages. When called upon for this type of inspection, it is important to inspect the vehicle and all damages very closely for clues that may help make this determination. For example: A vehicle that was involved in an accident 2 days ago would typically not show significant rust in the area of damage. It is possible, depending on climate conditions, for surface rust to form in 2 days since the accident, but significant amounts of rust would not be expected -Customer Concern Management – when handling customer complaints, the APDA must be equipped with the necessary skills to resolve the customers concern. Successful conflict resolution requires managing emotions, empathy, active listening, and assertiveness -Managing Emotions – when dealing with customer concerns, is it important to always remain calm and remember that customers with concerns may be emotional about the situation. These emotions are typically not consciously controlled and are often linked to past experiences and memories. While the customers complaint may be valid, the emotion displayed may not necessarily be related to the current situation, but rather to past experiences -Empathy – an APDA should use empathy when dealing with a customers complaint. Begin to understand the customers point of view, even if it differs. Remain professional and respectful -Active Listening – active listening is more than not interrupting a customer talking. Its paying close attention to what the other person is saying. While a customer is relaying a complaint or concern, use appropriate verbal and nonverbal communication, such as maintaining eye contact, nodding your head in understanding, and saying “yes”, “okay”, and “I understand”, at appropriate times. These elements ensure the customer feels heard, respected, and that you are interested in hearing what is said -Assertiveness – assertiveness is the ability to express and defend opinions and thoughts firmly and calmy. Being assertive is not becoming aggressive with the customer, nor is it passively accepting an idea or action that is known to be incorrect in an effort to keep the peace -Resolution and Documentation – it is important to ensure the customers concerns have been resolved and that all conversations and agreed actions to resolve the customers concerns are documented in the file notes. It is also important to recognize when the interaction is not going well and when the concern needs to be escalated to a manager for assistance -Fulfill Special Requests – occasionally, an APDA will receive requests for assistance with activities outside the scope of a standard damage appraisal -Picking Up Police/Accident Reports – picking up police or accident reports requires coordination with the responsible police department to confirm the report is ready, and then forwarding the report to the file handler at the insurance company. common information required when requesting a copy of a police/accident report includes the name(s) of the party involved and the date the accident occurred -Obtaining Accident Scene Information – an ADPA may be called upon to perform an accident scene investigation. This may require driving to the location of the accident, taking photos, preparing a diagram of the scene, and/or monitoring and recording traffic light timing and sequence. Photographs of the scene will typically need to show what the driver would have seen based on their direction of travel, along with photos from any other directions of traffic showing what the other drivers would have seen. If a diagram is requested, this will typically require taking measurements of the accident scene showing any tire tracks and documenting the distance at which the photos were taken from the intersection or landmarks. Monitoring and recording traffic light sequence may also be requested and will typically require documenting the sequence in which the traffic signals appear, from each direction, and the length of time each light is illuminated -Respond to Communications – an APDA may receive communication in the form of phone calls, emails, fax, voice mail, etc. regardless of the method of communication, all inquiries must be addressed in a timely fashion. An APDA must evaluate each inquiry to identify those that can be addressed using standard SOPs, those that may need to be treated as urgent or emergency situations, and those that will require escalation to a manager for assistance Maintaining Equipment -Maintaining Company Equipment – in some cases, an APDA may be required to conduct maintenance on company equipment. -Software Updates – while many of the systems are now cloud-based and update automatically, some estimating systems will require a software update. This may require a manual upgrade using a DVD provided by the data provider on a monthly or bi-monthly basis -Estimating Tools – estimating tools must be inspected to ensure proper operation. These tools include: -Cameras -Phones -Flashlights -Tread depth gauges -Measuring equipment Mirrors -Company Records – APDAs need to maintain logs based on company guidelines. Some of these logs include: -Vehicle mileage logs -Time logs -Payment logs Basic Brake System Overview -Basic Brake System Overview – a vehicle in motion has kinetic energy. A brake system applies friction at each wheel to slow down or stop vehicle motion. The friction turns kinetic energy into heat, which is absorbed and dissipated in the brake system parts. Service brake systems use hydraulic pressure to slow or stop wheel movement. Hydraulic fluid is used because it does not compress. Therefore, when pressure is exerted on the fluid, it is transmitted equally in all directions in the system, regardless of distance. A typical basic brake system is made up of all the following -Brake rotor -Brake drum -Brake caliper -Wheel cylinder -Master cylinder -Lines and hoses -Brake fluid -Brake fluid reservoir -Basic Brake System Operation – pressure is created in the brake system by applying pressure to the brake pedal. This creates hydraulic pressure in the master cylinder by forcing the piston against the fluid. The master cylinder creates hydraulic pressure in the brake lines. The brake lines carry hydraulic pressure to the wheel cylinders and calipers, which change hydraulic pressure into motion. The piston in the caliper or wheel cylinder moves outward as a result of the hydraulic pressure. This stops the vehicle by forcing the friction material against the rotor or drum. * Modern vehicles with anti-lock brakes will not use a proportioning valve. The distribution of brake pressure to each wheel will be controlled by the anti-lock brake system. -Basic Brake System Inspection – when inspecting the brake system for damage, be sure to inspect all brake system components including the brake rotors, drums, master cylinder, booster, hoses, lines, and parking brake system. It is important to inspect the braking system closely to identify collision-related damage vs. maintenance items. If brake system issues are identified that do not appear to be collision-related, these items must be fully documented and reviewed with the customer. If the customer elects not to have these required services performed, the final documents signed by the vehicle owner should include a summary of the necessary repairs and the customer’s decision to not complete the recommended repairs. * Leaking brake fluid indicates damage to the brake system. Trace the leak to its source to locate the damaged part. -Basic Brake System Inspection, cont’d – it is always important to include inspection of the brake pedal and fasteners as part of your brake system inspection. Some vehicles may be equipped with a safety feature that allows the brake pedal to drop away in a collision situation. This creates additional space and reduces potential impact to the driver’s legs. -Brake Warning Light – a warning light on the dash will likely illuminate if the hydraulic system has low fluid levels -Master Cylinder Description – parts of a master cylinder include: - The reservoir, which supplies the master cylinder with fluid. Some systems have a remote reservoir that uses a low-pressure supply line to supply fluid to the master cylinder. -The pushrod, which connects the brake pedal and power assist -Primary and secondary pistons, which force pressure into the brake lines -* As the piston is moved by the brake pedal pushrod, the brake fluid forward of the piston seals is forced into the brake lines, raising the pressure in the lines. Vehicles are built with multi-circuit braking systems. These systems have front and rear, or diagonally separated brake systems to ensure partial braking is retained in case of a brake line failure. -Collision Damage to Master Cylinders – collision damage to the master cylinder can be difficult to detect. When inspecting the master cylinder for damage, common types of damage may include -Broken lines -Detached reservoirs -Damaged housings -Dirt contamination -Damage to the body mounting locations or cowl panel -Brake Assist Systems – Vacuum and Hydraulic are two common automotive brake assist systems -Vacuum assist brakes have a vacuum assist that contains a diaphragm. Vacuum assist brakes apply vacuum to one side of the diaphragm and atmospheric pressure to the other. Vacuum is obtained from the engine intake manifold. They may require an additional vacuum pump to supply adequate vacuum on some engines -Hydraulic power, is typically done where engine vacuum is a concern, such as diesel engines, or vans where available space is a concern. Vehicles with an integral anti-lock brake system (ABS) use brake fluid under pressure to assist in the application of the brakes, Vacuum assist on the left. Hydraulic assist on the right -Collision Damage to Power Assists – collision damage to power assists include: -Hydraulic leaks -Vacuum leaks -Visible damage to the unit -Possible internal damage -* When repairing collision-damaged brake systems, assist units are usually not repaired, they are replaced when damaged. When installing a brake booster, be sure to refer to vehicle maker service procedures and requirements to ensure the pushrod is properly adjusted to avoid improper braking. -Damaged Power Assists – hard pedal can be described as excessive pressure required to stop a vehicle. Hard pedal can be caused by collision damage to power assists. While the engine is running, inspect the brake system for brake pedal feel. If the pedal feels too hard, it may indicate a booster that is not working or has a vacuum leak. Additional symptoms of damage to power assist units may include. -A hissing noise -Sensitive brakes -A slow releasing pedal -Pedal chatter -Incorrect engine idle (vacuum leak) -Disc Brake Assembly – key parts of a disc brake assembly include the: Caliper assembly and disc brake pads, Rotor and splash shield. -Caliper assembly – -the caliper assembly is attached to the spindle assembly and contains the -Caliper piston (s) -Caliper piston seal -Caliper piston dust boot -Bleeder screw -Hardware used with the caliper assembly includes the -Spring clip, which holds the inboard pad to the piston -Caliper bushings, which allow movement along the anchoring bolts -Anti-squeal shims -* Do not apply the brakes with the caliper or rotor removed -Disc Brake Pads -Brake pad replacement considerations include the amount of material remaining on the pad. Each vehicle maker will have recommendations indicating when brake material should be replaced. Depending on whether the friction material is riveted or bonded affects when the brake material is replaced. When inspecting brake material thickness, a part with riveted brake material will require replacement when more material is remaining as compared to bonded brake material. This is because the attaching rivets will contact the rotor before the pad will wear away. Check the condition of the brake lining. Look for signs of contamination, cracks, or deterioration. -Rotor and Splash Shield – -A rotor may -Be attached to the wheel spindle -Have vents for cooling -Be placed over the studs on the wheel hub -Be held in place by the road wheel or secured with screws to the hub assembly -A splash shield -Is bolted to the steering knuckle on the front brakes -Minimizes water, dirt, and dust from accumulating on the brake assembly -May be designed to direct air flow to help cool the brakes -Collision Damage to Disc Brakes – Collision damage to the caliper assembly may include bends, shifted slides, torn seals, or broken mounts. Damage to disc brake pads may include contamination, cracks, bends, or delamination. Damage to fasteners and other hardware may cause them to be bent or broken. Damage or wear to the caliper assembly can cause a number of problems. These include -Pulling during braking -Fluid leaks -A soft pedal -Dragging -Brake failure -Rotor Inspection – when inspecting brake rotors, check for -Bent mounting areas -Cracks and scoring -Glazing or corrosion -Improper thickness -* Inspect rotors for hardware and fasteners damage or warpage. Warpage of brake rotors is measured by lateral runout measurements. Runout can be identified by making measurements with dial indicators. * Improper tightening of the wheel fasteners can cause brake rotors to warp. -Brake Pads – when inspecting the brake system, the condition of the friction material should be checked. When parts must be replaced, the replacement alternatives must be carefully considered. -Inspecting Other Brake Hardware -Check other brake hardware for -Broken holding clips -Damaged, missing, or corroded attaching bolts -Dry caliper bushing -Check splash shield for -Bends or cracks -Excessive dirt and debris -* Check wheel mating surfaces for damage or debris – if unaddressed, vibrations and/or braking system issues may arise. -Replacement Part Options -When servicing brakes, it is important to use parts recommended by the vehicle maker. Using pads that are not recommended may cause a vehicle to brake differently than designed. Special pads with electronic wear sensors are required for vehicles that electronically monitor friction material wear. These pads use a wire in the friction material that creates an open in the circuit as the pads wear. When an open occurs, the driver is notified that it is time for replacement. -Drum Brake Assemblies – include the drum, backing plate, wheel cylinder, brake shoes, springs and hardware, and automatic mechanical (star wheel) adjuster. -Backing Plate – is bolted to the axle. This part is a fixture for attaching other brake parts -Brake Shoe Linings – are either riveted or bonded to a metal shoe. May contain asbestos. May contain metallic particles to increase stopping power. Have a stamped and reinforced steel backing for attaching other brake hardware. Are often designed to be mounted facing forward for the leading shoe or rearward for the trailing shoe. -Springs and Hardware – Types of springs and hardware used in a drum brake system include: -A shoe anchor pin, which is attached to the backing plate, and is used to transfer the braking torque from the shoes to the backing plate -Brake Drum – -Encloses the brake mechanical parts -Fits over the wheel studs -Is help in place by hub bearings, retaining screws, or a road wheel -May have fins for cooling -May be aluminum with a steel or iron friction surface insert -Mechanical (Star Wheel) Adjuster) – the star wheel adjuster has left-hands threads on one side of the vehicle and right-hand threads for the other side. Do not switch these adjusters from side to side or the rear brake will end up out of adjustment and the driver will experience a sinking pedal. -Return springs, used to bring the shoes back into position after braking and releasing hydraulic pressure. -Shoe hold-down springs, used to mount the shoes to the backing plate. These hold the shoes in place while still allowing some movement. -The parking brake actuator lever works with the parking brake cable to push the brake shoes against the brake drum when the parking brake is applied. -Collision Damage to Drum Brakes -Twisted, cracked, or out-of-round drums -Bent shoes -Contaminated or missing friction materials -Broken or stretched springs -Bent hardware -Leaking or cracked wheel cylinders -Damage to backing plates -Inspecting Drum Brakes – symptoms of worn or damaged drums include -A vibrating brake pedal, indicating a possible out of round condition -A low pedal where the adjuster is frozen due to corrosion or collision damage -Grabbing or overly sensitive brakes, indicating possible contaminated friction material -Abnormal noises -Pull or drag -The parking brake is not operating properly -* If there is suspected damage, remove the brake drum for inspection of all brake parts -Checking the Drum and Backing Plate – -Check the drum for -Cracks around the lug nut holes -Cracks in the friction surface -Black or blue discoloration that shows excessive heat -Scores or grooves in the surface -A wear pattern, which may indicate uneven shoe contact -* If a brake drum is cracked, it must be replaced. Minor scratches or grooves and minor discoloration or signs of overheating can be repaired by resurfacing. -Check the backing plate for -Bends in the plate -Proper parts attachment -A leaking wheel cylinder -A leaking axle seal -The condition of the hardware -Checking the Brake Linings and Wheel Cylinder -The brake linings should be checked for -Even lining wear pattern -Lining thickness -Broken or missing lining sections -Cracks in linings -Reversed (leading and trailing) linings -The wheel cylinder should be checked for -Proper contact with shoes -Leaks behind the seals -Checking the Springs and Hardware -Check the springs and hardware for -Damage -Stretched springs -Parts that are out of position -Check the adjusting mechanism for -Correct positioning -Proper movement -Excessively corroded threads -A binding adjustment lever -Check the parking brake lever for -Ease of movement -Correct positioning -An attached cable -Effectiveness -Parking Brakes – parking brakes apply brake pressure to the rear wheels using a foot-actuated floor pedal or hand lever. Brake pressure is typically applied using a mechanical cable attaching the pedal or lever to the rear brakes -Drum Parking Brakes – the drum parking brake is actuated by the parking brake lever or pedal, mechanically forcing both shoes outward against the brake drum. Return springs pull the shoes away from contacting the drum after the pedal or lever is released. -Electronic Parking Brakes – There are two basic designs for electronic parking brake systems. -The first design still uses cables to actuate the parking brakes. Cables from the wheels attach to a motor that pulls and releases similarly to the way a manual lever pulls and releases. -The second design uses actuators attached directly to the rear calipers. In this case, a signal controller will relay power to the motors to clamp the brake pads against the rotors. In most cases, this type of system will have a switch to activate the parking brake, but some systems activate the parking brake when the transmission is shifted into park. -* On some vehicles with caliper-mounted motors, the electronic parking brake can double as a hill-hold for vehicles with manual transmissions. -Drum-In-Hat Parking Brakes – drum-in-hat parking brakes have separate regular and parking brake systems. These parking brakes actuate the regular brakes hydraulically and the parking brake mechanically. Drum-in-hat parking brakes actuate the parking brake by mechanically operating a small drum brake just outside of the hub and inside the rotor. -Inspecting Parking Brakes – the parking brake pedal or lever and cables should be inspected for wear or damage. A parking brake that is worn or damaged often will not hold a vehicle stationary when parked on an incline. Cable adjustment may be required to determine if damage is present -when inspecting the parking brake pedal or lever, verify the parking brake -Engages easily -Holds the brake in place -Releases easily -Does not bind -Brake drag may also be a symptom of a faulty parking brake system. This can be caused by -Damaged or corroded brake parts -A bent, collapsed, or corroded parking brake cable -Bent or corroded parking brake pivots or brackets. -Brake Line Ends – the brake lines and hoses transmit fluid pressure from the master cylinder to the calipers or wheel cylinders. Brake line ends may be ISO flare fittings or double flare fittings, which are typically found on older model vehicles. Brake lines are made of double-wall steel and must be carefully formed and routed to avoid creating fluid restrictions. Brake hoses connect rigid brake lines to moveable parts, and they are flexible to allow movement with the wheel and suspension system. -Types of Brake Fluid – common brake fluid types include DOT 3, DOT 4, and DOT 5. * Do not allow brake fluid to come in contact with painted surfaces. If this happens, wash the area thoroughly with water. -DOT 3 Brake Fluid – DOT 3 brake fluid is non-petroleum based, but has additives that aid internal system lubrication and prevent corrosion. It maintains its viscosity in high temperatures. DOT 3 brake fluid should not be stored after a container is opened and is designed to absorb moisture. Moisture in a braking system can vaporize, creating air in the system and lowering the brake fluid boiling point. This process can cause corrosion on brake system parts. -DOT 4 Brake Fluid – has a higher boiling point than DOT 3. Can be mixed with DOT 3. Absorbs more moisture than DOT 3. -DOT 5 Brake Fluid – is silicone-based. Does not absorb moisture. Compresses more than DOT 3 or DOT 5. Is not compatible with DOT 3 or DOT – do not mix. Is used and recommended by only a few vehicle makers. Is purple in color. Basic Electronics for Estimators (1/2) Electrical Systems and Basic Circuit Flow Understanding the System -Communication – estimators and Auto Physical Damage Appraiser (ADPAs) should have a basic understanding of electrical circuits and automotive electrical systems to communicate issues with the vehicle owner and the repair facility technician. This understanding will also aid in the ability to make an accurate assessment of damage and communicate electrical diagnostic and repair concerns. Estimators and ADPAs are not responsible for performing electronic diagnostics, testing, or repairs; however, a basic understanding of how common electrical repair processes are completed is beneficial -I-CAR Troubleshooting Flowchart – the I-CAR Troubleshooting Flowchart lists the five steps to solving an electrical problem. Although damage analysis will not require an in-depth knowledge of these steps, it is helpful to know the systematic method of troubleshooting. The I-CAR Troubleshooting steps are: -1) Define the problem -2) Know the system -3) Find the cause -4) Make the repair -5) Check the system -Note: Typically, this flowchart is not used for damage analysis purposes; however, it is important to consider these throughout this course -Defining the Problem – finding an electrical problem can be a challenge, as circuits run behind and under trim panels. To complete this task, an estimator should use the collision advantage, as well as other visual cues, to define the problem. One of the major estimators run across is the lack of power to the system. This may be either a dead or a low battery. Without a proper charge, its very difficult to do any damage analysis to electrical systems if the proper power is not available. -Use the Collision Advantage – an extremely valuable strategy for finding electrical problems is the collision advantage. Since automotive electrical systems are expansive, locating the source of an electrical issue for a vehicle that has not sustained any damage can be a timely and difficult task. After a collision, a logical starting point for locating the source of an electrical problem is in the area of the collision. This is the collision advantage. If a vehicle is brought into a mechanical repair facility with an electrical problem, the entire vehicle may still need to be diagnosed -Test Electrical Functionality – if the battery has enough charge, test all of the electrical loads by: -1) Turning the headlights on and off -2) Operating the horn -3) Turning on the radio -4) Switching the cooling fan and the heater to all the settings -5) Operating all the switches in the doors -6) Turning on and off interior accessory lamps -7) Operating all of the power accessories -Note: Note any lit warning lamps on the instrument panel, which electrical loads work properly, and which ones do not. Loads that do not work may have circuit commonalities -Rule Out a Dead Battery – if no electrical parts are working as designed, the battery may be the source of the issue. If the battery has a low charge, or there is wiring damage that stops the electrical flow, none of the lights or power accessories will operate. It is also likely that the odometer will not be readable. A partially discharged battery may be recharged with an appropriate battery charger, however, it is important to know the safety precautions when doing this. Note: Jump packs do not recharge a battery that is dead or low -Inspect Battery Safety Terminals – some vehicle makers have a safety device installed in the positive battery cable of some vehicles. This battery safety terminal (BST) is a self-contained explosive-type device that deploys during a collision; usually when the front airbags deploy. BST deployment creates an open in the positive cable connection, which stops the flow of power on the primary starter cable, reducing the risk of fire. Power is still maintained to some circuits. Note: The entire cable or a section of the cable requires replacement following the BST activation -Identify Spilled Battery Acid – a visual inspection under hood may reveal spilled battery/sulfuric acid. This acid will burn away wiring insulation and connector housings, leaving wires exposed. It will also corrode any part that it comes in contact with. Due to the fact that, in its liquid form, battery acid may travel, specific time, attention, and considerations to neutralize and clean up a battery acid spill must be accounted for in the estimate. Corrosion protection should be restored to any affected areas -Electrical vs Electronic Systems – an electrical system refers to the load, path, power source, etc. that make up the electrical circuit. This would include wires, connectors, switches, resistors, power, and ground. An electronic system includes a control module, all of the parts it controls (loads), and parts that gather information for the control module (inputs and sensors), connected together. For example, the electronic stability control system (ECS) consists of the ESC module, wheel speed sensors, yaw rate sensor, steering wheel angle sensor, and the anti-lock braking system (ABS) module -Know the System – knowing the system, which is the second step in the troubleshooting flowchart, may require in-depth service information. Even a very simple circuit, such as a horn, could have some complicated subsystems, like an alarm system routed in with the horn circuit. -Wiring Diagram – a wiring diagram is essentially a road map of how the parts are connected together. Although a wiring diagram helps in understanding a circuit, the diagram only shows what makes up the circuit path. It does not show the size of the parts, the lengths of the wires, or the location of the circuit in the vehicle -Part Locator Diagram – part locator diagrams may be available on the service website. These are line art or photos of where the parts of the circuit are located. Part locator diagrams are helpful after a collision, as it is an opportunity to use the collision advantage. Paired with the diagram, you can identify parts or wiring that are located near the site of the collision, and use that information to identify the source of the electrical problem -Power and Ground Distribution Diagram – the power and ground distribution diagrams show the power source and the circuits that share common goods. Knowing which circuits share common power feeds and common grounds will dictate what other loads to check. For example, if the horn, dome light, and accessory power outlet are all powered by one fuse and the horn does not work, check to see if the dome light and the accessory outlet work. If none of these are working, perhaps the fuse is blown. If these three share one ground location and none of these parts are working, the ground may be the issue. Check the area of the collision to see if the ground cable is there, and if it has been damaged -Diagnostic Flowchart – troubleshooting a complex, computer-controlled system generally requires the technician to follow a diagnostic flowchart from the vehicle maker service information. Flowcharts contain steps directing the technician to perform specific tasks, such as making electrical measurements. If Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs) are present, service information will likely have a corresponding flowchart. If no DTCs are present, then symptom charts may be available to help diagnose the problem -Technical Service Bulletin – Technical Service Bulletins (TSBs) are issued by a vehicle maker when there are several occurrences of an unanticipated problem. TSBs are deemed valuable, as many times the subject of a TSB will be a common electrical problem with a particular make or model. The TSB may include a short explanation of how to correct the problem. Diagnostic flowcharts and TSBs do not take into account the fact that the vehicle was just involved in a collision. Before turning to a diagnostic flowchart, the collision technician should use the collision advantage to inspect the damaged area for pinched or broken wires, loose grounds, and possible shorts. In the end, no matter how complicated the circuit, the problem is usually an open, short, or a bad ground Understanding the Basic Circuit Flow -Basic Parts of a Circuit – electrical parts are connected into a continuous path of wiring, which could stretch the entire length of the vehicle. The three necessary parts for a circuit to be a complete working circuit are the power source, the load, and the path. Without one of those three, there is no circuit. -Power Source – a vehicles battery is the power source and the power storage device. When the vehicle is running, the alternator becomes the power source and the recharging battery becomes a load. Electronic systems rely on a good power source to operate correctly -Load – an electrical load is an electrical part or portion of a circuit that consumes the active electric power ad does the work. Examples of loads include: -Bulbs -Motors -Solenoids -Path – circuit paths are the wires, terminals, and connectors bundled into wiring harnesses. Conductors carry electrical current through the circuit. Although all metals are conductors, copper is the most commonly used metal for conductors, wiring, and terminal ends. Other conductor metals include aluminum, silver, and gold. Connectors have a plastic housing with metal contacts inside -A Complete Circuit -a circuit path must be complete. If there is a break or open anywhere in the path, the flow stops, and the load will not work. If there is a restriction or unwanted resistance in the path, such as a pinched or partially broken wire, the load may partially work or may not work at all -Circuit Protectors and Controls – in addition to the necessary power source, load, and path, there are other parts in most circuits -Circuit Protectors – every circuit on a vehicle has at least one protection device to safeguard from overloads. An overload can result in damage to other system parts or can even result in a fire. Circuit protector devices include: -Circuit breakers -Fuses -Fusible links -Switch (Circuit Control) – every circuit has a circuit control. A control can be a simple push switch that opens and closes a lamp circuit to turn on and off the lamp. Most switches on modern vehicles are an input to the computer that processes the user request and manages the operation of the load. Switches usually have two or more sets of contacts. Opening these contacts opens the circuit. Closing these contacts completes the circuit. Common problems with a switch after a collision can be identified using the collision advantage. These include cracked casings, loose internal contacts, and loose or damaged connections. In most of these cases, the switch should be replaced -Circuit Protector Locations – fuses and other circuit protectors may be located in fuse blocks, or in multiple areas under hood. The inside cover of the fuse block usually labels the location, rating, and use of each circuit protector contained in the block. Common locations for fuse blocks include under the instrument panel, behind kick panels, and under the rear seat. Larger circuit protectors, called maxi-fuses, fusible links, and fuse elements, are designed to carry larger amounts of flow than fuses. They are used in high-powered circuits, such as the main battery feed, and may be grouped together near the battery. Note: Refer to manufacturer vehicle service information for exact location of the circuit protectors -Hot and Ground Sides of a Circuit – there are two sides to every circuit, the hot side and the ground side. The hot side of a circuit is from the power source up to one side of the load. The ground side of a circuit is from the other side of the load to ground. Electronic systems may use computer outputs to open or close the circuit path to the load. This is commonly done on the ground side of the circuit, but can also be done on the hot side of the circuit -Ground – ground is the common meeting place for electrical flow to the battery. Because all circuits are not physically wired to the negative battery post, common ground is needed. To create a common ground, the negative terminal of the battery may be connected to the vehicles frame, metal body, or engine block. For example, methods of connecting to ground include: -Multiple circuits connected to a single bolt, which are attached to the frame or body -Bolting the metal case of a part to the frame or body, called a case ground -Attaching a ground strap from the part to the frame or body -Note: One of the more frequent problems with post collision electrical circuits is a bad ground connection, making the circuit incomplete -Opens – an open is a break in the circuit stopping current flow. In a collision situation this could be a cut wire, damaged connector, or some other damaged part -Shorts – a short occurs when the current flow takes a different path than intended. Current flow will always take the easiest path, and will also seek every available path. A short is often caused by insulation scraped off a wire, allowing the wire to touch another bare wire or ground. The exposed wires may touch only from vehicle movement or engine vibration, causing intermittent circuit operation such as the flickering of a bulb or sounding of a chime. -Short to Power – if two wires are shorted together, and they power different circuits, the loads may operate when the circuit is turned on or they may not work when intended or as designed. The circuit protection device may blow due to a current overload. The wipers moving when the headlamps are switched on is an example of a short to power -Short to Ground – a short to ground will cause different problems depending on where the short occurs. If the circuit is grounded before the load, the load will be taken out of the circuit and the fuse will likely blow. A short to ground after the load may show no problem at all, but since the circuit is wired incorrectly, there may be additional problems in another circuit that will be difficult to find. If the circuit is ground side switched, a short to ground between the load and the switch would cause the load to stay on -Electricity and Magnetism – whenever current is flowing through a circuit, an electromagnetic field is present around the path. This field only exists when current is flowing. The higher the current flow there is, the larger electromagnetic field. Some of the electrical parts that use electromagnetic fields to work include solenoids, relays, motors, and magnetic pickups -Solenoids – a solenoid is a type of electromagnet that generates a controlled magnetic field. This cylindrical coil of wiring acts as a magnet when carrying an electric current. Solenoids are used to perform several functions on a vehicle. These functions include remotely opening a fuel filler door or deck lid, opening some door locks, opening and closing a valve that controls fuel delivery, called a fuel injector solenoid, and controlling brake fluid system pressure on an anti-lock braking system. Note: Solenoids can be physically damaged in a collision. If damaged, replacement is the only option. If a solenoid does not appear damaged, the solenoid should be tested for proper operation -Relays – a relay is a type of switch that is opened or closed using an electromagnet. A relay uses a low current circuit to control a high current circuit. It can also provide electrical isolation of the control circuit. Using a relay eliminates the need to run heavy wires into the passenger compartment or through a computer module. Relays may be located alone or in blocks, just like fuses, and are often grouped together with fuses in the same block. Relays are commonly computer controlled -Unwanted Magnetic Fields – an electromagnetic field around any wire or cable when current is flowing can cause problems when the electromagnetic field is not wanted. A starter circuit, for example, has a high amount of current flowing when the vehicle is being started. If those high current wires are routed wrong, the surrounding electromagnetic field can interfere with other circuits, possibly causing damage. This can lead to intermittent problems or, in the case of a control module, even permanent part failure. -Twisted/Shield Wires – some circuits or parts that cannot tolerate any unwanted electromagnetic fields have twisted wiring harnesses and shields on the wires to reduce the effects of the magnetic fields. These shielded wires are common on anti-lock braking system wheel speed sensors and other sensitive electronic circuit wiring, such as computer network communication busses. Twisted and shielded wires may be repairable depending on the location, application, and vehicle maker recommendations -5-Volt Systems – computers are extremely sensitive to electromagnetic fields, and can be damaged by a poorly routed cable. Computer-controlled circuits operate on lower voltage and current levels, making them more susceptible to interference. While most electrical circuits typically operate at 12 volts, electronic circuits typically operate at 5 volts. A soldering fun, even static electricity, can also damage or destroy control modules -Fiber-Optic Cables – information may travel through fiber-optic cables on some systems. Fiber-optic cables transmit signals using light instead of current through a wire. A fiber-optic cable may appear the same as any other wire, and may be bundled with other wires in a wiring harness. When looking at the connector or cable end, there will be a hole in the connector body of fiber optic cable, instead of a terminal cavity. The end of the cable will also be hollow, so light can pass through. Note: If a fiber-optic cable is damaged, the only repair is replacement of the cable between connectors. Special tools are required Basic Electronics for Estimators (2/2) Circuit Operation and Repair Checking Operation of a Circuit -Voltage, Current, and Resistance – when a technician checks the operation of a faulty circuit, more than just a visual inspection may be required. Troubleshooting the circuit may require measuring circuit valves, which are voltage, current and resistance. -Voltage – voltage is the electrical pressure that moves electrons through a circuit. Voltage is measured in volts, often shown abbreviated with a capital V. A fully charged conventional storage battery on a vehicle measures between 12.4 V and 12.6 V -Current – the electron current is measured in amperage as a number of amperes or amps. Amp is often shown abbreviated with a capital A. Direct Current (DC) is current that flows in one direction only. Alternating Current (AC) is current that flows both ways. Current from a household electrical outlet is AC. Automotive circuits are mostly DC, because batteries store only DC. An alternator produces AC, but it is changed to DC in order to charge the battery and power the vehicle circuits -Resistance – resistance opposes the current flow, and is measured in a number of ohms. Ohms are often shown as the Greek letter omega. The load is the main resistance in the circuit. By consuming most of the supplied voltage to operate, the load is resisting the flow of current. A wire connected directly between the hot and the ground battery post would not make up a proper circuit. Though there is some resistance in the length of the wire and the connectors, it is not enough. The wire would quickly burn, due to too much current flow. If the wire is large enough, the battery would quickly discharge and be unusable. A more substantial resistance, the load, is required -Types of Circuits – the two types of circuits are the parallel circuit and the series circuit, named for the flow of current within the circuit. -Parallel Circuit – automotive circuits are mostly wired in a parallel circuit where, even though there may be several loads branching off one wiring harness, each circuit brand has its own complete path. Each brand has access to full source voltage. In a parallel circuit, if there is a cut wire or an open in one of the branches, only the load in that branch if affected -Series Circuit – wiring a circuit in series, where there is only one path for more than one load, requires each load to share the available voltage. All of the bulbs in the circuit shown are 12-volt bulbs are sharing one 12-volt source, which causes the bulbs to appear dim compared to the parallel circuit. One open in a series circuit will stop the circuit from working. For example, on some Christmas lights, if one bulb burns out, the entire strand of lights will not work -Test Equipment – special tools are needed to test the operation and faults of a vehicles electrical system, as well as to measure voltage across a circuit -Digital Volt-Ohm Meter – the DVOM is one of the most commonly used test tools and is used to measure voltage, resistance, and current. An Amp probe allows for current measurement without breaking into the circuit -Scan Tools – a scan tool is needed to read DTCs, which may lead to specific testing that requires the use of a DVOM or other OEM-specific test equipment. Some scan tools have built-in scope and meter capabilities -Test Harness Probes – special test harness probes and back probe connector kits should be used to avoid harness and connector terminal damage, which may cause intermittent problems. OEMs may have other special test harnesses and breakout boxes for advanced testing requirements -Measuring Voltage – measuring voltage is the most common measurement taken in a circuit. Even though voltage remains consistent at the source, voltage can be measured anywhere in the circuit and is designed to be mostly used, or ‘dropped’, across the load. If a voltage measurement shows that nearly full voltage is not dropped across the load, there is likely trouble somewhere in the circuit. A voltage measurement can be taken anywhere in a live circuit -Measuring Across any Points – to measure voltage, the circuit must be powered, or turned on. After selecting the proper scale on the DVOM, the two leads are positioned across any two points in the circuit. The measurement on the meter is the number of volts between those two points. Note: When measuring voltage the DVOM does not affect, or is invisible to, the circuit being measured -Measuring across a Battery – battery voltage is critical to the operation of the vehicles electronic systems; this should be verified at the battery terminals, and possibly, at remove access locations. This checks the sate of charge of the battery, and shows the full available voltage, also called full source voltage. If the measurement is: -12.66 volts, the state of charge is 100% -12.45 volts, the state of charge is 75% -12.24 volts, the state of charge is 50% -12.06 volts, the state of charge is 25% -11.89 volts, the state of charge is 0% -Note: Removing the surface change from a battery provides a more accurate reading. To do this, turn on a load, such as the headlamps, for a short time with the ignition on the OFF position -Measuring across an Open – this measurement shows full source voltage across an open ground. The DVOM will always show full source voltage across an open no matter where it occurs in the hot or ground side of the circuit. When there is an open in the circuit, voltage does not get used by the load. On a vehicle, this is one way of detecting an open in a circuit. Note that the rest of the circuit must be complete with power applied -Voltage Drops – a voltage measurement taken anywhere in a live circuit can be revealing. For example, the 11-volt measurement across the headlamp means a dim bulb and a problem somewhere else in the circuit. A voltage drop measurement of about 2 volts across a connector would reveal a problem with the connector. It could be corrosion on the connector contacts or perhaps a loose terminal -Voltage Drop Reveal – a voltage drop test may also reveal a few wire strands broken inside a wire sheathing, hidden corrosion under a bolt, and hidden corrosion on a switch. Any one of these problems could cause a dim or flickering bulb, a slow running motor, or the load not working at all. It should also be pointed out that if these same areas were checked for resistance with the DVOM set to ohms, the measurement may show zero ohms, or no resistance problem at all -Voltage on Hybrid Electric and Pure-Electric Vehicles – on hybrid-electric and pure-electric vehicles, the high-voltage battery may be as much as 300 volts, or higher. The voltage and current available is high enough to kill. Always approach a damaged electric and hybrid electric vehicle assuming that the high-voltage system is energized. These vehicles are usually equipped with a 12-volt battery providing power to the vehicle computers and accessories and may allow some systems and accessories to be checked for proper operation. Before looking under the hood or in other areas of the vehicle, ensure the vehicle is powered down and secured to prevent unwanted movement. Turning the ignition OFF does not disable the high-voltage system. Follow the vehicle makers procedures to disable the high-voltage system -Resistance Measurements – to measure resistance, the circuit part has to be isolated, and no power can be applied, which is why resistance measurements are uncommon when looking for circuit problems. For example, if two sides of a wire connector are measured for resistance with the connector wired into the circuit, the meter will show the resistance of the entire circuit. Even if a part is isolated, a resistance measurement provides little information since the resistance may be correct, or the part may be damaged. A step in a diagnostic flowchart may say to measure resistance across two points, or across a part such as an impact sensor. This specific measurement is used in the flowchart to determine how to proceed. Other than a specific direction like that, it is not very common to take a resistance measurement -Continuity Check – a resistance measurement can be used to check for continuous path in the circuit – one with no breaks or opens. This is called a continuity check and can be done on individual wires within a wiring harness. Very low resistance (less than a few ohms) indicates that the two points are connected electrically. If the resistance is more than a few ohms, there may be unwanted resistance in the path. If the meter indicates OL, the circuit has no electrical connection, which means the circuit is open. A voltage drop test may also be used to check for continuity in a circuit. Zero or near zero voltage drop is continuity. A continuity test is important when troubleshooting a wiring harness or parts that have been involved in a collision, which may develop circuit opens or shorts Connector and Wire Repair -Wiring Issues – wiring issues are usually at the forefront of collision damage. Wires are pinched or cut, including wiring harnesses. This means that several wires are cut in one location. Wiring harnesses are several sizes, and it is often more practical to replace a small, isolated wiring harness. If a wiring harness is damaged, the decision to repair or replace is based on several factors, with the vehicle makers service information having the final decision. There will be general statements on wire repair recommendations, usually found in the mechanical/electrical service information or in TSBs -Offset Splice Locations – some vehicle makers may allow multiple splices to offset specific distances to avoid a bulk of repairs in one location; this may limit the number of splices. If there is not enough room to complete the splices, the harness may require replacement. Wire repairs should not be performed in an area that will continually flex, such as a doorjamb. This would be prone to failure. If replacing a portion of a wire, it is important to match the wire thickness, or gauge. Splicing in a different gauge wire affects the resistance of the wiring, affecting the current flow, and therefore changing the circuit design -Wire Repair kits – most vehicle makers offer wire repair kits, which contain the tools and materials required for wiring repair. The kit may contain splice sleeves or heat-shrink tubing, which is melted over the soldered splice. Verify that wire repair is permitted in service information. Some vehicle makers will not honor warranties on their vehicles if their wire repair kits are not used. Note: Some vehicle makers do not allow wires to be repaired -Connectors – connectors are used to join wires together. There are several types of connectors, each designed for certain usage. Examples include high density connectors, and waterproof connectors. If a bad connector is suspected, first perform a visual check looking for: -Damage -A missing seal -Corrosion -Discoloration -Cracks -Swelling -Note: If the connection doesn’t appear damaged on the outside, but damage is suspected, separate the mating connectors for closer inspection Basic Estimate Writing (1/4) Damage Report Overview Function of a Damage Report -Damage Report Estimate – repair estimates, which may also be referred to as Damage Report, Damage Appraisal, or Visible Damage Quote are typically computer-generated but can also be manually written. The damage report has many functions, including keeping a record of the estimated repair costs and describing the work that will be done when repairing the vehicle. Having a written record of this information will help to prevent misunderstandings between the repairer and customer or insurer as to what will be done during the repair process. The damage report may be considered an agreement between the three parties as to how the vehicle will be repaired Types of Damage Reports -Estimating Systems – computer-generated estimates are typically written using point-and-click entry instead of manual insertion of part prices and labor units. Parts are chosen from drop-down lists or on-screen diagrams, and the prices and labor units for their replacement are automatically entered. Keyboarding in entries is typically only required for customer, vehicle, and insurer information, and also for notes and entries for uncommon procedures or overrides. Computer-generated damage reports also have the capability of being submitted to the insurer online, along with digital images of the vehicle and the damage. This may help to speed up the approval process, reducing cycle time for the repair Other Features – other features of the damage report include the ability to store claim-related documentation. Notes on discussions with the vehicle owner or insurer can be included, as well as documentation of any changes made to the estimate during the repair process -Event Logs – most estimating systems also track the estimating process with some form of an event log. This log typically tracks things like file creation date, estimate date, date estimate locked, supplements, etc. if the estimate is written for an insurance partner direct repair program (DRP, it will also track items like assignment date and upload dates -Repair Authorization – some estimating systems may also provide optional tools, such as a repair authorization that can be printed as part of the estimate for the customer to sign, authorizing repairs. This may be a separate document in some repair shops. In some cases, the final repair estimate may also serve as the repair invoice as well -Parts Code Table or Prestore – the estimating systems may also offer a tool, often referred to as a parts code table or prestore, that allows you to set up common items that are required on repair estimates, but not included in the estimating database. These tables will typically include things like hazardous waste, stripes, detailing, cover car, NVH foam, corrosion protection, and chip-resistant coatings, to name a few Customer and Vehicle Information -Administration Information – the damage report contains basic identifying information for the repair facility, customer, insurance company, and the vehicle, as well as information regarding the damage and costs -Shop Information – the estimate will include shop information, such as: shop name, address, phone number, and estimator name and contact information. Some shops also include their fax number and tax ID number. It is important to review this information with each customer to ensure they have all the necessary information to contact you should they have any questions or additional requests -Customer Information – when preparing an estimate it is important to capture the name, address, phone number, and e-mail address. It is also important to confirm your customers preferred method of contact for status updates and any communications related to the repair facility of their vehicle. -Vehicle Information – while inspecting the vehicle it is important to obtain the necessary information such as: vehicle identification number (VIN), model year, make and model, trim packages, vehicle options, mileage, production date, engine/transmission type, license number, paint code, and trim code. All of this information could be necessary to ensure the correct parts are ordered for the repairs -Insurance Company Information – a damage report should contain identifying information and contact information about the insurance company involved in the claim. This includes the insurance company name, claim number assigned to the loss, and the date that the loss or collision occurred. In addition, the report should list the name and contact information of the person handling the claim, if known. You will need this information for any communication with the insurance company Basic Estimate Writing (2/4) Items on the Estimate -Operation Types – when preparing an estimate there are several common labor operations you will need to be familiar with and understand -Overhaul (O/H) – overhaul refers to the multiple operations commonly done to an assembly or part. For example, when replacing a bumper cover and reinforcement, the estimating database may provide overhaul time, which includes: removing an assembly from the vehicle, disassembly of the assembly, cleaning and inspecting the various parts of the assembly, repairing or replacing damaged parts, reassembling the parts of the assembly, and installing and aligning the assembly back to the vehicle. Note: Labor required to repair damaged pieces of an assembly is not included in overhaul time -Remove and Replace (R/R) – remove and replace (R/R) is required when a panel or part is removed and replaced with a new or equivalent part. Operations typically covered in R&amp;R include: removing the damaged part from the vehicle; transferring any necessary parts from the damaged parts to a new part (an example of this would be transferring the movable glass, glass regulator, latch and outside handle when replacing a door shell assembly); installing a new part on the vehicle or an assembly; and aligning the part on the vehicle or assembly -Remove and Install (R&amp;I) – remove and install (R&amp;I) may be done on an undamaged part to allow access to a damaged part in need of repair or replacement. This could also be done to facilitate the refinish process. The undamaged part or assembly is then set aside with no further disassembly done. Operations that may be covered in the R&amp;I procedure include: removal of a part or assembly, installing the part or assembly back on the vehicle or part that it was attached to, and aligning the part or assembly for proper fit -Disconnect and Reconnect (D&amp;R) – another additional labor operation that may be seen on some damage reports is “disconnect and reconnect’ or D&amp;R. D&amp;R includes disconnecting, unplugging, or unbolting a part at the connection point, but not removing it from the vehicle. When the necessary repairs are completed, the part is reconnected, plugged back in, or removed fasteners are installed -Drill Time – drill time is the labor time that may or may not be included in the labor time supplied by the information provider. This time may be for drilling out spot welds, or for drilling holes in a new panel for the purposes of plug welding as part of the installation process. To determine if drill time is part of the included operations or not included operations, please refer to the data provider procedure pages -Repair – repair labor listed on an estimate refers to a damaged part written for repair vs. replacement. The labor times attached to these items are a judgement decision made by the individual preparing the estimate based on damage visible at the time the estimate was written -Included Operation – included operations are labor operations the information provider has taken into consideration when developing the database (Book) time for another larger operation. For example, removal of moldings and emblems attached to a grill, when written by themselves would be an individual operation, but the information provider would likely consider them included operations when replacing the grille. Be sure to reference the estimating system Procedure Pages (P-Pages) to determine specifically what operations are included or not included -Sublet – items listed on the estimate as sublet are typically repairs the shop is not equipped to handle or the shop does not have expertise to handle in-house -Labor Categories – when preparing an estimate there are also several labor categories available for selection that you will need to know and understand. The decision on which category to select is based on the skilled labor required to complete each particular operation. In some cases, the data provider may identify a part as a mechanical or structural part, but this alone does not determine the labor category on the estimate. -Body – this category will usually encompass labor related to parts and assemblies classified as ‘Body’ parts or operations that will be completed by a body technician. This is typically the default labor category and will not have any special identification on the estimate -Paint – this category will include all operations related to the vehicle refinish process, or operations that will be performed by a refinish or prep technician. Some estimates may have separate repair operations listed for ‘Refinish’ and others may not have the ‘Refinish’ operation listed, but will show refinish labor in a separate column on the estimate -Mechanical – this category includes operations that will be performed by a technician with a mechanical skill set. In some cases, the information provider may identify the part as a mechanical component, but that alone may not cause the operation to appear in the mechanical labor category on the estimate. In the example shown, the lower case ‘m’ on the left side of the labor time is the information provider -Frame/Structural – items typically included in the frame/structural category include items identified as structural components, activities performed by a frame or structural technician, and/or will involve the use of specialized frame repair equipment. In some cases, the information provider may identify structural components, but that alone may not cause the operation to appear in the frame or structural category. In the example shown, the information provider lists the labor category frame (FR) but other information providers may identify structural components with an “S” -Diagnostic – this category is typically used to identify the labor required to perform additional inspection and/or troubleshooting necessary to identify the cause of a problem. This will then allow for the appropriate repair or replace items to be added to the estimate of repairs after the source of the problem has been identified -Electrical – this labor category will typically include items classified as electrical parts and/or electrical system repairs -Glass – items found in this category will typically include stationary glass repairs and/or replacement performed in-house and not sublet to an outside vendor -Overlap – overlap is a deduction taken to account for duplicate actions, such as when adjacent panels are worked on. The estimating P-Pages can be utilized to identify included and not included operations that are applied to labor times within their estimating database. Most computerized systems will automatically calculate the overlap deduction, but may not display them on the estimate. Some systems may provide a breakdown of the labor on a separate report, such as a labor report -Body – when replacing two adjacent panels, there may be some redundant operations between each panel that may result in an overlap deduction. For example: -Some surfaces are common to both the quarter panel and the rear body panel -Removal of both panels includes some common operations, such as spot weld and seam sealer removal where the two panels are joined -Refinish – refinish overlap applies when two or more panels are refinished at the same time. This occurs because there are some operations included in base color refinish allowances that are not necessary to duplicate an additional panels being refinished. Although the estimating system database will typically make these adjustments for you, it is important to understand the formula used by your estimating system -Manual Entry Items – manual entry items are entries made by the person preparing the damage repair estimate. These entries can be manual overrides, manual line entries, or estimate/line notes -System Override and Manual Line Entry – a system override is any chance the user makes to information supplied by the estimating database. These changes will typically include labor times or part prices. A manual line entry is an entry made by the person preparing the estimate for a unique item that is not identified in the estimating database or user defined parts code table. These entries may be designated by an asterisk (*) or a pound sign (#) and/or the entry may be underlined -Line Notes – line notes are used on estimates to provide explanations for any deviations in the repair plan; expectations limiting certain repairs; prior damage descriptions; any repair requests made by the customer; explanations for why automatic entries have been modified; or for providing parts vendor information when alternative parts are utilized. For example, if a manufacturer provides a sectioning procedure for replacing a quarter panel, but the information provider only lists a full uniside replacement in the database, the estimator may elect to manually override the labor time to reflect the sectioning operation. In this case, the estimator should enter an estimate/line note explaining the change so everyone involved is clear about how the repairs are to be completed and the reason for overriding the database-provided labor time -Part Types – often, there are several options when selecting replacement parts for completing repairs. The decision about which part type is selected may depend on several different factors, such as owner preference, insurance company guidelines, availability, and economics. Estimating systems will typically provide several part types to choose from when preparing an estimate -OEM – OEM parts are replacement parts manufactured by the original equipment manufacturer (OEM). These parts typically do not have a special designation on the repair estimate -Aftermarket – aftermarket parts are replacement parts manufactured by a vendor other than the OEM -Reconditioned – reconditioned parts are parts that have been repaired or reconditioned and now are being sold as repair parts -LKQ/Used – Like, Kind, and Quality (LKQ), or used parts removed from another damaged vehicle and resold as repair parts -Optional OEM – optional OEM are parts manufactured by the OEM, but not sold to the shop directly. These parts are typically run off or blemished parts, and not all makers offer these parts for purchase -Book Time – book time, also called flat rate or database time, refers to the labor units that are estimated to perform a procedure. Flat rate units for replacing parts of a vehicle are listed in the estimating database or crash guide and are based on the use of new undamaged parts on a new undamaged vehicle. They are considered to be stand-alone operations. If the labor units for replacing a part include the removal of another part or parts, this will be listed in the labor notes or procedure pages (P-Pages) related to the replacement of the part being replaced Basic Estimate Writing (3/4) Refinish Operations -Types of Finish – when preparing an estimate you will need to be able to identify the type of finish you are working with. Common types of finishes found on modern vehicles include single-stage, basecoat/clearcoat, and multi-stage finishes. Basecoat/clearcoat finishes are the most common OEM finishes used. Multi-stage finishes have a basecoat, tinted translucent mid-coats, and a clearcoat -Paint Code Finish Type – in most cases, your estimating system will assume a two-stage finish; but it is always necessary to confirm the finish type when preparing a damage estimate. Identifying finish type starts with determining the paint code and then making the necessary adjustments within the estimating system to ensure the estimating database will make those necessary adjustments to the way refinish times are calculated. The type of finish on the vehicle may also play a role in determining additional refinish operations required. Be aware that color matching and how current paint conditions like chips and scratches may affect the refinish process -Color Blending and Trim Removal – color blending may be a necessary addition to many damage estimates. Color blending helps to disguise slight differences in color, and reduces any visible color difference between the original finish color and the refinish color. It will not, however, hide a severe color mismatch. Panels that are going to be refinished or blended may require removal of trim that sits against the paint finish. In addition, when considering panel blending, remember that while the color coat can be blended the clearcoat should be applied edge to edge of the panel to ensure proper coverage and UV protection -Refinish Time – the base refinish allowance from an estimating system is typically based on a single color applied to a new, undamaged panel. This may only include outside surfaces, unless otherwise stated in the document. For example, an underside color may be different than the body color and not included in the refinish time. Base refinish times typically do not include operations such as tint matching, edging, or the inside/underside of a panel. Although tinting and other operations are not always required, they may be necessary in some situations such as special colors and custom finishes. It is also important to remember that in some cases, when selecting refinish of edges or undersides of panels, the database may not automatically add the necessary clearcoat allowance for these operations. In these cases, there may be additional steps required to take within the estimating system to allow the database to add the necessary clearcoat allowance. Otherwise, a manual entry may be required. Consult the P-pages for your specific estimating systems to determine what is included in the refinish times Paint and Materials -Paint and Materials – paint and material charges typically include the liquid materials used to refinish the repaired areas and any materials used to prepare the repaired areas for refinishing such as surface cleaners, sanding products, and tape. Paint and material charges are calculated automatically by the estimating software, but the paint and material rate may vary from one market to another, and entry of this rate is part of the rate profile setup process. In some cases additional repair materials may be required which are not typically included in standard refinish material calculations. Some examples of these materials include: -Noise, vibration, and harshness (NVH) materials -Seam sealers -Chip-resistant coatings -Corrosion protection materials -Note: When these types of materials are required to complete repairs it may be necessary to create a manual entry or select these items from the parts code table or pre-store list -Additional Charges – another expense related to the refinish process which is not typically included in the paint and material calculations is hazardous waste disposal or EPA charge. Fluids such as antifreeze, oils, and refrigerants may also require hazardous waste disposal when they are collected. Basic Estimating Writing (4/4) Finalizing the Damage Report Subtotals -Subtotals – damage reports typically have subtotal columns for various items. Subtotals typically include: -Parts totals -Labor totals -Sales tax -Sublets -Gross total -Customer responsibility, which typically include deductibles and any betterments -Net total -Note: The subtotal area will also typically show the original estimate total and the totals for any subsequent supplements -Supplements – supplements may be designated by an “S” next to the appropriate line item. Supplements may be additions or subtractions that are made as a result of changes to the original repair plan or hidden damage found after the damage report is finalized. They also may be caused by a change in part prices that occurs in the time between when the damage report was written, and the vehicle is repaired Other Charges -Appearance Allowance – when a vehicle has a damaged part that is a high cost part, but the damage is very minor and of cosmetic nature, the insurance company may request the customer be offered an appearance allowance vs. replacing the part. In this case, the insurance company would offer the vehicle owner a cash settlement for the damage to the part vs. listing the part for replacement on the estimate -Betterment – betterment is the increase in value when a worn or previously damaged part is replaced. This is sometimes referred to as depreciation, but the most common industry term is betterment. The insurance company pays for the unused portion of the item being replaced and the vehicle owner is then responsible for the cost associated with the used portion of the part. Local laws may apply to the application of betterment. Depending on local laws, betterment may be limited to describe the increase in the value of the entire vehicle, while other lays may allow for an adjustment for the increase in value of the damaged part itself. An example of this would be a vehicle that has tires with 40,000 miles and the tires were damaged in a collision. Another example would be a rusted chrome bumper that is damaged in a collision and is replaced. Note: Betterment is typically not applied to non-OEM crash parts used to complete repairs Basics of Bodywork Collision Repairs (T0458E-US.TTC) T0458E-US.TTC-Pre-Test Which statement is NOT correct? MIG/MAG welding of ultra high-strength hot-formed steel is not permitted – X wrong Which statement is correct? Work with aluminum may only be carried out in a separate area of the workshop What criteria does not play a part in the choice of materials? Vehicle weight – X wrong Which statement about the materials of the car body is correct? Aluminum has much greater thermal conductivity than steel Which joining method does NOT require overlapping panels? MIG Soldering Which statement about straightening systems is correct? The straightening system by CAR-BENCH uses software to calculate the fixture installation position – X wrong Which material has the highest thermal conductivity? Aluminum Which material has the lowest melting point? Soft steel – X wrong Which joining method can be used on soft steel, high-strength steel, ultra-high strength hot-formed steel and aluminum? MIG/MAG welding – X wrong With what materials can resistance welding NOT be regularly and efficiently used in the workshops? Ultra high-strength steel, hot-formed – X wrong T0458E-US.TTC-Lesson 1 Intro eLearning Basics of bodywork collision repairs (T0458E-US.TTC) Target group(s) Bodywork experts and bodywork specialists collision repairs -Example: E-Class (W213) – From how many different materials is this bodyshell constructed? 8 -What criteria play a part in the choice of materials? -Passenger safety in collision -Vehicle weight -Production costs -Production technology -Environmental compatibility -Ease of repairs -Body of the E-Class (W213) is composed of 8 different materials. -High-strength steel -Modern high-strength steel -Ultra-high strength steel -Ultra high-strength steel, hot-formed -Aluminum sheet metal -Extruded aluminum sections -Aluminum casting -Composites -Tensile strength = external force and mechanical resistance (impact) -Aluminum has a lower melting point -Aluminum conducts much better than steel T0458E-US.TTC-Lesson 2 -Overview of joining techniques used in the workshop -Procedure -Applicability -Materials -Advantages and disadvantages -Thermal joining techniques (joining by heat) -Unavoidable when heat is applied: -Deformation of the workpiece -Susceptibility to corrosion around the joining point -Change in the material properties (especially strength) -Examples are – Resistance welding, MIG/MAG welding -Non-thermal joining techniques (mechanical connection) -Completely avoid the risks of thermal joining methods -Examples are – Blind rivets, Self-piercing riveting, Flow-form riveting, Clinching, Flow-form bolting, Bonding -Joining techniques -Production -Resistance welding -MIG/MAG welding -MIG soldering -Self-piercing riveting -Clinching -Flow-form bolting -Bonding -Workshop -Resistance welding -MIG/MAG welding -MIG soldering -Blind rivets
