-Flow-form riveting -Flow-form bolting -Bonding -Overlapping necessary -Resistance welding -Blind rivets -Flow-form riveting -Flow-form bolting -Bonding -Access on both sides needed -Resistance welding -Flow-form riveting -Resistance welding (spot welding) -Overlapping sheets or flange required -Access on both sides required -Thoroughly clean metallic surfaces -Possibly fill zinc dust paint or adhesive into the joint -Current flows between the electrodes -The workpieces begin to melt -Spot-weld forms after cooling -No material is added -Advantages: -No material is added -Thermal load is extremely localized -Disadvantages: -Not suitable for butt joints -Not usable if access is only one-sided -MIG/MAG Welding: -Butt joints possible -One-sided access is sufficient -Thoroughly clean metallic surfaces -Electric arc between electrode and workpieces -The workpieces begin to melt -Melting electrode forms a welding bead -Active or Inert gas prevents oxidation with the surrounding air -Solid joint after cooling -Welding bead forms a sealing seam -Advantages: -Also suitable for butt joints -One-sided access to the joint is sufficient -Creates a sealing seam -Disadvantages: -Adds material -High thermal load -Use with aluminum very complex -MIG Soldering: -Usual for butt joints -One-sided access is sufficient -Electric arc between electrode and workpiece -Solder diffuses into the heated material surfaces -Service information for MIG solder materials: -CuSi3 for non-structural exterior paneling components -CuAI8 for structural components (special case) -Advantages: -Creates a sealing seam -Lower thermal load than when welding -Bridging of joint gaps -One-sided access is sufficient -Disadvantages: -Only suitable for exterior paneling components -Lower joint strength compared to welding -Blind Rivets: -Overlapping sheets or flange required -One-sided access is sufficient -Panels are drilled, deburred and cleaned -Rivet is positioned -Rivet head breaks at the predetermined fracture point when pulled -Mashing tightens the rivet -Advantages: -One-sided access to the joint is sufficient -High joint strength -No adverse material effects from heat -Connection of different materials possible -Disadvantages: -Adds material -Not usable for butt joints -Swarf is formed when drilling -Flow-form Riveting: -Replaces the self-piercing riveting used in production -Overlapping sheets or flanges required -Access on both sides required -Remove self-piercing rivet -Stamp and calibrate rivet hole -Position the rivet -Compact the rivet -Advantages: -Connection of different materials possible -No change in material properties -Connection has very high tensile strength -Appearance close to the original -Disadvantages: -Access on both sides required -Flow-form bolting: -Overlapping sheets or flange required -One-sided access is sufficient -Panels are predrilled: lower panel with a smaller diameter -Tighten the screw -Form the rim hole -Molded thread ensures a strong connection -Compact the rivet -Advantages: -One-sided access is sufficient -No thermal load on the workpieces -Appearance the same as the original -Disadvantages: -Material is added -Swarf is formed when drilling -Bonding: -Overlapping panels -One-sided access is sufficient -Prepare the surfaces (remove paint, roughen, degrease) -Carry out indicator test -Apply two-component adhesive -Connect the workpieces -Fix parts to be joined in place -Avoid mechanical stresses at the joint as the adhesive is curing -Advantages: -Connection of different materials possible -Strong connection over a large area -No thermal load on the workpieces -Adhesive acts as an insulation layer -Disadvantages: -Great cleanliness required -Limited application temperatures -Maximum strength often only after several hours T0458E-US.TTC-Lesson 3 -Overview of straightening system manufacturers approved by Mercedes-Benz -Differences and special features of straightening systems -Celette: -Vehicle-specific -Straightening tool set for each vehicle model series -Fixed installation plan for straightening tool set -Completed installation -Z axis adjustable -Car Bench: -Universal -Only one straightening tool set -Fixed installation plan for each vehicle model series -Completed installation -X, Y, Z axes adjustable T0458E-US.TTC-Post Test Which statement is NOT correct? MIG/MAG welding of ultra high-strength hot-formed steel is not permitted – X wrong Which material has the lowest electrical resistance? Mild steel – X wrong Which material has the lowest melting point? Aluminum Which statement is correct? Work with aluminum may only be carried out in a separate area of the workshop Which material can ONLY be joined by riveting or resistance welding? Ultra high-strength hot-formed steel Which joining method does NOT require overlapping panels? MIG soldering Which joining method can be used on soft steel, high-strength steel, ultra high-strength hot-formed steel and aluminum? Riveting What criteria does not play a part in the choice of materials? Insurance requirements Which material has the highest thermal conductivity? Aluminum Which joining method requires access to both sides of the joined materials? Flow-form riveting Blueprinting for the Estimator Blueprinting -Traditional Estimates – traditional damage estimates identify damages that are visible without the benefit of removing damaged exterior panels to expose the full extent of damage. This will typically result in supplements, multiple parts orders, and repair delays after the vehicle is brought into the repair shop and disassembled for repairs. These repair delays can result in frustration for your customers and insurance partners. -The Blueprint Process – blueprint damage analysis is not just an estimate, it is a discovery process to identify all collision related damage. Blueprinting addresses potentional obstacles at the beginning of the repair process to prevent them from ever becoming a problem. As a part of the blueprinting process, the estimator and the technician will need to: -Review vehicle maker repair information that pertains to the damaged vehicle -Conduct an initial inspection to obtain pre-disassembly photos -Perform a vehicle ‘pre-scan’ prior to starting any disassembly or repairs -Completely disassemble the vehicle in the area of damage -Thoroughly inspect the vehicle and all parts removed to identify all damages -Perform quick checks for suspension and structural damage -Take three-dimensional measurements of the entire vehicle -Review all damages with the blueprint technician, including all parts in need of replacement and any items in need of repair. -Traditional Estimate vs. Blueprinting – lets do a one-to-one comparison of a traditional estimate with the complete estimate that results from blueprinting. -Hidden Damage – with a traditional estimate, hidden damage is not included in the plan because there is not a complete teardown. With a blueprint, all damaged parts are exposed for inspection; there is no hidden damage. -Parts Availability – with a traditional estimate, parts availability is unknown, and pricing is only as accurate as the estimating database. With blueprint, parts availability and pricing may be verified with vendors. -Inspection Location – traditional estimates may be done outside of the facility in a parking lot with no access to a lift. Blueprinting is always done in the facility, ideally where a lift may be used for better access. -Estimates – with a traditional estimate, the estimator makes a decision about the repairability and judgement times without consulting a blueprint technician or disassembling the vehicle. With blueprinting, a blueprinting team sees all the damage and provides input regarding repair times. -Accuracy – during a traditional estimate, structural damage assessment is limited due to a lack of access and no three-dimensional measuring. During blueprinting, measurement identifies any structural damage and puts required repairs in the plan. Pre-pulling may be needed in some scenarios to determine repairability. -Importance and Benefits of Blueprinting – a blueprint for collision repair is like a blueprint for building a house. The construction blueprint and collision repair blueprint identify all the parts, equipment, labor, workforce, and workflow required to get the job done before any work is authorized to begin. Both blueprints contain thorough details for the Statement of Work, leaving nothing open to interpretation. The blueprinting process improves estimate accuracy, reduces repair delays, improves productivity, and reduces cycle time, which leads to improved customer satisfaction index (CSI) scores. -Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) – a Key Performance Indicator (KPI) is a measurable value that demonstrates how effectively a company is achieving key business objectives. Production KPIs include cycle time, touch time, labor efficiency, and others. -Supplements are KPI Killers – one of the goals of the blueprinting process is to eliminate repair supplements. Within the collision repair industry, supplements are viewed as waste and killers of a shop’s KPIs. Supplements have a negative effect on: -Cycle time, as supplements add time to the repair -Technicians touch time, since supplements stop the repair midstream -The parts ordering process, as what you thought was a completed task is no longer and now you must add another parts order -CSI scores, as supplements affect CSI scores due to dissatisfaction with wait time. -Vehicle Maker Repair Information and Pre-Scan – Prior to starting any repairs, vehicle maker service information should be obtained and provided to the blueprint technician. This information can be obtained from many sources, including vehicle maker websites, OEMOneStop, AllData, and the I-CAR RTS website. In addition, it is very important to complete a vehicle scan prior to starting any repairs. This can provide valuable information for the damage analysis process and documents all diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) present when the vehicle arrived for repairs. -Complete Disassembly – blueprinting involves removing all required parts of the vehicle, not merely to see all the damage, but to discover all hidden damage in order to write a complete estimate. During the blueprinting process, all fasteners or clips are removed for the repair, which allows for the identification of fasteners that will break during disassembly, so they can be ordered with the rest of the parts. -Parts Storage – establishing a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for parts storage is also important. A popular and efficient method of storing parts is the use of a parts cart. During the disassembly process, as parts are removed from the vehicle they are placed on the parts cart. Typically, the parts are arranged on the cart based on their repairability. Also, fasteners may be bagged or placed in plastic storage compartments for easy retrieval. Whatever your method of choice, remain consistent for an efficient blueprinting and repair process. Note: This SOP should also include standards for marking parts to identify repairability and damage location to make identifying damages quick and easy. -For Every Repair – does every vehicle need to go through the blueprinting process, even if the damages appear to be only a crack in the bumper cover? Why differentiate? When you remove the bumper cover, there could be hidden damage underneath or maybe a clip will break during disassembly process. You do not want to discover hidden damage after the vehicle has entered the repair stream. Making 100% teardown an SOP will help ensure that you will avoid surprises later. -For Every Repair Facility – blueprinting is for every size repair facility. If nothing more than complete disassembly is done, this will allow facilities to see what is needed, what will beak, and what parts and sublet labor are needed to get a complete estimate, resulting in a rise in productivity. -Insurance Industry Benefits – blueprinting benefits the insurance industry by reducing the number of supplements, which are the result of an incomplete damage report. Additional benefits include faster in/out times, fewer rental car days, fewer returned parts, and less wasted time. Insurance companies realize that supplements can contribute to repair delays and require resources to process the supplement and possibly re-inspect the vehicle, if necessary. -Staffing a Blueprinting Department – staffing for the blueprinting process varies and is based on the size of the facility and technician expertise. A small facility may have people in dual or even multiple roles, a situation that may have existed even before the implementation of a blueprinting process. In a small facility, the body technician may do the disassembly, working closely with the estimator. A large facility may set up a dedicated blueprinting team. On that team, there may be a body technician responsible for all vehicle disassembly and a dedicated estimator. Diagnostic, mechanical, and frame technicians may be easily accessible during the blueprinting process. -There is Still a Need for Traditional Estimates – traditional estimates are still performed every day for many repair facilities. Traditional estimates are written for a variety of reasons including: -Determining if the vehicle is repairable or a total loss -Scheduling a drivable vehicle in for repairs and pre-ordering parts -Helping a customer who will be paying for the repairs out-of-pocket to get an estimated repair cost. -Traditional Estimates and Blueprinting – while traditional estimates may serve as a guide and to document several facts about the vehicle and the scheduled repairs, the blueprinting process will still be performed when the vehicle arrives for repairs, using the initial estimate as a baseline. This will ensure any damages that were not visible when the traditional estimate was completed are identified before the vehicle enters the repair stream, and any additional parts required can be ordered to minimize delays. -What is Mapping – on-vehicle notes, or vehicle mapping, are notes the shop makes directly on the vehicle exterior using colored markers and symbols shortly after the customer interview. -Mapping – vehicles are mapped with text and symbols to provide a quick, strong visual indication of what will be repaired. The following are examples of information that you may include when mapping -Customer name -Repair order (RO) number -Arrival and delivery dates -Insurance company -Specific areas that should be repaired and not repaired -Agreed-upon customer-pay repairs -Mapping Legends – the facility gains efficiency when everyone in the shop knows the codes and symbols. Vehicle mapping serves as a communication tool throughout the repair and may also become a quality control tool. The production manager can initial on the vehicle to confirm the color, verify that the disassembly is complete, and signal that repair should begin. -What is NOT Marked – do not mark items such as textured parts, oxidized or sun-faded parts, cloth parts, etc., during vehicle mapping. It is highly likely that marking on these parts would result in staining or residual marks remaining on the part, even after washing the vehicle. -Getting Started – implementing a blueprinting process is not a magical cure for all challenges within the collision repair process. However, spending a little more time writing a complete and accurate blueprint for the repair of each vehicle will result in improved efficiency, reduced cycle times, and improved CSI results. The first steps include taking a look at the size and volume of your shop, and next making decisions about your blueprinting SOP and how to staff your blueprint department. It is important to engage all staff members in the development of your blueprinting SOP to ensure everyone is on the same page and working to reach the same goals. Collision/Service: High Voltage Level 1 Training – Awareness (Rivian) High Voltage training – awareness of hazardous voltages, low voltage hazards, battery technology -Hazardous Voltage Definition – The National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) and the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) recognize voltage greater than or equal to 50V (AC/DC) as Hazardous Voltage -Alternating Current – an electric current that reverses its direction many times a second at regular intervals, typically used in power supplies -Direct Current – an electric current flowing in one direction only -Hierarchy of Controls – see image in Rivian Folder -Hazard Control: Medical Device Signage – development work and vehicles may involve high magnetic and electric fields. Pre-production vehicles are NOT fully tested for compatibility with off-board devices, including active implantable medical devices -if you have an implant, avoid specifically designated areas of high field strength that cause interference. In the event of any interference, leave the area of high field strength immediately. -How to Properly Use a Fire Extinguisher -1. Remove the fire extinguisher from the wall -2. P ull the pin -3. A im at base of fire -4. S queeze the lever -5. S weep from side to side -Summary -Stay calm and remember -1. If a fire arises, use PASS: Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep -2. DO Not attempt if the fire is larger than a wastebasket -3. Do not attempt on batteries in thermal Runaway -4. Evacuate, and sound the alarm if you feel uncomfortable in any way -Incident Reporting – all near-miss events, incidents, and injuries related to electrical hazards MUST be reported to your supervisor and Safety/EHS manager. All shocks must be reported. All employees sustaining shock around high voltage must go for medical examination. -Hazardous Energy Control – Live parts of equipment at 50 volts or higher must be guarded against accidental contact either by: -Locating it in an area only accessible to qualified persons -Placing permanent screens/partitions around the equipment -Placing it on a platform or balcony only accessible to qualified persons -Elevating it up above 8 feet from the floor -Hazard Control: Limited Approach Boundary -Never enter a limit approach boundary without an escort and required PPE -Hazard Control: Lockout – Control of Hazardous Energy – lockout is the process of preventing the flow of stored energy -1. It consists of installing a lock, block, or chain to the energy-isolating device(s) -2. It requires an authorized employee to apply/remove a lock -3. It covers maintenance and servicing tasks -Rivian’s Lockout Program establishes requirements for the control of hazardous energy to prevent injury: -Ensure awareness of hazardous energy sources -Ensure authorized personnel is sufficiently trained -Establish procedures for ensuring a zero-energy state -It is vitally important that you know your role in the lockout program, as related to HV. There are three types of employees who must receive training: affected, authorized, and other -Affected Employees – an affected employee is an individual who operates equipment or an individual who works in an area in which servicing or maintenance activities under lockout are being performed -Authorized Employees – -An authorized employee is an individual who’s authorized to lockout a piece of equipment in order to perform servicing or maintenance activities that piece of equipment -Authorized employees must read and understand the Hazardous Energy Control Procedure -Understand your role in controlling energy -Ensure that affected employees are inform -Must be able to recognize: -Hazardous energy sources -Type and magnitude of the energy available -Methods and means to isolate and control energy -Other Employees- an employee whose work tasks are or might be in an area where lockout/tagout is being performed -Hazard Control: Low Voltage – a low voltage sources with the ability to provide high current are the source of a different type of electrical hazard. High current can cause arc-flash (sparks), high heat, and molten metal which in-turn can burn. Regular 12V batteries or a single lithium-ion cell (~4V), when shorted by a tool or jewelry, for example, can source many hundreds of amps, causing very hot jewelry or arc flashes from the short -Although typical automotive batteries and a single lithium-ion cell are not considered high voltage, please take care in preventing shorts. Remove jewelry, badges, belt buckles, etc., and use insulated tools when necessary -Although typical automotive batteries and a single lithium-ion cell are not considered high voltage, please take care in preventing shorts. Remove jewelry, badges, belt buckles, etc., and use insulated tools when necessary -Hazard Control: Energy of a Single Battery – low voltage sources with the ability to provide high current are the source of a different type of electrical hazard. High current can cause arc-flash (sparks), high heat, and molten metal which in-turn can burn. Even a single battery cell, under the right conditions, can cause a signification reaction -Electricity &amp; Hazardous Voltages -Hazard Understanding: Critical Mass-three key items associated with Shock Injuries are Path of Current, Amount of Current, Amount of Current, and Duration of shock. -Hazards- Current Loop in Building Wiring – - Building wiring has a high potential for a single point of failure. The “hidden path” is through the ground or building itself. -During charging – when plugged in, the vehicle becomes referenced to the earth. In a Pre-Production vehicle, the system is safe to use and touch when plugged into a production charger. All current Rivian vehicles have a very robust Onboard Charger -Future vehicles may not. If not, they will be tagged with a sign if you should take extra precautions. -Wireless charging (if Rivian is involved) will have additional hazards. These hazards can be eliminated by stopping the charging process. Signs will be used. -If you have any concerns, simply unplug the vehicle -Hazards: Preventing Current Loops – don’t make the loop -Isolated HV System- -An isolated system prevents current loops by restricting contact to only one part of the system -No current loop can be formed with a single point of contact -Isolation Monitoring- -Due to an imperfect world, isolation is not guaranteed -Because of this, an isolation monitoring system continuously checks for faults when the vehicle is on -Preventing Loops No Access to HV – 2 key features that keep the general public safe during operation &amp; general maintenance -Non-Accessible HV System and Equipotential Bonding -All energized parts are covered to prevent access. Fingers do not fit in connections (finger-proof) -All metallic parts are connected through the car chassis -Should an isolation fault occur, no current loop can be formed from outside the enclosure -Access Monitoring by BMS or HVIL – -Accessible panels and connectors are monitored by the Battery Management System (BMS) or High-Voltage Interlock Loop (HVIL) system to ensure that the HV system remains closed -If the BMS/HVIL detects an open system, it may shutdown and prevent operation -Instrumentation – R&amp;D Vehicles may have instrumentation built into them for development work. Components may be connected to high voltage systems. All components with orange or high voltage labeling – whether permanent or temporary – must be treated as high voltage electrical work and performed by Qualified Level 2 personnel only. These markings identify components that IEPs are NOT to be working with. -Glued on warning markings? Treat as high voltage -Cables ringed with orange bands made of tape? Treat as high voltage -Cable-tied into something else and wasn’t there yesterday but orange, nonetheless? Treat as high voltage -High Voltage Areas – each facility may have a specific area(s) for HV work. Special care should be taken around and prior to the entry of high voltage areas. Only authorized personnel should enter -Areas of Special Concern- -Battery Production -HV Component Testing and Validation -Static Bucks -Vehicles Under Maintenance -ANY Orange Cables and Conduit -Hazards Control- -Covers and Finger Proofing – covers and finger proof connectors provide protection from inadvertent contact with energized systems. KEY SAFETY ELEMENTS – NO INDIRECT ACCESS. Do not take covers off HV components unless you have the correct Qualification Level (Level 2+). -Bonding and Grounding – the importance of bonding and grounding high voltage equipment cannot be overstated. The purpose of bonding is to ensure that systems have the same electrical potential and avoid electrical shock to the user. Grounding provides a path for conducting electrical energy to the earth to prevent arcing, heating, and explosion during an energy surge. Screws and wires connecting HV parts to the vehicle are used to keep them bonded and grounded. They work to keep you from creating a current loop through your body. Do not disconnect unless you hold the proper Qualification Level. -Marking and Labeling – High voltage labels warn workers and others of the electrocution and arc flash dangers present. This form of visual communication is elemental to HV safety and is a recognized industry practice of OSHA and the NFPA. Many HV components are purposely colored orange to indicate and warn workers of an electrical hazard. High voltage marking includes stickers, labels, imprints, and warning signs that vary in size and color. These markings identify components that you (Level 1) should NOT be working with directly when energized. -Tools and PPE – the importance of the proper tools and PPE when conducting HV activities cannot be overemphasized. As the “last line of defense” they can reduce exposure to hazards when engineering and administrative controls are not feasible or effective at reducing risk to acceptable levels. Both OSHA and the NFPA require their use in certain HV situations. -1. NFPA 70E and OSHA Require Insulated Tools When Working On or Near 50V+ Equipment While Energized. Do not sue HV tools for non-HV work -2. ARC FLASH PPE OSHA 1910.269 &amp; 1926.960. 2.0 calories and above, arc flash clothing is required -3. Insulated tools and PPE are required for LIVE work or when working inside the limited approach boundary. You have not been trained for live work, do not take or use the PPE -Emergency Response Summary – an Emergency Response Guide (ERG) will be published prior to production that first responders can access electronically using the QR code on the vehicle. Until then, this will provide important information in case of an emergency. The label will be affixed to production vehicles. First responders can scan the QR code to obtain emergency information - Preventing Loops – controlling HV hazards boils down to preventing loops and the flow of electrical current through the body. -HV First Response – in the event that someone is actively being shocked – if you can safely break the current loop, do so immediately. Safe means of breaking the loop include Equipment E-Stop Switch, Power Disconnect, and Insulated Rescue Hook. -1. Energy isolating devices are the preferred means to safely break the current loop -2. In the event that no energy isolating devices are able to break the loop; locate the nearest insulated rescue hook/rubber glove kit. Ensure that no part of your body is within 1ft of any metal object, have a steady base stance, wrap the hook securely around the victim and pull the victim clear of all live parts before assessing the individual’s medical needs. Call 911 -HV Program Qualification Levels – qualification levels are attained through the completion of training and demonstration of competency. Additional Qualification Levels are obtained in continuing courses -Level 0 – Lay-Person (LP) – not yet trained. Unaware of electrical systems and risks -Not permitted to work on or operate pre-production vehicles -Follow all safety rules -Must be escorted at all times by someone at a higher qualification level -Level 1 – Informed Electrical Person (IEP) -May escort layperson(s) -Perform tasks that do not present any electrical hazard or have otherwise been addressed by a Skilled Electrical Person -Consult with a Level 2 or higher if a situation is outside their expertise or comfort level -Level 2 – Skilled Electrical Person – Qualified (SEP) -Level 3 – Skilled Electrical Person – Qualified (SEP)+ Live Work Training – Qualified -HV Program Definitions - -Vehicle Operation = Normal Owner Functions -Driving -Cleaning -Wiper Fluid -Charging -Mechanical Work = Work that is very unlikely to cause a HV hazard -Changing tires -Replacing wiper blades -Wiper fluid -Interior work -Mechanical Work w/ Potential HV Hazards = Work that might result in a HV Hazards (now or in the future) -Installing or replacing hoses next to HV cables -Installation of screws into cabin floor (above battery) -HV Electrical Work = any work on any HV components -Ex. Boxes, cables, connectors, sensors, etc. -LIVE HV Electrical Work = work on a component or system that cannot be de-energized -Inside the battery enclosure -Any work while a pre-production vehicle is charging -As Level 1 IEP’s, you will not be able to perform any live work on HV systems until trained and qualified to Level 3 -Lay Person- -Pre-production vehicles -Unsupervised Lay Persons Cannot: -Drive -Service -Clean -Etc. -Informed Electrical Person- -May escort layperson(s) -Perform tsks that don’t present any electrical hazard -May perform some additional work: -Mechanical Work after system deactivation by a SEP -HV Electrical work after systems deactivation by an SEP and with written work instruction High Voltage – Level 1 Quiz True or False: If you have an implant, avoid specifically designated areas of high field strength that cause interference – true In the event of a fire, locate the nearest extinguisher and remember__ - PASS All near-miss events, incidents, and injuries related to electrical hazards MUST be reported to your ____ and the Safety/EHS Manager – Supervisor Never enter a limit approach boundary without an escort and required __- PPE Lockout is the process of ___ the flow of stored energy – Preventing What are the 3 key items associated with Shock Injuries – Path of current, amount of current, duration of shock Controlling HV hazards boils down to preventing loops and the flow of electrical current through the __ - body In the event that someone is actively being shocked – if you can safely break the current loop, do so immediately. Safe means of breaking the loop include (choose the 3 correct answers) – Equipment E-Stop Switch, Power Disconnect, Insulated Rescue Hook True or False: Covers and finger proof connectors provide protection from inadvertent contact with energized systems – true Screws and wires connecting HV parts to the vehicle are used to keep them bonded and __ - grounded Collision Repair of the 2020 General Motors (GM) Corvette C8 (1/2) Module 1 – Corvette C8 Repair Overview -Corvette History – lets being this course with a brief history overview of the Corvette. The C8 is the latest model, making it the 8 th generation. The image shows each generation of the Corvette, and the list below shows the associated model years: -C1, built from 1953-1962 -C2, built from 1963-1967 -C3, built from 1968-1982 -C4, built from 1984-1996 -C5, built from 1197-2004 -C6, built from 2005-2013 -C7, built from 2014-2019 -C8, built in 2020-present -Redesign Overview – for the 8 th generation, only a single plan has been carried over from the prior generation, the seat pan. The most obvious changes it he transition from a front-engine to a mid-engine design. Some additional design changes include: -The door handles, hatch, and hood release. They have been hidden for a clean appearance and improved aerodynamics -A magnetic ride control, available only with the Z51 performance package -A front suspension lift and more -Trim Levels – -The C8 comes in three different trims which include the: -1LT -2LT -3LT -All three trims come with standard features such as: -Rear park assist -Teen driver -The following features are only available on the 2LT and 3LT Models: -HD front and rear vision cameras -Rear cross traffic alert -Side blind zone alert -Repair Information – GM has continued their approach to developing detailed repair procedures with the C8. As with all repairs, technicians must follow the collision repair procedures found at acdelctds.com when performing repairs on the C8 Corvette. This is a pay website with various subscription options -Support Information – there is additional support information available through my.chevrolet.com. you must create a free account which will grant you access to the information instantly. The website includes videos about the C8’s features, operations, configurations, and more -Special Tools – there are special tools required as part of the Collision Repair Network (CRN) to perform repairs to GM vehicles and some that are specific to the C8. An example of these specific tools is a dedicated or universal fixture system. A comprehensive list can be found at genuinegmparts.com. there are other special tools required to perform certain repairs found in the body repair manual (BRM). An example of these tools is a countersink cutting tool. The list includes the suppliers, part numbering, and ordering information -Aluminum Parts- -The majority of the structural parts that make up the vehicle are made from aluminum. This includes the: -Front structure -Floor -Side structure -Top structure -Most of the rear structure -Replacement of these parts include the use of flow drill screws, structural blind rivets, and impact resistant structural adhesive. To view a full list of materials used in the construction of the C8 Corvette, visit the structure identification section in the BRM -Restricted Structural Parts – GM recommends structural repairs to be made by certified GM Collision Repair Network (CRN) or Cadillac Aluminum Repair Network (CARN) shops. The majority of the structural frame parts for the C8 Corvette are put on parts restriction. GM dealerships, as well as independent collision repair facilities, can enroll at genuinegmparts.com. Note: Exterior bolt-on parts are not restricted -Steel Parts- although the new Corvette is primarily made from aluminum, carbon fiber, and sheet-molded compound (SMC), there is some steel found on the vehicle. There are ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS) reinforcement beams found in the door and a small high-strength steel (HSS) reinforcement inside the windshield reinforcements. The steel parts found in the C8 will not be serviced individually but may be found in a service part assembly -Sheet-Molded Compound (SMC) -An array of SMC parts are used on the Corvette. Detailed repair procedures are available in the BRM for these parts which include the: -Rear compartment panel frame -Body upper rear panel -Rear compartment panel -Side door opening frame -Hood -Liftgate -Quarter outer panels -Side doors -Front fenders -Sectioning or partial-panel replacements is not supported by GM unless specifically documented in a service bulletin or repair procedure -Structural Impact Resistance (IR) Adhesive – GM requires the use of adhesive for many repairs. The use of impact resistant adhesive is used in combination of one or more mechanical fasteners such as structural blind rivets and flow drill screws. Adhesives currently meeting GM service requirements include: -Fusor 2098 -3M 07333 -Pilogrip 5770P -Sem 39757 Structural Impact Resistant Adhesive -Structural Carbon Fiber – the rear bumper reinforcement is one part of the vehicle structure made from carbon fiber. GM does not allow repairs to this reinforcement. The bumper reinforcement can be replaced with or without the rear tub removal. There are two part numbers available for the rear reinforcement, depending on which repair is being performed. If the tub is being removed, the new part comes with the brackets. However, if the tub is not being removed, the new part does not come with the brackets -Lifting and Jacking Considerations- -During lifting or jacking of the vehicle, there are special considerations to keep in mind such as: -Using the special lift pad adapter, part number J-43625 -Having the proper clearance for the lift arm or jack to fit under the vehicle -Supporting the vehicle with jack stands at the opposite end from which major parts are being removed. Also, strap the vehicle to the hoist -The pad adapters are inserted on dedicated spots in the front and rear of the undercarriage. Make sure to use and follow the procedures in the BRM to find these locations -Aluminum Repair Recommendations -GM provides repair recommendations and guidelines for aluminum structure replacement on the following: -Aluminum sheets (stamping) -Extrusions, which can only be replaced -Castings, which can only be replaced -Welding of the aluminum should be performed with a dedicated aluminum MIG welder (220 V/Pulse/200 amp minimum) with 0.035”, 5554 wire. If replacing aluminum panels with impact-resistance adhesive, the use of non-open flame heat can be used, but not exceed 392 degrees Fahrenheit (200 degrees Celsius) for more than 3 consecutive minutes to help release the adhesive -Attachment Methods- -Attachment methods for service parts on the C8 include: -Flow drill screws -Structural blind rivets -Aluminum MIG welding -IR structural adhesive -There is very little MIG welding used during production or service of the C8. GM does not recognize any aluminum STRSW for service use -Floor Structure – -The floor structure is constructed of aluminum, and it is compromised of the following parts: -Number 2 crossbar -Number 3 crossbar -Tunnel panel -Tunell panel reinforcement -Front floor panel -These parts can be replaced individually. Attachment materials for the replacement of these parts include impact resistant (IR) structural adhesive, structural blind rivets, and flow drill screws. Some repairs may call for one or a combination of all materials -Windshield Replacement- -During the replacement of the windshield, the multifunction sensor bracket needs to be removed. It is not necessary to remove the parts from the bracket. If paint repairs of the pinchweld are required, mask the flange bonding area prior to applying the color coat in order to provide a clean, primer-only surface. Approved products for this application include: -BASF DE15 -DuPont 2610 -Sherwin-Williams PSE 4600 and NP70 Martin-Senour 5120 and 5130 -PPG DG90LF SPIES/HECKER 3688/8590-3688/5150-4070/5090 -STANDOX 11158/13320-14653/14980 products are approved for this application -Convertible/Retractable Hardtop – the C8 convertibles have an electric top that includes four motors and four modules. The Hall effect sensors that are part of the system come with the complete top assembly. The motors and modules are the only parts that are serviceable from a mechanical standpoint. There are mechanical overrides that allow the top to be removed. One thing to note is that when removing the top manually, make sure to operate and inspect for any odd movement or binding due to bend linkages Collision Repair of the 2020 General Motors (GM) Corvette C8 (2/2) Module 2 – Mechanical, ADAS, and Electrical Overview -Suspension – the C8 Corvette is available with a couple different suspension optinos including a standard short/long arm (SLA) double wishbone system or a magnetic ride control system. The standard SLA suspension includes forged aluminum upper and L- shaped cast aluminum lower control arms. The magnetic ride control system adds accelerometers in the suspension to actively read the road surface and provide input to the ECU, which makes adjustments to the suspension. It is important to identify which systems the vehicle has. This avoids repair delays resulting from incorrect parts orders. -Front Suspension Lift- there is also a front lift with memory option available on the 2LT and 3LT models. This feature allows the driver to lift the front of the vehicle as much as two inches, at speeds under 24 mph (39 km/h) to help clear obstacles like speed bumps, uneven roads, and curbs. This option integrates GPS, making it possible for the driver to set specific locations that the system will remember and automatically adjust. The front suspension lift system consists of front shock absorbers with built-in two-position lift actuators, hoses, lines, hydraulic fluid reservoir, and an electro-hydraulic control unit. If the hydraulic system is opened for any type of repair, a bleeding procedure needs to be performed. Make sure to follow the Front Hydraulic Suspension Bleeding procedures in the BRM. NOTE: the suspension uses DOT 4 brake fluid, just like the brake system -Battery Sensor Module – -Some vehicles may be equipped with a battery sensor module (BSM) that is used to determine the battery condition, including state of charge and health. This sensor is attached to the negative terminal and monitors all current entering or exiting the battery. Any action that charges or discharges the battery, that is not monitored by the BSM, requires the system to be calibrated. Examples requiring calibration include: -Replacing the battery -The battery is charged outside the vehicle -The BSM is disconnected -When repairing a vehicle equipped with a BSM, it is important to reference the service information to determine if any of the repairs performed require a calibration -Access to Dead Battery – if the battery is dead and you need to gain access to it, there is a manual key on the fob to gain access to the vehicle. The tumbler is located under the driver door vent. There is a latch cable under the dash that allows you to open the front hood. The latch has two points. Pull once to release it and the second time to open it -Transmission Fluid and Fill – when filling the transmission with fluid, it is important that you use TITAN EG FFL4 which is the recommended fluid by GM. Make sure that the containers are shaken to stir the additives before pouring. GM also recommends using a six point metric socket to avoid rounding of filler plug. There are two detailed fluid fill procedures available in the service information. One is for normal driving conditions and the other is for track use -Transmission Park Release Tool – in the event that the vehicle needs to be moved, but does not allow to shift out of park, there is an electronic park brake release procedure. If the procedure does not release the transmission park brake, it may be necessary to mechanically release the transmission park brake. When performing this procedure, the manual park release tool must be held in position to move the vehicle. The tool will break free at 40 Nm (29.50 ft-lbs), so the park pawl cannot be overstressed -Front and Rearview Camera – -The curbview camera system consists of the following parts: -Rearview camera -Frontview camera left -Frontview camera right -Video processing control module -Radio -There are no static calibrations for any of these cameras in the event they are removed or replaced. Calibrations are automatically initiated during each ignition cycle. However, if the video processing module is replaced, programming needs to be performed, followed by a calibration. In the event that a frontview camera is replaced, there is a self-learn cycle that requires the vehicle to be driven -Video Processing Module Programming and Calibration – if a video processing module is replaced, make sure that before programming or diagnostics, the scan tool is equipped with the latest software. Also, make sure that the battery voltage is stable by using a battery maintainer. Do not use a battery charger. The procedures for programming are found in the service procedures. They have step-by-step tasks to include follow-up procedures with links. After you program the module, drive the vehicle to perform a dynamic calibration. There are certain conditions listed in the procedure that will reduce calibration time. If the module is not calibrated properly, you will see an hourglass icon on the surround vision system screen. Additionally, a DTC is set. One or more of the scan tool camera alignment learn parameters will display “Not Calibrated” -ADAS Features and Calibrations -The C8 is equipped with the following ADAS: -Rearview Camera -Heads-Up Display -Side Blind Zone -Rear Park Assist -Lets take a look at some of these systems and calibration/programming requirements -Bumper Repairs Near ADAS – per GM, repairs to bumpers/fascias with ADAS is limited to topcoat refinishing only. Topcoat refinish materials thickness must not exceed 13 mils in thickness. Any bumper or fascia equipped with ADAS that requires substrate repair must be replaced -Vehicle Intelligence Platform -Vehicle Intelligence Platform (VIP) is GM’s new electrical architecture. Some examples of features found on the new platform include: -More bandwidth for advanced vehicles -Energy efficiency -Upgraded diagnostics -Programming in OFF mode, unlike current architectures -Increased security against cyber attacks Collision/Service: Rivian EHS High Voltage Glove Protocol The purpose of this course is to provide you with necessary information on how to perform inspections that comply with the rules and regulations of High Voltage Insulating Rubber Gloves -Rubber Insulators – -High Voltage Insulating Rubber Gloves must be tested annually -After each annual test, there is a one-year timeframe for the rubber insulators to be put into service -They can only be in service for a total of 6 months -All rubber insulators must undergo an end-user inspection prior to each use -Any item which is damaged or has obvious defects must be taken out of service immediately -Leather Protectors – -All leather protectors must undergo an end-user inspection prior to each use -Any item which is damaged or has obvious defects must be taken out of service immediately -Training- -All employees who will be using high voltage gloves will need to complete the high voltage levels 1&amp;2 courses Where to order? Grainger -Testing and Storage -Pre-Task Testing – all High Voltage Insulating Rubber Gloves must undergo an end-user inspection before each use. -Six Month Testing – all High Voltage Insulating Rubber Gloves must undergo a six-month inspection by Grainger. Follow the steps below when submitting for inspection -Step 1 – gather all gloves that need to be tested -Step 2 – get a count of sizes and pairs -Step 3 – Complete a Grainger Dielectic Testing Services Order Form: -1. Input customer information -2. Input the number of pairs in the “Quantity Sent” column and the appropriate row -3. Input the PO# in the “Customer Purchase Order Number” -Step 4 – -1. Create a shipping label and mail it to: -Skarshaug Testing Laboratory, INC 505 South Bell Ames, IA 50010 -2. Create a return label to come back to your location -Step 5 – place the order form and return label in the package and ship the package to the recipient -Storage -HV Glove Bags – ensure gloves are stored in a bag when not in use -HV Cabinet – store the gloves in a high voltage cabinet or an area where other objects will not impact them -Spares – each site should have at least 2 spares in each size -Expiration Date – write the expiration date on the outside of each glove box -Summary – -Always Wear Your Gloves – High Voltage Insulating Rubber Gloves are the first line of defense against shock and electrocution. It is imperative that you follow the guidelines outlined in this course to ensure your safety and the safety of others. If you have questions or concerns, reach out Alex Rich – email – Alex.Rich@grainger.com Rivian EHS Service – email – ServiceEHS@rivian.com Collision Network Team – email – CertifiedCollisionNetwork@rivian.com For questions or comments about this course, please email CollisionTraining@Rivian.com Composite Repair Overview (1/3) -Composites – composites are materials made up of two or more parts or elements -Fiber-Reinforcement – composite plastics contain some type of fiber-reinforcement as one of those elements and is also infused with a resin. The versatility of design possibilities allows a wide range of use. Vehicle makers may use composite materials for a variety of applications such as aerodynamics, fenders, hoods, roofs, doors, trim, or reinforcements as well as other applications. Composites are very common on RVs and heavy trucks. -Plastics – most of the composites used are thermoset type materials; however, thermoplastic composites are used in some applications. All repairs to composite parts should follow original equipment manufacturer (OEM) procedures. While many parts are repairable, I-CAR warns against repairs to parts such as reinforcements or safety-related parts unless an OEM procedure exists. -Repair Materials – parts are strongest when manufactured as the materials are formed together in a mold. Repairs act as a secondary bond that depend on the ability of the repair materials to adhere to the original materials; therefore, it is important to use the repair materials designed for the composite being repaired. -Composite Types – FRP is a general term for Fiber-Reinforced Plastics and represents any fiber a composite contains. Composites can contain glass fibers or carbon fibers. Carbon Fiber-Reinforced Plastics (CFRPs) are simply FRPs where the fibers are made of carbon. FRPs do not always contain carbon fibers but when they do are considered a CFRP -Composite Working Hazards – there are certain working hazards present when working with composites. Product specific safety data sheets (SDS) identify the specific personal safety equipment required when working with composites. Some hazards include: -Dust from sanding, cutting, or grinding the composite -Skin contact with cleaning solvents and repair materials -Inhaling cleaning solvent vapors -Composite materials -* It is important to perform composite repairs in a properly ventilated area -Safety Equipment – when working with composites, the right safety equipment will play a key role in preventing injury and protecting your health. It is important to always wear eye protection to help prevent eye injury. You should wear either safety goggles or safety glass. Fractured or splintered composites, particularly carbon fiber, can be extremely sharp; wearing hand protection such as leather gloves, especially when damaged fibers are exposed, is important. Wear nitrile gloves to avoid skin contact with any type of adhesive used during the repair. Dustless sanding uses a vacuum to collect grinding dust which will help minimize your exposure to it. Sanding dust generated when working with composites, including carbon fiber dust is a lung and eye irritant. You should wear a NIOSH P100 combination respirator to prevent fibers or any gasses from getting into your lungs when grinding or cutting carbon fiber parts. Composite Repair Overview (2/3) FRP Characteristics -Fiberglass and composite repairs may be uncommon but they are still performed in the industry. When working with composites, there are certain abbreviations that are used, which may be confusing at times. Some of the most common abbreviations refer to; Fiber-Reinforced Plastic (FRP) which is a term generally used to describe several different composites. It refers to composites that contain some amount of fiber-reinforcement material, sheet-molded compound (SMC) which is smooth on both sides with no visible fibers, unless the part is cracked. SMCs may require both sides of the part to be finish sanded. An SMC part cannot be repaired using fiberglass repair materials or plastic welds. Do NOT use fiberglass repair materials with SMC because of the fillers and mold release agents in it that can cause adhesion problems when you combine the two. Do NOT use adhesion promoters, or attempt the pinning process on any FRP repairs. -FRP and SMC Identification Continued – SMCs can be used in multiple locations on a vehicle primarily for weight reduction and their ability to be molded into complex shapes. Carbon-Fiber-Reinforced Plastics (CFRPs) specify the type of fiber being used in a part. Carbon fibers are used as the reinforcing material in a CFRP. Long-Fiber-Reinforced Plastic (LFRT) is a type of fiber-reinforcement combined with a type of thermoplastic. This type of FRP is also more specific about the makeup of the part. Traditional fiberglass is made by saturating fiberglass cloth or mat with polyester resin. It is not commonly used in modern vehicle construction, but is very popular in add-on custom parts, as well as water craft and RVs. Fiberglass parts usually start with a gel coat that is applied to a waxed mold; then, the fiberglass and resin are hand applied, or spray applied when parts are manufactured. This leaves a smooth outer surface and a rough backside texture. Repairs can be done using polyester resin and fiberglass cloth or mat -One-Sided Cosmetic Repair – one-sided cosmetic repairs primarily include scratches or gouges. Perform one-sided repairs on damage that does not go through to the backside of the part. One-sided repairs are sometimes done on multiple areas of the same part, and may only require feathering and priming the repair area. Feathering of the repair area might be necessary as well as the use of filler. -Two-Sided Repair – two-sided repairs include cracks, tears, or punctures that go through to the backside or have damage to the fibers. This type of damage requires a filler on the front side and a backing material applied to the backside -FRP Repair Considerations – before making any type of repair it is important to ensure that the repair area is free from dirt and other contaminants. -Step 1 – wash the part with soap and water. Tape over damage to prevent liquids from entering the damaged area. Fibers can wick solvents and trap them in the repair area. -Step 2 – clean with a plastic cleaner recommended by the product maker to remove non water-soluble contaminants -Starting a Single-Sided Repair – to complete a single-sided repair, scuff the part by hand or with a sander using the product makers recommended sandpaper grit. Take care not to damage the fibers. Gradually taper the repair area then clean the area with a vacuum or a dry cloth. Apply repair material to the area following product maker mixing instructions. Sand the repair area to contour after the product has cured, finishing with the paint maker’s recommended grit for primer. -FRP Repair Plan Inspection – FRP parts will not dent or crush, but will crack when struck with enough force. Check FRP parts for secondary damage, such as cracks near mounting locations. Make sure to inspect the backside of the part for any micro-cracking. -Starting a Two-Sided Repair – there are certain steps that should be performed when starting a two-sided repair -Step 1 – remove any loose or damaged fibers with an air saw or a die grinder -Step 2 – taper the front side of the repair. Sand the repair and/or taper the backside of the repair, if recommended. Start by using a grinder with P40 grit. Then, sand with a power sander and P80 grit -Step 3 -clean the repair area with a dry cloth or a vacuum -FRP Backing – use FRP backing on any two-sided FRP repair. This can be in the form of a scrap piece of the same material. It can also be made of a fiberglass cloth or backing tape. This backing must overlap the damaged area. The recommended amount of backing overlap varies by product maker. A general recommendation is a ¾” to 2” overlap on all sides of the damage Fiberglass cloth on left. Scrap material on right -Applying FRP Backing – to apply FRP backing to a repair you will need to take certain steps -Step 1 – apply aluminum tape to the front side to prevent the repair material from falling through the front of the repair area -Step 2 – apply a layer of composite repair adhesive to the repair area in a pool, keeping the static tip submerged in the product. Spread the product while applying firm pressure to ensure tight application of the adhesive. Static tip submerged on the left. Spread product while applying firm pressure on the right. -Step 3 – press the backing material into the composite repair adhesive, making sure to use firm pressure -Step 4 – apply another layer of composite repair adhesive over the backing and cover it, making sure to cover all edges with adhesive. * Note: some product makers have special instructions for their composite repair adhesive. For example, some makers have adhesives that need to be heated in order to cure -Applying Exposed Backing – when applying a backing that is exposed, you must use additional backing material to ensure repair strength and stability. The backing material must be below the final level of the repair. Otherwise, it may be visible and the repair will have to be re-done -Pyramid Patch – a pyramid patch is an alternative method of repair involving a patch built up with alternate layers of the repair product and a fiber cloth or mesh. To make a pyramid patch, you will need to take specific steps. Red = adhesive. White = reinforcement Gray = aluminum tape Black = SMC -Step 1 - clean and scuff the backside repair area, then clean up dust with a vacuum. Once the part is clean, apply aluminum tape to the front side to prevent adhesive from seeping through -Step 2 – Once aluminum tape is applied to the front side, apply the composite repair adhesive to the backing of the backside. Make sure to cover the entire backing patch and allow the layer to cure -Step 3 – remove the aluminum tape from the front side and sand down the top or face of the front side of the part, creating a repair taper -Step 4 – apply another layer of composite repair adhesive followed by more cloth or mesh followed by another layer of composite repair adhesive. Continue to add layers to increase the size of the backing until you achieve the desired thickness. The last layer applied should be adhesive. Allow the patch to cure then sand the front side of the part to a smooth finish. -FRP Front-Side Repair – to perform an FRP front-side repair, remove the plastic film or tape. Sand any exposed repair material that came through from the backside. Next, clean the repair area. Do not use solvents, as the fibers are still exposed and can wick the solvents into the FRP. Overfill the repair material to allow for sanding. The material used for the front side repair may be different than the material used for the backside repair because some product makers have different materials for front-side and backside repairs -Sanding FRP Front-Side Repairs – once the repair material has cured, begin by sanding the repair. Progression from P80 to P220 grit is typical, and you will need to featheredge the repair area with a power sander, and then with a block sander to featheredge and ensure even panel lines. Once the repair area has been block sanded, clean the area with a dry cloth or a vacuum, then check the area for pinholes. Apply a glaze coat if needed. Composite Repair Overview ( 3 /3) -Chopped/Forged Carbon Fiber Composites – chopped carbon fiber comes in a wide variety of appearances, including parts that look similar to SMC. While these parts are smooth on both sides they are typically stronger, lighter, and thinner than traditional SMC. The main different between the two is that a carbon fiber part contains carbon fibers for reinforcement of the composite -Chopped Carbon Fiber – chopped carbon fiber may look similar to a fine febered fiberglass. This type of carbon fiber panel will have the appearance of very fine fibers that lie in random directions. While these parts look similar to fiberglass they are extremely thin, light, and stronger in comparison -Painted Body Panels – identifying painted body panels as carbon fiber may be easy to miss. Use the same steps to identify them as those used for traditional composites -Woven Carbon Fiber – woven carbon fiber parts are lighter and stronger than chopped carbon fiber parts. The way that the different weave patterns are oriented can give the part extreme strength while keeping the part very thin. Structural and non-structural parts can be carbon fiber. Carbon fiber is not limited to body panels. Drive shafts, suspension parts, the passenger cell, structural reinforcements, and even engine and transmission parts can be carbon fiber as well. Exposed weave carbon fiber parts are what everyone thinks of when they hear the words “carbon fiber”. If the part is on the exterior of the vehicle, the part will have a special clear gelcoat or clearcoat from the vehicle maker to give it ultraviolet (UV) protection. -Carbon Fiber Challenges – some challenges with a carbon fiber repair include its inability to be repaired if the damage extends to an edge. It also cannot be duplicated if the exposed carbon fiber weave is cracked. You cannot recreate the exact weave pattern of a tool-faced surface and it cannot be buffed if damage goes through the clearcoat on tooled-faced surfaces -Carbon Fiber Manufacturing Process – epoxies, vinyl ester, polyester, and nylon make up carbon reinforcement resin in many cases -Autoclave Process – the resin and carbon fibers are cured in an autoclave with high heat (300 degrees F) and pressure at 100 psi. An autoclave is kind of like a pressurized over that can be used to create a carbon fiber part. The parts can also be made without the use of an autoclave, but most of the vehicle makers use the autoclave to produce their parts -Carbon Fiber Downfalls – carbon fiber dust can short circuit electrical tools because it is a conductive material. The dust is also very hazardous to breathe, so you should use the appropriate particulate respirator. The raw or exposed carbon fiber can cause galvanic corrosion when it comes into contact with metal. But once it has been imbedded in the resin, the galvanic corrosion problem is eliminated -Carbon Fiber Failure Types – there are several types of failure that can occur when working with composites. Disbonded = top left Punctured or Cracked = top right Core Damaged = bottom -Delamination – delamination of a composite part occurs when one ply separates from another ply. This may happen as a result of low-energy impact or a manufacturing defect, and is sometimes visible if the delamination is near the surface -Disbonding – disbanding occurs when a ply separates from a dissimilar material from the core material ply separation -Core Damage – core damage is damage to the core material of a part that can happen along with any other type of damage -Cracking and Punctures – high energy impacts can crack or puncture composite parts and is the easiest failure type to see. Damage often occurs to the leading edge of a part. Damages that extend to an edge are more complicated to repair. When working with carbon fiber it is also important to inspect the part for secondary damage. * The edge may or may not be repairable depending on whether or not the manufacturer has a suggested repair procedure -Visual Inspections – when performing a visual inspection, a slight wave or ripple may be an indicator of damage. Scuff marks may also indicate an area of damage. Inspect the surrounding area even if damage appears to be minor. The part may have visible damage to the fibers on the backside of the impact area. If access is limited to one side of the part, a visual inspection can be limited. Looking for delamination damage can also be limited. Visual indicators of delamination are not always present on the outside of a part. -Tap Testing – a tap hammer is effective at identifying ply delamination. The technician listens for a change in sound while tapping back and forth across the suspected damage and places a small mark just where the tone changes -Testers – sophisticated test equipment can be used to detect damage to composite parts. Thermal imaging test equipment detects damage and can create an image or graphical representation of the damage using heat variations to create a visual display. Extensive training is required to accurately interpret the information produced by this equipment. You may also use resonance and ultra-sonic testers to detect damage. Both resonance and ultra-sonic testers use frequency waves to detect damage and create an image or graphical representation of the damage. Resonance testers use lower frequency waves to produce this information while ultra-sonic testers use higher frequency waves. Extensive training is required to interpret the information produced by these testers as well. -Repair Options – there are several repair options for carbon fiber depending on the damage and the specific vehicle maker repair procedures. Repair options include buffing or cosmetic repair, composite-type repair, and vacuum bagging. -Cosmetic or Buffing – cosmetic repairs are performed on surface scratches and damage that can be buffed out easily -Composite – composite-type repairs use the same products and techniques as SMC repairs when a pyramid patch is recommended. Keep in mind that this type of repair can only be used for cosmetic applications because they are weaker than carbon fiber and do not have the same performance standards -Vacuum Bagging – there are many different options and steps required to make a vacuum bagging repair. This type of repair involves each of the damaged plies being removed and replaced in the damaged area. The repair is then compressed and heated to cure the repair. Definitions and Operations - Carbon Fiber-Reinforced Plastics (CFRP ) – are simply Fiber-Reinforced Plastics (FRP) where the fibers are made of carbon. FRPs do not always contain carbon fibers but when they do are considered CFRP. -Fiber Glass- parts usually start with a gel coat that is applied to a waxed mold. Then, the fiberglass and resin are hand applied, or spray applied when parts are manufactured. This leaves a smooth outer surface and a rough backside texture. Repairs can be done using resin and fiberglass cloth or mat. -Fiber-Reinforced Plastics (FRP)- represents any fiber a composite contains. Composites can contain glass fibers or carbon fibers. DO NOT use adhesion promoters, or attempt the pinning process on and FRP repair. These parts will not dent or crush, but will crack when struck with enough force. Check FRP parts for secondary damage, such as cracks near mounting location. Make sure to check the backside of the part for any micro-cracking. -FRP Backing- use FRP backing on any two-sided repair. This can be in the form of a scrap piece of the same material. It can also be made of a fiberglass cloth or backing tape. This backing must overlap the damaged area. The recommended amount of backing overlap varies by product maker. A general recommendation is a ¾” to 2” overlap on all sides of the damage. -Applying Exposed Backing – when applying a backing that is exposed, you must use additional backing material to ensure repair strength and stability. The backing material must be below the final level of the repair. Otherwise, it may be visible and the repair will have to be re-done. -Long Fiber-Reinforced Plastic (LFRP) – is a type of fiber-reinforcement combined with a type of thermoplastic. This type of FRP is also more specific about the makeup of the part. -Pyramid Patch – is an alternative method of repair involving a patch built up with alternate layers of the repair product and a fiber cloth or mesh. -Step 1 -Clean and scuff the backside repair area, then clean up dust with a vacuum. Once the part is clean, apply aluminum tape to the front side to prevent adhesive from seeping through. -Step 2- Once aluminum tape is applied to the front side, apply the composite repair adhesive to the backing of the backside. Make sure to cover the entire backing patch and allow the layer to cure. -Step 3- Remove the aluminum tape from the front side and sand down the top or face of the front side of the part, creating a repair taper. -Step 4- Apply another layer of composite repair adhesive followed by more cloth or mesh followed by another layer of composite repair adhesive. Continue to add layers to increase the size of the backing until you achieve the desired thickness. The last layer applied should be adhesive. Allow the patch to cure then sand the front side of the part to a smooth finish. -Sheet Molded Compound (SMC) – is smooth on both sides with no visible fibers unless the part is cracked. SMC’s require both sides of the part to be finish sanded. An SMC part cannot be repaired using fiberglass repair materials or plastic welders. DO NOT use fiberglass repair materials with SMC because of the fillers and mold release agents in it that can cause adhesion problems when you combine the two. - Single Sided Repair - primarily include scratches or gouges. Perform one-side repairs on damage that does not go through to the backside of the part. One-sided repairs are sometimes done on multiple areas of the same part and may require only feathering and priming the repair area. Feathering of the repair area might be necessary as well as the use of filler -Step 1 -wash the part with soap and water. Tape over damage to prevent liquids from entering the damaged area. Fibers can wick solvents and trap them in the repair area. -Step 2 -clean with a plastic cleaner recommended by the product maker to remove non water-soluble contaminants -To complete a single sided repair, scuff the part by hand or with a sander using the product makers recommended sandpaper grit. Take care not to damage the fibers. Gradually taper the repair area then clean the area with a vacuum or a dry cloth. Apply repair material to the area following product maker mixing instructions. Sand the repair area to contour after the product has cured, finishing with the paint makers recommended grit for repair -Two-Sided Repair - include cracks, tears, or punctures that go through to the backside or have damage to the fibers. This type of damage requires a filler on the front side and a backing material applied to the backside. -Step 1 – remove any loose or damaged fibers with an air saw or a die grinder. (AKA literally cut a circle around the loose or damaged fibers) -Step 2 -taper the front side of the repair. Sand the repair and/or taper the backside of the repair, if recommended. Start by using a grinder with P40 grit. Then, sand with a power sander and P80 grit. -Step 3 -clean the repair area with a dry cloth or a vacuum. -Applying FRP Backing -Step 1 -Apply aluminum tape to the front side to prevent the repair material from falling through the front of the repair area -Step 2 -Apply a layer of composite repair adhesive to the repair area in a pool, keeping the static tip submerged in the product. Spread the product while applying firm pressure to ensure tight application of the adhesive. -Step 3- Press the backing material into the composite repair adhesive, making sure to use firm pressure. -Step 4 -Apply another layer of composite repair adhesive over the backing and cover it, making sure to cover all edges with adhesive. Note: some product makers have special instructions for their composite repair adhesive. For example, some makers have adhesives that need to be heated in order to cure. -FRP Front-Side Repair- To perform an FRP Front side repair, remove the plastic film or tape. Sand any exposed repair material that came through from the backside. Next, clean the repair area. Do not use solvents, as the fibers are still exposed and can wick the solvents into the FRP. Overfill the repair material to allow for sanding. The material used for the front side repair may be different than the material used for the backside repair because some product makers have different materials for the front-side and backside repairs. -Sanding FRP Front-Side Repair- Once the material has cured, begin by sanding the repair. Progression from P80 to P220 grit is typical, and you will need to featheredge the repair area with a power sander, and then with a block sander to featheredge and ensure even panel lines. Once the repair area has been block sanded, clean the area with a dry cloth or a vacuum, then check the area for pinholes. Apply a glaze coat if needed. Convenience Systems Operation and Service (1/4) Vehicle Access -Introduction to convenience systems – comfort and convenience systems used to be found primarily in luxury vehicles. Now many of these systems come as standard features, such as: -Keyless entry -Security -Integrated smartphone apps -Remote app-based vehicle functionality -Power Lock Systems- -Power lock systems typically include: -Door lock assemblies -Cables or linkages -Switches -Actuators -Control modules -Control modules process commands to lock or unlock the vehicle. When a specific speed is reached or when the transmission is shifted into DRIVE, the locks may automatically engage. On some vehicles, these settings can be customized in the driver information center. Newer vehicles may use interior switches, key fobs, and smartphone applications to lock and unlock vehicle. -Part Replacement Considerations- -To service power locks, you will need to access the inside of the door or tailgate. Some considerations for removing the lock actuator include the following: -The actuator is usually part of the latch assembly -The outside handle, glass, and run channels may need to be removed -The latch assembly may come out with a door module assembly that includes the window regulator -The cables and linkage can be difficult to remove and may be attached to plastic parts that can break -Follow the removal procedures found in the service information to avoid damage and be efficient. Remember that an adjustment, calibration, or initialization may need to be performed after parts are reinstalled -Keyless Entry – -a keyless entry system controls access to the vehicle without using a traditional mechanical key. These systems typically use an electronic control module to activate motors or actuators that control the vehicle locks. Keyless entry may be divided into two categories: -Active keyless entry, which requires a button be pushed on the fob to unlock the doors or deck lid -Passive keyless entry, where the fob only needs to be within a certain distance from the vehicle to unlock the doors and other closure panels. Having the fob inside the vehicle will allow the engine to start using a push-button -Keyless Entry Fob – some vehicles have a button on the outside door handle. This allows you to lock the doors if the key fob is in your pocket. The antenna system knows where the key fob (remote) is in relationship to the vehicle. It consists of several antennas with small detection zones. Knowing this and their locations can assist troubleshooting when a fob may have an intermittent communication issue. Fobs may be customizable to allow different driver profiles to be assigned. The vehicle settings will adjust depending on the fob used. Decklids may have a button on the outside to open them, but you may need the fob on you for it to work -Keyless Entry by Smartphone – most vehicles manufactured today have some way to integrate a smartphone with the vehicles system. Many now allow you to use a smartphone as your key fob. Some advantages to using a phone are: -You do not need to carry a fob -Phones may offer better encryption than a fob -You can transfer the “Key” to someone else that needs to use the vehicle -Keyless Entry Parts – common parts of a keyless entry system include: -Keyless entry transmitters -Antennas which are located throughout the interior to cover the entire vehicle -Control modules -Exterior lighting -Exterior speaker for confirmation chirp or beep -Security System – the typical theft-deterrent system is designed to sound an alarm and flash the exterior lights during forced entry. These systems have been improved over the years to include advanced electronics that detect intrusion and limit theft. Some factory security systems include: -A telematics subscription service to locate and strategically disable the vehicle if in motion -Engine immobilizer that activate if anything other than the proper key is used to start the vehicle -A security horn, which may be powered independent of the battery -A security indicator lamp -Security System Sensors- -Theft-deterrent systems operate when the doors are locked. The system may monitor interior movement, noise, latches, and vehicle movement. It sets off an alarm when specific criteria are met. Some of the sensors include: -Motion detection sensors inside the vehicle -Listening devices to detect the sound of glass breaking -Inclination sensors to detect an attempt to raise the vehicle -Some systems use the ajar switches on the doors, hood, and other closure panels. These may be checked three times per second by the security system to make sure the panels remain closed. If a switch status changes, it will set off the alarm -Security System Smartphone Features -smartphone alerts may be available through subscription services. They may include alerts for an attempt to enter the vehicle or when the vehicle is stolen. Depending on the system, additional features may include: -Ignition block -Stolen vehicle slow down -GPS location service -Some vehicles include a subscription service with the purchase of a new vehicle for a limited time. If it isn’t renewed after that time, many of the features may not be available. Additionally, the subscription service may not work if the vehicle is taken out of the country it was purchased in -Service Considerations -Most theft-deterrent system parts are integrated with other vehicle systems. Look for related diagnostic trouble codes (DTC) when performing a vehicle scan to aid in the troubleshooting process. Common system faults may include the: -System not arming -Alarm not sounding or the proper lights not flashing -System arming when the doors or lids are open -The horns are the most susceptible part to incur damage in a front-end collision. Working on a vehicle with low-battery condition may cause the security system to activate or act erratically. Disconnecting and reconnecting the battery may also activate a security system. Take steps to keep the battery charged and reference service information for battery disconnect and reconnect procedures Convenience Systems Operation and Service (2/4) Power Accessories – Mirrors, Glass, and Wipers -Power Mirrors- -In addition to power adjustment and features of the mirror itself, power side mirrors on vehicles may be integrated with a number of systems including: -Blind spot alert -360 degree surround view -Park assist -On some vehicles, the mirrors may angle downward when the vehicle is in reverse. It may be programmed to automatically fold in when the ignition is off and the vehicle is locked. Always reference vehicle-maker service information to identify which systems are on the vehicle. Be sure to perform all necessary calibrations and initializations -Power Mirror Operation – Power mirrors are typically controlled by a switch that directs power to the appropriate mirror control circuit when the directional button is pushed. On older vehicles, pushing the button may have provided power directly to the mirror motor. On modern vehicles, pushing the button typically sends a signal to a control module that sends the command signal to the mirror motor relay.
