System parts commonly associated with a power mirror assembly include: -Control switches -A fuse or relay -Position sensors -Mirror motors or drive mechanisms -A motor for the power folding option -Power Mirror Options – optional equipment that may be included with a power mirror assembly includes: -Heated mirror glass for defrosting -Electrochromatic or auto-dimming mirrors to reduce headlamp glare -Memory systems to recall a previous adjustment -Turn signals in the mirror -Telescoping mirrors -Passenger side park assist mirrors -Puddle lighting -Power Windows – -A power window system: -Uses a two-directional motor -May use a cable and pulley system -May use a spring-assist device to reduce the amount of current required to lower and raise a window -To operate a simple system, the wiring harness transmits a signal from the switch to the relay. A relay is used because the switches cannot handle the amount of current necessary to power the window. The relay sends current to the motor that powers the window. Most newer vehicles send the signal from the switch to a control module. The control module then signals a relay to power the motor to either raise or lower the window -Power Window System Parts – system parts commonly associated with a power window assembly include a: -motor -switch -relay -regulator -wiring harness -control module -fuse -Door Glass- -most newer vehicles use a cable-type regulator that mounts in the center of the glass. The glass is guided up and down by run channels at each end. The regulator is typically bolted to the door shell and the glass is attached in a variety of methods including: -rubber insulated clamps -Plastic clips -Ball and socket type connectors -bolts -Rivets -Some vehicles have express up and down features that require initialization if the battery is disconnected or the regulator is unplugged. These also have anti-pinch functions that can be checked following vehicle service information. Rear doors may have power sunshades that can be activated to block the sun for passengers -Vent and Sliding Glass – power vent glass is usually limited to quarter panel glass on vans or sport-utility vehicles (SUV). They use a window that is bolted in at the front with a flexible hinge and a motor at the rear. The motor is activated to move the rear part of the window out. Typically, these have a pinchweld-mounted weatherstrip in the window opening to seal the glass when closed. Power sliding rear windows in pickup trucks are a different type of powered glass. The center of the backglass opens via a motor and cable system. Motors may be available separately, but the cables usually need to be replaced with a complete backglass assembly -Sunroof Glass – a power sunroof assembly opens and closes using either a motor and worm gear system or a cable and pulley system. Both systems use a reversible, or bi-directional, motor. Some sunroofs slide open between the headliner and the roof panel. Other types open up and above the roof and slide back. They may also have a vent feature that only raises the rear of the sunroof for ventilation. Some sunroofs also have a powered sunshade. -Mechanical Inspection – -When inspecting the operation of power sunroof assemblies: -Look for the full range of travel -Verify the proper operation of the express feature -Verify proper operation of the vent open feature -If the glass panel is broken, a close inspection is necessary. Look for broken glass that may be stuck in the tracks or drive cables. Once all broken glass is cleaned up, cycle the sunroof through its full range of travel, looking for proper operation. If there was an airbag deployment, the sudden increase in cabin pressure may force the glass upward and break the sunroof guides. When installing the glass be sure it is adjusted to fit flush with the roof according to service manual specifications to avoid leaks or wind noise -Sunroof Timing – the timing of the drive motor and cables that move the glass panel in a sliding sunroof assembly is critical to the proper operation of the unit. The timing and automatic features may be set or adjusted by a variety of methods. Procedures used to initialize or adjust timing are typically vehicle-specific and may require a scan tool. Always reference vehicle-maker service information to identify proper service and adjustment procedures and any calibration or initialization requirements -Heated Glass – nearly all newer vehicles are equipped with heated glass systems. One example is the rear defroster, which typically uses a thin electrical element to warm the glass. When the switch is pressed, power is sent to the system control module which energizes a relay to power the defroster grid. Resistance in the grid heats to warm the glass. When the time cycle is complete, the relay is de-energized to open the circuit. A timer circuit is used to prevent battery drain and charging system failure. Most have a 10-minute cycle. Additional services that may use a heating element include: -A front facing, windshield-mounted camera -An adaptive cruise control radar module -Side mirrors -Heated Windshield – some vehicle makers use heated windshields which are made by laying a nearly invisible web of tungsten wires within the windshield glass. Heated windshields may also feature a heated wiper park area that resembles and works in conjunction with the backglass defroster. When the rear defroster switch is pressed, it typically also energizes the heated mirror system, if equipped. The defroster relay energizes the heated mirror grid. The computer-controlled timer controls both the heated mirror and defogger. Advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) that use a heated grid system typically work automatically. They use the data from the ambient temperature sensor to determine when to activate -Heated Glass Service Considerations – tests used to determine electrical problems with heated glass may include voltage or continuity. Voltage tests are typically done at the defroster grid terminals to confirm the appropriate voltage is reaching the defroster grid. Continuity checks are done to confirm there are no breaks in the defroster grid lines. Using a thermal imaging camera to view defroster grid operation is also a good way to detect issues with all or part of a grid not functioning. Repairs to the defroster grid system may include repairing: -Loose terminals with a conductive epoxy or solder -Small sections of damaged grid lines with a conductive paint -Glass-mounted antennas using a grid similar to a defroster may be repaired in a similar manner -Windshield Wiper System Features – windshield wiper systems include variable speeds with intermittent wipers and may include a wipe-after-wash feature. Other systems may use depressed park. This features engages the wiper motor so that it drops the wipers below the hood when they are not in use. -Wiper Operation- -When the wiper switch is activated -Current is sent to a control module which triggers a relay to send power to the wiper motor -The activated wiper motor rotates a spindle that is connected to the wiper linkages -Wiper systems only operate when the ignition switch is in the ON or ACC position -Rain-Sensing Wipers – -Rain-sensing wiper systems have a windshield-mounted infrared sensor. It is located on the interior side of the windshield in a housing near the base of the rearview mirror. The sensor is designed to detect rain on the windshield and activate the wipers when needed. Parts included in the rain-sensing wiper assembly include the: -Rain sensor -Wiper motor -Wiper control module -Wiper arm assemblies -Rain sensors use a clear gel pad between the sensor and windshield for proper operation. If the gel pad is damaged from the windshield replacement or sensor removal, it needs to be replaced. Some pads are replaceable, and some vehicle makers only offer them as part of the new sensor assembly -Rain-Sensing Wiper Operation – in general, the rain-sensing system operates like this: -the user sets the system to the desired sensitivity -the wiper switch is in one of the ON settings or on an automatic setting -the rain sensor sends an infrared beam of light towards the windshield -The infrared light reflects back off the outside surface of the windshield into a photo diode sensor -When rain or other precipitation is present, the light is deflected and less light makes it back to the photo diode -When the sensor module detects reduced light, a signal is sent to the wiper motor module to automatically start the wipers. -Always check the vehicle-maker service information for system description and operation Convenience Systems Operation and Service (3/4) Power Accessories – Seats and Doors -Seat System Features – -There are many options in seating systems including: -Heaters and coolers -Adjustable lumbar -Massage -Seat position memory systems -Advancements in seat technology are, for the most part, designed to increase comfort and reduce driver fatigue. One such technology is climate-controlled seats. These seats provide heat through electronically-controlled heating elements and ventilation through perforated seat covers. They may be integrated with the air conditioning system. The seats cannot heat and cool at the same time -Climate-Controlled Seating System Parts- -The serviceable climate-controlled seat system parts may include: -An air filter -cushion and backrest manifolds -cushion and backrest foam pads and seat covers -A heat/cool seat switch -Fuses -Ventilation ducts -Blowers -Heating elements -Some climate control seats use a blower fan combined with a Peltier, or thermos-electric device. This device can become hot or cold depending on the polarity. The fan then blows over it and up through the cushions of the seat -Lumbar Support and Massage – some seat systems use an air pump to inflate a bladder located inside the seat. An example of this is found in power lumbar systems. The lumber switch controls the lumbar air pump some seats use a manual adjustment knob to move built-in supports in the back of the seat to adjust lumbar support. Another type of lumbar support uses electric motors to adjust the support inside the seat back. These types may be adjustable up and down as well as the amount of lumber support. Air bladders and electric motors are also used for other seat functions including: -a massage function -vibration alerts -adjustable side bolsters -Power Seats and Memory Systems – power seats use switches, relays, sensors, and motors similar to other power accessories. There are many different power configurations for seats that determine the number of directions the seat can be moved and tilted. Some vehicles makers feature memory systems that allow drivers to store and quickly recall their desired seat positions with the push of a single button. In some vehicles, this memory feature may also store pedal, steering column, and outside mirror position preferences -Stowable Seats – some vehicles, such as minivans and SUVs, may have folding seats designed to be folded down and stowed or removed from the vehicle to create additional cargo space. In some cases, these seats may be power-operated and use switches, sensors, and motors much like standard power seats. Removal of a power folding seat may require the use of a scan tool for reinstallation. Always reference the service information as these seating systems may require calibration or initialization procedures. -Seat Removal Considerations – seats are mounted to the floor pan through the carpet. To access the seat-mounting hardware, adjustable seats may need to be in complete forward or rearward position. Refer to the vehicle-maker service information for proper removal procedures. Seats with a manual adjuster may require them to be placed in the full forward position to remove the rear-attaching hardware. If the seat is not in the forward position when removing the hardware, the seat track can spring forward causing personal injury. Never sit on the seat when the seat and track assembly are not installed. Some occupant classification systems use seat-track-mounted sensors and may require calibration if the vehicle has collision damage or the seat hardware was loosened. -Power Sliding Doors – when the OPEN switch is pressed, the power sliding door module sends a signal to the latch release actuator. Once the door is released by the latch actuator, the driver assembly actuator slides the door. The door latch switch or an optical sensor may help determine the position of the door and sends the information to the power sliding door modules. Depending on the vehicle, the driver motor may be located inside the sliding door or behind the quarter panel interior trim panel. Typically, there is either a cable system or a gear drive that mechanically opens and closes the door. -Operation by Motion Sensor – the hands-free access system only operates when the key fob is within range and only under specific conditions. Motion sensors are programmed to respond to a specific sequence of movement. In general, this is the user approaching the vehicle, and the foot kicking in and then out. This is to prevent the door from opening while the vehicle is in motion, or when animals, balls, or other objects pass near the sensor. As an additional safety feature, the key fob must be outside the vehicle. Once triggered, the sensors alert the control module, which sends a command to the electric motor to either open or close the door. -Power Sliding Door Parts – system parts commonly associated with a power sliding door assembly include: -A drive assembly actuator -A latch release actuator -A position sensor -Door switch plungers and pads -A flexible wire harness attached to the lower track roller assembly -A power sliding door latch switch -An ON/OFF switch for both doors -Liftgates and Decklids- -Liftgates, decklids, and even truck tailgates are available with convenience features. Optional features include: -Remote release -Power opening and closing -Hands-free operation -Integrated step assemblies -Additionally, some liftgates have an adjustable opening height to accommodate parking situations that have a low ceiling. This is accomplished by monitoring a liftgate position sensor. When the command to open or close is sent to the control module, the system will check the status of other systems such as the gear selector position and door ajar switches. If all parameters are met, the command to the latch and power motor is sent -Remote Operation – remote operation can be activated by: -A key fob button -An interior-mounted switch -An exterior switch on the gate -Motion sensors under the rear bumper -Anti-Pinch Safety Systems – anti-pinch safety systems may have sensors along the edge of a power door or gate. If the sensors detect an obstruction while closing, they send a message to the control module to reverse operation. Another type of system monitors the current draw of the power motor assembly to detect any resistance while opening or closing. If the resistance is detected, the motor reverse to avoid injury or damage -Motorized Latches – the latch assemblies on power doors, gates, and decklids have an actuator to release them when commanded open. To close, they usually have a motorized mechanism. This allows the panel to close slowly to the secondary step of the latch. The drawdown latch then pulls the panel all the way closed. -Power Door System Parts – the alignment of the door, strikers, hinges, rollers, tracks, and associated parts is critical for proper operation. If the body structure is out of alignment, it can affect power door operation. If a malfunction is detected in the power door assembly, you should scan for applicable DTCs. Also reference service information to understand the system and its operation. Parts that need to be checked include: -Power door position sensors -Motion sensors -Wire harnesses or connectors -The power motor assembly -The control module -Related system parts such as the gear selector position sensor -Initialization – power doors, gates, or decklids may need to be initialized to operate properly. Initialization may be required when disconnecting the battery or replacing a power door control module. Each make and model will differ on the procedures. Some may require a scan tool and others may require you to operate the closure panel through and entire open and close cycle Convenience Systems Operation and Service (4/4) Driver Information -Infotainment Systems – -Infotainment systems are available on most vehicles as standard or optional equipment. Infotainment system features may include: -GPS navigation -Satellite radio -Wi-Fi hotspot connectivity -Multi-zone audio/video control, which allows passengers to select individual audio/video programming -Climate control -The system may also provide access to a variety of other vehicle systems as well -Navigation Systems- -navigation systems receive signals from satellites to identify the vehicles position and direction by combining received data with information obtained from an onboard computer with various sensors. The GPS transmits geolocation and time information, which is received by a GPS receiver located in the vehicles navigation system. Navigation systems also: -Calculate travel distance -Display maps on the navigation system screen -Check GPS and sensor information against a map database -Depending on the system, other features such as weather data or points of interest may be display as well -Navigation System Parts – navigation system parts commonly include: -A GPS or satellite antenna. These may also be known as a shark-fin antenna on the roof. However, the actual GPS antenna may be located somewhere else in the vehicle. -Wheel speed and other dynamic sensors to track the vehicles movement if the GPS signal is temporarily lost -A display which is commonly a touch screen -A navigation computer -Coaxial cables -Antenna Service Considerations – when servicing a GPS or satellite antenna, considerations need to be made for removal depending on where it is located. It may require accessing the backside of the panel to which it is mounted. Antennas located on the roof typically require removing or repositioning the headliner to access the mounting fastener and antenna wiring. Most roof-mounted satellite antennas are fastened with a bolted retainer. There are some that are bonded to the roof with urethane adhesive making removal difficult. The GPS antenna may be located under the top surface of the dash or another location with clear view of the sky. Occasionally, the system may not immediately recognize the correct location of the vehicle and require moving it outdoors away from power lines or other sources of interference -Audio- -The audio system works with the infotainment system to: -Play music -Make phone calls -Give voice commands -Most audio systems include speaks and a microphone for use with voice commands and other communication options -Driver Information Center- -Many vehicles have a driver information center in the instrument panel. Drivers have options to control and customize the look and information it displays. This may even include the look and style of the gauges. In addition to speed, RPM, and engine data, information displayed may include: -Warnings and malfunction indicator lamp (MIL) alerts -Lane keep assist -Adaptive cruise control -Performance data -Performance data can include average gas mileage, trip timers, and odometers. Hybrid and electric vehicles may show how much battery life is left or when the regenerative brakes are charging the battery. The information center, along with steering wheel buttons, may be used to access menus to select vehicle options -Head-up Display – a head-up display shows vehicle speed and driver assistance information on the windshield. an image is projected and reflected on the windshield within the drivers line of sight. Essential information can be featured on the head-up display with the intent to keep the drivers attention on the road ahead. Every vehicle is different, some head-up displays may display ADAS related information and navigation directions. Use vehicle-maker service information and the correct windshield glass when servicing a vehicle with a head-up display. Some service information may abbreviate head-up display as HUD -Head-Up Display Service - -Calibration of the head-up display may be required if any of the following parts are removed and reinstalled or replaced: -The head-up display -The windshield -The dash assembly -A scan tool and targets may be needed when calibrating a head-up display. Be sure to follow vehicle-maker service information Damage Analysis of Convenience Systems Keyless Entry Systems -Keyless Entry System – a keyless entry system controls access to the vehicle without using a traditional mechanical key. These systems typically use an electronic control module to activate motors that control the vehicle locks. Keyless entry can be divided into two categories, active and passive. -Active Keyless Entry – active keyless entry is the traditional keyless entry where a key fob is pressed to unlock the doors or trunk, or to start the engine -Passive Keyless Entry – passive keyless allows the vehicle entry without pushing buttons on a key fob. Simply carrying a pass key will unlock the door and start the engine with a push button. This system may also be part of the vehicle push-button starting system -Passive Keyless Entry Operation – a passive entry system does not require the user to push a button. Instead, the system uses a transmitter in the key fob and multiple, low-frequency antennas inside and outside of the vehicle. When the transmitter is within range, the antennas receive signals from the keyless entry transmitter, allowing various features to operate. For example, the doors may unlock when a front outside door handle is gripped. If the driver door is gripped, only the driver door will unlock. If the passenger door is gripped, all doors unlocked. -red = exterior antenna range -yellow = interior antenna range -Passive Keyless Entry Parts – common parts of a passive keyless entry system include: -Keyless entry transmitters -Antennas (located throughout the interior to cover the entire vehicle) -An exterior antenna -Control modules -Exterior lighting (if equipped) -Push-Button Start – vehicles with passive keyless entry may be equipped with a push-button start feature. In order to use the push-button start: -1) Be sure the key fob is inside the vehicle -2) Depress the brake pedal -3) Push the start button -4) The vehicle power management receives a signal from the control module that the proper key is in the vehicle -5) Engine starts -Keyless Entry Innovations – some vehicle makers may offer an optional key that allows special parameters to be set for the vehicle while in use. This may be desirable when the vehicle will be operated by a family teenager or fleet operator. The key: -Has a built-in microchip that gives special instructions to the vehicle -Can be switched to limit the top speed of the vehicle -Limits the audio volume of the sound system -Encourages seat belt us with a chiming pattern and muting of the sound system -Includes additional features, such as an earlier low-fuel warning and speed alert chime -Parts Susceptible to Collision Damage – key parts susceptible to collision damage may include the: -Electronic control module -Junction box -Wireless door lock buzzer -Door and handles -Note: all of the electrical parts are generally located under the hood, on the driver side, by the strut tower. However, the system is tied into the power door locks, switches, exterior and interior electrical key oscillators (which receive request signals from the electronic control unit (ECU) and create an actuation area around the doors or the vehicle interior), etc. These can also be damaged in a collision, and typically require a scan tool and service information to diagnose malfunctioning parts. -Diagnostic Procedure – when analyzing damage to the passive keyless entry system, verify that the keyless entry system works. If there are problems with the push-button start feature, a malfunction code may be stored in the power management control module or an indicator lamp on the start button may flash when a fault is detected, if the vehicle is equipped. In either case, you should investigate further to determine what repairs are necessary. Use of a symptoms table may be useful in the diagnosis of any non-operation. Diagnosis may require a scan tool, additionally a scan tool may be used for calibration following repairs. Theft-Deterrent Systems -Theft-Deterrent Systems – the typical theft-deterrent system is designed to sound an alarm and/or flash the exterior lights during forced entry. These theft-deterrent systems have been improved over the years to include more advanced electronics to detect intrusion and limit theft -Types – there are three common types of theft-deterrent systems: -Telematics subscription service -Listening devices -Engine immobilizers -Note: A telematics subscription service that has a Stolen Vehicle Slowdown feature allows the service provider to slow down, stop, and recover stolen vehicles that might be involved in a high-speed chase. This feature is in addition to features that use GPS technology to locate lost or stolen vehicles. -Parts – parts that make up a theft-deterrent system include: -Door lock control system parts -A control module -A security horn, which may be powered independent of the battery -A security indicator lamp -Door, hood, trunk ajar switches -A glass breakage sensor and a glass breakage sensor ECU, if equipped -Operation – the theft-deterrent system operates when the doors are locked, either manually or remotely. The system monitors movement, noise, latches, and/or vehicle movement, and sets off an alarm when specific criteria are met. On some systems, the switches are checked three times per second to make sure switches are closed. These systems may also have a movement sensor that detects movement inside the vehicle. This particular system requires windows to be closed for proper operation. Some systems have a heartbeat sensor. The heartbeat sensor is used to detect the presence of someone inside the vehicle, with an indicator on the key fob. -Common System Faults – the use of a scan tool may be required to diagnose theft-deterrent system faults. Common faults may include the: -System not arming -Alarm not sounding and/or the proper lights not flashing -System arming when the doors/lids are open -Replacement Considerations – during damage analysis, note that the horns are most susceptible to damage and that most theft-deterrent system parts are integrated with other systems Rain Sensing Wipers System -Rain Sensing Wipers – a popular option, especially in rainy areas, is rain sensing wipers. Rain sensing wipers are designed to detect moisture on the windshield and auto-start the wipers when on. Contrary to the name, it is not the wipers that sense rain on the windshield, but rather it is an infrared sensor module. The sensor module is mounted on the interior side of the windshield, in a housing either next to, or part of, the base of the rearview mirror. The module connector is also usually in the same housing. -Parts – parts included in the rain sensing wiper assembly include the: -Wiper assembly (blades, linkage, etc.) -Wiper motor -Rain sensor -Junction boxes -Wiper motor module -Operation – always check the maker’s service information for the specific system operation. In general, the rain sensing system operates like this: -With rain sensing wiper systems, the user sets the system to the desired sensitivity -The wiper switch is on one of the ON settings, or on an automatic setting -The rain sensor sends an infrared beam of light toward the cabin side of the windshield and the light reflects back into a photo diode sensor -When rain is present, the light is deflected and less light makes it back to the photo diode -When the sensor module detects reduced light, a signal is sent to the wiper motor module to automatically start the wipers -Damage Analysis – the presence of a housing next to the rearview mirror with an electrical connection, or a flat sensor pressed against the glass on the front side of the mirror are indications that the vehicle is equipped with a rain sensor. When checking for damage, look for cracks or damage to the windshield near the sensor module that would affect its operation. In addition, check the housing and the electrical connection. Initial troubleshooting described by some vehicle makers is as simple as applying water to the windshield. -Replacement Considerations – typically, a special windshield and system calibration are not required. The sensor is generally adhered with special tape that is supplied with the part Climate-Controlled Seats System -Climate-Controlled Seats – advancements in seat technology are mostly designed to increase comfort and reduce driver fatigue. One such technology is climate-controlled seats. Climate-controlled seats: -Provide heat and ventilation -May be tied into the HVAC system -Cannot heat and cool at the same time -Climate-Controlled Seat Operation – heated and cooled seat operation includes switches located on the instrument panel, center console, or door panels, and seat temperature monitored by the seat module -Seat Heaters/Coolers – some vehicles have a climate-controlled seat system that uses seat heaters to provide heat and ventilation (blower units) to provide cooling. The cooling system uses interior ambient air, rather than air from the A/C unit. Similarly, the heat function does not use heated air. The climate-controlled seat system seat heaters have adjustable temperature areas and fixed temperature areas. Adjustable Temperature areas are used for the shoulder and lumbar areas and the area of the thighs and knees that easily become cold. -HVAC Temperature Control – other vehicles may use a thermo-electric device to heat or cool the air supply. The climate-controlled seat system is able to heat and cool the front seats. Each climate-controlled seat is operated by push buttons on the HVAC module located on the instrument panel. Each front seat temperature is then monitored and controlled by the dual climate-controlled seat module located on the passenger front seat track -Climate-Controlled Seat Parts – the serviceable climate-controlled seat system parts may include: -An air filter -Cushion and backrest manifolds -Cushion and backrest foam pads and trim covers -A heat/cool seat switch -Fuses -Ventilation ducts -Blowers -Damage Analysis – diagnosing issues with the operation of the climate-controlled seats requires several tools, such as wrenches, screwdrivers, and a scan tool. Use a scan tool to check problems that may set DTCs. Check for signs of obvious physical damage, such as torn seat covers, damaged seat frames, or punctures in the seat cushions. Reference the vehicle maker diagnostic charts, when available, to diagnose the problem Hands-Free Access Systems -Hands-Free Access System – the hands-free access system allows the user to open doors or the tailgate of the vehicle by waving a foot under a sensor, provided the key fob is within the sensor range -Hands-Free Access System Parts – common hands-free access system parts include the following: -Keyfob -Motion sensors -Controller module -Electric motor -Pinch sensors -Note: Depending on the vehicle maker design, the motion sensors may be located in different locations under the vehicle. -Hands-Free Access System Operation – the hands-free access system only operates when the keyfob is within range and only under specific conditions. In order to prevent the liftgate from opening while the vehicle is in motion, or when animals, balls, etc. pass near the sensor, the motion sensors are programmed to respond to a specific sequence of movement. In general, this is the user approaching the vehicle, and the foot kicking in and then out. As an additional safety feature, the keyfob must be outside the vehicle. Once triggered, the sensors alert the control module, which sends a command to the electric motor to either open or close -Hands-Free Access System Damage Analysis – any collision damage to areas near the sensors is likely to result in damage to motion sensors or require calibration of the motion sensors. Always refer to vehicle maker service information to identify sensor location, type, and service requirements. Damage Analysis of Brake Systems (1/2) Basic Brake System Operation -Basic Brake System Overview – a vehicle in motion has kinetic energy. A brake system applies friction at each wheel to slow down or stop vehicle motion. The friction turns kinetic energy into heat, which is absorbed and dissipated in the brake system parts. Service brake systems use hydraulic pressure to slow or stop wheel movement. Hydraulic fluid is used because it does not compress. Therefore, when pressure is exerted on the fluid, it is transmitted equally in all directions in the system, regardless of distance. A typical basic brake system is made up of the following parts: -Brake rotor -Brake drum -Brake caliper -Wheel cylinder -Master cylinder -Lines and hoses -Brake fluid -Brake fluid reservoir -Disc Brake Assembly – -Key parts of a disc brake assembly include the: -Caliper assembly and disc brake pads -Rotor and splash shield -Caliper Assembly – -The caliper assembly is bolted to the spindle assembly and contains the: -Caliper piston(s) -Caliper piston seal -Caliper piston dust boot -Bleeder screw -Hardware used with the caliper assembly includes the: -Spring clip, which holds the inboard pad to the piston -Caliper bushings, which allow movement along the anchoring bolts -Anti-squeal shims -Note: Do not apply brakes with the caliper or rotor removed -Disc Brake Pads -Have lining material riveted or bonded to the backing plates -May contain asbestos -Can be semi-metallic -Wear with use and should be inspected if parts are removed for collision repairs -Rotor and Splash Shield -A rotor may: -Be attached to the wheel spindle -Have vents for cooling -Be placed over the studs on the wheel hub -Be held in place by the road wheel or secured with screws to the hub assembly -A splash shield: -Is bolted to the steering knuckle on the front brakes -Minimizes water, dirt, and dust from accumulating on the brake assembly -May be designed to direct air flow to help cool the brakes -Drum Brake Assemblies – include the drum, backing plate, wheel cylinder, brake shoes, springs and hardware, and automatic mechanical (star wheel) adjuster. -Backing Plate – is bolted to the axle. This part is a fixture for attaching other brake parts -Brake Drum – -Encloses the brake mechanical parts -Fits over the wheel studs -Is held in place by hub bearings, retaining screws, or a road wheel -May have fins for cooling -May be aluminum with a steel or iron friction surface insert -Brake Shoe Linings – -Are either riveted or bonded to a metal shoe -May contain asbestos -May contain metallic particles to increase stopping power -Have a stamped and reinforced steel backing for attaching other brake hardware -Are often designed to be mounted facing forward for the leading shoe or rearward for the trailing shoe -Mechanical (Star Wheel) Adjuster – the star wheel adjuster has left-hand threads on one side of the vehicle and right-hand threads for the other side. Do not switch these adjusters from side to side or the rear brake will end up out of adjustment and the driver will experience a sinking pedal -Springs and Hardware – types of springs and hardware used in a drum brake system include: -Return springs, used to bring the shoes back into position after braking and release hydraulic pressure -Shoe hold-down springs, used to mount the shoes to the backing plate. These hold the shoes in place while still allowing some movement -A shoe anchor pin, which is attached to the backing plate, and is used to transfer the braking torque from the shoes to the backing plate -The parking brake actuator lever works with the parking brake cable to push the brake shoes against the brake drum when the parking brake is applied -Wheel Cylinder – the wheel cylinder is attached to the backing plate with bolts or clips and forces the brake shoes outward against the drum. The wheel cylinder is similar to the caliper pistons on a disc brake system Brake System Parts -Master Cylinder Description – Parts of a master cylinder include: -The reservoir, which supplies the master cylinder with fluid. Some systems have a remote reservoir that uses a low-pressure supply line to supply fluid to the master cylinder -The pushrod, which connects the brake pedal and power assist -Primary and secondary pistons, which force pressure into the brake lines -Note: As the piston is moved by the brake pedal pushrod, the brake fluid forward of the piston seals is forced into the brake lines, raising the pressure in the lines. Vehicles are built with multi-circuit braking systems. These systems have front and rear, or diagonally separated, brake systems to ensure partial braking is retained in case of a brake line failure. -Brake System Operation – pressure is created in the brake system by applying pressure to the brake pedal. This creates hydraulic pressure in the master cylinder by forcing the piston against the fluid. The master cylinder creates hydraulic pressure in the brake lines. The brake lines carry hydraulic pressure to the wheel cylinders and calipers, which change hydraulic pressure into motion. The piston in the caliper or wheel cylinder moves outward as a result of the hydraulic pressure. This stops the vehicle by forcing the friction material against the rotor or drum. Note: Modern vehicles with anti-lock brakes will not use a proportioning valve. The distribution of brake pressure to each wheel will be controlled by the anti-lock brake system. -Brake Assist Systems – vacuum and hydraulic are two common automotive brake assist systems. Vacuum assist brakes have a vacuum assist that contains a diaphragm. Vacuum assist brakes apply vacuum to one side of the diaphragm and atmospheric pressure to the other. Vacuum is obtained from the engine intake manifold. They may require an additional vacuum pump to supply adequate vacuum on some engines. Some vehicles use hydraulic power, instead of vacuum, to assist in the application of the brakes. This is typically done where engine vacuum is a concern, such as diesel engines, or vans where available space is a concern. Vehicles with an integral anti-lock brake system (ABS) use brake fluid under pressure to assist in the application of the brakes. -Assisted Emergency Braking – some vehicles have an assisted emergency braking system. This system recognizes an emergency braking situation by measuring the rate of the brake pedal being depressed and comparing it to normal brake pedal movement. The system then automatically applies full braking force to help decrease stopping distances. When the driver pushes the brake pedal much quicker than normal, signaling an emergency, the computer applies an electronic valve on the power assist system for full braking. At the same time, the ABS is engaged to prevent wheel lock up. -Parking Brakes – parking brakes apply brake pressure to the rear wheels using a foot-actuated floor pedal or hand lever. Brake pressure is typically applied using a mechanical cable attaching the pedal or lever to the rear brakes. -Drum Parking Brakes – the drum parking brake is actuated by the parking brake lever or pedal, mechanically forcing both shoes outward against the brake drum. Return springs pull the shoes away from contacting the drum after the pedal or lever is released. -Electronic Parking Brakes – there are two basic designs for electronic parking brake systems. The first design still uses cables to actuate the parking brakes. Cables from the wheels attach to a motor that pulls and releases similarly to the way a manual lever pulls and releases. The second design uses actuators attached directly to the rear calipers. In this case, a signal controller will relay power to the motors to clamp the brake pads against the rotors. In most cases, this type of system will have a switch to activate the parking brake, but some systems activate the parking brakes when the transmission is shifted into park. Note: On some vehicles with caliper-mounted motors, the electronic parking brake can double as a hill-hold for vehicles with manual transmissions. -Drum-In-Hat Parking Brakes – drum-in-hat parking brakes have separate regular and parking brake systems. These parking brakes actuate the regular brakes hydraulically and the parking brake mechanically. Drum-in-hat parking brakes actuate the parking brake by mechanically. Drum-in-hat parking brakes actuate the parking brake by mechanically operating a small drum brake just outside of the hub and inside the rotor. -Brake Line Ends – the brake lines and hoses transmit fluid pressure from the master cylinder to the calipers or wheel cylinders. Brake line ends may be ISO flare fittings or double flare fittings, which are typically found on older model vehicles. Brake lines are made of double-wall steel and must be carefully formed and routed to avoid creating fluid restrictions. Brake hoses connect rigid brake lines to moveable parts, and they are flexible to allow movement with the wheel and suspension system Brake Fluid -Types of Brake Fluid – common brake fluid types include DOT 3, DOT 4, and DOT 5. Note: do not allow brake fluid to come in contact with painted surfaces. If this happens, wash the area thoroughly with water -DOT 3 Brake Fluid – DOT 3 brake fluid is non-petroleum based, but has additives that aid internal system lubrication and prevent corrosion. It maintains its viscosity in high temperatures. DOT 3 brake fluid should not be stored after a container is opened and is designed to absorb moisture. Moisture in a braking system can vaporize, creating air in the system and lowering the brake fluid boiling point. This process can cause corrosion on brake system parts -DOT 4 Brake fluid – - has a higher boiling point than DOT 3. -Can be mixed with DOT 3. -Absorbs more moisture than DOT 3 -DOT 5 Brake Fluid – -Is silicone-based -Does not absorb moisture -Compresses more than DOT 3 or DOT 4 -Is not compatible with DOT 3 or DOT 4. Do not mix -Is used and recommended by only a few vehicle makers -Is purple in color Damage Analysis of Brake Systems (2/2) Disc Brakes -Basic Brake System Inspection – when inspecting the brake system for damage, be sure to inspect all brake system components including the brake rotors, drums, master cylinder, booster, hoses, lines, and parking brake system. It is important to inspect the braking system closely to identify collision-related damage vs maintenance items. If brake system issues are identified that do not appear to be collision-related, these items must be fully documented and reviewed with the customer. If the customer elects to not have these required services performed, the final documents signed by the vehicle owner should include a summary of the necessary repairs and the customer’s decision to not complete the recommended repairs. Note: Leaking break fluid indicates damage to the brake system. Trace the leak to its source to locate the damaged part. -Brake System Inspection, continued – it is always important to include inspection of the brake pedal and fasteners as part of your brake system inspection. Some vehicles may be equipped with a safety feature that allows the brake pedal to drop away in a collision situation. This creates additional space and reduces potential impact to the driver’s legs. -Collision Damage to Disc Brakes – collision damage to the caliper assembly may include bends, shifted slides, torn seals, or broken mounts. Damage to disc brake pads may include contamination, cracks, bends, or delamination. Damage to fasteners and other hardware may cause them to be bent or broken. Damage or wear to the caliper assembly can cause a number of problems. These include: -Pulling during braking -Fluid leaks -A soft pedal -Dragging -Brake failure -Moisture Contamination – an open brake system will absorb moisture. The amount of moisture absorbed into the system depends on the humidity level and the length of time the system is open. A refractive, electronic tester, or paper test strips can be used to determine if the brake fluid is contaminated. A system that has been open for an extended period should be tested for moisture. Moisture greatly reduces the boiling point of brake fluid. Absorption of even 4 percent moisture can reduce the boiling point 50 percent. Heat can cause water in the brake fluid to change from a liquid to a gas. When this occurs, the brake pedal stroke is used up, compressing the gas. This condition could go unnoticed since the water vapor returns to a liquid as the system cools. Note: A contaminated system should be flushed -Rotor – when inspecting brake rotors, check for: -Bent mounting areas -Cracks and scoring -Glazing or corrosion -Improper thickness -Note: Inspect rotors for hardware and fastener damage or warpage. Warpage of brake rotors is measured by lateral runout measurements. Runout can be identified by making measurements with dial indicators. Improper tightening of the wheel fasteners can cause brake rotors to warp -Inspecting and Resurfacing Rotors – a damaged rotor can cause a vehicle to shake during braking. This symptom can be caused by the rotor being cracked across the surface, cracked down the center of the cooling fins, warped from cooling too quickly or uneven cooling, and bent from collision damage. Rotor surfaces may also become scored from brake pads that have been worn to their backing plates or pitted from corrosion. This type of damage, depending on the depth, can normally be removed by resurfacing. Note: Petroleum product of brake fluid rotor contamination can cause a number of problems-including those similar to a damaged rotor. -Initial Inspection Areas – all areas of the disc brake system should be inspected. To inspect a brake rotor: -1) Remove the wheel -2) Check both the inside and outside of rotor surfaces -3) Inspect for cracks -4) Inspect for black or blue discoloration, which indicates excessive heat -5) Inspect for scores, pitting, or grooves in the surface -6) Check for runout (warped rotors) with a dial indicator -Note: Rotors with slight surface defects or runout can be resurfaced on a brake lathe, depending on the severity of the defect. Follow the vehicle maker’s recommendations for minimum thickness and runout tolerances -Rear Disc Brakes – rear disc brakes have: -Similar hardware, operation, and inspection requirements as front disc brakes -Different parking brake systems, depending on the vehicle make and model -Brake Pads – when inspecting the brake system, the condition of the friction material should be checked. When parts must be replaced, the replacement alternatives must be carefully considered. -Brake Lining Replacement – brake pad replacement considerations include the amount of material remaining on the pad. Each vehicle maker will have recommendations indicating when brake material should be replaced. Depending on whether the friction material is riveted or bonded affects when the brake material is replaced. When inspecting brake material thickness, a part with riveted brake material will require replacement when more material is remaining as compared to bonded brake material. This is because the attaching rivets will contact the rotor before the pad will wear away. Check the condition of the brake lining. Look for signs of contamination, cracks, or deterioration. -Inspecting Other Brake Hardware – -Check other brake hardware for: -Broken holding clips -Damaged, missing, or corroded attaching bolts -Dry caliper bushings -Check Splash shield for: -Bends or cracks -Excessive dirt and debris -Note: Check wheel mating surfaces for damage or debris – if unaddressed, vibrations and/or braking system issues may arise. -Replacement Part Options – when servicing brakes, it is important to use parts recommended by the vehicle maker. Using pads that are not recommended may cause a vehicle to brake differently than designed. Special pads with electronic wear sensors are required for vehicles that electronically monitor friction material wear. These pads use a wire in the friction material that creates an open in the circuit as the pads wear. When an open occurs, the driver is notified that it is time for replacement. -Collision Damage to Master Cylinders – collision damage to the master cylinder can be difficult to detect. When inspecting the master cylinder for damage, common types of damage may include: -Broken lines -Detached reservoirs -Damaged housings -Dirt contamination -Damage to the body mounting locations or cowl panel Drum Brakes -Collision Damage to Drum Brakes - collision damage to drum brakes may include: -Twisted, cracked, or out-of-round drums -Bent shoes -Contaminated or missing friction material -Broken or stretched springs -Bent hardware -Leaking or cracked wheel cylinders -Damage to backing plates -Inspecting Drum Brakes – -symptoms of worn or damaged drums include: -A vibrating brake pedal, indicating a possible out of round condition -A low pedal where the adjuster is frozen due to corrosion or collision damage -Grabbing or overly sensitive brakes, indicating possible contaminated friction material -Abnormal noises -Pull or drag -The parking brake is not operating properly -Note: If there is suspected damage, remove the brake drum for inspection of all brake parts. -Checking the Drum and Backing Plate – -Check the drum for: -Cracks around the lug nut holes -Cracks in the friction surface -Black or blue discoloration that shows excessive heat -Scores or grooves in the surface -A wear pattern, which may indicate uneven shoe contact -Note: If a brake drum is cracked, it must be replaced. Minor scratches or grooves and minor discoloration of signs of overheating can be repaired by resurfacing. -Check the backing plate for: -Bends in the plate -Proper parts attachment -A leaking wheel cylinder -A leaking axle seal -The condition of the hardware -Checking the Brake Linings and Wheel Cylinder - -The brake linings should be checked for: -Even lining wear pattern -Lining thickness -Broken or missing lining sections -Cracks in linings -Reversed (leading and trailing) linings -The wheel cylinder should be checked for: -Proper contact with shoes -Leaks behind the seals -Checking the Springs and Hardware - -Check the springs and hardware for: -Damage -Stretched springs -Parts that are out of position -Check the adjusting mechanism for: -Correct positioning -Proper movement -Excessively corroded threads -A binding adjustment lever -Check the parking brake lever for: -Ease of movement -Correct positioning -An attached cable -Effectiveness Braking Assists -Collision Damage to Power Assists – collision damage to power assists includes: -Hydraulic leaks -Vacuum leaks -Visible damage to the unit -Possible internal damage -Note: When repairing collision-damaged brake systems, assist units are usually not repaired, they are replaced when damaged. Upon installation, be sure to properly adjust the pushrod, or improper braking may result. When installing a brake booster, be sure to refer to vehicle maker service procedures and requirements to ensure the pushrod is properly adjusted to avoid improper braking -Left is power steering pump, right is hydraulic boost -Damaged Power Assists – hard pedal can be described as excessive pressure required to stop a vehicle. Hard pedal can be caused by collision damage to power assists. While the engine is running, inspect the brake system for brake pedal feel. If the pedal feels too hard, it may indicate a booster that is not working or has a vacuum leak. Additional symptoms of damage to power assist units may include: -A hissing noise -Sensitive brakes -A slow releasing pedal -Pedal chatter -Incorrect engine idle (vacuum leak) Brake Lines and Hoses -Brake Warning Light – a warning light on the dash will likely illuminate if the hydraulic system has low fluid levels after a brake line has broken -Leaking Brake Lines and Hoses – a vehicle with a firm, but sinking pedal may have a leak in one of the brake lines or hoses. Depending on its location, a leak may contaminate the friction material. If the leak is large enough, the brake pedal could go to the floor, losing at least 50 percent of its braking action. Brake lines may be kinked or broken during a collision. If a brake line is damaged, it must be replaced. The line must be routed in its original factory. Use all the original style clips and fasteners to position the brake lines. Do not straighten kinked lines. Be sure the lines are positioned away from exhaust heat and suspension movement. -Constricted Brake Lines – a vehicle that pulls to one side while braking may have a partially blocked brake line, which restricts fluid flow. This can be caused by a dent, bend, or internal blockage in a brake line. In many cases, the master cylinder and assist will provide enough pressure to force the brake fluid past the obstruction to apply the brakes. However, the fluid pressure may not be able to release due to the obstruction once the brakes are released. This would result in a constant pressure to the caliper or wheel cylinder and cause a dragging brake. Brake drag is easiest to diagnose and most noticeable in slippery conditions. This condition may also be noticeable on dry pavement as an intermittent pull immediately after the brakes are released -Top is no damage. Bottom is damage. Parking Brakes -Inspecting Parking Brakes – the parking brake pedal/lever and cables should be inspected for wear or damage. A parking brake that is worn or damaged often will not hold a vehicle stationary when parked on an incline. Cable adjustment may be required to determine if damage is present. -When inspecting the parking brake pedal or lever, verify the parking brake: -Engages easily -Holds the brake in place -Releases easily -Does not bind -Brake drag may also be a symptom of a faulty parking brake system. This can be caused by: -Damaged or corroded brake parts -A bent, collapsed, or corroded parking brake cable -Bent or corroded parking brake pivots or brackets Damage Analysis of Electronic Brake Systems (1/2) ABS Operation -Anti-Lock Brake Systems Purpose – modern vehicles have a variety of safety features that help the driver maintain control of the vehicle in a variety of situations. Traction control, stability control, and other related features are enhancements to the ABS, that provide additional programming, sensors, and networking of systems. The ABS can release and re-apply brake fluid pressure rapidly to provide brake control to axles or individual wheels. This control of brake fluid pressure is called modulation, which helps the driver maintain steering control during hard braking events. It also reduces hydroplaning during braking and flat-spotting of tires during panic stops. Note: The ABS, and enhanced features provide greater braking control to the axles and individual wheels. -Basic ABS Operation – an ABS monitors wheel speed during braking; if a wheel begins to lock up, the electronic control module (ECM) sends a signal to hydraulic valves in the system that reduce braking pressure to that wheel. When the wheel starts turning again, brake pressure is reapplied. This happens many times per second and produces an action similar to manually pumping the brakes, only much faster -Brake Lockup – brake lockup occurs when the brakes stop the wheel and tire assembly, but not the vehicle. Friction from the brake surfaces exceed the friction of the tire to the road surface. This causes the tire to slip across the road surface, resulting in increased stopping distance and reduced steering control -ABS Types – you should be aware that there are a few different ABS types -Top to bottom – Four-Channel, Four-Wheel Three-Channel, Rear-Wheel -Four-Channel – a four-channel ABS can control each wheel individually and provides the best straight-line stability and steering control. Each wheel has a speed sensor providing information to the ECM, which can apply, hold, or release the brakes as needed -Wheel Speed Sensor – in each corner -Four-Wheel Three-Channel – a four-wheel, three-channel ABS can control the front wheel brakes individually and the rear wheels together as an axle. This system has two front wheel speed sensors and usually only one rear speed sensor. Three-Channel systems are more common on solid rear axle vehicles, such as pickup trucks, and they also provide straight-line stability and steering control -Rear – Wheel Speed Sensor (typically located in the different) -Front – Wheel Speed Sensor in each -Rear-Wheel – a rear-wheel ABS regulates pressure equally to the rear wheels only and uses one rear speed sensor. The rear speed sensor is mounted in the transmission, differential housing, or the transfer case. A rear-wheel ABS is designed to improve straight-line stability in panic stops, but does not prevent the loss vehicle steering during front wheel lockup. These systems are not as common on modern vehicles, but may be found on some pickup trucks and vans. A rear-wheel ABS can help reduce the effects of varying load and weight transfer on trucks and vans -Wheel Speed Sensor in rear ABS Parts -An ABS is made up of several parts -Accumulator (if present) – ABS with an accumulator, will store hydraulic fluid under pressure -Diagnostic Connectors – diagnostic connectors are points where test equipment connections are made -Modulator Valves (Sometimes known as ABS Valves), Fluid Pump, Relays, and Pressure Switches – modulator valves, sometimes known as ABS valves, control the flow of fluid in the brake system -Wheel Speed Sensors and Tone Rings – wheel speed sensors, and tone rings create a signal used by the ECM to determine wheel speed -Warning Lamps – warning lamps inform the driver of problems in the brake system or ABS -ECM Functions – when the vehicle is first started, the ECM checks the operation of its own circuits and performs a self-test of the ABS electrical parts and circuits to determine if there are any faults. When the vehicle is put into motion, there may be additional tests for wheel speed signals, pump motors, and/or modulator valves, which may be activated. The ECM also communicates with other onboard systems, stores diagnostic trouble codes, and commands the operation of the ABS warning lamp. If problems are detected, the ECM will shut down ABS operation and cause the ABS warning lamp to remain lit. normal braking is typically available without ABS operation. Other names used for an ABS ECM include: -Electronic control unit (ECU) -Electronic brake control module (EBCM) -Anti-lock brake controller -Non-Integral and Integral ABS – anti-lock braking systems area classified in two general categories, non-integral and integral. -Non-Integral – a non-integral ABS utilizes a conventional brake master cylinder, power assist assembly, and a separate hydraulic modulator unit with a pump motor. Some anti-lock brake systems may also use a separate fluid pump -Integral – an integral ABS can be identified by a special hydraulic unit located at the master cylinder. Integral systems typically package the anti-lock valves, pump motor, accumulator, and other system parts together with the master cylinder Traction Control Parts and Function -Traction Control Systems (TCS) Function – a TCS is designed to prevent the loss of traction of driven wheels, limiting spinning drive wheels during acceleration. TCS is activated when throttle input and engine torque exceed the drive wheel road surface traction conditions. -TCS and ABS parts – TCS utilize many ABS parts. Signals received from the wheel speed sensors are used to identify which wheel or wheels are spinning too fast. The information is used by the ECM in a different program to aid in acceleration (traction control) instead of deceleration (anti-lock braking) -Torque Reduction – during acceleration, if a drive wheel is detected to be spinning faster than the vehicle is accelerating, the traction control system will send a request to the powertrain control module to reduce engine torque output. If torque reduction is not effective in bringing the slipping wheels under control, the ECM will apply brake pressure to the spinning drive wheels until traction is regained -TCS for All-Wheel Drive – TCS for all-wheel-drive vehicles uses the slowest turning wheel speed information, and limits the other drive wheels from turning within 20 percent of that speed or faster. The exact logic of how each of these systems works will vary from vehicle maker to vehicle maker, but the general principles remain the same -Traction Control Deactivation – the driver may switch off the TCS if desired. Once deactived, the system will remain off until the next ignition cycle, or until it is turned back on by the driver. When deactivated by the driver, the TCS will display a “TRACTION OFF” message on the instrument panel or on the center console warning lamp. The message or lamp will remain lit until the system is returned to operation. Stability Control System Parts and Functions -Stability Control Systems – the stability control system helps maintain control of the vehicle during extreme steering maneuvers when the vehicle nears or exceeds the limits of road traction and may experience loss of control. This is accomplished by monitoring data from sensors that report what the vehicle is doing and comparing it to what the driver inputs indicate the vehicle should be doing -Stability Control Parts – the stability control system relies on the ABS and its parts to apply brakes strategically. It also communicates with the PCM to limit torque, and may network with other systems as well. To determine if the vehicle is under control or not, sensors provide input to the ECM -Yaw Rate Sensors – yaw rate sensors measure a vehicle’s actual turn rate, or change in direction, and report the information to the ECM. These sensors are usually located in the passenger compartment and may be under the rear package shelf, in the center console, or under the driver’s or passenger’s seat -Steering Angle Sensor – the steering angle sensor provides the ECM with information about the driver’s steering wheel movements, and measures the steering wheel position relative to straight ahead. Some models also measure the rate (speed) that the steering wheel turns. It may be self-centering or have to be calibrated after any steering, suspension, or unitized structure / frame straightening work is performed, or the sensor is serviced -Lateral Accelerometer – the lateral accelerometer sends a precise reading to the ECM that represents the cornering force that the vehicle is experiencing. The ECM then uses the lateral acceleration information along with other information to calculate the desired turn (yaw) rate. There may be more than one sensor. Lateral acceleration sensors are typically located in the passenger cabin and may be located ahead of the B-pillar, on the vertical surface of the inner rocker panel (one on each side of the vehicle). They may also be located in the C-pillar behind the rear seat cushion or in the engine compartment near the cowl. Note: Most vehicles use two lateral acceleration sensors -Other Parts – the stability control ECM may be the same ECM as the ABS or it may be a separate module. ABS wheel speed sensor information is used to determine speed and wheel lockup. Brake pressure sensor data may inform the ECM of the driver’s braking intentions, while the throttle position sensor data informs the system of a driver’s acceleration request -ECM Function in a Skid – the ECM is programmed to look for a vehicle’s correct path when steering through a corner. Steering angle and speed information allow the ECM to determine intended path, while yaw rate and lateral accelerometer data inform the ECM if this is what the vehicle is actually doing. If the information being sent to the ECM does not match, the computer determines that the vehicle is in a skid. Engine torque may be limited, and brake pressure can be applied by the computer to one or more of the wheel circuits to help maintain control of the vehicle -Understeering and Oversteering – loss of control (skids) can be divided into one of two categories, understeer or oversteer -Oversteer without SCS on left. Understeer without SCS on right. -Understeer Front-Wheel Drive – understeer occurs when a driver attempts to turn and the front tires begin to slip. The front wheels are turned, but the vehicle does not respond and continues on its original course. On front-wheel drive vehicles, this may be caused by the drive wheels spinning too fast for the corner (too much throttle). Front-wheel drive vehicles can also understeer if a corner is entered too fast. When a front-wheel drive vehicle understeers, the skid control system may instruct the ECM to adjust the throttle and/or apply braking -Understeer Rear-Wheel Drive – when a rear-wheel drive vehicle understeers, the skid control system instructs the ECM to reduce the throttle before engaging the appropriate brake circuit to correct the skid -Oversteering – oversteering occurs when a vehicle is turning and the rear tires begin to slide away from the direction of the turn. This occurs most often on rear-wheel drive vehicles with slipping drive wheels. When a rear-wheel drive vehicle oversteers, the skid control system instructs the ECM to open or close the throttle, depending on if the drive wheels have too much or too little torque applied for the situation Damage Analysis of Electronic Brake Systems (2/2) Anti-Lock Brake System Damage Analysis Initial Damage Inspections -Inspecting a Conventional Brake System – the vehicle’s conventional brake system must be functioning properly in order for the ABS to operate as designed. It is best to inspect the conventional brake system when inspecting ABS and other related vehicle systems damage, since the ABS operates based on an assumption that the conventional brake system is in good working condition -Inspecting the ABS – with an ABS, it is impossible to diagnose brake system problems judging by brake pedal feel alone. When inspecting the ABS: -Look for physical damage to system parts -Scan the system for diagnostic trouble codes -Note: Keep in mind that some codes may not set until the vehicle has been driven -Observing the Indicator Lamp – there are a variety of dash indicator lamps on vehicles, which typically illuminate when the ignition key is first turned ON. This is considered a lamp test. The dash lamps are used to inform the driver of the status of different systems. They may also light up if the ECM identifies a detectable fault or malfunction in the system, commonly referred to as a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL). An illuminated ABS MIL is an indicator there is a problem and the ABS system will be turned off. When this happens, many of the dependent systems will be offline as well. -ABS Diagnostic Information – a scan tool is required to troubleshoot the ABS and other related electronic systems. Diagnostic trouble codes will not typically tell you if a specific part needs to be replaced, only that a problem exists in that circuit. Further specific testing is usually required to determine the root cause. Always begin with a thorough visual inspection of the parts of the system; this may identify the problem. Some ABS problems may not set trouble codes. It is important to note that if a trouble code is not present, you should not assume that the ABS is functioning normally. A test drive with ABS active stopping event is required to verify the system. Some systems will require specialized tools and equipment to diagnose and repair ABS Parts Damage Analysis -ABS Modulator/Fluid Pump Damage – the modulator valves and fluid pumps may become damaged in a collision. Warning lamps may light up and diagnostic trouble codes will generally be stored if damage has occurred. Damage may include: -Physical damage -Cut or shorted wires -Damaged connectors -Poor terminal contacts due to corrosion or paint overspray -Damaged mounting pads and brackets -Brake fluid leaks -Damaged brake lines -Note: Damaged system parts require replacement. Some electronic brake control units are serviced with the control module and typically require reprogramming when replaced. Bleeding of air from the system may require special tools and equipment. Check the vehicle maker service information for wire or connector repair procedures -Speed Sensors and Tone Rings – speed sensor circuits and tone rings are often damaged during a collision. -Speed Sensor Locations – speed sensors may be located on the: -Steering knuckle or bearing housing -Transmission housing -Rear differential housing -Transfer case housing on rear-wheel ABS systems -Tone Ring Locations – tong rings may be located on the: -Wheel bearing -Brake rotor -Outer CV joint -Transmission shaft -Differential pinion gear -Transfer case on rear-wheel ABS -Speed Sensor and Tone Ring Problems – trouble codes will normally be stored if damage has occurred. The warning lamp may not light until the vehicle is driven. Some problems may include: -Physical damage to the speed sensor -Damaged wire connectors -Cut or shorted sensor wires and shielding -Cracked tone ring or missing teeth -Bent steering or spindle parts, causing improper sensor air gap -The permanent magnet losing its magnetism due to the impact -Trouble codes being set by using different diameter tires or a space saving spare tire -Metal fillings or grease sticking to the sensor, which will cause improper signals -Note: Visual inspection will reveal most collision-related speed sensor circuit problems. Some systems may allow speed sensor circuits to be monitored using a scan tool. Speed sensor wire repairs are typically not permitted on the flexible sections of the wire dur to durability concerns -Switches, Relays, Wires, and Connectors – ABS switches, relays, wires, and connectors may be damaged in a collision. Switches are normally either ON or OFF. When their circuits become damaged during a collision, the ECM may be able to detect the damage. Some ABS systems may turn on warning lamps and set trouble codes for damaged switch circuits. Visual inspection of switch circuits will locate most collision-related problems. Relay circuits may be damaged during a collision or during the repair process. Warning lamps may be illuminated and ECM trouble codes will generally be stored if damage has occurred. Problems may include: -Physical damage -Cut or shorted wires to either coil or contact circuits -Poor terminal contacts due to corrosion or paint overspray -Wires backed out of connectors -Electronic Brake Control Additional Features – ABS and electronic brake controls enable a variety of additional features on vehicles. Damage analysis of many of these systems is similar to ABS damage analysis and should include the inspection of the additional parts associated with each system. -Traction Control -TCS operation is typically integrated into the ABS and/or stability control system, so damage inspection will usually be similar to, and inclusive of, these systems. Some vehicles may have separate unique indicators, switches, and sensors that may be susceptible to damage. Keep in mind the system usually communicates with the PCM over the vehicle communications network. -Stability Control – stability control system operation may be integrated ino a module with the ABS and other related vehicle functions and communicates with other modules and systems on the vehicle. Damage inspection will usually be similar to, and inclusive of, these systems. The steering angle sensor (SAS), lateral accelerometers, and other system parts should be inspected for damage. The SAS should be calibrated if it is serviced or operations are performed on the vehicle that affect the centering or calibration of the SAS, such as suspension work, or anything that affects the alignment angles. Programming or coding of the SAS is required on some vehicles -Adaptive Cruise Control – adaptive cruise control (ACC) utilizes a radar unit that sense vehicles on the road ahead and has the ability to slow the vehicle and maintain a set distance behind lead vehicles while limiting speed to a preset maximum. This is accomplished through network communications, where the ACC requests the application of brakes through the ABS and throttle control through the PCM. Vehicles equipped with this feature vary in their ability to slow or stop the vehicle completely. There are additional features available on some vehicles that may include collision mitigation or braking. If the ABS system is inoperative, all of the additional features will be offline as well. -Other Features – vehicles may have additional brake system-related features, such as rollover protection, trailer sway control, integrated trailer brake control, hill start assist, and hill descent control. These features usually operate using the ABS/TCS systems and incorporate a dash or steering wheel switch, an indicator light, or information system message, and may include additional control modules and system parts. Inspection and damage analysis considerations are similar to those that apply to ABS and other related systems Damaged Analysis of Drivetrains (1/2) Drivetrain Overview -Drivetrain Overview – the drivetrain consists of a collection of components in a vehicle that transfers power from the engine to the wheels. The engine converts fuel into motion to move the vehicle. Vehicles are required to comply with specific laws regarding the fuel economy and emission (pollution) generated. The entire vehicle is designed to help meet these requirements. This includes the shape of the body to reduce aerodynamic drag, powertrain design, including engine and transmission design, and drive gear ratio to tire size and design. This course will cover inspection considerations for the engine and the transmission, the driveshaft, and the subframe, which are drivetrain components. -Damage Analysis of Drivetrains – when conducting damage analysis of the engine, transmission, or other drivetrain-related parts, look for cut or damaged wires, and damage to connectors, sensors, actuators, and modules. Any connecting linkages, lines, hoses, or tubes should be inspected for damage and leaks. Follow original equipment manufacturer (OEM) service information when considering repairs. Some part replacements will require initializations, coding, and/or programming -Powertrain Control Module – the powertrain control module (PCM) precisely monitors a variety of sensors, and manages air/fuel delivery, spark timing, and variable valve timing (on some engines) for engine operation, efficiency, and emissions compliance. The PCM or a separate transmission control module usually controls automatic transmissions. These modules have the ability to self-test, and test the electrical circuits connected to them. If faults are detected, diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) will be stored. Scanning the vehicle’s electronic systems will identify if DTCs are present. Some emissions-related codes may not set until the vehicle is driven, and has met specific conditions. -Communication to the PCM – there are varieties of sensors on the engine that provide information to the PCM. For example: -Temperature sensors provide temperature information to the PCM for engine/transmission and cooling fan operation. -The throttle position sensor provides information about the throttle plate opening -The oil pressure sensor reports oil pressure information to the PCM -Crankshaft and camshaft sensors on the engine send signals to the PCM, which are used to determine spark timing and fuel delivery -Note: The crankshaft position sensor is usually located near the harmonic balancer on the front of the engine and camshaft position sensors are usually mounted at the end of the camshaft on the cylinder head. -Damage Analysis of the PCM – damage to the PCM is possible. The PCM should be inspected for: -Cut, pulled, or shorted wires -Cracked or broken connectors -Physical damage to the PCM module -DTCs -Note: Wiring harness and connector service parts vary by vehicle maker. In some situations, wiring harness replacement may be require due to the severity of damage or parts availability. A damaged PCM will require replacement and programming. Physical damage to the PCM case may cause internal damage to the PCM module. Engine -Engine – an engine is essentially an air pump. The air intake system brings in air and delivers it to the cylinders. Fuel is either mixed with the air, before it enters the cylinder, or is directly injected into the cylinders. Once the air/fuel mixture is ignited, resultant exhaust gasses are removed from the cylinder and vehicle via the exhaust system -Engine Inspection – when inspecting the front of the engine (crankshaft end with the accessory belt) inspect the belt and crank pulley. Follow the belt path, inspecting the condition of all pulleys and belt tensioners. If equipped, inspect the harmonic balancer and engine timing covers for any signs of damage. Inspect the alternator and other engine-mounted accessories for any signs of damage to the pulleys, shaft, case, mounting brackets, wires, connectors, and connected lines or hoses. The inability to identify potential problem areas can lead to high supplemental costs. When estimating damage to an engine, it is important to consider that if there is major exterior damage, there is also the possibility of internal part damage as well. -Engine Mounts – the engine mounts support the engine. They also work with the transmission mounts to control movement and vibration, and maintain drivetrain alignment. Mounts are typically rubber isolators or bushings molded to metal brackets. They may be oil-filled and/or vacuum operated using valves controlled by the PCM. Inspect for bent or broken mounts and hardware. Damaged oil-filled mounts may leak oil. Damaged mounts should be replaced. -Engine Cover Damage – the engine cover is located near the upper portion of the engine and is used to protect the engine. Damage may occur if something falls on it. If the engine cover is damaged, a careful inspection of all parts under it is required to identify any possible hidden damage. Air Intake, Fluids, and Fuel Systems -Air Intake Systems – the air intake system brings in air through air ducts, filters it through the air filter box, and supplies it to the mass airflow sensor. The mass airflow sensor measures the amount of air entering the engine. Next, the air is routed to the throttle body, which regulators how much air enters the intake manifold. The intake manifold distributes the air to each cylinder. The throttle body permits control of engine speed. When inspecting the air intake system, inspect each part of the system for damage. This includes bent, cracked, cut, or broken parts, crushed or restricted ducts, hoses, or tubes, and damaged electrical wires, connectors, and sensors, -Turbochargers – some vehicles have turbochargers, which compress the incoming air being supplied to the engine. A turbocharged engine has additional parts in the air intake system that require inspection including the turbocharger, intercooler(s), and associated connecting hoses and tubing.
