When inspecting turbochargers, look for damage to any connected lines, hoses, tubes, linkages, actuators, heat shields, turbo housing, attaching hardware, and brackets. Inspect any oil or coolant lines and hoses connecting to the turbo for bends, kinks, or other damage. Restricted oil or coolant to a turbo will lead to premature failure. -Intercoolers – intercoolers are designed to cool the compressed air from the turbocharger before delivering it to the throttle body and intake manifold. They are typically mounted behind the front bumper or grill, in front of or below the air conditioner (A/C) condenser, and are vulnerable to debris impact or collision damage. They may also be located between the A/C condenser and engine radiator. Inspect an intercooler for cracks, punctures, and broken or bent brackets and fins. Repairs are typically limited to minor straightening of the brackets and fins. -Superchargers – superchargers, which compress air entering the engine, are typically only found on very high-performance vehicles. Superchargers are belt-driven devices that are usually attached to the air intake manifold and should be inspected for damage to the belt, pulley, and body of the supercharger -Repair/Replace Decisions – it is important to identify if any broken parts, foreign objects, or debris have been ingested into the turbocharger(s), supercharger, and/or engine, as internal damage can occur. Further disassembly of the intake system or engine components may be required for inspection. A borescope may allow some internal inspections to be done. If any damage is present, servicing or replacement of the affected items, following OEM service procedures by a qualified technician is necessary. -Fluids – the engine uses motor oil to keep internal parts lubricated. Coolant (also known as antifreeze) keeps the engine from overheating. The coolant and the motor oil are contained in their own separate, sealed systems. Fluids should be checked for contamination and proper levels. An engine that has been run with contaminated fluids, an incorrect type of fluid, or improper amounts of fluids may have damage. It is important to note loss-related issues versus maintenance concerns -Contaminated Fluids – contaminated fluids must be removed from the vehicle and replaced with proper fluid types, in accordance to the vehicle-specific service information. It is important to determine the reason fluid contamination occurred and resolve this before replacing the fluid. Flushing of the affected system and additional service work will likely be required depending on the extent of other parts affected or damaged. Internal engine bearing damage is likely in situations where a vehicle has been run with oil contaminated by antifreeze or water. Note: Higher than normal fluid levels may indicate contamination. -Valve Cover – valve covers are typically plastic or aluminum and may develop hairline cracks if impacted. If crushed or broken, valve covers may conceal damage to valve train parts. Broken parts or debris may fall into internal engine passages, requiring additional internal engine inspection and disassembly. Damaged valve covers should be replaced. Damage to cylinder head or valve train components will likely require removal of the affected cylinder head to service. -Oil Pan – the oil pan is located at the bottom of the engine, which makes it susceptible to damage from situations such as contracting a parking bumper, terrain impact during off-road driving, or road debris. The oil pan should be inspected for cracks, dents, and leaks. If the oil pan is damaged, the oil pick-up tube may also be damaged. This may cause oil delivery issues in the engine leading to internal damage. Damaged oil pans and pickups should be replaced. The oil filter and mount should also be inspected for damage. Some vehicles may have an oil cooler and lines that should be inspected for damage as well. -Fuel Systems – when inspecting the fuel system, inspect the fuel pump, fuel lines, the fuel tank, the fuel filler cap, the inertia switch, and the fuel filter. -Fuel Pumps and Lines – fuel pumps are usually located in the fuel tank and, in rare situations, may dislodge from their mounting due to collision forces. Fuel lines are generally metal or plastic, and may have flexible rubber or braided hoses where the lines attach to the engine. All lines and hoses should be inspected for kinks, cuts, crushing, or leaks. These should be replaced and not repaired. Along with pressurized fuel lines, the fuel tank filler hose should be inspected for damage. Lines or hoses that are moved out of position should be restored to their original routing and retainers installed. Note: Some diesel and classic vehicles may have an engine-mounted fuel pump -Fuel Tank – fuel tanks may be plastic or steel. Damage may include gouges, dents, cuts, cracks, or punctures. Steel tanks should be inspected for leaks that could be a result of the collision damage or from corrosion. Fuel tank leaks may create a hazardous situation that can result in severe injury or death and cause property damage. Do not repair metal fuel tanks that have corrosion damage. If a plastic fuel tank is damaged, it should not be repaired. Fuel tanks pose an explosion and fire hazard. If any questions exist as to the long-term durability, replace the fuel tank. Note: Specialty restoration shops may be able to repair some metal fuel tanks. -Fuel Tank Filler Neck – the fuel tank filler neck is a rubber hose that connects the metal fill tube to the fuel tank. The rubber hose is fastened with clamps. Some filler neck assemblies have a smaller tube running beside the main fill tube to help control fuel splash-back during refueling. The filler neck attaches to the body with a pocket, usually made of plastic, which has a small drain at the bottom. Upon inspection, damaged parts are typically replaced. -Fuel Filler Cap – the fuel cap seals the system at the filler opening. Some vehicles are capless and may have a seal around the fuel pocket that seals to the fuel door, as well as a spring-loaded flap to seal the fill port. If the cap does not seal and allow the fuel tank to become slightly pressurized, or hold a vacuum, a DTC may be set. Fuel filler caps should be inspected for any damage or conditions that would prevent them from sealing and should be replaced if damaged or missing -Fuel Pump Inertia Switch – the fuel pump inertia switch, also known as the fuel pump shut-off switch, is designed to shut off the fuel pump when it is detected that the vehicle has come to an unusually sudden or forceful stop. The fuel pump inertia switch should be pushed to reset. If the switch is defective, replacement is required. -Fuel Filter – most late model vehicles use an in-line fuel filter mounted under the vehicle. Depending on the situation, the filter may incur damage and not allow fuel flow, or may leak fuel. Depending on the type of damage, contamination of the fuel system can cause drivability problems after repairs have been completed. -Ignition System – the PCM controls the ignition system, which provides high voltage to the spark plugs to ignite the air/fuel mixture in the cylinders at the appropriate time. A coil on plug (COP) arrangement is most common on modern vehicles. Some vehicles may use a single coil or two coils, and use spark plug wires to carry the high voltage to the spark plugs. When inspecting the ignition system, look for cut or broken wires, and damaged connectors, coil(s), and spark plugs. Note: Distributors were mechanical devices with a rotating contact used to direct the high voltage to the appropriate cylinder and may be found on older vehicles. Emission and Exhaust -Emissions Control – the catalytic converter, the evaporative emissions control (EVAP) system, the positive crankcase ventilation (PCV) system, and the exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) system are examples of vehicle emission control systems found on vehicles. Each of these systems should be inspected and, typically, damaged components are replaced. Many tests performed on these systems require special equipment and procedures not usually done by a collision repair technician. Damage or problems to an emission system usually results in a driveability problem. Likewise, a driveability problem usually causes an emissions problem. -Emissions Label – the emissions label is located in the engine compartment, and contains emission specifications/certification information. It should be replaced if damaged or defaced. If the component that the emissions label attaches to is replaced, verify that the correct emissions label is installed. Document all attempts in obtaining a replacement emissions label. Save the existing label in case it is not possible to obtain an exact replacement -PCV System – the PCV system relieves crankcase pressure by recirculating “blow-by” gases that escape past the piston rings into the crankcase during the combustion process. The PCV valve is typically installed in the rocker arm cover or intake manifold, with a passage to the crankcase. It opens when the engine is running at idle. The PCV valve is pushed nearly closed when the engine is under load. When inspecting the PCV system, look for loose or damaged lines, connections, valves, and gaskets. If the large diameter vacuum line is pulled off, it may result in a high idle, stalling, or DTCs to be set -EVAP System – the EVAP system is a closed system designed to contain excess fuel vapors in a charcoal-filled canister until a purge valve is opened, allowing vapors to be drawn into the engine and consumed. System parts typically include the fuel tank, filler neck and cap, EVAP canister, vapor lines, a rollover valve in the vapor line or at the fuel tank, vacuum/pressure sensors, service port with green cap, and a vapor purge valve. EVAP systems have a vent valve for the canister, which may be built into the canister or separate, a purge valve, and line from the EVAP canister to the engine. -EVAP Inspection – damage to the EVAP system may include a cracked or broken canister, loose or damaged vapor lines or connections, damaged or stuck valves, fuel tank, filler neck and cap, and damage to sensors, wiring, and connections. Damaged EVAP system parts should be replaced. Do not repair unless there is an OEM-specific procedure. Damage that results in pressure or vacuum leaks will typically cause a DTC to be set. This may require specific conditions and more than one drive cycle. Special smoke generators can be used to locate leaks in the EVAP system. Scan tools may be required to test some system parts. Note: Fuel odors may indicate a compromise to the EVAP system -EGR System – the EGR system is controlled by the PCM, which opens a valve between the exhaust and the intake manifold, recirculating a portion of the exhaust gases back through the combustion process. This reduces oxides of nitrogen (NOx) emissions by reducing the temperature of the combustion process. The EGR system may have tubes that connect the valve, intake, and exhaust manifolds as well as a sensor to detect gas flow. Some vehicles do not require an EGR system to meet the required emission standards -EGR System Inspection – when inspecting an EGR system, look for visible signs of damage to the system parts such as bent or broken tubes, valves, wiring, sensors, or connectors. The EGR valve can seize open and create rough idle, detonation, and power loss, as well as excessive emissions. Electronically controlled valves can be checked using a scan tool to activate the valve. Vacuum-controlled valves may be checked by applying vacuum and checking the valve movement. Service manual procedures should be followed. -Exhaust Systems – exhaust system parts, including the exhaust manifolds, oxygen sensors, exhaust pipes, catalytic converter, and muffler should be inspected for damage -Exhaust Manifolds – exhaust manifolds may be damaged during a collision. A cracked exhaust manifold can cause inaccurate oxygen sensor readings, setting a DTC. Damaged exhaust manifolds must be replaced. New gaskets, if applicable, should be installed. Inspect heat shields and other related parts for damage. Damaged heat shields are typically replaced; however, minor straightening may be permitted -Oxygen Sensors- oxygen (O2) sensors are used to determine if the exhaust gases are oxygen rich or lean, allowing the PCM to adjust fuel delivery. O2 sensors are commonly found on the exhaust manifold and on the exhaust pipes, before and after the catalytic converters. O2 sensors that are on the exhaust may be vulnerable in front end collisions, where the radiator core support and cooling components are pushed back into the engine area. Inspect for damage to the sensors, wires, and connector. Oxygen sensor damage may cause the vehicle to run rough. Damaged oxygen sensors must be replaced. -Exhaust Pipes – exhaust pipes are used to connect various parts of the exhaust system. Inspect exhaust system parts for bends, kinks, collapsed areas, cracks at joints and flanges, and broken or cracked welds. A kinked exhaust pipe can reduce engine efficiency and cause a rough running condition. A flexible exhaust decoupler is a section of flexible pipe that isolates the exhaust system from engine movement and may damaged in a collision, causing it to leak. Damaged exhaust parts are typically replaced. Any straightening should be confined to minor bends to ensure that no restrictions, leaks, or damage to coatings on the pipe occurs -Catalytic Converter – the catalytic converter contains an internal element that may be damaged if the exhaust system or the converter is impacted. Catalytic converts are emission control devices and are not repairable. Federal law states that they must not be permanently removed. If a catalytic converter is replaced in the U.S., the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) requires removed converters be kept on site for a minimum of 15 days from the date removed and be marked to indicate the vehicle, the work order number, or the date of removal and customer’s name. copies of work invoices, statements, and warranties must be retained at the facility for six months, -Muffler – mufflers are used on the vehicle to reduce the noise emitted by the exhaust system. The muffler can become damaged and cause restrictions to the exhaust gas flow or undesirable noise from holes. Damaged mufflers should be replaced -Diesel Exhaust Systems – diesel exhaust systems reduce the oxides of nitrogen (NOx) and particulates, or soot, emitted from diesel exhaust. The system typically has a diesel oxidation catalyst, selective catalytic reduction, and a diesel particulate filter, in additiona to mufflers and pipes. A diesel exhaust fluid (DEF) storage tank, lines, pump, and selective catalytic reduction (SCR) comprise an exhaust aftertreatment system. The aftertreatment system injects a small amount of DEF into the exhaust. DEF is mixed with the exhaust in the presence of a catalyst turning NOx into nitrogen and water vapor. Inspect for damage to any of the system parts or contamination of fluids. Note: The DEF must not be contaminated as it can damage the SCR Inspecting for Rollover Damage -Inspecting for Rollover Damage – during a rollover, the engine may sustain internal damage or physical damage from impact with the ground or other objects. Internal damages may result from operation with no oil or if oil or fluid exists in places where it should not. The engine running without oil may cause bearing damage. All internally lubricated systems with a pressurized oil system, such as the engine, transmission, and power steering systems, may incur this type of damage when deprived of oil. -Key Component Inspection - vehicles with rollover damage should be inspected for liquid-filled cylinders and internal damage to the engine and transmission. Engine damage may include the pistons, connecting rods, and crankshaft. Internal damage may be indicated if there are unusual noises from the engine or transmission. If internal damage is suspected, disassembly may be required to verify the extent of damage. Do not start the engine if damage is suspected. Ensure that all fluids are clean and at the proper level, and that the engine will turn over properly before attempting to start the engine -Key Inspection Procedures – hydraulic lockup occurs when fluid is in a cylinder. To check for hydraulic lockup, remove the spark plugs and manually rotate the engine. If liquids are present, continue to manually rotate the engine to remove the liquid. After most of the liquid has been removed, turn the engine over using the starter to force any remaining liquid from the cylinders. Exhaust color can aid in diagnosis, as burning fluids in the engine will cause exhaust to change colors. White exhaust indicates water or coolant, black exhaust indicates a rich fuel condition, and blue exhaust indicates burning oil. Note: Fluids do not compress, hydraulic lockup can cause internal engine damage. Damaged Analysis of Drivetrains (2/2) Transmission -Transmission – the transmission is designed to transfer torque from the engine to the driveshafts, and ultimately the wheels, while increasing or decreasing torque to help optimize fuel economy. A visual inspection of the transmission must be completed to determine if damage is present. -Visual Inspection – a visual inspection of the transmission includes inspecting for damage to linkages, dipstick, wires, sensors, connectors, lines, and hoses, fluid leaks/leaking seals, fluid pan damage, and cracks in the case. Inspect external transmission coolers and a manual transmission clutch operation. Check transmission fluid levels and for transmission fluid contamination, which may include water, coolant, foreign fluids, engine oil, or particulates. If a burnt odor is detected, the fluid should be changed using the recommended fluid type. If safe to do so, test-drive the vehicle to verify proper operation of the transmission in all gears and shift points, and to verify clutch operation on manual transmission. Note: A scan tool is required to identify if transmission related DTCs are present. -Repair/Replace Decision – if the wrong fluid or amount of fluid has been used in the vehicle, damage to the transmission may occur. It is important to note this damage, as it may be deemed non-collision related. If a pan is dented or crushed, internal damage is possible and will require disassembly for further inspection. Repairs to a damaged transmission case are limited due to durability concerns and usually require replacement. Most damage to other transmission parts will require replacement of the damaged parts Driveshafts -Driveshafts – a driveshaft is a mechanical component for transmitting torque and rotation from the transmission to the differential, or from the engine to the transaxle on vehicles with a front engine-rear transaxle configuration. Universal joints (U-joints), or constant velocity (CV) joints are incorporated to accommodate rotating parts at different angles. Yokes and slip joints connect the driveshaft tube at each end. Some driveshafts are multi-piece with a center support bearing. Front-wheel drive vehicles have two half shafts that transmits torque to the front wheels, one for each side. They are referred to as half shafts because a half shaft only runs from the transaxle to one wheel. -Driveshaft Inspection – when inspecting driveshafts, check for free-play in the splines, bearings, and supports, loose or damaged U-joints, missing weights, dents, or other damage. A vibration that increases as vehicle speed increases may indicate a bent or out of balance driveshaft. Clunking when shifting into gear, or shaking when loading and unloading the drivetrain are signs of parts that are worn or damaged and require replacement. Note: Document wear and tear items unrelated to the collision and communicate to customer -Driveshaft Repair/Replace Decisions – damaged driveshafts should be replaced. Do not attempt repairs to the tube or damaged ends (yokes). Replacement carrier bearings, U-joints, and yokes are typically unavailable. Some machine shops specialize in servicing, balancing, or new construction of driveshafts. -CV Joint – if a vehicle was driven to a repair facility, the customer should be consulted about how the vehicle handles. This may be helpful for identifying half shaft problems. Note: Do not test-drive before doing a complete visual inspection -Inspection – reports of clicking noises during turns may indicate a damaged outer CV joint. Clunking noises, a shudder, or shaking during acceleration may indicate that either CV joint may be damaged. Damaged or severely worn inner tripod joints may cause the steering wheel to shake slightly due to the torque loading and unloading inconsistently; this typically happens under light load conditions. On high mileage vehicles, this may be more common where the inner CV joint is in close proximity to the exhaust. When doing a visual inspection, look for torn CV boots, grease leaks, and bent or otherwise damaged parts -Repair/Replace Decision – damaged CV joints and CV joint parts should be replaced in accordance to the vehicle specific service information. Individual service parts for half shafts are typically available, but depending on the extent of damage, an assembly may be more viable. If a CV boot is damaged, inspect for dirt or other contaminations that may have entered the joint this will require further disassembly and inspection and may require joint replacement -Halfshafts Inspection – when inspecting vehicles with suspected suspension or drivetrain damage, a visual inspection of the vehicle’s halfshafts should be conducted. Inspect for incorrect position in the transaxle. This can be caused by a shifted engine, damaged engine mount, or suspension damage. In some situations, the inner tripod may separate from the inner joint inside the boot. Ensure that there are no incorrect, missing, or damaged parts. Inspect the tone rings for damage or missing teeth. When inspecting the splines and joints, also check for free-play. Depending on which part is damaged, halfshafts may be repaired using service parts. Bent halfshafts should not be straightened. Note: Never pull on the shaft portion of the CV axle, as this may cause the tripod bearing to come out of the inner joint. Always pull on the inner joint assembly. -Checking Drive Axle Position – positioning of the drivetrain parts in relation to the vehicle structure in front-wheel drive vehicles is critical, because the drive wheels are also steering wheels. Inspect the drivetrain mounts and brackets for damage as well as subframe position. To check the position of the drivetrain and axles: -1) Measure forward from a symmetrical reference point to both ends of the drive axle shafts -2) Cross measure these points -3) Compare side-to-side measurements -Note: Most vehicles will be symmetrical. A three-dimensionally correct vehicle structure will help ensure the drivetrain position and output shaft angles from the transaxle are correct. If inner CV joint tripod engagement depth is not adequate, the joint may separate when making a turn. Subframe -Subframe – a subframe is a component of a vehicle that typically bolts on to the unitized structure of the vehicle to support certain components, usually steering and suspension parts. An engine cradle is a type of subframe that supports the engine and transmission as well. The rear subframe supports suspension components that are at the rear of the vehicle as well as the rear differential on some rear-wheel or all-wheel drive vehicles. Subframes and engine cradles may be made of steel, aluminum, or magnesium -Engine Cradle – the engine cradle is bolted to the unitized structure and may support the engine, transaxle, front suspension, and steering gear. Depending on the vehicle maker, an engine cradle may also be called a subframe or engine frame. An engine cradle is susceptible to underbody damage. A damaged engine cradle/subframe may affect steering, suspension, and drivetrain alignment. If damage is suspected, measurements can be compared to dimension sheets, the vehicle collision repair manual, or to a known good part. Damaged subframes and engine cradles are not normally repaired, unless specific OEM procedures exist. -Engine Cradle Position – engine cradle position can be checked by inspecting for bent or out-of-position mounting fasteners and bushings and the position of dowel pins or alignment holes, or by comparing side-to-side measurements by selecting symmetrical locations on the vehicle to locations on the engine cradle. Fasteners that are not centered in the mounting pads of the cradle may indicate that the cradle has shifted. When inspecting an engine cradle/subframe, look for signs of impact or cracks. New corrosion, flaking, or cracked paint may indicate bent areas. Using dye penetrant and help locate small cracks. Note: An alignment report can help identify engine cradle/subframe position issues, steering axis inclination (SAI) angle issues can indicate movement from side to side, caster issues may indicate rearward movement. -Rear Subframe – rear subframes (steel or aluminum) bolt to the unitized structure and connect to the rear control arms, and may house other rear suspension parts. Positioning of the rear subframe can affect the vehicle thrust angle and tracking. If the vehicle is rear-wheel or all-wheel drive, the rear differential will be mounted to the rear subframe. Rear subframes are inspected similarly to an engine cradle, and are typically replaced when damaged. Servicing of subframes and engine cradles will require wheel alignment, and may require specific calibrations depending on how the vehicle is equipped. Note: Some fasteners used on subframes and engine cradles may be one time use. Be sure to consult vehicle-specific service information. Damage Analysis of Driving Assist Systems (1/2) Blind Spot Detection System -Blind Spot Detection System Introduction – many vehicles are now equipped with blind spot detection systems that detect vehicles approaching from behind an adjacent lane. On some vehicles, the blind spot detection system has an indicator lamp in the door mirror. The blind spot detection system: -Engages when the turn signal is activated and signals the driver when a vehicle is approaching -Generally uses radar to gauge the distance of approaching vehicles -May use a camera in the door mirror to watch for approaching vehicles -Is designed to ignore stationary objects -Blind Spot Detection System, cont’d – blind spot detection systems that use radar sensor -Have an indicator lamp on the door mirror that alerts the driver to the proximity of the approaching vehicle -May have a warning chime and a message in the instrument panel cluster to indicate an approaching vehicle -May allow the brightness of the indicator lamp to be adjusted to compensate for peripheral perception deterioration -Note: In some cases, the indicator lamp glows continuously when a vehicle is approaching and flashes when the vehicle is in the critical area -Radar Technology – the radar sensor for most blind spot detection systems is based on existing technology. For example, the advanced phased array radar technology system designed for the military guides missiles to their target. This system is miniaturized for use in blind spot detection, and is equipped with reduced power requirements. The system design tracks approaching vehicles by determining distance and closing rate. Other systems use Doppler radar technology. This type of radar, most commonly associated with weather radar, detects moving targets while ignoring the stationary targets. -Blind Spot Detection System Parts – primary blind spot detection system parts include: -Sensors, which are generally located behind the rear bumper cover, on each side. -Cameras, which are typically mounted beneath door mirrors to aid in detecting approaching traffic -A steering wheel, which on some systems vibrates when vehicles are detected in the blind spot -A control module, which calculates speed, object position, and vehicle tracking. Control modules cannot be adjusted or repaired -Blind Spot Detection System Operation – the blind spot detection system uses calculations to determine when an approaching vehicle enters and leaves the blind spot area. The computer software calculates when, in what manner, and in which situations a warning should be issued. The system applies voltage to the indicator lamp in the door mirror, illuminating the warning indicator. The range varies according to the vehicle maker. For example, on some vehicles, the range of the sensors extends rearward from the door mirrors to about 10 feet beyond the rear bumper, while on other vehicles the range may extend 11 feet beyond the rear bumper area. Note: On vehicles that use a camera-based system, the cameras can detect objects up to 31 feet rearward from their location on the door mirrors. -Does the Vehicle have Blind Spot Detection? – some indications that the vehicle is equipped with a blind spot detection system include: -Door mirrors equipped with an LED that lights when the approaching vehicle comes into the blind spot -An icon on the driver information center or instrument cluster -A switch to turn the system on or off -Note: On some vehicles, the mirror LEDs light when the vehicle is started -Blind Spot Detection System Damage Analysis – when performing damage analysis, check for a damaged: -Bumper bracket and/or bumper beam -Bumper cover -Door mirror -Note: Even in light impacts, a sensor can be damaged, and bent or collapsed bumper parts can affect sensor positioning and alignment. This will cause improper readings by the sensors. With some systems, the sensors are identical. In these cases, the sensors may be swapped from one side of the vehicle to the other when troubleshooting potential problems. However, sensors should not be swapped from one vehicle to another, as these sensors may perform differently from one model to the next. -Road Conditions and System Performance – in some cases, road conditions may result in system performance issues not related to mechanical damage. Some examples include: -Debris such as dirt, snow, or mud that builds up over the sensor, preventing an accurate reading -Severe weather, such as rain or snow, which affects the operation and causes occasional missed alerts -Towing a trailer or an object, such as a bicycle rack attached to the rear of the vehicle, will cause the system to not operate properly -Road Conditions and System Performance cont’d – other conditions that may cause the system not to operate properly include when: -Several vehicles accelerate from a standing start with a vehicle alongside -A vehicle moves from two lanes over to the adjacent lane -The rate of speed of the approaching vehicle is too fast -Note: On some vehicles, the system only engages if the blind spot detection system is switched on and vehicle speed is above 10 mph -Blind Spot Detection Diagnostics – beyond physical damage, a scan tool will be required to assess a malfunctioning blind spot detection system for proper diagnostics and trouble code retrieval -Blind Spot Part Replace Considerations – if the blind spot detection system sensors have been replaced, calibration is generally required using vehicle-specific diagnostic equipment. Calibration may also be required after rear bumper cover removal or replacement of the control modules. Calibration of the sensors is particularly vital, because the measurements taken from these points determine if a signal should be sent to indicate an approaching vehicle. The calibration essentially informs the computer of the sensor’s exact position so that the proper calculation can be made Cross-Traffic Alert System -Cross-Traffic Alert System Description – some vehicles are equipped with a feature called a cross-traffic alert system. This system assists the driver with oncoming traffic when backing out of a parking space. The system warns of an approaching vehicle with: -An audible alert from the parking aid speaker -A visual alert on the right or left door mirror -A message on the instrument panel cluster -Note: Some vehicles may display a warning that states either “Vehicle Coming From Right” or “Vehicle Coming From Left”. The system may detect vehicles approaching from up to 45 feet away. However, this distance may vary. -Cross-Traffic Alert System Operation – the cross-traffic alert system: -Uses many of the same parts as a blind spot detection system, such as sensors located behind the rear bumper cover. -Utilizes radar that detects an approaching vehicle -Has sensors that work at a 90 degree angle -Works for straight and angled parking -Only operates in reverse -Note: Some cross-traffic alert systems can be turned on or off with an ON/OFF function in the message center -Cross-Traffic Alert System Damage Analysis – because the system uses the same parts as the blind spot detection system, damage to the rear bumper or rear portion of the quarter panel can cause damage to the system. Additionally, the repair considerations are similar to the blind spot detection system. If a fault is detected on the instrument panel cluster, a scan tool is recommended for troubleshooting the problem. Similar to the blind spot detection system, items such as dirt, mud, snow, and other debris built up on the sensors can result in system malfunction Parking Assist System -Parking Assist System Description – parking assist has progressed to the point where most vehicle makers offer the system as an option or standard equipment on their new models. There are two types of parking assist. These include: -Assist only, where the system provides visual and/or audio feedback to help determine the distance to obstacles. -Hands-free parallel parking assist -Parking Assist – Assist Only – the parking assist system is designed to: -Calculate the distance to an object from the rear of the vehicle. Some systems engage when they detect an object 8 feet behind the vehicle -Provides audible and/or visual signals to the driver while parking and maneuvering the vehicle in REVERSE -Works at low speeds, typically less than 5-11 mph -Note: Some systems may also calculate how close an object is to the front of the vehicle when the vehicle is in DRIVE. Additionally, some systems do not detect objects below the bumper, such as people or pets and can be turned on or off manually using a switch on the instrument panel. -Hands-Free Parking Assist – some systems have been designed to automatically parallel park the vehicle into an open parking space. The driver only needs to confirm the desired parking space and apply the brakes when required to control speed. The vehicle handles the proper steering -Parking Assist System Parts – parts that make up the parking assist feature include: -Parking aid sensors, which are located in bumper covers and used to judge the distance to other parked vehicles -Parking aid modules – locations vary -A parking aid speaker that, when the vehicle is in reverse, emits an audible beep sound to alert the driver of any obstacles behind the rear of the vehicle -Cameras, that on some systems are located in the rear or front to provide a visual of the area around the vehicle -Note: Cameras are commonly mounted in the spoiler, deck lid, hatch, or mirrors -Parking Assist System Damage Analysis – many parking assist system parts are positioned in locations that are highly susceptible to damage including collision, wiring, and corrosion damage. Damage to parking assist system parts will typically result in a parking assist malfunction indicator lamp (MIL), but the system may need to be activated to determine if it is operating properly. -Parking Assist System Part Replacement – check parking assist sensors for damage, even after a minor collision. When analyzing damage to the parking assist parts, note that sensors and control modules cannot be adjusted or repaired. Damaged sensors are typically replaced and are generally identical in construction, so there is no specific sensor for reach position in the bumper. The sensors on the rear and front bumper can typically be refinished. However, excessive coats of refinish can hinder sensor performance. Note: Some vehicle makers do not recommend sensors with minor finish damage because excessive paint thickness may adversely affect the operation of the parking assist system. Refer to vehicle-specific service information for repair or replacement recommendations for parking sensor -Hands-Free Parallel Parking Assist Parts – in addition to the parking assist system parts already discussed, there are additional cameras and sensors that are integral parts of the hands-free parallel parking assist system including: -Cameras -Parking assist ECU -Multi-display -Steering angle sensor -Skid control ECU -Park/neutral position switch -Display and navigation -Note: The hands-free parallel parking assist system may utilize cameras mounted in various locations on the vehicle, including the deck lid, tailgate, side mirrors, bumpers, and grille. Identifying damages, other than physical damages, will typically require the use of a scan tool. -Examples of Hands-Free Parallel Parking Assist Operation – when the driver puts the car in reverse, the rearview camera activates and displays on the vehicle dashboard. Pressing the parallel park icon activates the hands-free parallel parking assist feature and displays an indicator square over the parking area. The driver can now go hands-free as the steering system begins to angle the car into the parking space. The system’s parking assist sensors judge the distance to other vehicles. To make calculations and estimates, the parking assist electronic control unit (ECU) uses signals from the: -Cameras -Steering angle sensor -Power steering ECU -Skid control ECU -Engine control module (ECM) -Parking assist sensors -Note: These results create various guidelines, which combine together with video from the camera, and output to the multi-display -Hands-Free Parallel Parking System Damage Analysis – when performing a damage analysis inspection on the hands-free parallel parking system, other than physical damage, use of a scan tool will be required to diagnose the parking assist system. If the parking assist system detects a malfunction: -A diagnostic trouble code (DTC) will be stored -The object alarm module may send a message to the instrument panel cluster to display the SERVICE PARK ASSIST message on the driver information center -A chime may sound -Note: The specific warning may vary depending on the vehicle maker. Depending on the size of the equipped tires (for example, the tire size may affect the rotation of the tire in relation to the steering wheel) and the vehicle angle (for example, the vehicle angle is too small), the parking position may deviate or the vehicle may not be straight -Hands-Free Parallel Parking Assist System Part Replacement – test drives are often used to calibrate specific sensors. If a fuse or the battery is disconnected, the system may require initialization. For example, on some vehicles, if you remove and reinstall the battery, the rearview monitor will be operational, but cannot assist backup parking operation. This is because the center position recognized by the steering angle sensor may not be initialized. When replacing the parking assist ECU, you may also be required to initialize the cameras, and calibrate the steering angle and height control sensors. Damage Analysis of Driving Assist Systems (2/2) Lane Departure Warning System -Lane Departure Warning Assist System Introduction – the lane departure warning assist system helps the driver keep the vehicle in the chosen lane. The system uses audio, visual, or steering wheel vibrations to warn the driver that the vehicle is drifting out of the lane. Some systems use the brakes to provide counter steering force to help keep the vehicle in the lane, but other systems may only provide a warning. The lane departure warning assist system typically operates at a speed of 35 mph or above, depending on the vehicle maker, and requires lane makers. -Lane Departure Warning System Activation – the lane departure warning system is not activated if the turn signal is engaged before changing lanes. If the system is activated, the warning ends when the driver steers back into the lane, a lane change is completed, the vehicle drives on the line for longer than 2.5 seconds, or if the brake pedal is pressed -Lane Departure Warning System Camera – the lane departure sensor is a camera, which is behind the windshield near the interior rearview mirror. The camera and computer are housed in the camera control unit above or within the interior rearview mirror unit, looking at the road ahead and monitoring the lane markings -Active Lane Keeping Systems – active lane keeping systems alert the driver who is drifting out of a lane and nudge the vehicle back into the lane if the driver does not react. The system uses a camera mounted above the interior rearview mirror to watch lane markings. Some vehicles have an instrument panel icon, which illuminate to indicate whether the active lane keeping system is on or off. On some models, an icon that shows a vehicle drifting out of its lane indicates if the active lane keeping system is on or off in the vehicle. Note: as with all active assist electronic systems, it is mandated in the U.S. that there be a driver option to turn the system off -Lane Departure Warning System Parts – primary parts of the lane departure warning system may include: -A camera -A control module -A lane departure warning on/off switch -A vibrator motor (if equipped) -The instrument panel cluster -The radio, for warning notification -Lane Departure Warning System Operation- the Lane Departure Warning System has three modes of operation that include Ready to Assist, Disabled (where the driver switches the system off), and Not Ready to Assist. The ‘Not Ready to Assist’ mode is where the system is active, but not ready to assist because: -The vehicle is traveling below a certain speed, generally 35-42 mph -The system cannot detect lane markings -The windshield in front of the camera has debris that is preventing the camera from detecting the lane markings -Lane Departure Warning System Damage Analysis – a warning in the vehicle message center indicates that the system is not functioning properly. Damage to the system may be indicated by: -Cracks in the windshield that obstructs the camera lens -Damage to the roof panel near the windshield above the camera -Note: When repairs to the lane departure warning system are required, be sure to follow vehicle maker service and calibration requirements -Lane Departure Warning System Limitations – road construction or poorly marked roads will prevent proper operation of the lane departure warning system. The lane departure warning system may not accurately detect: -Temporary markings along construction sites that would confuse the system -Worn and faded road markings -Road markings covered by snow or ice -Road markings on roads with sharp bends -Road markings under very poor weather conditions -Note: These non-mechanical issues can cause the lane departure warning system to appear not to be operating properly Attention Assist System -Attention Assist System Introduction – similar to the lane departure warning feature is the attention assist system. The attention assist system may also be called “drowsy driver”, “attention assist”, or “driver alert control”. The attention assist system monitors the drivers’ attentiveness. The system uses sensor technology to create an overall driver profile at the beginning of a trip and continuously compares the behavior pattern with real-time data from the sensors throughout the journey. A camera monitors the movement between lane markers and assess whether the vehicle is being driven in a controlled way. The sensor information determines the exact time the driver should take a break and provides an audible and/or visual warning -Attention Assist System Parts – many of the same parts used for the lane departure warning system are used for the attention assist system. These include: -A forward sensing camera -Driver information control module -Forward sensing control module -An infotainment module (silences the sound system to provide an audible warning) -A driver information center -Attention Assist System Operation – some systems give the driver an audible alert and a message on the instrument cluster display to recommend the driver stop for a break when the system detects a decrease in concentration. The system may also display bars on the instrument cluster to indicate the driver’s concentration level, with five bars being the highest level of concentration -Attention Assist System Damage Analysis – the attention assist system uses the same camera as the lane departure warning system and may experience similar problems, which may occur from: -Weather and road conditions -Physical damage to the windshield or camera housing -Note: For issues other than physical damage, use a scan tool to diagnose the attention assist system. As with the lane departure warning system, be sure to reference vehicle maker service information to identify calibration requirements following service of the attention assist system. Cracks in the windshield can obstruct the camera’s vision Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) System -Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) System Introduction – the adaptive cruise control (ACC) is an extension of conventional cruise control systems. The ACC system automatically maintains a set distance from a vehicle ahead. The system is not a collision warning or avoidance system; however, parts of the ACC system may be integrated into the collision avoidance systems. The ACC ONLY assists the driver and is not a fully independent driving system. The ACC does enhance driver convenience, since accelerating and braking operations occur without driver intervention. Note: As with conventional cruise control systems, manual inputs from the driver, both to the accelerator and brake, take priority over the ACC system -ACC System Parts Introduction – lets review parts common to the ACC system -Distance Sensor – the distance sensor is a radar or laser sensor depending on the vehicle maker. The sensor is located on the front of the vehicle to detect the presence of a vehicle ahead, and the clearance between the ACC vehicle and the forward vehicle. Some sensors can be found under or behind the front bumper, or behind the grille -Control Modules and Sensors – the control modules include a module specific to the ACC in addition to the ABS and engine control modules. The primary function of the ACC module is to process the radar information and determine if a forward vehicle is present. When the ACC system is in ‘time gap control’, the system sends information to the engine control and brake control modules to control the clearance between the ACC vehicle and the target vehicle. Wheel speed sensors read the speed of the vehicle’s wheel rotation and send the information to the ABS module. The wheel speed sensors determine if a wheel is moving at a different speed than the other wheels on the vehicle -ACC System Damage Analysis – certain conditions may prevent the ACC from engaging. These conditions include, but are not limited to: -A damaged or misaligned sensor, which is the most common problem -Sensor obstruction from paint, stickers, a vehicle front protector, excessive mud, snow, etc. -Bad weather conditions, which may limit the effectiveness of a laser-based distance sensor -Vehicle speed that is too high or too low -Heavy cargo causing the front of the ACC vehicle to rise -The rear of the forward vehicle being extremely dirty, or having no reflectors -Curved road, which prevents the system from contacting a preceding target -Sensor Part Replacement – during replacement of the distance sensor, you must calibrate the ACC system using a scan tool and following vehicle maker service requirements. A sensor alignment tool may also be required for calibration. Similar to other systems, improper vehicle alignment can also cause problems with the ACC system. If the vehicle is unable to track the vehicle ahead due to an excessive thrust angle issue, the system will not work properly. Note: Ensure the vehicle is aligned properly before calibrating the adaptive cruise control. If the vehicle is dog tracking down a road, the sensor may not pick up the vehicle ahead. Collision Avoidance System -Collision Avoidance System Operation – collision avoidance systems detect situations in which a collision may be imminent. The collision avoidance system: -Is designed to predict a collision -Helps prevent the collision from happening or minimizes the severity -Can be used to detect front and rear collisions -Uses forward-object detection with additional information, such as vehicle speed, to predict an imminent collision -Note: A properly operating system determines if the distance between vehicles is at a dangerous level and if a collision is about to happen. The system will forewarn the driver (through audible, visual, and/or physical alerts) of a possible collision to prompt the driver to take preventative action. -Brake Assist – brake assist automatically adjusts the brakes to provide full stopping force as soon as the driver steps on the pedal. Depending on the system, this may be done by increasing the brake pressure and/or moving the brake pads closer to the discs. -Automatic Braking – on vehicles equipped with collision warning with auto braking, both a radar sensor an a camera are used to detect forward vehicles. One advantage of the camera is the possibility of detecting stationary vehicles and warning the driver while maintaining a low false-alarm level. If the driver does not act when a collision is considered imminent, the system will pre-load the brake system and automatically slow or stop the vehicle -Seat Belt Tensioners – the system also adjusts the driver and front passenger seat belt tensioners by reducing slack to restrict occupant movement -Other Warnings – other tasks that may be performed by collision avoidance systems include: -Adjusting the seats into a crash safety position and/or adjusting the seat contour -Closing the sunroof and/or side windows -Raising the head restraints -Adjusting the dampening action of the shock absorbers to help keep the vehicle level -Note: If a collision does not take place, some systems, such as the seat belts and brakes, return to their normal state -Collision Avoidance System Parts – the collision avoidance system: -Is dependent on system options -Uses radar sensors to detect vehicles in front and behind the vehicle -Control module calculates speed, object position, and vehicle tracking -Note: On some vehicles, detecting vehicles and obstacles in front of the vehicle is determined from information provided by the front millimeter wave radar sensor and an object recognition camera. The millimeter wave radar sensor is also used by the adaptive cruise control system. The object recognition camera consists of two side-by-side cameras typically located in the center front area of the headliner -Collision Avoidance System Damage Analysis – correct alignment of a forward object detection sensor is critical for proper system operation. Misalignment may result in incorrect object detection. Situations that result in an out-of-alignment condition may include tampering, damage to the sensor and/or mounting brackets, or normal wear. Collision warning systems provide MILs in the event the system detects an error. The light or message displays on the instrument panel or message center, sometimes combined with an audible warning. This will engage from bad weather, sensor malfunction, or control module malfunction. A complete inspection will require the use of a scan tool to check for diagnostic trouble codes -Collision Avoidance System Part Replacement Considerations – radar replacement requires specific adjustment of the radar sensor beam axis. Correct alignment of sensors is necessary to ensure the proper operation of the system. Calibration may be required following a collision if there is damage in the area of a sensor or if a sensor is replaced. Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify required service procedures and calibrations Damage Analysis of Full-Frame Vehicles (1/2) Full-Frame Vehicles Overview Full-Frame Vehicles -Body-Over-Frame Vehicles – body-over-frame vehicles, also referred to as full-frame vehicles, have a frame independent of the unitized structure. Body-over-frame vehicles have the engine, drivetrain, and steering and suspension parts as well as a unitized structure that bolt to the frame. Body-over-frame designs in non-commercial applications are used for pickup trucks and several sport-utility vehicles, vehicles that are primarily used for heavy-duty purposes Frame Construction -Frame Construction – frames may be constructed of mild steel, high-strength steel (HSS), Ultra-High-Strength Steel (UHSS), high-strength low-alloy steel (HSLA), or a combination of these steel types. There are different frame construction designs used on vehicles as well. These different designs include open C-channel frames and box frames. -Open C-Channel Frames – open C-channel frames are not completely enclosed structures. Depending on the extent of the damage, there may be more opportunity for repair since you have access to the backside of the rail with this type of design -Box Frames – box frames either may be enclosed structures that are assembled with two C-channels seam-welded together or they may be hydroformed. A closed “C” refers to two C-channels welded together to form a box. When viewing them from the end of the rail, the weld overlaps can be seen. Hydroformed box frames are enclosed structures that are made using one piece of steel. Fully enclosed frame rails are more difficult to straighten out compared to open C-channel frame rails because there is no access to the backside -Designs for Strength – some of the designs for strength in newer full-frame construction include the use of welded-on brackets, mounts, and crossmembers. On older model vehicles, crossmembers were riveted to the frame. Crossmembers are now commonly welded to the frame side rails, instead of using rivets. Crossmembers are sometimes passed through the frame rails, and welded on both sides. Using welds instead of rivets adds to the rigidity of the frame -Unitized Structure on Full-Frames – unitized structures bolted to the frame are repaired in a similar way to that of a unibody vehicle. Pillars, rocker panels, and front upper rails must be repaired according to vehicle service information, while taking into consideration the different types of construction materials and part designs -Unitized Structure on Full-Frames Continued – when analyzing full-frame damage, note that similar to a unibody structure, energy created by a collision can transfer and damage areas away from the direct impact. This can cause distortions to the frame that can result in stress to the unitized structure. Sometimes, when the frame is returned to factory dimensions, the stress on the attached unitized structure is relieved and the measurements return to specifications. However, it is important to monitor both upper body and frame dimensions Collision Energy Management -Collision Energy Management – like unibody vehicles, full-frame vehicles also have collision energy management built-in to protect the occupants. To manage collision energy, full-frames may use collapse or crush zones to absorb collision energy. Indentations or convolutes in the front frame rails may be designed to crush. A part collapsed in these areas usually requires replacement, because a straightened collapse zone may not react the same way if involved in another collision. Full-frames may also manage collision energy by using elongated holes or slots, a change in the shape of the part, and reinforcements Damage Analysis of Full-Frame Vehicles (2/2) Full-Frame Repair Considerations Full-Frame Vehicle Damage Analysis -Full-Frame Damage Analysis – when inspecting a full-frame, check for bends in the structure. Depending on the thickness and strength of the metal, bends may or may not be repairable. Heat use when straightening bends is generally limited. You should also check for kinks, which are not repairable, and cracks caused by a collision, which may not be repairable. Some cracks may be repairable, but only if they are stress cracks. Stress cracks may require welding a reinforcement on the front or backside of the crack. When repairing cracks in a frame, reinforcements should NOT be used in collapse zones. When you are inspecting a full-frame, there are other things to check for as well -Non-Repairable Cracks – frame cracks that should not be repaired include cracks: -Near the steering box area -Near a suspension mounting area -On the top or bottom flange of an open C-channel design -Caused by straightening an area that was previously kinked -Note: Follow vehicle maker recommendations for crack repair -Other Inspection Considerations – other items that must be inspected on full-frames include: -Damaged cab mounts -Bent crossmembers -Damaged control arms or suspension mounts -Bent steering mounting brackets -Note: Generally, damaged crossmembers are not serviceable parts unless they are part of an assembly. However, some vehicle makers design vehicles with serviceable crossmembers. Control arm and suspension mounting brackets are difficult to straighten out while also ensuring they are not deformed due to repeated stress. Depending on replacement recommendations, parts may be available. The frame or frame section may need to be replaced. Damage related to trailer hitches is a common occurrence on full-frames. The impact often pushes the frame downward, bending at a point near the end of the trailer hitch. Damaged bumper mounting brackets should not be straightened. These are usually replaced using a bolt-on replacement bracket -Visual Damage Indicators for Full-Frames – on body-over-frame vehicles, the frame assembly may not have visual damage, but there are several indicators that structural damage has occurred. Check for shifted body mounts. The radiator core support on a body-over-frame vehicle is generally bolted to the frame assembly. It is common for the radiator core support to slide during a frontal impact, causing the hood-to-fender gaps to be inconsistent. The gap will generally be tight on one side and wide on the other. Also look for panel misalignment. Inconsistent gaps between the cab and the bed on a pickup truck are an indicator of a diamond condition. One side will generally be wider than the other side. Finally, inspect wheel position in the wheel opening to make sure it is centered Partial Replacement -Partial Replacement Information Sources – there are a number of vehicle-specific partial replacement procedures available. Procedures can be found in: -Vehicle-specific body repair or service information -Procedure bulletins (bulletins usually included with the part, similar to the procedures found in manuals) -Documentation provided with the service part -Note: It is important to reference the vehicle maker’s repair information for procedures and include copies of the repair documentation in the repair order file -Frame Replacement Options – partial replacement at a factory seams may be done depending on the vehicle makers recommendations. Some vehicle makers have front and/or rear frame kits available. Pieces of the frame that are commonly replaced include: -Frame tips or ends, usually in front of a crossmember. The front-end service part may include the crush zone and body mount -Front or rear assemblies that may include both rail ends and a crossmembers -Crush boxes, which are sacrificial parts designed to collapse in a frontal collision, reducing the amount of damage radiating into the structure -Sectioning – if the vehicle maker procedures or recommendations are not available, DO NOT SECTION. This is I-CAR’s position: Vehicle makers design parts to manage collision energy in a specific manner. Introducing a sectioning joint can alter how the part will respond in a collision. For this reason, it is recommended to section a part ONLY if it is recommended by the vehicle maker. Vehicle maker recommendations help ensure the repair does not make the repair area any stronger or weaker than the factory design. Sleeves and reinforcements should only be used when recommended by the vehicle maker. Keep in mind that safety and improper repair techniques can affect airbag deployment. Sectioning cannot be done in the same spot twice. If the damaged part was previously replaced using a sectioning procedure and there is no other acceptable sectioning location, the part cannot be sectioned again. Mounting Areas, Crossmembers, and Accessories -Mounting Area Types – -There are often questions raised about how to repair damaged mechanical or non-mechanical part mounting areas on full-frames, and if repair is allowed or possible. Some examples of mechanical parts mounted to frames include: -Steering and suspension brackets -Cables -Drivetrain mounts -Some examples of non-mechanical parts mounted to frames include: -Body mounts -Support brackets -These parts may be bolted, welded, or riveted directly to the frame rails or crossmembers -Types of Mounting Area Damage – different types of mounting area damage that can occur to attachment points, brackets, or mounts include: -Cracked parts or welds -Tears -Bends in the bracket or mount -Bends in the adjacent frame or crossmember -Elongated holes, such as bolt holes -Repair Choice Considerations – the answer to whether a damaged mount area can be repaired or not is not always clear. All repair choice considerations should be evaluated. -Questions to Ask – -There are certain questions you may ask to determine whether or not a repair should be made: -Is the repair under continuous stress? -If the damage is in an area that moves as the vehicle travels on the road or every time the steering wheel turns, it could have damaged stress cracks in the future -What is the chance for repeat damage? -This not only includes parts in continuous motion, it also includes vulnerable areas if another collision takes place -Are there any safety-related parts involved? -Any repairs made should ensure the vehicle safety systems will respond as designed -Mounting Location Questions – damage to mounting locations of parts that, if not mounted correctly, could cause the vehicle to lose control or lessen the chance of a safe turn or stop, should also be considered. Some questions you can ask to help determine if repairing is an option are: -Is there enough access for welding or another procedure? -Will there be enough access to apply corrosion protection? -Are there any OEM warnings that warn against the repair? -Note: Parts should not be repaired if there are existing warnings against making a repair. The vehicle maker may not have a recommendation for repair, at least repairs related to the bracket or mounting the part itself. With damage to mounting areas, especially suspension or steering mounting areas, whether to repair and how to make the repair is a business decision, since every type of damage is unique and there are limited OEM recommendations -Replacement Considerations – when considering replacement options for damaged frame mounting areas and crossmembers, verify whether or not the vehicle maker provides a service part. If the part is not available as a separate service part, replacement of a major part of the frame, or the entire frame, may be the only option. Replacement procedures for frame mounting areas and crossmembers are rare. If there is no existing replacement procedure, duplicate the factory attachment method. -Frame Accessories – accessories that may be attached to the frame include: -Trailer hitches -Snow plows -Tow hooks -Brush guards -Grille guards -Running boards -Colored display lighting -Frame Accessory Considerations – there are certain points to consider when analyzing frame accessories -Vehicle Maker or Aftermarket Accessories – when repairing collision damage, it may be necessary to determine if a frame accessory is: -A vehicle maker-installed part -An aftermarket-installed part -Note: If the part was installed by the vehicle maker, there may be existing guidelines for repair or replacement. If the part was installed as an aftermarket accessory, there are certain guidelines that may have to be followed for replacement of the part -Aftermarket Considerations – some vehicle makers offer body builder manuals, which are designed for the aftermarket industry. The manuals provide information related to accessory installation and vehicle structure alterations. Information contained in these manuals includes, but is not limited to: -Precautions for drilling -Welding -Suspension alterations -Engine performance alterations -Note: If a customer wants to install a new accessory to an undamaged frame, check with the vehicle makers body builder manual for guidelines related to mounting accessories to the frame. Installation of a non-vehicle maker accessory may present a liability issue. Body builder information may be provided on a website -Damaged Accessories – considerations when analyzing damaged aftermarket or vehicle maker accessories may include: -Checking for maker branding. Vehicle maker parts will need to be acquired through an appropriate distributor -Whether or not a customer consultation is necessary to determine where replacement parts can be purchased or acquired -How the owners insurance policy or company guidelines may apply to the damage caused to accessories -Repair options. One example would be kits that may be available for repairing grille guards -The brand name of the tonneau cover is shown here -Trailer Hitches – the majority of full-frame vehicles have trailer hitches attached to the frame. In a rear-end collision, the trailer hitch acts like a fulcrum and often damages the rear frame rails where the hitch is attached to the frame. Repair options for this type of damage include straightening if the frame is bent, as well as full or partial replacement if the frame is kinked. Partial replacement may be done using a vehicle maker-serviced rear rail section, or a rear frame replacement module. Note: A collision with the trailer hitch, may result in damage that radiates deeper into the frame Damage Analysis of Lighting and Vision Assist (1/3) Adaptive Front Lighting Description -Adaptive Front Lighting – adaptive front lighting was developed to create better visibility while cornering by turning the headlamps in the direction of the turn. This system also: -Automatically levels the low beam headlamps (up/down) to compensate for varying vehicle loads, in addition to quick starts and sudden stops. -Has a fail-safe feature that in case of communication error, the system returns the headlamps to the straight-ahead position. -Note: Some vehicles may be equipped with only the auto-leveling feature, while some contain both the auto-leveling and cornering feature -Adaptive Front Lighting Movement – the headlamp swivel assembly control module drives the headlamp swivel actuator and moves the headlamp to the left and right operational limit. Then it returns to the proper position. On some systems, the right and left headlamps do not rotate simultaneously. While turning, the headlamp swivel actuator responds as follows: -The amount of headlamp movement is dependent on steering wheel rotation. Some systems, have their motorized lamps turn up to 15 degrees in either direction when driving -On other systems, the headlamp control module controls the left headlamp movement by 15 degrees to the left and 5 degrees to the right, and the right headlamp movement by 5 degrees to the left and 15 degrees to the right. Adaptive Front Lighting System Operation -Adaptive Front Lighting Operation – the adaptive front lighting generally does not operate with the transmission in reverse or at vehicle speeds less than 2 mph. This may vary by vehicle maker. Movement of the headlamps may be restricted at low vehicle speeds, and full movement of the lamps may not be allowed until vehicle speed is greater than about 30 mph. -System Operation – for the adaptive front lighting system to work, the headlamp control module receives information from a variety of sources, such as the: -Engine control module (ECM) -Transmission control module (TCM) -Electronic brake control module (EBCM) -Body control module (BCM) -Steering wheel angle sensor -Headlamp switch status -Transmission gear selection -Speed sensor -Note: The headlamp control module calculates the headlamp angle and sends commands to the left and right headlamp actuators, while the direction the headlamps move is controlled by the steering wheel angle sensor. Also, the headlamp actuators drive the headlamps to the position commanded by the headlamp control module. -Automatic Vertical Alignment – for the automatic vertical alignment of the headlamp system to function, the: -Leveling sensors send an output to the headlamp leveling controllers as the vehicle suspension compresses and rebounds -Controllers calculate the difference in vehicle pitch and send a command to the high-intensity discharge (HID) ballast -HID ballast drives the headlamp leveling actuators to the position commanded by the controllers. The headlamp leveling system also monitors the performance of the HID bulb and ballast. -Adaptive Front Lighting Parts (AFS) – adaptive front lighting parts include swiveling and leveling actuators, control modules, and sensors such as: -Steering wheel angle sensor -Vehicle speed sensor -Vehicle height sensor. The height sensor linkage is installed on the front and rear axle Adaptive Front Lighting System Damage Analysis -Adaptive Front Lighting Operation – the adaptive front lighting generally does not operate with the transmission in reverse or at vehicle speeds less than 2 mph. this may vary by vehicle maker. Movement of the headlamps may be restricted at low vehicle speeds, and full movement of the lamps may not be allowed until vehicle speed is greater than about 30 mph. -System Operation – for the adaptive front lighting system to work, the headlamp control module receives information from a variety of sources, such as the: -Engine control module (ECM) -Transmission control module (TCM) -Electronics brake control module (EBCM) -Body control module (BCM) -Steering wheel angle sensor -Headlamp switch status -Transmission gear selection -Speed sensor -Note: The headlamp control module calculates the headlamp angle and sends commands to the left and right headlamp actuators, while the direction the headlamps move is controlled by the steering wheel angle sensor. Also, the headlamp actuators drive the headlamps to the position commanded by the headlamp control module -Automatic Vertical Alignment – for the automatic vertical alignment of the headlamp system to function, the: -Leveling sensors send an output to the headlamp leveling controllers as the vehicle suspension compresses and rebounds -Controllers calculate the difference in vehicle pitch and send a command to the high-intensity discharge (HID) ballast -HID ballast drives the headlamp leveling actuators to the position commanded by the controllers. The headlamp leveling system also monitors the performance of the HID bulb and ballast. -Adaptive Front Lighting Damage Analysis – when checking for damage to adaptive front lighting systems, check for: -Physical damage to the headlamp assembly -Damaged sensors -Damaged motors -Note: An initialization procedure may be required when parts are replaced. The headlamp control module monitors the headlamp actuator motor control circuits for proper circuit continuity and for shorts to ground or proper voltage. If a malfunction is detected, a diagnostic trouble code (DTC) will be stored in memory and the driver will be notified with a message displayed on the driver information center (DIC). If a fault is detected, the system will default to setting the headlamps to a straight-ahead position. Damage Analysis of Lighting and Vision Assist (2/3) High Beam Assist Description -High beam Assist – high beam assist systems automatically turn the high beams on and off to ensure the best headlight setting for illumination of the road ahead regardless of whether you are driving on highway, a country highway, or in the city. Automatic high beam assist system may: -Detect approaching vehicle headlamps and turn off high beams -Detect a preceding vehicle’s tail lamps and turn off high beams -Monitor the outside light and turn off high beams, if it is bright enough that the high beam headlamps are not required -High Beam Assist Part Locations – high beam assist systems use a forward-facing camera that detects light color, intensity, and movement. The camera is typically located near the interior rearview mirror High Beam Assist Damage Analysis -High Beam Assist Damage Analysis – when performing damage analysis of high beam assist systems, it will be necessary to check for DTCs set by the electronic control unit (ECU). Therefore, a maker-approved scan tool will be required. When servicing high beam assist systems, it is important to always reference vehicle maker service information to obtain testing procedures and service requirements. An initialization procedure may be required after part replacement. Damage Analysis of Lighting and Vision Assist (3/3) Night View Assist System Description -Night View Assist Systems – night vision assist systems extend the perception of the driver beyond the reach of the headlights through the use of thermo-graphic cameras, infrared lights, head up display, and other technologies -Overview – night vision assist systems are broken into two categories: active and passive. Active systems use infrared light sources to illuminate the darkness, while passive systems rely on thermal radiation that is emitted from cars, animals, and other potential hazards. Both systems rely on infrared data, but utilize the data differently -System Functionality – Night-view assist systems: -May use far-infrared technology, which detects heat emitted from objects. This type of technology may be referred to as thermal imaging. -May use near-infrared technology. Night vision enhancement systems that use this technology require special infrared “floodlamps” to project infrared light onto the surrounding area. The projected light is similar to what is used for many household remote controls -Are activated when the low beam headlamps are on -May detect objects a set distance ahead in darkness. One system can detect objects nearly 500 feet in front of the vehicle. -When activated, display the images on a black-and-white screen in the instrument panel or console -Black-and-White Systems – some systems provide the driver with a black-and-white image of the driving environment ahead of the vehicle in the control display or central information display. This system uses far-infrared technology without active infrared illumination. It essentially converts thermal radiation into electronic signals. The sensor elements alter the resistance in proportion to the temperature. The higher the temperature, the higher the signal, creating a whiter pixel. This system also: -Shows objects ahead in varying degrees of brightness depending on the temperature of these objects -Provides a warning in the head-up display and the central information display -Has a heater element on the inside of the protective window to prevent the camera lens from misting over or freezing up -Night View Assist System Operation – -In order for the night view assist system to be active: -The sensor must detect proper lighting conditions -The ON button must be pressed -Speed parameters must be met. For example, some systems cannot activate if vehicle speed is below 9 mph. -Other common considerations include: -The system can be restricted by environmental conditions, such as heavy rain and snow or extreme temperatures. -The daytime running lights must be switched off -Night View Assist System Parts – night view assist system parts may include: -The night vision camera -IR Floodlamps located near the headlamps -A control module beneath the instrument cluster -A video switch on the instrument panel -A washer jet for the camera lens -A sensor system -Note: The night vision camera may be mounted on or behind the grille, or located inside the passenger compartment near the windshield. These are normal halogen bulbs, equivalent to ordinary high-beam headlamps, but they use filters to prevent visible light rays from passing through. IR floodlamps are only used for near-infrared night vision systems. Night View Assist System Damage Analysis -Night View Assist System Damage Analysis – when checking the night view assist system for damage: -Note that the camera on some systems is located on or behind the grille. This area is subject to damage -Note that IR Floodlamps may be mounted to the bumper cover. There may not be specific aiming procedures for IR floodlamps, but deformed bumpers should be repaired or replaced to ensure that they are pointed in the proper direction. -There may be a protective cover over the camera lens that can be replaced -Camera replacement or alignment may require a vehicle maker-specific scan tool -System defects are displayed on the message center -Note: Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify necessary calibrations and initializations following camera replacement or system service Damage Analysis of Restraint Systems (1/2) Restraint System Damage Analysis Damage Analysis Overview -Restraint System Overview – restraint systems are designed to protect vehicle occupants during a collision. Examples of these systems include: -Airbags -A collapsible steering column -Head restraints -Roll bars -Seat belts -Seat belt pre-tensioners -Note: The Collision Repair Industry has an obligation to correctly repair the customers vehicle to ensure these systems will perform as designed in the event of another collision. All restraint system repairs must be performed in compliance with vehicle maker repair procedures and requirements. Repairs must also be completed using OEM-approved replacement parts and materials -Inspection Safety Precautions – it is important to take certain precautions before or during inspection of airbags and other safety restraint system (SRS) parts. These precautions include: -Disabling the restraint system to prevent accidental deployment -Wearing the proper personal protective equipment -Avoiding biohazards -Cleaning up broken glass -Ensuring airbags are deployed before disposing -Disable the Restraint System – before beginning repairs on any restraint systems, it is critical to first review vehicle maker service information. Be sure to follow the system disabling procedures to ensure the system has been completely discharged. Even if an airbag looks fully deployed, it may capable of a second deployment -Wear Protective Equipment – the powdery residue present after front airbag deployment may contain a small amount of sodium hydroxide, which may irritate the eyes, nose, mouth, or throat. Protective equipment that may reduce the chance of skin contact may include nitrile gloves, safety glasses or goggles, and a long-sleeved shirt -Avoid Biohazards – biohazards, such as blood and other body fluids, may be potentially infectious.
