Bloodborne pathogen training is required to clean up or work around blood spills. Always wear personal protective equipment when working around blood. This may include rubber gloves, and eye and respiratory protection -Clean Broken Glass – when analyzing interior damage, broken glass should be cleaned up prior to entering the vehicle to begin the damage analysis process. This will help to protect against personal injury or causing additional damage to upholstery -Ensure Airbags are Deployed – some airbags have two igniters. Depending on the severity of the collision, the igniters may be designed to fire simultaneously, milliseconds apart, or only one may fire. This may require manual deployment before disposing of the airbag module. Vehicle-specific service information must be referenced for safe handling and disposal procedures for deployed airbags, and recommended steps to ensure that the second stage is deployed -System Identification – to ensure an accurate estimate, it is important to identify what restraint systems the vehicle is equipped with and which systems have deployed. Check the airbags, seat belts and pre-tensioners, and warning lamp -Airbags – it is important to identify the number and location of airbags equipped on the vehicle, as well as the number that have deployed. It is also recommended that the vehicle maker service information be reviewed to identify the locations of all SRS components before starting the damage analysis process. Airbag locations may also be identified with the SRS acronym printed on a tag or label, or embossed on an interior trim panel. Deployed airbags are noticeable as they will protrude from the airbag module -Seat Belts and Pre-Tensioners – a thorough inspection of the seat belt assemblies, including retractors, buckles, anchors, and height adjusters, may be necessary to determine deployment. Inspect for damage to adjacent parts, as seat belt pre-tensioners may damage trim panels during deployment. Some seat belt pre-tensioners may require the use of a scan tool to determine deployment -Airbag Warning Lamp – an airbag warning lamp that remains lit may indicate that restraints have deployed. If this is the case, airbags, sensors, modules, occupant classification system (OCS) components, and other SRS components may need to be inspected further to determine deployment -Information Sources – to ensure an accurate estimate, it is important to identify what restraint systems the damaged vehicle is equipped with and the required replacement parts. In addition to vehicle maker websites, vehicle-specific required replacement and inspection items can be obtained from various information sources, including: -ALLDATA, which provides service information for most vehicle makes and models -Computerized estimating systems -I-CAR, using the OEM restraints system part replacement search -Restraint System Servicing – damaged or deployed restraint systems almost always set a diagnostic trouble code (DTC). However, it is important to remember not all DTC’s will turn on a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL). Therefore, the first step when servicing vehicle restraint systems should be performing a system scan using a vehicle maker approved scan tool. This will allow you to retrieve stored DTCs, perform system diagnostics, and may help identify damaged system components that may not have visible signs of damage. It is also important to remember that any services performed on the restraint system will likely result in the need to calibrate the system and may require programming of the module, if replaced. Seat Belt Systems -Seat Belt System Inspection – following a collision, seat belt parts should be inspected. These parts include the: -Belt buckle -Warning lamp -Webbing -Anchoring -Retractor -Emergency locking retractor -Automatic locking retractor -Note: In addition, vehicle seats and head restraint systems should be inspected as well -Buckle Inspection – when inspecting the belt buckle, ensure an audible click is heard when the tongue is inserted, and the seat belt remains buckled. Check the buckle for cracks or a loose release button. Notice the amount of pressure required to release the tongue. Excessive pressure is not normal. Look for indications of stress such as deformation or flaking of chrome. These things are not normal and indicate buckle damage -Warning Lamp Inspection – when inspecting seat belt buckles, verify that the seat belt warning lamp is operating properly. A seat belt buckle that is not functioning properly may cause the seat belt warning lamp to remain lit. the seat belt warning lamp should turn off when the driver seat belt is buckled. Some vehicles have an OCS that requires verifying that the seat belt warning lamp turns off when the passenger seat belt is buckled. In these vehicles, the seat belt warning lamp is designed to light when the passenger seta belt is unbuckled while the passenger seat is occupied -Webbing Inspection – the entire seat belt webbing and retractor assembly may require replacement if damaged or worn. Seat belt webbing should be fully extended and inspected for: -Tears, cuts, or broken threads -Signs of stretching or bowing -Visible seat belt replacement warning or indicator labels -Stains -Cigarette burns or other types of burn damage -Deterioration -Note: Seat belt webbing inspection may require determining whether or not damage is related to the collision. Stains on seat belt webbing should not be cleaned using solvents. The accident-related damage listed above will require webbing replacement -Anchoring Inspection – seat belt anchors should be inspected for damage and potential replacement. Common locations for seat belt anchors include the pillars, outside seat frame rail, and floor pan areas. Always refer to vehicle-specific service information to determine the required inspected or replacement recommendations -Anchor Mounts – anchor mounts should be inspected for cracks and distortion of the mounting locations. This includes the mounting location for retractors, D-rings, and the height adjuster. Inspect the panel flange areas of the mounting locations for damaged spot welds. Damaged spot welds located near the anchoring point may create a weak link and may affect the function of the seat belt system -Anchoring Bolts – anchoring bolts should be inspected for cracks, distortion, or looseness. Inspect for dirt and corrosion. Although dirt may be cleaned, corroded parts should be replaced. Seat belt assembly mounting bolts should be replaced with the seat belt assembly -Replacement Considerations – never replace individual parts that should be replaced as an assembly. Dismantling and only using parts of a seat belt assembly may comprise the safety of the vehicle occupants in future collisions. Refer to vehicle-specific service information for replacement procedures, such as torque specifications and mandatory replacement items or calibrations -Retractor Inspection – check for proper extension and retraction of the webbing. If the action is not smooth and easy, inspect for: -Twisted webbing -Dirt or other contamination on the webbing or in the retractor housing -Proper movement at the anchor mounts -ELR Inspection – the operation of the emergency locking retractor (ELR) should also be inspected. The retractor should lock the seat belt when it is pulled out quickly or when the retractor is tilted. Follow vehicle maker recommendations for testing the proper operation of the ELR. An improper ELR function may require replacement of the seat belt and retractor assembly -ALR Inspection – the automatic locking retractor (ALR) function of the seat belt can be used to secure a child safety seat. It must be tested for proper operation. To test the operation of an ALR: -1) Pull the seat belt all the way out of the retractor -2) Allow the seat belt to retract into the retractor slowly. This typically causes an audible clicking sound -3) At regular intervals, pull on the seat belt with normal force with the intention of extracting it from the retractor. The belt should lock up -4) if the seat belt does not lock up at any point, replace the retractor -Note: Although used to secure a child safety seat, the ALR function may be found on all seats with the exception of the driver seat. The operation of an ALR is similar for most vehicles -Seat Inspection – following a collision, it is important to inspect any seats occupied during the collision for damage including: -Seat frames -Springs -Foam -Seat covers -Reclining mechanisms -Seat tracks -Head Restraint Systems – head restraint systems are designed to aid in protection from whiplash injury resulting from rear collision. Types of head restraints include: -Adjustable, which require manual adjustment to achieve a good geometry to aid in reducing whiplash from a rear collision -Reactive, which use mechanical linkages to reposition the restraint up and forward based on pressure from the occupants torso -Proactive, which automatically move the head restraint up and forward when a collision occurs -Note: Always refer to vehicle maker service information to identify mandatory replacement items related to collision-damaged seats and head restraints systems. Some head restraints have vehicle maker approved procedures to reset or re-arm head restraints while other systems may require replacement after being deployed Seat Belt Pre-Tensioners -Seat Belt Pre-Tensioner Inspection – inspect seat belt pre-tensioners to determine if they have been deployed. The conditions under which seat belt pre-tensioners deploy vary depending on the system. Deployment of the seat belt pre-tensioner may occur when the airbags deploy or during minor collisions with no airbag deployment. A scan tool may be required to determine deployment of some rear seat belt retractors, as they may not lock up after deployment -Warning Lamp – inspect the vehicles instrument panel for illuminated warning lights. Seat belt pre-tensioner deployment may cause the seat belt or airbag warning lamps to illuminate -Retractor – the seat belt may lock up following deployment of the seat belt pre-tensioner. Rough operation or grinding noises may indicate a deployed pre-tensioner -Belt Buckle Assembly – a shortened buckle assembly may indicate a deployed pre-tensioner. For this type of system, it may also be helpful to compare a suspected deployed seat belt pre-tensioner with one that is known to not have deployed. A front passenger seat belt pre-tensioner may not deploy if the seat belt was not in use during the collision -Other Deployment Considerations – inspecting the adjacent parts may be useful when determining if a seat belt pre-tensioner has deployed. A seat belt pre-tensioner may damage trim panels during deployment. In addition, check all rear seat belt assemblies after any airbag deployment. Rear seat belts may be equipped with built-in seat belt pre-tensioners. In some instances, the rear seat belt assemblies are not equipped with seat belt use sensors; therefore, the SRS module may deploy all rear seat belt pre-tensioners with an airbag deployment -Replacement Considerations – it is vital to replace any deployed seat belt pre-tensioners, even if the seat belt retractor seems to be operating properly. Seat belt retractors that lock up when deployed may unlock when removed from the vehicle. This does not mean that it can be reinstalled Roll Bars -Pop-Up Roll Bars – pop-up roll bars and roll bar parts with physical damage must be replaced. Surface imperfections, such as scratches or blemishes, may be refinished as long as there is no damage affecting the integrity and operation of the part. Some pop-up roll bars may be reset if there is no damage. Resetting pop-up roll bars may require a special tool or may be as simple as pushing them back down into place. Some hydraulic roll bars are lowered by pressing a switch o the vehicle instrument panel. A MIL may indicate a malfunction with the pop-up roll bar system -Stationary Roll Bars – inspection of stationary roll bars should include: -Evaluating the structure of the vehicle where the roll bar is attached -Replacing damaged mounting hardware and fasteners -Inspecting the roll bar and mounting areas for damage -Note: Although some vehicle makers may allow refinishing, do not repair damaged stationary roll bars Damage Analysis of Restraint Systems (2/2) Airbag System Damage Analysis Front Driver Airbag -Driver Airbag Inspection and Damage Analysis – the driver airbag system has many parts that will require inspection and damage analysis, including: -Airbag module -Clock spring -Clock spring wiring -Steering column -Knee bolster -Knee bolster airbag -Clock Spring – the clock spring may require replacement following deployment. If the vehicle maker does not require replacement of the clock spring, it must still be inspected for damage. Damaged parts must be replaced. Inspect for: -Heat damage to the coil housing or electrical connectors -A broken ribbon wire, which provides an electrical connection to the airbag and steering wheel -Damaged electrical connectors, such as broken terminals and corrosion -Note: The system scan, performed prior to starting the damage analysis process, may have identified DTCs indicating a problem with SRS or other circuits to the steering wheel. This can aid in the damage analysis process. Damaged airbag system components require replacement. Consult vehicle maker service information to identify all required replacement items and services following an airbag deployment -Steering Column – steering columns should always be inspected for damage following a collision or airbag deployment. When inspecting the steering column for damage, look for sheared plastic pins or inserts, deformation of steering column brackets, or a tight gap between the steering wheel and steering column cover. It may also be necessary to take measurements of the overall steering column length or between specific points compared to manufacturer specifications. Some vehicle makers mandate replacement of the steering column following airbag deployment. A vehicle may also be equipped with a collapsible brake pedal assembly. This allows the pedals to detach during a moderate-to-severe collision, reducing the risk of trapping the drivers feet, or causing leg or ankle injures. Note: Do not test-drive or release the vehicle to the owner if damage to the steering column is suspected -Knee Bolster and Knee Bolster Airbags – knee bolsters are integral parts of the occupant protection system for frontal impacts. The knee bolster is the lower portion of the instrument panel of a passenger vehicle and are typically padded structures designed to absorb impact by the knees during a frontal collision. Knee bolster airbags act as a replacement to the knee bolster and are designed to inflate, holding the occupants knees safely in place against the lower portion of the seat. Note: Knee bolsters, knee bolster airbags, and system components should be closely inspected for damage and replaced in accordance with vehicle maker service requirements Front Passenger Airbags -Occupant Safety System Inspection – for children, the impact of an airbag inflating at more than 200 mph can cause permanent or deadly injuries. For that reason, OCS using sophisticated computer technology were developed to determine if the passenger is a child or adult, eliminating the need for an on/off switch for airbags -OCS Inspection – when inspecting an OCS for damage, it is important to inspect all seats and OCS components. Remember that the vehicle maker may mandate specific OCS part replacement after a collision. These parts may include the sensor pad, seat cushion foam, control module, or the complete OCS assembly. Do not repair parts of an OFC assembly nor replace individual parts from a new OCS assembly unless directed by the vehicle maker service information. Note: Identifying OCS damage may require the use of a vehicle maker approved scan tool to retrieve DTCs and perform diagnostic tests -OCS Recalibration – always refer to vehicle maker service information to identify OCS calibration requirements following a collision or system service. Calibration is typically done using a vehicle maker-specific scan tool and may require a specific set of weights provided by the vehicle maker -Front Passenger Airbag Replacement Considerations – damage inspection of passenger side front airbag systems is very similar to inspections performed on driver side airbag systems -Vehicle Interior – during deployment, some passenger airbags may cause damage to other related parts, including the instrument panel and the windshield. Damages to the instrument panel resulting from airbag deployment should not be repaired. In some cases, airbag system design may cause the airbag to deflect off the windshield, resulting in damage to the windshield -Airbag Parts – some passenger airbag modules have a built-in cover and may not damage the instrument panel or windshield during deployment. Damaged airbag covers should not be repaired. It is also important to inspect all system wiring harnesses and connectors for charring or heat damage. Any damages found to the wiring or connectors should be repaired or replaced following vehicle maker service requirements Side Airbags -Side Airbag Inspection – when inspecting side airbags, the inspection process will be very similar to the process used for front airbag system damage analysis. Following side airbag deployment, inspect for: -Deployed airbag modules -Interior panel damages, including impact damage, cracks, or deformation -Heat damage to wiring, connectors, modules, and sensors -Misalignment to adjacent parts and panels -Note: Also inspect areas on the vehicle such as sunroofs, plastic door panels, the roof, or movable and stationary glass for damage resulting from the pressure, or force, of an airbag deployment. Following a collision, in addition to replacing deployed airbag modules, the side impact sensors, damaged wiring, and the restraints control module (RCM) may also need to be replaced. Consult vehicle-specific service information for mandatory replacement items and system calibrations Impact and Safing Sensors and Wiring -Sensor Inspection – when inspecting sensors for damage, check for: -Cracked or deformed cases -Bent brackets -Corroded cases, brackets, or connectors -Note: Impact sensor wires or connectors may be cut or crushed in the area of impact. Internal electronic damage will be indicated by a lit airbag warning lamp. Always consult vehicle maker service information to identify mandatory replacement or repair options before performing repairs to any restraint system wiring Restraints Control Module -RCM Inspection – -Some collisions do not deploy any restraints; however, the RCM should be inspected for damage if it is located near the area of damage. When inspecting the RCM, check for: -Case damage -Cut or crushed wires or connectors -Bent mounting brackets -Water damage or corrosion -Refer to vehicle-specific service information for RCM replacement guidelines. Vehicle-specific recommendations may require the RCM to be replaced -After any type of SRS deployment -Only if damaged -After a certain number of deployments -For other reasons called out by the vehicle maker -If a certain code is present -Note: Always wear an electrostatic discharge strap when handling any electronic control module -RCM Programming – when programming a new RCM, transferring data from the original module to the new module may be required. This will require keeping the original RCM on hand until the replacement procedure is complete. Depending on the vehicle, subletting the replacement procedure to a local dealer may be required, as this process may include downloading the RCM programming data from a vehicle maker service information website. This process may involve having a subscription with special dealer access. Note: Performing the RCM programming and/or replacement procedure incorrectly may damage the RCM and require obtaining another new RCM Damage Analysis of Stationary Glass (1/2) Stationary Glass Role -Stationary Glass Role – stationary glass, especially the windshield, is part of the vehicle structure and contributes to the strength of the roof and the pillars. This helps manage collision energy. For example, in a front collision, a urethane-bonded windshield helps direct energy around the A-pillar and over the roof, away from the passenger compartment. Stationary glass must be installed by using the proper procedures, materials, and following the specified cure times. This is important for ensuring that the structural integrity of the glass is maintained. Note: Some vehicles may require special urethane adhesive for stationary glass installation -Retains Occupants – stationary glass also keeps unrestrained occupants inside. Severe injuries or fatalities can occur when occupants are ejected from the vehicle. -Works with Passenger Airbags – another role for the windshield is to work with the passenger airbag. A deploying airbag may use the windshield to deflect the deployment in the proper direction. It may also be rendered ineffective by loosening or forcing the windshield out of the opening if it has been installed incorrectly -Structural Role of Stationary Glass – this video explores how stationary glass plays a part in transferring collision energy, and keeping passengers safe Types of Stationary Glass -Laminated or Tempered Glass – there are two types of glass used in vehicles today. -Laminated Glass – Laminated glass is made of two sheets of glass with a plastic inner layer. It: -Holds together when broken due to inner plastic laminate -May have a thick, PVB layer, which serves as a sound deadener and is called ‘acoustic’ glass -Helps prevent occupants from being ejected from the vehicle during a collision -Is used in all windshields -Is sometimes used for side glass, glass roofs, and back glass -Can be repaired, to a limited extent -Tempered – tempered glass is heat treated to increase impact resistance. The glass is heated to about 1,100 degrees Fahrenheit, and then rapidly cooled. In addition: -It cannot be repaired -It may be brittle, and will shatter if cut, drilled, or ground -It may be layered with two sheets of tempered glass -Shattered pieces are not as sharp as laminated glass slivers -It may be used anywhere but the windshield -If previously damaged or stressed, the glass can shatter at a later time -Breaking Glass – this video briefly shows how laminated glass breaks from an impact versus how tempered glass breaks from an impact -Tinting – OEM glass is often tinted. Common tint colors include: -Blue -Green -Bronze -Gray -Note: Extremely dark tinting that covers the entire glass is called ‘privacy’ glass. Privacy glass can only be used behind the B-pillar because it reduces the driver’s vision if used for the windshield or front door glass -Frit – the frit is the black band around the perimeter of most stationary glass. The frit: -Helps prevent UV rays from deteriorating urethane -Is made of ink and glass -Can be repaired with ink only if it is lightly scratched -Shading – shading on glass is: -The tinted, horizontal band across the top -Used on some windshields -Made by darkening the plastic layer, not the glass -Glass Coding – codes printed on glass indicate that the glass has passed American national Standards (AS). These standards require that the windshield and front seat area allow at 70% visible light. The codes include: -Department of Transportation (DOT) number AS1 code on the windshield -AS2 code on the side glass and backglass -AS3 code for privacy glass -Note: On windshields, the label may be on the bottom middle of the glass -DOT Number – this number is the identifier for the glass part and is required on all documentation. If the replacement glass is defective for some reason, it is the DOT number that is referenced -AS1 Code – the AS1 code is only for the laminated windshield. Besides the 70% light transmittance requirement, AS1 has additional requirements for certain amounts of distortion and a specified penetration resistance. The code is below the top shading, with an arrow pointing down, indicating that the 70% light transmittance standard is met only below the shading. The code also helps glassmakers align the shading on the inner plastic laminate. -AS2 Code – the AS2 code is on side glass and the backglass. The AS2 code is used for both tempered and laminated glass with the exception of the windshield -AS3 Code – the AS3 code is for darkly tinted or privacy glass. AS3 is used for both tempered and laminated glass. If the glass does not meet the 70% light transmittance requirement, it is coded AS3 -Windshield Park Indicators – windshield park indicators are where the windshield wipers should be parked when in a resting position. The indicators are located near the bottom of windshields, and serve as guides for reinstalling the windshield wipers -Encapsulated Glass – encapsulated glass has a polyurethane plastic molding that is installed when the glass is made, and is permanently attached to the edge. Encapsulated glass is designed to help the automated installation of glass on the assembly line, as a robot can simply position the glass in place without a need to install a separate molding. Encapsulated glass can be any glass part, but it is mostly found on side glass and back glass -Encapsulated Glass Considerations – if it is difficult to determine whether the glass is encapsulated, try pulling the molding off when the glass is out of the vehicle. If the glass is encapsulated, the molding will not come off the glass when pulled. If it does come off, the molding is either bonded on or a separate molding. Some makers use a pre-applied adhesive system, which has a layer of urethane applied to the glass when the glass is made. When the replacement glass is installed, an activator is applied to the pre-applied urethane before adding another layer of urethane just before the installation -Trim and Moldings – exterior trim or moldings may surround stationary glass. The molding that surrounds the windshield may be T-shaped, sometimes called push-in moldings -Added Features and Integrated Accessories – there are features and accessories being added to glass every model year. Some accessories may be attached to or integrated with the glass. Examples of accessories related to stationary glass include: -A telecommunications system antenna -The radio antenna -The rain sensors -The defroster grids on the backglass and the windshield -Features in the rearview mirror -Forward-facing cameras -The head-up display. A head-up display reflects projected images, such as a speedometer, onto the windshield and requires a special type of glass -Ordering Glass – a stationary glass part can be ordered by calling a parts department at a dealership and asking for the glass part by the vehicle make, model, year, and VIN. Features of the glass, such as tint, shading, and special attachments, also need to be specified, as each feature gets a different number. There is also a standard code and numbering system for ordering replacement glass from glassmakers. The code and number for each type of glass are created by National Auto Glass Specifications (NAGS), and are copyrights of NAGS. The codes and numbers are listed in NAGS catalogs, calculators, and in the NAGS database GLASSMATE. Types of Stationary Glass Adhesive -Glass Installation Considerations – glass replacement is often a sublet operation that is done by professional glass installers. Following installation, the vehicle must sit stationary while the glass adhesive cures. The adhesive must reach full strength before the vehicle is allowed to leave the repair facility. This ensures the integrity of the upper structure in the event of a rollover, and may be used to deflect passenger side airbags during a deployment. Note: Cure times depend on temperature and humidity levels, but generally take several hours. This is important to note when informing the customer on when they can expect their vehicle repairs to be complete -Glass Urethane Adhesive – urethane adhesive specifically made for stationary glass installation helps maintain the structural integrity of the installed glass and vehicle, and has the consistency of tire rubber. When pulled laterally from lap shear, urethane adhesive has a strength of 500-1,200 psi, which is much stronger than other adhesives and sealants. Lap shear is one of the forces that a windshield is subjected to in a collision. Urethane adhesives may also have unique characteristics, such as a requirement to be non-conductive, as some glass adhesives contain carbon, which can be reactive with aluminum and cause galvanic corrosion -Moisture-Cure vs. Fast-Cure – urethane adhesives generally cure from the outside-in. after a stated minimum time, there will be an outer skin that holds the glass to the body, but there will still be a liquid center. If a cut is made through a bead of moisture-cure adhesive and fast-cure adhesive after a few hours, the fast-cure adhesive will show an outer skin with a liquid center, and the moisture-cure adhesive will show no outer skin beginning to form - Moisture-Cure on left. Fast-Cure on right -Minimum Driveaway Time – minimum driveaway time is the time from adhesive application until the product reaches full strength. There is a time specification that the glass industry has agreed on when a vehicle that has had stationary glass replacement can be released to the customer. Driveaway time for fast-cure products may be as little as 30-60 minutes. For conventional moisture-cured products, it may be 2-8 hours, or longer. The shortest driveaway time for moisture-cured products is based on 75 degrees Fahrenheit and 50% relative humidity. Lower temperature and/or humidity requires a longer time. The time will be specified on the performance data sheet that comes with the product. Note: Some vehicle makers have specific recommendations regarding stationary glass installation. Information regarding driveaway times and full cure times should be communicated to the vehicle owner -Foam Tape – open-cell foam tape should be replaced if it was used in the original installation. Foam tape serves primarily for sound deadening, as foam reduces wind noise, and vibration around the glass. If it is not replaced, the vehicle will has a different sound as it travels along the road, resulting in customer comeback. Foam also helps provide the look of a finished installation, as the clean line, is more aesthetic than an uneven bead of urethane when viewing it from inside. Foam tape is available in two sizes: 6mm and 7mm. several vehicle makers use foam tape, and provide recommendations for the proper size. Note: Foam-core butyl must be replaced with the same product. Do not substitute butyl tape for foam-core butyl -Types of Adhesive Failures – when an adhesive is put under stress, there are two ways it can fail. Adhesive failure is when the adhesive pulls loose from the surface it is bonded to. Cohesive failure is when the body of the adhesive pulls apart. A cohesive failure is often due to an adhesive being used after its expired shelf life. This can also cause adhesive failure. During testing, there must be cohesive failure, not adhesive failure -Adhesive is the black line in-between the glass -Causes of Adhesive Failures – when the adhesive pulls loose from the surface it was bonded to, there could be a number of reasons, including: -Contamination on one of the bonding surfaces -Corrosion on the pinch weld flange -Ambient conditions during installation, such as freezing temperatures -Material incompatibility. All products used should be from the same maker -Expired products Damage Analysis of Stationary Glass (2/2) Types of Damage -Stationary Glass Damage Analysis – -When checking stationary glass for damage, look for visible damage. This includes damage to: -Trim and moldings -The integrated radio antenna -The defroster grid. Damaged defroster grids may be repairable -The interior side of the glass caused by mass inertia -Cracks, pits, scratches, and leaks are also signs of damage to check for. Some types of minor damage are repairable, as long as the damage is not in the drivers line of sight. Note: Stationary glass will often require removal when repairing or replacing adjacent panels -Signs of Water Leaks – moisture at the interior seams may indicate hidden damage to the urethane adhesive. Repairing the leak may require removal and reinstallation of the glass. Corrosion on the interior surface of the pinch weld flange is another indication of a water leak -Delamination – delamination may appear as white streaks or spots and cannot be repaired. If it is determined that the delamination was preexisting, it should be communicated to the customer -Fire Damage – stationary glass that has been exposed to fire may require replacement. Excessive heat may weaken the urethane adhesive that holds the glass, as well as the inner plastic laminate. Delamination may cause large pieces of glass to come loose and injure the occupants during another collision. Cracks or warpages of the glass also may indicate that it has been exposed to excessive heat Stationary Glass Repair vs. Replace Considerations -Glass Repairability – the decision to repair or replace damaged glass will depend on the type of glass. Only some minor scratches can be repaired on tempered glass. Minor repairs are defined as repairs to scratches that cannot be felt with a fingernail. These may be able to be buffed out. Windshields are made of laminated glass. Repairs to laminated glass may include bullseyes, star beaks, and some small cracks. However, repairs are only possible if the damage is contained to the outer laminate or if the damage is not located in the drivers direct view -Glass Defects – defects in stationary glass, usually on the edge of laminated glass, include: -Chips in one of the outer laminates -Lamination separation, indicated by white streaks or spots -Burrs, or raised bumps -Distortion, due to uneven layers -Dirt of hair between the laminations -Gaps in bonded moldings -Note: This is a delamination problem within the glass and cannot be repaired -Laminated Glass Repair – key points regarding laminated glass repair include that repairs are only possible if the damage is contained to the outer layer of glass. Repairs cannot be made in the drivers direct view – this is generally the area covered by the driver side windshield wiper. This type of repair is possible because the visible damage is mostly air trapped between the outer layer of glass and the plastic laminate. The air can be drawn out and replaced with resin (making the damage nearly invisible) and the damage is still present after the repair, but not as visible without the trapped air Stationary Glass Installation and Calibration -Determining Calibrations – many vehicles include equipment for advanced driver assist systems (ADAS), such as cameras and radars that may need to be reset or calibrated if glass is replaced, or even just removed and reinstalled. Therefore, it is crucial to always refer to the makers service procedures and requirements when replacing stationary glass. Failure to properly reset or calibrate this equipment can cause the following ADAS systems to malfunction: -Lane Departure systems -Dynamic cruise control -Collision avoidance systems -Automatic high beam systems -Rain sensors -Document the Installation – stationary glass must be installed correctly. An installer, and the facility that contracted an installer, can be held liable if the glass is improperly installed and the vehicle occupant is injured. To prove that the installation was done correctly, document the installation -Documentation Considerations – when documenting an installation, include the: -Glass part that was replaced -Product makers procedures used -Products used, including the adhesive lot number, primers, cleaners, etc. list of expiration dates, if applicable -Vehicle owner information and vehicle identification number (VIN) -Time when the vehicle was released -Note: Also document the ambient conditions at the installation site, including the temperature and humidity, and whether the glass was replaced inside a facility or outside. List the driveaway time of the adhesive based on these conditions. Be sure to save the installation record. Installation record forms are available in brochures from adhesive makers Damage Analysis of Steering and Suspension Systems (1/2) Suspension System Damage Analysis Customer Consultation and Visual Inspection -Damage Analysis – damage analysis is achieved by consulting with the customer and conducting visual inspections. In many cases, damage will be obvious. The customer can provide clues to help the estimator focus on where to start the inspection, which may point to less obvious issues. Understanding how to interpret the different types of damage will aid in wiring a complete estimate. Minor damage might not be easily detected and will require additional testing to verify the cause. Some suspension and steering damage may be discovered by performing quick checks, precision measuring, and/or alignment checks -Customer Consultation Information – consult with the customer and gather information that includes a description of the collision, as well as known vehicle concerns. A collision description would include the speed and angle of the collision, whether the vehicle left the road surface, and any secondary collisions such as hitting a curb or large pothole. Other information, such as the size of the other vehicle and the road conditions during the collision, may also be included. Vehicle concerns would include any handling problems and any abnormal noises noticed before or after the collision. Information gathered should include the type of noise made, as well as the perceived location of the noise, and the conditions present when the noise occurs -Vehicle Pulls or Drifts – a customer may indicate the vehicle pulls or drifts after an accident. If the vehicle is drivable and the situation permits, a road test can be done to check for vibration, pulling, an off-center steering wheel, abnormal handling. Be sure to note if the problems occur, or are affected by, acceleration, coasting, turning, or braking. If the vehicle has a pull or drift to one side when driven straight ahead, this could indicate various problems. -Wheel Alignment – common damage causing wheel alignment issues include: -Bent, twisted, or broken steering or suspension parts -Damage to steering or suspension part mounting locations -Structural damage affecting part mounting location or alignment -Shift or movement at mounting locations causing parts to be out of position -Power Steering Problem – while not common, power steering problems can cause a pull or drift under acceleration. To verify if a pull is caused by a power steering problem, raise the front wheels off the ground pointing straight ahead, and increase the engine RPM. Watch for a toe change of the front tires – there should be none. Internal leaks in the power steering gear valve may send more pressure to one side of the gear and cause a pull. A restriction in a line on the steering gear may cause unbalanced pressure, resulting in a pull. Note: Vehicles with electric power steering may require scanning, sensor calibration, updated programming, or other service to correct pull/drift concerns -Abnormal Noises – abnormal noises can be classified into different categories. Types of noises include clunking or rattling, clicking, and rumbling. -Clunking or rattling noises may indicate loose fasteners or bushings were steering or suspension parts attach to the chassis. If a bushing is loose in a part, it can cause unwanted movement where it attaches to the vehicle. Other causes of clunks include loose ball sockets on tie rods and ball joints, and part interference during suspension deflector -Clicking noises may indicate interference problems or problems with parts that have a rotational movement -Rumbling or squealing sounds are typically caused by bearings or power steering problems -Note: Test equipment is available that utilizes clip-on microphones to allow listening to wheel bearings and other parts suspected of making noise -Visual Inspection Overview – a visual inspection includes: -Looking in the area of impact. Think about damage based on the angle and speed of the collision -Observing the positions of the wheels in relation to each other, the steering wheel, and the wheel openings -Checking for visible impact marks on the wheel and tire assemblies -Looking for ride height differences from side to side, or front to back -Inspecting for damage of the vehicle structure at any steering or suspension part mounting locations -Note: Look for symmetry in relation to how the vehicle sits and the position of the wheels and tires. If there are any unrelated or wear/service-related concerns, this should be documented and communicated to all parties involved. Inspect the vehicle with the estimating system at the vehicle. When inspecting/writing the estimate, inspect each part in the steering/suspension system to ensure nothing is overlooked -Quick Checks – steering and suspension system quick checks may only verify that a problem exists. Quick checks may not pinpoint the exact cause of a problem or condition, but may lead or point to more advanced testing in order to find which part is damage. Quick checks typically requires minimal or no tools and equipment. An example of a quick check that can be done with no tools is using your fingers to gauge the distance between a tire and wheel opening. Additionally, they may be done with minimal or no vehicle disassembly. Note: Use a bright flashlight, inspection mirror, and camera to inspect for and document damage Tire, Wheel, and Suspension Inspection -Tire Inspection – tires should be inspected for: -Blowouts, cuts, abrasion, or missing pieces -Bulges in the sidewall or tread area indicating separation of plies or belt damage -Flat spots or unusual wear patterns -Tire bead damage at the rim interference -Note: Custom tires and wheels may result in ride height and alignment measurements different from the original specifications. Many vehicles have a doorframe label that will identify the factory-recommended tires and wheels. Road force balance equipment can help identify tire issues that may not be visually detectable -Tire-Related Pull – to verify that a pull or drift is caused by a tire problem, switch the left and right front tires from side to side. If the pull is tire-related, the side that the vehicle pulls toward will also switch. Uneven tire air pressure from side to side will cause a vehicle to pull to the side with the lowest pressure. Verify that the air pressure in the tires is correct before switching sides. Note: Verify if the spare tire is on the vehicle, and the condition of the removed tire and wheel. -Tire Wear – tire wear that is uneven can be used as a diagnostic tool. Uneven tire wear will typically take time to show up and may indicate that the problem has existed for a period of time. Note that all-wheel drive/four-wheel drive vehicles may have specific tire diameter tolerance requirements due to differentials and/or viscous couplings that are designed to allow momentary differences in wheel speeds when the vehicle turns a corner or spins a tire. If the differentials or viscous couplings are forced to operate 100% of the time because of mismatched tires, they may experience unwanted wear and may eventually fail. Note: When inspecting tire wear, knowing which problems that specific tire wear patterns might indicate may be helpful in reducing damage analysis cycle time -Left is diagnol wear. Right is full shoulder wear. Bottom is feathered or sawtoothed wear -Alignment Problems – tire wear may indicate alignment problems if the wear is not consistent across the tread surface. For example, a tire with a feathered or sawtoothed wear pattern going across the tread in one direction may be the result of out-of-specification toe. When toe wear is present, it will likely be present on both tires on the same axle. This is because toe on one side will affect the wear on the other side. In another example, a tire with excessive inner tread wear pattern or outer tread wear pattern may be the result of out-of-specification camber. Camber wear may only be on one tire -Left is full shoulder wear. Right is feathered or sawtoothed wear -Shocks, Springs, or Balance – tire wear may indicate problems with struts/shocks or springs. Weak struts/shocks or springs, or out-of-balance tires, will typically cause a cupping wear pattern on a tire. Cupping may also be the result of bent suspension parts. Cupping is when the tire wears in patches or creates bald spots on the tread surface, usually around the edge of the tread on one side or the other side of the tire. -Inflation-Related Wear- a thin or worn tread wear pattern around the tire edges may be the result of under inflation. A thin or worn tread wear pattern around the center of the tire may be the result of over inflation. A patchy flat spot tread wear pattern around the tire may be the result of an out-of-balance tire and wheel -Wheel Inspection – wheels should be checked for: -Cosmetic damage. Repairable wheel damage is generally limited to minor scratches or nicks that can be sanded and refinished -Missing lug nuts and bent or broken studs -Bent wheel studs, which require removal of the lug nuts and wheel -Cracks -Broken bead edges, which may be found on aluminum wheels -Bent wheel centers, which can be found by doing a lateral runout check -Bent rims or bead edges, which can be identified with a radial runout check -Missing balance weights. An unbalanced tire may cause the vehicle to have a vibration when -Note: If tire and or wheel damage is evident, the hub and wheel bearings should be inspected for damage -Tire Pressure Monitors Direct – there are several repair considerations with direct tire pressure monitoring system. First, check for damaged antennas or wiring. Receiving antennas are located in areas susceptible to collision damage. Repair of the antenna wiring harness is not recommended. Another consideration is that electronic malfunctions will require a scan tool for diagnosis. Next, replacing damaged sensors. The valve stem core and cap have a special design and function as the sensor antenna. Replacing them with valve stem cores and caps from conventional valve stems causes radio signal interference and a system malfunction. Replace the sensor if the correct valve stem core or cap is not available. Lastly, do not use tire sealers. Tire sealers can clog the pressure sensor and cause a system malfunction. Note: A TPMS scan tool may be required to perform relearn and/or programming if the tire positions are changed on the vehicle and/or replacement sensors are installed -Tire Pressure Monitors Indirect – indirect tire pressure monitoring collision repair includes the following issues: -No new or added sensors or electronics are used in the systems -The ABS MIL will typically be lit if a system problem is detected -Scan tools are typically required for system diagnosis and to retrieve DTCs. DTC-based repair flowcharts are typically available in vehicle service manuals for repairing electronic system problems -Wheel Runout Quick Check – wheel runout may be difficult to detect with only the use of your eyes. However, with a minimum of tools, it can easily be checked. When inspecting wheels, look at all wheels. Do not forget the inboard side of the tires and wheels. -Ride Height Difference – inspect the vehicle for visible differences in ride height from side to side or front to rear. -Measuring Ride Height - ways of measuring ride height include measuring from the ground up to specified points on the chassis. It should be done with a level surface with the wheels pointed straight ahead. Or, it may require measuring between suspension parts and reference points on the chassis. This method may identify a weak spring or possible bent part as the cause of the ride height problem. Tire size and pressure, as well as vehicle load, can affect height. Note: Having a location that is pre-determined to be level, for making measurements that need to be done from a level surface, can help to reduce damage analysis cycle time -Causes of Ride Height Problems – causes of ride height problems include: -Structural damage -Damaged springs -A bent strut or shock absorber -Bent control arm(s) -Note: ride height specifications may be found in alignment equipment manuals or vehicle maker service information. Custom suspension parts and wheels/tires, as well as chassis modifications, may affect ride height. A damaged sway bar may cause a ride height issue. Sway bars are made of spring steel and are not easily damaged, but can be in some situations. Inspect for signs of bending or twisting of the sway bar, as well as damage to end links and mounting brackets. -Jounce and Rebound Check – a jounce and rebound check may identify a bent strut rod, shock absorber shaft, or suspension interference. Compress the suspension at each wheel and look, feel, and listen for travel restrictions or noises. Excessive bouncing on rebound indicates worn struts or shock absorbers. If toe changes when jouncing the vehicle in the front, this is called bumper steer; it is caused by steering linkage that is out of level to the suspension. Jounce both sides of the front at the same time by pushing down on the center of the front end. Excessive toe changes on jounce or rebound may indicate steering linkage damage or misalignment. -Strut and Shock Absorber Inspection – inspect struts and shocks for: -Damage to the housing, shaft, mounting points, or bushings -Oil leaks where the shaft goes into the housing and along the housing itself (minor seepage at the shaft seal is considered acceptable by most OEMs) -Note: Because they affect ride and handling, struts and shocks may nee to be replaced in pairs if a match to the undamaged side cannot be identified or obtained, or if the undamaged side is aged or worn. A worn and new part can react differently and create handling problems. Struts and shocks may have wires, connectors, lines, or hoses that should also be inspected for any damage -Spring Inspection – because they affect ride and handling, springs may need to be replaced in pairs if a match to the undamaged side cannot be identified, obtained, or if the undamaged side is aged or worn. A worn and new part can react differently and create handling problems. The visual inspection criteria for a spring depends on the type of spring being inspected. -Coil Spring – coil springs should be inspected for visible damage such as breaks, cracks, bends, or corrosion. Also, inspect for proper positioning of the spring in the spring seat -Leaf Spring – leaf springs should be inspected for bends, broken eyes, cracks, bushing damage, damage to the mounting brackets and shackles, and movement of the axle on spring. Most leaf springs are steel, some are composite; in both cases, replacement is the only solution if damaged -Torsion Bar – torsion bars should be inspected for damaged or broken adjusting cams, adjustment bolts, control arm engagement, mounting location, or any other visible damage such as bends, cracks, or corrosion -Air and Hydraulic Springs – inspect air and hydraulic springs for any visible damage or signs of leaks. Inspect lines, hoses, fittings, wiring, connectors, pumps, reservoirs (tanks), and valves for damage. Also check the height sensors for any damage and for proper positioning. Some vehicles may have air springs or hydraulics built into the strut assembly and/or electronic control of the suspension system requiring special procedures, tools, and equipment when servicing -Diagnosing a Weak Spring – use the following method to determine which end of a vehicle the bad spring is on: -Using the vehicle chassis, lift the tires on one end of the vehicle off the ground -Measure the ride height to the ground at the end of the vehicle with the tires on the ground. If the spring is located on the end with the tires on the ground, the sag of the vehicle on the side with the bad spring will be exaggerated. -Note: If the bad spring is on the end that is raised, the end on the ground will sit level side to side. Some auto level and electronically controlled suspension systems require disabling before lifting the vehicle to avoid system damage Toe, Caster, and Camber Inspection -Toe Overview – toe is the direction that the front of a tire points in relation to the vehicle centerline. Negative toe is when a tire is pointed away from the vehicle centerline. Positive toe is when a tire is pointed toward the vehicle centerline. A zero toe reading is when the tire is parallel to the vehicle centerline. Total toe references the left and right side tires to each other. Zero total toe is when the left and right tires are parallel. Toe-out would have the front of the tires pointed toward each other -Top is positive toe. Middle is negative toe. Bottom is zero toe. -Total Toe Quick Check – to perform a total toe quick check, raise and support the vehicle so that the tires can be marked in the middle of the tread. With the tires back on the ground, measure the distance between the marks at the front and rear of the tires. Make the measurement at spindle height in both locations. Toe should be measured with the vehicle at ride height. Having the tires on turntables will provide a more accurate measurement. A tram gauge may be required to measure around obstacles. If turntables are not used, roll the vehicle back and forth after lowering the tires back on the ground before measuring. -Total Toe Quick Check (continued) – a total toe quick check includes subtracting the front measurement from the rear measurement. This will give you the total toe as a distance measurement, with a positive number representing toe-in. it compares the measurement to a listed specification to identify a problem. Most vehicles will have a toe specification fairly close to zero with a plus or minus deviation that is acceptable. A total toe quick check only identifies that a toe problem exists, not which side it is on. -Individual Toe Quick Check – to determine which side of the vehicle has damage, the individual toe of each wheel needs to be referenced. One quick check for individual toe includes: -Centering the steering wheel -Inspecting both sides for visual toe-out or toe-in. the undamaged side will have the tire pointed straight ahead with the steering wheel centered -Note: Small differences may not show with a visual inspection and may only be discovered with an alignment check -Problems and Causes of Total Toe – ways to identify which part is damaged and causing the toe problem include: -Knowing the type of steering system -Identifying the damage condition that exists. The damage condition will point to certain parts and rule out others. Some parts can cause a total toe problem when bent and other parts only cause an off-center steering wheel. -Understanding that toe is affected by changes to other alignment angles. If a bent suspension part causes a change to either the SAI, camber, or caster, it will likely cause a toe change -Steering Arm Inspection – steering arms are usually part of the steering knuckle and sometimes the strut housing. They should be inspected for bends. A bent steering arm will typically cause toe to change. Signs of fresh corrosion are another indication that the part may have been bent -Tie Rod/Tie Rod End Inspection – to visually inspect tie rods and tie rod ends: -Compare the suspect part to a known good part -Look for bends that are not designed into the part. Turn the steering wheel and look for interference between the tie rods and other part -Look for deformed or torn grease boots at the ball socket -Inspect for loose ball sockets on tie rod ends along with vertical free play -Steering Arm Quick Check – a quick check to measure a steering arm for damage includes: -Measuring to the center of the tie rod end and to the steering arm using a straightedge placed on the hub or hub surface of the steering knuckle, or to other symmetrical reference points on the steering knuckle; this can help identify steering arm damage and tie rod end damage -Comparing the measurement to the same measurement on the opposite side or that of an undamaged part -Setback and Caster – this section goes over what setback and caster are -What is a Setback? – setback is when a wheel is moved back in the chassis compared to the same wheel on the opposite side of the vehicle. This causes the wheelbase on the side with setback to be shorter than the wheelbase on the opposite side. Setback of a front wheel will typically cause a change in caster for that wheel. The only exception to this would be if both the upper and lower pivot points are pushed back an equal amount. This would cause wheel setback, but no caster change. This is more likely on solid axle front suspension. -What is Caster? – caster is the angle between true vertical and the steering axis when viewed from the side of the vehicle. It is changed whenever the upper or lower pivot points are moved forward or rearward. Moving the lower pivot point results in setback and a caster change. Moving just the upper pivot point changes caster, and usually only has a minor effect on setback. Caster is not an angle that typically causes tire wear. An excessively out-of-specification caster angle will only cause tire wear issues during cornering. Note: If caster is low on one side, it may cause the vehicle to pull to that side -Checking for Setback/Caster – the ways of checking for setback of a front wheel include: -Measuring between the lower ball joint and a symmetrical reference point rearward on the same side of the chassis. Compare to the same measurement on the opposite side of the vehicle -Using a three-dimensional measuring system to compare the location of the left- and right-side lower ball joint centers -A wheel alignment check. Most computerized alignment machines will indicate if there is setback of a wheel -Note: Setback can also be identified by visual inspection, or by inserting your fingers between the tire and wheel opening and comparing to the opposite side -Problems and Causes of Setback and Caster – these charts list the possible causes of setback and caster problems for each suspension type. When inspecting control arms for movement or damage, inspect bushings, ball joints, and attachment points for possible damage Problems and causes of setback -MacPherson Strut and SLA-Style Suspensions – -Structural damage that moves lower control arm mounting points -Bent lower control arm -Shifted suspension cradle -Bent lower ball joint -Solid Axle Suspension – -Structural damage that moves mounting points for axle housing -Bent axle housing -Bent leaf spring -Bent trailing arm -Twin I-Beam Suspension – -Structural damage that moves mounting points for I-Beam -Bent I-Beam -Bent radius rod Problems and causes of caster -MacPherson Strut Suspension – -Setback of front wheel -Structural damage that moves shock tower forward or rearward -SLA-Style Suspension – -Setback of front wheel -Structural damage that moves upper control arm mounting points -Bent upper control arm -Solid Axle Suspension – -Twisting of axle housing that changes upper and lower pivot point relationship -Twin I-Beam Suspension – -Twisting of I-beam that changes upper and lower pivot point relationship -Camber – camber is defined as the inward or outward tilt of the tire at the top compared to true vertical when viewed from the front of the vehicle. Camber is the angle between a line drawn through the center of the tire and true vertical. Camber specifications are given in degrees. It is positive when the tire is leaned out at the top and negative when the tire is leaned in at the top. If camber is out of specification, the vehicle may pull to the side with the most positive camber. Camber is one of the main tire wear angles. Excessive positive camber will wear the outside edge of the tire and excessive negative camber will wear the inside edge of the tire -Steering Axis Inclination (SAI) – steering axis inclination (SAI) is the angle between the steering axis and true vertical when viewed from the front of the vertical. SAI may also be called kingpin inclination. Changing the SAI angle on a vehicle will also change the camber angle of the tire. It can be used as a diagnostic tool when used in conjunction with other alignment angles. SAI may also be affected by structural issues and/or repairs. -How SAI is Measured – SAI can be measured by measuring the structure of the vehicle and comparing the measurement to specifications. Width measurements of the upper and lower pivot points can be made; these may be comparative measurements to symmetrical points on the structure or reference vehicle centerline. If the SAI is correct and there is a camber problem, there is a bent suspension part. SAI can also be measured by a wheel alignment check. Most computerized alignment equipment is capable of measuring the SAI of the front suspension. -Included Angle – included angle is the sum of the camber and SAI angle. If camber is positive, included angle will be larger than SAI, but when camber is negative, included angle will be less than SAI. It is mainly a diagnostic angle used to help identify if parts are bent between the upper and lower suspension pivot points. To identify bent parts, use a straight edge or square and tape measure to make comparative measurements to the opposite side of the vehicle for points on the knuckle, strut housing, and ball joint -Camber out of Specification – if a tire appears to be tipped inward or outward at the top it is considered a camber change. This chart lists the possible causes of a camber change for each suspension type. Problems and causes of Camber -MacPherson Strut Suspension – -Structural damage that misaligns strut tower or lower control arm mounting points -Shifted suspension cradle -Bent strut -Bent lower control arm -Bent steering knuckle -Bent ball joint -Loose wheel bearings -SLA-Style Suspension – -Structural damage that misaligns upper or lower control arm mounting points -Shifted suspension cradle -Bent strut -Bent upper or lower control arm -Bent steering knuckle or spindle -Bent ball joint -Loose wheel bearings -Solid Axle Suspension – -Bent axle housing -Bent axle shaft (rear) -Bent steering knuckle or spindle -Bent ball joint -Twin I-Beam Suspension – -Bent I-Beam -Bent steering knuckle or spindle -Bent ball joint -Camber Quick Check – camber problems can be verified using a camber quick-check gauge. There are a variety of different styles of these gauges available. The gauge may mount to the wheel rim or to the face of the hub. These gauges can be used to verify camber problems when visual methods are not conclusive. Damaged sheet metal can create an optical illusion that the camber of a wheel is incorrect when it is within specification. Measure the camber with the vehicle on a level surface after rolling the vehicle back and forth on the tires. Be careful to not scratch the wheel when attaching the quick-check gauge, especially with aluminum wheels. Note: Smart phone camera level apps ay be helpful to identify camber issues, but should not be relied upon for final verification Wheel Tracking -Thrust Angle – the thrust line is the direction that the rear wheels track. It is the imaginary line that bisects the total rear toe angle of the vehicle. A thrust line pointing to the right will cause a vehicle to pull to the left. This is because the rear of the vehicle will walk to the right, which will cause the front wheels to be pointed to the left. To keep the vehicle going straight, the steering wheel will have to be turned so that the front tires are parallel to the rear tires. Visually inspect the vehicle while following it as it is driven straight ahead. If the rear axle has an excessive thrust angle, one of the front tires will be more visible than the other, or one rear tire will appear closer to the wheel opening lip on the body. Note: Ideally, the thrust line coincides with the centerline of the vehicle -Checking Wheel Tracking – drive the vehicle through water and across a level surface while driving straight ahead. The position of the front tire tracks in relation to the rear tire tracks will indicate a tracking problem. This method will typically not indicate whether it is an axle offset or thrust angle problem. Using something that is saturated with water, such as towels, for the vehicle to drive over to wet the tires may be one way to control excessive spreading of the water and potentially minimize cleanup time. This may help to reduce the damage analysis cycle time -Thrust Angle Quick Check – a thrust angle quick check is done with a length of string and a tape measure -1) Attach the string to a point on the chassis behind the rear tires -2) Stretch the string around the side of the tire at axle height and move it until it contacts the front of the tire -3) Measure from the string inward to two different points on the chassis -4) Repeat steps 1-3 for the opposite side of the vehicle -5) Compare the measurements. Differences in the side-to-side measurements at the same location on the chassis may indicate a tracking problem. If the difference is the same at both measured chassis locations, the tracking problem is caused by axle offset. If the difference in measurements is not the same at both chassis locations, there is a thrust angle problem -Note: This technique can be used on full frame or unitized structure vehicles to help identify toe or tracking problems -Alignment Check – a four-wheel alignment check on the vehicle can help identify issues with the rear suspension. Examining the rear toe angles, thrust angle, and camber measurements can help identify damage conditions. If toe and camber are within specification on one side, but not the other, the issue is likely isolated to one side. If the total toe is within spec, but individual toe angles are out of specification in opposite directions, it may indicate an axle positioning issue or diamond on a full frame vehicle. Close inspection and measurements of the suspension parts, their mounting locations, and the structure of the vehicle should be done. Note: Thrust angle should be near zero, however, vehicles typically gave an allowable tolerance -Causes of Wheel Tracking Problems – improper tracking of the rear wheels may be caused by one of the following issues: -An excessive rear thrust angle – thrust angle problems are the result of rear toe problems and cause an off-center steering wheel when the vehicle is driven straight ahead -Offset of the rear axle – although it will create a tracking problem, axle offset by itself will not cause a dynamically off-center steering wheel as long as the individual rear toe is within specification -Damage or movement of the structure or subframe -Bent suspension parts -Note: Vehicles with rear steering will have additional related parts to inspect and may require special tools and equipment when servicing or aligning the suspension -Left is excessive rear thrust. Orange arrow between orange line and center red line is the thrust line. Right is offset of the rear axle. Thrust line is the orange line and center red line -Thrust Angle Causes – there are two main causes of an excessive thrust angle. -Left image, LT/Rear wheel has positive toe. Orange line is thrust angle. -Rear Toe – one cause of excessive thrust angle is rear toe that is out of specification. If the individual toe of either rear wheel is out of specification, it will typically cause the vehicle to have an excessive thrust angle. An excessive thrust angle will cause a vehicle to pull opposite of the direction that the rear wheels are pointing, and result in an off-center steering wheel to keep the vehicle driving straight ahead -Solid Axle – another cause of excessive thrust angle is a solid axle that is positioned crooked in the vehicle. Causes of a crooked axle include a diamond condition in a full frame, out-of-position mounting points for the axle, or a shifting of the axle on the leaf spring pads. On vehicles with a leaf spring, the mounting point for the front spring eye is what controls axle position. The length position of the rear spring shackles usually has no effect on thrust angle; however, width positioning may. If total toe and or camber is out of specification on a solid axle, it is likely the axle housing is bent and will require replacement. Note that wheel run out may be the result of a bent axle or hub flange. -Axle Offset – axle offset is when the wheel on one side of an axle is positioned closer to the vehicle centerline than the wheel on the opposite side of the axle. It does not affect thrust angle by itself. If the axle is shifted sideways, but there is no individual toe change of the rear wheels, the front and rear wheels will not track correctly to each other, but the vehicle will not pull and the steering wheel will not be off center when driving straight ahead. This is typically caused by a bent track bar (panhard bar), mounting bracket, or other axle positioning part/structural damage Alignment Angles -Wheel Alignment Check – a wheel alignment check can be used as an aid in diagnosing damage to steering and suspension system parts. If used, a wheel alignment check: -Should reference all four wheels, the steering wheel, and all alignment angles -Compares the alignment angles to vehicle maker specifications -Does not include making adjustments to any of the measured angles or replacing parts -Note: Conditions affecting wheel alignment angles may also affect the steering angle sensor and other systems which may require calibration before returning the vehicle to service -Conditions Affecting Wheel Alignment – conditions that affect wheel alignment angles include: -A direct impact to either a tire or wheel -Side collisions that result in a change of wheel position -Vehicle rollovers -Damage to any steering or suspension part or mounting location -Replacement of any steering or suspension part -Movement of the vehicle structure that affects steering or suspension part mounting location -Damage to the structure that affects the steering or suspension -Note: Vehicles that have left the road surface may have steering or suspension damage that is not immediately apparent with a visual inspection. Collision energy transfer through the vehicle can damage suspension parts that are not in the immediate impact zone -Using Alignment Angles – to determine which steering or suspension part is damaged, alignment angle readings can be used to help narrow down the list of possibilities. Look at the SAI, camber, and included angle measurements. Understand the relationship between each of these and the different parts of the suspension system. It can be determined whether or not the damaged part is between the upper and lower pivot points of the suspension. It mush be understood that the type of suspension system will have a bearing on which alignment angles are affected by part damage. Note: Toe angles and thrust angle can help identify damage conditions that affect the direction the tires point. -Suspension Troubleshooting Charts – these diagnostic charts can help to determine which suspension parts may be damaged using SAI, camber, and included angle readings on an alignment report for a specific suspension system -Strut-Style – this diagnostic chart can help to determine which suspension parts may be damaged using SAI, camber, and included angle readings on an alignment report for a strut-style suspension system. For example: if on the alignment report SAI is correct, and camber and included angle are both above specification, then it is most likely a bent knuckle/spindle and/or strut -SLA-Style – this diagnostic chart can help to determine which suspension parts may be damaged using SAI, camber, and included angle readings on an alignment report for an SLA-style suspension system. For example, if on the alignment report SAI is correct, and camber and included angle are both above specification, then it is most likely a bent knuckle/spindle -Solid Axle – this diagnostic chart can help to determine which suspension parts may be damage using SAI, camber, and included angle readings on an alignment report for a front solid axle suspension system. For example: if on the alignment report SAI is correct, and camber and included angle are both above specification, then it is most likely a bent knuckle/spindle. Some solid axle designs will have adjustments for camber that may also change SAI. If the adjustment is at the pivot point, SAI and camber will change together. Caster may also be affected. -Alignment Numbers – a complete alignment report should include caster, camber, toe, SAI, and included angle for the front wheels and toe, thrust angle, and camber for rear wheels. Analyzing all of the numbers together can help identify damage. For example: on a strut-style suspension, if the right front toe is out of specification and the caster reading is low, it is likely the lower control arm is pushed back and/or the mounting area is damaged. The tie rod end and/or steering arm may also be damaged. SAI, camber, and included angle can help identify other possible damage conditions as well. Rear toe, thrust angle, and camber may indicate a problem isolated to one wheel, axle damage, or a position issue. Note: Caster is not typically referenced on the rear suspension Damage Analysis of Steering and Suspension Systems (2/2) Steering System Damage Analysis Steering -Abnormal Steering Effort – abnormal steering effort can include excessive steering effort at any position, or uneven steering effort. If excessive effort is required in all positions, the power steering system should be examined. The effort required to turn the steering wheel should be consistent throughout the full range of steering. Steering effort that increases and decreases as the wheel is turned may indicate a problem in the steering system. Uneven steering effort may be caused by internal steering gear damage such as a bent rack, broken teeth inside the steering gear, or interference between steering parts and other parts of the vehicle. -Steering Wander – causes of steering wander include certain types of wheel alignment problems. Front tires that are not pointing the same direction, or toe misalignment, may cause the vehicle to “hunt” back and forth and follow irregularities in the road surface. Causes may include loose attachment of parts of the steering system. Check for excessive free play in the tie rods and tie rod ends. Steering wander may be caused by loose wheel bearings, unevenly worn tires, or excessive play in the steering gear. Insufficient caster can also cause a vehicle to be unstable at speed and tend to follow road surface irregularities. Note: If caster is low on only one wheel, the vehicle may pull or drift to that side. -Steering Wheel Not Centered – a steering wheel that is not centered statically means that the steering wheel will be off center when the wheels are pointed straight ahead and the vehicle is not moving. A steering wheel that is not centered dynamically would be off center when the vehicle is in motion, being driven straight ahead. Note: If the steering wheel is centered statically, but has to be turned to keep the vehicle straight when driven, inspect the rear toe/thrust angles -Centering the Steering Gear – to center the steering gear: -1) Turn the steering wheel one way until the steering hits the stop -2) Turn the steering wheel back the other way until the steering wheel hits the opposite stop -3) Count the revolutions the steering wheel turned from stop to stop -4) Divide this number by 2 and turn the steering wheel back that many revolutions -Note: After centering the steering gear, the steering wheel should be centered. If the tires are not pointed straight ahead or the steering wheel is not centered, some part of the steering linkage is bent or out of position. If the steering wheel is turned substantially on a vehicle with hydraulic power steering and the pump is not running, it may cause the reservoir to overflow. -Rack Inspection – rack and pinion steering gears should be inspected for: -Noise and binding when the steering wheel is turned – this may indicate a bent or broken rack -Damaged lines, hoses, wires, connectors, sensors, or torn bellows boots -Damage to the steering gear housing, mounting locations, or bushings -Fluid leakage from power steering system parts while the pump is operating -Note: Bellows boots and other external parts of the gear may be serviced separately – damage to the rack and pinion unit generally requires replacement of the assembly -Internal Seal Damage – on power rack and pinion systems, internal seal damage in the rack causes a condition where the steering is stiff when the vehicle is first started, but functions properly after the power steering fluid reaches operating temperatures. Rack and pinion assembly replacement is the repair for this problem -Parallelogram Steering System Inspection – when inspecting a parallelogram steering system, inspect the gear box and mounting locations for damage as well as the center link, tie rod ends, pitman arm, sector shaft, and idler arm. Inspect the ball sockets and attachment points for looseness. Inspect dust boots for improper positioning. The steering linkage should be level in the chassis. Out-of-level steering linkage may be caused by damaged vehicle structure, as well as bent, and/or out of position parts. This may induce bump steer, which causes the vehicle to dart back and forth as it goes over bumps and dips, due to uneven toe changes -Inspecting a Sector Shaft – the sector shaft should be inspected for twisting where the pitman arm attaches to the shaft. A twisted sector shaft can cause off-center steering wheel with no toe change. The splines should be straight if the shaft is undamaged. A twisted sector shaft may be cracked and may completely break apart at a later time, resulting in a loss of steering. The pitman arm should have no movement where it attaches to the sector shaft -Off-Center Steering Problem – an off-center steering problem may be caused by an out-of-position rack and pinion steering gear. If the steering wheel is centered and the front wheels are straight when sitting still, but when drive, the steering wheel has to be held off center to keep the vehicle going straight, this is an indication of a rear wheel toe/thrust angle problem. The front wheels are responsible for steering the vehicle, but the rear wheels are responsible for proper vehicle tracking. Note: A diamond condition on a full frame vehicle may be the cause of a dynamically off-center steering wheel. -Problems and Causes of Off-Center Steering (Static) – this chart shows the types of damage that can cause a statically off-center steering wheel in parallelogram and rack and pinion steering systems. Problems and Causes of Off-Center Steering (Static) -Rack and Pinion Steering – -Shifted suspension cradle -Out-of-position rack and pinion -Parallelogram Steering – -Twisted sector shaft -Bent idler arm -Bent pitman arm -Out-of-position steering gear -Steering Wheel Inspection – check for a bent steering wheel. A bent steering wheel will wobble when it is turned. Some steering wheels are asymmetrical by design. Listen for abnormal noises when turning the steering wheel.
