Inspect all switches, wires, connectors, and the airbag for damage. If the steering wheel is damaged, replacement is typically required. Note: Steering column or shaft replacement may be mandatory following an airbag deployment. Check OEM documentation for required parts when an airbag has deployed. -Steering Column Inspection – inspect steering columns for damage to the column, mounting locations, shrouds, attached levers, and controls. Steering columns are designed to collapse during an accident that drives the steering gear rearward or upward. -Left arrow is impact force. Right arrow is collapse -Steering Column Considerations – measuring steering column length usually requires removing the column from the vehicle. Some vehicles have a pyrotechnic charge that causes them to collapse under certain conditions. Deployed pyrotechnic columns will set a diagnostic trouble code (DTC). Column brackets may include breakaway mounts that allow the column to move forward when impacted on the steering wheel. Inspect that the tilt mechanism functions properly. They should not be jammed or loose. Individual service parts may be available. Note that some columns are only to be serviced as an assembly. -Shaft and Coupling Inspection – steering shafts and couplings are designed to connect the steering wheel to the steering gear. Steering shaft designs typically include telescoping sections that may collapse. Some designs incorporate accordion sections that may deform in a collision. Universal joints allow parts to rotate at angles and allow the shaft sections to fold if the steering gear is pushed rearward. Inspect for damaged couplings or U-joints, steering shaft collapse, or bends/runout. If the injected plastic on the steering shaft has sheared, a new steering shaft must be installed. Damaged steering system parts should be replaced. Note: Some fasteners in the steering and suspension may be one-time use and require replacement. Consult OEM service information for specific applications -Power Steering Inspection – inspect power steering systems for leaks, damaged hoses and lines, and damaged pulleys. Check for leaks at any part of the system. To inspect a power steering system for leaks, the power steering pump must be operating. This involves running the engine and turning the steering wheel back and forth. Look for cuts to hoses and lines, and crushed areas on hard lines. Hoses should be inspected to ensure that there is no evidence of the hose swelling. Pulleys may be metal and can be bent, or may be made of plastic and can be broken. Note: Some vehicles may be equipped with variable assist and/or variable ratio systems that may include additional parts in the steering system to inspect. -Hydraulic System – additional parts of the hydraulic system that should be inspected for damage include the power steering pump. Look for physical damage and listen for abnormal noises during pump operation. Inspect the power steering fluid reservoir. Reservoirs may be attached to the pump or may be remotely located. They may be made of metal or plastic. Inspect the reservoir for physical damage or any signs of fluid leakage. Ensure that the fluid level is within the operating range. Also, inspect belts on hydraulic systems that are engine-driven. Inspect belts for fraying, missing pieces, cracks, and proper alignment. Note: If the incorrect fluid is used, leaks or permanent damage may result to hydraulic power steering systems -Electro Hydraulic – electro hydraulic power steering utilizes an electric motor-driven hydraulic pump and control module. Inspect the control module, motor, pump, lines, hoses, wires, connectors, and sensors for damage. Check the dash for a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL). Retrieval of DTC’s typically requires a scan tool. If the pump and or control module are damaged, the replacement will likely require programming. Note: If battery voltage is not sufficient or there is a charging system issue, electro hydraulic and electric steering systems may begin to malfunction -Electric – electric power steering may have the assist motor attached to the steering column or the rack. Inspect all wiring, connectors, control modules, sensors, and the motor for any signs of damage. Follow specific service procedures for repairs. Damaged parts are typically replaced. DTCs can be read with a scan tool. Part replacement may require specific programming operations and calibrations. Note: Electric power steering systems typically operate at high current levels and may be 12 volt, however many operate at higher voltages Damage Analysis of Unitized Structures – Front (1/2) Front Body Inspection Damage Analysis Considerations -Damage Analysis Process – a thorough and accurate damage analysis process for exterior body panels: -Must be established to ensure a complete and safe repair -Will help reduce supplements, which may also affect the timeliness of the repair -Requires a good understanding and knowledge of repair and replacement options for parts of the vehicle exterior -May also require making note of any suspected structural damage. This will indicate that further inspection is required before beginning repairs, to help ensure there are no delays in the repair process -May help develop a repair plan based on the identified damage. The earlier that damage is identified, the more accurate the repair plan -Damage Analysis Precautions – disabling of the supplemental restraints system (SRS) may be required when working on or near parts of the SRS. This is commonly done by removing and isolating the negative battery cable. Refer to vehicle-specific service information to determine how to disable the SRS. It may be necessary to clean up broken glass from the vehicle interior before beginning the inspection process. Protective gloves should be worn when inspecting areas where there are sharp edges. It is also important to prevent further damage to the vehicle. Do not try to force open or close closure panels that are not working properly. Avoid unnecessary force when removing parts during inspection, and store removed parts where they will not be damaged or lost -Repair vs. Replace Considerations – damaged exterior panels will either require repair or replacement -Considerations – considerations regarding repair or replacement of damaged exterior panels include the type of material. Common materials that exterior panels are made of include steel, aluminum, plastic, and composites, such as fiber-reinforced plastics. Identifying the material that a part is made of may determine which repair method is used or if the part will require replacement. Removal of adjacent parts, even though they are not damaged is another consideration -Additional Considerations – additional repair versus replace considerations include the extent and location of damage. Damage located on contour lines and edges may not be able to be returned to the proper state and shape, cracks may develop due to work hardening. Minor damage to mild steel exterior panels is often repairable; however, vehicle maker service information should be referenced to determine repair limitations. Another consideration is how replacement parts are supplied. Some parts are only available as part of an assembly -Panel Straightening Considerations – repairing damaged exterior panels often involves straightening -Vehicle Maker Recommendations – considerations regarding straightening of exterior panels include vehicle maker recommendations. This includes whether straightening is allowed, or whether heat may be applied. -Types of Material – the type of material used for the panel is another consideration. High-strength steel (HSS) and aluminum panels are more prone to cracking than parts made from mild steel. Determining whether or not welding may be used to repair cracked exterior panels may depend on vehicle maker recommendations. Magnesium is typically not used for exterior panels, however it may be used in other body part construction and is limited to cold straightening of minor deformation that does not include cracks or tears. Deformation in some plastics may be reshaped using heat. Gouges, cracks, holes, or tears may be repairable using approved materials and techniques. Always reference vehicle maker recommendations on the type and location of damage to be repaired -Durability of the Repair – even though a part may not be considered structural, durability of the repair may be a concern if damage is repaired in an area subject to repeated stress, such as road vibrations. After time, the repair area may fail -Passenger Protection – additional panel straightening considerations include passenger protection. Some parts are designed to collapse in a specific manner. Straightening or welding a bend or kink in a collapse zone may affect the safety of passengers in a subsequent collision. -Paintless Dent Repair (PDR) – another consideration is paintless dent repair (PDR). PDR is a technique used to fix minor dents on exterior panels without the need to refinish. This repair method is done using tools designed to access the backside of panels from various angles and work the dent outward to the original shape. Additionally, glue-on pulling tabs may be attached to the outer panel to remove some dents. A repair facility may be able to provide this type of repair, or it can be sublet -Business Decision – if the vehicle maker does not provide repair procedures or recommendations, determining whether a part is repaired or replaced is a subjective business decision. The decision to repair or replace a part must be communicated and agreed upon by the repair facility, insurance company, and vehicle owner. The damaged panel must be straightened as close as possible to the original shape. The integrity and durability of the part must be similar to the original part. The use of body filler must not exceed the vehicle recommendations. If none exist, follow product maker recommendations Emblems, Moldings, and Pinstriping -Emblems, Moldings, and Pinstriping – many parts of the vehicle exterior have various types of trim and moldings attached. Trim and moldings attached to the vehicle exterior may include, but are not limited to: -Appliques -Weatherstripping -Belt-line moldings -Pinstriping -Emblems -Bodyside molding -Cladding -Graphics -Attachment Methods – -Common methods for attaching trim, moldings, and emblems include: -Adhesive -Two-sided tape -Transfer adhesive -Mechanical fasteners such as plastic clips. Fasteners are sometimes used in conjunction with other attachments, such as adhesive or tape, for proper alignment. Many plastic fasteners are difficult to remove without damage -Note: The attachment method may be included with the replacement part. Plastic clips may be provided with replacement trim and moldings. Emblems may have pre-applied adhesive attached adhesive attached -Other Considerations – other considerations for replacement or removal and installation (R&amp;I) of trim and moldings include: -Refinishing moldings. Trim and moldings ordered by color may require refinishing -Verifying that all clips and fasteners that do not come with the part are included on the damage report -Removing adjacent parts for access -Pinstriping and Graphics – some vehicles have pinstriping or graphics applied to the exterior body panels. These may be applied. These may be applied by the vehicle maker, dealer, or aftermarket -Replacement Considerations – pinstriping and graphics require replacement if damaged or if the panel is repaired or replaced. Pinstriping and graphics may also be painted on or applied as a decal. Replacement must match adjacent panels. The pinstripe on the entire side of the vehicle may require replacement is this is not possible. For example, if the replacement pinstripe for one panel does not match the original application, it may be necessary to replace the entire pinstripe along that side of the vehicle -Additional Considerations – decals may only be available as a kit for one or both sides of the vehicle. Another aftermarket option for vehicles is vehicle wraps. Vehicle wraps are common on fleet vehicles. It will most likely be a sublet operation, but it is necessary to have it removed and replaced. A customer consultation may help identify where to purchase replacement decals, or the business that did the custom graphic work Front Bumper -Bumper Assembly Parts Overview – the primary parts of the front bumper assembly include the: -Bumper cover -Energy absorber -Bumper reinforcement -Air deflectors that direct air through the grille opening -Closeout panels that divert airflow, reducing drag -Attachment brackets and fasteners that secure the bumper assembly to the vehicle structure -Bumper Cover Inspection – scratches, tears, or cracks are just some of the things to look for when inspecting bumper covers -Initial Inspection – when inspecting bumper coves, look for damage to other parts that may be hidden behind the bumper cover. Damaged bumper covers may return to the original shape immediately following the impact. For this reason, it is important to consider parts located behind the bumper cover that may be damaged, such as the energy absorber and bumper reinforcement. Never assume that a hidden part is damaged or undamaged. Visual damage to the bumper cover may indicate more severe damage behind the cover. Disassembly may be required to identify all damage -Additional Inspection – additional front bumper cover inspection points include: Deformation. This may be difficult to see and typically requires looking at all areas of the bumper cover from various angles to properly identify signs of deformation. Damaged mounting tabs or fasteners. This may be apparent if the bumper cover shows signs of misaligned gaps or obvious detachment -Bumper Cover Replace vs Repair Considerations – replace versus repair decisions for damaged bumper covers may be determined by the extent and location of the damage -Bumper Cover Replacement Considerations – bumper covers are often supplied according to various options and equipment. These may include, but are not limited to: -The trim package or model of the vehicle. Different packages may have additional molding or are a different style -Fog lamps -Parking sensors for a parking-assist system. Parking-assist sensors are often reused if undamaged -Headlamp washers -Lower spoilers -Additional Replacement Considerations – other factors that may have to be considered when ordering bumper covers include: -A radar sensor -The engine size -The color -The vehicle year -Tow hooks -Note: The vehicle identification number (VIN) may not identify all options on the vehicle. This is why it is important to be able to identify the various options the vehicle is equipped with. Repair options may be limited in locations where a sensor is present. For example, application of plastic repair adhesives on a front bumper cover may adversely affect the function of a sensor located behind the damaged area -Bumper Cover Repair Considerations – considerations for bumper cover repair: -Include reshaping deformed areas with heat -Include options for repairing cracks or tears with adhesive or plastic welding repair methods -Include options for repairing mounting tabs. Mounting tabs on a plastic cover may be more difficult to repair than a small tear in the center of the cover. For this reason, the number of damaged mounting tabs may reduce the feasibility of repair -Additional Repair Considerations – additional repair considerations for bumper cover repair may: -Depend on the location of damage. For example, damage located on contour lines, or formed areas, may not be repairable. The extent of damage to the bumper cover may make repair a less feasible option -Depend on previous repairs. For example, the finish buildup from previous repairs may require removal, which would make repair a less feasible option -Bumper Reinforcement – the energy absorber and bumper reinforcement are parts of the front bumper assembly -Bumper Energy Absorbers – bumper energy absorbers: -Are designed to absorb minor collision energy, thus reducing physical damage to the vehicle structure. Most energy absorbers absorb collision energy by crushing or collapsing. -May be made of a high-density foam -May be in the form of molded plastic, usually a square cell structure. When inspecting energy absorbers in the form of molded plastic, inspect the cell structure for crushing or other deformation -Are typically designed for one-time use and must be replaced if damaged -Bumper Reinforcement – the bumper reinforcement: -May be called the impact bar -Distributes collision energy and may affect timing of the SRS -Is typically made from very strong materials such as UHSS or aluminum -Is located behind the energy absorber and attaches to both front lower rail ends or crush boxes -Crush Boxes – crush boxes: -May be separate parts that are attached with bolts, or are designed into the bumper reinforcement and replaced as an assemble -Are designed to absorb crash energy. The crush boxes are one of the first parts of the vehicle to collapse during a front impact collision -May prevent structural damage to the front lower rails if the collision was not too severe -May also be called crush caps or crash boxes -Bumper Reinforcement Inspection – when inspecting bumper reinforcements: -Look for cracks or deformation -Inspect the attachment points. Inspect the mounting points for tears or deformation -Inspect any crush boxes that are designed into the part. Some bumper reinforcements are supplied with built-in crush boxes -Grille Assembly – the front grille assembly may attach to the front bumper assembly or hood -Grille Assembly – besides the grille itself, parts of the grille assembly may include, but are not limited to: -A cover -Moldings -An emblem -Brackets and fasteners -Electric vehicle charging ports -Note: Parts of electronic systems, such as adaptive cruise control sensors or cameras, may be located on or behind the grille. These types of electronic devices may have the appearance of a small camera lens or electronic sensors with attached wires. A flashlight may be beneficial when looking for devices located behind the grille -Grille Repair and Replace – parts of the grille may be available separately, or be part of an assembly. Plastic repair techniques are limited on these parts because it is difficult to make an invisible repair, such as matching the texture of the part. The feasibility of repair is also a determining factor. Mounting tap repair may be an option, depending on the type of plastic and extent of damage Front Lighting -Front Lighting – projector-type headlamps, which create a focused beam of light, typically used for the low-beam headlamps, is just one example of front exterior lighting -Types of Headlamps – various types of headlamps include -Composite headlamps that use a replaceable halogen bulb -High-intensity discharge (HID), or xenon, headlamps. HID headlamps use an electronic control unit (ECU) or HID ballast unit that changes DC voltage to AC voltage -Light-emitting diode (LED) headlamps. LED bulbs cannot be replaced separately. Damaged LED headlamps will require replacement as a complete assembly -Sealed beam headlamps, which have separate bulbs for low and high beams, or one bulb with two filaments, for the low and high beams. This type of headlamp assembly must be completely sealed in order to light and not commonly used on modern vehicles -Personal Safety – high amounts of voltage are used to light HID headlamps, so serious injury may occur from incidental contact. Do not touch the ballast or any nearby parts unless the power has been disabled on the vehicle. Disabling the power is typically done by disconnecting and isolating the negative battery cable and waiting a specific time for the power to discharge. Refer to vehicle-specific service information for proper procedures when working around HID headlamps -Adaptive Front Lighting Systems – adaptive front lighting systems: -May include self-leveling headlamps that adjust vertically to prevent glare into oncoming traffic -May include swiveling headlamps, which swivel horizontally when the steering wheel is turned -Use electric motors to adjust the angle of the lights -May have an electronic control unit that may be located near, or is part of, the headlamp assembly -May light a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL) if there is a problem with the system -Front Lamp Assembly Inspection – the headlamp assembly may require removal for a thorough inspection -Front Lamp Assembly Inspection – when inspecting front lamp assemblies, verify proper operation. Check for looseness, which may indicate damage. Check both headlamps. Collision energy may have transferred to the non-impact side of the vehicle and damaged the other headlamp. This may be a concern in cold temperatures, as parts become more brittle when cold. Service parts may be available for some broken mounting tabs, note the appearance may be different from original -Additional Inspection Considerations – some disassembly may be required to access mounting areas, such as bumper cover removal. Mounting points may be damaged even if there are no visual indications of damage on the exterior. Also check adjacent parts, such as brackets or the headlamp mounting panel, for damage. Damage to the backside of the lamp assembly may affect reflective material and be a safety concern for oncoming drivers, and be sure to check the wiring and connectors for damage -Headlamp Assembly Considerations – considerations for damaged front lamp assemblies may include removing minor scratches on the lens. Abrasive methods, such as buffing, may degrade the ultraviolet (UV) coating on the lens; therefore a chemical product may be used as an alternative. Matching to the opposite side. Due to lens clarity degradation from UV rays, a repaired or replaced headlamp may not match the other headlamp. Restoration products may be a consideration for the original headlamp to match the repaired or new one. How the replacement parts are serviced. A cracked lens may only be available as a lens and housing assembly or as an entire front lamp assembly. Note: Parts of lamp assemblies may include the lamp housing, bulbs, sockets, and wiring harness and connectors -Additional Assembly Considerations – additional assembly considerations include, replacing the lamp assembly if the housing is cracked. Cracks in the lamp assembly housing may cause the bulb sockets to corrode. Also, determining if wiring repair is an option. Some lamp assembly wiring is integrated with the engine compartment wiring harness and not available as a separate replacement part. Thus, it may be more feasible to repair damaged front lamp wiring if allowed by the vehicle maker -Headlamp Aiming – if there has been damage to the headlamp assembly, or the headlight mounting area, an aiming procedure may be required. Aiming headlamps may: -Be required if lamps are replaced or removed and installed (R&amp;I) -Be done using special aiming equipment -Requires verifying proper operation of the adaptive front lighting systems. A damaged motor may cause an improper or non-functioning adaptive lighting system. A scan tool may be required to verify operation or identify diagnostic trouble codes. -Note: Headlamp aiming should be checked every time the headlamps are removed or replaced. This will verify if the lights need adjustment Fenders -Fender – the fender is the outer panel located above the front wheels and is commonly bolted to the vehicle structure. -Fender Inspection – fenders may be made of steel, aluminum, plastic, or composite materials. When inspecting fenders for damage -Look for visible damage -Check the gaps between adjacent panels for misalignment. Gap issues may indicate structural damage -Check the mounting locations -Note: The top mounting locations for the fender on some vehicles are designed to collapse in the event of a pedestrian impact. Vehicle makers may not allow repairs to the fender mounting brackets. The service part may be available separately or may only be part of an assembly. The vehicle maker may allow them to be removed and used independently. Finally, look for indications of previous repairs -Fender Repair vs Replace – vehicle makers may have specific repair limitations for fenders. Considerations for fender repair include the extent and location of damage as well as the material type. Damage to metal fenders is generally repairable; however, highly formed areas like the back edge may limit the repairs to less involved damage. Complex shapes, body lines, and access may also limit the extent of damage that can be repaired. NVH materials may be on the backside of some fenders. Plastic and composite fenders are typically repaired with appropriate repair materials and methods. Vehicle makers may limit repairs to damage that does not extend to the edge of the part. Repairs to a fender usually require some adjacent part removal and may require R&amp;I of the fender -Fender Replacement – when replacing or R&amp;I of the fender is required, adjacent parts usually require R&amp;I, this may include, but is not limited to, the bumper cover, front lighting, rocker panel molding, cowl area trim, and fender liner. Some vehicle makers seal the back edge of the fender to the A-pillar with foam. Fender bolts may be one-time use -Fender Liners and Splash Shields – damaged fender liners and splash shields with minor damage may be required using heat or plastic repair techniques but the repair may be difficult due to thin materials in most situations. Typically, damaged fender liners and splash shields are replaced. Plastic repairs to damaged fender liners are typically not feasible because it is not a refinished part and it is difficult to match some textures. However, tab repair may be an option. Finally inspect for damaged clips and mounting holes -Other Fender Attachments – other parts attached to the fender that may require replacement or removal include: emblems, nameplates, moldings, and electric charging ports. Mechanical attachments and electrical grounds may be found on trucks and SUVs Hood -Hood Crush Zones – hoods typically have crush zones in the underside panels to cause them to fold during a collision, preventing hood intrusion into the passenger compartment. Another repair or replace consideration is whether or not the crush zones can be repaired without affecting the: -Integrity of the part. Crush zones must initiate folding during a frontal impact -Durability of the part. The hood must retain the proper shape for the life of the repair -Hood Repair – there are specific factors to take into consideration when determining a hood repair or replacement -Hood Inspection – hoods should also be inspected for: -Misalignment with the adjacent body panels and cowl. Also inspect for proper alignment of the latch and striker -Proper operation of the main and secondary latch mechanisms -Damage to hinges. When inspecting the hinges, check for the proper alignment of the safety stops. These reduce the likelihood of the hood entering the passenger compartment from a frontal impact -Indications of previous repairs, such as excessive film thickness or the use of body filler. Excessive film thickness of the finish could potentially lead to the removal of the existing finish before refinishing -Hood Repair Considerations – repair considerations for hoods include the extent and location of damage. Damage to panel contours may be difficult to straighten. Access to the backside of the damage may be required to apply corrosion protection. Repairs to composite hoods may be limited due to no access to the panel backside to make a two-sided repair. Another consideration is the type of substrate, such as mild or high-strength steel, aluminum, or composite. High-strength steel and aluminum may be more difficult to straighten compared to mild steel -Aluminum Hood Repair Considerations – vehicle makers may have specific repair limitations for aluminum hoods. Considerations for repair include the extent, location, and type of damage. PDR techniques including glue pull tabs may be an option for dent removal. Aluminum has less memory than mild steel and is not easily returned to the proper shape following collision damage. Aluminum work hardening characteristics can cause cracks to form during repairs to highly formed areas. Welding of cracks or tears may be challenging due to limitations in access to properly clean the backside of the panel. Some vehicle makers may have recommendations against welding cracks on aluminum hoods. -Hood Replacement Considerations – considerations for hood replacement include: -Replacing all damaged parts. The hood is typically supplied separately from the attached parts. Parts of the hood that may be damaged and may require replacement include the hood hinges, latch mechanisms, support rod, hood cushions, molding, and trim -Reinstalling the hood insulator, if it is undamaged. Replace all fasteners that were damaged during the collision or removal -Ensuring all labels that were attached to the hood are replaced -Additional Hood Replacement Considerations – additional hood replacement considerations include the application of chip-resistant coatings on the leading edge of the hood. Also, seam sealer may be required. Some replacement hoods do not have seam sealer applied by the factory. Another consideration is making adjustments to ensure the hood is properly aligned Aftermarket Parts -Accessories – front body accessories may be OEM or aftermarket, and may include: the grille guard, colored display lighting, tow hooks, and a snow plow -Damaged Accessory Considerations – some damaged accessory considerations include: -Checking for branding on the part, be it OEM or another brand -Customer consultation as to where to obtain parts -The owners insurance policy, or company guidelines, and repair options Damage Analysis of Unitized Structures – Front (2/2) Front Unitized Structure Damage Analysis Front Impact -Front Structure Commonly Damaged Parts – front impacts may damage one or more of the following parts of the unitized structure: -Radiator core support -Front rails – upper and lower -Strut towers -Aprons -Cowl -Engine cradle -Radiator Core Support Repair vs. Replace – the material type should be considered when making repair versus replace decisions. For example, minor straightening of a steel part may be allowed; however, a cracked magnesium or composite radiator core support is generally replaced. Considerations include replacing only the damaged portion of the radiator core support, if provided by the vehicle maker. Replacement of one side may eliminate having to remove the fender and other adjacent parts on the opposite side. This may be done along factory seams. Generally speaking, this option is model specific. It is also important to remember to re-order labels for the upper tie bar if it is replaced. -Upper Rail Repair Considerations – when making repair and replace decisions on the upper rail: -Sectioning options may be available depending on the vehicle maker. While not the most common type of repair, there are some procedures available. -It may be allowed to just replace the outer or inner portion of the rail -Replacement procedures generally require separation from the strut tower -Straightening may be possible, depending on the steel strength, but note heat considerations or if heat may be used at all -Lower Rails – lower rails: -May use mild, HSS, or UHSS -Have sectioning procedures. Many models have multiple optional locations; however, some do not recommend any sectioning locations, requiring complete rail assembly to be replaced -May allow replacing either the inner or outer portion of the rail -Note: It is recommended to only follow the part replacement guidelines outlined by the vehicle maker. If a vehicle maker does not offer sectioning guidelines, then sectioning should not be done -Strut Towers – strut towers are generally replaced when damaged -Strut Tower Overview – strut tower position is critical for proper suspension alignment and affects angles such as steering axis inclination (SAI), caster, or camber. Underhood measurement quick checks can be made to determine if the strut tower has moved. This requires measuring the distance between the strut towers, as well as measuring to other areas under the hood. Point-to-point specifications can be found in vehicle service information or estimating guides. Note: Strut towers are commonly made from stamped steel. However, some vehicle models are made from cast aluminum -Replacement Considerations – Considerations for replacing the strut tower assembly may include: -Partial replacement as an option depending on vehicle maker recommendations, the design of the part, and the extent of damage -Complete part replacement of the strut tower rail assembly -Extensive vehicle disassembly which may include but is not limited to parts such as powertrain, suspension, adjacent body parts, etc. -Rivets and bonding adhesive which is commonly used for aluminum strut towers -Apron or Skirt – the fender apron, also called a skirt -Fills in the area between the strut tower and radiator core support -Connects the upper and lower rails -Adds rigidity to the front structure -Note: Aprons are typically light-gauge construction. Minor repairs may be possible. Some apron panels are highly formed and difficult to restore the original shape -Cowl (Dash Panel) – the cowl panel is not often repaired. Damage that deep into the vehicle structure may have too many other issues, and oftentimes the vehicle is a total loss. Repairs to the cowl may require removal of vehicle parts in the area of the damage. This may include: -Engine and transmission assembly -Windshield -Heating, ventilating, and air conditioning parts -Instrument panel -Carpet -Interior trim -Note: Upper cowl panel replacement may require VIN transfer -Engine Cradle – the engine cradle: -May also be called a sub frame -Is used to hold the engine and transmission in place -Attaches to the front lower rails -Is made from aluminum, steel, or magnesium -Is very difficult to straighten, so replacement is common. Some vehicle makers prohibit straightening the engine cradle -Dimension specifications are difficult to find -May contain engine, transmission, steering, and suspension mounting locations -Must be properly aligned to the vehicle structure -Front Structure Damage Analysis – a thorough damage analysis requires examining all areas of the vehicle to ensure a complete and accurate estimate -Front Structure Inspection – when analyzing the front structure for damage: -Start at the area of impact and work toward the rear of the vehicle -Visualize the direction of impact -Check the operation of the doors, hood, and trunk. Do not operate the closure panel if opening it will cause additional damage -The fender-to-door gap is the most common indicator Damage Analysis of Unitized Structures – Center (1/2) Exterior Parts Door Construction and Inspection -Overview – a thorough and accurate damage analysis process for exterior body panels: -Must be established to ensure a complete and safe repair -Will help reduce supplements, which may also affect the timeliness of the repair -Requires a good understanding and knowledge of repair and replacement options for parts of the vehicle exterior -May require making note of any suspected structural damage. This will indicate that further inspection is required before beginning repairs to help ensure there are no delays in the repair process -May help develop a repair plan based on the identified damage. The earlier that damage is identified, the more accurate the repair plan -Door Construction – a vehicle door contains several parts, including the: -Door shell, also referred to as the door frame -Outer panel, also referred to as the door skin -Intrusion beam -Note: Replacement door shells often include the door intrusion beam and may include the outer door panel. Depending on the replacement door shell design, an outer door panel may be serviced either as a separate part or only with the complete door assembly. Door intrusion beams are commonly made of ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS) and are designed to reduce the chance of the door collapsing into the passenger compartment during a side impact. The doors may be constructed with a frame around the glass, or they may be frameless -Door Inspection – when inspecting doors for damage, you will need to take certain steps that include: -Checking for visible damage to the outer door panel and door shell -Checking for proper alignment by checking the edges of the door for uneven gaps to adjacent panels -Checking for proper operation by opening and closing the doors -Checking that the window regulator, lock, and power accessories are operating properly -Checking for damaged weatherstripping. Weatherstripping may be attached with adhesive and not removable without causing damage -Damaged Door Intrusion Beams – there may be one or more intrusion beams attached to the door shell. Damaged door intrusion beams should not be straightened and must be replaced. Door intrusion beams may be attached to brackets. Any damaged parts attached to the door intrusion beam should also not be straightened. Straightening damaged UHSS intrusion beams can cause the metal to be brittle, so it may not react as designed in a subsequent collision. Door intrusion beams are typically supplied with a replacement door shell. However, vehicle makers may provide a bolt-on replacement door intrusion beam for some vehicles -Handles, Locks, and Lock Cylinders – inspect door handles, locks, and lock cylinders for proper operation. It is also necessary to inspect them for loose or improper mounting and damage to the keypad -Lock Cylinders -lock cylinders or attaching lock parts cannot be repaired. Replacement lock cylinders must be re-keyed to match the other lock cylinders on the vehicle -Door Handles – during inspection, look for damage to rods, linkages, cables, clips, and hardware associated with the handle and lock cylinder. Due to durability concerns, broken door handles are typically replaced, not repaired. Door handle and lock trim may be ordered according to the color or trim package, but may also require refinishing to achieve the proper color match -Electronic Keypads – some doors have an electronic keypad that can be used to unlock the door. You may need to consult with the customer to get the keypad code to determine if the keypad is working properly -Door Mirror Inspection – due to the variety of options or equipment available, door mirrors may be simple or complex. Mirrors may include mechanical parts or electronic systems that require identification and inspection. -Visual Inspection – inspect exterior mirrors for visual damage such as cracks in the housing or broken glass, as well as loose or improper mounting. Also, check for secondary damage to the door shell or adjacent parts. Verify proper operation of the adjustment control. This may be a pivot-action control or an electronic system using motors to adjust the mirror. Check for proper operation of any heating elements -ADAS Features – during the inspection, determine if the vehicle is equipped with any advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS). If the vehicle has a blind spot detection system, indicator lamps may display in the mirrors. Some vehicles have cameras built into the mirrors requiring inspection as well. Be aware that after repair, there may be additional procedures required to calibrate and/or verify proper operation -Mirror Lighting – for door mirrors, be sure to include inspection of different built-in lighting. Turn signals may be visible in the exterior door mirrors. They may be displayed with a series of LEDs. Verify proper operation of courtesy lighting, which is usually located under the door mirrors -Door Mirror Replacement Considerations – door mirror replacement may include removal and installation (R&amp;I) of the interior door trim panel for access. Mirror assemblies may vary within the same vehicle year, make, and model. It is important to identify the trim package and vehicle options to ensure the mirror is replaced properly. Considerations may include: -Identifying the part or trim color -Determining the type of adjustment controls -Determining if the mirror is heated -Determining whether the mirror contains and advanced systems, such as a blind spot detection indicator or cameras -Note: a power folding mirror may be included as part of a towing package. This may also have to be identified when replacing a door mirror assembly -Door Hinges and Latch Mechanisms – door hinges may be either welded or bolted to the door shell and A- and B- pillars. When inspecting door hinges and latch mechanisms: -Check for visible damage such as distortion or a cracked weld bead -Check for proper operation by verifying that the door swings smoothly on the hinges -Pay attention to any odd movement when the latch engages or disengages from the striker -Ensure the latch mechanism engages and disengages properly -Verify that the door does not drop or pop up; if so, damage to the following is possible -Hinges -Door frame -Vehicle structure -Note: Replacing welded-on hinges with bolt-on replacement parts may be recommended. This may require drilling holes for the bolts Door Part Repair and Replacement -Outer Door Panel Repair – repair options may depend on the type of material. Outer door panels may be made of steel, aluminum, or sheet-molded compound (SMC). Damage to the outer door panel may require straightening or replacement, depending on the extent and location of damage. Minor dents may be repaired with paint less dent repair (PDR), however, this may require removing the glass to prevent breaking it. -Outer Door Panel Replacement – considerations for outer door panel replacement include: Part availability and attachment methods and application of materials -Part Availability and Attachment Methods – replacing the outer door panel alone is not an option if it is only supplied with the door shell. Attachment methods must also be considered, including: -GMA welding -Squeeze-type resistance spot welding (STRSW) -Adhesive -Rivets -Any combination of welds, adhesive, and rivets -Note: Outer door panel replacement may require combining welding and adhesive bonding. This should not be confused with the process of weld bonding, which uses adhesive and spot welds in the same joint. Adhesives may be used along the outside flanges, while welds are used in the pinch weld of the window opening or a sectioning joint. Outer door panel replacement may include sectioning on or near the door belt line or window frame -Application of Materials – application of materials is also a consideration for outer door panel replacement. This includes applying: -Anti-corrosion compound to the lower hem flange seam area -Noise, vibration, and harshness (NVH) materials. -Note: This also includes replacing any seam sealer or foam pads removed during the repair process -Door Assembly Replacement – there are a few things to consider when replacing a door assembly. Considerations for replacing a door assembly include transferring the original inner mechanical and electronic parts, as well as interior panels, to the replacement part. On some vehicles, with the exception of the door panel, these parts may be contained in a door cassette and transferred as one piece. If replacing a door with an entire recycled door assembly, all of the inner electrical and mechanical parts, as well as the interior trim panel may have to be transferred from the original door assembly. If the interior trim color and accessories do not match, removing parts from both the original door assembly and from the recycled door assembly may be required. The original parts would then be installed in the recycled door assembly. -Van Doors – van sliding doors may be equipped with a variety of options and features. Sliding doors are similar to other doors; however, the attachment is different, using tracks and rollers instead of hinges. The glass may be fixed or moveable. Most sliding doors have power opening and closing features with remote operation. Some have the hands-free option. Similar to other doors, look for any damage to the skin or frame and verify gaps and fit-up to adjacent panels. Check the opening and closing operation using all available options. Inspect the rollers, tracks, latches, and strikers for proper operation. Hands-free operated doors typically have a sensor mounted in the rocker panel. -Repair – repair considerations for a sliding door are similar to those of a hinged door. NVH materials or other materials may be present which should be identified if using weld-on dent removal techniques. Generally, outer panels are only serviced with the entire door shell assembly -Replacement – sliding door replacement requires transferring all undamaged parts from the original door to the replacement. Sometimes door alignment can be challenging. It may require an assistant or a door support dolly. NVH, seam sealer, and corrosion protection application are additional considerations. The power operation feature will typically require an initialization procedure for the door to function properly. Verify all aspects and features of the sliding door function -Other Door Considerations – other considerations regarding repair or replacement of the door include: Inspecting the side impact sensor for damage and replacing labels attached to replacement parts -Side Impact Sensors – replacement of any side impact sensor located near collision damage may be recommended. On some vehicles, the side impact sensors are located in the doors. Some vehicles may have the airbag mounted in the seat side, while some have them mounted in the door. A deployed side airbag will have to be replaced. This may require replacing the interior door trim panel as well. Some vehicle makers specify replacement of any supplemental restraint system (SRS) sensor located near collision damage, regardless of deployment. The SRS may have to be disabled before inspection. Disabling is commonly done by disconnecting the vehicle battery and waiting a few minutes. Check the vehicle service information for the specific SRS disabling procedure -Replacing Labels – when replacing a door, labels attached to original parts may have to be replaced. There may also be labels attached to replacement parts that should not be removed Movable Glass Inspection and Repair -Movable Glass – movable glass may include door glass, quarter glass, and sunroofs. Movable glass may also include rear glass on some SUVs and pickups. It may also be present as a sliding panoramic roof assembly. Movable glass may be laminated, but it is typically tempered. Tempered glass will shatter upon impact, limiting repair options to minor surface scratches. Minor cracks in laminated door glass may be repairable if the damage is contained to the outer layer of the glass -Glass Inspection – inspect movable glass for: -Cracks -Deep pits -Chipped edges -Scratches -Water leaks -Signs of improper, previous installation -Note: Damage that can be felt with your fingernail is typically not repairable. There may also be damage to the interior side caused by mass inertia. Disassembly may be required to completely inspect movable glass. This is especially true if the door glass was lowered or open during the collision. If the glass is broken, cover the area to prevent moisture from damaging the vehicle interior -Movable Glass Considerations – considerations for damaged movable glass include determining the correct window tint. Placing a white sheet of paper behind the glass on the undamaged side may identify the level or color of the tint. Coding information located on the bottom of the glass may also include the type of tint. Aftermarket tint may be identified by a seam line near the top portion of the glass. Pieces of glass may also fall into areas that can restrict the movement of mechanical parts. Examples: -Glass run channels -Seat tracks -Seat belt retractors -Note: Glass between the door intrusion beam and the outer door panel also needs to be removed -Movable Glass Parts Inspection – inspect movable glass parts for proper operation through the whole range of movement. A damaged module will result in inoperative power windows and may have blown fuses, damaged wires, or damaged motors. Loose or corroded grounds, connectors, or terminal pins may also be the cause of an inoperative power window. Inspect for loose or missing parts, binding, or improper alignment. To prevent further damage, do not try to force the glass if it is binding. Shake the door and listen for clanking sounds, which may indicate loose interior parts or damage -Heated Glass – heated glass is common with back glass, but may also be found on movable glass, such as movable lift gate glass. Heated glass typically has wires or grid lines printed onto the glass. The heating parts may be repairable. Repairs include reattaching loose terminals with conductive epoxy or solder. Minor damage to grid lines may be repaired with conductive paint. Grid lines with major damage or those that are falling off will require replacement Sunroof Inspection and Repair Considerations -Sunroofs – roof panels may be equipped with a sunroof, which is typically removed and reinstalled into the new roof panel following replacement. Sunroofs may be designed to fully open by sliding the glass backward, or may be hinged at the front to only partially open. Roof panels may also be equipped with a movable panoramic glass roof. Panoramic roofs are wider than sunroofs and extend closer to the sides of the vehicle for a wider view and to allow in more light -Sunroof Inspection – sunroofs should be inspected for proper operation through the whole range of movement. Again, be cautious of binding glass, and do not force movement. Additional damage may include: -Misaligned or damaged guide rails -Damaged weatherstripping -Damaged or missing clips -Damaged switches, cables, or gears -A damaged air deflector -Pinched drain tubes -Note: Plan to replace the glass if it is damaged. Determine if the mounting parts will be replaced or reused -Sunroof Assembly Inspection Considerations – there are other inspection considerations for replacing sunroof or movable panoramic roof assemblies. Determine which parts require replacement and which parts can be ordered separately. For example, only the glass may require replacement if it is damaged. If damage extends to parts of the frame, the frame assembly has to be replaced. Determine whether the parts are bolted or bonded. The glass may be bolted to the frame. The frame may be bolted and bonded to the roof -Sunroof Assembly Removal Considerations – some considerations for removing a sunroof or movable panoramic roof assembly may include removing the appropriate adjacent parts. For example, the headliner may require removal or repositioning. It may be recommended to remove an adjacent panoramic roof when the sunroof assembly requires replacement. Vehicle maker procedures must be followed to ensure the sunroof is installed properly and to prevent damage to adjacent parts. Perform an initializing procedure, if required, either manually or with a scan tool Damage Analysis of Unitized Structures – Center (2/2) Structural Damage Analysis Side Body Structure and Pillar Repair Considerations -Side Body Structure – pillars and rocker panels are part of the passenger compartment structure. To protect vehicle occupants, the passenger compartment must remain intact. A-, B-, C-, and D-pillars, along with rocker panels and roof rails, may be part of a one-, or two-piece uniside for the outer panel depending on how the quarter panel is attached. Most have multiple reinforcements made from HSS and UHSS. This may include inner and outer reinforcements. Pillars and rocker panels also use inner panels to close out the backside of the part -Servicing Outer Uniside Assemblies – vehicle build sequence may influence how the uniside is replaced. If adjacent parts such as the roof overlap the uniside, you may have to remove an undamaged roof in the process of replacing the upper uniside. Undamaged roof panels should not be reused once they are removed. Unisides may be serviced as an assembly or smaller parts. Vehicle makers may have replacement requirements that include specific sectioning locations. They may identify sectioning areas with no specific measurements. In some situations, vehicle makers may recommend sectioning locations be determined based on the service part and the amount of damage -A-Pillar Considerations – when inspecting an A-pillar, look for visual damage and verify gaps to adjacent parts. It is important to determine if damage is limited to the outer panel or if it radiates deeper into an underlying reinforcement. Vehicle makers may have profile or cutaway views of pillars showing the layers of construction in the body repair manual. A-pillar outer panels may be made from a variety of materials which may affect repairability -Possible Limitations – when deciding whether or not to repair an outer A-pillar, note that most A-pillars contain foam or other materials that may present a fire hazard if using weld-on dent removal equipment. Also, there may be curtain airbags behind the A-pillar trim. You must deactivate the airbag system and remove the airbag module when working in this area -Sectioning – consult the repair information to determine if sectioning is permitted. When repairing the A-pillar, sectioning joints are often made in the windshield area and near the rocker panel. Sectioning in these areas requires removing the windshield. If not recommendations exist from the vehicle maker, the part should be replaced at factory seams -Replacement – if full part replacement is required, be aware that: -The A-pillar may be serviced as part of the uniside -Extensive adjacent part separation or removal may be required. This may include the roof, upper fender rail, rocker panel, or other parts -Any foams inside the pillar rea affected require removal and reinstallation -The instrument panel is generally removed if the inner A-pillar must be replaced -Windshield removal is required -Most vehicle makers require complete replacement of the reinforcement if it is damaged -A-Pillars on Convertibles – the A-pillars on convertible vehicles: -May have a tubular-shaped reinforcement -May be two-part construction -May be multi-part construction that includes an inner, outer, and reinforcement -B-Pillars – the B-pillar has: -An inner panel -An outer panel that is generally part of a uniside -At least one reinforcement -Note: The reinforcement material is generally HSS or UHSS -B-Pillar Considerations – there are several considerations you should be aware of regarding the design, inspection, and repair methods of B-pillars -Inspection – it is important to determine if damage is limited to the outer panel, or if it radiates deeper into the reinforcement. Unless there is specific design information that shows how close the reinforcement is to the outer panel, it may be difficult to determine if the reinforcement is damaged -Repair – for repairs, the B-pillar reinforcement sectioning procedures are limited. Most vehicle makers require complete replacement of the reinforcement if it is damaged. If no recommendations exist from the vehicle maker, the part should be replaced at factory seams -C- and D-Pillar Considerations – when determining procedures for repairing the C- and D-pillars, keep in mind: -The back glass typically requires removal for C- and D-pillar installation -Inner assemblies are generally replaced at factory seams -C- and D-pillars may be separated from the quarter panel at the roof-to-pillar seam -C- and D-pillars may be included with the replacement quarter panel -C- and D-Pillar Considerations Continued – additional considerations for C- and D-pillar repair or replacement procedures include removing adjacent parts and any foam -Adjacent Parts – adjacent parts may require removal when replacing parts at factory seams. For example, the roof, rocker panel, or quarter panel may require removal to gain access to a pillar. Interior trim will also likely require removal -Foam – pillars may be filled with foam. Foam that is removed during the repair process should be replaced with the same type and amount of foam -Overlapping Panels – on some vehicles, the roof panel overlaps the outer panel. Removal of the outer C- and D-pillar panels also requires removing enough roof panel flange spot welds to gain access to the outer panel, while being careful to not damage the roof panel. The same may be an issue with the B-pillar. In some instances, just a portion of the roof can be lifted to remove the part. In others, the entire roof panel may have to be removed Rocker Panel Inspection and Repair Considerations -Rocker Panels – the rocker panels are typically three or more layers. Outer rocker panels are generally part of a uniside. Reinforcements are usually UHSS or aluminum and may be in close proximity to the outer panel and subject to damage. The rocker panel may have multiple reinforcements at the base of the pillars -Repair – when considering rocker panel repair, there is typically no access to the backside of an outer rocker panel to push out damage. Therefore, glue pull tabs or weld-on dent removal tools are used. Be aware that some rocker panels are filled with foam or other materials which may limit weld-on dent removal operations. Foam or other materials that are removed during the repair process should be replaced with the same type and amount -Replacement – when replacing an outer rocker panel, the vehicle maker may have sectioning procedures or require the installation of the complete part. Some vehicle makers service portions of the rocker panel while others only service it with an entire uniside. Multiple sectioning locations may be provided for the outer rocker panel. Vehicle makers may permit sectioning according to the damage for the outer rocker panel, based on specific criteria. Typically, outer rocker panel replacement involves sectioning near the base of the pillars. Always follow vehicle maker procedures -Reinforcements – reinforcement sectioning is more limited and should only be done following vehicle maker recommendations. If no sectioning procedures exist, full part replacement is required. This may require extension disassembly and removal of adjacent parts Floor Pan Inspection and Repair Considerations -Floor Pan Inspection – a complete inspection of the floor generally requires the vehicle to be raised on a lift. Look for any damaged coatings, dents, crushed areas, tears, or punctures. Some vehicles will have aerodynamic panels underneath that may be damaged and require removal to inspect the floor. Interior parts, seats, and carpet may also require removal for a thorough inspection. Measuring can be done to verify the proper dimensions. Determine if the damage is isolated to the floor or if there is damage radiating into the floor from other adjacent areas. -Repair – repair considerations to a damaged floor include: -Vehicle maker repair recommendatiosn -Material type and strength -Damage type, extent, and location -Note: Floor pans may be mild steel. However, some vehicle makers are using HSS, UHSS, or other materials such as aluminum or composites. Be aware that damage to the floor may affect the occupant classification system (OCS) on some vehicles. This may require zeroing or calibration of the OCS -Crossmembers – floor crossmembers may be made of HSS or UHSS and are considered structural parts that need to be inspected for damage -Cowl Panel Attachment – some floor pans are attached to cowl panels. Refer to vehicle maker recommendations to identify the joining process used when making floor pan repairs that involve attachment to cowl panels. If damage has radiated into the cowl panel, service parts are available but repairs are not very common. Damage that radiates dep into the vehicle structure may have caused many other issues, and often the vehicle is a total loss -Floor Pan Replacement Considerations – there are several factors to consider when determining whether you should repair or replace a floor pan -Part Availability – replacement floor pans may be available in one-piece or in multiple-panels. Some vehicle makers also provide replacement parts for floor pans, such as NVH material and floor plugs -Replacement Procedures – front pans are typically replaced at the factory seam. Some vehicle makers have sectioning procedures which may identify cut lines, while others may be based on specific criteria. Repair and replacement procedures may not be provided by some vehicle makers. In a situation like this, the insurer, vehicle owner, and repair facility need to discuss all options to determine how the vehicle should be repaired -Accessibility – most vehicles are built starting with the floor pan with the rest of the parts installed around it. As a result, it will be difficult to remove a complete floor pan to install a replacement. The entire uniside, rails, or the roof may need to be replaced in order to install a replacement floor pan Roof Panel Inspection and Repair Considerations -Roof Panel Inspection – roof panels may be constructed from steel, aluminum, composites, glass, or a combination of any of these. Inspect for damage including dents, tears, punctures, scratches, chips, cracks, and cracked seam sealer. There may be damage to antennas or other roof-mounted accessories such as luggage racks or bicycle carriers. Vinyl roofs are not common; however, if equipped, check for damage or tears. Convertible tops may be fabric, vinyl, or retractable hardtops and should be inspected for damage as well. Inspect the underlying structure including the roof bows, side rails, as well as the front and rear glass attachment areas. Check the operation of power roofs -Roof Panel Repair – when considering roof panel repair, there may be limited accessibility to the backside of the panel to push out damage. Therefore, glue pull tabs or weld-on dent removal tools may be used if permitted by the vehicle maker. Repair to roof bows and side rails may be limited due to construction materials. Some may be UHSS or aluminum. Be aware that foam or other NVH materials may be present. Damage repair may require headliner and other part removal. Vehicle makers may have limitations on repairing certain materials such as carbon fiber. This may limit the repair to minor cosmetic scratch removal -Roof Replacement – vehicle build sequence may influence how the roof panel and supporting structure are replaced. If adjacent parts, such as the uniside, overlap any panels requiring replacement, you may have to section or replace the uniside. Follow vehicle maker attachment procedures when replacing a roof. Stationary glass, side curtain airbag, and additional interior trim removal are typically required. Attachment methods may include: rivet bonding, STRSW, MAG welds, and adhesives. Consider foams, NVH materials, and corrosion protection materials. For any roof repair or replacement procedures, identify ADAS service and calibration requirements that may be applicable. Damage Analysis of Unitized Structures – Rear (1/2) (1 of 2) Rear Body and Aftermarket Parts Inspection Rear Bumpers and Lighting -Overview – a thorough and accurate damage analysis process for exterior body panels: -Must be established to ensure a complete and safe repair -Will help reduce supplements, which may also affect the timeliness of the repair -Requires a good understanding and knowledge of repair and replacement options for parts of the vehicle exterior -May also require making note of any suspected structural damage. This will indicate that further inspection is required before beginning repairs to help ensure there are no delays in the repair process -May help develop a repair plan based on the identified damage. The earlier that damage is identified, the more accurate the repair plan -Rear Body Inspection – when analyzing the rear structure for damage: -Start at the area of impact and work toward the front of the vehicle -Visualize the direction of impact -Use the same procedures as a front end analysis when examining bumpers, lighting, and closure panels -Rear Bumper Considerations – repair considerations for a damaged rear bumper cover include checking vehicle maker recommendations, location, type, and severity of damage. Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) as well as other technologies may affect repairs -ADAS – the rear bumper may have sensors or other electronic parts that require inspection. This may include, but is not limited to, blind spot detection, cross-traffic alert, parking assist sensors, and rear collision detection. These sensors may be mounted behind, through or on the sides of the bumper cover. Damage to sensors may set diagnostic trouble codes (DTC), turn on a system malfunction indicator lamp (MIL), or display a driver information system message. Damaged sensors require replacement. Most sensors may be replaced independently. Replacement parking sensors may require refinishing to match the vehicle. Some vehicle makers do not recommend refinishing sensors because the excessive paint thickness may adversely affect the parking assist system. Refer to the vehicle-specific service information for service requirements. New bumper covers may require drilling or punching of parking assist sensor holes -Bumper Absorbers and Reinforcement – most vehicles have an energy absorber and reinforcement beneath the bumper cover. Molded plastic or foam energy absorbers are typically designed for one-time use and must be replaced if damaged. Mechanical energy absorbers are not as common and are typically replaced if damaged. Inspect for leaks, bends, correct length, and proper operation. Rear bumper reinforcements may be a separate bolt-on part or part of the rear unitized body structure. Construction materials may include steel, aluminum, or composites. Any cracking or tearing of the reinforcement, as well as any damage that may affect the crash characteristics of the vehicle, will require replacement of the part. Steel reinforcements are often made from ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS); therefore, they are generally not straightened -Rear Lamp Assembly Inspection Introduction – there are various parts of lighting to inspect -Bulbs, Sockets, and Housings – check for broken or burned-out bulbs and damaged or corroded bulb sockets. Also, look for cracked or broken lenses or housings. This type of damage allows water and moisture inside and could lead to corrosion or part failure. Inspect mounting locations and hardware for damage. Some lamp assemblies may have additional sensors or ADAS components that should be inspected as well -LEDs – some lamp assemblies use multiple LEDs for illuminating rear lamps. Individual LEDs cannot be replaced because they are typically mounted directly to a circuit board -Electrical – check for cut, pinched, or corroded wires. Inspect for damaged, loose, or corroded grounds or connectors. It may be necessary to partially disassemble the lamp assembly to determine the condition of the connectors and other related electrical parts. Electrical wires for lamp assemblies may be repairable following vehicle maker service information. Connectors and repair wires and terminals may be serviced. Lighting problems related to rear collisions may include blown fuses, shorts, opens in the circuits, and may set a DTC, MIL, or driver information system message. Ensure service parts meet OEM specifications to reduce post-repair lighting system problems -Rear Closure Panel – the rear closure panel may be a deck lid, liftgate, door, or hatch. A deck lid may also be called a trunk lid. Construction materials may include steel, aluminum, magnesium, composites, or plastics. Some vehicles are equipped with a rear spare tire carrier which is in close proximity or attached to the rear closure panel. Inspect the spare tire carrier and attachment points for damage -Inspection – when inspecting rear closure panels, look for signs of visual damage to the outer panel and inner frame. Inspect panel gaps and verify proper operation when opening and closing. Ensure any power open/close features are functional, as well as remote and hands-free operation. Inspect hinges, latches, locks, strikers, alignment guides, support struts, glass, and trim for signs of damage. Check operation of any attached accessories such as lighting, rear wipers, and cameras. Damage to a closure panel may cause the gaps to be off. Additionally, damage to the structure, hinges, latches, or adjacent panels may also affect the gaps. -Repair Considerations – repairability of rear closure panels depend on the construction material, extent, and location of damage, as well as access to the backside of the panel. Rear closure panels made of aluminum or high-strength steel present greater repair challenges compared to a mild steel part. Outer panels have the greatest opportunity for repair. Damage the extends to the inner frame is difficult to access and restore to original shape. This typically requires complete part replacement. Some rear closure panels may have a magnesium inner frame which cannot be straightened or welded. Composite or plastic closure panels may permit outer panel repairs; however, if the inner frame is damaged, they are generally not repaired due to the design of the part. Note: Lock cylinders or attaching lock parts cannot be repaired. Replacement lock cylinders must be rekeyed to match the other lock cylinders on the vehicle. -Replacement Considerations – most rear closure panels are serviced as a complete shell assembly. Some vehicle makers may service an outer panel which is attached with adhesives and a hem flange similar to a door skin. When replacing a rear closure panel assembly, it will necessary to transfer undamaged glass, parts, and accessories. It may be necessary to seam seal the new part. Depending on vehicle options, initializations, calibrations, or both may be required Pickup Truck Box Assembly -Box Assembly Parts – common parts of a pickup truck box assembly include the: -Front panel -Inner side panel -Bed floor -Tailgate -Crossmembers -Outer side panel -Pickup Box Assembly Inspection Introduction – there are a number of areas to check when inspecting the pickup box assembly. Note: Remember to consider aftermarket accessories when inspecting the pickup box assembly. Examples include tonneau covers, caps, and toolboxes -Cab and Front Panel – check the alignment between the front panel of the box assembly and the back panel of the cab. Inconsistent gaps on both sides of the vehicle may indicate structural damage -Front Panel – check the front panel for damage. Mass inertia damage may occur from cargo shifting during a collision. Collision forces from a front or side impact may transfer to the front pickup box panel. If the front panel is damaged, inspect the cab back panel for possible damage as well. Repairs to the front panel may be possible. However, the shape of the panel generally limits the extent of repairs that can be done -Inner Panels, Bed Floor, and Tailgate – check the inner panels, bed floor, and tailgate for direct, indirect, and mass inertia damage caused by objects in the box assembly at the time of the collision. Damage to composite bed floors should follow vehicle maker recommendations and may be limited. Composite floors with cracks or punctures are typically replaced -Crossmembers – inspect the bed floor crossmembers for crushing or other damage. If damage is present, identify if there is damage to the vehicle frame and bed floor as well as attaching hardware. Crossmembers may only be available with the bed floor -Box Mounting Hardware – check the box mounting hardware for damage. This could be an indication that the box has moved. Hardware may be one-time use fasteners which require replacement if loosened or removed. Damaged hardware requires replacement -Pickup Box Assembly Considerations – when considering box assembly repairs, keep in mind the type of material influences the repair methods. Steel and aluminum bedside panels can be repaired using common panel straightening tools and techniques. The box sides on some vehicles are made from sheet molded compound (SMC) which requires access for a two-sided repair if there are any cracks or punctures. If an outer box side requires replacement, it may only be serviced with the inner bedside and wheelhouse, or it may be available as a separate panel. Some repairs and most panel replacements on the bedside will require the bed to be loosened and set back or completely removed from the vehicle. When ordering parts, determine the size of the bed, the attachment method specified by the vehicle maker, and how parts are supplied -Bedliner Repair Considerations – a damaged pickup truck may have a bedliner installed. A bedliner may be OEM or aftermarket, and may be either a plastic drop-in type or a spray-on type. Damaged spray-on bedliners will require repair if it was damaged during the collision or removed during the repair. Repair kits for each type of bedliner may be available. Bedliner repairs may be sublet -Other Considerations – other considerations for pickup truck box assemblies may include inspecting the rear bumper, tailgate, and spare tire hoist -Rear Bumper – full-frame rear bumpers are commonly made of steel and may be painted or chrome-plated. They may have plastic trim or other parts as well as ADAS sensors. Inspect the bumper, mounting brackets, hardware, and other associated parts for damage. Damaged parts are typically replaced. If the rear bumper is damaged, conduct a thorough inspection of the bed for possible damage as well -Tailgate – the tailgate may be equipped with a variety of options including cameras and sensors, steps and assist handles, remote opening, load stops, half-gate opening and more. Inspect the tailgate for visible damage as well as proper gaps and it. This includes inspecting the inner and outer tailgate. Open and close the tailgate to verify the hinges, handle locks and latches operate smoothly with no binding. Damage to the outer panel may be repairable using common panel repair tools and techniques. If damage is excessive and extends to the inner panel, replacement is typically repaired. When doing repairs or replacement, note any moldings, decals, or other parts or hardware that are needed -Spare Tire Hoist – some unibody and full-frame vehicles have an underbody spare tire storage location which utilizes a tire hoist or winch. If the vehicle has sustained collision damage, it is possible the tire hoist is damaged. Inspect the hoist for visible damage and proper operation. If the tire is loose, this may indicate damage to the hoist. A damaged hoist requires replacement Damage Analysis of Unitized Structures – Rear (2/2) (1 of 2) Rear Structural Damage Analysis -Trunk Floor – trunk floors are generally single-layer mild steel. The floor may be part of the rear rail structure in that it contributes to the structural integrity of the rail and can affect how the rear rail collapses. For example, trunk floors often close out three-sided rear rail hat channels. The trunk floor may be one- or two-piece construction. You should inspect the trunk for any attaching parts and damage to those parts. The vehicle battery may be located in the trunk -Trunk Floor Repair Considerations – repairability of the trunk floor will depend on the vehicle maker recommendations, extent and location of the damage, type of construction material, and accessibility for repairs. There may be convolutions and compound shapes to absorb collision energy, limiting repairs in some situations. When determining possible replacement procedures for the trunk floor, note that some vehicle makers have sectioning procedures that may allow a less intrusive repair. Some vehicles have a composite spare tire well that is bonded into the vehicle floor. Repairs may be possible using appropriate plastic repair techniques depending on the extent of damage. Adhesive will be required for complete part replacement. -Rear Floors on SUVs and Minivans – SUVs and minivans have a rear floor pan instead of a trunk. The large rear door opening allows easy access to the rear floor pan. This may reduce the number of adjacent parts that require removal when replacing the floor compared to a trunk floor in a typical passenger car. Some SUVs and minivans have storage tubs integrated into the floor as a storage place for the seat when it is folded down. This storage tub may be bonded into the floor by the vehicle maker. If damaged beyond repair, the replacement tub is bonded using the recommended adhesive -Rear Body Panels – the rear body panel is beneath the deck lid or liftgate and attaches the left and right inner quarter panel structures. The panel may be made from multiple steel strengths, depending on vehicle design. For example, some vehicles have rear body panels made of boron-alloyed steel while other models use mild steel. The rear body panel may be made of multiple panels and serviced as an assembly. -Inspection – rear body panels may have complex shapes which accommodate the rear closure panel, lights, bumper cover attachments, and weatherstripping. Inspect for any obvious physical damage to the rear body panel. Inspect gaps between the rear closure panels and adjacent panels. Look for any inconsistencies which may indicate the rear body panel is damaged. Inspect gaps on tail lamp assemblies and the bumper cover which may also indicate problems with the rear body panel. -Repair Considerations – repairability of the rear body panel will depend on the vehicle maker recommendations, extent and location of the damage, as well as construction material and accessibility for repairs. Repairs may be limited due to multi-layer construction. The complex shape of many rear body panels can make it challenging to restore and align all of the different adjacent parts in some situations. UHSS rear body panels are usually replaced if damaged. -Replacement Considerations – rear body panels are usually replaced at factory seams unless the vehicle maker indicates a sectioning procedure. Quarter panels or other adjacent parts may overlap and be affected when replacing the rear body panel. Installation procedures commonly include using spot welds and may also require adhesive. Panel seams should be sealed to prevent moisture and exhaust fumes from entering the vehicle. -Rear Rail – the rear rails and crossmembers form the rear underbody structure on a unibody vehicle. The rear rails also support the floor pan and trunk floor. Rear rails are made from various steel strengths, depending on vehicle design, and may be closed out by a rear body panel, floor pan, and bumper bracket. The rails may be made from tailor-welded blanks and may be available as a complete sub-assembly. In some configurations, the rear rails serve as the attachment point for the trailer hitch. -Repair Considerations – repairability of rear rails will depend on the vehicle maker recommendations, extent and location of the damage, as well as the type of construction material, and accessibility for repairs. The floor and other panels limit access for repair. Internal rail reinforcements may not be accessible to properly straighten if damaged.
