Unless it is specified in the repair procedure, an access window should not be cut in the trunk floor to assist with straightening, sectioning, or replacing a rear rail. Cutting out a section of a part, then re-welding the removed section back in place, is considered sectioning and should not be done unless the vehicle maker has a procedure -Replacement and Sectioning Considerations – replacement of a complete rear rail assembly is usually a very involved procedure requiring substantial disassembly of the vehicle which includes interior trim, mechanical parts, and adjacent body components in some situations. Some vehicle makers service rear rail extensions which are installed at factory seams. Sectioning should only be performed following vehicle maker procedures. Considerations for sectioning the rear rail include possible removal of the trunk floor, removal of the rear crossmember, and multiple locations for sectioning, depending on the vehicle maker recommendations -Quarter Panel – parts of the quarter panel that should be inspected include the: -Outer panel -Wheelhouse -Inner panel -Fuel door -Filler neck -fuel label -Quarter glass -Note: When conducting your inspection, be sure to inspect the seams for separation and panel gaps. Removal of the rear bumper cover and rear lamps may be required to inspect for hidden damage. Check the air vents for damage and verify the proper operation of advanced vehicle systems, such as blind spot detection systems. Sensors for the blind spot detection system may be attached to the quarter panels -Repair – repairability of quarter panels will depend on the vehicle maker recommendations, extent and location of the damage, as well as construction material and accessibility for repairs. Metal panels can usually be repaired using common panel straightening tools and techniques. Foam or other materials may be located on the backside of the quarter panel and other inner panels. Foam location may limit backside access -Quarter Panel Replacement – determine if the panel can be sectioned or if it should be replaced at factory seams by considering the location and extent of damage, published procedures from the vehicle maker, and panel design. Replacing a quarter panel at factory seams is recommended by several vehicle makers. However, this may require replacing the roof panel depending on flange design -Additional Considerations – a replacement quarter panel may only be serviced as part of a uniside. This will require cutting the quarter panel section from the assembly at the specified cut locations. It may be necessary to remove and install undamaged glass during the replacement. Common sectioning locations can include the quarter glass or sail panel area, which is most common, at the belt line, and at the dog leg. Similar repair and replacement procedures may apply to cab corners on pickup trucks -Weld Bonding – weld bonding quarter panels is recommended by some vehicle makers. Weld bonding is the process of combining adhesives with spot welds or gas metal arc (GMA) welds. Refer to the body repair manual for guidelines on the location of adhesives and welds. If weld bonding using GMA welding, ensure that there are no adhesives in the weld area Damage Analysis of Unitized Structures – Rear (1/2) (2 of 2) Inspection of the Rear Unibody -Overview – a thorough and accurate damage analysis process for exterior body panels: -Must be established to ensure a complete and safe repair -Will help reduce supplements, which may also affect cycle time -Requires a good understanding and knowledge of repair and replacement options for parts -May also require making note of any suspected structural damage. this will indicate a further inspection is required before beginning repairs to help ensure there are no delays in the repair process -May help develop a repair plan based on the identified damage. the earlier that damage is identified, the more accurate the repair plan -Rear body inspection – when analyzing the rear structure for damage: -Start at the area of impact and work toward the front of the vehicle -Visualize the direction of impact -Use the same procedures as a front-end analysis when examining bumpers, lighting, and closure panels -Rear Bumper Considerations – -Considerations for a damaged rear bumper cover include checking: -Service information of the vehicle maker -Damage location -Type of damage -Severity of damage -Advanced driver assistance system (ADAS) as well as other technologies may affect repairs because some ADAS sensors are located in the rear. Damaged sensors require replacement and may require calibration depending on the vehicle maker procedures. Most sensors may be replaced independently. Replacement parking sensors may require refinishing to match the vehicle. Some vehicle makers do not recommend refinishing sensors because the excessive paint thickness may adversely affect the parking assist system. Some vehicle makers offer sensors that match the vehicle color. -Bumper Reinforcement Assembly – most vehicles have an impact pad and reinforcement behind the bumper cover. Molded plastic or foam impact pads must be replaced if damaged. Inspect the reinforcement assembly including the crush tubes for bends and correct lengths. Most rear bumper reinforcements are a separate bolt-on part. Construction materials may include steel, aluminum, or composites. A visual inspection may not be enough, because the damage may not be apparent initially. Consider the hardware, fasteners, electrical connections, and sensitive electronic parts potentially damaged in a collision -Lamp Assemblies and Hardware – in earlier model vehicles, check for broken or burned-out bulbs and damaged or corroded bulb sockets. Also, look for cracked or broken lenses and housings. This type of damage allows water and moisture inside and could lead to corrosion or part failure. Late model vehicles may have light emitting diode (LED) lamp assemblies. Inspect mounting locations and hardware for damage. in addition, some lamp assemblies may have additional sensors or ADAS parts that should be inspected -LED Lighting – some lamp assemblies use multiple LEDs for illuminating rear lamps. Inspect for damaged, loose, or corroded grounds and connectors. It may be necessary to partially disassemble the lamp assembly to determine the condition of the connectors and other electrical parts. Electrical wires for lamp assembles may be repairable following vehicle-maker service information. Connectors and repair wires and terminals may be available and serviced separately. Lighting problems related to rear collisions may include blown fuses, shorts, or opens in the circuits. They may set a DTC, MIL, or driver information system message. Ensure service parts meet vehicle-maker specifications to reduce post-repair lighting problems -Electrical Inspection – check for cut, pinched, or corroded wires. Electrical wires for lamp assemblies may be repairable following vehicle-maker service information. Connectors and repair wires and terminals may be available and serviced separately. Lighting problems related to rear collisions may include blown fuses, shorts, or opens in the circuits. They may set a DTC, MIL, or driver information system message. Ensure service parts meet vehicle-maker specifications to reduce post-repair lighting problems. -Closure Panel Considerations -Rear Closure Panels – the closure panels on the rear of the vehicles vary in design and construction material. Vehicle makers widely differ in what they include on the deck lid or lift gate -Closure Panel Construction – rear closure panels made of composites, aluminum, or high-strength steel present greater repair challenges compared to a mild steel part. Some rear closure panels may have magnesium inner frame which cannot be straightened or welded. Composite or plastic closure panels may permit outer panel repairs. However, if the inner frame is damaged, they are generally not repaired due to the design of the part -Closure Panel Inspection – when inspecting rear closure panels, look for signs of damage to the outer panel and inner frame. Inspect panel gaps and verify proper operation when opening and closing. Damage to a closure panel may cause the gaps to be off. Ensure any power open/close features are functional, as well as remote and hands-free operation. Inspect hinges, latches, locks, strikers, alignment guides, support struts, glass and trim for signs of damage. additional, this damage may affect the gaps of adjacent panels. Check the weatherstripping for proper sealing and be sure to verify operation of any attached accessories such as lighting, rear wipers, and cameras -Closure Panel Repair – repairability of rear closure panels depends on the construction material, extent, and location of damage, as well as access to the backside of the panel. This typically requires complete part replacement. Some vehicles are equipped with a rear spare tire carrier which is in close proximity or attached to the rear closure panel. Inspect the spare tire carrier and attachment points for damage -Closure Panel Replacement – most rear closure panels are serviced as an assembly. This panel attaches the left and right inner and outer quarter panel assemblies -Body Panel Construction – the construction of the panel may be made from multiple steel strengths, depending on vehicle design. For example, some vehicles have rear body panels made of boron-alloyed steel while other models may use mild steel. The rear body panel may be made of multiple panels and serviced as an assembly -Further Inspection – after vehicle disassembly, a more thorough inspection must be done to locate all the damage. rear body panels may have complex shapes to accommodate the rear closure panel, lights, bumper cover attachments, or weatherstripping. Inspect for any obvious physical damage to the rear body panel. Inspect gaps between the rear closure panels and adjacent panels. Look for any inconsistencies when inspecting the liftgate, hinges, and supports. Check for improper fit and operation which may include the rear body panel is damaged. Inspect gaps on tail lamp assemblies and the bumper cover which may also indicate problems with the rear body panel -Three-Dimensional Measuring – measurements is an important consideration during damage analysis. Three-dimensional (3-D) measuring systems help provide damage mapping to develop a repair plan, monitor repair progress, and provide documentation of vehicle measurements. 3-D measuring ensures the unitized structure is within specification indicated by the vehicle maker -Body Panel Repair – repairability of the rear body panel depends on the vehicle maker repair procedures, extent and location of the damage, as well as construction material and accessibility for repairs. However, UHSS rear body panels are replaced if damaged -Body Panel Replacement – rear body panels are usually replaced at factory seams unless the vehicle maker has a sectioning procedure available. Removal of the rear bumper cover and rear lamps may be required to inspect for all the damage. check the air vents for damage. verify the proper operation of ADAS and hands-free sensors which may be attached to the quarter panel lower extension. Refer to the service information for vehicle-specific requirements -Quarter Panel Repair – repairability of quarter panels depends on the availability of vehicle-maker repair procedures, extent and location of the damage, as well as construction material and accessibility for repairs. Metal panels can usually be repaired using common panel straightening tools and techniques. Foam or other materials may be located on the backside of the quarter panel and other inner panels. The foam location may limit backside access. Be aware that foam poses a fire hazard as well. -Quarter Panel Replacement – determine if the vehicle maker has a sectioning procedure available or if it should be replaced at factory seams. Consider the location and extent of damage, published procedures from the vehicle maker, and panel design. Replacing a quarter panel at factory seams is recommended by several vehicle makers. However, this replacement may require replacing the roof panel too, depending on the vehicle construction and flange design -Additional Considerations – a replacement quarter panel may only be serviced as part of a uniside. This requires cutting the new service part at specified cut locations. It may be necessary to remove and install undamaged glass during repairs. Common sectioning locations can include the belt line, the dog leg, the quarter glass opening, or sail panel area -Weld Bonding – some vehicle makers have repair procedures that use weld bonding to attach quarter panels. Weld bonding is the process of combining adhesives with squeeze-type resistance spot welding (STRSW) or gas metal arc (GMA) plug welds. Refer to the body repair manual for guidelines on location of the adhesive and welds. If you are GMA welding, check the vehicle makers information for how close the adhesive can be to a weld. Adhesive should only be applied where the vehicle maker had it originally. -Trunk Floor – trunk floors are generally constructed from a single layer of mild steel. The floor may be part of the rear rail structure. The floor may be part of the rear rail structure. This is because it contributes to the structural integrity of the rail and can affect how the rear rail collapses. For example, trunk floors often close out rear rail hat channels that are three-sided. The trunk floor may be one- or two-piece construction. Remember to inspect the trunk for any attaching parts and damage to those parts. The vehicle battery may be located in the trunk of some models and may require additional inspection -Trunk Floor Repair Considerations – to decide on the repairability of the trunk floor, you must reference the service information from the vehicle maker. In addition, consider the extent and location of the damage, the type of construction material, and the accessibility to complete repairs. Convolutions and compound shapes in the trunk floor are designed to absorb collision energy. These can limit repair in some situations. When determining possible replacement procedures for the trunk floor, note that some vehicle makers have sectioning procedures that may allow for a less intrusive repair. Some vehicles have a composite spare tire well that is bonded into the vehicle floor. Repairs may be possible using approved plastic repair techniques, depending on the extent of the damage. adhesive is required for complete part replacement -Rear Floors on SUVs and Minivans – sport utility vehicles (SUV) and minivans have a rear floor pan instead of a trunk. The large rear door opening allows easy access to the rear floor pan. This may reduce the number of adjacent parts that require removal when replacing the floor compared to a trunk floor in a typical passenger vehicle. Some SUVs and minivans have storage tubs integrated into the floor as a storage place for the seat when it is folded down. This storage tub may be bonded into the floor by the vehicle maker. If damaged beyond repair, the replacement tub is bonded using the adhesive recommended by the vehicle maker -Rear Underbody – the rear underbody structure on a unibody vehicle deserves careful inspection and repair planning -Rear Rails – the rear rails and crossmember form the rear unibody structure on a unibody vehicle. The rear rails also support the floor pan and trunk floor. When planning a repair, always follow vehicle-maker procedures which could include weld bonding or rivet bonding -Rail Construction – steel rear rails can be made from various strengths, depending on vehicle design, and may be closed out by a rear body panel, floor pan, and bumper bracket. The rails may be made from tailor-welded blankets and may be available as a complete sub-assembly. The same sub-structure designs apply to aluminum rear rails. in some configurations, the rear rails serve as the attachment point for a trailer hitch -Rail Repair Considerations – repairability of rear frame rails depends on the repair procedures of the vehicle maker. In addition, you must consider the extent and location of the damage as well as the type of construction material and accessibility for repairs. The floor and other adjacent panels may limit access for repair. Internal rail reinforcements may not be accessible to properly straighten if damaged. An access window should not be cut in the trunk floor to assist with straightening, sectioning, or replacing a rear rail -Rail Replacement and Sectioning – replacement of a complete rear rail assembly can be a very involved procedure. Replacement requires substantial disassembly of the vehicle which includes interior parts, mechanical parts, and adjacent body parts in some situations. Some vehicle makers have rear rail extensions which can be installed at factory seams. Sectioning should only be performed following the current published procedure of the vehicle maker. Considerations for sectioning the rear rail can include the possible removal of the trunk floor, removal of the rear crossmember, and multiple locations for sectioning Determining Overall Vehicle Repairability Condition of the Vehicle -Introduction to Determining Overall Vehicle Repairability – when completing vehicle inspections and preparing damage estimates, it will sometimes become necessary to determine the actual cash value (ACV) of the vehicle so it can be determined if the vehicle is repairable, or should be considered a total loss. When inspecting a vehicle that appears to be approaching the total loss threshold, there are several additional requirements to consider including: -Additional photo requirements to support vehicle condition and any unrelated prior damages -Obtaining complete and accurate vehicle ID information -Obtaining advance charges -Protecting the salvage -Completing the necessary vehicle condition/valuation request form -Photo Documentation – when inspecting a vehicle and preparing an estimate of damage, there are typically standard photos required, such as four corner shots, license plate, VIN, close up photos of the damage, etc. However, when it is determined a vehicle may be approaching the total loss threshold, there may be additional photos required to support overall vehicle condition, options, and any unrelated prior damage. It is important to understand the photo documentation requirements for your company to remember to obtain all necessary photos to avoid the need to return the vehicle to obtain additional photos that were not taken at the time of vehicle inspection. -Complete and Accurate Vehicle Identification – while inspecting the vehicle, it is important to completely and accurately identify the vehicle, including mileage, options, trim packages, aftermarket accessories, major mechanical upgrades, and tire tread depth measurements. Inaccurate or incomplete identification of the vehicle can have significant impact on the vehicle valuation. Mistakes in the vehicle identification and condition rating process will result in overpayment of the claim or undervaluing the customer’s vehicle, resulting in a dissatisfied customer who feels their insurance company is not treating them fairly. -Preparing an Unrelated Prior Damage Estimate – when inspecting a vehicle, it is important to differentiate between claim-related damages and unrelated prior damages. When unrelated prior damage is identified, it should always be documented with a fully itemized estimate of all unrelated damage. This estimate can then, based on company and/or vendor policy, be used to determine the appropriate adjustment to the vehicle value prior to making a settlement offer. -Secure Advance Charges – any time a vehicle is inspected and it is determined the vehicle may be a total loss, the advance chargers should be obtained. These charges may include towing, storage charge to date, teardown fees, etc. It is also important to obtain the storage accrual rate, which is typically a “per-day” expense. The storage rates charged per day are typically determined by the facility and could differ from one location to the next. -Protecting the Salvage – when it is determined a vehicle may be considered a total loss, it is important to take the appropriate steps to protect the salvage. This will help to ensure the highest possible returns when the salvage is sold. In most cases, this will require working with the repair shop or towing facility to ensure all areas sensitive to moisture are covered. In the event the vehicle was disassembled, keep all parts that have been removed with the vehicle, so they can be included in the sale of the salvage. Determining Actual Cash Value (ACV) -Determining Actual Cash Value (ACV) – when making a decision regarding overall vehicle repairability, repair vs. total loss, it is necessary to determine the ACV of the vehicle. This may be done a few different ways, but the most common are using a third-party vendor who will provide a valuation report, or using a combination of preferred publications such as NADA, Red Book, Blue Book, etc. in either case, the vehicle identification information submitted on the vehicle condition/valuation request form will play a significant role in establishing the vehicle ACV -Confirming Vehicle ID Information with the Owner – when completing a condition report, it is a good practice to contact the vehicle owner to review the current mileage, options, trim packages, any major mechanical upgrades, and prior unrelated damages noted during the vehicle inspection. This will help to ensure the vehicle identification information is complete and accurate. This will also help to ensure everyone is on the same page, which will help to avoid frustration for the vehicle owner resulting from an inaccurate valuation of their vehicle due to missed options or disagreements on condition ratings. -Completing a Condition Report – -When completing a vehicle condition/valuation request form it is important to know the condition rating system used by the vendor you are working with. Some valuation vendors may use condition ratings such as: -Major wear -Normal wear -Dealer ready -Exceptional -Other vendors may use condition ratings such as: -Excellent -Above average -Average -Below average -Poor -Note: knowing the condition rating system used by your preferred vendor and how to properly rate the vehicle condition within their system is critical to valuing the vehicle property -Reviewing the Valuation Report – when the final ACV report is received from the preferred vendor, it is important to review the report in detail to ensure accuracy. Some things to consider: -Is the customer vehicle information correct? For example, VIN, year, make, model, options, and condition -Is the information for any comparable vehicles used to support the valuation correct? -Are any adjustments made to the value of the comparable vehicles correct? For example, if the customer vehicle is a 2017 Corvette with 25,000 miles and a comparable vehicle found and used for this report has 50,000 miles, there would typically be a mileage adjustment to the value of the comparable vehicle to offset the mileage variance. Note: It is important to review the report in detail to ensure the vehicle information used to determine the ACV is accurate before making a repairability decision. -Using Preferred Publications – In some cases, the preferred method for determining vehicle ACV may include the use of one or more industry accepted valuation publications, such as NADA or Red Book. When using vehicle valuation publications, arriving at the ACV will usually include obtaining the published ACV from two published sources including all appropriate adjustments for vehicle options, mileage, and condition. In addition, this may require obtaining dealer quotes on similar vehicles available on their lot for sale, and then calculating an average value based on the established ACV from the publications and dealer quotes. Determining Repairability -Total Loss Matrix – in some cases, there may be an additional document that must be completed to show the calculations used to make the decision to consider a vehicle a total loss: The Total Loss Matrix. This form will typically include a calculation that may include some of the following items: -Salvage value – this may require contacting local salvage buyers to obtain bids for the salvage vehicle. There are also outside vendors that allow you to enter the vehicle information (VIN, Year, Make, Model, etc.) and based on this information will provide an anticipated salvage value. -Damage Estimate Value – this is simply the repair estimate total -Estimated Rental Costs – this is typically a formula based on the estimated number of days the owner would be in a rental multiplied by an agreed rental reimbursement rate -Advance Charges – this would be the total of any towing and storage expenses -Note: The total of the items would then be divided by the ACV to show the fully loaded cost of repair vs. ACV for the vehicle. (Hint: discovery questions are not yet or no questions) Why is safety so important to you Electronic Braking and Stability Control Systems Overview (1/2) Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS) ABS Operation -Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS) Purpose – during vehicle disassembly and repairs it is important to identify any damage to anti-lock brake system (ABS), traction control system (TCS), stability control system (SCS) or any related systems parts so the vehicle is returned to the customer in a safe condition. A non-structural technician does not typically complete brake or electronic systems related work, but this does not mean they should not have some familiarity with the parts, operation, and basic damage inspection knowledge as they may encounter undocumented damage or issues during vehicle disassembly and repairs. When conducting a visual inspection look for damaged wires, connectors, sensors, lines, hoses, modules, valves, pump motors or other system parts damage, and scan the system for diagnostic trouble codes. * If there are any unrelated issues or service concerns, this should be documented -Basic ABS Operation – an anti-lock braking system (ABS): helps the driver maintain steering control during hard braking, reduces hydroplaning during braking, reduces flat-spotting of tires during panic stops. Anti-lock brakes can release and reapply brake fluid pressure rapidly to axles or individual wheels. This control of brake fluid pressure is also known as modulation. With an ABS, stopping distances may not be shorter than with conventional braking systems, and it is not possible to diagnose ABS problems by judging brake pedal feel -ECM Interaction – an ABS has wheel speed sensors and an electronic control module (ECM) or ABS module, which monitors the rotation speed of each wheel. When the wheels begin to lock up, the ECM sends a signal to the hydraulic system that reduces braking pressure to that wheel. When the wheel starts turning again, brake pressure is reapplied. This happens many times per second and produces an action similar to manually pumping the brakes, except much faster, Front wheel speed sensors in the center of the front wheels. ECM under the dash. Rear wheel speed sensors in the center of the rear wheels -Wheel Slip – wheel slip is measured as a percentage between 0% and 100%. When a wheel is rolling freely, this is 0%, and it is 100% when a wheel is locked and not rotating. At 100% slip, the brakes stop the tire, but not the vehicle and direction steering is lost. Maximum braking occurs when wheel slip is between 0% and 20% At the end of the areas top n right. It is 100% On the left is Force On the right is Slip Ratio The ideal slip ratio is 20% -Brake Lockup – brake lockup occurs when the brakes stop the wheel and tire assembly, but not the vehicle, and the friction of the brake surfaces exceeds the friction of the tire to the road surface. This causes the tire to slip across the road surface, resulting in increased stopping distance and reduced steering control. Factors that contribute to brake lockup include: -Tire design, condition, and inflation -Driver pressure on the brake pedal -Road surface (blacktop, concrete, gravel, dirt) -Conditions affecting the road surface such as oil, water, or ice -Vehicle weight. The heavier the vehicle, the harder it is to stop -Vehicle weight transfer -ABS Types – there are a few different anti-lock braking system types used on modern vehicles -Four-Channel – four channel ABS: -Uses four wheel speed sensors, one at each wheel -Can regulate brake pressure at each wheel individually -Provides the best straight-line stability and steering control -Rear-Wheel – rear-wheel ABS: -Regulates pressure equally to the rear wheels only -Uses one rear speed sensor. The rear speed sensor is mounted in the transmission, differential housing, or transfer case -Improves straight-line stability in panic stops -Does not prevent loss of vehicle steering because of front-wheel lockup -Is often used on pickup trucks and vans. Rear ABS can also help reduce the effects of varying load and weight transfer on these vehicles -Four-Wheel, Three-Channel – four-wheel, three-channel ABS is most commonly found on rear drive automobiles and light duty trucks and vans. Four-wheel, three-channel ABS: -Uses two front wheel speed sensors -May use one or two rear wheel speed sensors -Regulates front wheel brakes individually -Regulates pressure equally to the rear wheels -Provides straight-line stability and steering control -An ABS is made up of several parts. -Modulator Valves, Fluid Pump, Relays, and Pressure Switches -Modulator Valves – sometimes known as ABS valves, provide brake pressure modulation for each of the individual wheel circuits -Fluid Pumps – transfer fluid from the brake calipers back to the master cylinder -Relays – are used by the ECM to control power to the fluid pump, solenoids, and motors in the modulator valve -Pressure Switches – signal the ECM of pressure differences in the ABS -Wheel Speed Sensors and Tone Rings – wheel speed sensors and tone rings create a signal used by the ECM to determine wheel speed -Accumulator (if present) – ABS with an accumulator will store hydraulic fluid under pressure -Diagnostic Connectors – diagnostic connectors are points where test equipment connections are made -Warning Lamps – warning lamps inform the driver of problems in the brake system or ABS -Electronic Control Module (ECM) – during braking, the ECM monitors wheel speed and sends commands to the hydraulic modulator -Integral and Non-Integral ABS – anti-lock braking systems are classified into two general categories, non-integral or “add-on” ABS, and integral ABS. -Non-Integral ABS – non-integral ABS uses: -A conventional brake master cylinder and power assist assembly -A separate hydraulic modulator that uses solenoids or quick-acting motors to modulate brake fluid -Non-integral ABS may also use a separate fluid pump to create high pressure for the ABS, and often mount the hydraulic modulator and fluid pump assemblies on the front frame rails -Integral ABS – integral ABS can be identified by a special hydraulic unit. This unit may have a visible accumulator, but does not have a vacuum booster. The hydraulic unit performs the same function as a conventional master cylinder and brake power assist assembly. On integral systems, power assist is available even if the ABS modulation system is disabled. The necessary power assist is created by the use of -An accumulator (pressure storage) -A fluid pump -Integral ABS systems are not very common. Non-integral systems are the most common ABS Damage Analysis -Modulator Damage – integral and non-integral modulator unit circuits may become collision damaged. Problems may include physical damage, as many vehicle makers locate non-integral modulator units on fender aprons, frame rails, or radiator core support areas. Problems may include -Cut or shorted wires -Damaged connectors -Damaged mounting pads and brackets -Damaged lines and brake fluid leaks -* Warning lamps may illuminate and ABS module trouble codes will generally be stored if damage has occurred to ABS parts -Fluid Pump and Accumulator Damage – fluid pump circuits and accumulators may be damaged during a collision -Inspection – collision damage to fluid pump circuits and accumulators may include: -Physical damage. On integral systems, the fluid pump and accumulator are generally part of the special hydraulic unit near the cowl. -Brake fluid leaks. Hoses and lines may be damaged, and sealing washers should be replaced -Damaged mounting pads. Many fluid pump units are rubber mounted to control noise and vibration -Damaged wires, relays, or switches that cause motors not to run, or to run continuously. Most ABS motors are not designed for continuous operation. If a motor runs continuously, damage to the motor or batteries may result -* Fluid pumps and accumulators on non-integral systems maybe mounted on front frame rails, radiator core supports, and rear frame rails -Personal Safety – any damage to the brake lines or modulator valve may trap pressure anywhere in the hydraulic system. Use caution and be aware of the location of collision damage and how the damage may have affected the hydraulic system -Wheel Speed Sensor Inspection – speed sensor circuits may be damaged during a collision -Visual Inspection – when wheel speed sensors are damaged, the warning lamp may present problems that include -Physical damage to the speed sensor -Damaged wire connectors -Cut or shorted sensor wires and shielding -Cracked tone ring or missing teeth -Bent steering or spindle parts, causing improper sensor air gap -The permanent magnet losing its magnetism due to the impact -Trouble codes being set by using different diameter tires (such as a space-saving spare tire) -Metal filings or grease sticking to the sensor. This will cause improper signals -Electrical Testing – visual inspection will find most collision-related speed sensor circuit problems. Some systems may require connecting a scan tool to monitor speed sensor circuits. Additionally, DVOMs may be used for electrical testing of speed sensor circuits to measure the following -Continuity between the sensor wires and ground. There should be no continuity -Continuity between the shielded wires and ground. There should be continuity -Resistance of the wire coil. Resistance should match the service manual specification -AC voltage output of the sensor while rotating the wheel. Output should match the service manual specification -* Speed sensor wires may be twisted or shielded to reduce unwanted signal interference -Relay Damage – relay circuits may be damaged during a collision or the repair process. Problems may include: -Physical damage. Relay clusters may be located on the fender aprons or radiator core support areas -Cut or shorted wires to either the coil or contact circuits -Poor terminal contacts due to corrosion or paint overspray -Wires backed out of connectors -* Visual inspection of relay circuits will locate most collision-related problems, but electrical testing may also be required -Brake Fluid Contamination – brake fluid absorbs moisture if the system is open to air or the environment. Moisture contamination will reduce the boiling point of the brake fluid and may cause a loss of brakes in operation. Moisture can also cause corrosion of system parts that may lead to failure. Brake fluid should be visually inspected and tested for contamination. Brake fluid may also be contaminated by the breakdown of rubber brake parts and improper fluid being added to the system Electronic Braking and Stability Control Systems Overview (2/2) TCS Overview and Operation -Traction Control Overview – traction control occurs when the ECM senses that one or more of the drive wheels are rotating at a faster rate than the slowest non-drive wheel. When this happens, the ECM sends a signal to the powertrain control module (PCM) to reduce the amount of torque to the drive wheels. If the torque reduction is not effective in bringing the slipping wheels under control, then the ECM applies brake pressure to the spinning drive wheels until traction is regained -Brake Activation – if the ECM determines that braking pressure is required at the drive wheels, the ECM closes a valve in the brake system. This isolates the master cylinder from the hydraulic brake circuit that leads to the drive wheels. Then, a second valve opens to allow pressure from the ABS fluid pump and/or accumulator into the hydraulic brake circuit, and directs it to where braking pressure is required. The drive wheel ABS circuit solenoids are then energized to allow brake pressure to increase, hold, or decrease -Traction Control Systems Parts – traction control systems use many of the ABS parts. These parts include the: -ABS/Modulator valves -Fluid pump -ECM -Wheel speed sensors -* The signals received from the wheel speed sensors are similar to the signals received during an anti-lock braking event. The information is used by the ECM in a different program to aid in acceleration (traction control) instead of deceleration (anti-lock braking) -Traction Control Deactivation – the driver may switch off the traction control system if desired. Once deactivated, the system remains off until the next ignition cycle, or until turned back on by the driver. This switch may be located on the: -Center console -Automatic transmission range selector lever (if so equipped) -Instrument panel -* When deactivated by the driver, the TCS will display a “TRACTION OFF” message on the instrument panel, or on a center console warning lamp. The message or lamp will remain lit until the system is returned to operation -Four-Wheel and All-Wheel Drive TCS – (this page was in Spanish on I-car, this will be broken English, I translated) on some four-wheel drive models, off-wheel drive road is managed by a traction control system that uses the brakes. ABS to slow down the drive wheel or wheels. With these systems specialized, it is possible to apply motor torque only to wheels that have traction. On some all-wheel drive models, traction is monitored and controlled using limited slip differentials with clutch packs electronically controlled that send torque to the wheels with traction. Some vehicles use these systems as an acceleration aid and only reduce torque as one of the last steps in the system’s attempt to improve traction. -Throttle Motor – the throttle motor works with the throttle position sensor to: -Control air flow into the engine -Replace the traditional mechanical throttle cable or linkage -Reduce torque output during traction-control events by closing the throttle. This is directed by the PCM. -* This configuration is sometimes called a drive-by-wire system. In a drive-by-wire throttle system, the throttle opening is adjusted by the PCM by varying the throttle motor signal -Mechanical Throttle Linkage – on a vehicle with a mechanical throttle linkage, the PCM adjusts the throttle opening using a specialized throttle-angle adjusting motor connected in series with the throttle cable. With a mechanical throttle linkage, the driver will feel a kickback in the accelerator pedal during a traction control event. -Indicator and Warning Lamps – there may be one or more indicator and warning lamps to show system status. These include the following -TCS OFF. This message is displayed continuously, as a reminder to the driver, when the traction control system is turned off -TCS ON. This indicator lamp will come on during vehicle start as part of the traction system self-test. It cycles on, then off with other indicator lamps and may be an icon. -TCS Active. This lamp will come on while traction control is required. The lamp may read “SLIP” or “LOW TRAC” to represent a slippery condition. -Service TCS. This message is displayed if a malfunction of the traction control system is detected by the ECM SCS Overview and Operation -Stability Control Systems – a skid happens when a vehicle exceeds the traction limits of the vehicle’s tires to the road surface. Stability control systems (SCS) assist the vehicle with maintaining control during a skid by reducing the amount of wheel slip during acceleration, and in harsh driving conditions. SCS also assists the vehicle with smoother starts and acceleration through all speed ranges without wheel-spin or fishtailing, while reducing the amount of oversteer and understeer when cornering. -Sensor Function – to determine what is happening during a skid, sensors provide input to the ECM. These sensors include the steering angle sensor, the wheel speed sensors, a yaw rate sensor, lateral accelerometers, a brake pressure sensor, and a throttle position sensor -Electronic Control Module (ECM) Function – the ECM is programmed to look for a vehicle’s correct path while steering through a corner. If the information being sent to the ECM does not match this programmed data, the computer determines that the vehicle is in a skid. Brake pressure is then applied by the computer to one or more of the wheel circuits to correct the vehicle’s skid. The ABS/TCS ECM is used to support SCS functions, these systems are networked with other vehicle systems to provide complete functionality. If there is a problem with one system, the others will likely be affected. -Yaw Rate Sensors – yaw rate sensors measure a vehicle’s actual turn rate. These sensors may be located under the rear package shelf, in the center console, or under the driver’s seat. When the vehicle changes direction of travel the sensor creates an output signal that tells the ECM the rate of change in direction, or yaw. -Steering Angle Sensor – the steering angle sensor measures the steering wheel position relative to straight ahead. It may be self-centering, or have to be calibrated after any steering alignment work is performed. Some models also measure the rate (speed) that the steering wheel turns. The steering angle sensor is commonly built into the clock spring assembly, but may be a separate part on some vehicles. -Lateral Accelerometer – a lateral accelerometer is very similar to an airbag system impact sensor. The lateral accelerometer sends a precise reading to the ECM that represents the cornering force that the vehicle is experiencing. The ECM uses the lateral acceleration information along with the steering wheel position and wheel speed information to calculate the desired yaw or turn rate. This is compared to the yaw rate data to determine if the vehicle is in an out of control situation. Lateral acceleration sensors are commonly located in the passenger cabin, but may be in other locations on the vehicle. Most vehicles use two lateral acceleration sensors Front Wheel Speed Sensors – center front wheels ABS Module – left side of underhood ECM – right side of underhood Lateral Accelerometer - driver seat area Rear Wheel Speed Sensors – center rear wheels -Skid Categories – skid can be divided into two categories: understeer and oversteer -Understeer – understeer occurs when a driver attempts to turn and the front tires begin to slip. The front wheels are turned, but the vehicle does not respond and continues on its original course. On front-wheel drive vehicles this may be caused by the drive wheels spinning too fast for the corner (too much throttle). Front-wheel drive vehicles can also understeer if a corner is entered too fast and the throttle opens abruptly. When a front-wheel drive vehicle understeers, the skid control system instructs the ECM to open or close the throttle, depending on the torque applied to the drive wheels. When a rear-wheel drive vehicle understeers, the skid control system instructs the ECM to reduce the throttle before engaging the appropriate brake circuit to correct the slide -Oversteer – oversteer occurs when a vehicle is turning and the rear tires begin to slide away from the direction of the turn. This occurs most often on rear-wheel drive vehicles with slipping drive wheels. When a rear-wheel drive vehicle oversteers, the skid control system instructs the ECM to open or close the throttle depending on whether the drive wheels have too much or too little torque applied for the situation -Indicator and Warning Lamps – the stability control system will have a dash lamp to indicate system status. A variety of additional safety systems are available based on the foundations of ABS, TCS, and SCS. With additional sensors, systems programming and networking of systems, vehicle makers may incorporate roll over protection, hill start assist, hill decent control, brake force distribution, as well as other safety and convenience options. Depending on options, a variety of additional controls, switches, dash indicator lamps and message center announcements may accompany these features. Pre and post scans will help identify system issues. * Reviewing the vehicles owners manual will help identify the different controls, dash indicators, messages, and systems Electric Vehicle (EV) Damage Analysis -Introduction – EV’s continue to gain popularity with more models entering the market each year. While there are many similarities, there are in most cases unique differences that may affect damage analysis and repair operation requirements. This course examines EV damage analysis using a generic approach compiling a broad spectrum of considerations that embrace the majority of EV systems. The information is not intended to replace vehicle-specific information resources, which should always be included when performing damage analysis and repairs on any vehicle. Having a good understanding of the high-voltage (HV) systems and parts, damage types, repair options, and service considerations can make for a more efficient analysis -Safety Precautions – determine if the vehicle is safe to start the estimate process. If there is battery damage, determine if the vehicle needs to be quarantined. Do not touch the vehicle unless the system is shut down or a qualified EV specialist technician gives the approval and puts it in a safe mode. Some vehicles may display that the HV systems have been deactivated on the instrument cluster. If there is any doubt about the integrity of the HV systems, DO NOT touch the vehicle. Wait until it has been inspected by a trained EV technician, and determined to be safe or is placed in a safe state. This is done by shutting down the HV system. There is always high voltage in the battery -Inspection Precautions – EV inspection, service, and repair procedures require certain safety precautions before starting any repairs or removal of HV parts. These are found in the service information and must be read before performing any type of work. These safety precautions cover personal protection and handling of HV parts. Do not make assumptions based on repairs of other vehicles. There are tasks that have safety considerations, like moving the vehicle. Some vehicle makers may have recommendations to move the vehicle with wheel dollies so the drive wheels do not generate electrical energy or damage an HV part. Not all EVs are the same, so make sure you follow each vehicle maker’s repair procedures. Understand the location of all HV system parts on the vehicle. The charging port is important for safety and damage assessment purposes. Note: Anytime it is connected to a charger, the vehicle’s HV system is active. -High-Voltage Shutdown-isolate the HV when performing an inspection on the vehicle. During inspections on EVs, if possible, perform a pre-scan before shutting down the HV system. There are multiple ways to shut off the high voltage. Let’s take a look at some of these in more detail -High-Voltage Shutdown Using Ignition – turning off the ignition generally shuts down the HV system if there are no HV system faults, and the vehicle is not being charged. Be aware that if the HV battery needs to be cooled or warmed, the HV system may automatically energize to perform these functions even with the ignition off. When the vehicle is plugged in for charging, the HV system is energized even though it may not appear to be. -High-Voltage Shutdown Using Other Methods – generally, if the low-voltage (12 V) battery is disconnected, the HV system will be shut down, isolating the HV to the battery only. Some vehicles shut off the high voltage when the 12 V battery is disconnected. Additional HV shutdown measures may include low-voltage control circuit disconnects under the hood and an HV battery safety disconnect. Note: while the 12 V systems are active, remote apps and scheduled climate control operations may power up the HV systems -Charging Provisions – Evs are similar to gasoline vehicles that enter a repair facility. Some may be full of gas, others not so much. In the case of EVs, they may be fully charged while others may not. Its important to think about having charging provisions in case the vehicle needs to be charge for: -Pre-and post-scans -Diagnostics -Road tests -Any repair that may need the vehicle to be powered -Some vehicles may come equipped with a portable 120 V charger that can be connected to a 120 V outlet -Work Authorization Procedures – some vehicle makers may require work authorization procedures that require a daily signature from an EV specialist. This needs to be done every day the vehicle is in the repair facility, even after the HV has been shut down, and HV systems are not being directly affected. -Pre-Scan – pre-scanning of an EV may reveal codes and help identify problems with the high-voltage battery isolation faults or other unique issues that do not exist with conventional vehicles. It is important to identify all potential problems that may exist early in the damage analysis process. Faults in the HV system may require specialized test equipment and procedures that can only be performed by trained EV specialists -Causes of Isolation Faults – isolation faults may be caused by: -Internal insulation breakdown -Moisture intrusion -cable or connector damage -Other sources within the HV system modules or parts -Diagnosing these conditions requires an exceptional knowledge of the vehicle systems, specialized tools and equipment, and specialty training on the specific vehicle. These are usually relegated to an authorized service center -Fault Conditions – some fault conditions may not be identified by DTCs. They are only detected by proper inspection and measuring equipment and techniques. One example is electrical bonding faults -High-Voltage Parts -Inverter/Converter – the drive motor requires three-phase, HV alternating current (AC) power to operate. The inverter takes the HV direct current (DC) power supplied by the HV battery and changes it to HV three-phase AC power. This is based on the driver request or cruise control setting. The converter function is to change AC from the motor during regenerative braking to DC to charge the battery. This module contains capacitors that store DC power for a short period of time after the HV system is shut down. It may be charged by turning the drive wheels in some situations even with the HV system shut down. These are typically mounted on the motor or in very close proximity to. Inspect for direct and indirect damage to the part, connectors, cables, coolant lines, and hoses -Electric Motor – the electric-drive motor is attached to the gear reduction unit that drives the axles and wheels. It may also contain parts of the HV system that are connected to it. Electric motors still need coolant to cool parts and oils to lubricate. Inspect the parts for: -Damage -Mounting locations -Damaged or loose connectors -Cables -Ground cables or straps -Coolant and oil leaks -Any other type of visual damage -The motor and gear reduction unit may need to be removed as a modular assembly to provide access when doing some body structure repairs. Similar to a conventional vehicle, EVs may require specific procedures, tools, and equipment to facilitate the repair. There are additional tasks in the event that the motor and drive gear assembly need to be removed. This varies by specific vehicle configuration -DC/DC Converter – the DC/DC converter takes HV DC and changes it to 12-14 V for low-voltage systems, control modules, accessories, etc. The DC/DC converter is usually under the hood but may be mounted anywhere on the vehicle. On some vehicles, it may be in a more damage vulnerable location. Inspect for damage, loose or broken connectors, cables, coolant leaks, and other visible conditions. Make sure the HV system is shut down and the vehicle is safe to work on before touching any of the parts -Charge Port – charging port locations vary. Some may be on the fender or quarter panel and others in the front. there is usually a charge port opening button on the dash and a manual or alternative opening method. These are identified in the owner’s manual or service information. Due to their exterior panel mounting location, it may be damaged in relatively minor collisions. Inspect the ports for direct or indirect damage. make sure the caps are not missing or damaged, as these may prevent the vehicle from engaging the charge connector. The HV cables connect the port to an on-board charging module or may connect directly to the HV battery. If there is any suspected damage to the charging port or cables, ensure the HV system is shut down before inspection or disassembly. When the charging cable is connected, HV may be present at the port. Excess moisture, water, or other potentially conductive contaminants may present a safety hazard -High-Voltage Battery Pack – the HV battery stores a large amount of electrical energy in the battery cells. The on-board, battery-monitoring system ensures the cells are maintained within their operating temperature and voltage during the vehicle operation. Inspect the battery enclosure for any signs of damage or other abnormalities to determine if service or replacement is needed. Not all HV batteries are the same. Therefore, specific procedures must be followed if removal is required. Some batteries are air-cooled while others may be liquid-cooled. If removing a battery that is liquid-cooled, the coolant needs to be drained or the coolant circuits isolated. Heaters may be built into the battery or located externally. Valves and pumps control temperature and coolant circulation -High-Voltage Battery Considerations – battery removal requires specialized equipment. This may include a lift platform table or special adjustable mobile battery fixtures. Some vehicles may require specific lift adapters to permit battery clearance -Service Operations – there are very limited service operations that a collision repair facility performs on the HV battery. These procedures are usually limited to a service part that does not require removing the large battery enclosure cover. Specialty trained, HV service technicians may be able to service some internal HV battery parts. This requires advanced training and a variety of unique tools and equipment. An authorized service center should be consulted as to what is possible -Sublet Considerations – vehicle makers may require the vehicle be transported to an authorized service center for any: -HV system inspection -HV shutdown -Battery removal -Other HV-related system work -Other Considerations – most of the hardware that attaches the battery to the vehicle structure is accessed from under the vehicle. However, there may be requirements to remove some interior parts, such as seats or other interior trim to access bolts that need to be removed. Generally, if the battery pack is damaged, complete replacement is required -Additional Operations – during the removal and installation of HV parts, additional operations may need to be conducted with the main procedure. This may include the removal of body, electrical, and mechanical parts to gain access to the HV parts. Another example is during the installation of HV system parts, the vehicle maker may require testing of the electrical bonding. A milliohm meter is used to perform this test. Make sure to follow the vehicle maker procedures to ensure all required tasks are performed. Some of these required procedures may require specialty training, tools, and equipment. Some of these may only be available at authorized service centers -Body and Structure Repairs – body and structure damage can affect how a repair is performed and if other tasks related to high voltage need to be performed. For example, if a vehicle has side collision damage, you will need to determine if the battery or other parts need to be removed for full access to conduct repairs -Special Tools – there may be special tools that are needed to perform tasks related to removal or testing of high-voltage parts. These may include the following: -Battey lift table -Fixture table for battery -Alignment dowels -Specialty test harnesses -Connector block-off plugs -Lock out, tag out devices -Battery cavity pressure tester -Milliohm meter -HV insulation tester -SRS – with certain collision situations, sensors on-board the vehicle detect impact and may shut down the high-voltage system. This is usually in collision events that deploy SRS parts. There may be a reset procedure that requires using a scan tool or other means. Some vehicles may have a pyrotechnic disconnect device that is: -Located in the HV battery or externally -Designed to isolate the HV battery -Accessible from the interior of the vehicle by removing an access cover in the floor and/or battery or may be located under the hood -Considered a one-time use device -Review and follow the specific vehicle service information for these one-time-use parts -Low-Voltage Battery- if a low-voltage battery needs replacement, the vehicle is likely to have a specific battery type and rating. Some vehicle makers may require specific batteries. These may be lithium, absorbed glass mat (AGM), or flooded cell. Make sure to replace with the current one because the charging strategy is based on the specific type. There may be additional battery registration procedures that require a capable diagnostic tool be used. This ensures the replacement battery is managed properly and is not over- or under-charged. Note: shutting down the low-voltage system shuts down the high-voltage system. It also isolates all HV to the HV battery, unless contactor faults are present -HVAC – the heating, ventilation, and air condition system )HVAC) in EVs function similar to its gasoline counterparts. There may be additional parts used to provide heat or cooling to some of the HV systems. Proper identification of these systems is important to provide a more accurate estimate and order the correct parts. Lets take a look at some of these in more detail -A/C System – similar to some hybrid vehicles, EVs use an electric compressor pump. It requires the correct service equipment and oil when service is performed. This avoids system contamination and possible HV isolation loss faults. The A/C may also tie into the cooling system via a chiller block. Chiller blocks are refrigerant-to-coolant heat exchangers used to reduce the temperature of the coolant for the high-voltage system. Depending on the part being replaced, the A/C refrigerant may need to be evacuated. Follow the vehicle maker procedures to ensure the HV and other systems do not get affected by poor service -Cooling System- EV cooling systems may use multiple cooling circuits with valves, pumps, hoses, lines, sensors, and other parts to manage temperatures in different parts of the vehicle and HV system. This is vital to the operation of the HV system. The cooling system may also be used to heat the high-voltage battery and cab. To cool the high voltage, the cooling system uses what some call a chiller heat exchanger block. Air-cooled batteries may use internal heaters in the HV battery -Cooling System Inspection – inspect the coolant reservoirs for loss of coolant, as this may be an indication of internal or external leaks. A pressure test may be performed on the cooling system to identify leaks. If the cooling system is low or empty and there is no sign of an external leak, coolant may be leaking into the HV battery enclosure creating a hazardous condition -Heating System – a coolant heater is used to warm the HV battery when ambient temperatures drop below certain levels. Inspect these parts for damage or leaks. Some vehicles have a safety built in so that if the coolant is low, the vehicle will eventually shut down. It is important to know how the A/C system is used for more than just cooling the cab. It may also be used to heat the passenger cabin if it is a heat-pump-type system. Battery and cabin heat may be provided by a heating module referred to as a positive-temperature coefficient (PTC) heater. The PTC heater has hoses connected to the coolant circuit and HV connectors for power. Some cabin heaters may use a grid-style PTC heater core. PTC heaters may be located on collision-vulnerable-locations on some vehicles -Other EV Damage Conditions – water intrusion into the HV battery and motor-drive unit due to flood damage is possible and requires part replacement. If water enters the battery enclosure, it may lead to HV fault conditions, corrosion, or other problems. While the risk of electric shock is minimal, risks do exist with flood damaged vehicle if not handled properly. Do not touch any of the HV system parts or remove the HV service disconnect if any water is present. The vehicle should be inspected, evaluated, and the HV system disabled and isolated by a trained HV specialist. Rodents may damage HV cables. This requires replacement as there are no repairs allowed -Post-Repair Scan – a post-repair scan ensures that all issues were fixed, and that there are no remaining DTCs. A test drive is also necessary to make sure everything is performing as it should after a repair. During the post-repair scan, it may be helpful to document the health of the battery and other live data related to the high-voltage, including system status Electric Vehicle (EV) Initial Inspection and Handling -Electric Vehicle Overview – EV popularity continues to increase with more vehicle makers entering the market. This, combined with continuing advancements in battery-power-storage capability and resulting range increases, makes EVs more attractive to consumers. As this trend continues, the likelihood of EVs showing up in more collision repair facilities increases as well -High-Voltage (HV) Battery Overview – HV batteries are used in battery electric vehicles (BEV), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEV), and most hybrid electric vehicles (HEV). HV batteries are assemblies containing a large number of battery cells or several modules with multiple cells in each to store electrical energy used by the vehicle. The most common battery type used is the lithium-ion battery. It has the highest energy density of currently mass-produced batteries and is the dominant rechargeable-battery chemistry used for electronic devices. It is also commonplace for transportation and power-storage applications. The batteries are safe if they are handled and managed properly and have no damage conditions or defects in the cells -Lithium-Ion Batteries – lithium-ion batteries are the main power source for electric vehicles. Lithium batteries contain an electrolyte that is integral to the storage and release of electrical energy. However, the volume of electrolyte in each cell is relatively small compared to a conventional flooded cell, lead-acid battery (12-volt vehicle battery). It is unlikely the electrolyte will escape the battery enclosure unless there is battery cell and enclosure damage -The Challenge with Lithium-Ion Batteries – the challenge of lithium battery technologies in electric vehicles is the unknown condition after a collision or other incident that may affect the battery cells. This energy is quite safe under normal circumstances. There are millions of these batteries in service with very low failure rate. However, in rare and unusual circumstances, HV batteries may experience cell failure due to damage or faults resulting in unsafe conditions. In the unlikely event there is an issue with the battery cells. It may take time for a problem to become evident. This is due to being packaged in an enclosure under most vehicles -Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) – vehicle makers may identify specific PPE when working with EVs and HV batteries that may include items such as: -Outer gear that is flame-resistant -Arc-flash protective face shield -Arc-rated hood -Class 0 lineman’s glove kit -Electrically insulated footwear -Electrically insulated mat -Respiratory protection -Storage bags -Initial Intake of an Electric Vehicle – the first step of working with electric vehicles is understanding the initial receiving, or intake, of the vehicle. This is an initial inspection and not to be confused with the damage analysis/blueprinting phase of the process. Look for obvious visible damage to areas of the vehicle where HV system parts, modules, cables, and connectors are located, as well as visible damage to the HV parts themselves. When inspecting or working on vehicles with HV systems, tehcnicians should be familiar with the specific vehicle, as parts and their locations do vary. This introduces different damage possibilities based on loss type and vehicle part locations. Only properly trained/qualified EV technicians using the required tools and equipment should perform work on these vehicles -State of the Vehicle’s HV System – during initial inspection, the first concern is the state of the vehicle’s HV system and battery. To determine this, ask yourself these questions: -Has the owner been driving the vehicle with no apparent issues? -Does the key fob unlock the vehicle? -Does the vehicle power up? -Are there any warning or caution lights or messages? -Does the ready light appear? -Is the vehicle capable of moving by itself? -There may be noticeable issues upon arrival such as damage caused by collisions, fire, or flooding -Battery Condition Considerations – when determing the state of the vehicle, it is important to inspect the condition and stability of the HV battery. Always assume the HV battery and all system parts are energized and fully charged. There are possible electrical, chemical, mechanical, and thermal risks associated with HV batteries on EVs. Note: Always check specific vehicle and battery inspection procedures to determine how they should be handled -Battery Inspection – some signs of battery damage may be dents, tears, punctures, or ruptures. Other signs suggesting the battery is compromised could be: -Evidence of a thermal event (Heat) -Smoke residue -Discoloration -Deformation -Melted seals or connectors -Metallic splatter -Unusual sounds -Abnormal odors -Heat – batteries work best within certain operating limits and may become unstable at extremes. The cells must remain within certain voltage and temperature limits. Overheating, physical damage, or improper charging can lead to instability. If a battery failure occurs, toxic gas release or fires may result. This happens when the internal chemistry of the battery reacts generating heat faster than it can be dissipated. With any fire involving lithium-ion batteries, clear the area, call the fire department, allow ample ventilation and stay upwind at a safe distance -Electrolyte Leakage and Odors – during initial inspection, check for unusual odors and leakage on floors and vents. Chemical smells may be a sign that there are reactions taking place. Electrolyte leakage can create toxic hydrofluoric acid and other noxious gases. The HV battery is designed to vent any gases outside the passenger cabin. Depending on damage condition, there is a possibility this could be compromised allowing an accumulation of hazardous gases in the vehicle cabin. In situations where this is suspected, the interior of the vehicle should be allowed to vent by leaving the doors, windows, and trunk open to ensure that no gases accumulate -Coolant Leakage – inspect the cooling system rservoirs to see if they are full, low, or empty. If low or empty, check for visible evidence of where the coolant is leaking externally. If there is no visible evidence of an external leak, there may be a coolant leak internal to the HV battery, presenting a possible hazardous situation. Any kind of fluid or moisture accumulation that involves the HV system parts or the battery safety disconnect may present an increased safety risk. It is important to not touch HV system parts if this is observed. -Moving and Storing – Evs that have indications of possible HV battery damage, fault conditions, or compromise to the HV system should be evaluated by a qualified EV specialist. Additionally: -Do not store a vehicle within a damaged lithium-ion battery inside a structure or within 50 feet of any structure, overhead wires, or other vehicles -Place a “Danger: High Voltage” sign on the vehicle -Park the vehicle on a surface that has been sealed for vehicles involved in a collision or place a drip tray under the vehicle where the HV battery is located. Note: Never attempt to tow the vehicle with its drive wheels down -Quarantining Following Initial Inspection – if the vehicle has any indications of HV battery instability, the vehicle should never be quarantined. This ensures the safety of the work environment and protects from damaging other vehicles and buildings nearby. The vehicle may require monitoring over several days to determine battery stability. If there is any damage to any of the HV system parts and the HV battery is determined to be stable, the EV technician will need to evaluate the vehicle and determine required repairs -Pre-Scan – after the vehicle has been visually inspected and the damage is not determined to be immediately dangerous, a pre-scan should be conducted to identify if there are diagnostic trouble codes (DTC) indicating underlying electronic systems issues. DTCs may also identify problems with the HV battery and other HV system parts. The technician performing the scan should understand the codes that related to the HV system. Some codes may identify issues such as loss of isolation system interlock loop, or other HV system issues. These conditions may present a safety or shock hazard -Live Data with EVs – it may be possible to view live data using a scan tool in order to identify HV battery state of health. This should be conducted by a trained EV specialist familiar with the specific vehicle. There are usually multiple temperature sensors located throughout the battery to monitor internal temperatures. The live data should report relatively even temperatures and within vehicle maker limits. Excessive cell voltage variations may also indicate a problem. Follow vehicle maker requirements for handling if this occurs -OEM Qualified Assistance – vehicle makers may have specific procedures set in place on how to evaluate and handle their EVs. This may include checklists and diagnostic procedures to determine if the vehicle needs to be quarantined and other actions to be taken. The vehicle may need to be transported to a qualified service center to be evaluated if there are issues. When working with EVs, ensure all OEM safety, inspection, handling, and repair procedures are followed -Preparing for Safe Handling and Inspections – vehicle makers are required to meet extensive safety requirements and have invested heavily to ensure the vehicles they produce are safe under a variety of conditions, including collisions. According to a National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) report, EVs are no more likely to catch fire than petroleum-fueled vehicles. Research continues to take place to improve battery safety and capacity. It is important to have properly trained staff with the specific knowledge, skills, tools, and equipment to ensure proper evaluation and handling of a customers vehicle. EHS: Personal Protective Equipment (Rivian) Personal protective equipment, or PPE, has been around throughout history in various forms. Even everyday clothing is a form of PPE, but many jobs and workplaces have unique hazards that require additional protection, such as gloves, boots, eye protection and many others. This course will teach employees to identify the need for PPE, define the concept of a hazard assessment, things to consider when choosing PPE, and the different types of PPE used to protect all parts of the body. This course is intended for general-industry employees who, during their regular work duties, are required to use personal protective equipment -Purpose – this course is meant to give you an awareness of workplace hazards that require Personal Protective Equipment, or PPE&lt; and the different kinds of PPE you can use to protect yourself against those hazards. By the time we’re done here, you’ll be able to: -Identify common types of PPE and their uses -Identify specialized types of PPE and their uses -Be aware of your responsibilities when using and maintaining your PPE -Physical hazards in the workplace are nothing new. Whenever we come across hazards, we use the hierarchy of controls to try and remove the hazard. Let’s take a look at how the process works for removing these hazards. -Engineering controls are the first step, we can try to remove the hazard or isolate it from the work environment. An example of an engineering control would be removing loose electrical cords from the factory floor that could pose a tripping hazard -Administrative Controls – if engineering controls aren’t enough, the next step is to use administrative controls, which involves changing the way employees work to minimize exposure to the hazard. If a specific workspace is restricted to authorized personnel only, this is an example of an administrative control that has been put into place -Personal Protective Equipment – sometimes neither engineering nor administrative controls are enough to counter the hazards in the workplace, which where personal protective equipment is required. Personal protective equipment is equipment worn to protect from exposure to hazards in the workplace and reduce the chance of workplace injuries or illnesses -Body Protection – even your normal clothes are a type of PPE: they protect you from heat, cold and embarrassment. However, there are some types of clothing designed specifically to be full-body PPE. -Leg and Foot Protection – your legs and feet are particularly vulnerable to all sorts of hazards: stepping on sharp objects, being crushed by heavy objects, being exposed to excessive heat or cold, exposure to electrical hazards and slip, trip and fall hazards -Arm and Hand Protection – your hands are probably the most important tools you work with, and they’re one of the most vulnerable parts of your body. Gloves will be chosen based on the tasks you will perform and the hazards associated with those tasks -Leather and canvas gloves are good for protecting against cuts, burns and heat -Chemical-resistant gloves such as neoprene or nitrile gloves are good at protecting against certain chemicals -Fabric gloves do well against dirt, splinters and abrasions, and are also good for maintaining your grip while carrying items -Head Protection – head injuries are especially dangerous because they often cause permanent, debilitating damage or death. Both hard hats and bump caps need to fit properly and be capable of protecting you from the specific hazards in your work area Question – Which of the following can protect a worker’s head from not only impacts and penetrations, but can also rated to protect against electrical shocks and burns – Bump caps Lesson 2: Specialized Types of PPE and Their Uses -Eye and Face Protection – your eyes are one of the most easily damaged parts of your body, so its no surprise that your eye and face hazards are some of the most common in workplaces. Rivian requires you to wear eye and/or face protection if your job will expose you to airborne particles, molten metal, liquid chemicals, acids or caustic liquids, chemical gases or vapors, or potentially harmful light radiation -Hearing Protection – if noise levels in your workplace exceed 85 decibels, or if the work environment is too loud to have a normal conversation and the noise levels cannot be lowered, you’ll need hearing protection -Respiratory Protection – there may also be situations where you might need respiratory protection to do your job safely. This can range from dust masks to keep dust and other particles out of your lungs, to air-purifying respirators with cartridges designed to keep specific particles and fumes out, such as paint fumes or toxic vapors -Additional Specialized PPE – some jobs will require more complex PPE than others due to very specific hazards. The more dangerous your job is, the more specific and sophisticated your PPE can get. If you’ll require any of these types of PPE, Rivian will ensure you receive proper equipment and training to use it Question – at what decibel level will you be required to wearing hearing protection? 85 Lesson 3: Your Responsibilities with PPE Use -Wearing PPE- one of the most important things to keep in mind when it comes to PPE is that it only works if you wear it properly. It is important to keep the following considerations in mind: -Its ultimately your responsibility to wear your PPE whenever you need it -Rivian may take disciplinary action against you if you aren’t wearing PPE when required -You’re taking a risk to your own health and well-being if you don’t wear it -Well-Fitting PPE – whatever PPE you use needs to fit its body part properly. Comfortable PPE that fits properly will be much easier to wear, and will be that much more effective -Proper Maintenance and Repair – no matter what PPE you’ll need to use, its important to make sure you take care of it properly. Rivian will train you on the proper maintenance of your equipment when you receive it, but you need to make sure you follow those instructions -Hazard Assessment – many workplaces will change as time goes on. New employees, new equipment and new policies may change the way you work or the specific processes you need to follow to do your job. Likewise, new hazards may appear in your workplace, or existing hazards can dimmish, or become more significant as time goes on Question – which of the following is a potential consequence of not wearing PPE or not wearing PPE properly? Disciplinary action may be taken against you, you may become injured, you may injure others Question – who should you contact if you need to replace damaged PPE? Your supervisor Engine Cooling and A/C Systems Overview -Course Introduction – vehicles are designed to meet specific emissions and fuel economy requirements while providing certain performance, durability, and occupant comfort. To help meet these goals, the cooling system manages the engine/drivetrain operating temperatures. The heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning (HVAC) system provides comfortable cabin temperatures for the occupants. These systems share a few parts. Many parts are located in areas where they may be damaged in a collision. During vehicle disassembly and repairs, it is important to inspect the different system parts. Ensure nothing is overlooked and damaged parts are repaired or replaced -Cooling Systems – cooling systems help maintain proper engine operating temperatures. Heat generated by the engine is transferred to the coolant, which is then circulated through the cooling system, reducing the coolant temperature.
