The cooling system is often damaged during a front end collision, and making a thorough inspection of all cooling system parts is necessary. -Cooling System Function – cooling systems use a constant flow of coolant through the cooling system. Coolant is moved through the cooling system by a water pump; however, heat is removed from the coolant by the radiator and heater core. The temperature is controlled by a thermostat Air flow from the front (arrows). Radiator is the black line on the left. Cooling fan is behind the radiator. Head is the gray odd shaped parts on top. Engine block is housing the arrows that go up n down -Radiator Parts Assembly – There are numerous parts that comprise a radiator assembly -1) Radiator Core – the radiator core is constructed of small metal fins that are mounted with tubes that flow horizontally or vertically. The fins conduct the heat from the tubes and transfer it to the air flowing through the radiator. The cores must be in good working condition to efficiently remove heat from the coolant. If the cores are bent or plugged, they will not function properly -2) Side Tanks – one side tank will be where the hot coolant flows in and then is distributed to the radiator core. The reduced temperature coolant flows back into the system through the other side tank. -3) Oil Coolers – depending on the system, one or both side tanks may house a transmission oil cooler. The transmission oil cooler exchanges heat with the coolant in the radiator -4) Gaskets – gaskets provide a seal between the side tank and radiator core -Radiator Drain Valves – radiator drain valves are typically located near or on the bottom of the radiator tank and may be made of plastic or metal. The drain valve is also referred to as a petcock or plug. It may be threaded requiring multiple turns to open or may only turn a partial rotation to open. Drain valves that are turned in the wrong direction or too much can cause permanent damage. Do not use impact tools on drain valves radiator drain valve -Draining Procedures – when draining a cooling system, be sure the engine is off, cooled sufficiently, and the system is not under pressure to avoid injury. Water pump and engine damage will occur if the engine is left running without coolant. Remove the radiator cap to avoid creating a vacuum in the system. Watch for sediment, metal flakes, and other material in the coolant. If present, the system will more than likely need to be flushed before being refilled. * Always reference vehicle maker service procedures to identify correct repair procedures, precautions, and mandatory repairs before beginning repairs on any cooling system. -Radiator Damage Analysis – most radiator damage is obvious and easily identified. When inspecting radiators look for damage to the tanks and core like – punctures, cracks, signs of leaking fluids. There are other situations to consider when inspecting a radiator -Blockage and Bent Radiator Fins – blockages in a radiator can be caused by debris flowing through the cooling system, corroded radiator metal, and chemical breakdown of the coolant. Bent or missing radiator fins can be caused by gravel, birds, and large bugs, and will reduce airflow and increase the operating temperature. The radiator fins may be straightened using a fin comb. Radiator fin spacing can vary, so it is important to select the proper comb spacing -Coolant Leaks – leaks may develop on some radiators between the core and the tanks due to a collision. A collision may also damage coolant hoses and lines, which could also leak -Monitoring Overheating – after a repair has been completed to the cooling system, verify the operating condition of the system for quality control purposes. An engine can overheat due to many things, including -Plugged radiators -A damaged water pump -Obstructions in the coolant flow -Air in the system -A damaged head gasket -* A non-contact thermometer can be used to monitor an overheating engine -Hose Clamps – the two types of hose clamps commonly used for radiator and heater hoses are spring steel and screw band. A spring hose clamp is made of spring steel with barbs on each end. Squeezing the ends opens and expands the clamp. Special hose clamp pliers should be used to remove or install the clamps. The pliers have a deep groove that will keep the clamp from slipping out of the jaws. A screw clamp is a steel band with a screw thread pattern cut or pressed into it. One end of the band contains a screw. When the screw is turned, it pulls the threads of the band, causing the band to tighten. Screw-type hose clamps should not be used where the vehicle maker originally used spring-type clamps. This may cause plastic hose attachment points to crack -Types of Cooling Fans – cooling systems utilize two types of cooling fans: electric and mechanical -Electric Fans – vehicles may have one or multiple electric cooling fans. Electric cooling fans can be either a single fan or side-by-side fans. They use temperature sensors to determine when the fans need to be engaged to assist the cooling systems. The electric cooling fan can either push or pull air into the system. Typically, a cooling fan will run at a constant speed and direct air from the front of the vehicle through the radiator. If wire repairs are required, be sure to get the positive and negatives wires on the correct terminals of the fan. Wiring backwards may damage or cause the fan to rotate backwards, not cooling the engine. Most electric fans can start running at any time with no warning when the ignition is in the run position. Some electric fans may start running, even when the vehicle is off. -Mechanical Fans – mechanical belt-driven fans pull air into the system and are operated by a belt that drives the fan. Mechanical fans may also use a clutch that is engaged by a thermostat, and may also utilize an electric fan to assist with cooling. Vehicle makers are moving away from mechanical belt-driven fans toward electric cooling fans to increase fuel economy and efficiency. Clutch fans are typically controlled by a thermostat. The thermostat-controlled clutch uses a bimetal coil in front to sense temperature. Heat expands the bimetal coil, which opens an internal valve that reduces belt slippage. This in turn alters the speed of the fan. -Fan Shrouds – fan shrouds may have cracks, chips, or other minor damage that may be repaired. Removing a fan shroud may give the appearance of freed airflow, but actually it reduces the directed airflow and requires the radiator to work harder to cool. Airflow under the hood helps cool the engine, coolant hoses, and radiator -Coolant Flow Through a Surge Tank – cooling systems may use a surge tank. Depending upon the vehicle maker, these tanks may also be called expansion or degas tanks. The tank provides a space for coolant expansion when it gets hot. It separates air from the coolant and is under pressure when the vehicle is running, and the engine is at temperature. The tank has a cap that functions like a radiator cap. This tank is also used to hold extra coolant and should never be filled beyond the fill line. -Surge Tank Inspection – inspect surge tanks for -Proper sealing caps -Visible damage -Correct fluid levels -* Do not repair the cap or surge tank due to high tank pressure and heat. Do not open surge tanks until after the system has cooled. This allows the system to depressurize, preventing hot coolant from spraying out of the tank -Overflow Tank Operation and Inspection – overflow tanks also play a pivotal role in proper cooling system. However, their function within the system differs greatly from surge tanks, as well as the precautions that must be taken when working with them -Coolant Flow in an Overflow System – some cooling systems use an overflow tank. Overflow tanks are not under pressure and are not subjected to high coolant temperatures. -Overflow Tank Inspection – when inspecting overflow tanks, look for proper sealing as the opening on top of the tank must be sealed. Check for the proper fluid level since fluid levels will change depending on the temperature of the engine. Look for sediment, as overflow bottles can collect sediment due to limited coolant flow and movement. Also, pay attention to visible damage. If damaged, plastic repairs may be performed on items such as overflow coolant bottles because the tank is not under pressure or the coolant is not at a high temperature. -Water Pump Types and Considerations – water pumps circulate coolant through the engine and cooling system. Water pumps can be driven by belts, electric motors, and gear. Water pumps that are used for primarily cooling are generally centrifugal and are mechanically driven. Auxiliary or secondary water pumps are generally electrically powered. The weep hole on a water pump allows coolant to leak when internal seals or bearings are beginning to fail and must be inspected. The leaking means that the water pump needs to be replaced. If it is not replaced, the impeller can grind into the engine block and cause damage. Weep hole on top left. Centrifugal on top right. Gear-Driven on bottom left. Electric on bottom right. -Belt Types – engine-driven accessories, including water pumps, may use one or more of the following belt types. -Serpentine belt – is used as the primary drive belt on many late model vehicles. This type of belt has lateral grooves that hold it on the pulleys -V-belt – is most commonly used on early model vehicles. These belts have a profile shaped like a “V”, and are used to run one or two accessories -Timing belt – can be rounded or square and have cogs that run perpendicular to the belt length. A timing belt can have two different styles of teeth that cannot be interchanged. Serpentine – left. V-belt – top right. Timing – bottom right -Belt Inspection – when inspecting belts, make sure the belt tensioners are operating properly. Also, check for -Lateral cracks -Cracks that extend to the nylon cords -Fraying of the belt -Pieces missing from the belt -* Replace the belt if any of these conditions exist -Thermostats – a thermostat helps manage engine temperature by controlling the flow of coolant to the radiator by opening and closing as the engine temperature changes. Thermostats are usually located in a housing on the engine with a hose connected to the radiator but may also be integrated into the water pump housing, on the intake, the cylinder head, or in the upper or lower radiator hose. Thermostat housings can be made from aluminum, steel, or plastic/composite -Coolant Sensors – coolant sensors monitor the engine coolant temperature and level. Multiple sensors may be used. Coolant level sensors are located in the coolant tank. Coolant temperature sensors are usually on the engine but may also be located in the radiator. If a sensor, connector, or any associated wiring is damaged, the engine may not run properly and may overheat as the cooling fans are not commanded to turn on when needed. -Heater System Parts – the two main parts that make up a heater system include the heater core and heater valves -Heater Core – a heater core is typically made of aluminum or brass and may have plastic end tanks similar to a radiator. Hot coolant flows through the core to warm the passenger cabin air. Heater cores are usually mounted behind the dashboard in the passenger cabin. If the heater core is leaking, there may be coolant leaking out of the A/C drain under the vehicle and/or dripping on the interior floor -Heater Valves – heater valves are operated by a cable, vacuum, or electronically. They prevent hot coolant from entering the heater core of the vehicle when it is not needed. When a heater shutoff valve is opened, coolant flows from the output side of the water pump, through the coolant lines, to the heater shutoff valve, through the heater core, and back to the engine. -Coolant Hoses and Lines – coolant hoses and lines are designed to carry coolant at system operating temperatures and pressures. Hoses are generally made of rubber which is flexible and easier to route within the system where some movement is necessary. Lines may be made of steel, aluminum, or plastic. These rigid lines are designed to be routed without risk of pinching and limiting coolant flow. Lines and hoses should be inspected for damage, metal lines should be inspected for possible corrosion issues as well. -Coolant – there are a variety of different coolants. Vehicle makers may identify a specific type to reduce internal part corrosion and maximize the life of the cooling system and engine parts. Use the type of coolant that is specified by the vehicle maker. -Coolant and Water Mixes – some coolants are ready to use and others require dilution with water to obtain the desired boil over and freeze protection, usually 50/50 in most climates. Refer to the product maker instructions to determine the appropriate mixture ratio. Never use just water except if flushing the system. It is not recommended to mix different coolants as they may not be compatible. Even coolants that look the same may not be compatible. -Refilling and Bleeding a System – when refilling a cooling system, always reference vehicle maker service information to identify recommended procedures, precautions, and service requirements. It is important that there is not trapped air in the system as engine or other part damage may occur. Some cooling systems have air bleed valves which should be opened to allow trapped air to escape. Ensure the valve is closed after filling the system. Hybrid and electric vehicles may have more than one cooling system. Some cooling systems may require a scan tool to activate valves and/or pumps during refill. Engine Cooling and A/C Systems Overview -Basic A/C System Operation and Refrigerant Flow – an air conditioning (A/C) system removes heat from the vehicle passenger compartment by circulating refrigerant through the system. The evaporator core is in the passenger cabin area and absorbs heat. The refrigerant is pumped out to the condenser where it releases heat, and then it is routed back to the evaporator. As the refrigerant circulates, it is changing from a gaseous state to a liquid state and back. It also changes pressure. There are two common types. Both contain desiccant to remove moisture from the system. Moisture can damage the system and cause insufficient cooling -Accumulator A/C – blue arrows – low pressure gas Blue – low pressure mist Red arrows – high pressure gas Red – high pressure liquid Receiver/Drier System – Blue arrows – low pressure gas Blue – low pressure mist Red arrows – high pressure gas Red – high pressure liquid -A/C Systems Inspection and Testing – a visual inspection of the A/C system should be done to identify any obvious breach in the system parts. If no issues are found, turn the system on to verify the system is working properly. This may include measuring the air temperature at the outlet vents on the dash, and comparing the readings to information found in the service manual. The A/C system can be checked for leaks using an electronic tester. Refrigerant is heavier than air, so be sure to test the lower areas of the part being tested. Dye may be present or added to the system and monitored to identify slow leaks. Fittings and joints are the most common areas for leaks to occur. Inspect these areas first. -Compressor Operation – the compressor is used to create flow and change the pressure of refrigerant in the A/C system. Refrigerant enters the compressor as a low-pressure gas and exits as a high-pressure gas. On most vehicles, the compressor uses a belt and pulley with a clutch to externally drive the compressor, allowing visual verification of operation. Some belt-driven compressors are variable displacement and may not use a clutch. Electric vehicles and some hybrid-electric vehicles use an electrically driven compressor. The compressor requires oil for lubrication. The oil travels through the A/C system with the refrigerant. Electric vehicles and some hybrid-electric vehicles use an electrically driven compressor. -Compressor Damage Analysis – mechanical and electric compressors need to be inspected for damage -General inspection – when inspecting an A/C compressor for damage -Check the clutch and pulley. These parts may be replaced individually or as a unit -Turn the clutch to verify there is no roughness in the bearings -Check the shaft and seal for damage or leakage if the clutch or pulley is damaged -Verify the compressor engages when the system is activated -Check the attachment points of the refrigerant lines -Inspect the pressure relief valve, switches, and wiring for broken insulation or shorted wires -Check the compressor case for signs of impact. Impact could cause internal damage, leaks, or other problems -Inspect the brackets that attach the compressor to the engine -Check the compressor pulley for impact marks if the compressor pulley is in close proximity to a frame rail or other parts -Electric Compressor – electric compressors should be carefully inspected for damage to the case and wiring. Do not touch any of the high voltage wiring, connectors, or parts unless the high voltage system has been disarmed and it has been verified that there is not a shock hazard present -Condenser – the condenser in the A/C system works similar to the radiator in the engine cooling system. In fact, the condenser and radiator are commonly stacked next to each other. The same fan or multiple fans removes heat from the refrigerant that flows through the condenser, just as heat is removed from the coolant flowing through the radiator -Condenser Damage Analysis – when inspecting a condenser for damage, depending on the severity of the damage, straightening may be possible. Bent fins may be straightened by combing. If the condenser tubes are damaged, there are typically no repair options besides complete part replacement. Do not automatically anticipate loss of refrigerant when a vehicle has been in a collision. Depending on the damage and type of impact, an A/C system may retain its charge -Orifice Tube – an orifice tube is found on A/C systems equipped with an accumulator. As the refrigerant flows through the orifice tube, it changes pressure and state before entering the evaporator. The orifice tube may become clogged if contaminants are in the system. The orifice tube must be the proper size and installed with the arrow pointing in the direction of refrigerant flow for the system to perform correctly -Evaporator Damage Analysis – the evaporator core is not commonly damaged in a collision due to its location inside the passenger cabin. If an evaporator is leaking, there may be dye or oil leaking from the condensate drain on the passenger side cowl/underbody. Evaporators are not easily accessible and are usually inspected last. Dirt and debris can accumulate on the evaporator and lead to corrosion and leaks in certain situations. If engine coolant is leaking from the condensate drain, the heater core is likely bad. -Switches and Sensors Damage Analysis – the pressure switches and sensors on the high and low side of the A/C system -Should be inspected for physical damage including connectors and wires that could cause the system to operate incorrectly or be damaged -May require evacuating the refrigerant in the system before replacement -May have replaceable O-rings -Lines and Hoses Damage Analysis – when inspecting lines and hoses, kinks in the aluminum or rubber may affect system performance. Damaged lines should not be repaired, they should be replaced. Typically, A/C service ports are located on the high- and low-side pressure lines -Types of Refrigerant – there are different types of refrigerants used in vehicles. Refrigerant R-134a has been used in automatic A/C systems since the mid-1990s. Some vehicle makers are using R-1234yf refrigerant instead of R-134a in some models. R1234yf is flammable, however, it has been determined to be safe for automotive use. Vehicles manufactured before 1994 contain R-12 refrigerant, unless retrofitted to R-134a. The type of refrigerant will be clearly labeled under the hood of the vehicle. Labels may be found on A/C system parts or other under hood locations. -Refrigerant Oils – there are a variety of different oils available for A/C systems. It is important to use the correct oil to ensure proper compressor operation and life. When the system suddenly loses its charge or when service work is performed, oil will be lost from the system and must be replaced. Service information should be referenced to ensure the correct type and amount is replaced when performing service work or replacing parts. The refrigerant label will show the total oil charge amount. Adding too much oil will reduce the system efficiency and may cause compressor damage -Electric Compressor Oils – electric compressors in hybrid/electric vehicles have unique oil requirements. Introducing the wrong oil may cause a safety hazard as well as a system failure resulting in the vehicle high-voltage system shutting down. Only A/C service equipment designed for electric compressors should be connected to these systems Estimating Guide Fundamentals (1/2) P-Pages Introduction P-Page Information -P-Page Overview – the Procedure pages (P-pages) provide insight into how the database supporting the estimating system was developed, how to utilize the database when preparing an estimate, and how to read an estimate prepared using the estimating software and database. Other common names for the manuals containing P-page information include: Database Reference Manual (DBRM), Guide to Estimating (GTE), and Collision Estimating Guide (CEG). This course will focus on where to find P-page information, and how to utilize the P-pages when preparing a damage estimate -Understanding Your Estimating System – when using P-page logic or computerized estimating system, it is important to have a good understanding of how the database is utilized to support the estimating software. Some systems utilize an inside-out theory, which means that when selecting an inner part for replacement, the database will typically add the necessary attaching or exterior panels operations necessary to replace the inner part -Outside-In Systems – other systems will use an outside-in theory, which means the system does not, by default, automatically include the necessary ‘remove and install’ (R&amp;I) of exterior or attaching parts to the labor allowance for replacing the inner part. For example, when utilizing an estimating system with an inside-out theory and replacing an inner part such as a uniside, the system will likely include the labor necessary to R&amp;I the door(s) and attaching parts. When utilizing a system using an outside-in theory, the necessary R&amp;Is and attaching parts will not, by default, be included in the labor time -Accessing P-Page Information – there are a few ways to access P-Page information, including links to the information provider reference manual. You may: -Access the information for Audatex: http://audatex.us ; CCC: http://cccis.com ; or Mitchell: http://www.mitchell.com directly through links -Access links to the reference manuals by going to the Database Enhancement Gateway (DEG) website (at www.DEGweb.org ) and then selecting the “Estimate Toolbox” -Use links within the estimating system, which typically provide direct links to the relevant P-page information -Headnote/Footnote – in addition to P-page information, some estimating systems will provide additional notes within the estimating system. These notes are sometimes referred to as headnotes (H-notes) or footnotes. These notes include important information about specific parts, operations, and database labor allowances. You must be mindful of this information in order to produce accurate estimates. Note: H-notes and footnotes may only be visible to the person writing the estimate and typically do not print on the estimate -Labor Time Premise – labor times produced by the estimating system database are typically based on replacing new undamaged parts on a new undamaged vehicle. These labor times typically do not include any additional labor requirements associated with non- OEM parts and accessories or procedures necessary to gain access to damaged parts for the purpose of repair or replacement. Note: Labor or operations not addressed in the ‘Included/Not-Included’ list within the P-pages were likely not considered in the work time developed for that procedure -Effect Zone/Adjacent Panel – it is important to remember that allowances listed in estimating systems do not typically include the labor required to repair the “effect zone,” which are the surfaces adjacent to the repair area. This may include repair areas requiring welding, drilling, grinding, or straightening. For example, one information provider states “Replace labor does not include additional labor to repair the replacement panel or adjacent panels, which may become distorted, burned, or damaged by welding, drilling, grinding, and straightening.” Similar information can be found in the P-pages for other systems as well -Database Enhancement Gateway (DEG) – the Database Enhancement Gateway (DEG) is an initiative to help improve quality and accuracy of collision repair estimates. The DEG uses proactive feedback from the collision repair industry, and other “end users,” to the information provides that supply the databases for estimating products. The DEG offers users a more standardized and streamlined process for the generation of database inquiries. The DEG Administration serves an advocacy role, processing all submitted databases inquiries until they reach conclusion. The DEG also provides a searchable database of prior inquiries, links to information provider user manuals, video training aids, and blogs on various industry topics Included/Not-Included Operations -Included/Not-Included Operations – when preparing an estimate, it is important to know the labor operations that are considered to be included and not-included within the estimating system you are working with. This is important because these items may not be consistent from one system to the next. This makes it necessary to add the operations that will be required to complete the repairs that are not included in the database-generated labor allowance. The following are examples of included/not-included operations for the same part from three estimating systems -Digital images. Cant bring them over -Calculating for Recycled, or Like, Kind, Quality (LKQ) Removal/Installation – when writing an estimate utilizing recycled or ‘like, kind, and quality’ (LKQ) parts, it is important to note that the labor times and included/not-included operations will be different from times and operations associated with new OEM parts. Some information providers include a separate set of included/not-included operations within their standard user manual for commonly recycled/LKQ parts, while others provide a separate manual to address the use of recycled/LKQ parts. Note that CCC uses the Recycled Assemblies Guide to Estimating (RACE) manual and Mitchell uses the Assemblies Time Guide (ATG) manual Estimating Guide Fundamentals (2/2) P-Pages and Refinishing P-Page Refinishing Information -Commonly Included Refinish Procedure – the base refinish allowance from an estimating system is typically based on a single color applied to a new, undamaged panel. Refinish times typically do not include tint matching, correcting defective finish textures, or correcting surface imperfections due to weather conditions, application errors, or environmental contamination. Consult the P-pages for your specific estimating systems to determine what is included in the refinish times. This image shows the included/not-included operations from one of the estimating system providers for basic color coat application -Refinish Overlap – refinish overlap applies when two or more panels are refinished at the same time. This occurs because there are some operations included in base color refinish allowances that are not necessary to duplicate on additional panels being refinished. For example, a common refinish overlap formula: -Uses the full refinish time for the first major panel -Deducts 0.4 for each additional adjacent panel -Deducts 0.2 for each additional non-adjacent panel -Deducts 0.2 for each additional part with less than 1.0 refinish time -Note: Although the estimating system database will typically make these adjustments for you, it is important to understand the formula used by your estimating system -Clearcoat and Multi-Stage Finishes – in most cases, the estimating system calculates clearcoat and multi-stage finish allowances based on the paint code or finish type entered into the system. However, the formulas used to calculate these allowances are not the same, so it is important to review the P-pages and understand the formula used by the estimating database you are using. For example, consider these two different formulas for a two-stage (clearcoat) finish: -System A – -First major panel – add 40% to the refinish time -Each additional panel – add 20% to the refinish time -System B – -Set-up time of 0.6 with the selection of the first refinished panel -Add 20% of refinish labor to each panel for the two-stage finish Fixed Glass Removal and Installation (1/3) Fixed Glass Removal Fixed Glass Overview -Role of Fixed Glass – stationary glass, combined with the urethane adhesive system, is a structural part of the vehicle. This type of glass, also referred to as fixed glass, contributes to the strength of the roof and pillars. It also provides bracing to make the structure more rigid -Structural Role of Stationary Glass – stationary glass helps to manage collision energy. For example, in a frontal collision, a urethane-bonded windshield helps direct energy through the A-pillars and over the roof, away from the passenger compartment. The glass is bonded to the vehicle on the pinchweld. A pinchweld is formed where two mating flanges are welded together. This was done to prevent leaks that were occurring with gasket-set installations. Stationary glass that is not bonded with urethane adhesive is not considered a structural part of the vehicle, but all stationary glass plays a role in maintaining a rigidity and strength -Works with Passenger Airbags – another role for the windshield is to work with the passenger airbag. A deploying passenger airbag may: -Deflect off the glass -Use the windshield to deflect the deployment in the proper direction -Be rendered ineffective by loosening or forcing the windshield out of the opening if it is a poor installation -Stationary glass also keeps unrestrained occupants inside. Severe injuries or fatalities can occur when the occupant is ejected from the vehicle -Types of Fixed Glass – stationary glass can be laminated or tempered. Laminated glass is made of two sheets of glass with a plastic inner layer. The plastic inner layer is polyvinyl butyral (PVB) -Laminated Glass – laminated glass holds together when broken due to the inner plastic laminate. This helps retain occupants. Even though the glass holds together when broken, small sharp slivers of glass break ff. these must be cleaned up and vacuumed out of vent ducts during repairs. Some stationary glass may have a thick PVB layer, which serves as a sound deadener and is called acoustic glass. Note: Laminated glass may be used for windshields, some side glass, glass roofs, and even the back glass on some vehicles. This type of glass can also be repaired, to a limited extent -Tempered Glass – this screen shows a sample label, which is located in the corner of the glass, to indicate the glass is tempered. Tempered glass is heat-treated to increase impact resistance. The glass is heated to about 1,100 degrees Fahrenheit, then rapidly cooled. This type of glass is brittle, and will shatter if cut, drilled, or ground. The shattered pieces are typically not as sharp as laminated glass slivers. Tempered glass cannot be repaired. If previously damaged or stressed, the glass can shatter at a later time. This type of glass may be layered with two sheets of tempered glass. In the United States, tempered glass may be used anywhere but the windshield -Fixed Glass Features – fixed glass features include: -Tinting -Frit -Shading -Windshield park indicators -Tinting – OEM glass is often tinted. Common tint colors include blue, green, bronze, and gray. Extremely dark tinting that covers the entire glass is called privacy glass. Privacy glass can only be used behind the B-pillar because it reduces the drivers vision if used for the windshield or front door glass -Frit – the frit is the black band around the perimeter of most stationary glass, which helps prevent ultraviolet (UV) rays from deteriorating the urethane adhesive. The frit is made of ink and ground glass. Any light scratches on the frit may be covered with black ink or even a black felt-tip marker -Shading – shading on glass is a tinted, horizontal band located across the top of the glass. This is used on some windshields, though a larger frit is more common. Shading is made by darkening the plastic inner layer -Windshield Park Indicators – windshield park indicators are where the windshield wipers should be parked when in a resting position. The indicators are located near the bottom of windshields, and serve as guides for reinstalling the windshield wipers -Encapsulated Glass – encapsulated glass has a polyurethane plastic molding permanently attached to the edge. The molding is installed when the glass is made. Encapsulated glass is designed to help the automated installation of glass on the assembly line. A robot can simply position the glass in place without a need to install the separate molding. any glass part may be encapsulated, but it is mostly found on just the side glass and back glass. * Note: To determine if the glass is encapsulated, try pulling the molding off after the glass is removed. If the molding will not come off, the glass is encapsulated. If it does come off, the molding is either bonded on or a separate molding Fixed Glass Removal -Types of Removal Tools – types of windshield removal tools include the cold knife, long blade knife, power cutter, and wire cutter. -Cold Knife – a cold knife has a 90-degree blade that is inserted under the urethane and a handle, which is used to pull the knife around the glass perimeter. The cold knife is less commonly used today. This tool makes it difficult to save the glass and moldings and prevent scratches on the pinchweld. Modern windshield installations are flatter to the body with tigher gaps. A cold knife is still a go-to tool for some technicians, provided the glass is already damaged and there is no need to save the molding -Long Blade Knife – a long blade knife is used typically for reaching the urethane on the bottom of the windshield where it attaches to the cowl -Power Cutter – power cutters, which are commonly used for windshield removal, are wide-bladed, reciprocating tools. Unless this tool is used very carefully, it is difficult to prevent scratching the pinchweld and damaging the molding -Wire Cutter – came up in Spanish -Glass Removal Accessories – removal accessories for helping a technician replace stationary glass include suction cups to handle the glass without having to touch the edges. Suction cups are useful for both removing and installing the glass. Another accessory is the glass stand, which is used to set the glass flat. Stands should be covered with foam to protect the glass. Glass stands may be used to both clean and prime the replacement glass. Another useful accessory is the urethane trimming tool, which is used for final-trimming the urethane on the pinchweld just prior to installation. Lastly, paddle sticks, made of hard plastic, are used to paddle the start and stop seams once the urethane is applied -Fixed Glass Removal – before removing the windshield, clean and vacuum the vehicle interior to clean up any broken glass. As part of the process, vacuum the defroster vent with the blower on to remove and broken glass from the vent. Remove loose glass parts and slivers from all interior surfaces. Note: Wear safety glasses and leather gloves to protect yourself against glass slivers that may blow out of the vents. Put on the safety glasses before turning the blower on -Interior Part and Trim Removal – begin by removing the parts to access the urethane. Interior parts that may require removal include the rearview mirror. The mirror may be installed on a mounting pad on the windshield interior or come down from the headliner. If mounted on the glass, the mirror usually comes off, but the mounting pad, or spade, remains on the glass. Other interior parts that may require removal include A-pillar trim and other necessary trim, sun visors, and wiring for accessories that connect to the glass or mirror, such as navigation antenna, heating grids, or a radio antenna. On rare occasions, the headliner may have to be loosened at the front as well to access the upper pinchweld -Exterior Part and Trim Removal – exterior parts that may require removal for access to the urethane include the: -Wipers -Cowl trim panel -Other trim, such as the exterior A-pillar trim panel -Antenna, if it is in the way of properly removing or installing the urethane or glass -Note: Also, the hood may need to be opened -Common Windshield Replacement Steps – to remove or install stationary or moveable glass, follow the instructions given in the service information. When working with glass, it is important: -Use the proper tools -Not to damage paint on the pinchweld -Wear safety glasses and leather gloves -Note: An example of a common removal procedure includes, the use of a cut-out knife lubricated with water. When cutting out the windshield, use caution not to damage the paint on the pinchweld. A piano wire type tool may be used to cut the urethane adhesive. Use suction cups to lift the windshield. Trim the adhesive on the flange. Only 1-2mm of the original adhesive should remain on the flange. Remove the rubber dams and fasteners from the pinchweld flange. The rubber dams should be replaced following vehicle service information Fixed Glass Removal and Installation (2/3) Fixed Glass Installation Preparation Glass Preparation -Preparing the Windshield – the glass must be prepared for installation to ensure proper adhesion. To prepare the windshield, clean both surfaces with a mild solvent cleaner to remove any fingerprints or oil contaminants. Fingerprints may appear near the edge, if the glass was handled without gloves. Fingerprints may lead to leaks, and a solvent cleaner will clean greasy contaminants better than glass cleaner. Suction cups are also recommended to eliminate the need for handling the windshield around the edges -Common Installation Procedures – when preparing the windshield for replacement: -Inspect the spacer blocks and replace if damage. The spacer blocks position the windshield at the correct height. These are located at the lower corners of the windshield on the cowl. -Test fit replacement windshield and mark the location by placing masking tape on the windshield and the vehicle body. Cut the tape at the edge of the windshield. Trim the remaining the urethane to a 1-2mm thickness -Clean the ceramic frit with an alcohol-free cleaner -Glass prep twice around the windshield -Apply glass primer around the windshield in the area that was prepared with glass prep -Apply adhesive metal primer to damaged areas of the pinchweld -Windshield – clean the entire surface with glass cleaner. Clean the bonding area, ideally with glass cleaner in the same product line as the urethane being used. Do not use a common house brand glass cleaner, because some of the these may contain silicone. If the original windshield is being reused, remove all but a thin film of existing urethane adhesive (1-2mm) from the windshield surface by using a clean utility knife or razor blade scraper. If equipped, apply the new windshield acoustic strip. Some vehicles are equipped with the acoustic strip to aid in noise reduction. Note: Attempting to clean the interior side of the glass after it is installed could apply enough outward pressure to weaken the bond -Glass/Frit Primer – after the glass is cleaned, use a new dauber to apply glass/frit primer to the bonding area, which is approximately a ¾ inch (20 mm) area along the perimeter of the windshield inner surface. For new windshield installations, refer to the removed windshield to determine the location of the adhesive and where to apply the frit primer. Use care when applying primer – it dries very quickly and may stain the viewing the area of the windshield if applied unevenly. Depending on the product, the primed surface is good for a limited time. Always refer to the product maker information regarding this time. If the glass is not installed within the recommended time, the primer may need to be re-applied. Some primers may also require multiple coats. After application, allow the primer to dry for the recommended time. Do not use paper towels or shop towels to apply frit primer. Paper towels are designed to absorb liquids, not apply them. Towels may also leave fibers or particles that may contaminate the bonding surface -New Glass Considerations – some cleaning products create a foam when applied, and where the foam separates, contaminants are present. In some cases, contaminants may need to be scrubbed with an abrasive pad before reapplying the glass cleaner. Some product makers have a special foam pad to scrub the bonding area and may require special cleaners. Note: Primers used with glass replacement and adhesives are skin and eye irritants. Wear safety glasses and chemical-resistant gloves, such as nitrile gloves whenever handling these products -Dry Fitting – the windshield should be dry fit to the opening to determine the proper position of the glass an ensure the glass opening is dimensionally correct. When dry fitting, make sure the glass is clean and dry. Dry fitting over fresh cut urethane with a dusty or dirty windshield surface may contaminate the bonding surface. Verify that the windshield locator pins (if equipped) are positioned into the locator slots on the pinchweld flange. Mark the locations of the glass in the opening with masking tape, cut the masking tape in the center and then remove the glass from the opening, look for even gaps from side-to-side, and determine if support spacers are required for proper height. Note: When performing the final installing, the masking tape on the windshield and body will be helpful with glass alignment Vehicle Preparation -Urethane Trim and Preparation – the best bond to urethane is urethane, so when reinstalling the same windshield, a thin layer of the existing urethane is left on the glass and pinchweld bonding areas. -Trimming the Existing Urethane – when trimming the existing urethane bead on the glass, trim down to 1-2mm. this is most easily done with a tool designed for the purpose. Do not do this step until just before reinstallation. If it is done hours before, the pores of the freshly trimmed bead could close. The surface can be activated with an activator, but the other problems is that contaminants can fall on the freshly trimmed bead, affecting the bond -Urethane Final Trim – there may also be a thick bead of existing urethane on the vehicle pinchweld after the glass is cut out. The existing bead protects the bonding area from contaminants. Just before installation, final-trim the urethane by cutting the bead to a height of 1-2 mm. Minor differences in the bead height are not a problem, but try to keep this bead as flat and even as possible. Clean the bead after the final trim only if necessary, and only with distilled water. The minerals in tap water will weaken the bond -Flange Preparation – follow the adhesive maker’s recommendations and procedures when installing stationary glass. Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify flange preparation requirements. Note: When trimming the existing adhesive bed, do not cut down to the bare metal. -Foam Tape – open-cell foam tape should be replaced if it was used in the original installation. Foam tape is primarily for sound deadening, reducing the wind noise and vibration around the glass. From the inside of the vehicle, the clean line of foam is also more aesthetic than an uneven bead of urethane. Foam tape is available in 6 mm and 7 mm sizes. Refer to the vehicle maker’s recommendation for the proper size. Note: Foam-core butyl must be replaced with the same product. Do not substitute butyl tape for foam-core butyl. Note note: The foam strip on the inside of this pinchweld is from the OEM, and must be kept intact or be replaced during glass replacement Fixed Glass Removal and Installation (3/3) Fixed Glass Installation Fixed Glass Adhesives -Glass Adhesives – follow all vehicle maker recommendations when using fixed glass adhesives. Types of glass urethanes include: -High modulus, which has an extra rigid cure bead -Conventional moisture-cure, high viscosity -Fast-cure, which sets much quicker than moisture-cure regardless of temperature and humidity -Primerless, which does not require primer be applied to the glass. Primer is still required on areas of the pinchweld where there is no existing urethane -Nonconductive, which is required on some vehicles with antenna grids, defrosters grids, and other electronic systems integrated into the glass. The bus bars on the side of the glass may contact the urethane, which draws power and weakens the signal. -Note: Nonconductive urethane may be required on some aluminum pinch welds. Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify vehicle specific requirements -Adhesive Tests – urethanes are tested for different properties, including: -Tensile and lap shear strength. With both of these tests, two coupons are bonded. Tensile strength testing pulls apart the coupons vertically. Lap shear strength testing pulls apart the two laterally. -Peel strength. The urethane should resist being peeled off easily like tape -Viscosity, or the thickness of the body of the adhesive when applied. Most urethane adhesives made today are high-viscosity, which helps support the glass at the proper level while curing without the need for dam -Modulus, or how rigid the adhesive is when cured -Elasticity, or how much the cured adhesive can stretch and come back to its original shape -Conductivity, or how much the adhesive can conduct electricity -Note: Lap shear and tensile strength tests are used because those are the forces applied to a windshield in a collision. There are forces to both push the glass out (tensile) during the initial impact, and slide upward (lap shear) when the vehicle is collapsing -Adhesive Failure – urethane adhesives specifically made for stationary glass installation helps maintain the structural integrity of the installed glass. When pulled laterally (lap shear), stationary glass adhesive has a strength from 500-1200 psi. This is much stronger than other adhesives and sealants. Urethane adhesive has the consistency of tire rubber and it is designed to maintain vehicle stiffness and rigidity. Some vehicles require a stiffer adhesive than others. When an adhesive is put under stress, there are two ways it can fail. -Failure Types – adhesive failure is when the adhesive pulls loose from the surface it is bonded to. Cohesive failure is when the body of the adhesive pulls apart. A cohesive failure is often to due an adhesive being used after its expired shelf life. This can also cause adhesive failure -Failure Causes – when the adhesive pulls loose from the surface it was bonded to, there could be a number of reasons, including: -Contamination on one of the bonding surfaces -Corrosion on the pinchweld flange -Ambient conditions during installation, such as freezing temperatures -Material incompatibility – all products used should be from the same manufacturer -Expired products -Adhesive Working Time – as with any adhesive, working time is also a factor that must be considered. Working time is how long the technician has, after laying down the adhesive bead, before the glass must be installed in place. This is typically a consistent time. For fast-cure products, the working time is usually 18 minutes. Moisture-cured products may be affected by temperatures and humidity – the working time may be as little as 3 minutes in some situations. Always refer to the working time that is listed on the products performance data sheet. -Moisture vs Fast Cure – urethane adhesives generally cure from the outside-in. After a stated minimum time, there will be an outer skin that holds the glass to the body, but there will still be a liquid center. If a cut is made through a bead of moisture-cure adhesive and fast-cure adhesive, after a few hours the: -Fast-cured adhesive will show an outer skin with a liquid center -Moisture-cure adhesive will show no outer skin beginning to form -Note: Total cure time of the two adhesives also differs, but both types are dependent on temperature and humidity for full cure -Minimum Driveaway Time – the minimum driveaway time is the amount of time before a vehicle with a fixed glass replacement can be released to the customer. This time is reached when the adhesive has achieved enough strength to pass Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard (FMVSS) 212 and 208. This may be as little as 30-60 minutes for fast-cure products. Conventional moisture-cured products may take 2-8 hours or longer. Always reference product maker performance data sheets to identify all necessary product information including driveaway time. The chart shown here procedures the minimum driveaway time for one brand of moisture-cure adhesive -Note: Glass urethane may take 24 hours to reach full strength, and longer for full cure. Information regarding driveaway time and full cure time should be communicated to the vehicle owner. -Adhesive Application – when installing fixed glass, bare metal flanges need to be primed as required. Use an approved glass urethane adhesive. Nonconductive glass urethane adhesive may be used, but it is not required. Some glass adhesives require a primer to be used on the bare glass. Ensure that urethane bead width and height will closely duplicate what was done at the factory. -Applicator Tips – the adhesive applicator nozzle is cut at the tip to the height of the pinchweld. The top nozzle cap shown on this screen is pre-cut. A special application gun may be required if using a two-part adhesive. Travel speed with the gun depends on the height of the pinchweld. The adhesive bead should not be above the height of the pinchweld. -Cutting the Applicator Tip – cut off the tab on the applicator tip when not applying urethane to the glass. The tab is for guiding along the edge of the glass when applying the adhesive and it will get in the way when applying the urethane to the pinchweld. Hold the tip with a pre-cut ‘V’ against the pinchweld wall to determine if it is the correct height. The tip of the ‘V’ should be slightly higher than the top of the pinchweld. If it is too small cut the ‘V’ groove more. If it is too short, make a ‘V’ cut in a tip that is not pre-cut with a ‘V’ groove. -Applying the Urethane – most applications involve applying the urethane to the pinchweld, not the glass. It is often difficult to determine exactly where on the glass the bead contacts the pinchweld, especially on the bottom along the cowl panel. Keep the applicator gun as close to 90 degree as possible. This helps force the adhesive into irregularities on the surface, and points the triangular shape of the bead straight up. Try to make the bead as consistent as possible. Most pinchwelds require 1 and a half to 2 regular-size cartridges. -Padding the Urethane – after applying the urethane and before installing the glass, use a padding stick to paddle the urethane. Paddling the urethane at the seam and on both sides makes a smooth seam transition and prevents leaks Windshield Installation -Windshield Installation Considerations – points to keep in mind when installing the glass include: -Avoid touching the primed area, even with gloves on – use suction cups -Try setting the glass in the proper position and flat the first time – removing and repositioning the glass may cause a leak -Press down to the desired height, trying to level it as evenly as possible -Do not slap on the glass, which may push the glass down too quickly and create air pockets in the adhesive bead -Keep the door glass slightly open until the adhesive cures – shutting doors with the glass shut may cause outward pressure on the stationary glass and weaken the bond -Note: A back support will help reduce back injuries when lifting a windshield or backglass. If possible, have a helper assist with the installation, or use a one-person installment aid -Post Installation – points to keep in mind after glass installation: -Leak checking is done shortly, if not immediately, after the glass installation -Leak checking may be done using the ultrasonic sound method. If any leaks are found, press down on the glass in that location and check again. If the leak is still there, extra urethane could be applied in that area -To prove that the installation was done correctly, document the installation -Ultrasonic Sound Method – the ultrasonic sound method involves the use of a special emitter that is designed to probe the exterior perimeter of the windshield and detect leaks. If a leak is discovered, push on the glass from the outside in the area of the leak. Be careful if pressing near or on a spacer, as this can stress the glass. After pressing on the windshield, test again. This method eliminates most leaks. If the leak persists, apply more urethane. There repairs work when the adhesive is wet. After the adhesive is cured, there may be more serious problems that may require glass reinstallation -Document the Installation – an installer, and the facility that contracted an installer, can be held liable if the glass is improperly installed and a vehicle occupant is injured. To prove that the installation was done correctly, document the: -Glass part that was replaced -Product maker’s procedures used -Products used, including the adhesive lot number, primers, cleaners, etc. List expiration dates, if applicable -Ambient conditions at the installation site, including the temperature and humidity, and whether the glass was replaced inside a facility or outside. List the driveaway time of the adhesive based on these conditions -Vehicle owner information and vehicle identification number (VIN) -Time when the vehicle was released -Note: Save the installation record. Installation record forms are available in brochures from adhesive makes Full-Frame/Unitized Structure Pulling Overview (1/3) Equipment Overview, Repair Planning, and Anchoring Equipment and Process Overview -General Precautions when Straightening – the process and equipment used for straightening a vehicle structure utilizes hydraulic pulling equipment capable of applying large amounts of force. Severe injuries can occur during the straightening process as a result of clamps, chains, or other attachments slipping, breaking, or coming loose, causing backlash. Warn people of the danger that exists if they wander into the path of the pulling chain when pressure is applied. Doorways that are in the path of a pulling chain should be blocked when a vehicle is being straightened. Check on coworkers to make sure they are okay if backlash occurs -Safety Chains or Cables, Overloading and Other Considerations – safety chains or cables should be used to minimize backlash if a chain or clamp slips or breaks. Blankets or tarps wrapped over the pulling chains can also be used to help reduce backlash. Avoid overloading the equipment. It is never safe to stand in front of, behind, or in line with the straightening equipment with pressure applied or if there is tension on the chain. If a chain breaks or a pulling clamp comes off while under tension, pulling towers can pivot or shift violently in the opposite direction. Stay alert at all times during the structural repair process -Overview and Safety Considerations for Drive-On Straightening Systems – drive-on straightening systems lower and/or tilt downward to load the vehicle onto the equipment. The systems may have an adjustable bench height for a convenient working height, removable or permanently fixed runways and pulling towers, and an anchoring system designed specifically for the equipment. After loading a vehicle, place it in park, set the brake, and use the wheel chocks provided by the equipment maker to prevent the vehicle from moving. Wood blocks, bricks, etc. are not acceptable as these could slip or fail, causing injury -Portable Straightening System – portable racks or benches typically have casters mounted to the frame rack or bench that allow the straightening system to be moved around in the repair facility. These systems use portable towers and may eliminate the need for a dedicated structural repair stall. Portable systems require a lift to mount the vehicle. This type of system typically uses universal or dedicated fixtures. Some systems may have ramps that allow the vehicle to be driven onto their benches and may also have a built in lift to position the vehicle at a convenient working height. -In-Floor Straightening System – in-floor straightening systems use tracks or pots mounted into the floor. The vehicle is anchored in the area with the tracks or pots. Straightening is done with portable towers and/or hydraulic rams that are secured in a track or pots in the floor. The vehicle may rest on its suspension or be mounted into the clamps for the pulling process. Some structural repair equipment utilizes a combination of the in-floor system and drive-on system. Access to the underside of the vehicle may be limited with in floor systems -General Steps – structural repairs require careful planning. These are the general steps that should be followed: -1) Measure the vehicle -2) Anchor -3) Pull to remove the damage using the first in, last out method -4) Monitor the vehicle closely to ensure that damage is not being pulled into adjacent areas and anchoring locations -5) Stress-relieve with tension still applied -6) Release tension -7) Verify: Measure the vehicle again. Measuring should be the final step of the straightening process to verify the vehicle has been returned to proper dimensions -8) Repeat as necessary, making adjustments to pulling angles as needed Repair Planning -Damage Identification – it is important to identify all damage conditions to help determine where to anchor and if initial straightening needs to be done. Anchoring generally requires that the center section be aligned. Damage is either direct or indirect. Direct damage is the area that had direct contact with the damaging force and is the most obvious to see. Indirect damage is caused by the collision energy as it moves through the vehicle structure and may not be evident without close inspection and measuring. It is important to identify where damage stops. The undamaged areas must be protected from the pulling forces by proper anchoring -Measuring for Damage – measuring for damage helps determine the extent, location, and direction of direct and indirect damage. This provides a map to help determine the damage removal sequence. Measuring should include printing the measurements. Notes may be written on a copy of the measurements when planning the sequence of the pulls and while monitoring the progress of the repair. These should be kept on file for documentation of the repair. It is important to know how to properly use the measuring system and know how to read the measurements to ensure that the vehicle is being repaired properly -Measuring Tolerances – vehicle repair tolerance is used during the straightening process to determine a specific location of a measuring point. Three to five millimeters was pretty common on older vehicles. However, this is not as common on late model designs where vehicles have much tighter tolerances. The vehicle must be straightened to a degree that allows all of the body panels to fit properly and so that the suspension is in proper alignment without requiring extreme adjustments. Some vehicles have little or no adjustment for some alignment angles, which will require more precision in structural alignment. Tolerances are generally tighter on newer vehicles and may require greater precision in repair -Best Practices – measure the vehicle over the entire length to help prevent overlooking indirect or previous damage. This may prevent unexpected delays in the repair process, especially if done early in the repair process. Working on many of the same makes and models of vehicles will allow you to get a better understanding of how the parts react during straightening, as well as a better understanding of dimensional build variations as you measure more vehicles -Material Strength and Pulling – dissimilar materials will respond to collision energy and repair pulling forces differently. For example, high-strength steel (HSS) and ultra-high strength steel (UHSS) parts can transfer energy to parts made from lower strength steel. Knowing the strengths of the different materials will help determine the repairability, and how pulling forces may affect other parts of the vehicle. Vehicle structures may have a mix of steel, aluminum, and composite materials, each responding differently. To help improve cycle times and ensure proper repair methods are used, ensure all materials are identified up front and considered in the repair plan -Work Hardening – metal becomes work hardened each time it is formed, bent, stretched, or compressed -Molecular Grain Pattern and Straightening – metal has a molecular grain pattern. These grains give the metal its strength and cannot be seen with the naked eye. When a metal part becomes deformed, the internal structure or state of the material changes. During straightening, parts must be restored to their original state and shape. This requires vehicle dimensions to be restored. In addition to restoring vehicle dimensions, it is also important to restore cosmetic appearance. If either of these cannot be performed, the part should be replaced - - top is undamaged. Bottom is damaged and there is work hardening around the top loop -Characteristics – work hardening causes an increase in metal hardness and strength. As a part becomes more work hardened, it becomes more brittle. Work hardening can occur: -Initially when it is formed at the manufacturing plant. The sharper the corner of the part, the more work hardened it becomes -When a part is deformed during a collision -During straightening -Where an area is kinked, the grain structure will likely be irreversibly affected and it is likely to have microscopic cracking of the grain structure. -Note: Different metals have different work hardening rates. For example, aluminum and UHSS have much higher work hardening rates than mild steel -Top is undamaged. Middle is damaged. Bottom is straightened -Kink vs. Bend – the kink vs bend rule is no longer as simple as it sounds. A part may not look like it is kinked, but it may not be able to be straightened without cracking. If the part is bent, but the thickness or strength of the metal will not allow for straightening without leaving an area of permanent deformation, then replacement, either partial or complete, would be appropriate. There are collapse or crush zones built into some parts so they can absorb collision energy. A part collapsed in these areas usually requires replacement since a straightened collapse zone may not reach the same way in another collision -Micro-Cracking – the stronger the steel, the less repairable it becomes. For example, a slight bend in a UHSS part may crack when straightened if it is able to be straightened at all. As a best practice, do not straighten parts that are above 600 MPa unless specific documentation exists from the OEM. This is due to possible cracking and tearing of the part and possible damage to adjacent panels. If straightening is allowed by the OEM, a dye penetrant should be used to ensure no micro-cracking has occurred in the steel. Some vehicle makers do not recommend straightening UHSS parts that are above 600 MPa due to cracking and tearing of the part and damage to adjacent panels. Pulling Direction -Determining Pulling Direction – one technique used to determine clamp placement and pull direction is to grab the vehicle and pull on it as if the damage could be removed by hand. The position of the technician’s hands would determine where clamps should be placed. The angle of the technician’s arms will help determine chain angle. There are a number of different types of pulls used to remove damage. The one used is dependent on damage condition, equipment type, as well as technician experience. Measure the vehicle to help determine pulling direction. Note: Damage is typically removed following the first in, last out rule, removing damage furthest into the vehicle and working outward -Straightening Length – a change in length, also called mash, collapse or short rail, is a condition where any section of a part is shorter than factory specification. Mash may be corrected by pulling the part in the opposite direction of the impact. Some mash damage may be repairable, however, depending on the extent of the damage, partial or complete replacement of the part may be necessary -pull direction -Straightening Height – correcting height misalignment commonly involves pulling the part forward in addition to upward or downward. This is because there is often some degree of mash from the collision forces. The forward pull also helps prevent buckling the part during the pull. Straightening height may also require holding adjacent areas. Blocking may be used during a downward pull to prevent adjacent areas from being moved out of specification. Sag is a term commonly used to identify a part or area that is too low -Straightening Width – sway is a term commonly used to identify a part or area that has been pushed to one side by collision forces, causing a width alignment. Sway may be corrected by making a forward pull that is slightly slanted toward the direction of impact. Placing a block near bends in a part helps straighten the part and keeps it from hinging further back in the vehicle, creating additional damage. If there is a buckle or deformation in one side of the rail, pull on that side of the rail, as it will likely be short and this may help remove the damage -Side Collision – older vehicles were prone to a banana condition when hit on the side and would require pulling for length to restore the vehicle dimensions. Pulling for length is not as common on newer vehicles due to the extensive use of HSS and UHSS reinforcements in the rocker panel and passenger cabin area. This creates a stiffer structure that is less likely to distort the same way as older vehicles did. Vehicles that do not have the same level of reinforcement in the passenger cabin may require pulling to restore length to return door openings to proper dimensions to achieve proper fit and door operation -First line is length after collision. Bottom line is length before collision. -Front/Rear Sections and Floor Crossmembers – the front and rear sections still need to be measured because the center section misalignment will affect those sections. The amount of indirect damage to the front and rear sections may be less than what was seen in older models, but it may still be present depending on the severity of the impact. Floor crossmembers located between the lower B-pillars can push damage to the center hump area (or tunnel) or to the opposite inner rocker panel. It may be necessary to inspect these areas on vehicles involved in side-impact collisions Anchoring -Purpose of Anchoring – anchoring holds undamaged points in correct alignment while resisting the pulling force and distributes pulling forces between multiple anchoring points. Generally, the more anchoring points closest to the location of the pull will receive more force compared to anchoring there are, the less force is applied to each. However, the anchoring points closest to the location of the pull will receive more force compared to anchoring locations farther from the pull. For this reason, additional anchoring may be required closer to the source of the pull. Anchoring also allows pulling forces to be focused on the area of damage. Note: Proper anchoring is essential for correcting structural misalignment -Anchoring Locations – structural repair equipment makers typically identify anchoring locations for specific vehicles. This helps ensure that the anchoring clamps do not impede the measuring equipment or process, and are positioned on an area of the vehicle that will support its weight. Anchoring locations may also be identified by the vehicle maker in the service information. Structural repair equipment makers may also provide special clamp designs or adapters that are used for the variety of rocker panel configurations -Anchoring by Pinchwelds – when anchoring with pinchweld clamps, clamps should be attached at each corner of the vehicle center section. This is because no one part of a vehicle can withstand the entire force of a pull. In fact, the four pinchweld anchoring clamps may not be able to withstand the force of the pull. -Additional Anchoring Support – additional anchoring support is often necessary. Locations for additional anchoring support will depend on the location of damage and the direction of the pulls. Adjacent parts, such as center section splash shields, fuel or brake lines, or wiring harnesses may require removal or relocation to properly attach the pinchweld clamps or access measuring points. Seam sealer or undercoating may have to be removed from pinchwelds to ensure a secure anchor point -Initial Straightening – initial straightening may be required. Anchoring locations may have been moved out of specification during the collision. This may require initial straightening to return the anchoring point to the proper position. This can be done by leaving the out-of-position anchoring point to the proper position. This can be done by leaving the out-of-position anchoring point unclamped to allow it to be pulled into proper position. The area must be properly stress-relieved to help ensure it remains in the proper location naturally before tightening the pinchweld clamps -Positioning Anchors – anchoring locations must be able to withstand the weight of the vehicle when placed in clamps and the vehicle is not resting on the suspension or unloaded. Some systems anchor the vehicle with the vehicle resting on the suspension, or being loaded. In addition, the anchoring locations must withstand pulling forces without being damaged. Pulling force greater than the anchoring strength will damage the anchoring location. The anchoring locations may require relocation if the center section needs some initial straightening. The original anchoring locations may inadvertently lock damage into the center section if the anchor is in an area that needs to be moved -Alternate Anchoring Methods – some vehicles do not have pinchwelds, have pinchwelds which are too small for proper anchoring, or have pinchwelds that are irregularly shaped, angled, or horizontal. Anchoring vehicles without pinchwelds requires special adapters. These are generally available from the structural repair equipment maker. Note: Identify if special anchor clamps are needed before the vehicle enters the production stream to prevent delays in the repair process -Additional Anchoring Support – the rocker panel may not have strength to withstand the pulling forces alone. Additional anchoring may be necessary to support the vehicle during the straightening process. This may be done using: -Fixtures -Chains -Hydraulic rams -Wooden blocks -Note: Anchoring and blocking must be done in strong areas of the vehicle that will not bend or distort. Fixtures or wooden blocks can act as a fulcrum at the point where they are positioned. When determining where to place additional anchoring, take into consideration which parts of the vehicle need to move and which should not move. Jack stands should not be used to provide additional anchoring support -Considering Part Removal – when determining if a part should be removed or left on the vehicle, consider if the part can be used as an attachment point for pulling to help distribute pulling forces. If the part has considerable damage, it may lock other damage in and may not have the strength to be useful during the repair process. Does the part add structural integrity to the vehicle during the pulling process. Consider if the part will cause damage to undamaged areas of the vehicle during the straightening process. Pulling on the part should not cause additional damage to undamaged areas of the vehicle -Implications of Leaving a Part in Place – leaving a part on a vehicle instead of removing it may: -Restrict other parts from being pulled into proper position -Restrict the access to inner panels -Restrict access or hinder the attachment of anchors and straightening equipment -Cause adjacent panels to be pulled out of alignment -Note: Panels work hardened from a collision will require more pulling force to remove the damage Full-Frame/Unitized Structure Pulling Overview (2/3) Straightening Equipment Pulling Attachments -Clamps – there are a variety of clamps available for structural repairs. Having several clamps available may improve efficiency with the straightening process. For example, stopping a pull to remove a clamp and position it to another location is not the most efficient process -Bolt-On Clamps and Plates – attaching the chain to vehicle is done with special clamps designed for the part being pulled. Types of pulling hardware include bolt-on clamps, which bolt to a flange. Bolt-on clamps can be used for multi-directional pulls. This means that one clamp may be used to make both an upward and outward pull. Typical designs include one or two pairs of jaws. Bolt-on plates (draw bars or hole bars) have a variety of holes drilled into a steel plate. Some are designed to match general bolt patterns commonly found on a vehicle’s structure. Bolt-on plates may be designed to be used on specific parts, such as rails and strut towers, or the may be designed for a specific vehicle -Self-Tightening Jaw Clamps – self-tightening jaw clamps have a wedge and eye sandwiched in a clamp. A chain is attached to the eye. When tension is applied, the clamp tightens on the part. Expanding, self-tightening enclosed rail clamps are positioned inside the end of a rail. As tension is applied to the eye of this clamp, the jaws expand, and tightens within the rail, allowing the rail to be pulled. These are typically designed for vehicle specific applications -Hooks – hooks are available in a variety of configurations to access different areas, such as hinge pillars, inside wheel wells, or under rocker panels. Additional types of hooks are used to extend or shorten pulling chains or anchor the vehicle to the rack -Clamp Maintenance – clamp bolts will wear over time. Be sure to inspect bolts and replace them if damaged or stretched. A bolt that is not threading properly may be an indication that it is worn and should be replaced. Inspect the jaws of the pulling hardware for seam sealer or other types of material buildup that may cause it to slip during a pull. A wire brush or solvents may be required to remove residue from the jaws of the pulling hardware. Note: Ensure clamps are properly maintained to help minimize the chance for them to slip. Damage to equipment, vehicle, or personal injury may result from a clamp that slips off during the pull -Attachment Points – some examples of pulling attachment points include: -Bumper reinforcement mounting locations -Pinchweld flanges, such as along rails -The ends of rails -Damaged sheet metal. On parts that will be replaced, it may be beneficial to weld the pinchweld flanges together to prevent it from tearing apart during the pull -Some mechanical, steering, and suspension mounting points. However, this only applies to the mounting points on the vehicle structure. Straightening equipment should not be attached to steering and suspension parts -Positioning Clamps on the Vehicle – vehicle structures may require multiple clamps to distribute the pulling force and prevent tearing the metal. Considerations for the placement of pulling clamps include: -Location of the damage -Ability to attach the clamp to the vehicle structure -Ability of the part to withstand the force of the pull -Making sure undamaged parts do not become damaged -Clamp Attachment Considerations – clamps should be placed in positions that allow the part to move without causing more damage. Set the pulling clamp so that an imaginary line extends from the chain and passes through the center of the clamp teeth. The clamp should be considered another link in the chain. Depending on clamp configuration clamps may be designed to pull in one direction only or multiple directions. Adjust clamp placement as necessary during the pulling process. As pulling progresses, angles can change, which may require repositioning the clamp to ensure the part is being straightened in the intended manner. Note: Ensure clamp attachment point is clean and free contaminants to prevent clamp from slipping. Stop the pull and reattach or tighten if the clamp begins to slip -Weld-On Attachments – weld-on plates and attachments are typically used with clamps or a special adapter for making a pull. Excessive heat from welding can weaken HSS and UHSS parts. Part replacement, either completely or partially, is often required when weld-on attachments are used for structural straightening. If used to straighten an exterior cosmetic panel, part replacement may not be required. To prevent an accidental fire, be sure to remove flammable materials, such as foams, seam sealers, and adhesives from the repair area Pulling Towers -Pulling Towers – pulling towers may be used for anchoring parts of a vehicle and to makle simultaneous multiple pulls. Increasing the number of towers allows for multiple simultaneous pulls, which are useful for removing multiple types of damage conditions -Types of Pulling Towers – types of pulling towers include mobile towers, which can be removed from the bench and positioned where required around the vehicle, and attached towers which include track-mounted and pivoting. Track-mounted towers slide along a track on the outside of the rack or bench. This allows 360 degree access to the vehicle. Pivoting towers may have multiple towers that pivot at a single point beneath the rack or bench or individually, depending on its design. Knowing the capabilities and limitations of the equipment can help determine the best direction and position to load the vehicle on the repair equipment to reduce delays -Securing Towers – to prevent damage to the equipment and personal injury, pulling towers need to be properly secured before pulls are made. Mobile towers often use a wedge to secure the pulling tower once it is positioned. If this is not done, some angles can put downward force or lateral force on the tower that may damage tower pivot bearings. Mobile pulling towers have pivot points that allow the tower to be positioned in various angle configurations.
