Pins may be used to hold them in place -Straightening Equipment – straightening equipment is available in varying capacities. Common capacities include 5-ton and 10-ton. The capacity chosen is generally dependent on the job to be done. A stronger tower may be required depending on the amount of force needed to remove the damage. For example, a frame rail on a full-frame vehicle generally requires more force to remove damage compared to a lower rail on a unibody vehicle. Multiple towers may be used to increase force applied to one area Additional Tools -Pulling Chains – pulling chains are used to attach the pulling attachments to the pulling tower or hydraulic ram. Pulling chains are designed to withstand a specific tensile strength. For example, if using a 10-ton ram, the chain must be able to withstand 20,000 lbs or more of pulling force. Do not use tow chains as they may not be strong enough to be used for making structural pulls. Always use the chains that were provided with the equipment to ensure the tensile strength of the chain matches or exceeds the pulling capacity of the equipment -Nylon Pulling Straps – nylon pulling straps or slings may also be used in place of chains. Pulling straps: -Are available in varying lengths -Help protect the part being pulled from chain scratches -May be used in areas that are difficult to attach a clamp -Similar to chains, are designed to withstand a specific pulling strength. Pulling straps are primarily limited to lighter pulls -May not be strong enough to withstand the pulling capacity of the towers and must be carefully monitored using a pressure gauge -Safety Considerations – check chains and straps for damage or wear before and after use. Look for signs of stretching, bent links, and excessive corrosion. Replace the chain if it shows any signs of damage. Do NOT allow chains to come into contact with heat. This will reduce the strength of the chain and may cause it to break under tension -Hydraulic Rams – hydraulic rams may be used in place of or in addition to pulling towers and are available in various sizes and capacities. The size of the hydraulic ram used may vary according to the strength of the pull/push required and/or the angle required. 10-ton rams are typically used with most frame benches or racks and vector pulling. 4-ton rams are typically used for smaller work where portability and lower force is required. Either may use a manual or pneumatic pump Full-Frame/Unitized Structure Pulling Overview (3/3) Processes and Techniques Types of Pulls -Single Pulls – single pulls -Concentrate the pulling force into a small area -May be used to correct areas of direct damage -Can distort, overpull, or damage the metal -May not correct indirect damage -Work best when damage is confined to one area on the vehicle -Multiple Pulls – multiple pulls – -Spread the pulling force over a large area to minimize tearing -Allow pulling at different angles at the same time. This allows simultaneous removal of length, height, and width misalignment -Are more efficient than making one pull in one direction at one time -May be used to correct indirect damage -Note: It is important to remember that every anchoring, fixturing, or blocking point has the potential to act as a pulling point -Vector Pull – a vector pull uses a hydraulic ram to push on a chain. This can be done on a frame machine or floor system. The angle in which the ram is positioned will affect the direction of the pull. A chain is anchored at one end and the other end is attached to the pulling clamp. The hydraulic ram is placed about the midpoint of the chain. Vector pulling can be used as an additional pulling method in addition to towers and may be used as an anchor or provide a counterpull -Vector System Pulling Force – monitor the position of the hydraulic ram in relation to the chains when using vector pulling. A properly set up system can provide a great amount of pulling force. An improperly set up system can damage the equipment, vehicle, and possibly break the chain, resulting in personal injury. The angle of the corner where the ram contacts the chain should not be less than 90 degrees. An angle less than 90 degrees will result in considerable pressure being placed on the length of chain between the anchor and ram, with very little pulling power applied to the vehicle Pulling Force -Pulling Tower and Pully Chain Positioning – tower design will affect pulling capacity depending on the position of the chain on the tower. Pulling towers that wrap chins around the spines on the back of the tower will have reduced force with the chain higher on the tower and increased force lower on the tower. Pully-type towers do not chain pulling force with changes in chain height. The puller/collar lock knob should be tight to allow you to position the chain at the desired height but loosen when pulling. The collar should be loosened and lowered to the bottom position when not in use. Note: Do not twist chains, this can damage the chain and cause it to break when making pulls -Monitoring Pulling Force – pressure gauges can be used to monitor the pressure applied to the hydraulic rams and can help when pulling and stress relieving the structure. Pneumatic-powered hydraulic pumps produce a rhythmic beat, as the hydraulic pressure increases, the beat slows down. Hydraulic pumps that are powered electrically do not create the same type of sound, but you can still hear the pump sound change as pressure increases. If the structure is not moving as expected, and the hydraulic pump is slowing, re-evaluate the pulling set-up. Avoid overloading the equipment to prevent excessive wear or damage to the straightening equipment or vehicle. Note: Monitoring pressure gauges may help avoid exceeding the equipment capacity and prevent overpulling Pulling Techniques -Pulling Force – it is important to pay attention to the amount of force being used to remove damage. If a tremendous amount of force is being exerted on the vehicle and very little damage is being removed, it may be necessary to release the tension on the chains, reposition the chains and/or clamps, and make another pull from a different direction. Additional pulling points may also be required to remove all the damage -Pulling Direction – examples of pulling direction may include making a forward pull that is slightly slanted toward the direct of impact. Placing a block near bends in a part helps straighten the part and keeps it from hinging further back in the vehicle, thus creating additional damage -Basic Setups for Downward Pull – basic setups for making a downward pull may include: -Placing the chain lower on the pulling tower -Placing a pulley beneath the part to be pulled to direct the chain downward. Position the chain low on the tower when doing this to reduce excessive force on the pulley. -Placing a ram beneath the frame machine bench and looping a chain to it to push downward -Connecting a chain between the rack and the part and pulling on the center area of the chain to force the part downward -Basic Setups for Upward Pull – basic setups for making an upward pull may include placing the chain high on the pulling tower or placing a hydraulic ram under the area that needs to be raised. Vector pulls may also be used but typically require using an extension and careful positioning to be successful. When pulling or pushing a part upwards, it may be necessary to hold down in an adjacent undamaged area to prevent the vehicle from being lifted out of the clamps or damaged the anchor locations. Note: When making an upward pull on some towers, it is required to bolt the tower to the machine bench to avoid equipment damage Monitoring Pulls -Monitoring Measurements – when making pulls, monitor the readings on the measuring system. Some computerized measuring systems will read the position of parts in real time. Other systems will have to be monitored by stopping the pull at various stages and manually checking the measurements. Also, if using a fixture bench, monitor how closely the alignment points line up to the fixtures -Monitoring Effects of Pull – during straightening monitor the area being straightened and adjacent panels for tears and cracks. Monitor the anchor clamp points when pulls are made to ensure the vehicle is not moving and no damage is being done to the anchor locations. Pinchweld damage or movement at anchoring points indicates that additional anchors are necessary or too much force is being applied. Noises may be generated as the vehicle is being straightened. Be sure the noises are not because there is damage being inflicted to adjacent areas or to the anchor points. If necessary, stop the pull and reevaluate the pulling and anchoring setup-up -Varying Strengths or Thickness – the part being pulled may be a different strength or thickness than the adjacent parts that are attached to it. Additionally, portions on the same part may be made of varying thicknesses/strengths. For example, the rear portion of a front rail may be made of higher strength or thicker steel compared to the front porton. Higher stngth steel or thicker steel may resist the pulling force and can affect the straightening process similar to block. A damaged part that is being pulled may transfer the pulling forces to an adjacent area or a part that is made of lower strength steel. Note: It is also important to consider material strengths when planning the pull and determining anchoring locations -first (1.5mm (350MPa)), second (1.6mm (350MPa)), third box (2.0mm (350MPa)) Stress-Relieving -Stress-Relieving – stress-relieving helps release the internal stress caused by the damage and can be accomplished using vibration or heat. When metal is heated, it allows the compressed or tightened grain structure to relax during the straightening process. Using heat for stress-relieving should only be done if permitted by the vehicle maker. Stress-relieving is done while tension is still applied to the part. Straightening without stress-relieving may restore shape, but the structure may not stay aligned when it is returned to service -Heat Considerations – heat should only be used if permitted by the vehicle maker and when following temperature and time limits. Remove any flammable materials in the area on the vehicle. When heating a part, gradually heat the area around the damage. Keep tension applied to the steel, and allow the steel to cool naturally. Do NOT Quench with water. A sudden variation in temperature may cause changes to the metal grain structure. Temperature indicators should be used to avoid overheating the part being heated. Using several different temperature rating indicators can help manage the heating process. Note: heat should never be used for straightening a UHSS part unless it is going to be replaced -Stress-Relieving Using Vibration – stress-relieving can be done using a hammer to vibrate the metal, helping to realign the molecular structure and shape of the metal. Relieve stress while tension is applied to the structure. A hardwood block or body spoon may be used to distribute the striking force. Swing the hammer so it creates a spring-like effect off the steel. Stress-relieving should be done by striking formed areas of the part, such as corners or where there are multiple layers. Avoid striking directly on the damage, which is work hardened. This may transfer the forces to undamaged areas. Note: Avoid striking flat areas of a part directly with a hammer as this may cause additional damage -Cold Stress-Relieving Procedure – to properly stress-relieve using vibration, follow the procedure below: -1) Apply pulling tension to the part being straightened -2) Hold a block of wood or body spoon on the part -3) Strike the block or spoon with a hammer and repeat on adjacent panels. This may require backside support while hammering -4) Observe the pressure gauge on the pulling equipment (if equipped). A pulling tower with a hydraulic pressure gauge allows a technician to monitor the effectiveness of stress-relieving. The pressure will drop as stress-relieving is applied to the part. -5) Repeat the process until all damage has been removed. The panel must be returned to its original shape with dimensional accuracy -Note: In time, using the pressure gauges to monitor stress-relief will allow you to build a better understanding of the repairability limits of materials and the progress of the repair -First is before stress-relief. Second is after stress-relief -Side Damage – the straightening of side damage is limited due to the strength of the reinforcements. Side impacts can transfer collision energy to the opposite side of the vehicle. The instrument panel support beam may transfer collision energy to the opposite side if there is an impact to the A-pillar. Inspect for uneven gaps between the A-pillar trim and instrument panel trim between sides, or an improperly functioning glove box. This type of damage can be difficult to repair and usually requires extensive disassembly to access the damage. Floor crossmembers and interior braces may also transfer collision energy this way -Rear Construction – parts of the rear structure are interconnected to each other and tend to move together during a collision and during straightening. Measuring and monitoring the position of adjacent parts remains critical when straightening damage to the rear structure to ensure proper gaps for the deck lid, doors, bumper, and lights. Parts made of lower strength steel may be repairable with straightening. Note that some rear body panels and frame rails may be made of higher strength steels and have limited repair options -Rear Construction – Inner Reinforcements – inner reinforcements in the rear structure may transfer collision energy forward and upward. As the collision energy reaches HSS parts, such as the B-pillar reinforcement, weaker parts on the upper door frame may start to deform and cause a door gap issue. This type of damage may be difficult to identify and can present challenges to straighten because of limited clamp placement options. It may require adjacent panels to be removed to access the damaged inner structure -Part Inspection and Fit-Up – inspect straightened parts that are not intended to be replaced for visible damage, such as shiny, silvery splinters in the stressed area and cracks. If defects are found or excessive overpulling has occurred, the parts usually have to be replaced. Clamp marks should be repaired. Clamp marks can become a corrosion hot spot. After pulls are completed, check for proper operation of closure panels and proper fit of outer body panels Straightening Full-Frames -Frame Construction – full-frames are constructed of a thicker gauge than unibody parts. The different sections of the frame may vary in thickness and in steel strength. Frame tips are designed to collapse when impacted and should not be straightened unless allowed by the vehicle maker. Crossmembers are typically welded to the inner and outer sides of the rails and require more force to straighten because of the added stiffness. Crossmembers may also be HSS, which can also change energy transfer and complicate damage removal -Blue arrows are crush zones -Equipment Requirements for Frames – the straightening equipment must be large enough to accommodate full-frame vehicles. Full-frames typically require a higher pulling force compared to unibody vehicles. The equipment must be able to withstand the higher pulling forces required for pulling thicker gauge steel -Full-Frame Anchoring – special full-frame clamps or adapters are required when straightening full-frames to securely hold them in position. Frames may also be anchored by bolting the frame anchors to specific mounting holes in the frame. Wrapping chains around the frame for anchoring or pulling can cause permanent damage to the area where the chain contacts the frame and should only be done on areas that will be replaced or with additional reinforcing plates to avoid damage -Unitized Structure Pinchweld Clamps – when straightening the unitized structure on a full-frame vehicle, pinchweld clamps and/or body mount adapters should be used for anchoring the body when pulling. This may be in addition to anchoring clamps on the frame. Do not anchor only by the pinchwelds if the frame requires straightening. This will not provide sufficient strength, and it is likely that frame mounts and anchoring areas would be damaged if the unitized structure is clamped and pulls are made on the frame. The frame may not require anchoring if only parts of the body require straightening -Indirect Damage – there may be differences in how indirect damage shows up in other areas of the vehicle between sport utility vehicles (SUVs) and pickup trucks. On a pickup truck, the bed is separate from the front body structure. An SUV has a full unitized structure. Also, the rigidity and strength of the frame and body, as well as nature of the loss, will affect how indirect damage is transferred throughout the vehicle. This reinforces the importance of measuring the entire length of the vehicle -Considerations for Full-Frames – depending on the damage, it may be beneficial to leave the body attached to the frame when making pulls to the frame, as this may straighten indirect damage on the body. Full-frames have a tendency to have more spring-back than unitized body constructions. This may require a higher amount of overpulling than for unibody pats. It may be necessary to loosen or remove the body to prevent pulling forces from affecting an undamaged body. Stress-relieving while pulling is crucial to help ensure the frame remains at the repaired dimensions after repairs are complete -First is undamaged. Second is damaged -Diamond Condition – diamond condition is damage that occurs in the center section where one side is moved forward or rearward, causing the center section of the frame to be out of square, taking on a diamond-like appearance. The condition may be determined by crosschecking diagonal dimensions of the center section -Arrow is rail pushed rearward -Removing Diamond – diamond requires pulling one rail back to specification, while holding the opposite rail in place during the place. This requires leaving some anchoring bases loose on the damaged side to allow the center section to be pulled into alignment. Inspect body mounts and hardware as some realignment or part replacement may be necessary. Diamond may require removing body mounting bolts to permit overpulling to correct diamond. Diamond affects the rail-to-crossmember mounting locations. Monitor these locations during the straightening process. It is important to stress-relieve rail-to-crossmember mounting locations when removing diamond. Note: Some vehicles have oil- filled body mounts. These mounts can be damaged if subjected to collision and/or pulling forces -Blue arrow is pull forward. Orange circle is anchor undamaged area General Motors (BrightDrop) High Voltage Safety Systems (1/ Course 18400 00W High Voltage Systems Safety High Voltage Systems Characteristics and Safety -High Voltage Ranges – General Motors classifies voltages into three ranges: low, intermediate, and high. A color code system is used to indicate intermediate and high voltage cables. Low voltage ranges from 0 to 30 volts Direct Current (DC). Intermediate voltage ranges from 30 to 60 volts DC and have blue colored cables. High voltage is any voltage greater than 60 volts DC and have orange colored cables --Voltage Range -Direct Current -Low Voltage: 30 volts or less -Intermediate Voltage: between 30 and 60 volts -High Voltage: over 60 volts -Alternating Current -Low Voltage: 15 volts or less -Intermediate Voltage: between 15 and 30 Volts RMS -High Voltage: over 30 Volts RMS -Note: Some diesel engines may have orange-colored cables supplying power to the injectors. Diesel engines that use the piezo-type injectors use high voltage to fire the injectors. -Note: RMS stands for Root-Mean-Square, this represents the effective voltage when measuring AC voltage. The RMS voltage is lower than the peak voltage -High Voltage Labels – high voltage labels enable quick identification of potential electrical hazards. Color-coded labels identify areas where potential electrical hazards exist in high voltage systems. The labels are attached in various locations on the vehicle, including on the high voltage cables, battery, power electronics, transmission, and air conditioning compressor. The labels are color-coded to indicate the potential high voltage state: a red label indicates that high voltage is present at all time, an orange label indicates that high voltage should be present only when the system is active. -Orange High Voltage Warning Labels – the high voltage warning labels are orange, and indicate a potential shock hazard if high voltage is not properly disabled. These labels are located on all high voltage components, except the drive motor battery -Red High Voltage Danger Labels – the high voltage danger labels are red and indicate that high voltage is present at all times. These labels are located only on the drive motor battery -First Responders Label – the emergency / service personnel warning label provides general instructions to emergency personnel regarding high voltage disable procedures. The label also indicates that high voltage must be disabled prior to servicing the vehicle. The warning label is affixed at the front of the engine compartment and provides specific procedures for emergency and service personnel -Safety Preparation Steps – when working on high voltage systems, the work area must be prepared for safety before work is started -Standing Water – water may conduct electricity and provide an additional path for current flow. Always clean up any standing water in the work area before servicing high voltage electrical systems -Mark Areas – place safety cones around the vehicle and the work area to alert others that a high voltage system is being serviced. Cones are available as part of the high voltage safety kit EL-48900 -Tripping Hazards – clear the area of loose items and other tripping hazards. Electrical cords and cables should be removed, covered with a cable guard, or secured to the floor using tape -Remove Jewelry – metal jewelry, including watches, rings, and necklaces, can conduct electricity. Remove these before servicing a high voltage system -Remove Metal Objects – remove metal objects, such as mechanical pencils or tools, form your pockets because they may fall and create an arc hazard -Belt Buckles – some metal objects cannot be removed, like belt buckles. These should be covered to prevent accidental contact with high voltage Personal Protective Equipment and Safety Practices -Personal Protective Equipment – before working on any high voltage system, be sure to inspect and wear the appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) -Gloves – insulation gloves provide protection when working on live high voltage circutis -Specs on gloves in image: Salisbury. 9. ANSI/ASTM. Made in USA. D120. Class 0. Type I. Max use volt 1000v AC -Class 0 Gloves – GM requires class 0 gloves when working on hybrid and electric vehicles. Class 0 gloves are rated for protection up to 1000 Volts Alternating Current (VAC). Class 00 gloves do not provide the required protection, even though hybrid and electric vehicle batteries are below the 500 VAC rating. The hybrid battery may only be up to 360 VAC, but the alternating current to and from the electric motors may be higher, often exceeding the 500 VAC rating of class 00 gloves. Gloves with a higher class than 0 are rated for higher voltages and may be used, but the thicker rubber makes them impractical for performing tasks on hybrid and electric vehicle high voltage systems -Electrical Insulation Glove Classes. Class maximum AC voltage maximum DC voltage label color -Class -AC Voltage -DC Voltage -Label Color -00 -500 -750 -beige -0 -1,000 -1,500 -red -1 -7,500 -11,250 -white -2 -17,000 -25,500 -yellow -3 -26,500 -39,750 -green -4 -36,000 -54,000 -orange -Electrical Gloves – electrical gloves consist of two parts, the leather outer shell and the rubber inner shell. The leather outer shell provides no protection from electricity, but it does protect the rubber inner glove from being damaged by sharp objects. Gloves should be stored in the box or bag provided by the manufacturer. Direct sunlight will dry out the rubber and cause it to crack. The gloves should never be stored when folded, compressed, or inside out, as this will cause the gloves to crease and crack. Never use either part of the electrical insulation gloves for anything except when working with high voltage. Contaminating the gloves with engine oil or chemicals will degrade the rubber and reduce the insulating properties -Gloves Identification – the label on the gloves provides several important pieces of information, including the size, class, rating, and manufacturer. Class 0 gloves have a red-colored label. A beige label indicates class 00 gloves, which do not provide sufficient protection. The glove color is not an indication of the protection they provide. -Date Stamp – new gloves may be used up to 12 months from the date of manufacture, if they are removed from an unopened box. When the gloves expire, they must be recertified every 6 months. When the gloves are recertified, the date of recertification is stamped on the glove, near the label. The timeframe for using the gloves starts on the date stamped on the glove, not the date of purchase -Safety Glasses – safety glasses are required when within 15 meters (50 feet) of a hybrid or electric vehicle, either indoors or outdoors. The safety glasses must have protective side shields -Rubber Soled Shoes – rubber soled shoes provide additional electrical insulation to the ground as well as increased traction to avoid slipping while working on high voltage electrical systems -Safety Practices – in addition to preparation and PPE, there are some best practices that improve safety. -Never work alone – always make sure that someone is available to provide assistance in case of any emergency -One hand rule – when possible, work using only one hand when working on live high voltage circuits. The other hand should not be touching anything, keep it behind your back. This limits the possibility of completing a circuit/ this also prevents the most dangerous current flow – in one had, through your heat, and out the other hand -Cover Terminals – removed terminals should be covered using the EL-50209 high voltage terminal covers or electrical tape rated for 600 volts or higher High Voltage Systems Safety Tools and Disabling -Disabling High Voltage – when servicing high voltage systems on a hybrid or electric vehicle, the system should be disabled to remove the power. Always consult Service Information (SI) for a detailed procedure for disabling the vehicle -Unplug the Vehicle – the vehicle must be disconnected from all external power sources. On plug-in hybrid and electric vehicles, the charge cord must be unplugged from the vehicle. Disconnect any 12 V battery chargers from the vehicle -Shut Off the Vehicle – turn the key to the OFF position, remove the key from the ignition, and secure the key in a locked drawer. If equipped with the Passive Entry Passive Start (PEPS) systems, push the button and ensure the vehicle powers down. Move the fob away from the vehicle and secure it in a locked drawer at least 5 meters (15 feet) away from the vehicle. After removing the fob, test the vehicle and verify that the vehicle will not power up. This ensures that another fob was not left in the vehicle. Securing the key or fob ensures that other persons will not attempt to start the vehicle while the high voltage system is being serviced -Disconnect the 12V Battery – disconnect the 12V battery. The contactors for the high voltage battery require power from the 12V battery to operate. Disconnecting the battery ensures the contactors will not close while the system is being serviced. Caution: it is possible for a contactor to fail in the closed position. Do not assume that disconnecting the 12V battery removes all high voltage from the vehicle. -Remove or Open the Disconnect – remove the manual disconnect plug or open the disconnect switch. If equipped with a disconnect plug, the plug should be secured in a locked drawer to prevent anyone else from installing it while the high voltage system is being serviced. If equipped with a switch, the switch should be wire-tied open to prevent anyone from closing the switch while the high voltage system is being serviced -Wait – wait at least 5 minutes after removing/opening the disconnect. Most high voltage systems contain capacitors that may remain charged for several minutes -Test – use a meter to confirm that no high voltage is present. Confirming that no voltage is present requires using the Live-Dead-Live test. To complete this test, set the meter to DC voltage. Test the meter on the 12V battery. A reading of 12 volts indicates the meter is working properly. Test the high voltage circuits for the presence of high voltage. Generally, a reading under 3 volts is considered dead. Finally, retest the meter on the 12V battery. This confirms that the meter did not fail while testing the high voltage. Consult Service Information for instructions related to voltage readings over 3 Volts on the high voltage circuits -Multimeters – digital multimeters come in a variety of categories and voltage ratings. -Digital Multimeter – the digital multimeter (DMM) must have a minimum rating of category III and 1000 volts. Lower ratings are not sufficient to protect against arc flash from high voltage transients or spikes. The Fluke 87 and the Fluke 1587 are both rated for category III and 1000 volts -Leads - the leads should have the category and voltage ratings stamped into them. The meter system takes on the ratings of the lowest rated part. If category II leads are used, the complete system becomes category II. The plastic covers on the leads are there to prevent arc flash. Leads that are missing the plastic covers are no longer considered category III leads. The leads should be inspected for damage before every use, similar to the gloves Internal Battery Service and First Responder Guides -Internal Battery Service – some faults required opening the high voltage battery case to service. When inside the case, the high voltage battery is considered live at all times. There is no method to disable the voltage from the battery. Depowering the battery removes most of the energy from the battery, but a depowered battery still contains high voltage and remains dangerous -First Responder Guides – the first responders guide contains information for first responders on handling the vehicle. It contains information on the location of warning labels and how to safely disable the vehicle. It also shows the cut points for cutting the 12V power to the vehicle, which will remove power from the contactors and power down the high voltage system -First Responder Information – the first responders guide for all GM hybrid and electric vehicles can be found on the GM Service Technical College website and the National Fire Protection Association website. -Test results 1) High Voltage Labels – enable quick identification of potential electrical hazards -High Voltage Ranges – any voltage greater than 60 volts and have orange colored cables -First Responder Labels – provide general instructions to emergency personnel regarding high voltage disable procedures 2) which three of the following statements are the best practices to improve safety -Use only one hand when working on live high voltage circuits -Never work alone -Cover removed terminals using the EL-50209 high voltage terminal covers or electrical tape rated for 600 volts or higher 3) which two of the following statements describe the type of information in the first responder guide - the first responders guide shows the cut points for cutting the 12v power to the vehicle -the first responders guide contains information on the location of warning labels and how to safely disable the vehicle 4) what category must the DMM be rated -the DMM must be rated a category III and 1000 volts or higher 5) which three of the following statements about the first responder labels are correct -the label provides general instructions to emergency personnel regarding high voltage disable procedures -the label indicates that high voltage must be disabled prior to servicing the vehicle -the label is affixed at the front of the engine compartment and provides specific procedures for emergency and service personnel General Motors (BrightDrop) High Voltage Safety Systems (2/ Course 18420 17W High Voltage Depower Tool Usage -High Voltage Depowering – in severe crashes involving high voltage vehicles where the airbags deploy, post-crash inspections of the high voltage systems are expected, the need to depower the high voltage systems in these vehicles, however, should be infrequent. If you have any questions or concerns regarding High Voltage inspections or depowering, please contact the Technical Assistance Center -High Voltage Disabling – the high voltage disabling procedure utilizes two possible methods to disable high voltage: -Note: the high voltage disable procedure, regardless of the method, only de-energizes the high voltage circuits and components outside of the Hybrid/EV battery pack, dangerous voltage levels always exist within the Hybrid/EV battery pack -1) – scan tool method: the on-vehicle control module software, initiated by the GDS2 scan tool, disables and discharges high voltage outside of the Hybrid/EV battery pack -2) – DMM method: if the GDS2 Scan Tool Method does not complete or cannot be implemented, a DMM-based physical measurement method is performed at certain high voltage connectors - A successful GDS2 Scan Tool high Voltage Procedure will: -1) perform an analysis of the vehicle condition by verifying specific DTCs have run and passed -2) calculate the high voltage isolation resistance to the vehicle chassis -3) open the high voltage contactor relays and discharge the high voltage systems -4) latch a Service Lockout condition to prevent unintended enabling of the high voltage systems Questions Questions 1)True or false, no part of the vehicle inspection or high voltage depowering procedure is covered under the GM factory warranty -True 2) after a collision of a high voltage vehicle, what are the two most unique inspection procedures GM recommends? -Component initial and high voltage operation -3) what procedure must be done prior to performing the component initial inspection -High voltage disable procedure -4) the only way to clear the High Voltage Lockout State is to use the Clear Secured High Voltage DTC procedures using the scan tool Control Functions for which 2 modules -HPCM and HPCM 2 -5) if a high voltage depowering procedure is necessary, what two modules must be isolated from the system by removing their fuses and jumping the non-powered legs of the fuses together -HPCM2 and BECM -6) how far away from other vehicles, buildings, or combustibles does General Motors recommend a damaged high voltage vehicle be stored? -50 feet (15 meters) -7) when a vehicle is involved in a collision of sufficient intensity, what causes the high voltage contactor relays to open -Control power is removed by the HPCM2 -8) what is the voltage threshold in which a high voltage battery depowering procedure would not be necessary -285 -9) what must be installed to create a series circuit within the battery during depowering -The manual service disconnect -10) where should a damaged high voltage battery pack or section be returned to -recyclemybattery General Motors (BrightDrop) High Voltage Safety Systems (3/ Course 22048. 55V Preventing Unintended Airbag Deployment Questions -1) on many GM vehicles the roll over sensor is incorporated into the ____ -SIR module -2) when servicing the SIR module ___ -disconnect the SIR module before disconnecting the battery, it prevents voltage spikes -3) when re-installing the SIR module the negative battery cable should not be connected until ___ -after the module connector is installed -4) which of the following is not a step in the SIR disable procedure -Disconnect all electrical connectors from the SIR module -5) according to the video which of the following constitutes many of the unintended airbag deployments during service -Roof rail airbag General Motors (BrightDrop) High Voltage Safety Systems (4/ Course 18044.40W Vehicle Network Security -Vehicle Network Security – network security protects modules on the General Motors Local Area Network (GMLAN) from being controlled by unauthorized network messages. This course will provide an overview of the network components purpose and function as well as the faults associated with the network. Vehicle Network Security Overview -Types of Vehicle Interfaces – vehicle interfaces provide customers with a greater level of connectivity and convenience. They also increase the risk that harmful software could enter the infotainment system or OnStar system and potentially affect the operation of the vehicle. -Cellular – the cellular interface is integrated to the Telematics Communication Interface Control module. The cellular interface supports the OnStar system -Wifi-with the appropriate OnStar subscription, the vehicles wifi interface, if equipped, provides customers with in-vehicle access to the internet. The WiFi interface may be integrated to the Telematics Communication Interface Control Module. The interface may also be built into the radio. A WiFi interface may also be built into the HMI module, if equipped -BlueTooth – the Bluetooth interface provides features like hands-free calling using a cellular phone and may support streaming audio from a compatible digital media player. Depending on the vehicles options, the Bluetooth interface may be integrated to the Telematics Communication Interface Control Module; the Radio or the HMI module, if equipped -USB – the USB interface allows the infotainment system to read media, such as MP3 files from the USB storage devices, interface with digital media players, and support infotainment features like phone projection. The USB interface is also used in the service department to carry out infotainment software updates. The USB interface may be an input to the Radio, or to the HMI module, if equipped. Identify that the USB interfaces with the Radio or HMI module depending on vehicle equipment -Types of Isolated GMLAN Networks -an isolated GMLAN is an electrically separate section of a network that connects the components of the Infotainment and OnStar systems. An Isolated GMLAN exchanges messages with the rest of the GMLAN network via the Serial Data Gateway Module -Isolated High-Speed GMLAN – the modules in the isolated High-Speed GMLAN are highlighted. Note that the Serial Data Gateway Module contains a terminating resistor for the isolated GMLAN -Isolated Low-Speed GMLAN – the modules in the isolated Low-Speed GMLAN are highlighted -Serial Data Gateway- the Serial Data Gateway Module, sometimes called the Central Gateway Module or the First Generation Gateway Module, behaves as a “firewall”” between the GMLAN and Isolated GMLAN data buses. The Serial Data Gateway Module allows only authorized network traffic to pass from the Isolated GMLAN to the rest of the GMLAN data buses. All abnormal messages are blocked. The Serial Data Gateway Module also helps to reduce the number of modules connected to the GMLAN by managing network traffic between other networks. Question – Which GMLAN exchanges messages with the rest of the GMLAN via the Serial Data Gateway Module -Isolated GMLAN Vehicle Network Faults -First Generation Serial Data Gateway Module – the Serial Data Gateway Module can set Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs) if a fault prevents communication over an Isolated GMLAN. -DTC U007D – a short-to-ground on the Isolated Low-Speed GMLAN may set DTC U007D -DTC U007B - a short between the Isolated High-Speed GMLAN wires may set DTC U007B -Second Generation Serial Data Gateway Module – the K56 Serial Data Gateway Module Generation 2 is used to handle communications between multiple GMLAN buses and functions as a gateway to isolate the secure networks from the unsecured networks -Uses – the K56 Serial Data Gateway Module mitigates bus loading to support cyber security and new active/advanced safety features (if equipped). It is used as a frame-to-frame gateway for all functional messages -Communication – communication between the K56 Serial Data Gateway Module and a scan tool is done through the primary High-Speed GMLAN bus. When the K56 Serial Data Gateway Module is not communication, the scan tool cannot communicate with the vehicle. If the communication does not exist or the particular modules have not been programmed, control modules will not be able to communicate with or through the K56 Serial Data Gateway Module -Microprocessors – the K567 Serial Data Gateway Module has two microprocessors within the electronic control unit. Each microprocessor is diagnosed/programmed independently via the scan tool because the two microprocessors do not talk internally. Each of these processors is responsible for managing the traffic for specific communication buses on the vehicle. The two particular buses that are managed are high-speed and low-speed. Note: the Data Bus Diagnostic Tool will not work with vehicles equipped with the K56 Serial Data Gateway Module Generation 2 -Serial Data Gateway Module Processor 1-High Speed Microprocessor- General Motors (BrightDrop) High Voltage Safety Systems (5/ Course 10041. 14W-R2 Service Information (SI) Overview Introduction- this module covers how to find service manuals and other publications from the Service Information (SI) home page. Upon completion of this module, you should be able to identify. -SI’s purpose, accessibility, and main characteristics -Types of publications and general searching techniques -Navigational functions of icons on the home page -Service Information – SI is a comprehensive source of the latest diagnostic and repair information for most GM products. SI helps technicians to ensure that GM vehicles continue to meet safety and quality standards. SI contains service bulletins, campaign information, repair procedures, component locations, and schematics. SI also contains a feedback interface that enables technicians to communicate any concerns they may have with the system or its content -Accessing Service Information – technicians can access Service Information through Techline Connect. After logging into Techline Connect, select the SI button on the dashboard’s left-hand navigation bar to open the home page for GM Service Information -Danger Statement – it is important for technicians to be safe and take all precautions when diagnosing and servicing vehicles. The danger statement in SI provides instructions to reduce the risk of personal injury and property damage. Technicians must read and accept the danger statement to view the information in SI -Service Information Home Page – the SI home page is divided into three areas. Beginning at the top. Select each highlighted to area to learn more. -Top Area – the top area provides drop-down menus to select a specific region and language. Selecting a region from the Region drop-down menu filters the information to the selected region region. Depending on the region, multiple languages are available. The top area is like many Web browsers with features such as forward and back, refresh, and search. -Middle Area – the middle area incorporates two ways to search for vehicle-specific information. To search for a vehicle using the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) all 17 characters are required. If the VIN is not available, search for the vehicle using the Year, Make, and Model drop-down menus -Bottom Area – the bottom area contains hyperlinks to additional information and search options. To access these options, select the icon or hyperlink. These icons may appear at the top of any page within SI and function in the same manner no matter which page the links are on -Finding Publications for a Specific Vehicle – to review publications for a specific vehicle, you would select the year, make, and model from the drop-down menus. In this example, the selections have been made for the 2022 Cadillac CT4. -Available Publications – the available publications for the 2022 Cadillac CT4 appear on the screen. To see listings in the CT4 Service Manual, select the highlighted hyperlink -Service Category Types – the next screen reveals a list of service category types from the service manual for the CT4. Select Engine/Propulsion to continue with this example -Service Categories – there are several service categories within the Engine/Propulsion service category type -Bulletin List – bulletins are grouped by the type of information they contain -Service Bulletin – service bulletins are identified by a bulletin number and a Document ID number. Highlighted above is the path followed to get to this point in SI. To return to the list of all bulletins for the vehicle, you would select the previous link in the path -Quick Links – eight hyperlinks, known as quick links, are arranged horizontally underneath the search fields. These are shortcuts to access information within SI for a particular vehicle. -Fastener Specification – the fastener specification quick link brings up a list of components or repair areas for the selected vehicle. This example shows the selection of fastener specifications for an automatic transmission, followed by the selection of the desired repair area. The path leads to a table that provides fastener information, including links to the available procedures related to each fastener -RPO Code List – selecting RPO Code List provides a list of all Regular Production Option codes that are available for the selected vehicle, along with their descriptions. Technicians may further search by RPO code or by description. This example demonstrates a search using the IOT radio RPO -Maintenance Items – the maintenance items quick link displays a list of links to maintenance information, such as fluid and lubricant recommendations, oil life system resetting, and tire rotation -Master Electrical Component List – this quick link leads to the Master Electrical Component List in table format, as shown. The table has the precise location of each component, Along with its name and code designation. Hyperlinks in the table lead to more data and graphics such as connector end views -Diagnostic Starting Point – the diagnostic starting point quick link leads to the hyperlink Diagnostic System Check – Vehicle, which offers a strategy to identify publications related to the customers concern. To identify a control module that is not communicating or has a set diagnostic trouble code (DTC), to address power mode concerns, to verify that the engine cranks and runs, and to make a diagnosis based on symptoms -Diagnostic Trouble Code List – this quick link provides a searchable list of all DTCs related to the selected vehicle -Symptom List – the symptom list quick link brings up symptoms for various automotive systems, which may be useful in the diagnostics of the selected vehicle -Control Module References – this quick link displays the Control Module References table, which contains global component codes and hyperlinks to control module and scan tool information, wiring schematics, repair instructions, and programming and setup instructions for control modules -Search Types – SI provides a way to search for all types of publications for a specific vehicle. For this example, begin the search by selecting the highlighted options from each drop-down menu. Year: 2022, Make: GMC truck, and Model: Yukon XL-4WD/ then select the highlighted next button to continue -Basic Search – the first search type, Basic Search, enables the technician to enter up to five keywords that may appear in any order in documents or document titles. To learn more about the second search type, select the search by phrase link -Search by Phrase – this search type enables the entry of a complete phrase of up to five words. The results will include documents or titles containing the exact phrase -Search Filters – the search types Basic Search and Search by Phrase provide drop-down menus and entry fields that serve as search filters, enabling the technician to refine the search results -Document or Title Search – using the drop-down highlighted here, you can choose to search within the content of all the documents for the vehicle or to limit the search to titles only -All or Any Search – match all, any, or beginning keywords using the drop-down menu highlighted here. These options are only available when using the Basic Search function -Keyword Search – enter one of more keywords into this search field. Valid characters for this search are letters, numbers, forwards slashes (/), periods, and hyphens. Another option is to select Search Help, which will provide a reminder of the search rules -New Bulletins – icons and hyperlinks on the Service Information home page provide access to search options, guides, and plentiful vehicle information. For this example, select the New Bulletins icon to go to the most recently released bulletins, campaigns, and preliminary Information. Documents that have arrived in the last week are displayed here. To view documents that have arrived for a different time interval or vehicle, you would make the appropriate selections from the highlighted drop-down menus -Finding the Next Term – the Service Information system offers a way to find the next instance of a term within the listings. The technician may enter the desired term in the search box at the top of the screen, and the system will automatically highlight all instances of that term. Selecting the up or down arrows highlights the next or previous instance of the term -Number Search – all SI bulletins, campaigns, and preliminary information have a document identification number. Instead of searching for information by vehicle type, try searching by a document number. The two types of numeric searches are Bulletin or Campaign Number search and Document ID search. Do not use any spaces or dashes when entering a Document ID number. Bulletin or Campaign numbers may be entered with or without the dashes. Select the highlighted Search button to view bulletin number 22-NA-022 -Year Range + Platform + Keyword Search – selected highlighted blue hyperlinlk -Unit Repair -the unit repair icon links to available unit repair manuals, specialty publications, and supplemental transmission technical guides -Unit Repair Categories – the unit repair content is organized like service manual content. To navigate, you would select each appropriate hyperlink until you find the desired document. In this example, we will locate the specifications for the 4T65-E Automatic Transaxle. -Unit Repair Category Type – this page contains hyperlinks to additional divisions in the Unit Repair Manual -List of Available Transmission – the next page provides a list of transmissions available in the 2006 model year. -Categories of Information – several categories of information are available for the 4T65-E automatic transaxle -Transmission General Specifications – view the information contained in the Transmission General Specifications document -User Guide – the User Guide icon provides hyperlinks to information on GM diagnostic tools, applications, and service procedures -User Guide – the user guide icon provides hyperlinks to information on GM diagnostic tools, applications, and service procedures. -Feedback – the feedback option enables you to communicate any questions, issues, or concerns experienced while using Service Information -Feedback Form – the Service Information Feedback Form provides an opportunity to describe problems that you may encounter and make suggestions for improvements. When reporting a problem, specific information is required. Describe exactly what happened, when the problem occurred, and whether the problem can be reproduced -Help – Service Information provides a link from the home page to various topics that help you to navigate and use the system. The subsequent page contains a list of hyperlinks that lead to the Help topics. To return to this list of topics from anywhere in SI, you would select the Help icon in the upper right corner of the screen Question : 1)Automotive technicians can access GM Service Information (SI) through which one of the following systems Techline Connect 2)Which one of the following items is NOT found within the Service Information (SI) Scan tool software 3) Drag the name of each Service Information quick link on the right to its correct description on the left Leads to the hyperlink Diagnostic System Check – vehicle, which offers a strategy to resolve the customers concern -&gt; diagnostic starting point Displays a table containing component details and hyperlinks that go to graphics, such as connector end views -&gt; master electrical component list Lists symptoms for various automotive systems, which may be useful in the diagnostics of the selected vehicle -&gt; symptom list Provides a path to scan tool information, schematics, repair instructions, and setup instructions for control modules -&gt; control module references Offers a list of vehicle components to choose from, leading to illustrations and repair procedures -&gt; fastener specifications 4) drag each label on the left to the correct hyperlink icon from the Service Information home page 1) new bulletins 2) number search 3)unit repair 4)feedback 5)help -Navigation Icons – service manual documents typically include tables, images, and electrical wiring schematics. After accessing a service manual, you will find wiring schematics under the Schematic and Routing Diagrams hyperlink. -Location Icon – the Location (LOC) icon links to the Master Electrical Component List containg the global component code, name, and location of each electrical component for the selected vehicle. The list also contains hyperlinks to locator views and connector end view for each component -Description Icon – the description (DESC) icon opens the associated Description and Operation document -Control Module References Icon – the control module references icon brings up a table containing hyperlinks to scan tool information, associated system schematics, repair instructions, and programming and setup instructions -Scalable Vector Graphics Viewer – SI uses an SVG viewer, which is an enhanced graphics viewer for all graphics, images, and wiring schematics. The SVG viewer features viewing tools such as hotspots, links, and larger illustrations. It is available for viewing components, harness routing diagrams, and other electrical sections, as well as viewing graphics on a tablet or smartphone. Each graphic and image in SI has a magnifying glass. -SVG Viewer Toolbar -the SVG viewer toolbar enables users to enlarge or rotate a graphic, zoom in or out, fit the image to the height or width of the window, print the graphic, and perform other functions. Edited graphics can be printed or saved to a desktop. When hovering over a toolbar icon on a personal computer, a tool tip and corresponding message appear in the browser status bar indicating the icons functionality. -Hyperlink Navigation – hyperlink navigation mode enables the detection of hotspots within the displayed image when the cursor moves over a hotspot. If the SVG viewer detects, the object is highlighted, along with either a screen tip or a link, depending upon the hotspots attribute. -Magnify Area – magnify area mode replaces the cursor with a movable magnifying glass rectangle. A 50% zoomed in view of the displayed image appears around the current cursor position. A partially enlarged view of the displayed image appears without having to change the zoom factor -Interactive Zoom – this mode enables you to zoom in on a rectangular area that you select. To select an area, you would move the cursor to a corner of the intended area, press and hold the left mouse button, drag the cursor to the opposite corner of the intended area, and then release the mouse button. An enlarged display of the selected area will fit into the current view window, while keeping the aspect ratio of the image -Pan – in pan mode, the image can be shifted within the view window. Shift the currently visible part of the image by moving the mouse to the intended original position, press the left mouse button down, and drag the cursor to the intended target position. The shifted view will be the new current view once the mouse button is released. This feature is only available when the image is zoomed into by at least 10% Etc etc more of the bar icons. Question 1) Which three of the following statements about viewing and editing schematics are TRUE Technicians can view schematics and other graphics within many service manual documents Red liner objects are visual additions to a schematic Every electrical schematic contains information and navigation icons 2) which three of the following are red liner objects Highlight Ellipse Text Test Questions 1)GM Service Information (SI) is available to everyone through the internet False 2)Which two statements about Service Information (SI) are TRUE SI Contains service bulletins, campaign information, repair procedures, component locations, and schematics SI Provides various ways to search for publications, tables and schematics 3)Which one of the following statements describe a valid characteristic of Service Information (SI) Icons on the home page have the same functions no matter where the user finds them 4) drag each icon Binoculars House Newspaper Mail 5) drag each icon Wrench Newspaper Question mark Magnifying glass 6)which three statements related to searching for information about a specific vehicle in Service Information (SI) are TRUE If the vin is not available – etc Choosing a vehicle service manual – etc One way to begin a search – etc 7) Number search Search by phrase Search filter Basic filter 8) Desc Arrow Loc Car arrow wrench 9) 1 10) Selecting A hand icon Red liner The tool 18430. 30W Battery Electric Vehicle, Introduction Heating and Cooling System Operation and Damage Analysis (1/2) Cooling System Operation Cooling System Overview -Cooling Systems – cooling systems help maintain proper engine operating temperatures. Heat generated by the engine is transferred to coolant, which then is circulated through the cooling system, reducing the temperature. This transfer of heat is accomplished by: -Conduction -Convection -Radiation -Note: If one of these methods is not operating efficiently, the other methods will be overloaded as they attempt to provide proper cooling. The cooling system is often damaged during a front end collision, and making a thorough inspection of all cooling system parts is necessary. -Liquid-Cooled Engine Systems – liquid-cooled systems comprise a large segment of cooling systems used in modern vehicles -System Function – liquid-cooled systems utilize a constant flow of coolant through the cooling system. Liquid (coolant) is moved through the cooling system by a water pumper; however, heat is removed from the coolant by the radiator and heater core. In a liquid-cooled system, the temperature of the system is controlled by a thermostat -System Inspection – when inspecting liquid-cooled systems, watch the surge tank for coolant flow. Also, feel the radiator hoses to monitor water flow, and monitor coolant temperature with a non-contact thermometer. Another system to be aware of is an air-cooled system. Air-cooled systems are cooled by a fan forcing air through the cooling fins and require unrestricted airflow for proper cooling. These systems are typically a thing of the past, so most repair facilities will not see air-cooled systems except during a restoration project. Note: When inspecting for coolant flow, be mindful that some vehicles pressurize very quickly. Radiator -There are numerous parts that comprise a radiator assembly -Drain Valve – radiator drains may be made of plastic or metal, and are typically located low on the side or bottom of the tank. The drain valve, also referred to as a petcock or plug, may be a threaded design requiring multiple turns to open, or may only require a partial rotation to open. A drain valve turned in the wrong direction, or too much, can cause permanent damage. Be sure not to use impact tools on drain valves. -Gaskets – gaskets provide a seal between the side tank and radiator core -Radiator Core – the radiator core is constructed of small metal fins that are mounted with tubes that flow horizontally or vertically. The fins conduct the heat from the tubes and transfer it to the air flowing through the radiator. The cores must be in good working condition to efficiently remove heat from the coolant. If the cores are bent or plugged, they will not function properly. -Transmission Oil Cooler – depending on the system, one or both side tanks may house a transmission oil cooler. The transmission oil cooler exchanges heat with the coolant in the radiator -Side Tanks – one side tank will be where the hot coolant flows in and then is distributed to the radiator cores. The reduced temperature coolant flows back into the system through the other side tank -Draining Procedures – when draining a cooling system, be sure that the engine is turned off. Water pump damage and engine part failure will occur if the engine is left running. Remove the radiator cap to avoid creating a vacuum in the system, and do not work on a cooling system that is under pressure. Be sure to inspect the coolant as it drains. Watch for sediment, metal flakes, and other material. If present, the system will more than likely need to be flushed before being refilled. Note: Always reference vehicle maker service procedures to identify correct repair procedures, precautions, and mandatory repairs before beginning repairs on any cooling system -Radiator Damage Analysis – there are several situations to consider when inspecting a radiator for various types of damage -Blockage and Bent Radiator Fins – blockages in a radiator can be caused by debris flowing through the cooling system, corroded radiator metal, and chemical breakdown of the coolant. Bent or missing radiator fins can be caused by gravel, birds, and large bugs, and will reduce airflow and increase the operating temperature. The radiator fins may be straightened using a fin comb. Radiator fin spacing can vary, so it is important to select the proper comb spacing -Radiator Tank Leaks – as a radiator ages, it may develop leaks. For example, leaks may develop on some radiators between the core and the tanks due to the brass composition and the soldered joint corroding. Coolant leaks may also occur where a plastic tank attaches to the core, as aging can cause the gasket to deteriorate -Monitoring Cooling System Operation – after a repair has been completed to the cooling system, it is important to verify the operating condition of the system for quality control purposes. An engine can overheat due to: -Plugged radiators -A damaged water pump -Obstructions in the coolant flow -A damaged head gasket -Air in the system -Note: A non-contact thermometer can be used to monitor cooling system temperature -Pressure Testing – cooling systems must be pressure tested for leaks and to ensure that pressure is maintained. Pressure tests are used to pressurize the system to verify that proper pressure is maintained and to identify any leaks in the system. When pressure testing a cooling system, visually inspect for leaks near the hose clamps and part joints, and heater box, both inside and outside the vehicle. It may be beneficial to leave the system pressurized for about one hour to watch for a pressure drop. Note: Do not over-pressurize the cooling system or the components may be damaged -Inspection Considerations – when pressure testing the cooling system and checking for internal engine cooling system problems, note that a failing head gasket or intake gasket can allow coolant out of the cooling system and into the engine. A damaged head gasket can also force coolant out of the system as a result of engine compression being forced into the cooling system. Note: Leaking head or intake gaskets allowing coolant to leak into the engine may result in the engine oil having a foamy or milky appearance -Radiator Caps – radiator caps perform many critical functions in a cooling system, such as regulating system pressure and keeping the system sealed from air and other contaminants. Radiator caps also hold the system under pressure to increase the boiling point of the coolant and to allow coolant flow from the coolant system into the overflow tank. Faulty radiator caps can cause many cooling system problems. Because of this, radiator caps should be pressure tested. Pressure testing helps identify the proper boil-over protection, and locates possible cooling system failure areas -Hose Clamps – the two types of hose clamps commonly used for radiator and heater hoses are spring steel and screw band. A spring hose clamp is made of spring steel with barbs on each end. Squeezing the ends opens and expands the clamp. Special hose clamp pliers should be used to remove or install round wire-type clamps. The pliers have a deep groove that will keep the clamp from slipping out of the jaw. Screw clamps are a galvanized or stainless steel band that a screw thread pattern has been cut or pressed into. One end of the band contains a captive screw. When the screw is turned, it pulls the threads of the band, causing the band to tighten. Cooling Fans -Types of Cooling Fans – cooling systems utilize two types of cooling fangs, electric and mechanical. -Electric Fans – vehicle makers may utilize one or multiple electric cooling fans on a vehicle. Electric cooling fans can be either a single fan or side-by-side fan, and utilize temperature sensors to determine when the fans need to be engaged to assist the cooling systems. The electric cooling fan can either push or pull air into the system. A normal cooling fan will run at a constant speed and direct air from the front of the vehicle through the radiator. If there is any rewiring required, be sure to get the positive and negative wires on the correct terminals of the fan. Wiring backwards will cause the fan to rotate backwards, not cooling the engine. Note: Most electric fans can start running at any time with no warning when the ignition is in the run position. However, some electric fans may start running even when the vehicle Is off. -Mechanical Fans – mechanical belt-driven fans pull air into the system and are operated by a belt that drives the fan. Mechanical fans may also use a clutch that is engaged by a thermostat, and may also utilize an electric fan to assist with cooling. Vehicle makers are moving away from mechanical belt-driven fans toward electric cooling fans to increase fuel economy and efficiency. Clutch fans are typically controlled by a thermostat. The thermostat-controlled clutch uses a bimetal coil in front to sense temperature. Heat expands the bimetal coil, which opens an internal valve that reduces clutch slippage. This in turn alters the speed of the fan. -Mechanical Cooling Fan Inspection – when inspecting a mechanical cooling fan, look for signs of oil leakage, as well as blade damage. Check the service manual for engage and disengage temperatures. Pay attention to customer complaints. A customer complaint of no heat in a vehicle may be caused by a fan clutch that is not disengaging. Inadequate cooling can be examined by spinning the fan by hand when the engine is cold. If the fan spins freely more than five times, the clutch assembly is worn out and needs replacement. Note: When inspecting a clutch-driven fan, there are multiple items that need inspection. Clutch assemblies are fluid-filled. If there are signs of leakage, the clutch should be replaced -Fan Shroud Inspection – fan shrouds may have cracks, chips, or other minor damage that may be repaired. Removing a fan shroud may give the appearance of freed airflow, but actually it reduces the directed airflow and requires the radiator to work harder to cool. Airflow under the hood helps cool the engine, coolant hoses, and radiator Cooling, Surge, and Overflow Tanks -Coolant Flow Through a Surge Tank – cooling systems may use a surge or overflow tank. Depending upon the vehicle maker, these tanks may also be called expansion or de-gas tanks. This tank: -Provides a space for coolant expansion when it gets hot -Separates air from the coolant -Is under pressure when the vehicle is running and the engine is at temperature -Has a cap that functions like a radiator cap -Is used to hold extra coolant -Should never be filled beyond the fill line -Surge Tank Inspection – inspect surge tanks for proper sealing caps, visible damage, and correct fluid levels. There are no recommended repairs to a cap or surge tank due to the high amount of tank pressure and heat. Do not open surge tanks until the system has cooled. This allows the system to depressurize, preventing hot coolant from spraying out of the tank -Overflow Tank Operation and Inspection – overflow tanks also play a pivotal role in proper cooling system operation. However, their function within the system differs greatly from surge tanks, as well as the precautions that must be taken when working with them -Coolant Flow in an Overflow System – overflow tanks allow coolant to be added or removed without opening the system. Overflow tanks are not under pressure, nor are they subject to high coolant temperatures -Overflow Tank Inspection – when inspecting overflow tanks, look for: -Proper sealing, as the opening on top of the tank must be sealed -Proper fluid level, since fluid levels will change depending on the temperature of the engine -Sediment, as overflow bottles can collect sediment due to limited coolant flow and movement -Note: Also pay attention to visible damage. If damaged, plastic repairs may be performed on items such as overflow coolant bottles, because the tank is not under pressure or the coolant is not at a high temperature Coolant System Components and Flow -Water Pump Types and Considerations – water pumps circulate coolant through the engine and cooling system. Water pumps can be driven by belts, electric motors, and gears. Water pumps that are used for primary cooling are generally mechanically driven. Auxiliary or secondary water pumps are generally electrically powered. The three different types of water pumps include: -Centrifugal -Electric -Gear-driven or direct-drive -Centrifugal Water Pumps – centrifugal flow water pumps draw coolant into the center of the pump and push the coolant out to the sides. When inspecting water pumps for damage or leakage, always inspect the weep hole -Electric Water Pumps – electric water pumps are typically used for auxiliary cooling purposes and to aid in increasing the volume of coolant through a cooling system -Gear-Driven Water Pumps – gear-driven, or direct-drive, water pumps eliminate the chance of belt slippage and poor water pump performance. Every time the engine moves, the water pump moves. When servicing a water pump, always reference vehicle maker service information to identify service procedures, precautions, and requirements. Note: When removing the original gasket material, use caution to not remove metal or gouge the sealing surfaces as this could result in leaks -Weep Hole – the weep hole on a water pump allows coolant to leak when internal seals or bearings are beginning to fail, and must be inspected. The leaking means that the water pump needs to be replaced. If it is not replaced, the impeller can grind into the engine block and cause improper clearance problems -Engine Block and Coolant Flow – coolant flowing through engine water jackets in an engine absorb heat caused by combustion and friction. These water jackets can become plugged due to coolant additive failure and debris. Also, some engines have cylinders that are held in place by friction rings. If the improper coolant is used, aluminum engine blocks may erode, causing a noise at cold startup that sounds similar to a main bearing failure. -Belt Types – there are three common belt types that are used in cooling systems -Serpentine Belts – serpentine belts are used as a primary drive belt on many late model vehicles. This type of belt is two to three times wider than V-belts, has lateral groves to hold it on the pulleys, and holds up well to the elements -V-Belts – v-belts are most commonly used on early model vehicles. These belts have a profile shaped like a “V”, and are used to run one or two accessories. V-belts are thin and have no lateral grooves running the length of the belt -Timing Belts – timing belts can be rounded or square, and have cogs that run perpendicular to the belt length. Timing belts have two different tooth styles that cannot be interchanged when servicing the timing belt. If the wrong timing belt is used on the wrong pulley, the belt will most likely jump off the timing sprocket -Belt Inspection – when inspecting belts, make sure the belt tensioners are operating properly. Also, check for: -Lateral cracks -Cracks that extend to nylon cords -Fraying of the belt -Pieces missing from the belt Heating and Cooling System Operation and Damage Analysis (2/2) Auxiliary Coolers, Temperature Controls, Hoses, and Coolant Oil Coolers -Water-Cooled Engine Oil Coolers – high-performance engines may run oil through a cooler to lower temperatures and enhance its lubricating ability. Additionally, engine coolant may be circulated through an oil filter adapter plate to cool the oil. Most engines are designed to operate without an oil cooler. However, a vehicles engine will operate properly with an air-cooled oil cooler installed, providing there is no loss in oil pressure or flow. Using an engine oil cooler will not permit longer change intervals, but proper oil maintenance will help the oil provide better protection and help keep the operating oil pressure up -Water-Cooled Transmission Oil Coolers – when working with transmission oil coolers, the radiator tank cooler must never be by-passed. This will cause the fluid to be too thick in cold water. Transmission fluid must be directed through the radiator tank to warm the fluid, as this allows for proper lubrication and fluid flow. In warm weather or harsh driving conditions, use of an external auxiliary cooler may be necessary to cool the transmission fluid properly. Damaged transmission oil coolers can cause cross-contamination between various engine parts and fluids and must be replaced. Because of this, do not drive any vehicle that has a damaged cooler -Power Steering Fluid Coolers – some vehicle makers utilize an auxiliary power steering cooler to prolong pump life, and to keep the power steering fluid temperature cool. Typically, power steering coolers are not repaired and are replaced when damaged Thermostats and Electrical Sensors -Thermostats – a thermostat is a self-contained modulating valve that controls the flow of coolant. It is controlled by a temperature-sensitive spring, which opens and closes the thermostat as the engine temperature changes. Thermostat housings can be made from: -Aluminum -Steel -Plastic/composite -Thermostat Locations – thermostats may be located at the inlet and outlet side of the engine block. Locating the thermostat at the inlet side of the engine block helps ensure that it Is immersed in coolant and does not have an air lock. Typically, the outlet side of the engine block is the most common thermostat location and the most accessible. Thermostats may also be in the following locations: -Integrated into the water pump housing -On top of the motor on the intake or the head -In the lower radiator hose -In the upper radiator hose -Coolant Sensors – coolant sensor monitor engine coolant level and temperature level, and some sensors are designed to monitor both. Coolant level sensors are located in the coolant tank, and typically illuminate a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL) on the instrument panel when coolant is low. Coolant level sensors do not send vital information to the engine controller. Coolant temperature sensors monitor coolant temperature, sending a reading to the instrument gauge, and to the vehicle computer to turn the cooling fan on and off. Sensors can malfunction and cause engine problems. For example, malfunctioning sensors can give false readings to the computer, which will cause the computer to try and compensate when not needed. Sensors with any amount of leaking must be replaced. Note: When replacing a coolant sensor, drain the cooling system before removing the system. Heater Core and Hoses -Heater System Parts – the three main parts that make up a heater system include the heater cover, heater valves, and coolant lines. -Heater Core – a heater core is typically made up of aluminum or brass, and is built similar to a radiator. The heater core operates by hot coolant entering one side of the core and passing through various water tube passages. To determine if there is a heater core leak, check for coolant. -Leaking out of the A/C drain -Dripping on the interior floor -Heater Valves – heater valves are operated by a cable, vacuum, or electronically and prevent hot coolant from entering the heater core or the vehicle. When a heater valve is utilized in a cooling system, coolant flows from the output side of the water pump, through the coolant lines, to the heater shutoff valve, through the heater core, and back to the engine -Hose and Coolant Line Types – hoses and lines are designed to carry hot fluids without losing their integrity. Typically, these hoses are described as being rubber or rigid. Rubber hoses are flexible and easier to route within the system. Rigid hoses and lines can be made from aluminum, steel, or plastic, and are designed to be routed without risk of pinching and limiting coolant flow. Steel and aluminum lines provide long-lasting coolant passages, and they should be monitored for corrosion or damage. These lines will hold up better than plastic lines -Hose Inspection and Replacement – rubber hoses should be soft and pliable. When the system is cold, the hoses should compress easily when squeezed. Replace rubber hoses if they are: -Bulging -Oil-soaked -Hardened -Weather-checked -Worn -Note: To inspect for leaks, pressurize the system with a pressure tester and monitor any fluid leakage. Coolant leaks can also be located with the use of fluorescent dyes that show up under a black light. This will determine the location of slow, intermittent leaks -Top left is swollen. Top right is hard. Bottom left is chafed. Bottom right is soft. Engine Coolant and Fluid Refilling -Overview – as vehicle engines have continued to evolve and become more complex, so has the need to ensure these engines maintain proper temperature and operation. Therefore, it is important to understand the role of coolant in modern cooling systems, and how different coolant types and properties contribute not only to the cooling process, but to condition of the vehicle and its parts as well -Coolant – be sure not to exceed the rated life of engine coolant, since the additives can physically fail and cause engine trouble.
