When replacing coolant: -Never use just water, except to flush the system -Replace per the vehicles makers recommendation -Flush the system if the coolant is contaminated -Note: Coolant colors may look the same, but each has distinct differences. For this reason, it is not advisable to mix coolants. When replacing/recycling coolant, be sure to store the used product in a container that is separate from waste oils. Do not mix waters and oils together. Many recyclers will not process cross-contaminated products -Coolant Testers – the two types of coolant testers include refractive testers and specific gravity testers. A refractive tester works by placing a small amount of coolant on a prism sight glass and holding the tool up to the light. The strength of the coolant can be seen on a graduated scale. A specific gravity tester measures the coolant strength by drawing coolant into a tube. There are six balls of different weight, or buoyancy, that will float depending on the mixture strength -Types of Coolant – there are several types of engine coolant that exist for different usage -Ethylene Glycol – ethylene glycol is green and has specific corrosion and rust inhibitors. This coolant is most commonly used to replace non-OAT factory coolant -Propylene Glycol – propylene glycol is green. It is not used by OEMs in their factory vehicles. However, it is considered less toxic than conventional ethylene glycol coolant, and is, therefore, safer for pets, people and wildlife -Conventional and Hybrid American Coolant – conventional American coolant is green or gold and contains silicates (sand) that act as an abrasive and corrosion inhibitor. This coolant type also protects aluminum, although its protective properties deplete relatively quickly. Hybrid American coolant may be green or orange and is an organic acid blend. Similar to conventional American coolant, this type also contains a moderate amount of silicates -Organic Acid Technology (OAT) – organic acid technology may be an orange or pink color and is a blend of two or more organic acids. This coolant type has slow acting, long life corrosion inhibitors, but does not contain silicates or phosphates. Organic acid technology is contained in products such as Havoline DEX-COOL, Prestone, and Peak -Conventional/Hybrid European and Japanese Coolant – conventional European coolant may be blue or yellow in color. This coolant has very few silicates and does not contain any phosphates. However, it does have one organic acid to help provide corrosion protection. Hybrid European coolant may be blue or green in color and is similar to conventional European coolant. This coolant has large amounts of organic acids, and is designed to have silicates provide the primary protection to the aluminum engine parts as the organic acids protect for the long term. Conventional Japanese coolant may be green or red in color and does not contain silicates. However, this coolant has a large amount of corrosion inhibitors (phosphates) and a small amount of one or two types of organic acids -Mixing Ratios – a proper mixture between coolant and water ensures cooling protection in all types of weather. A coolant mixture that is too rich or weak will not protect properly -Blue at top is boiling points. Left is temperature. Bottom is percent of coolant -Freeze and Boil-Over Protection – in weather below 32 degrees Fahrenheit, 100% water should not be used in a cooling system. If 100% water is used in a cooling system, it should be drained and replaced with a coolant/water mix to avoid damage. The mix ratio that provides the best coolant protection is 65% coolant and 35% water. This lowers the freezing point of the coolant mixture down to -60 degrees Fahrenheit. Coolant in its pure form has a freezing point of -6 degrees Fahrenheit. Water has a lower boiling point than coolant. Water has a boiling point of 212 degrees Fahrenheit. Coolant has a boiling point of 320 degrees Fahrenheit. When mixed properly (65% coolant/35% water), a coolant mixture has a boiling point of about 245 degrees Fahrenheit. -Blue is freezing point of 100% coolant. Left is temperature. Bottom is percent of coolant -Refilling and Bleeding a System – when refilling a cooling system, always reference vehicle maker service information to identify recommended procedures, precautions, and service requirements. An example of a common filling procedure includes always mixing coolant outside the engine, turning the engine OFF, and opening any bleeder valves. Bleeder valves may be located in the: -Thermostat housing -Upper radiator hose -Highest point of the engine -Note: The bleeder valve is left open while filling the system with coolant. This allows the air trapped inside to escape. Close the bleeder valve when coolant begins to come out of the bleeder valve High Voltage Awareness (T0103E-US.TTD) T0103E-US.TTD-Pre Test What action should you take if you survive a serious electric shock? Immediately seek medical assistance, even if you feel OK Accidents are usually caused by? All of these answers are correct (being tired, not being careful, taking short cuts) What is the purpose of the padlock when de-energizing a high-voltage system? Provides a high-current discharge path for the residual high-voltage – X wrong In Mercedes-Benz vehicle, what color is used for High Voltage cables? Without knowledge of the content of this e-training course you are excluded from many operations on series production vehicles with intrinsically safe high-voltage on-board electrical systems. -Five Chapters: T0103E-US.TTD-Lesson 2 T0103E-US.TTD-Lesson T0103E-US.TTD-Post Test What first aid measures should you take after someone has survived an electrical shock? Technician A states that a voltage of 400 volts at a current of 150 amps is 60 kilowatts of power. Technician B states that 150 amps can be carried by wires comparable to those used with a conventional pinion starter. Which technician is correct? Both technician A and B Introduction to Electric Vehicle (EV) Service Considerations Introduction to Electric Vehicle Service Considerations -Introduction – this course takes a generic approach that covers a variety of topics and will introduce the student to EV repair concepts. This course is not intended to replace the vehicle-maker procedures that must be used for all service operations. Collision repair facilities should always reference the documented procedures for the specific make, model, and year of the vehicle being worked on to ensure a complete, safe, quality repair. Always make sure that you are wearing the proper personal protective equipment (PPE) for the work being performed. -EV-Certified Technician – vehicle makers require that before an EV can be serviced, it must be made safe. That responsibility is entrusted to the EV-certified technician. The technical demand from EVs has led to a new technician training, certification requirement. In most cases, vehicle makers require that the diagnosis and replacement of the high-voltage system parts be performed in a service center that has qualified and certified service technicians. There may be strict regulatory requirements in some regions and countries for EV-service technicians -EV-Certified Technician Education – electric-vehicle technicians are increasingly required to have formal training because of the rapidly growing sophistication and safety aspects of EV technology. There are numerous EV certification programs that are typically required. The training for these programs may start with some vocational school or community college. These training programs can last from a few weeks of on-the-job training to a 2-year associate degree. A technician progresses through distinct levels of certifications. Such as: -How to safely work around EVs -Basic maintenance, non-HV related -HV System power-down and -up -HV system repair and replacement -Working on live systems -Vehicle-Maker Training Programs- -Electric vehicles use new and unique technologies, so technicians generally are trained to work on a particular type of vehicle. Vehicle makers typically require this specialized training to provide certification on a particular vehicle type. These courses may train the technician in: -Electrical principles and HV safety measures -Personal protection and safety equipment -System description and operation -Safely disabling the HV system -Special service tools to test and make effective diagnosis and repair on select EV system parts -In most cases, an EV technician needs a combination of online and live formal vehicle-maker training -Designated HV Repair and Service Area – collision repair facilities may have a separate, designated EV repair area. This area may even be a separate building for EV service and repairs. There are specific parameters for a safe EV repair area. A safety zone around the vehicle, using cones, plastic chains, safety signs, specific safety tools, and equipment are used. Its important to note that across different EV vehicle makers, they all have different levels of what repairs a certified-EV technician is allowed to perform. Some vehicle makers require the vehicles HV system be disabled at the dealership -HV System Power-Down – -Depending on the work being performed, the HV system may need to be powered down. All vehicle makers have a specific shutdown procedure. These steps may include the following: -Pre-scan and record and DTCs in all systems -Ensure the vehicle power switch is OFF -Secure the key the required distance from the vehicle -Disconnect the 12-volt battery negative terminal -Ensure you are wearing proper PPE -Remove and secure the service plug to prevent it from being replaced by mistake -Validate that the HV power-down is complete on the information display or through a diagnostic scan tool -Some vehicles may only have a low-voltage disconnect solution -HV Battey Removal – HV batteries are large and heavy. To safely remove, they require specialized tools and equipment to support the battery evenly. The specific equipment needed is identified in the service information. The type of connection between the battery and the vehicle is important to consider when removing and installing. These can be either a fixed- or flexible-type connection for the HV power, cooling lines, and low-voltage data connectors. Fixed connectors require extreme care during service to avoid damage -HV Battery Removal Requirements – Typical requirements for HV battery removal include: -Raising the vehicle on a twin-post lift using the proper lift points -Positioning lift pads so the battery has clearance upon removal -Using a scissor-lift platform or a mobile-battery-table fixture -Using alignment dowels or rods -Confirming that no HV is present -Disconnecting external connections -Following a specific bolt removal sequence -Lowering the battery from the vehicle or raising the vehicle away from the battery -Installing protective connector covers or dummy plugs -HV Battery Storage – store the battery on the mobile table fixture. The fixture is adjusted specifically to the battery, vehicle, and hoist relationship when removing it. This will help with alignment when installing. Store in a clean, climate-controlled area to prevent dust, overspray, or other contamination. Ensure that all protective moisture barriers are not damaged -HV Battery Installation – Depending on the types of battery connections, there are specific requirements for installation. Alignment dowels ensure that the battery aligns with the vehicle connections. With fixed connections, there is no room for error. Even a small error can damage a connector which may not be serviced separately from the battery or the harnesses. Installation procedures may include: -Positioning the battery under the vehicle in the same orientation as when removed -Raising the battery to the vehicle or lowering the vehicle to the battery -Using a borescope to visually determine connections are precisely aligned before mating the battery to the body. Adjacent parts may be removed to provide access -Installing mounting hardware in a specified sequence and torqued to specifications -Verifying all one-time-use hardware is replaced -HV-Battery-Enclosure Leak Test – some vehicle makers have an HV-battery-enclosure leak test. This is a specialized test to determine if the battery enclosure is properly sealed. This is not typically performed in a collision repair facility. Vehicle makers require a special, low-pressure-test, tool kit that is attached to ports or connections on the battery. This may use shop air attached to a regulator and valve assembly. Additional vent and connection seals may be required. During the test, very low air pressure is applied. The system is isolated and monitored for a set time to see if there is a drop in pressure, indicating a leak. This should only be performed by a qualified EV-service technician using vehicle-maker procedures and equipment -Electric Drive Assembly Removal and Installation – typically, the electric drive assembly is removed as a modular unit with the motor, gearbox, and other attached parts. This may be compared to removing an engine and transmission in a regular vehicle. Specific requirements vary by vehicle. Typical steps include shutting down the HV, draining the coolant, and disconnecting electrical cables, coolant hoses, drive axles, and other required parts. The A/C system compressor may remain in the vehicle or be removed with the drive module, depending on the vehicle. Vehicle makers may have specific tools, fixtures, and lifting equipment requirements to perform this operation. The assembly is usually removed from beneath the vehicle and may include the suspension crossmember on some vehicles -Charging Port Removal and Installation – the charge port may need to be removed for access to repair attached or adjacent panels. It may need to be removed due to damage. this is a bolt-on part operation that may require the removal of trim, access panels, or other parts. The HV should be shut down before performing the service operations. Protect the charge port and cables from damage, debris, overspray, or other contaminants. Special alignment tools may be used during installation. Verify the charge port door is properly aligned with adjacent panels and operates as designed with all release mechanisms, both electrical and manual. Connector and wiring harness repair are not authorized. Verify operation of the charge cable and lock operation after reassembly. -Cooling System for EV Parts – EV, cooling system parts may be the most diverse across different vehicle makers. How the EV, HV system parts are cooled can vary greatly. Many of the cooling system parts are in the front area of the vehicle. These parts may connect to the HV battery, rear drive motor, and a variety of different modules. Most of the system parts such as the radiator, lines, and hoses are similar in nature to conventional vehicles. It is important to reference the specific service information for the vehicle on which you are working -Positive Temperature Coefficient (PTC) – Evs may have very complex thermal management system designs and requirements for service. EVs typically have PTC heating devices in the system to heat the coolant. The EV cooling system may tie in with the A/C system of the vehicle using a chiller which is a coolant-to-refrigerant heat exchanger. Liquid cooling systems circulate the coolant through the system parts using different circulation pumps and valves. This manages the flow and temperature for proper operating temperatures as needed. -Cooling System Pressure Test – pressure testing the EV system is very similar to a conventional cooling system test. This test determines if there is a leak. Specific adapters may be required. If the system is not holding pressure and there aren’t visual signs of coolant externally, additional testing is needed. This determines if there is an internal cooling-system leak within the battery or other EV system parts connected to the cooling system. This pressure test typically connects a pressure gauge to the reservoir. Pressure is applied and measured over a speficied period of time to determine if the pressure holds. EVs may use single or multiple cooling systems, requiring pressure testing of each -Cooling System Air Evacuation Service – after cooling system parts have been removed and replaced, make sure the system is filled, and no air remains in the system. In may HV cooling systems, a mixture of deionized or distilled water and ethylene glycol-based antifreeze is used. Always refer to the vehicle maker’s procedures for the proper fluids and mixture ratios. Coolant air evacuation procedures are done using recommended tools and service equipment. Procedures may require using a diagnostic tool to command a coolant fill or air purge routine. This turns on pumps and valves to allow coolant to flow into all areas of the system and forces air to the de-gas bottle. Some of these procedures may be extensive and time consuming to complete, require monitoring, and adding coolant. Some coolant pumps may be damaged if operated dry -Electrical Bonding Validation Test – this test, also referred to as equipotential test, is required after installing HV system parts. The chassis-ground connections for all HV system parts are extremely important for the safety of the system. When doing this test, the HV system should not be powered ON. All system parts are installed as designed. In some steps, you may need to disconnect or remove parts to further test. HV system parts must be properly grounded or bonded to the chassis. Typical steps to test-bonding include: -Cleaning all test probe contact points so they are free of paint, dirt, grease, or corrosion -Testing connectivity using a special milliohm meter and vehicle-maker-specified procedures -Using a Milliohm Test Meter – a milliohm meter is a special tester which passes current through the circuit or part to determine resistance. This is similar to a DVOM on a resistance range, but there are major differences as well. The milliohm meter operates at much higher current levels when testing a circuit or path. Very low resistance values can be measured that far exceed the accuracy of most DVOMs. The meter uses a 4-wire probe set, where the current supplied by the meter is flowing through one of the wires on each side of the lead set. The other wires are reading the voltage drop. The meter calculates the milliohm value. This permits verification of ground or the bonding paths very accurately. Some vehicle makers may integrate this function into their diagnostic tool requiring a special adapter with probes -HV-Isolation Test – an Hv-isolation test is performed to check the HV cables and parts for a malfunction that may cause electrical current leakage. This could present a safety hazard, arcing, fire, or other vehicle damage. a special tester that uses CAT III, 1,000 V-rated multimeter leads is required. The HV-isolation test amplifies HV of 500, 750, or 1,000 VDC to the circuit. HV applied by the meter, using very low current, reveals any potential isolation issues. A DVOM resistance test may not reveal these issues. Only a qualified HV-service technician using specialized test equipment performs this test. Always wear the proper PPE. -HV System Power-Up – after all the services has been performed, the EV must be returned to full operating condition by powering up the high-voltage system. This is typically a reverse order of the shutdown. Vehicle makers may have additional requirements identified in the service information -Conclusion – I-CAR encourages anyone working on EVs and HV systems to be properly trained, and acquire the necessary tools, equipment, and service information before attempting any repairs or service. Each step in a procedure is there for a reason. Skipping a step or omitting some required operation may introduce an unseen compromise or increased risk of injury or death. Don’t attempt to do any EV service repair that you are not qualified to perform. Get it right the first time; the customer is depending on it. Introduction to Workshop Information Systems (WIS) for Collision (X0009E-US.TTB) (Mercedes Benz) -X0009E-US.TTB – Pre-Test 1) Where does a red hyperlink direct you to? -Information which can be found within the current document such as filling capacities, torque values, or special tools X - wrong 2) What information can be used to identify a vehicle in WIS? -World manufacturing code X - wrong 3) Why is WIS valuable to the technician? -It gives technicians access to relevant repair documentation 4) In the document number AR09.4 1-P-6834MND, what does AR stand for? -General Information, quality information X - wrong 5) What search type is performed with all groups and information types selected by default? -Full-text search 6) Where can the online help function can be accessed? -In the WSM module X - wrong 7) What information does the Workshop Information System (WIS) module contain? -Literature on maintenance, repair, diagnosis and retrofitting of special equipment 8) What does the Operation Steps sub-tab display? -A step-by-step list of repair information and instructions 9) Which module is NOT used by MBUSA? -EPC X - wrong 10) In the document number BR01.30-Z-1005-06A, what does BR stand for? -Repair Materials X0009E-US.TTB - Lesson 1 Introduction to Workshop Information Systems (WIS) -WIS is short for Workshop Information System -Wis consists of four modules – WIS, ASRA, SSL, and MSS, with two external modules WSM and EPC -Not all modules, brans and vehicle types will be available to the user depending on his or her authorization -Vehicle Identification Number -&gt; the vehicle context is always visible. The display is similar to that in the EPC -Vehicle Identification Number -&gt; the vehicle model designation engine model description and major assembly model designations are automatically displayed. After a valid Vehicle Identification Number is entered, the vehicle model designation, engine model description and major assembly model designations are automatically displayed in the drop-down menus. -The standard functions are always available as symbols at the top right -They allow the vehicle data card to be displayed, the current search context to be printed, the vehicle context to be completely erased, display model designation, current search context, accessing online help, and finally creation of feedback X0009E-US.TTB – Lesson 2 Vehicle Identification -A vehicle is identified in WIS through one of the following inputs: -External Chassis Number (VIN), Internal Chassis Number (FIN) or the Baumuster -Vehicle Identification Number has a World Manufacturer Code or WMC -This is Vehicle Identification Number minus the WMC -You can see the vehicle model, vehicle model designation -Sales designation e.g. S 550 -&gt; vehicle details also have a Sales designation -Engine model designation -&gt; vehicle details also have a Sales designation, Engine model and Engine model designation -Transmission model designation -&gt; vehicle details also have a Sales designation, Engine model and Engine model designation, and Transmission model, transmission model designation -Overview of major assemblies -&gt; hitting the arrow opens a drop-down overview of major assemblies. Note: Major assembly menu is replaced with the Transmission assembly when using VIN for search -Selecting the icon opens Vehicle data card -The vehicle data card contains information which is sometimes required when searching for literature -Sales designation -&gt; sales designation S 550 -Information on paint and equipment -Information on major assembly numbers (engine, transmission, axles, steering etc.) -Information on special equipment -SA Codes -The top section of the data card displays the vehicles master data -The bottom half of the data card displays the SA codes and SA numbers -Which of the following is NOT a part of the Vehicle data card -&gt; Current search context to be printed -&gt; Current search context to be printed is a symbol of the Standard functions bar at the top-right corner of WIS and not a part of Vehicle data card X0009E-US.TTB – Lesson 3 WIS Groups and Information Types -In the WIS module, the literature is divided into information types and groups -1) Information types – the information types identify the type of literature -Repair info -Diagnosis info -Circuit diagrams -Basic data -Organization -2) Group types – the groups designate to which group a vehicle component or vehicle system is assigned -Overall vehicle -Complete engine, crankcase ventilation, cylinder head, crankcase -Crank assembly -Engine timing -Fuel cell system -etc. -3) Range of Groups – the range of groups start with group 00 and ends with group 99 -The division of literature into information types and groups allows literature to be found in a targeted manner -Find the document that illustrates how much oil to put in an engine -Look at the IT column -Find the letters bf that sand for Filing capacity -Every WIS document is identified by a document number. The information types and groups are components of a document number -&gt; Document identified with IT and document number -Look at the screen. You are looking for information on Testing and repair work information on oil filter housing. What information will you check in the document? -&gt; Search for testing and repair information type – AR and group type 18:20 X0009E-US.TTB – Lesson 4 WIS Document Numbers -In the WIS module, all documents feature a document number. The document number is a unique identifier. A document can be found using the document number in document number search mode -If this document is found in another language, the document number is identical -AR20.00-p-1142GZC -AR -&gt; Information Type -20 -&gt; Group Number -00-&gt; Sub-group Number -p- -&gt; Author Index -&gt; -D=Dussldorf -G=Gaggenau -L=Ludwigsfelde -M=Mannheim -N=Commercial vehicles-Stuttgart -P=Passenger cars -T=Turkey -W=Commercial vehicles-Worth -Z=Head office -1142GZC - In the end is the number issued by the database and cannot be interpreted by the user -All documents feature the same structure, which makes them easier to use for users -The Document sub tab is the main sub tab and combines all the information for the other sub tabs into one convenient tab -The Picture sub tab displays only the pictures within the selected document -The Operations steps sub tab displays the risk and dangers of the job, as well as the step by step instructions for the task -The Red document number hyperlink takes you to an additional document which matches the vehicle data entered -A Gray document number hyperlink will take you to an additional document which DOES NOT MATCH the vehicle data entered -Selecting the Blue document hyperlink will take you to adjustment values within the document -The Basic data sub tab displays general information with regards to the task -The Tightening torques sub tab will display the Newton Meters require to tighten specific bolts -The Filling capacities sub tab illustrates max and min levels. See the example of different mixtures depending on temperature -The Picture2 sub tab displays pictures of tools and equipment required to perform the requested task -Please note: It is possible to navigate directly to the individual objects using the tabs at the top of the document. If a topic is selected via the tab and printing is carried only the scope of the selected topic is printed and not the entire document -A Red hyperlink in WIS document?-&gt; directs you to document number of a further document which matches the vehicle data entered X0009E-US&gt;TTB – Lesson 5 Search Variants -Standard Search -&gt; -Vehicle data -Group(s) and -Information type(s) -Table of Contents -&gt; -This type of literature search requires initial selection of an information type (e.g. Maintenance and care = AP, Special tools = WS etc.) The user is then guided step-by-step to the desired document. -Search can be carried out without first entering vehicle data -Document Number -&gt; -Searching for literature by entering a complete or incomplete document number is a quick way of searching for documents whose document number is known or the ones with similar document numbers -Search can be carried out without first entering vehicle data -Operation Number -&gt; -WIS finds the documents which are directly linked to the operation number which is entered -It is not necessary to first specify groups and information types -Full-Text Search -&gt; -In this type, the search term must be contained within the text of the document. -The search is performed with all groups and information types selected by default -Begin search by entering Vehicle Data input like complete VIN. Type 4JG1660241A040677 in the Vehicle Identification Number bar. - Note: you can also just select the vehicle model to the vehicle model and the vehicle model designation. This should only be done in exceptional cases because the system will then also find literature which may not applicate to the vehicle in question. Searching for literature using vehicle data simplifies the search. You can see the information type in the left column -Select and view the required literature -A user needs literature which describes a work operation. The user does not know the group and information type of the document. However, the user can use a keyword. In this case, full-text search mode may be helpful. This search mode allows the user to search for a document by entering a search term -You can define the selection criteria and the system searches for the search term in the title or in the entire text (contents) of the literature -Select the Search mode as Full-text search and enter the Search term of hit the advance search button -Select the required search option in the Advanced full-text search pop-up window and type in the search term -Choose “Capacities: Passenger cars: Engine lubrication, engine oil cooling” from the list of documents -Select the Filling capacities tab and scroll the document -Select the Filling capacities sub tab and scroll the document A user is looking for information on Metris Passenger Van and needs information on removal of an upper-longitudinal member. The user does not have the document number or the details of group type and information type. Which search mode will be most suitable? -&gt; Use of keywords or search term in Full-text search. -&gt; Using keywords or search term in Full-text search is most appropriate when you do not have document numbers or details of group type or information type. X0009E-US.TTB – Lesson 6 WIS Online Help -Wis provides the user with a contextual online help function which is very comprehensive and easy-to-use. Selecting the HELP icon opens the WIS Online Help. You can search for information using either the Help topics tab or the Start search tab -A list of topics is displayed in the Help topics tab. Selecting the Help topics tab displays a list of help topics in the left navigation bar. You can open the required topic by selecting it from the list. -A quick search of the required topic can be conducted in the Start search tab by using a search term. Selecting the Start search tab shows the Search input field. You can type the term or the keywords you want the information on. -The search brings up a few topics matching the search term. Choose the appropriate topic to explore further. You can select the most appropriate topic from the list of related topics displayed through this search You want to search for some information on Vehicle Data Card. However, you are not sure of the exact topic name in the help menu. Which of the following will be most appropriate search option? – Select the START SEARCH Tab and type “Vehicle Data Card” in the SEARCH TERM field. Open the most appropriate topic from the list displayed. VIN Glue Pull Repair (GPR) -What is GPR? – glue pull repair was born out of the paint less dent repair (PDR) industry. Early GPR technology was not suited for the advancements in today’s vehicles. Adhesives and tabs were weak and did not perform very well, oftentimes breaking or releasing from the panel surface. Innovations have helped the GPR industry to improve the dent removal capabilities. There is more diversity in the size, construction, and surface area of the tabs. The adhesives formulas are capable of withstanding greater pulling forces. Thinner metals used by vehicle makers are also contributing factor to the success of this method. New designs in lifting tools have increase the options for different types of damage. For example, GPR tools have the ability to repair a wide range of dents from large area dents to very small dents. GPR is a viable candidate for almost all sheet metal dent removal for both steel and aluminum. -How Does GPR and PDR Compare? – both GPR and PDR are techniques for removing panel dents. With PDR, the action for dent removal is by pushing the dent from behind. This may require that trim and moldings be removed for access. With GPR, the action for removing a dent is by pulling the dent out, making R&amp;I of trim and moldings unnecessary in most cases. Technicians can also use a combination of pushing and glue-pulling to achieve the desired results. Both GPR and PDR require an understanding of how the metal will react during the removal process. They both require a well thought-out repair plan for the best results -Advantages of GPR – one of the greatest advantages of GPR, when compared to weld-on stud-pulling techniques, is GPR does not require coatings, such as paint and E-coat, to be removed. Additionally, GPR does not damage the backside of the repair, so corrosion treatments are not disturbed -Advantages of GPR (cont’d) – another advantage is GPR can be used during the blueprinting process to understand how the metal or dent is going to react. This is useful when determining if the panel can be repaired or needs to be replaced. A technician can make an initial pull with little preparation required -Limitations – GPR is limited to steel and aluminum sheet metal. Although GPR is typically successful on most panel dents, some small dents, such as those that are less than a quarter in size or adjacent to two body lines, are difficult to achieve a successful pull. Some GPR adhesive is formulated to provide the greatest adhesion to the OEM clear coat. When this type of adhesive is applied to bare metal, the adhesion strength is half of what it normally is on an OEM clearcoat surface. If the panel has been refinished, its likely that paint damage may occur if GPR is attempted -Pull Tabs – glue pulling tabs come in a variety of different shapes and sizes to match unique dents. One of the biggest advancements is the design of tabs with a smooth surface. This provides increased adhesion strength. Some tabs have an articulated design that permits flexibility to match contours or adhere to deep dents. Different round-shaped tabs allow for a wide range of dent sizes and dent depth. Some are small enough to allow for small, precise pulls for hail damage-sized dents. Depending on the manufacturer, tabs may have different attachment designs for the lifting devices. Some manufacturers have designed tabs with a plastic coated metal framework to provide increased strength to allow for increased pulling forces to be applied when pulling -Adhesives – the most common GPR adhesive are hot glue sticks. Adhesives are heated to specific temperatures using a hot glue gun. The adhesive is typically applied directly to the pull tab being used. The temperature at which the glue is being applied to the tab and temperature when pulling is very important. Often, failures in GPR can be attributed to the glue not being the correct temperature. Safety must be observed when applying the hot glue to prevent burns to the skin. glue sticks are formulated to provide many different options based on strength, temperature range, and working time. Most shops typically require only a couple of different types due to their controlled ambient temperature. Another option is the use of cold-type adhesives. This cold-glue adhesive system uses a standard slide hammer for dent removal. The greatest advantage of cold glue is that it requires very little setup which saves time in the process. The adhesion levels of cold glue are less than that of hot glue, and therefore, is not a one-for-one replacement -Lifting Tools – in many cases, lifting tools used with welded tab-type dent repair can also be used with GPR tabs. Typically, the size and location of the dent determine the type of lifter to use. Pulling beams and bars are designed to provide controlled, steady, pulling pressure for larger, deep dents in panels, doors, seams, and bodylines. Slide hammers provide a quick and forceful pull when used with hot and cold glue tabs. Mini-lifters are useful for precision pulls and are compatible with the tiniest of detailing glue tabs to larger body tabs. A selection of these types of lifting devices typically cover most GPR dent types -Lifting Tools (cont’d) – another example of a lifting tool is this portable 5-ton pulling tower that can be used to remove damage that requires additional strength during the pull. In this example, the 5-ton ram is being used to repair a damaged outer rocker panel -Knockdown Tools – knockdown tools are used in the GPR process to tap down the high spots. There are many different types, shapes, and sizes of knockdown tools. To perform a successful dent repair, its vital that the technician understands when each of these types of tools are used. A blending hammer has a polished metal face and a plastic tip that can be used to knock down the high spots during the pulling process. Knockdowns and holders are used when precision corrections need to be made. The holders ensure that the knockdown tool maintains a 90-degree angle to the area being worked -Developing a Repair Plan – regardless of the GPR system being used, the basic process will be very similar. In every case, the technician should evaluate the damage to determine a repair plan and then perform these typical steps. -1) clean the surface to remove all dirt and grime -2) check the damage to determine the type of metal and the panel temperature -3) choose the glue, tabs, lifting tools, and knockdowns that are required -4) coat the tab with glue -5) correct by pulling out the lows and knocking down the highs -6) continue until the dent is removed or has reached the level of repair required for paint -Handoff or Continue GPR? – during the GPR process, a decision is made about how much of the damage will be removed. The dent may be reduced to the point where 80-90 percent of the damage is removed. The repair may then be handed off for completion using conventional body filler and paint methods. Depending on the damage, the decision may be to continue refining the damage-removal process until it is completely removed using more precision tools and techniques. The determination is typically a business decision or based on the skill level of the technician performing the GPR Hem Flange panel replacement -Hem flanges are commonly used to attach the outer panel to the inner panel/frame of doors, hoods, deck lids, liftgates, and similar parts. They may also be used on some quarter panel wheel openings to the outer wheel housing. Adhesives are typically used to add strength to hemmed joints. -removing a hemmed panel requires opening the hem or grinding the panel edge to release the hem. -Grinding Method – removing the edge of the hem flange using a disc grinder. Grind until separation between the outer panel and the door shell is visible. Some panel designs may require cutting an access hole to help remove the skin. Removing the spot welds around the window frame (if equipped) -Hem Flange Opening Tool – an air hammer removal bit is another tool that may be used on the door skin flange to unfold the flange from the door frame. A special bit installed in a pneumatic (air) hammer may be used to open the hem flange on some panels. There is a left and right bit to accommodate different direction operation and access. Care must be taken when using the tools to avoid damaging the door shell or underlying panel. When approaching highly/contoured areas and body lines, it may require stopping short of these areas and grinding the hem to avoid inner panel damage. Use an air hammer with a good feathering trigger to manage control of the bit. -Heat/Adhesive Release – after opening or grinding the hem flange, some panels may release easily from the mating flange. Heat may be required to release adhesives or foam. If using heat, it is recommended to use a flameless heat source and not to exceed 400 degrees F. The panel and remaining strip of flange may also be removed with a hammer and chisel (if necessary). Take care to avoid damage to the underlying panels or door frame. -Aluminum Skins – aluminum hem flanges may crack along the edge if hemmed flat; therefore, a rope hem flange is required. The rope hem flange leaves a bigger radius at the panel edge when completed. Extra care must be taken when hemming the flange to avoid striking the panel edge, making it flat. Special hem flange tools can assist with hemming flanges on aluminum. -Installation Area Preparation – After panel removal, clean any remaining adhesive from the flange area. A plastic-coated abrasive wheel is recommended to prevent thinning the metal. Foam or other materials that are in good condition may be left in place on the inner structure unless it presents a fire hazard. Damaged materials should be removed. Prepare the mating flanges for replacement panel installation following the vehicle maker and adhesive maker procedures. -Panel Fit Up – perform a test fit of the replacement panel. Straighten the attachment area with a hammer and dolly as required to ensure the correct shape and that the mating flanges fit properly. Make sure the vehicle is resting on its own suspension or supported evenly. With door skins, it is recommended to hem a small area of the flange on both sides of the door skin to hold the panel in place temporarily while the door is test fit to the vehicle to verify panel gaps and flushness. -Adhesive Application – prepare flanges and apply adhesive to the required areas, ensuring all mating flanges have coverage and adhesive squeeze out when the flange is hemmed. Door intrusion beams may have anti flutter foam, sealer, or adhesive between the door shell and skin. It is critical to use the vehicle maker’s recommended materials. If the flanges are bare metal, spread adhesive to ensure all bare metal is covered. Repeat this process on the outer panel. To ensure proper bond-line thickness, an additional bead may be applied to either the door skin or door shell. This bead does not get spread out. ** do not use one-part products between the door skin and intrusion beam as they may shrink and distort the skin. -Installation Considerations – most hem flange panels will require adhesive in the hem area and require completing the hem flange while the adhesive is wet. The part must have the correct shape and fit before the adhesive cures. After hemming the flanges, placing a door on the vehicle will permit adjustments to be made while the adhesive is wet. If the adhesive cures and the door is not the right shape (twisted), the panel may have to be removed and replaced to correct the issue. -Hammer and Dolly Considerations – when hemming the flange with a hammer and dolly, aim is critical to strike only the hem flange on the panel while not damaging the frame. There is a balance that needs to be realized between the hammer and dolly. Hold the dolly to support the skin in the area of the hammer strike. Changing the dolly size, weight, and amount of support will require adjustment to the strength of the hammer strike. Changing the amount of support can cause the panel to distort -Hemming Technique – with adhesive applied, set the panel in place and adjust the position by sliding it. Light-duty spring clamps can help hold the panel initially. Hem small areas at the corners and at the body lines to ensure the panel is positioned correctly, then continue to hammer the flange over in small increments, working around the panel. Caution: Hammering too aggressively can distort the panel and underlying door frame or wheel housing. Quarter panel wheel opening hems require a similar incremental approach, as the metal stretches to finish the hem and maintain the correct panel contour. A door skin hammer is designed to keep the hammer contact area focused to the hem. -Other Hem Tools and Considerations – there are specialty hem flange tools available that can reduce the time to complete the hemming process. Care must be taken to ensure the panel is positioned correctly and fixed in place so it does not move out of position while using these. Tightly curved or highly formed areas usually require hammer and dolly work. With the hemming complete and the panel fit to the vehicle, welds, rivets, or other fasteners (if required) can be installed. Assistance during the installation of larger panels can help with adhesive application and cleaning of tools during installation, as adhesive squeeze-out is likely. -Recycled Quarter Panels – Outer quarter panels with rolled hem flanges are not candidates for recycled part usage. This is due to the removal process required; the edge of the hem flange is either ground off or peeled open. Both removal methods damage the panel beyond reuse. During initial installation, the hem flange area stretches as the radius and hem are formed to close the flange. Attempting to open the flange back up causes the flange area to distort excessively, and introduces additional work hardening. Hybrid, Electric and Alternative Fuel Vehicle Service (1/2) Working safely -Consult Service Information – the procedures, requirements, and safety guidelines listed in this training are high-level examples and are NOT intended to serve as actual repair procedures. You MUST consult the vehicle maker service information for the vehicle you are servicing for the specific procedures and safety guidelines. -General Safety Information – hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) and electric vehicles (EVs) contain enough voltage to be lethal. Special care must be taken when servicing these vehicles to avoid injury. Careless handling of these systems can lead to serious injury or even death, and precautions given by the vehicle maker must always be followed. To find the specific precautions for the vehicle you are servicing, reference the vehicle maker service information. -Powering the System Up or Down – powering down the system, regardless of the method, only de-energizes the high voltage circuits and components outside of the HEV/EV battery pack. Dangerous voltage levels ALWAYS exist within the HEV/EV battery pack. For some vehicles, the system may be powered down by using the scan tool to disable and discharge the high voltage outside of the battery pack. If the scan tool cannot be used to power down the system, you will need to physically disable the system and then use a digital multimeter (DMM) to verify that the applicate connectors have a safe voltage level. Powering up the system involves reversing the actions taken to power down the system. -Manually Disabling the High Voltage System for Service – an example disabling procedure may include the following steps: -Secure the work area to prevent individuals with electronic medical life and health support devices from coming in contact with the vehicle -Shut off the vehicle and secure the keys -Disconnect the negative 12 volt battery cable and wait the prescribed amount of time; this varies by maker -Wearing appropriate PPE, disable the high voltage battery pack -Check the high voltage system for voltage using a DMM rated as CAT III 1000V -Delay the beginning of repairs until the DMM indicates zero volts on the high voltage system -Identifying High Voltage System Parts – for safety reasons, all high voltage wires are wrapped in an orange-colored insulation. Intermediate voltage wires may be any color, although blue or yellow are common. There are many different potentially dangerous electrical circuits, in excess of 100 volts, on electric and hybrid electric vehicles. Some examples of high voltage circuits include the -High voltage battery -Inverter -High voltage cables -Electric A/C compressor -Motor generator -Note: intermediate voltage wires typically carry between 30-60 volts and though some of these wires will be inside blue or yellow casing, there are no standard colors. Exercise caution around the intermediate voltage wires Troubleshooting -Battery and High Voltage System Inspection – damage to a lituim-ion battery pack could result in fire, loss of electrical insulation, or exposure to high voltage. When inspecting a battery pack, example procedures may include the following steps -Disconnect the 12 V battery -Inspect the battery pack for evidence of exposure to fire, foreign object intrustion, and water exposure -Inspect the battery coolant system for proper level -Inspect the following components for visible damage: electric A/C pump, 14 V power module, coolant heater, power/inverter module, battery charger, transmission case, HEV/EV battery charger receptacle, and the high voltage orange cables -Note: when performing an inspection of the high voltage system, it may be necessary to ensure the 12 V battery system is functioning properly before installing a scan tool to check for diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) -Note Note: when inspecting high voltage systems, always wear the appropriate PPE, including safety glasses and insulated gloves, even if the system has been disabled. -Battery Removal and Installation – the hybrid battery is actually a battery pack that is housing many individual battery cells. Before performing any work on the battery, consult the service information for all applicable warnings and cautions. Batteries are typically located under the floorboards, under the rear back seat, or in the cargo compartments. -Preparation – below are some example steps you should take before safely removing the battery -Disconnect the negative cable to the 12 V battery -Wait at least 90 seconds to prevent airbag and seat belt pre-tensioner activation -Remove the trim and/or floorboards to gain access to the hybrid battery -Wear insulating gloves and remove the service plug or turn the module switch to OFF, depending on the model -Wait 5 minutes before touching the high voltage connectors and terminals -Remove any additional trim as necessary to access the battery -Battery Removal/Installation – the following are example steps required to remove a hybrid battery -Disconnect the high voltage cables (orange) -Remove any electrical connectors (such as harnesses, interlocks, and relays connectors) -Disconnect and remove the battery cooling assembly, fans, and hoses -Remove the hybrid battery hose assembly -Remove the carrier bracket -Remove the bolts, connectors, and clamps -Install the hangers or eyes as directed by service information, and attach appropriate lifting tackle and shop cranes to lift the battery -Lift the battery out of the vehicle, being careful not to scratch the finish -Note: installation is the same steps in the reverse order -The Diagnostic Process – when diagnosing a concern with a hybrid/electric vehicle, the procedures for gathering information about the concern are the same as when diagnosing a concern with a conventionally powered vehicle -Talk to the customer in order to thoroughly understand the concerns and symptoms -Inspect the vehicle for obvious damage or conditions related to the concern -Check for technical service bulletins that may relate to the concern -Verify that there are no purely mechanical concerns -Note: after verifying that the concern is NOT purely mechanical, you should -Verify the vehicle powers up normally -Use a scan tool to confirm that the vehicle networks are communicating properly and to check for DTCs -Note: if the concern persists, continue with the symptom diagnostic procedure provided in the vehicle maker service information -Scan Tools – when using a scan tool to perform system diagnostics, some example procedures may include the following steps -Verify the vehicle is in service mode and powers up correctly -Perform a vehicle DTC scan and confirm the control modules are communicating with the scan tool -Verify there are no control module internal performance DTCs -Verify there are no control module power or communication DTCs -Perform any additional tests required by the vehicle maker, and perform necessary repairs following vehicle maker service procedures -Digital Multimeters – digital multimeters are indispensable when trying to diagnose the HEV/EV. Depending on the circuit, some diagnostic flow charts will indicate the need to use either a mega ohmmeter or a milli ohmmeter. The mega ohmmeter, or insulation meter, is capable of safely testing the high voltage components on the vehicle, provided the vehicle’s high voltage system is disconnected. The milli ohmmeter is designed to read very small amounts of resistance. Be aware that temperature and humidity can greatly affect smaller ohm readings. -Note: any DVOMs used on HEVs/EVs must be safety rated as Category III (1000V) to safely test the circuits on the vehicle -AC Inverter Troubleshooting – the following is an example procedure for troubleshooting an AC system high voltage DTC. Inspect the AC inverter -1) Wear appropriate PPE and disconnect the high-voltage connectors -2) Disconnect the AC electric inverter connector. -3) Measure the resistance of the body ground. If the resistance is not within specification, replace the inverter. -Inspect the Inverter Compressor -Disconnect the electric inverter compressor -Measure the resistance of the body ground. If resistance is not within specification, replace the compressor -If resistance is within specification, continue testing by reconnecting the inverter compressor -Reconnect the high voltage connector -Turn on the power switch -Set the air conditioning (AC) setting temperature to 77 degrees F -Operate the compressor for 10 minutes to collect as much compressor oil as possible -Turn the power off and allow the vehicle to cool overnight -Disconnect the high voltage connector -Measure the resistance of the body ground. If the resistance is within specification, the system is OK. -Replacing an Electric A/C Pump – an electric A/C pump/compressor is located in the C/C cooling loop and is used to cool the passenger compartment. In conventional engines, this would have been a belt-driven pump; in electric and hybrid vehicles, it is electric and is driven only by the high voltage battery. The electronic control module (ECM) takes into account a number of inputs before allowing power to be sent from the battery to the A/C pump -Compressor Oil – electric A/C systems use polyester (POE) oil. This oil has insulating properties that prevent it from conducting electricity. Compressor oil should be clean, with no debris. Dark brown/black colors, debris, or pungent and unusual odors all indicate the system should be inspected and that it possible needs to be flushed and the oil replaced. If the pump is replaced, be sure to follow vehicle maker service requirements to identify the correct type and amount of oil to be added to the system. -Resistance Checks – after disabling the high voltage system, use a mega ohmmeter or insulation meter to check the resistance of the electrical connectors on the A/C pump. The resistance should be very high, around 2 mega ohms. -Preventing Contamination – even a small amount of moisture in the A/C system can cause corrosion and degraded system performance. That is why it is important to protect lines and parts from moisture and air. Before servicing the system, you should have all the necessary parts and tools on hand. Always clean around fittings before removal to reduce the possibility of contaminants entering the system. Open the system for the shortest amount of time possible, and immediately cap or seal any open lines, fittings, and ports when opened. Keep system parts capped or sealed until you are ready to proceed with the assembly and sealing of the system. -Leak Detection – inspect the system to identify any obvious breach in the system parts. If no obvious issues are found, turn the system on to verify the system is cooling properly and the blower motor and cooling fans are working. Compare the air temperature at the outlet vents with the expected readings listed in the service manual. Check the system for leaks using an electronic tester and dye, beginning with the fittings and joints. Refrigerant is heavier than air, so be sure to use a sniff tester to “sniff” the lower areas of the part being tested. Electric and Hybrid Parts Service -Cooling System – EVs and HEVs may have separate cooling systems, with individual roles. For instance, some HEVs have three separate cooling systems: -The electronics cooling system cools the accessory power module, battery charger, and drive motor generator power inverter module. -The battery pack cooling system cools and heats the battery. The battery is cooled and heated using a heat exchanger and the electric A/C compressor motor control module assembly. A high voltage heater inside the battery can also heat the coolant entering the battery when needed. -The engine cooling system cools the engine and provides heat to the passenger compartment -Note: depending on the model and vehicle maker, the A/C system may be used to help with battery cooling. -Battery Charger – the following is an example procedure for removing and installing a battery charger -Remove the battery park upper component and cover -Disconnect the high voltage cable terminals and battery charger cable terminals -Disconnect the battery charger connector -Remove the battery charger -To install, reverse the steps -Note: always ensure the high voltage system is disabled and discharged prior to starting system service -Electric Motors – depending on the vehicle maker, the electric motor may be called a generator or drive motor. An example procedure for replacing the motor includes the following steps -Review and comply with the high voltage safety information -Disable the high voltage system -Remove the fasteners and connectors -Drain the transmission fluid -Remove the components connected to the motor, including the driveshafts, A/C compressor unit, transmission control connector, any electrical connections, transmission connections, power inverter, and gasket. -To install the motor, reverse the steps. -Inverter/Converter – the inverter/converter may be called the power inverter module or the drive motor generator power inverter module, depending on the vehicle maker. The inverter/converter converts high voltage direct current (DC) to 3-phase alternating current (AC) electrical energy. It is typically mounted directly to the transmission. -The Cooling System – the inverter/converter is cooled by a cooling system that is separate from the engine cooling system. The system typically uses a heat exchanger at the front of the vehicle and coolant that is circulated by electric pumps. -Intelligent Power Unit – some vehicles are equipped with an intelligent power unit (IPU), which combines a DC-AC converter, a DC-DC converter, a battery pack, engine control unit, and call voltage sensor into one component that powers the electric propulsion motor. An example of an IPU removal procedure may include the following steps -Review and comply with all high voltage precautions -Turn off the service plug -Disconnect the high voltage motor power inverter module cable -Remove the trunk rear side trim panel -Remove the rear IPU frame bar, the IPU outlet joint, and the air inlet duct -Disconnect the electrical connector -Remove the IPU assembly -Remove the IPU outlet duct, connectors, harness clips, inlet joint fitting, and the upper cover. -To install, reverse the steps Hybrid, Electric and Alternative Fuel Vehicle Service (2/2) Repair Considerations -Shop Safety – in addition to typical shop safety requirements, servicing alternative fuel vehicles requires the shop to observe additional safety rules. Check the state and federal requirements for specifics. As an example, if hydrogen fuel-cell vehicles require service to the hydrogen storage tanks or the fuel-cell stack, or associated fittings, regulations may require the shop to be ‘hardened’ or the vehicle to be in a ‘minimum-fueled state’. Hardened shops may be required to have hydrogen detectors, curtains around the work area, and explosion-proof light fixtures. Minimum-fueled state typically means that no more than 0.5 kilograms of hydrogen remains. Shops servicing compressed natural gas (CNG) or liquid propane gas (LPG) vehicles may also have special requirements. These requirements may include lighting fixtures ceiling fans classified as Class 1, Division 2, and increased ventilation and methane detectors. -CNG and LPG Identification – most CNG fueled vehicles are conversions. CNG vehicles made by a vehicle marker are generally identifiable by the nameplate. CNG conversions are identifiable by: -1) A label inside the fuel-filler door, or a decal on the vehicle -2) A fuel-filler valve instead of, or in addition to, a gasoline filler neck -3) Stainless steel fuel lines, which may have a sheathing cover. -Most liquid propane gas (LPG) vehicles are conversions with no official emblem or nameplate for identification. These vehicles are identifiable by the: -1) Fuel-filler valve, instead of, or in addition to, a gasoline filler neck -2) Torpedo- or doughnut-shaped fuel tanks either in the cargo area, underside, or on the truck bed -3) Copper fuel lines, which may be covered by a sheathing. -4) A label inside the fuel door -Disabling a CNG or LPG Fuel System – disabling a CNG or LPG fuel system will involve similar procedures and typically involves removing the fuel pressure from the lines. This procedure will be different based on system design, so it is important to identify the maker of the system you will be servicing and the service requirements and safety precautions. An example service procedure for releasing fuel pressure from a CNG system may include the following steps: -1) Place the vehicle securely on a lift -2) Relieve the fuel pressure between the engine and the manual shut-off valve by closing the manual shut-off valve and running the engine until it stalls -3) Relieve the fuel pressure between the fuel tank and the engine (or between the fuel receptacle and the fuel tank) by opening the manual shut-off valve and disconnecting the connector from the tank to the engine. Run the engine until it stalls -4) Relieve the fuel pressure at the fuel tank. The method for doing this may vary, depending on whether the engine runs. -Checking for Leaks – to check for leaks on a CNG or LPG vehicle, follow the product maker procedure for leak detection, stop and repair it, then move on to the next inspection point. An example leak detection procedure may include the following steps: -1) Verify the fuel pressure regulator holds a vacuum. Replace it if it does not -2) Inspect the fuel lines and hoses for damage -3) With the manual shut-off valve open, leave the engine off and turn the ignition switch to ON and then to LOCK 2-3 times to raise the fuel pressure in the fuel lines -4) Use a leak detector to check the lines and the vent hose outlet -5) Raise the vehicle on a lift and remove the under-floor cover -6) Inspect all fuel lines -7) Remove the rear seat and check all fuel lines and fittings at the fuel tank and the fuel joint block -Note: stop the engine and keep heat, sparks, and flames away from the vehicle -Compressed Gas Cylinders – when servicing a CNG or LPG fuel tank, it is important to remember the fuel is highly flammable and under pressure. Use caution to avoid heat, sparks, and flames while servicing the fuel tank. An example service procedure for removing and installing a CNG or LPG fuel tank may include the following steps: -1) Relieve the fuel pressure between the fuel tank and the engine by opening the manual shut-off valve and disconnecting the connector from the tank to the engine. Run the engine until it stalls and then turn the ignition switch to LOCK -2) Remove the lines attached to the tank -3) Remove all vehicle trim and accessories to gain access, and then remove the mounting hardware -4) Remove the tank from the vehicle -To re-install the tank, reverse the steps and then follow the leak detection procedures to ensure the fuel system is sealed properly -Note: when disconnecting and reconnecting hoses, make sure all fittings are clean prior to disconnecting and that all open fittings are protected to prevent contamination. -Hydrogen Vehicles – hydrogen fuel cell vehicles are uncommon in most areas, but they can typically be identified by badging containing the phrase Fuel Cell or FC. Some vehicles will contain badging identifying the vehicle as Hydrogen EV. Vehicles that utilize a hydrogen fuel cell combine hydrogen and oxygen to produce electricity, which runs an electric motor and charges the battery. These vehicles are considered EVs, but their range and refueling process are comparable to conventionally powered vehicles. Because hydrogen-powered vehicles convert the hydrogen into electricity, the only by products are heat and water, meaning the vehicles do not create tailpipe pollution. Hybrid Vehicle Identification and Damage Analysis (1/3) Hybrid Electric and Electric Vehicle Parts -Terms and Acronyms – this course will refer to electric vehicles as a collective term that includes all types of vehicles with some level of electric propulsion. Common course acronyms include: -HV = High Voltage -HEV = Hybrid Electric Vehicle -PHEV = Plug-In Hybrid Vehicle -EV = Pure-Electric Vehicle -DC = Direct Current -AC = Alternating Current -V = Volt -Electric Vehicle Types – types of electric vehicles include -EVs, where the only means of propulsion is an electric motor to power the transmission and turn the wheels -HEVs, where a conventional internal combustion (IC) engine is combined with an electric motor to propel the vehicle. The HV battery is recharged by the IC engine and regenerative braking. -PHEVs, which have batteries that can be restored to full charge by plugging into the power grid. This allows the vehicle to run on electric power longer than HEVs. There is still an IC engine, though it is smaller than HEV engines. -Extended-range EVs, which use a small IC engine to power an electric generator that charges the battery system. Only the electric motor propels the vehicle. The IC engine charges the HV battery and assists the electric motors during extended range. -HEV Types – there are two basic categories of HEVs. These include full hybrids that are capable of being propelled by the electric motor alone, and mild hybrids that are not capable of propelling the vehicle with only the electric motor. On a mild hybrid, the electric motor only assists the IC engine during acceleration. Within those two broad categories, there are different ways to configure HEVs. These configurations include. -Series hybrids, where the IC engine turns a generator, which both powers an electric motor to propel the vehicle and charges the HV battery. -Parallel hybrids, where both the IC engine and the electric motor are connected to the wheels in two different power paths – only the IC engine can power the vehicle on its own. -Combination series/parallel hybrids, where there are still parallel power paths, but either the IC engine or the electric motor can propel the vehicle by itself – the electric motor is also used as a second power source to assist the IC engine. -HV Parts – the HV battery is commonly either a lithium-ion or lithium-polymer type. Some hybrids use nickel-metal hydride batteries and these may be rated up to 350 volts or higher depending on the vehicle. The battery is often located in the back of the vehicle, either behind or under the rear seats, or in the trunk area. HV batteries are typically heavily protected by their central location and with steel reinforcements. Some vehicle makers suggest only having their factory-trained technicians perform HV battery removal and installation. The HV batteries typically have extended warranty parameters, such as 10 years and 100,000 miles, and damaging a battery during removal would void this warranty. In addition to the battery, HV parts include the control module, electric motor, inverter, and converter. * Damaged batteries should be recycled. Call the telephone number printed on the battery case for instructions on what to do with a damaged battery. -Control Module – a control module is necessary to both regulate and monitor the HV parts of the system. The control module: -Regulates the electrical energy between the HV battery, motor, and inverter -Controls the electrical energy between the converter and 12-volt electrical system, including the charging of the 12-volt auxiliary battery -Monitors the condition and state of charge of the HV battery -Is part of the 12-volt electrical system Control module -Electric Motor – all HEVs and EVs have at least one electric motor. Some have a two-motor system. The electric motor propels the vehicle or helps propel the vehicle. This motor assists the IC engine on most HEVs. The vehicle is propelled with both the electric motor and the IC engine, or with the electric motor alone for short periods. The electric motor is the only means of propulsion on EVs. The motor acts as the main source of propulsion on extended-range vehicles is used to assist the electric motor for propulsion during extended range and for charging the battery. In some vehicles, the electric motor also serves as a generator to charge the HV battery during regenerative braking. Gold motor in the middle is the small motor. Gold motor on the right is the large motor. -Inverter – an inverter changes DC from the HV battery into AC to run the electric motor. The AC generated by the electric motor is changed to DC to help recharge the HV battery. An inverter contains capacitors that store and release electrical energy. Once battery power is removed from an inverter, it typically takes a few minutes for the capacitors to discharge their stored electrical energy -Converter – a converter, housed in the inverter/converter assembly, acts like a conventional vehicle alternator. The converter -Changes the HV DC to 12 volts DC to run the 12-volt accessories on the vehicle. Excess 12-volt power is stored in the 12-volt auxiliary battery -Acts as the distribution center for power within the 12-volt system -Maintains the state of charge of the 12-volt battery -HV Charger – an HV charger is found on any electric vehicle that is plugged in, which includes all EVs, extended-range HEVs, and PHEVs. HV chargers contain a receptacle for an external charger to plug into a 110- or 220-volt AC charging station. An HV charger requires its own inverter to change the AC into DC for use by the HV battery. The charger may be in a relatively vulnerable location, such as behind the headlamp assembly or behind the front bumper. -HV Cables – HV power cables -Transmit the HV electrical energy from the battery to other HV parts -Typically have bright orange sheathing to make them easy to identify -Are placed in orange-colored corrugated plastic tubes for added protection from cuts and abrasions -Are covered by, or placed in, an additional housing where the cable is exposed to hazards on the vehicle underbody – the protective housing is typically not orange -12-Volt Auxiliary Battery – HEVs and EVs typically have an auxiliary 12-volt battery that powers the 12-volt electrical system. This battery powers the control modules for the HV system. The HV system will not function if the 12-volt battery is discharged. The 12-volt battery must be charged for the vehicle to start, even if the IC engine is started by the HV battery and electric motor. The 12-volt battery may be a special battery design – if the 12-volt auxiliary battery in an HEV requires replacement, it must be replaced with the correct battery. Dual 12-volt battery High Voltage Hybrid Vehicle Identification and Damage Analysis (2/3) Approaching a Damaged Electric Vehicle -Identifying an Electric Vehicle – the first indicator to identify an electric vehicle is the nameplate or emblem. The word “electric” is a positive indicator that this is an electric vehicle. -Emblems and Nameplates – for the purpose of this course, an emblem is the vehicle make emblem, such as the vehicle maker’s logo. Nameplates include model names and other attaching identifiers such as “Hybrid” or “Electric”. The first way to help determine if the vehicle being approached is an EV or HEV is to look for the emblems or nameplates. These may include the words “Electric” or “EV” for a pure-electric vehicle. The word “Hybrid” is used to indicate an HEV vehicle. Some vehicle makers use a blue shadow around their emblem if the vehicle is an EV or HEV. -Underhood Labeling – the next best place to look for identifiers of an EV or HEV is under the hood. Underhood labeling may include the word “Hybrid” or another identifier on the engine cover. Warning decals on the underside of the hood may detail precautions for HV parts. A first responder label calls attention to the cut tape label on the electric vehicle’s 12-volt battery cable to disconnect the 12-volt battery, thus disabling the HV system. There may be more than one cable that must be cut. Emergency responders are directed to cut through all the cables, as leaving one uncut will still supply 12 volts. A yellow first responder cut tape label displayed on the 12-volt battery cable indicates where a first responder should cut to disconnect the 12-volt battery. -Cables and Cable Colors – cable color is helpful to identify an HEV or EV. Low-voltage cables are typically colored black. There may be a range up to 30 volts DC and 15 volts AC for low voltage. Cables that carry HV have sheathing that is universally colored bright orange. Look for bright orange sheathing on underhood cables. Some vehicle makers use orange-colored sheathing for all voltage levels higher than 12 volts. There are cables on HEVs and EVs that carry intermediate voltage, and these may be colored blue or yellow. Color is just an indicator and cannot be used alone to determine voltage levels. Warning labels and service information are a better guide for the presence of high and intermediate voltage. -GM eAssist Vehicles – eAssist vehicles are equipped with a 130-volt lithium-ion battery in the rear. With no exterior nameplates, the vehicles can be identified by: -A bright orange cable underhood, which is connected to a starter/generator -Yellow first-responder cut tape labels around the 12-volt cables, also underhood -A battery icon in the trunk area -An “Auto Stop” position and economy gauge at the bottom of the speedometer or tachometer -Warning labels on top of the radiator core support and battery itself. -VIN Indicator – the vehicle maker may indicate on the VIN whether the vehicle is an EV or HEV. However, not all vehicle makers use the VIN for electric vehicle identification Ex: (picture did not load) 3FAD(P0L)30AR100001 – The P0L is a Fusion Hybrid FWD -Where to Find Information – when estimating a vehicle with electric technology, refer to -Vehicle maker service information -Emergency response guides (ERGs) -Dismantling guides -* Each of these are available online via the I-CAR Repairability Technical Support (RTS) Portal, i-car.com/RTS. Under the Technical Knowledge banner, select OEM information and then select the vehicle maker of choice -Vehicle Maker Service Information – vehicle maker service information specific to the vehicle model and model year is the best resource for repair information on any vehicle, whether electric or not. All of the information is aimed at the repair technician. Service information includes: -A description and operation of all systems and parts on that specific vehicle -Diagnostic information, procedures for replacing parts, and any special tools and equipment required for the specific vehicle -Up-to-date technical service bulletins that may affect the repair of the vehicle. -Dismantling Guides – another resource from the OEMs is an online dismantling guide, which -Is aimed at the recycling parts industry -Is also free information on some vehicle maker websites – not all vehicle makers have dismantling guides -Includes similar information as ERGs, such as safety precautions, identification information, and HV part locations -Includes additional information such as HV part functions, vehicle operation, and methods for part removal -* Getting the dismantling information up front in the repair process will save the technician valuable time once the repairs have been started. -Approaching a Damaged Electric Vehicle – when approaching an EV or HEV for doing an estimate, general safety considerations include -Be aware that the collision may have comprised HV safety systems and present a potential HV electrical shock hazard -Always assume that parts of the HV circuit are energized even though steps have been taken to disable the system. After a collision, anything is possible -Do not wear metallic objects or jewelry. Do not have metal objects, such as mechanical pencils or tools, in your pockets. A metallic object may fall from a pocket onto an HV part or connection and cause a short circuit -Make sure your skin is not wet and there is no moisture present on or around the vehicle. -Floating System – a high-voltage system is referred to as a floating system, in that with the 12-volt system, the negative terminal of the battery is connected to the chassis, making it a common ground. In an HV system, both terminals of the HV battery are connected directly to the HV parts via the HV cables and connectors. There is no common ground. The ground is floating. -Built-In Safety – electric vehicles are not designed to be HV shock hazards for anyone that opens the hood. There are built-in safety features, such as -Insulation and sheathing on the HV cables. HV current flows through these cables, not through the metal vehicle body -Various onboard sensors that shut off the HV if a problem is detected, such as a collision -For example, some vehicle makers use a passive restraints control unit that uses information from the airbag impact sensors to stop the flow of current from the HV battery in the event of a collision -Warning labels posted wherever there could be a potential for shock -Multiple methods of disabling the HV system -Collision Sensor to Disable High Voltage – some HEVs have a dedicated onboard sensor to automatically disable the HV in a collision. For example, an -HV circuit impact detection sensor to disabled HV whenever there is an airbag deployment -Circuit breaker sensor mounted on the inverter that shuts off the HV in the event of a collision -Thermal sensor on the HV battery to automatically disconnect the battery if the ignition is on and the HV battery exceeds 140 degrees F -HV interlock circuit that disables the HV whenever any HV connector is disconnected -Inertia Switch – some vehicles may have inertia switches that cut off the fuel in a collision. A front switch cuts off both the fuel and the HV in a collision. A rear inertia switch cuts off power to the HV contacts inside the battery case.
