When the powertrain control module (PCM) senses the loss of power at the contacts, the PCM shuts off power to the fuel pump. Therefore, the rear inertia switch also, indirectly, cuts off power to the fuel pump -Electronic Key – remove electronic keys for electric vehicles and hybrid/electric vehicles, and keep them away from the vehicle. The required distance from the vehicle varies from 4 to 16 feet. If not kept away at the required distance, the vehicle could power up to start without warning and there will not be any noise. Disconnecting the 12-volt battery will also prevent unintentionally starting the vehicle. -Safely Moving the Vehicle – when servicing a damaged HEV or EV, do not push the vehicle with the drive wheels on the ground. Use wheel dollies to move the vehicle without turning the drive wheels. Moving the vehicle with the drive wheels on the ground -May generate electrical energy and charge the condensers in the inverter -Could start a fire if there is damage to an HV cable that shorts it to the vehicle chassis Hybrid Vehicle Identification and Damage Analysis (3/3) Approaching a Damaged Electric Vehicle -High-Voltage Disabling – since the high voltage on an HEV or EV is the primary safety issue, high voltage must be disabled in situations -When collision repairs are done on the vehicle, the HV system must be disabled with the HV battery service disconnect -When damage is being inspected under the hood, the HV can be disabled by turning off the key -If there is an electronic key, keep it a safe distance away -If everything is working properly, this step will confine the HV to the HV battery -* The HV can also be disabled by disconnecting the 12-volt battery, as the high voltage will not power up without the verification from the control module and the control module is powered by the 12-volt battery. Be sure to read any existing DTCs before disconnecting the 12-volt battery. -HV Service Disconnect – the HV service disconnect is used to disable the HV system. The service disconnect -Is located on the HV battery case -Isolates the HV to the HV battery -Has a number of types -The most common type is a slide/flip/pull. With this type, slide the handle from a lock position, flip the handle outward, then pull to remove -It may be a toggle switch -It may be a spring-loaded switch under the battery cover -HV Service Disconnect Fuse – the HV system service disconnect on some HEVs contain a fuse. This HV fuse -Is contained within the body of the service disconnect -Is a replaceable part. For one vehicle maker, the service information states that the standard resistance of the HV fuse should be blow 1 ohm. If it is not, it should be replaced -May be on the negative terminal of the HV battery, help in place by a replaceable, nonconductive (plastic) nut -May not be a service part. On some vehicles, an open HV fuse requires installing a new service disconnect -Will set a DTC if it is blown -HV Service Disconnect Reinstallation – reinstalling the HV service disconnect to enable the HV system is just as critical as removing it. Reinstalling the service disconnect -Requires wearing lineman’s gloves -Must be done properly, or it can set a DTC -Typically requires reversing the steps of the removal procedure -For example, with a slide/flip/pull type of service disconnect, the steps are to insert the service disconnect into the plug, rotate the handle inward, and slide the handle to lock and complete the connection -* Operating the ignition/power switch when the service disconnect is removed or is not properly locked may result in a malfunction or damage to hybrid system electronics. * Not following proper disable and reconnect procedures could result in damage or additional steps, which could cause repair and delivery delays. -Electric Vehicle Features – engine stop/start is a common feature on all HEVs. This feature completely stops the engine when the vehicle comes to a stop and immediately starts the engine when the brake is released. Engine start/stop may need to be bypassed in a repair facility if the engine needs to be running for post-repair inspections. This may not be an issue for some vehicles that keep the vehicle running if the hood is not fully closed. For some vehicles a scan tool may be required to set the vehicle to a maintenance or inspection mode -Regenerative Braking – regenerative braking is a universal feature on electric vehicles. This feature uses the drag of the electric motor to slow the vehicle while generating electricity to help recharge the HV battery. Regenerative braking does not generate enough energy to keep the battery charged on its own, but it does help. This is why an HEV is more efficient in city stop-and-go traffic than in rural areas. -Adding Sound to Electric Vehicles – vehicle makers must add sound to their electric vehicles to alert pedestrians of electric vehicles in motion as of February 13, 2017. Full compliance is required by September 11, 2019. -Other Electric Systems – HV batteries make it possible to power other systems on a vehicle. The two most common are: Electric Air Conditioning (A/C) and Electric Power-Assisted Steering (EPAS) -Electric Air Conditioning – an electric A/C system: -Allows the A/C to keep running when the engine shuts down during idle due to the stop/start system -May have a combination electric/belt-driven compressor that, depending on conditions and the demand on the A/C system, can switch between belt only, electric only, or belt and electric operation -Conductive Compressor Oil – conventional A/C systems that are belt-driven by the engine use polyalkylene glycol (PAG) compressor oil. PAG oil cannot be added to electric A/C systems because PAG oil attracts moisture, which conducts electricity. If sensors within the HV compressor detect a level of electricity being conducted, the entire HV system, and therefore the vehicle itself, will shut down. It the wrong oil is added to a part, that part may require replacement. If the A/C system is charged and operated with the wrong compressor oil, the entire A/C system, including the lines, may require replacement. Adding the wrong oil can happen as easily as the technician using a recovery/recycling/recharging machine for an HEV that was previously used for adding PAG oil to a conventional A/C system. The compressor oil is normally added using an injection feature on the machine, and remnants of PAG oil can remain in the service lines, enough to cause contamination of an HEV system. The only way to prevent this from happening is either by using a dedicated machine or equipment designed to prevent cross-contamination. -Electric A/C Compressor Oil – electric A/C systems require Polyol Ester (POE) oil, which has insulating properties to prevent it from conducting electricity. Not just any POE oil can be used. Some recommended oils include ND-11 and SE-10Y. At least one vehicle maker states that once a container of POE oil has been opened for use, it cannot be stored for late use. This is because it will absorb moisture. It is important to only use OEM-branded compressor oil purchased directly from a local dealership. There is no SAE standards for POE oil for use in electric compressors, so just because a generic bottle of POE oil states that it meets the proper specification does not guarantee it really does meet the specification. It is also important for technicians to pay attention to OEM guidelines for how much compressor oil to use. * Adding to much compressor oil can reduce system efficiency or even result in compressor lock-up -Electric Power-Assisted Steering (EPAS) – electric power-assisted steering (EPAS) on electric vehicles: Uses an electric motor to help turn the steering shaft or gear. Has the motor mounted on the steering column or steering gear - If the motor is mounted internally around the rack, the motor is serviced as part of the rack-and-pinion assembly. May have intermediate voltage capacity (34-46 volts) or 12-volt EPAS. -Cooling Systems – All HEVs and EVs have, at the very least, a system to air-cool the HV battery. HV battery liquid cooling and inverter/converting cooling may also be part of the cooling system. -HV Battery Air-Cooling – the system to air-cool the battery may include a vent fan motor, either inside the battery assembly itself, or separate from the battery, where a cooling fan is mounted just above the HV battery in the trunk area. Air-cooling systems many include vents, for example under the rear seats, on the side panels, and on the package shelf, all aimed at allowing air to flow over the HV battery in a compartment underneath. Air-cooling may be provided using a separate rear A/C unit. The rear A/C is not for passenger comfort. It is only there to help cool the HV battery. The unit switches on automatically when sensors detect the HV battery under the trunk floor is approaching its upper temperature limit. -Inverter/Converter Cooling – HEVs and EVs typically have a cooling system for the combined inverter/converter that: -On HEVs, is separate from an engine cooling system, though typically uses the same type of coolant. It may have a separate radiator, water pump, and coolant reservoir -On HEVs, it may have a radiator integrated with an engine radiator, though there is still a separate water pump and coolant reservoir -Should be inspected after a collision looking for damaged radiator fins, hoses, connections, and coolant leaks. It must be determined whether the source of any coolant leak is the inverter/converter or an engine cooling system. -HV Battery Liquid-Cooling – some HEVs and EVs have a liquid-cooling system for the HV battery. These systems may be maintenance free, being completely contained inside the battery enclosure. This type of cooling system cannot be serviced. It is filled with a 50/50 mix of coolant and water when the battery is originally installed. Replacement of the HV battery is required if there is any damage to the battery cooling system. This type of cooling system has a separate cooling system for the HV battery. These are only serviced at a dealership -Service Notes – when servicing EV or Hybrid vehicles refer to service information for service notes and precautions. Service tasks that may expose the HV battery to high temperatures or involve repairing HV cables are commonly called out in the vehicle maker’s precautions. -Welding Precautions – when welding on an HEV or EV, follow the same precautions as when welding on a conventional vehicle. In addition, there are precautions to take with the high-voltage battery. Some examples of vehicle maker recommendations include -To disable the HV system is welding within 12 inches of the HV battery. Disable the battery by removing the service disconnect -Before cutting or welding near the HV battery, the HV battery must be removed to avoid heat damage -The HV battery must at least be protected with a welding blanket to protect it from sparks whenever welding -HV Battery and the Spraybooth – high temperatures, such as spraybooth bake cycles, can affect the performance and life of HV batteries. There is no harm to a clearcoat integrity in baking at a lower temperature for a longer time. To that end, most paint makers have a clearcoat available that cures quicker at a lower baking temperature -No Cable Repair- no vehicle maker allows repair of high or intermediate voltage cables or connectors. Any damage requires replacement of the cable harness Interior Trim and Convertible Top Damage Analysis (1/2) Module 1 – Interior Trim Interior Trim Overview – this course provides an overview of interior trim pieces, with a focus on damage analysis. The interior trim of a modern vehicle consists of many different types of parts ranging from seating to plastic parts. Although very durable, plastic interior-trim parts may be damaged in a collision. Damage to plastic interior-trim parts may range from small scratches to cracks or even complete breaks in the trim molding. Trim may be made from other materials aside from plastic. For example, from other materials aside from plastic. For example, luxury vehicles often use woodgrain trim to form dash and center console parts -Door and Door-Sill Trim – late model and current vehicle interior door-trim panels are made of a durable plastic and may include- -Courtesy lamps -Speakers -Lock and window controls -Airbag covers -Vents -Power-mirror switches -Note: Inspect door trim and door-sill trim for damage. The severity of damage may vary from light scratches to cracks and breaks, repair options for door and sill trim may be limited due to the difficulty of matching texture and leaving a visible repair. Repairs should not be made in the location of safety systems such as airbags. It is common to replace damaged door-trim panels and sill-trim panels -Pillar Trim – inspect the pillar trim for damage. Repair options may be limited. Most pillar trim is located near airbags and might be easily damaged during an airbag deployment. Verify that the attachment methods are undamaged. Some attachment methods may use one-time-use fasteners or clips and can impact repair vs. replace decisions. Pillar trim is commonly replaced, if damaged -Trunk and Rear Hatch Trim – collision damage may cause items in trunk to shift or move, causing damage to the rear hatch and trunk trim. Inspect for cracks, tears, and misalignment, as well as staining. Batteries located in the trunk that sustain damage from a collision may cause battery acid to spill onto interior trim panels. This could cause staining or deterioration of the panel trim. Trim that may have moved as a result of a collision may affect operation of closure panels. It may be necessary to remove items from the trunk to allow for better inspection of the trim panels. Typically, items that sustain damage are replaced -Sunroof Trim – inspect the sunroof trim for damage. Removal and replacement is common when the sunroof trim is damaged. Consult OEM repair manuals to determine if repairs might be possible -Woodgrain Trim – modern woodgrain trim may have a top layer that is only a few milimeters thick. Under this thin veneer, there are typically two layers of a more durable wood. Following a collision, this trim should be inspected for scratches, cracks, chips, and for other damage that may cause the thin veneer to lift or lose adhesion to the other layers of wood. Because repair options are often limited, damaged woodgrain-trim items are typically replaced -Seat Cover – seat covers require inspection for damage following a collision or a loss. Seats equipped with airbags will usually require replacement following a deployment. Seat covers are designed using a special fabric and thread and cant be replaced separately. Some stains may be removed with the appropriate cleaners; however, some stains may require seat cover replacements. OEM service information may further explain the limits of repairs that can be made to seat covers -Interior Lamps – when analyzing damage to the interior lamps, verify the proper operation. This is done by either manually operating the switch or by opening closure panels that have switches integrated into the latch mechanisms. If the interior lighting is inoperable, verify that this isn’t simply a burnt-out bulb or blown fuse. If these pass visual inspection, you may need to account for some diagnostic time to further determine the cause. Some vehicles have fiber-optic lighting, which is generally replaced, if damaged -Privacy Covers – items stored in the rear of the vehicle may damage the privacy cover during a collision. Inspect privacy covers for tears, scratches, and deformation. This type of damage usually requires the privacy cover to be replaced. Check that the privacy cover performs as designed. If inspecting a retractable privacy cover, verify that the retracting mechanism is operational -Carpet – inspect the interior trim for any damage or staining. Repairs to the carpet may be possible. Check the service information for any repair guidelines, as well as cleaning guidelines. Light stains, such as coffee or soft drinks, can be removed with stain removal products. Severe damage, such as tears or burns, may not be serviceable and may lead to replacement -Center Console/Center Stack – when inspecting the center console or center stack, check for any: -Scratches -Chips -Gouges -Breaks -Deformation -Note: Center stacks and center consoles may be made of textured plastic or painted plastic surfaces, which may be hard to replicate during the repair process. Replacement is likely when damage is severe. Consult the OEM service information for repair options. Also, inspect the gear selector for damage and proper operation -Visors, Headliners, and Overhead Consoles – verify proper operation of the visors through their full range of motion. Damage to headliners typically requires replacement because the construction materials limit repair options. Overhead consoles are typically made of plastic and may be textured, which limits repair options. -Dash, Instrument Panel, Glove Box, and Knee Bolster – the dash panel, instrument panel, glove box, and knee bolster are all areas that require inspection following a collision. Inspect for proper operation of the gauges and lights on the instrument panel. Check for normal operation of the glove box. Abnormal movement may indicate a misalignment. Construction materials, as well as textures, may limit repair options. Note: Check the OEM repair manual for any repair options for the dash, instrument panel, glove box, and the knee bolster -Interior Vents – interior vents are placed in many different locations around the vehicle. Verify that the airflow-direction adjusters are still operational. It may be necessary to run the vehicle and turn on the HVAC system for testing purposes. Look at the OEM repair information to verify if there are any repair options, but most likely broken vents will require replacement -Speakers, Audio, and Audio-Visual – audio and audio-visual systems may be mounted in the center stack and other locations throughout the vehicle interior. Following a collision, check for proper operation of all audio and audio-visual components. Damage to the infotainment system screen will likely require replacement. Speakers that sustain damage from a collision will likely be replaced. -Part Replacement Considerations – following a collision, some interior trim items may be repairable depending on the amount of trim damage and whether the parts are made up of repairable materials. Replacement considerations also include the cost and availability of the interior trim parts. In some cases, trim replacements may not be available as a serviceable part, but may be available as an assembly. For example, damage to woodgrain trim requiring replacement will likely only be available as part of the door-panel-trim assembly Interior Trim and Convertible Top Damage Analysis (2/2) Module 2 – Convertible Top -Types of Convertible Tops – a soft-top roof is made of fabric. Soft-top roofs may be deisnged to raise and lower manually or electronically through the use of either hydraulics or electric motors. When closing the roof, latching mechanisms may be used to secure the soft-top frame just above the windshield. Other types of convertible tops include hardtop, targa, and T-top -Hardtops – hardtop convertibles are more durable than soft-top roofs and commonly feature an automatically operated, multi-link, retractable, self-storing roof. Instead of using fabric, hardtops are commonly constructed of metal. Non-retractable hardtops may feature manual latches to allow the roof to be lifted off and removed from the vehicle -Targa and T-Tops – a targa top is a semi-convertible body style with a removable-roof section. These tops differ from T-tops, which have a solid, non-removable T-bar centered between the top of the windshield and the rear roll bar. Targa tops are repaired like other exterior body panels. Refer to OEM service information for specific repair information. T-tops generally have two separate roof panels located above the seats that fit between the windshield and the central T-bar. Repair options may be limited with T-tops. Typically, the glass sections on a T-top are replaced when damaged -Parts of Convertible Tops – soft-top, convertible-top parts commonly include the: -Top and window -Frame and bows -Pivot brackets -Side rails and tension cables -Retainers -Seals and weatherstripping -Note: Soft-top, convertible roofs may be constructed from multiple layers of textile material to provide sound dampening and insulation. Some soft-top roofs may feature vinyl rear windows. The soft top shown here features a rear window with heated glass -Frame and Bows – a typical four-bow, convertible frame may include: -A tack rail -Diagonal or vertical frame members -Side members -Bows that span over the passenger compartment when the top is raised. The bow that latches at the front of the vehicle may be referred to as the header bow or top header -Pivot Brackets, Side Rails, and Cables – the pivot-bracket mechanism allows the frame to fold at the diagonal or vertical frame member. Side rails attach to the top of the pivot bracket to support the roof bows. Frame members may be supported and guided by tension cables. In addition to tension cables, tops may be held to the folding top frame, along the side members, with special cables that are referred to as hold-down cables -Retainers – reinforcements are necessary when attaching the fabric to the convertible frame. The headliner lines the inside of the roof, under the outer edges of the soft top fabric. Headlining provides a sturdy edge reinforcement. The fabric and headliner then fasten to the convertible frame at the top header and at other key frame areas with the support of the retainer strips. These strips may be fastened to the convertible frame with rivets or with other fasteners -Seals and Weatherstripping – convertibles rely on seals and weatherstripping to insulate the passenger compartment and prevent leaks. Seals are commonly not part of the top itself but instead are attached to the folding framework. If a seal is a permanent part of the top, then it is commonly manufactured into the replacement top -Other Common Parts- convertibles may feature manual top or automatic latches located on the header bow to secure the top. Automatic latch assemblies may include lock motors and drive mechanisms. These latches typically have limited repair options. Sensors monitor the operation of parts, such as the: -Convertible top -Side windows -Tonneau cover -Latches -Note: Tops may not operate if the sensors signal a fault to the movable-top, control module. When a top fails to open or close, a scan tool may be required to determine the location of the fault -Hidden vs. Visible Retracted Tops – modern sport vehicles may feature hidden, retractable hardtops with complex systems for tucking the folded roof neatly behind the rear seat. Vehicle makers have many designs for hiding retracted tops. Some vehicles feature flat panels that slide over the retracted roof, so that a cover panel locks in flush with the trunk lid. With less exposure, hidden retracted tops often sustain less wear and damage than visible retracted tops -Hydraulic vs. Motorized Systems – hydraulic systems include a hydraulic pump assembly and hydraulic lines. Where motorized systems use an electric motor and cables, hydraulic systems energize solenoids and supply pressure to the hydraulic-pump cylinders to raise and lower the top. Both systems should be tested for full range of operation and alignment. If the top does not open or close for a hydraulic system, check for leaks in the hydraulic lines and for leaks at the pump. Note: Verify communication between sensors and modules before checking for hydraulic leaks -Common Types of Damage – - Top fabric may be damaged by mold and mildew, which requires the application of cleaners. Check OEM documentation for cleaning recommendations. Fabric damage may also include: -Rips -Tears -Punctures -Burn marks -Staining -This type of fabric damage typically warrants replacement of the soft-top fabric. Other common types of damage may include: -Frame damage -Leaks -Faulty latch mechanisms -System Inspection – if the top is not moving, whether open or closed, check the open/close switch to verify it is set to automatic. Also, check that the sensors and fuses associated with the roof are in working order. Commonly, a scan tool may be used to verify the operation of convertible sensors -Frame Damage and Leaks – check the frame hinges, side members, and bows for bends, breaks, or improper alignment. Leaks may be caused due to damaged seals and weatherstripping, misalignment, and fabric damage -Faulty Latch Mechanisms – faulty latch mechanisms may affect whether or not the removable top will lock down above the passenger area. Latching mechanisms may fail due to severe collision damage that bends or breaks the latch mechanical components. For an electronically actuated latch mechanism, check for damage to wiring harnesses or other convertible-top sensors -Repairing Damaged Hardtops – depending upon the severity of the damage, some hardtops may be repairable. Minor damage can often be repaired much like other exterior panels on the vehicle. Verify the OEM repair procedures for the damaged hardtop components -Complete Replacement Considerations – severe top and frame damage may warrant a complete top replacement. Frame-alignment issues may include top-latch mechanisms that do not line up or tops that do not correctly fold. Frame replacement may be required if there are no adjustments that resolve these misalignments. Initializations program vehicle systems to learn the full range-of-motion for a newly installed top. Vehicles must be independently assessed to determine what initializations are necessary. Depending on OEM specifications, a scan tool may be required to perform these processes Introduction to Electric Vehicles (1/2) Introduction to electric vehicles -Future of Transportation – electric vehicles are no longer thought of as the future of transportation. The future of transportation is here, now. Today, there are over 1 million electric vehicles operating on the roads of the United States. Globally, that number jumps to almost 5 million. Those numbers are projected to continue to grow each year. Due to tremendous battery technology advancements and the increased reliability and range, the electric vehicle is becoming a more viable transportation solution. For comparison, in 2011, there were only nine different models of EVs available. As of 2020, there are approximately 43 models from a variety of different vehicle makers -Are You Seeing More EVs Today? – with the transition to EVs fully underway, a new report released by the Edison Electric Institute (EEI) and the Institute for Electric Innovation (IEI), Electric Vehicle Sales and the Charging Infrastructure Required through 2030, projects that U.S. EV sales will exceed 3.5 million vehicles in 2030. That will be more than 20 percent of all annual vehicle sales. With the rise in the number of EVs on the road, the collision repair industry must prepare for electric vehicles being a higher percentage of their business -Types of Electric Vehicles -Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) – HEVs are powered by both gasoline/diesel and electricity. The electric energy is produced by the vehicle’s on-board generator and regenerative braking system. Regenerative braking uses an electric vehicles motor as a generator to convert much of the kinetic energy lost when decelerating back into stored energy in the vehicles battery. Mild hybrids always rely on an internal combustion engine (ICE). A single electric motor assist with acceleration and some regenerative braking. Full hybrids typically have greater electric propulsion capabilities compared to mild hybrids. They typically start off using the electric motor. Then, as the load or speed rises, the engine will kick in. both motors are controlled by an internal computer to ensure the greatest economy for the driving conditions. Visual indicators are – Combustion engine, fuel door, exhaust pipe, badging and labels -Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)- PHEVs are powered by both an ICE and electricity. The difference between HEVs and PHEVs is that PHEVs can recharge the battery by an on-board generator, regenerative braking, and an electric charging port. Typically, the HV battery will be larger. Depending on the manufacturer, the PHEVs can differ based on the primary energy source. Some PHEVs favor the fuel as their primary energy source where others rely on the electricity. Visual indictors are – Combustion engine, fuel door, exhaust pipe, charging port, badging and labels -Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) – BEVs are fully electric vehicles. These vehicles do not have an ICE, fuel system, or exhaust system. They are only powered by the electricity stored in the high-voltage battery system. BEVs must use an external charging station or cable to an AC outlet to charge the battery. A regenerative braking system is typically part of their operational strategy as well. Visual indicators are – Charging port, badging and labels -EV Systems – regardless of the make, model, or year of the electric vehicle, all electric vehicles contain some type of energy storage, control, and propulsion system -Energy Storage System – -High-Voltage Battery – the HV battery provides all of the electricity needed for the vehicle propulsion and other on-board systems. These batteries are typically lithium-ion batteries and are rechargeable. Lithium-ion batteries are known to have a high energy density for their weight and are able to retain their charge when not being used. The HV battery will produce a large amount of heat when the vehicle is operating. Thermal management requires that the battery cells be kept within a specific temperature range. This is accomplished by internal cooling of the battery. In most cases, the HV battery pack is engineered and designed to work with the vehicle structure to manage collision energy. -Low-Voltage Battery – the low-voltage battery system powers the vehicle electronics, control modules, and some accessories. The low-voltage battery provides power to the health and monitor functions of the high-voltage battery system. The charge is typically maintained by the HV battery and on-bard DC/DC converter. Depending on the make, model, or year of vehicle, the battery may be placed in different locations -Charging Port – the charging port allows the vehicle to connect to an external power supply in order to charge the high-voltage battery pack. Charging connections come in different types depending on the vehicle. The charging cable usually has an interlock to the charge port when the vehicle is actively charging. This is a two-step process involved an electrical and mechanical cable locking and release mechanism. When the charging cord is plugged into the vehicle, there is a validation, or handshake, between the external charging system and the vehicle system. This ensures a safe electrical connection and conditions before charging begins -On-Board Charger – the on-board charger takes the incoming AC from the charging port and converts it to DC power for charging the HV battery. It monitors battery characteristics such as voltage, current, temperature, and state of charge while charging the HV battery. Similar to many of the parts found on an EV, the on-board charger is in different locations based on the vehicle maker design. -Control System – -Power Electronics Controller – the power electronics controller manages the flow of electricity between the HV battery and the electric motor. Alternating current (AC) is electric current that continuously changes direction, like power from the electric company. Direct current (DC) is power that only flows in one direction. Batteries store and supply DC power. Controllers may be a separate part or built onto the motor. The HV battery stores and produces DC power. The power electronics controller changes it to AC power to permit smooth, efficient control of the drive motor speed and torque. During regenerative braking, the drive motor generates AC power. This is converted to DC and sent to the HV battery. Power electronics controllers may be called a: Propulsion motor controller, Inverter/Converter -DC/DC Converter – the HV battery supplies several hundred volts of DC used for the high-voltage systems. The other electric systems in the vehicle typically run on much lower voltage requirements. The DC/DC converter changes the higher-voltage DC power from the HV battery to the low-voltage DC power needed to run the vehicle accessories and recharge the low-voltage battery. Some have intermediate voltages of 36-48 volts for electric power steering and other systems -Cooling – the cooling system maintains a proper operating temperature range of the HV battery pack, electric motor, power electronics, and other EV parts. The most common battery cooling method circulates a water-based coolant through cooling passages within the battery structure. The A/C system may use a chiller, a refrigerant-to-coolant heat exchanger to remove heat from the coolant, thus removing heat from that system. Some vehicle makers may use air cooling to manage the battery temperature -Propulsion System – -Electric Motor – using energy from the HV battery, the electric motor drives the vehicle wheels. Electric motors perform both the drive and regeneration functions. In an electric car, there is no alternator. The motor in an electric car acts as both the motor and an alternator. That’s one of the reasons why electric cars are so unique. When your foot is on the accelerator, energy from the battery is used to power the motor, which supplies torque to the gears, which rotate the tires. When your foot comes off the accelerator, the motor turns into an alternator, and this energy may be used to help recharge the battery. Electric motors are capable of very high torque output at startup and across a wide rpm range -Transmission or Gearbox – the transmission transfers mechanical power from the electric motor to the drive wheels. Electric vehicles don’t have multi-speed gearboxes like an Ice vehicle does. Instead, they only need a single gear. This is because the electric motor does not operate in a limited rpm band typical of internal combustion engines. Electric motors have a much higher rpm capability compared to the ICE. Designers of EV can decide on a gear ratio that provides a good range between acceleration and top speed and use a single-gear ratio. There isn’t a need for a reverse gear. This function is handled electronically by reversing the direction rotation of the motor -Other EV Systems – PTC – all battery chemistry slows at lower temperatures reducing power capacity, lithium-ion batteries included. Batteries may need to be heated in colder temperatures. Vehicle makers typically use positive temperature coefficient (PTC) heaters to warm the coolant of the HV battery when its cold. Additionally, BEVs don’t have a conventional, internal combustion engine, cabin-heating system. They rely on PTC heaters to provide cabin heating. Air-cooled HV batteries have internal-heating elements to warm the battery modules -Other EV Systems – Electric Power Steering – electric power steering is very common on todays vehicles, including electric vehicles. Electric power steering replaces the need for hydraulic pumps, reservoirs, and fluid lines with electric motors. Sensors are used to detect the position and torque of the steering input from the column. A computer module controls the amount of torque applied based on the driving conditions. Other electric driven systems include the air conditioning compressor which operates using power from the high-voltage system -EV Instrument MILs – malfunction indicator lamps (MILs) don’t always provide a complete understanding of the vehicles health. Although, MILs do offer a great start in identifying possible faults. With an EV, there are a number of unique Mils that have been created. Each of the indicators can be vehicle-maker specific. There are common indicators that most EVs will display. These include ready, charging, battery warning, and service warning indicators -Ready Indicators – these indicators effectively inform a driver when an electric vehicle is ready to move. This is a replacement, of sorts, for the sound of a running gasoline engine. When the ready indicator is illuminated, normal driving is possible. Indicators related to vehicle movement include the park brake light and the Park/Fwd/Rev icons -Charging Indicators – these charging symbols display while the battery is being charged. Different colors provide further indication of the charge status. Oftentimes, the indicator displays when charging and then turns off when the battery is fully charged. Additional charging status indication and displays may be provided as well -Battery Warning Indicators – battery warning symbols indicate that there is a problem with the battery or that the battery will not take a charge. The entire propulsion system may be shut down. The vehicle should be evaluated by a qualified technician at a service shop. -Service Warning Indicators – these are some examples of EV power system warning indicators. For some EVs, these are the equivalent of a check engine light found on some combustion engine vehicles. These symbols may indicator a possible problem with some of the following EV systems: -Electric motor -Inverter system -Charging system -HV battery -Cooling system -Similar to a check engine light, this indicator does not provide a detail of what is wrong, only that something is wrong, and service is required -Other Indicators – there may be other indications of possible EV failures besides those shown on the instrument display. It’s a good practice to look, but don’t touch, for obvious damage that may have compromised the cooling system, high-voltage system wiring, connectors, modules, high-voltage battery, or other parts. Other indicators may include: -Smoke -Leaking fluids -Odd noises -Smells -If there is any doubt on the integrity of the HV system, battery, or cooling systems, the vehicle needs to be evaluated by a qualified HV service specialist -EV Charging Stations – when performing collision repair to an electric vehicle, the vehicle may need to have high-voltage battery charged. There are different power ratings, connector types, and cabling requirements based on the specific EV. Most vehicles come with a charging cable; however, the owner may not keep it in the vehicle. You may have to ask the customer to provide it, if you do not have one on hand. Charging times will vary depending on the state of charge, battery capacity, temperature, and other conditions. There are 3 different levels of charging: -Level 1 is slow charging using a 120 volt circuit. Typically, the charging time is 17-24 hours or more -Level 2 is fast charging using a 240 volt circuit. The charging time is between 4-5 hours or more -Level 3 is rapid charging using a higher-voltage DC circuit. Charging times can be as low as 30 minutes -Its important to have an understanding of how to connect, what the charge indicators mean, and how to disconnect the charging system by reviewing the owners manual -Embrace the Trend – the era of the electric vehicle is here today. More and more vehicle makers are producing different variants of EV. Within the collision repair industry, we need to embrace this trend and be prepared for it. Understanding the differences between an EV and an ICE vehicle is a good place to start. But its only a start. Each vehicle maker has specific guidelines, policies, and procedures that must be followed when working safely around EV. Ensure that you are aware of these Test – 1 – the high-voltage battery is typically what type of battery – lithium-ion 2- when this light is illuminated what does it indicate – the vehicle is able to move 3- of the three types of electric vehicles, the battery electric vehicle has the smallest high-voltage battery system – true – x wrong 4- the on-board charger converts AC power from the charging power to DC power for charging the HV battery – true 5- which part converts the high-voltage DC power from the HV battery to the low-voltage DC power – DC/DC converter Introduction to Squeeze-Type Resistance Spot Welding (1/4) Module 1 – STRSW Process -STRSW Beginnings – vehicle makers have been using STRSW on the assembly line for many decades and recommend this type of welding for a variety of applications -STRSW Process Recommended – STRSW is the method called for by most vehicle makers for collision repair. This is due to improvement in equipment. STRSW allows technicians to make welds that are as strong as the original welds. Vehicle makers recommend this type of welding due to the minimal heat-affect zone. STRSW reduces the concern when welding on advanced high-strength steels that are sensitive to heat. These steels are being used more in vehicle construction. Low heat-affect also allows preservation of most of the existing corrosion protection -STRSW Application – STRSW is not just the specification for exterior body panels. This type of welding may also be specified on some structural applications such as pillars, rocker panels, and rail flanges. Before using the machine on a vehicle, it is critical that test welds are made and destructively tested. This is done using the same type and thickness material, with the same coatings that will be welded on the vehicle. STRSW is not specified on aluminum during collision repair. Because of the lower resistance in aluminum and high-heat transfer characteristics, spot welding aluminum requires much higher levels of current than steel. Even with the onset of inverter technology in STRSW repair equipment, these high-power requirements may not be met -Process Characteristics – the STRSW process is the main OEM welding method. Repair welds can be made to look just like original welds. Original construction can be duplicated in the field with less chance of error compared to other welding methods. With STRSW, there is minimal sparking. Though there is some sparking with some welds, there is much less sparking than with other welding processes. Vehicle and personal protection are still required. STRSW requires less preparation in some instances. For example, there is no need for drilling holes in replacement panels, such as when making gas metal arc (GMA) plug welds. No filler metal is required. It is unnecessary to dress the weld in most applications -Personal Safety – personal safety equipment that should be worn includes safety glasses with side shields or a full-face mask. A filtered lens is not required. Always wear leather gloves. Remove all flammable materials before welding. Always follow STRSW equipment maker’s specific safety guidelines. Do not operate or place the welder near water or in wet locations. Do not place the welder on unstable or uneven ground. Unplug the welder from the wall outlet before servicing, cleaning, or maintenance -STRSW Setup – STRSW requires access to both sides of the panel. Vehicle makers may specify a maximum combined panel stack thickness. Different configurations of arm sets and electrode tips may be needed for different areas of the vehicle. Seam welds are not done with the STRSW process. Where a seam weld is required, such as many sectioning joints, a GMA welder is usually required -STRSW Stages – STRSW is a pressure-welding process. A spot weld is made in three stages, including: -Squeeze time -Current-on time (or weld time) -Hold time -Note: The squeeze time stage occurs when the electrode tips close against the panels to be welded. The current-on time stage occurs when the current is turned on, lasting for a specified time. The hold time stage occurs after the current is turned off, allowing the weld to solidify. STRSW machines may have pre-programmed or automatic settings. Some welding machines allow some or all of these stages to be fine-tuned in a manual setting Introduction to Three-Dimensional (3-D) Measuring (1/3) Principles of Measuring -Why do we Measure Vehicles? – measuring the vehicle structure helps identify the full extent of damage and find hidden damage during the estimating/blueprinting process. It is also used to: -Develop a repair plan -Monitor repair progress -Provide documentation -Note: measuring helps to ensure a smooth repair process, with reduced supplements and/or internal turnbacks. If measuring is not performed the likelihood of encountering problems later in the repair increases. For example, the repair may seem complete, but when the bolt-on panels are installed, the do not close properly. This indicates that the vehicle was not properly straightened during repaired -Measuring – measuring provides direction during the structural straightening process. It shows where specific vehicle points are currently located and where those points need to be for the vehicle to be repaired properly. It also helps to make sure the vehicle is not overpulled and additional damage is not created. Measuring also provides documentation that straightening was performed to the vehicle-maker specifications. This can be used to show both insurers and customers that the work was performed. Finally, measuring helps to restore the vehicle structure so safety systems operate properly. Sensor operation, such as airbag sensors and the advanced driver assistance system (ADAS), rely on a structure that is restored to specification -When Should we Measure? – one of the primary questions asked is, “When do I measure?” vehicles should be measured when: -There is suspected structural misalignment, meaning the body lines are off -They have been in light, moderate, or severe collisions -There is a restraint system deployment -A wheel is out of position -Type of Damage – when examining outer body panels, determine if a dent or deformation is related to a direct impact, indirect impact, or inertial damage. If it is indirect damage, it is an indication of a structural issue that should be examined further. Some recommendations exist that state every vehicle should be measured. However, this may not be necessary if the damage is limited to the outer panel only. But if there is any doubt, measure the vehicle. This will help improve key performance indicators (KPIs) during the repair process -Why Measure for Damage Analysis? – measuring can be done for damage analysis, as well as during collision repair. This type of measuring can indicate if there are structural issues that need to be repaired and further measuring needs to take place. Measuring helps determine if there is any hidden, secondary, or pre-existing damage that needs to be accounted for on an estimate and helps determine a repair plan. If the system is computerized, it can provide documentation of structural damage and can be attached to the estimate and/or provided to the insurer, if necessary -Damage Analysis Measuring Equipment – measuring equipment for damage analysis must be easy to set up, portable, quick to use, and generally should not mounting a vehicle to a bench. Measuring can be done from a lift or while on the ground, resting on its suspension. Several examples of measuring equipment are shown here -Where to Find Measurements – before any measuring can begin, vehicle-specific dimensions are required. These are available from: -Vehicle makers -Measuring equipment makes, often supplied with their measuring equipment -Information providers, such as ALLDATA, Mitchell, CCC, etc -Vehicle-Maker Measurements – vehicle-maker measurements often include both point-to-pint and three-dimensional measurements. Measurements are provided online at the vehicle-makers websites -Measuring Sheets – measuring sheets may be supplied by the equipment makers or information providers -Equipment Makers – equipment makers provide complete specification that can be accessed for each vehicle. The measuring information typically comes from: -Vehicle makers, which may include computer-aided design (CAD) drawings -Measurements from new vehicles using their own equipment. Generally, measurements are taken away from only one new vehicle, rather than multiple vehicles -Data from other information providers (such as Mitchell, ALLDATA, etc) -Information Providers – information providers, such as Mitchell, CCC, and ALLDATA, are also good sources for vehicle measurements. The majority of their numbers are provided by the vehicle makers. The graphic shown here is from ALLDATA -Additional Sources – there may be times when there are no measurements available from any of the previous sources. This may be because the vehicle is too new or the measurement required is not listed on any of the data sheets. If this is the case, comparative measurements can be taken from one side of the vehicle to the other, assuming the measurements are symmetrical, or from an undamaged vehicle of the same model and year -Symmetrical Measurements – symmetrical measurements are the same on each side of the vehicle. For example, a door opening on the driver side has the exact dimensions as a door opening on the passenger side -Asymmetrical Measurements – asymmetrical vehicles have different measurements from side-to-side or changes in the vehicle structure that can cause variations in measurements from side-to-side. Asymmetrical measurements may be caused by reference points that are in different locations. As shown in this example, some vehicles may have strut tower bolts in different positions. Since the strut tower bolts are reference points, the measurements may be different, even though there is no structure variation between the left and right side of the vehicle. It is important to identify asymmetrical measurements when making comparative measurements. The measurements may appear off, but that may be by design -Tolerance – tolerance is the amount of variation in vehicle dimensions from vehicle-to-vehicle that is considered to be within specification. Maintaining tolerance ensure that when vehicles are assembled, all parts fit properly, such as closure panels (i.e. doors, hoods) suspension parts, and drivetrain parts. Measuring systems can be set so that measurements that are not within tolerance are displayed in different colors. In this example, out-of-tolerance measurements are displayed in red -Tolerance Example – without tolerances, there will be too much variation which in turn leads to a product that does not function as designed. For example, consider a table with parts (legs, table top, fasteners, etc.) that are manufactured at different sites and then assembled at another site. If the parts are not built within a specific tolerance, there will be an issue at the assembly plant. Most likely, parts will not line up and the table will be difficult to assemble or not look correct when assembly is complete. -Build Tolerance – build tolerance is the allowable variation in specific measurements during vehicle construction at the assembly plant. Build tolerance can vary, depending on the assembly line, and can vary from vehicle-to-vehicle of the same make and model and still be within specification. For some vehicle makers, this can be less than one millimeter, for others it may be more -Build Tolerance: Vehicle-to-Vehicle – variations in build tolerance can differ even between the same make and model. This may be due to vehicles being assembled on different manufacturing jigs in different vehicle assembly plants and can be caused by variations from part suppliers. The vast majority of vehicle makers do not provide a tolerance number, they only provide specifications. Build tolerances can vary from year-to-year for the same vehicle make and model -Vehicle Repair Tolerance – repair tolerance is used during straightening process to determine the specific location of a measuring point. The repair tolerance varies between vehicle makers. For example, some North American vehicles may have a little more tolerance compared to some European or Asian vehicles. Previously, 3 or 5 mm tolerances were typically acceptable on older model vehicles. However, this recommendation is not as common with late model vehicle design. Today, vehicle structures overall have a tighter tolerance regardless of the vehicle maker -Tolerance on Different Parts of a Vehicle – some parts of the vehicle have tighter tolerances than others. These may include: -Sensor mounting locations (airbag sensors, lane change sensors, adaptive cruise control sensors -Suspension mounting locations -Door openings, hinges, and strikers Introduction to Three-Dimensional (3-D) Measuring (2/3) Identifying Damage Conditions -Measuring Types – there are two types of measuring: -Point-to-Point measuring is a simple measurement of distance between two points -Three-dimensional measuring is a measurement that determines the location of a point using length, height, and width -Point-to-Point Measurements – point-to-point measurements are made from center-to-center or edge-to-edge of a set of reference points and are often used as a quick check during the damage analysis process. They may be used to verify a three-dimensional measurement, or as a quick check during vehicle repair. Point-to-point measurements are not considered a reliable method for measuring a vehicle. A point-to-point measurement only measures the distance between point A and point B. this measurement cannot be used to reliably determine if a point on a vehicle is at the right height, length, or width specification. In many situations, assumptions are being made as to the integrity of the point-to-point measurements -Point-to-Point Measuring Tools – the most common tools used for point-to-point measurements include a tape measure and a tram gauge or tram bar. Measurements should be documented for vehicle estimates and repairs. Tape measures and most tram bars provide limited capability to generate a measuring report and therefore require manual recording of measurements. Tram bars may tie into a three-dimensional electronic measuring system and allow recording of measurements. Additionally, three-dimensional electronic equipment can provide point-to-point measurements -Tape Measure – some measuring tapes have a T-hook to keep the end in place while measuring. The tape measure should lie flat between points for the most accurate reading -Tram Gauge/Tram Bar – tram gauges are available in different sizes and have adjustable tips. The adjustable tips allow you to extend the points of the gauge so measurements can be made around protruding objects, such as exhaust systems. Tram gauges may either have either measuring scales built-in or digital displays and typically have tips that fit various-sized reference holes. If pointers are set at different heights to avoid an obstruction, the measurement on the tram gauge does not reflect the true measurement between the pointers. A tape measure should be used to measure between the tips of the tram gauge extensions -Point-to-Point Measuring – point-to-point measuring is often used for checking that a section of the vehicle is square. This requires making a diagonal measurement from one side of the vehicle to the other. Point-to-point measuring is used for checking the under hood area and the under body area in the front, center, or rear sections. When checking if portions of the vehicle are square, this technique can provide correct measurements even if the structure is out of alignment. This why other dimensions must be referenced during point-to-point measuring. Making length and width measurements can help verify that a section is symmetrical -Why Three-Dimensional Measuring is Necessary – the width (centerline plane), length (zero plane), and height (datum plane) each represent one dimension that, when combined, are used to locate a specific point. Three-dimensional measuring is the only way to determine if the length, width, and height are within specification. It is also the only way to determine if the vehicle points are in the proper position relative to other points on the vehicle -Datum Measurements – in some instances, tram gauge pointers may be set at different heights to make datum measurements. This type of measurement generally does not provide accurate results, because it is difficult to determine if the gauge is exactly parallel to the datum plane. Not holding the bar parallel to the datum plane may cause the measurement to be longer or shorter than specification -Definition – the three-section principle divides the vehicle into three sections -Front includes the under hood and underbody areas forward of the cowl -Center includes the passenger compartment -Rear includes the deck lid opening, rear body panel, trunk floor, and rear rails -Center Section – the center section is located beneath the passenger compartment area between the front and rear wheelhouses. The center section is the foundation of measuring and is generally measured first. Three undamaged measuring points are required to establish a foundation for measuring. If the center section is undamaged, it provides a reference for all other measurements -Torque Box – torque boxes were parts of a full frame where weak portions of the frame were boxed in to add strength. These were typically where the front of the frame connected to the center, and the center of the frame connected to the rear. With the advancement of vehicle design and construction, the torque box is not all that common and therefore, can be confusing to some technicians. Now, the torque box is more of a description of a location on a vehicle, not so much referring to a specific part on a vehicle Introduction to Three-Dimensional Measuring (3/3) Measuring the Vehicle -Vehicle-Maker Measuring Sheets – measuring sheets from the vehicle maker often have specific symbols, icons, and other identifiers to help technicians locate specific measuring points. Icons are used to indicate if the measurements are point-to-point or three-dimensional. They may also be used to describe which side of the vehicle is being refenced. For example, Toyota uses upper and lowercase letters to identify the left and right side of the vehicle. In the example shown here, letters are used to identify measuring locations. Additionally, the vehicle-maker measuring sheets will have specific instructions, such as how to identify symmetrical measurements and how to identify the length, width, and height measuring points -Measuring Sheet Information Introduction – there is other information available on the measuring sheet -Loaded vs Unloaded Measurements and Movable Parts – a loaded measurement is made when the vehicle is resting on the suspension. An unloaded measurement is when the vehicle is lifted in the air, off the suspension. There may also be different measurements based on whether or not the engine is in or out of the vehicle, since the weight of the engine can affect height measurements in the front. However, this is becoming less of an issues since higher-strength steels are used in the construction of the structure. Measurements for movable parts include such things as suspension parts, for example, ball joints -Underbody and Under Hood Measurements – upper body measurements often include under hood, pillars, door openings, the deck lid opening, or windshield and back glass openings -Reading Vehicle-Maker Measuring Sheets – when reading vehicle-maker measuring sheets, keep in mind: -The locations of the zero plane and datum plane are provided in the manual. It is written as a specific distance and point. For example, the datum plane may be listed as 300 mm below the rocker panel, lower flange edge -Reference points may also be identified by a letter on a vehicle diagram that corresponds to a letter and measurements listed in a chart -The three-dimensional measurements are taken from the datum, zero, and centerline planes -Note: This measuring sheet shows the zero plane location in the rear torque box area -Selecting the Correct Data Sheet- it is important to select the correct data sheet when measuring a vehicle. While this may seem obvious, selecting an incorrect vehicle data sheet is a common error when setting up a measuring system. Mistakes include choosing the wrong make/model/year or the wrong cab style of box length (for example, extended cab vs crew cab). Some systems may also require entering the two-wheel or four-wheel drive option -Setting Up Measurements – after setting up the base measurements or center section, if the measurements do not make sense when compared to the damage, check the vehicle make, model, and year. Ensure that the vehicle matches what is being used by the measuring system to track the measurements. This includes making sure the points being measured on the vehicle are the correct ones. The measuring system uses what you enter. If a location to measure is chosen within the measuring system and the technician measures an incorrect data point on the vehicle, the measuring software will take that input as the point, even if it is incorrect. -Establishing Base Measurements – one of the first tasks in measuring is to establish base measurements. For electronic measuring systems, base measurements are specific points that all other measurements will be based on when working. Base measurements require at least three good points to ensure an electronic measuring system can establish length, height, and width. You must choose locations where there is high confidence that the points are in the correct locations. Typically, base measurements are made in the center section of the vehicle, generally in the four farthest corners. -Establishing Base Measurements (cont’d) – the center section is chosen because those points typically do not move during the straightening process. Think of the center section as being similar to the foundation of a home. The vehicle is built around the center section. If the base-measurement locations are incorrect, they will affect all other measurements and the vehicle will not be measured properly. Note that with most systems, the vehicle does not have to be on a level surface to be measured properly. For most systems, level is relative to the establishing datum plane -Triangles – you must identify three good points for three-dimensional measuring. Two points do not provide the reference needed to establish a datum measurement. The chair illustrates how three points establish a good foundation and two points do not. Until a triangle known good points is established, the three-dimensional measurements throughout the vehicle cannot be established -Common Base-Measurement Errors – problems that can occur with setting up the base measurements include using a point that is damaged or accidentally using a point that is different than what the equipment maker stated to use. This can cause a reading to be incorrect, as the user told the computer it was the correct location, but it is not. Other problems include changes in vehicle construction that caused the measurements to change and having undercoating or sealing affecting base measurement readings -Center Section Base Point – if there is a center section base point that is not correct, generally there will be multiple areas that show up as damaged, even if no damage exists. This is where the technician must determine if the damage matches what the computer identified as damaged areas. For example, if the damage is in the rear of the vehicle, and the front is not damaged but showing sway or sag, then maybe there is a problem that is affecting the other measurements, and that may stem from an improper base measurement setup -Troubleshooting Measuring Points – to help identify the point that is creating problems, begin by removing the base reference targets. As stated, only three good points are necessary, so removing the point that is causing problems will help with the remainder of the measurements. Perform this check one target at a time, until that point is found. In the graphic, the elongated reference hole should not be used for establishing a base measurement -Vehicles with a Damaged Center Section – if the vehicle center section is out of alignment or one of the four base-measuring points is damaged, look for at least three good points. Measuring systems will be able to make length, height, and width measurements using three good points in the center section. If there are only two good points, you must find a third good point from within the center section. Note that two good points do not allow a height measurement, so a third must be established for the three-dimensional measurement process. This may require some preliminary pulling to restore a damaged point before other damage can be repaired -Determine Which Points to Measure – when you determine which points to measure, choose points based on the extent of damage, and measure as far as possible into the damaged area, or as far away from the base measurements as possible, to get the most accurate picture of the damage. Short measurements may be within specification, but as measurements are projected out toward the end of the vehicle, you may find that the measurement is now out of specification -Determine Which Points to Measure (cont’d) – you should choose points such as holes, slots, bolts, etc. Based on how the vehicle is assembled, it is recommended to use the points that were used to jig the vehicle structure during assembly. This graphic highlights multiple areas of the underbody that can be measured. -Measuring Outside the Center Section – small amounts of damage or structural distortion can distort other areas of the vehicle. Because of this, you should choose points outside the base-reference points to measure as much of a part as possible to determine the extent of damage. For example, if a front rail is damaged, continue making rearward measurements to determine where the damage stops. Note; Sometimes parts must be removed on the vehicle underbody to access the measuring points and take measurements. Taking the time to identify problems with the vehicle structure will ultimately reduce cycle time -Sectioning – during the straightening process, if a part is going to be sectioned, measure closest to the sectioning location. The area behind and adjacent to the sectioning location is what needs to be repaired. There is no need to measure into the area that will be removed, except for documentation purposes -Radiating Damage – vehicles with moderate damage or more can have damage that radiates to the opposite side of the vehicle. With the use of high-strength steel, especially around the passenger compartment, vehicles may look like there is no damage away from the point of impact, but the transfer of collision energy around the passenger compartment can put damage into the opposite side of the impact. Measuring the entire vehicle can help identify any issues, such as indirect damage or prior damage -Measuring Full-Frame Vehicles – measuring full-frame vehicles is similar to measuring the underside of a unitized structure. However, note that with full-frames, there is a separate unitized structure (cab) that is mounted to it. Depending on the measuring system, you may need to treat the cab and the frame separately, with each having their own centerlines. Using the frame centerline when measuring the cab can create problems if the cab is not exactly centered on the frame. You may use the frame centerline to make measurements on the unitized structure. It is important for you to know your measuring system to understand how cab measurements are made -Measuring Full-Frame Cab Points – measuring equipment makers provide specifications for the frame, but not always for the cab. To measure the cab, use four symmetrical points under the cab to establish the base points. These points will be manually entered into the measuring system: -Common points include: -Drain holes -Seat belt bolts -Rocker panel pinch welds -To measure the cab, use comparative measurements. Comparative measurements include: -Door openings -Hinges -Radiator core support mounting locations -Other cross measurements, such as the under hood area, pillars, and glass openings -Measuring Suspension Points – measuring suspension-mounting points helps identify whether the suspension mounts are in the correct locations and identifies damaged suspension system parts. This ensures that a vehicle may be aligned. If mounts or parts are damaged, the correct angles for steering and suspension, such as caster, camber, toe, steering axis inclination (SAI), or thrust angle, may not be able to be achieved. Note: Measuring suspension points can reduce cycle time by reducing the number of times a vehicle is sent out for alignment. Each time a vehicle is sent out, there is an increase in labor times and repair time. This can be avoided by ensuring the vehicle structure is aligned before the vehicle is sent out for a wheel alignment -What the Numbers mean – after the vehicle has been measured, it is time to interpret the numbers. You need to determine: -Do the measurements match the damage? -How far into the vehicle does the damage go? -Is there damage beyond the area of direct impact? -Note: At this point, you can see how far the damage extends into the vehicle and can being formulating your repair plan to determine how the damage will be removed from the structure -Do You Believe in Numbers? – if the measuring results are showing damage in an area that is not damaged, the numbers may not be right. This may be due to changes to measuring points that occur from changes to vehicle design. This can occur mid-model year on some vehicles. It may also be due to changes to the type of construction material used for vehicle design. This, too, can occur mid-model year or even in different model years of the same body style. If the vehicle maker uses different vendors, the part design may not be exactly the same as the previous vendor. Finally, numbers which don’t make sense may be due to problems with the base-reference-point setup -Troubleshooting the Numbers – if the numbers do not look like they match the damage, examine the base-reference points for potential problems. Look for damage, obstructions, etc. If you are using four base-reference points, removing or checking one of the base reference points at a time may help determine if the problem is related to a specific base-reference point or if the problem is somewhere else -Troubleshooting the Numbers (cont’d) – when checking the points, make sure the targets or sensors are hung properly. If using a laser-type measuring system, make sure there are no obstructions between the target and the laser sensor. The image here shows an improperly seated target. This will provide a false reading. Additionally, be sure that the measurement is taken from the correct point and that the correct adapters are used -Use Common Sense – three-dimensional measuring systems do not remove the need for common sense. When measuring, some things simply cannot occur. For example: -Vehicle parts cannot get longer in a collision -If there is absolutely no damage to a frame rail, does it make sense that it measures 25 mm too short? -Can a vehicle have a banana-shape with light damage to the front rail? -Note: Examine the data from the measuring system. If the measurements do not match the damage, start thinking of what could be causing the problem. Are the base measurements in specification? Are the correct measuring points chosen? Has anything changed to the vehicle construction from previous models? -Interpreting the Numbers – -When interpreting the numbers, you should identify: -The specifications that are out of tolerance -The location of the primary damage -The location of secondary damage, if any -Note the measurements of the front rail, front torque box, and rear rail on the dimension sheet shown here: -Using this graphic, you should be able to identify the following: -The left and right front rails are out of specification. All numbers in red are out of specification -The primary damage is located on the front of the vehicle -There is no secondary damage -Diamond Causes – diamond occurs from an impact to one side of a full-frame vehicle or the vehicle takes a hard hit to the suspension. In a front collision, inertia causes one side of the frame to continue its forward momentum, while the other side stops. In a rear collision, the impact to one side can push that side of the frame forward. It is important to note that with late-model frames, severe diamond is less likely a repairable option. Due to the increase in steel strength and more complex frame designs, kinks often occur to the frame crossmembers -Confirming Diamond – if diamond is suspected, use the zero plane to measure for diamond. When using electronic measuring systems, the zero plane often splits the measurement difference. In a measuring printout, one side of the frame will have positive measurements and the other side will have equal negative measurements. The computerized display for electronic measuring systems may show one side forward and the other side rearward -Upperbody Measuring Considerations – when measuring the upperbody, realize the upperbody and underbody may react differently in a collision. Design and material content of a vehicle will determine how a vehicle reacts in a collision. For example, with a cargo van, there can be significant damage to the upper structure in a rear or side collision, but there will be less on vehicles with lower profiles. Damage can radiate upward, in addition to forward and rearward. For example, sway in the lower body structure can cause panel gaps to be off on the doors, hood, and deck lid, as well as a wrinkle in the roof or roof rails -Where to Get Upperbody Measuring Data – most electronic measuring systems do not come with three-dimensional upperbody measurements. Many will provide point-to-point measurements, which can be used to make sure the upper structure has been restored. This will help ensure panels fit during the repair process. Because three-dimensional upperbody measurements may not be supplied by the equipment makes, measuring systems have features that allow technicians to create their own measurement points. This can be done through comparative measurements. This requires taking measurements from an undamaged side of the vehicle to set the baseline and then taking a measurement from the damaged side -Common Measuring Points – common points measured on a vehicle include: -Points along the upper rail (e.g., hood attachment and radiator core support attachment points) -Door openings, including hinge mounting points -Notches along the hem flanges -Door striker bolts -Stationary glass openings -Using Three-Dimensional Equipment for Upperbody Measurements – most electronic and fixture systems have extensions to measure upperbody locations. Some have specific equipment designed to measure the strut tower positions, but can be adapted to measure many of the upperbody points -Upperbody Bar – the upperbody bar for this universal fixture system is used to measure the strut tower position -Strut Tower Measurements – in this graphic, the long vertical bar next to the fender is part of the upperbody measurement tool and is being used to take strut tower measurements on this electronic measuring system -Using Tram Gauges for Point-to-Point Measurements – tram gauges can also be used to verify upperbody dimensions and to verify vehicle openings, such as the doors, underhood, and deck lid. They can also be used in areas where it is difficult to measure with three-dimensional equipment. Using point-to-point measurements can increase confidence that three-dimensional measurements are correct. Both point-to-point and three-dimensional measurements are used to verify each other MIG Brazing -Brazing is a form of adhesion joining that melts a filler metal onto the base metal. The metals are joined at a relatively low temperature where the base metal does not melt or fuse with the filler. Because the base metal does not fuse with the filler, brazing joins metals that otherwise would be incompatible. -The MIG Brazing welding - is a nonfusion process which generates heat from 1,675 degrees F to 1,866 degrees F. This allows the electrode to flow and bond to the surface of the metal. Brazing melts only the silicon bronze electrode wire and does not damage the molecular structure of the steel. The flowing, similar to a sweating process, gives the weld a larger footprint or surface area contact than a steel fusion weld. Because there is less heat and no melting of the base metal, MIG Brazing creates minimal damage and preserves zinc coatings for corrosion protection. -GMA Welding – steel welding is a fusion process which creates higher temperatures of 3,000 degrees F. GMA welding uses greater amperage than MIG Brazing. This process melts the filler metal and fuses it with the base metal. -Capillary action – is the flow of silicon bronze which spreads rapidly between the surfaces of the metal being joined. On an open butt joint, the capillary action forms a bead on the front and back of the metal while bonding to the edges too. Unlike steel welding, the root gap is larger to accommodate the capillary action. This gives a large surface bond, creating strengths similar to fusion welds. -Electrode Wire – use a different electrode wire when MIG brazing than when GMA welding. -Alloy – the alloys most commonly used for MIG brazing include – CuSi3 or CuSi-A which is a copper/silicon alloy with about 3% silicon, CuAI8 or CuAI-A1 which is a copper/aluminum alloy with about 8% aluminum. - The numbers on the end of each alloy are the European method of specifying the percentage of the alloying agent. CuSi3 is a copper/silicon alloy with about 3% silicon. CuSi-A is the same electrode as specified by AWS, where ‘A’ represents about 3% silicon. CuAI8 is the European specification for a copper/aluminum alloy. Copper is the main element. CuAI-A1 is the AWS specification. Bronze electrodes are softer than steel electrodes, but not as soft as aluminum. -Common electrode wire diameter are 0.30” (0.8 mm), 0.35” (0.9 mm), 0.40” (1.0 mm). Wire selection should be based on vehicle maker recommendations. When brazing an open butt joint, larger electrode wire diameter achieves better capillary action. -Open Butt Joint – for open butt joints, allow a root gap between the panels of about one-panel thickness for capillary action on the backside. A root gap that is too wide will tend to cause the arc to extinguish. Maintaining the root gap is important for successful capillary action on an open butt joint. Therefore, it is recommended to add tack welds along the joint before starting the bead. The molten metal must be allowed to flow into the joint and around both sides. Look at the backside of the joint to find a brazed bead as proof of capillary action. A butt joint with backing may be specified, depending on your market. -The open butt joint should have the entire joint filled on the face side. There should be a high-profile bead, not a flat bead. The bead should be wide enough to overlap the coupons on each side. The backside of the open butt joint should look similar to the face side, with a high-profile bead along the entire length, overlapping each coupon along the length. -Plug and Double Plug- There should be no sign of the original hole edges showing on the face side.
