For the backside of the plug and double plug, there should be a heat mark, not a ripple that can be felt. The heat mark should be at least the size of the original hole. The double plug should have an oval heat mark with little or no sign of the break between the original holes. -Shielding Gas – protects the MIG braze puddle. -Protection – shielding gas protects the arc and the molten electrode wire puddle from the atmosphere and airborne contaminants. This is important to avoid contamination which may cause porosity in the MIG braze. -Argon – the shielding gas used for MIG brazing is 100% argon. It provides good arc stabilization and less zinc vaporization. -Adjusting Flow – the typical flow rate is 25-30 cfh (11.8 – 14.6 L/min). It is important to verify that the gas regulator (flow gauge) has the correct scale for pure argon (Ar) -Short Circuit – once a welding machine is set to weld in the short-circuit transfer method allows the electrode wire to touch the workpiece momentarily. This short-circuits the electrode, creates an arc, and allows current to flow from the base metal into the electrode wire. This process occurs many times per second. The advantage of short-circuit transfer is that it uses lower amperage and voltage than other transfer methods. -Slot on Lap Joints – the slot on lap joints configuration is most commonly used by vehicle makers for MIG brazing. It may be required when installing panels either made from, or attaching to, High Strength Steel (HSS) or Ultra High Strength Steel (UHSS). Slots are made by drilling or punching consecutive holes and cleaning up the edges with a die grinder. When making lap joints, verify capillary action on the backside of the top workpiece. Do not apply too much pressure when clamping, as this may adversely affect capillary action. One vehicle maker specifies a related configuration by using one or two holes in place of the slot. -Slots – for slots, the vehicle maker may specify MIG brazing only one edge to replicate the original appearance and strength. If the vehicle maker specifies the slot weld be completely filled, there should be no sign of the original slot edges showing on the face side after you perform the peel test. The heat mark on the backside should be at least the size of the original slot. -Spray Arc – pulse spray arc uses a background current to preheat the electrode and base metal to maintain the arc. With pulse spray arc, tiny droplets of molten metal are sprayed across the electric arc and deposited onto the metal. The power source rapidly pulses between high peak current and low background current. The background current keeps the arc continuous, yet is not hot enough for metal transfer to occur. It is also designed to keep the electrode warm, reducing splatter and stabilizing the arc. A pulse spray arc welding machine is synergic, in that with one dial setting, voltage and wire speed (current) are both changed proportionally. -Techniques for MIG Brazing -Push, Pull or stitch - The push technique is recommended for MIG brazing the slot, double plug, or single plug. Pushing directs the shielding gas forward for better arc stabilization. This technique also pre heats the workpiece, allowing the MIG brazing electrode to flow better. Pushing helps the vaporized zinc to escape. It can also allow the technician to see the arc for better control. Using the pull technique does not preheat the metal as much. The pull technique traps more zinc in the MIG brazing zone, creating more porosity. Because of the higher zinc vapor content trying to escape the seam, the arc stability will fluctuate more. When brazing open butt joints, use the stitch method . -Work and Travel Angle – welding gun work angle is the angle of the welding gun to the joint. It affects where the electrode wire is aimed at the joint. The work angle for most MIG brazing is near 90 degrees to the joint. The travel angle is about 60 degrees. For a stitch method on the open butt joint, use an 80 degree angle. -Travel Speed – the joint configuration determines the travel speed you will use. For slots, begin slowly and end faster. For a double plug, also being slowly to ensure capillary action on the backside. Unlike the other configurations, the open butt joints use the stitch method. The continuous method does not achieve capillary action if it is too fast. -Pattern – with a single plug, double plug, and slot joint, make the braze using a zigzag pattern. Start outside of the opening in the top piece, work across, and end outside of the opening. Use the machine in pulse to help achieve the capillary action. This method allows the silicone bronze wire to melt and flow between the workpieces on the perimeter of the holes. The zigzag pattern forms a larger footprint beyond just the base of the holes, giving strength to the MIG brazing joint. -Stick Out – stick out is the distance from the end of the contact tip to the end of the electrode wire. Stick out may also be called contact tip-to-workpiece distance. When MIG brazing, stick out should be considerably longer than when GMA welding ¾ (19 mm) or longer. Longer stick out helps preheat the filler wire for greater capillary action and helps prevent making holes along the joint. -Dressing – if finishing the joint with a grinder, use 50-80 grit discs. Apply light pressure to the grinder. There are fewer sparks when grinding a bronze bead. Be alert to where you direct the grinding debris. Also, use caution when grinding to avoid removing excess filler material or base metal that will weaken the joint. Some vehicle makers may have statements regarding dressing. The joint may not require dressing if the joint is not visible after repairs are complete. -Wire Feeder and Drive Rolls – the wire feeder delivers the electrode wire to the arc automatically at a constant speed. Generally, the wire speed control also regulates the current flow of the machine. As the wire speed rate is increased, the amperage is increased as well. With pulse spray arc transfer, the amperage is constantly changing as the machine pulses. MIG brazing electrode wire is softer than steel electrodes, but not as soft as aluminum electrodes. Dedicated drive rolls that are U-shaped are typically recommended over the V-shaped type. Using these recommendations can help reduce cross contamination and limit birdnesting of the electrode wire. Moveable Glass Removal and Installation -Moveable Glass – moveable glass may include door glass, quarter glass, and sunroofs. Moveable back glass is also available on some sport-utility vehicles (SUVs) and pickup trucks. Moveable glass may be laminated, but is typically tempered. Tempered glass will shatter upon impact, limiting repair options to minor surface scratches. Minor cracks in laminated door glass may be repairable if the damage is contained to the outer laminate. (Laminated on the left, Tempered on the right) -Glass Inspection – inspect movable glass for: -Cracks -Deep pits -Chipped edges -Scratches -Damage to the interior side caused by mass inertia -Signs of improper previous installation -* Disasembly may be required to completely inspect movable glass. This is especially true if the door glass was lowered, or open, during the collision. If damage restricts the operation of the window regulator, further disassembly will likely be needed, and may include the removal of the glass to completely inspect movable glass for damage. If the glass is broken, cover the area to prevent moisture from damaging the vehicle interior. *Damage that can be felt with your fingernail is typically not repairable. -Tint Identification – it is important to identify the correct window tint for the replacement glass. Coding information, or markings, located on the bottom of the glass may indiciate the type of tint. If that is not availabe, you may place a white sheet of paper behind the glass on the undamaged side to help identify the level or color of tint. A seam line near the top portion of the glass may identify aftermarket tint. The percent of tint, as well as the contact information of the facility that applied the tint originally, may be necessary information in order to complete the repair. -Glass cleanup – Thoroughly remove shatterd glass to ensure occupant safely and proper operation of mechanical parts. Pieces of glass may fall into heater vents. Turn the blower motor on high, with the selector in the A/C or Vent position, while vacuuming to clear the vents of any glass fragments. Glass pieces may also fall into areas that can restrict the movement of mechanical parts. Clean the glass run channels, seat tracks, and seat belt retractors. Also, remove glass that has fallen between the door instrusion beam and the outer door panel. -Removing Door Glass – some front and rear door glass is attached using ball and socket joints into sliding clips. In some cases, door glass may be removed by prying off the spring clips with a screw driver and releasing the socket hold on the glass. In other cases, it may be removed by pulling the ball joints out of the sockets and lifting the glass out from the top of the door. Other door glass may be bolted to the window regulator. Bolts will need to be removed before the door glass can be removed. -Fastener Considerations – when installing glass fasteners that hold movable glass and hardware, there are several fastener considerations: -Torque – when installing glass fasteners that hold movable glass and hardware, ensure the proper torque specification and tightening sequences for multiple fasteners are observed. Torque specifications can be found in original equipment manufacturer (OEM) service information -Compounds, Sealers, or Lubricants – only use locking compounds, sealers, or lubricants on fasteners where recommended by the vehicle maker. Using lubricants where not recommended can affect torque readings and lead to improper torque. Failure to use locking compounds where called for may result in the loosening of fasteners due to vibration and use. -Bolted Glass Fastener – there are many considerations for removal and replacement of door glass that is fastened to the regulator with bolts or nuts. Retaining nuts that hold the glass to the regulator may be present. These nuts may have a special design with a large, thin, flat head. Special tools are required to remove these nuts. Plastic protective washers may be located between the glass and the fasteners. -Sunroof Glass – roof panels may be equipped with a glass panel called a sunroof or a movable panoramic roof. Panoramic roofs are wider than sunroofs and extend closer to the sides of the vehicle for more view or light. Sunroofs may be designed to fully open by sliding the glass backwards. Certain considerations must be taken for inspection and replacement of sunroof and panoramic glass. -Sunroof Inspection – prior to starting the repair, sunroofs should be inspected for -Proper operation through the whoile range of movement -Misaligned or damaged guide rails -Damaged weatherstripping -Damaged or missing clips or other parts, such as switches, cables, or gears -Damage to the air deflector -Pinched drain tubes -* Plan to replace the glass if it is damaged. Determine if the mounting parts will be replaced or reused -Sunroof Assembly Replacement Considerations – when preparing to replace a sunroof or movable panoramic roof assemblies there are many things to consider. Consider what parts require replacement and which parts can be ordered seperatly. For example, only the glass may require replacement, while the frame and assembly are unharmed. Determine whether the parts are bolted or bonded, as the glass may be bolted to the frame and the frame may be bolted or bonded to the roof. Consider which, if any, adjacent parts will need to be removed. The headliner may require removal or repositioning. It may also be recommended to remove an adjacent panoramic roof when the sunroof assembly requires replacement. Vehicle maker procedures must be followed to ensure the roof sunroof is installed properly and to prevent damage to adjacent parts. * An initialization procedure may be required when replacing sunroof or panoramic roof parts. The use of a scan tool may be required. Consult OEM service information for the exact procedure and tools required. -Sliding Sunroofs – sliding sunroofs are typically power-driven, cable-operated, for the glass panel movement and fature a modular design. Factory-installed sunroof installations usually have the glass panel slide back between the roof outer skin and the interior headliner, although there may be exceptions. In many cases, when it is necessary to remove and install a complete sunroof assembly, such as for roof skin replacement, the sunroof may be removed and installed as a modular assembly, requiring little disassembly of the sunroof parts. -Removal – when working with the sliding sunroof assemblies, where possible, remove and install as a complete assembly. This will ensure that the alignment and cable timing is not disturbed. Use caution to avoid damaging the interior trim parts, such as the headliner. When removing the sliding sunroof, consider that side curtain airbags may be located between the roof structure and the headliner. Ensure the airbag systems are disabled when working around side airbags. * Follow the vehicle maker’s procedures at all times. OEM service information typically has information on the replacement and service of sunroof assemblies, including procedures for disabling the airbag system -Replacement – replacing the sliding sunroof may include -Removing the headliner, connectors, and drainage tubes -Removing bolts and spacers around the perimeter of the sunroof -Dropping the sunroof down from opening and then lifting out of the top -Adjusting the replacement sunroof after installation -Calibrating and/or initalizing the sunroof, if required. -* Bolts and spacers have various thicknesses and lenghts. When removing, note their position for reinstallation. Nissan GT-R -GT-R Heritage – -Nissan Skyline GT-R – High performance versions of the Skyline coupe -The current GT-R is a stand-alone model. Available in the North American market since 2008, as a 2009 model. -Model Availability – as of 2018, the Nissan GT-R is available in four models. -Pure -Premium -Track Edition – available with the 2017 model year -NISMO – introduced in model year 2015 -Vehicle Design and Construction – -Mixed material construction – -High-strength steels (HSS) -Aluminum -Plastics -Carbon fiber -Follow best practices – -Avoid cross-contamination -Ensure personal safety -Ensure vehicle protection -Ensure quality repair -Steel – all steel over 370 MPa is high-strength steel (HSS) -Steel that is 980-1350 MPa is considered ultra-high strength steel (UHSS) -All GT-R steel parts are galvannealed -Aluminum- - Aluminum parts may be stamped, extruded, or cast. -Stamped aluminum parts on the GT-R -Hood -Deck lid -Door panels -The aluminum bumper assemblies are extruded -Aluminum castings -Front strut towers -Door shells -Rear seatback support -Plastics and Carbon Fiber – front and rear bumper covers are polypropylene. Door mirro housings and the rear spoiler are ABS plastic. Carbon fiber parts are the valence at the bottom of the bumper fascia and side rocker panels or sills. All models have a carbon fiber radiator core support. The NISMO edition has more parts made of carbon fiber. -Undercovers and Diffusers – undercovers are on the front underside. Diffusers are on the rear underside. The three undercovers are polypropylene. The front diffuser is also made of polypropylene, while the rear diffuser is made of carbon fiber. Inspect undercovers and diffusers for damage following a collision. -Ground Clearance – low ground clearance. Tapered ramps allow clearance for jacking and lifting. -Foam – two types of foam used in the structure -Flexible NVH foam -Carrier foam -Carrier foam -High-stiffness forming material -Highly rigid foaming agent two types of foam include flexible foam (left) in the A-pillar and carrier foam (right) in the quarter panel. -Attachment Methods – identifying the joining process is important to provide an accurate repair. Body has many factory lap joints. No conventional outer uniside – body side construction done with separate parts. -Material Identification – service information. -Material identification -Repair or replace decisions -Limits of repairability -Note: Identification determines removal and attachment tools, equipement, or techniques. -Body Center Marks – body center marks are small idents along the body centerline, such as in the windshield opening. -Repair Network Overview – shops must be in the Nissan GT-R Certified Collision Repair Network to work on the vehicle. -Must be inspected annually -Shop must meet certain requirements -Training – Nissan and I-CAR -Parts -Equipment -Aluminum capable -Note: to locate a Nissan GT-R Certified Collision Center, us e the search option at collision.nissanusa.com/gt-r -Certified Shop Requirements – Genuine Nissan parts and Nissan repair procedures. This helps ensure: -Safety systems are operational -Original appearance and performance are restored -Factory warranty is protected -Brand expectations are met -Note: one of Nissan’s repair requirements for the GT-R is mounting the vehicle on a Celette fixture bench -Assessment of Damage – refer to the Structural Damage Grade Chart for assessment of damage. Grade A and B repairs must be done at Nissan GT-R Certified Collision Repair network facilities. -Category grade A are structural repairs. -Category grade B are non-structural repairs. -GR-R Parts Considerations – Nissan does restrict the sale of certain GT-R parts to their network of Dealers. Shops requesting affected GT-R parts will be asked to provide additional documentation on repairs before parts will be released. Only GT-R repairs done at Nissan GT-R Certified Collision Repair Network shops are acceptable by Nissan. -Nissan Position Statements – Nissan Position Statements call out the position of the manufacturer regarding certrain repair procedures or practices. For ultimate repair guidance, reference Nissan’s technical service information. The most up-to-date Nissan position statements can be accessed free of charge from the collision.nissanusa.com website. May also be accessed on the I-CAR.com website under the Technical Knowledge and OEM information tabs. Panel Removal and Preparation for Installation 1/3 -Three commonly used welding machines are -Squeeze-type resistance spot welding (STRSW) -STRSW welding uses a machine with two arms to apply pressure and electric current to the welding location. -Gas Metal Arc (GMA) -GMA welding process, an electrode filler wire is fed continuously into the weld puddle while the puddle is protected by shielding gas -Metal inert gas (MIG) brazing -MIG brazing is a type of GMA welding that uses a silicon bronze filler wire. -Hem Flange Removal -one method for removing the door skin includes the following steps -Removing the edge of the hem flange using a disc grinder -Grinding until separation between the outer panel and the door shell is visible -Removing the spot welds around the window frame (if equipped) -Separating the bonded flange (if bonded) using heat (not to exceed 400 degrees F) -Removing the remaining strip of flange and the door panel with a hammer and chisel (if necessary) * Some panels may require an access hole cut -Spot Weld Removal -Squeeze-Type resistance spot welding (STRSW), sometimes called spot welding, is one of the most common part attachment methods for steel panels and may also be used by the vehicle maker on aluminum panels; however, this is not common. Removing spot welded parts begins by first locating, then removing, the vehicle maker original spot welds. Depending on the tool, a center punch may be useful to prevent the drill bit from wandering. When removing spot welds, make sure not to drill through the inner panel flange if welding is to be performed on the outside of the panel. Spot weld removal tools are sensitive to heat and require cooling between cuts. Make sure the tools are properly sharpened or replaced when dull. -Identifying Spot Weld Locations -Use OEM procedures to see the illustration -Remove seam sealer and coatings at suspected joint areas -Look near panel edges and on areas where there are overlapping pieces. -Spot Weld Removal Considerations – removing spot welds is a misleading term, in that with spot welds: -There is no nugget to remove. No metal was added to the joint -The metal between the electrode tips was made molten by the heat formed from the high resistance. After the current shut off, the metal solidified, forming the weld -A hole through both panels will eliminate the weld site -A hole through the outer piece or pieces, or ground down to the bottom piece, will allow the outer piece or pieces to be pried off -The pieces can be separated with a chisel -Spot Weld Removal Choice Factors – the method of removing the spot welds and the tool selected to remove a particular set of spot welds: -Access to the joint, as some tools will not fit where the spot welds must be removed -Whether the panel will be reused, as some methods make the part unusable, such as when using a grinder -The type of replacement attachment method: the most common is Squeeze-Type resistance spot welding (STRSW), but other welding and/or riveting may be required depending on the procedure, which may or may not require holes in the flanges. -Spot Weld Removal Choice Factors, cont’d -If repair spot welds will be used, there should be no holes left on the inner or base panel. Other methods, like drilling all the way through both panels (commonly done when using two twist drill bits), require plug welds be made from the backside. * Drilling through both panels eliminates the need to punch plug weld holes into the replacement panel. Make sure there is access on the opposite side to weld the panel. -Removing Spot Welds from Boron-Alloyed Steel -Some parts are made of boron-alloyed steel, a type of ultra-high-strength steel that is difficult to drill. When removing these parts using a drill, it helps to drill the spot welds from the opposite side, if the steel is a lower-strength steel. In many cases, an air chisel can be used to separate the mild steel panel from ultra-high strength steel by working the chisel between the two metals and breaking the spot weld free. Caution must be taken to prevent damaging the boron-alloyed steel reinforcement. If damaged, the reinforcement will need to be replaced. Boron-alloyed steel or any ultra-high strength steel cannot be repaired. -Panel Removal Tools –When removing a panel it may be beneficial to cut away a large portion of the panel being replaced to assist in the panel removal process. This may provide greater access to the flange areas. Doing this ensures that no damage is caused to underlying parts. After removing the spot welds, a hammer and chisel can be used to separate the two panels. -Bonded Panel Removal using Heat – vehicle makers may use adhesive on some parts, but more commonly, adhesive is used in conjunction with other attachment methods; therefore, heat may be required to help with part removal. Follow vehicle maker recommendations on heating temperature limits. Most adhesives will release at 400 degrees F or less. * An induction heater may be used to loosen some adhesive bonded parts, however care must be taken to avoid overheating adjacent parts. -Bonded Panel Removal – Mechanical – while not as common, some panel installations may use an adhesive bead similar to windshield urethane to attach the part. Mechanical cutting tools may be used to cut the adhesive bead. To remove a bonded panel using mechanical cutters: -Determine if a layer of existing adhesive needs to be left intact -Remove any mechanical fasteners -Use a piano wire or pneumatic knife to cut the adhesive -Remove loose urethane adhesive when done -Trim the remaining urethane adhesive to remove any high spots. High spots may result in the uneven application of replacement adhesives -* The type of flange design may dictate the type of method used to separate the adhesive bond. For example, a roof structure may prevent a pneumatic knife from accessing the adhesive. Therefore, piano wire may be a better tool for cutting the urethane adhesive -Hem Flange Removal – when removing an adhesively bonded panel along the hemmed area: -Grind the outer edge of the panel using an angle grinder or other hem flange removal tool to release the hem -Insert a panel separating tool along the edge to separate the panels -Separate the panels by applying heat to soften the adhesive if required -* If grinding, a 36-50 grit disc may be used. Do not remove material from the supporting panel -Laser Welds – several vehicle makers use laser welds instead of spot welds for attaching some parts. Laser welds: -Have a low-heat affect, are much longer than spot welds, and access to only one side is needed -Look similar to a skinny GMA weld with the bead concave rather than convex -May be continuous across the flange or finished in shorter intervals -May use a silicon bronze filler metal, in the case of laser brazing -* Examples of laser welds are on tailor-welded joints, which combine different thicknesses or strengths of steel along the length of the same panel -Removing Laser Welds – -The laser weld removal tool is designed specifically for allowing depth control -A die grinder tool can be used, similar to grinding off the top of a spot weld site. Care must be taken to avoid grinding into the underlying part -The panel must still be pried off once the weld is removed -* Laser welds cannot be duplicated and are usually replaced with spot welds, GMA plug welds, or adhesive -Removing a MIG Braze Slot Weld -Vehicle makers may use MIG brazing in slots in certain locations where it is desired to reduce heat input to preserve metal strength and lessen damage to coatings, and may be used on ultra-high strength steel (UHSS) parts where spot welds do not reach. Silicon bronze is typically the filler material used. Removing a MIG braze slot requires careful grinding of the silicon bronze filler and usually requires grinding away some of the outer panel around the slot due to capillary action of the filler, placing it between the panels as well. Care must be taken to avoid removing material from underlying panels -Riveted Exterior Panels – Riveted panels are most common on vehicles with aluminum parts, but may be used on steel and other construction materials. Adhesives are typically used in conjunction with the rivets; this is commonly referred to as rivet bonding, to increase joint strength. Before removing rivets, it is important to identify the replacement attachment method, as this may help inform as to the best removal method. Rivet (rivet bonded) panels usually require rivet bonding during replacement; however, some part replacement procedures may specify welding. Some attachment methods require holes in one or both panels, while others do not. -Types of Rivets -Solid rivets, known as straight, conventional, or solid shank rivets -Identified by the shape of the head, material (steel or aluminum), and the diameter and length of the shank -Identified by the vehicle maker for use in specific location -Installed with a bucktail on the backside -Used in place of an SPR or factory weld location, if specified by the vehicle maker -* Solid rivets may have a protruding head, such as a pan head or button head, or be countersunk. Countersunk is also referred to as flush mounted. The bucktail may also be called the shop head, upset head, driven, or bucked head. -Solid Rivet Removal -Grinding -Grind off the head or bucktail (whichever is exposed) of the rivet, and tap out the body using a punch that is one size smaller than the rivet body -Avoid grinding into the panel if it is to remain in service -Drilling -Center-punch the head of the rivet in preparation for drilling -Select a drill bit one size smaller than the head of the rivet -Drill through the center of the rivet to remove the head only -Tap out the rivet using care to not damage the area where the rivet was installed -Do not oversize the hole or stretch the metal during the removal process -* Use caution not to drill into the panel -Self-piercing rivets (SPR) -Self-piercing rivets (SPRs) are made of tin/zinc coated steel. SPRs are most commonly used on vehicles with aluminum construction, but may also be found on steel parts in some situations. Adhesives are usually used with SPRs -SPR Removal -There are four ways to remove SPRS, including pressing, weld-on stud, drilling, and grinding -Pressing – SPRs may be removed by pressing them out using a special tool. When an SPR is removed, a hole is made in the panels as the rivet is removed. The metal may be distorted after pressing the SPR out, so straightening the flange may be necessary. Follow vehicle maker procedures. If holes remain, they will be filled with adhesive upon installation of the replacement panel. -Weld-On Stud- A weld-on stud can be used for removing an SPR. The steel pulling stud is attached to the head of the rivet using special equipment, and then the SPR is removed using a slide hammer or pneumatic rivet gun with special tip adapters. -Drilling – An SPR can also be removed by drilling through it from the backside of the rivet. The drill bit remains centered by the SPR. Although it is not common, when drilling from the front side, center-punching the rivet head will help keep the drill bit centered. SPRs are usually very hard steel. Drill at low speed to preserve the bit cutting edge -Grinding – Grinding is performed from the backside of the SPR. Analyze the risks of grinding the steel rivet on an aluminum part. Grinding too much will weaken the metal. Some vehicle makers recommend not grinding SPRs due to potential of galvanic corrosion. -* For vehicle protection, remove self-piercing rivets, shavings or grinding particles that drop inside of the vehicle. Use a magnet or a sparkless vacuum to retrieve the material. If the material is not removed, the rivets could rattle and make noise, or cause galvanic corrosion. -Blind rivets -A blind rivet does not require backside access for installation. Specialty blind rivets may be recommended. These types of rivets may: -Be all aluminum or steel. -Require a special blind rivet gun to set the rivets -* Blind rivets commonly have a protruding head; however, there are flush rivets specified in some procedures. Blind rivets applications may include structural areas as well as cosmetic panels, and usually require adhesive in the joint area. -Clinches Introduction – Clinching is a vehicle maker press and die process for joining nonstructural panels. The press and die process uses the panel as its own fastener. Clinching locks the panel together and does not make a hole in the panel. * Clinches are not duplicated in a repair facility. -Clinch Locations – Locations of clinched aluminum exterior panels include -Inner and outer hood panels. The inner and outer panels are clinched by the vehicle maker and replaced as an assembly -Areas of the outer quarter panel to the structure of the vehicle -Areas of the door skin to the door shell -Inner fender reinforcements to the fender -Cowl-to-plenum area -Deck lid inner panels -Clinch Removal and Replacement -During repairs, clinches are usually replaced with rivet bonding using solid or blind rivets at locations identified in the vehicle service information. Clinches can be removed by drilling through the clinch, or by pressing the clinch flat with dies on a pressing tool. If drilling, a bit sized for the replacement rivet that will be used to reassemble the panels can be used. Panel Removal and Preparation for Installation 2/3 -Preparing a mating flange after outer panel removal requires -Cleaning the flange so it is free of corrosion and/or adhesive -Straightening mating panel flanges -Applying corrosion protection to bare steel areas along the flange as required by vehicle maker installation method. -* While cleaning the flange, try to avoid removing the zinc coating. A plastic-coated abrasive wheel may be used to clean a flange of remaining adhesive. Avoid grinding unless necessary. Exercise caution to avoid thinning the attaching surface. * The corrosion protection type will vary based on the method of attachment. After applying corrosion protection, it may be beneficial to remove primer/E-coat from the weld zones to improve weld quality. -Panel Preparation on Replacement Part – Similar to flange preparation, preparing the replacement panel also requires: -Straightening mating flange deformations using a hammer and dolly -Removing primer from the weld zone -Cleaning the mating flange -Applying corrosion protection to bare steel areas -Vehicle Maker Panel Alignment Specifications – Some vehicle makers provide panel fit standards to prevent excessive wind noise and water leaks, and to ensure a uniform gap between panels. Common panel gaps are between 3-8mm for most vehicles. If no specifications exist from the vehicle maker, make sure that the gaps are even around the entire panel or correspond with the panel gaps on the opposite side of the vehicle. Check panel fit with the suspension loaded, as the body may flex with the suspension unloaded. Adjusting the panels while the vehicle is flexed changes the panel gaps. It is also possible that weld-on panels will dent or buckle once the suspension is loaded. -Test Fitting a panel – Locking pliers, clamps, or other fasteners temporarily hold the panel in place while test fitting. -Weld Attachment Techniques – There are several welding techniques used for panel attachment. The three common types of panel attachment methods are: -Squeeze-type resistance spot welding (STRSW) -When using STRSW to attach panels, follow the vehicle maker flange preparation procedures for mating flanges. Coatings should be removed from the spot weld area where the tips will contact bare steel. -GMA plug welding -GMA plug welding requires: Drilling or punching holes, Identifying the number and location of the plug welds, A 5/16 inch (8 mm) hole, it no recommendations exist. Some vehicle makers provide recommendations for the number, locations, and size of the plug welds required to attach a panel. Also, check the flange size. If the hole is too big for the flange width, a smaller plug weld hole is required. -MIG brazing, silicon bronze -Replacing panels at factory seams may include locating silicon bronze joints on the panel; this material may look like brass. Use the GMA process (also known as MIG brazing) to apply silicon bronze. MIG brazing produces a small heat affect zone. There are different alloys of electrode wire for this process; therefore, it is important to reference service information for the correct selection. MIG brazing should not be confused with oxyacetylene torch brazing, which is not commonly recommended due to the large heat affect zone. It is important to note that neither process is designed to fuse the joint, but forms an adhesive bond with the base metal. -Hem Flange Installation – When installing a panel using a hem flange -Apply adhesive to the panel where the hem flange will be made and to both sides of the flange to seal out moisture and contaminants -Use a hammer and dolly or a hem flange tool to make the hem flange -Remove excessive adhesive from the panels -* If replacing a door panel, apply adhesive to both sides of the doorframe flange that will be attached to the door panel -General Rivet Bonding – Typically, adhesives are used when riveting panels. This is called rivet bonding. Adhesives provide good shear strength and rivets have good peel strength. When rivet bonding, it is important to follow vehicle maker and product maker recommendations include: -Removing remaining adhesive, coatings, and surface contaminants -Straightening the flange -Making rivet holes -Countersinking for countersunk rivets -Dimpling, if using countersunk rivets on thinner aluminum -Removing burrs -* Some vehicle and product makers recommend the use of a silica stone and/or other special treatment to bare aluminum prior to applying adhesive -Adhesive Bonding Joints – The vehicle maker will usually specify the type of adhesive for the repair being completed. Adhesive is to be applied following the adhesive maker’s recommendations unless the vehicle maker has specific unique requirements. Adhesives may require application over a bare metal flange, an E-coated or primed flange, or a layer of existing adhesive (generally for urethane applications only) -Solid Rivet Installation – Solid rivet installation requires access to both sides of the panel to compress the rivet. The rivet must be the proper size and alloy for each specific application. Always refer to the vehicle maker for specific rivet recommendations. Solid rivets require holes in both panels for installation. If a rivet was removed, use the existing hole in the inner panel to mark the locations for the hole in the replacement panel unless otherwise directed by the service information. A rivet installation hole is made using a punch or drill size specified by the vehicle maker for the specific rivet type. Flanges must be straight and remain free of burrs to prevent gaps between the panels and poor fit-up. Remove burrs using a deburring tool. For vehicle protection, do not use the rivet gun for panel fit up. Panels must be properly fit with the correct gaps before installing the rivets. -Countersunk Solid Rivet Installation – A countersunk solid rivet head is flush with the surface of the metal and uses a hole that is commonly countersunk to a 100 degree angle. A 100 degree countersunk rivet is the most common rivet, but some vehicle makers use other angles. The benefit of this rivet is that it can be used in the same hole where the SPR was removed. Installation requires a countersinking bit, particullary a travel stop countersink tool, to prevent damage to the panel. The countersunk solid rivet uses a dimpling tool to install the rivet in thin metal. This creates a depression for the countersunk rivet head without removing any metal and maintains panel integrity at the rivet locations. -Self-Piercing Rivet (SPR) Installation – Self-piercing rivets (SPRs) are installed by the vehicle maker. If a repair facility has the proper tools, SPRs can be installed during some vehicle repairs. Vehicle makers may have a specific tool requirement to install SPRs. SPR installation does not require a hole. During installation, the rivet pierces the panels and spreads outward (radially) through the panels. SPRs are one-time use fasteners and are never installed into an existing hole. SPRs may be different sizes depending on vehicle maker requirements. -Flange Preparation for Blind Rivets – Blind rivet installation requires: -Straightening damaged flanges -Test-fitting the panel -A hole in both panels being riveted together -Countersinking for countersunk rivets -Deburring of the exterior and mating surfaces -* The same tools used to countersink solid rivets can also be used for blind rivets. -Panel Installation/Sound-Deadeners – Types of sound-deadening materials include foam pads, foam blocks, foam filler, and anti-flutter material. Transfer sound-deadening materials from the damaged panel to the replacement panel, if possible, or install new sound-deadening material. Heat can be used to loosen sound-deadening materials from the panel, do not use an open flame. In some situations, it may be required to install the replacement sound-deadener or anti-flutter material before installation due to location/access restrictions. Panel Removal and Preparation for Installation 3/3 -Roof panel Replacement Considerations – When removing a roof panel it will be necessary to identify where and how it is attached to the vehicle structure. The roof may have flanges that sit on top of underlying parts, as is common on the front and back flanges, typically concealed by the windshield and back glass. It may have areas of the flange that are sandwiched between other panels, as may be found on some upper outer body side panel to roof joints, or there may be laser welds or MIG braze filets joining some areas, in addition to adhesives and welds. Some vehicles may require additional panels to be replaced or section when replacing the complete roof panel. -Removing Adjacent Parts – Additional items that must be removed or disassembled prior to roof panel replacement include: -Sunroofs (if equipped) -Electrical accessories -Moldings -Drain tubes -Airbags or side airbag curtains (if equipped) -Interior parts such as seats and carpeting -Headliner -Doors -Heating/cooling tunnels -Windshield and back glass -Sunroof drain panels -Antennas -Luggage racks -* Remove all electrical parts. Electrical parts may include dome lamps, stereo control, heat or air conditioning controls, etc. Glass removal is generally required for roof panel installation. Typically, the windshield and back glass are removed. Some designs, however, allow the glass to remain installed during roof panel replacement. -Anti-Flutter Material – Vehicle makers place anti-flutter materials between the roof bows and the roof panel. Anti-flutter materials prevent the roof panel from moving up and down due to air movement or pressure. Sound-deadeners are also placed on the panel to reduce outside noise. -Working with Roof Bows – Roof bows provide support for the roof. The number of roof bows varies from vehicle to vehicle. Steel roof bows are typically welded into place when replaced. Damaged roof bows need to be repaired or replaced depending on the severity of damage, construction material and type of construction. A roof bow has an adhesive and/or anti-flutter material between it and the roof panel. Heat may be required to loosen during panel removal, along with a utility knife, scraper, piano wire, or pneumatic cutting tool. -Sequence of Multiple Panels – Roofs may be connected at the A-pillar, B-pillar, and/or the sail panel area. The joint design could have multiple layers. If replacing multiple panels, it is important to determine the proper replacement sequence. To do this, determine where the roof panel is placed in relation to the pillars and sail panel – between, on top, or beneath. -Weld-Bonded Roof Panel Removal Introduction – while the steps of removing a roof panel depend on the exact make, model, and year of the vehicle, here are some of the typical removal steps for a vehicle with weld bonding and laser braze attachment from the factory. -Removing Panels – when removing a roof panel, make sure adjacent panels and the vehicle structure are in alignment before removing the roof panel. Some vehicle makers recommend cutting a large section from the center of the roof panel for access. Do not cut into roof bows or other underlying structure. Continue the removal process by separating the welds along the roof edges. -Performing the Removal – identify spot weld locations and remove remaining spot welds. Release any adhesive and anti-flutter material using heat from a no-flame heat source. Remove the roof panel and straighten the vehicle flanges -Completing the Removal – if the roof was attached with laser welds or laser brazing, it usually requires removal of any of the remaining roof flange. Remove the flange by grinding off or by grasping with a pliers and moving back and forth until it breaks free of the weld. Exercise caution that metal from the damaged part is breaking free of the weld and that the underlying panel is not being damaged. Repeat this process for the entire length of the flange. Grind off remaining weld. Use caution when grinding to remove only the weld material, do not grind into the base metal. -Weld-Bonded Roof Panel Installation – in preparation for weld-bonding installation, clean the headers to bare metal. Remove the coatings only where the spot weld locations have been marked. Some of the tasks required to install weld-bonded roof panels may include: -Perform a test-fit of the roof panel -Clean the mating surface on the vehicle headers and replacement part -Remove coatings from the replacement panel -Determine if the panel should be welded using spot welds or GMA plug welds -Drill plug weld holes, if required -Position the roof panel on the vehicle -Check the fit and gaps -Make test welds -Apply adhesive -Weld the panel in place -Remove the squeezed-out adhesive -Removing and Installing Bonded-On Roof Panels Introduction – The steps for removing and installing bonded-on roof panels depend on the roof design and the adhesive bonding used to bind the vehicle parts together. Unlike welding, adhesives distribute stress uniformly and form a moisture barrier that minimizes the chance of corrosion. While the process for removing and installing bonded-on roof panels varies by vehicle, there are some common steps that typically apply. -Bonded-On Roof Panel Removal – when removing a roof panel that is bonded on with urethane: -Cut an access hole, if necessary -Unscrew the fasteners (if equipped) -Use piano wire or a pneumatic knife to cut adhesive -Remove the roof panel -* For vehicle protection, do not gouge the flanges or roof bows with the scraper, and replaced all damaged corrosion protection. Note: some procedures require leaving a bed of the original adhesive on the vehicle structure to act as a bed for the replacement adhesive. Only remove adhesive if required by a procedure or due to damage. -Bonded-On Roof Panel Installation – in preparation for bonded-on roof panel installation: -Clean the bonding area on the roof panel and the vehicle -Apply corrosion protection where necessary -Prepare the bonding area per the adhesive maker’s instructions -Trim the remaining adhesive to ensure a smooth surface for the replacement adhesive -Apply the adhesive -Place the panel in position -Clamp in place or install fasteners or welds as required by the vehicle maker -* Note: Some urethane products are applied to a freshly trimmed bed of the remaining original adhesive if it is in good condition. -Completing the Repair - after replacement of any panel complete the repair by: -Dressing the welds, if required -Removing adhesive squeeze-out (if bonded) -Applying corrosion-resistant primer, seam sealers, etc., as required to duplicate the appearance -Refinishing as required -Applying corrosion protection -Reinstalling all trim, wiring, glass, etc., that were removed -Ensuring calibrations are performed (if required) on lane departure cameras and other affected systems -Performing initialization steps on other features such as sunroofs, if required. Part Replacement at Factory Seams (1/2) Preparation -Repair Planning (When to Completely Replace a Part) – replacement of a damaged part at factory seams (complete part replacement) is required when specified by the vehicle maker if straightening is not possible or permitted. This may be for design reasons or to maintain structural integrity. Some structural parts are required to be replaced only in their entirety -Damage – complete part replacement is also required when the damage warrants. For example, there may be a sectioning procedure on a front lower rail, but if the damage extends beyond the designated cut line, the entire rail must be installed. Sectioning should only be done following the vehicle maker recommendations for the specific vehicle make, model, and year -Access/Design – complete part replacement is required when its practical due to access or part design. For example, it may be impractical to replace only part of a radiator core support when it is just a one-piece tie bar connecting the two front upper rails -No Recommendations – complete part replacement is also required when there are no published part replacement or sectioning procedures. With the design of today’s vehicles, it is no longer practical to section a part where it looks convenient and safe to do so. Hidden reinforcements, varied strengths of steel, and other factors known only to the body engineer make this no longer an option -Vehicle Build Sequence/Layering – the vehicle maker may require complete part replacement or removal because of complications due to the vehicle build sequence, for example: -The layering of one part over another may make it necessary to remove a complete undamaged part to access the damaged part -A B-pillar reinforcement that overlaps the rocker panel reinforcement -An apron/strut tower that is layered over an upper or lower front rail, making it difficult to remove the rail without removing the apron/strut tower -Part Removal Planning – when planning part removal, the installation method needs to be considered so the appropriate removal method is used. For example, if spot welding will be used to install the replacement part, then holes should not be drilled through the flanges. Instead, the original welds should be drilled or ground only through the panel being removed, leaving the underlying panels intact at the original weld locations -Service Information – information on removal and installation of complete parts may be limited for some manufacturers, consisting of only a statement to replace the part at factory seams. Illustrations in the manuals may show welds, but they may not show enough detail to identify the number and location of the welds, whereas other vehicle makers may have detailed illustrations that often show the number and locations of welds, rivets, adhesives, etc., to be removed and replaced. The illustrations may also show panel layering and reinforcement locations, often with dotted lines to show these hidden features -Common Parts Completely Replaced – parts of the structure that are commonly replaced at factory seams, rather than repaired or partially replaced, include: -Pillar reinforcements, usually made of UHSS -Rocker panel reinforcements, also usually made of UHSS -UHSS roof rails, usually from the upper A-pillar to just behind the B-pillar -Engine cradles, as they are difficult to return to proper alignment, and can cause drivability or steering and suspension problems if not properly aligned. Also, there are typically no alignment specifications available for engine cradles. -Note: UHSS is being used in more locations to strengthen the passenger cabin and increase occupant protection -Installing Part of an Assembly – using part of an assembly: -Is typically a less intrusive repair, as fewer parts need to be removed or temporarily moved aside -Disturbs fewer original welds -Disturbs less original corrosion protection -Will likely require less refinishing -May improve cycle time -Vehicle Maker-Approved Partial Assembly – the vehicle maker may have a procedure for using part of an assembly, and procedures for disassembling the assembly may or may not be shown. If the repair information shows only a portion of the assembly being used, then it is permissible to separate the identified part from the service part assembly -Adjacent Panel Removal – parts often need to be removed, either for access to damaged parts or for protection when adjacent parts cannot be protected with covers, etc. -Part Removal for Access/Protection – in general, the more complete the replacement, the more adjacent parts must be removed. Radiator core support replacement, for example, requires removal of the radiator, air conditioning condenser, etc. Rear rail replacement, for example, may require removal of the rear body panel, trunk floor, fuel tank, rear suspension and wheels, and exhaust parts -Part Removal for Uniside Replacement – unisides are the one-piece outer panel with front and rear door openings that may include the quarter panel. Part removal for access or protection: -May require roof removal. This is because the uniside attachment to the roof rail may be under the roof panel and cannot be accessed -May require windshield and backglass removal -Will require interior trim removal -Note: Due to the complexity of complete uniside replacement, most vehicle makers allow partial replacement at sectioning locations -Vehicle Detrim – detrimming of the vehicle for part replacement at factory seams will vary depending on the specific part being replaced. For a complete uniside replacement this can be extensive, and typically requires disabling restraint systems and removal of a variety of parts on the affected side of the vehicle including, but not limited to, some restraint system parts, door(s), fender, seat(s), dash, console, headliner, carpet (rollback may be sufficient), trim, and glass, as well as other necessary parts depending on the vehicle and options -Rerouting Wires – it is often necessary to move lines, wires, sensors, and modules out of the way during repairs for drilling, grinding, or welding. This includes: -Removing them if necessary, and/or using wire or twist ties to attach the lines or wiring to another location away from the immediate work area. -Unplugging wiring connectors -Note: Protect the harness and parts from welding sparks and refinishing overspray. Cover connectors and other parts with a plastic bag, welding blankets, or spark paper as necessary to keep contaminants out. Replace any clips and fasteners that are broken, and always put harnesses and parts back to their original locations after repairs -Accessing the Top of B-Pillar Reinforcements – because many damage B-pillar reinforcements have to be removed and replaced in their entirety, there is a question of how to access the top, since the top of the B-pillar reinforcement is under the upper uniside roof rail and may be under the roof panel. How do you access the spot welds on the top? Recommended options include: -Cutting a window out of the uniside roof rail and a portion of the roof, which is considered sectioning. This becoming the most popular option recommended by vehicle makers -Holding the roof temporarily out of the way to access the top welds -Removing the roof, which requires stationary glass removal and is highly labor intensive, and may require replacing the roof panel with a new part -Restore Vehicle Dimensions (Test Fit) – before installing a replacement part or pane, the vehicle dimensions must be restored. Then, the part is fit-up in place to ensure it aligns with the mating and adjacent parts and/or panels -Verifying Service Part – when verifying the service part, first determine if the part comes with or needs separate internal reinforcements. Then, mirror-match parts to ensure it’s the correct service part. While not common, a service part may be slightly different from the part being removed from the vehicle due to a design change, or the part may not have a formed or pierced area on it because it was not on a vehicle body to have that operation performed on the assembly line at the factory. If different, ensure that the supporting documentation is in the repair order file for the vehicle -Determining Weld Locations – when determining the location of repair, spot, or GMA plug welds, always reference the current vehicle maker service information / specifications. In general, spot welds or plug welds should be as close to the original number and location of spot welds as possible. Some vehicle makers provide options. Three representative examples follow: -Space spot or plug welds every 40 mm if original locations cannot be found -Space repair welds 25-30 mm apart, ignoring the original sites -Space welds 20 mm apart -Note: It is not our intent to identify the specific vehicle maker in this course, only reference examples to show there are different service specifications. Some procedures may require the use of rivets and or adhesives in certain locations -Replacing Welds on Scalloped Edges – some vehicle makers use scalloped edges on panels. This is for weight savings, as every little bit counts in making the body weigh less. With these scalloped edges: -The problem with repair is that there is not enough room on the flange to relocate the repair spot welds -The best practice may be to make the repair spot weld in the same spot -If the vehicle maker recommends against using the same spot for the repair welds, use GMA plug welds or other vehicle maker specified attachment method Preparation for Installation -Foam Fillers – noise, vibration, and harshness (NVH) foam fillers: -Are for noise control -May be installed after assembly by the vehicle maker, and commonly replaced by injecting foam into holes after the part is installed. Some foam may be installed before the part is installed -Should be replaced in the same amounts as was removed. This requires knowing how much the foam will expand, which is best determined by dispensing a sample in a container. -Note: Some foams expand ten-times their original size. Try to estimate exactly how much foam is needed for the cavity. It is common error to apply too much, which is not better -Personal Safety – wear a vapor respirator and nitrile gloves when working with cartridge-based foam fillers. Do not weld near exposed foam filler. Most foam filler is combustible and will begin to melt and/or burn if exposed to heat higher than about 575 degrees Fahrenheit. When cured foam filler begins to melt, it emits toxic chemicals, including carbon monoxide and cyanide gas. The foam filler will also melt when heat is applied during straightening or if studs are welded to be the part for pulling -Foam-Filled Areas – one consideration for working with foam filler is determining the type of foam, such as rigid or semi-rigid. The vehicle maker may not specify the type of foam. In this instance, the technician may have to make a determination based on the physical characteristics of the original material. Note: Structural foam may be used in some locations. Follow vehicle maker recommendations for replacement -Foam Preservation – it may be helpful to preserve foam fillers. If foam is inside the part, it may be beneficial to try and preserve the shape and integrity of the original foam material when separating panels or assemblies. In some instances, the foam piece can be reinstalled by bonding it back in place. Note: Some replacement parts come from the vehicle maker with foam filler installed -Foam Reinstallation – another consideration for working with foam filler includes reinstalling it if removed. If the foam filler is directly behind a welded joint, it is replaced following the welding process. This is done through an access hole. Foam filler may also be applied after corrosion protection is applied. Make sure the foam filler is removed from the weld zone before welding. Foam is flammable and will burn if ignited from the heat of the welding process -Determining What Is Inside – vehicle maker service information may identify additional internal baffles, support adhesives, or other materials that will require replacement following the service procedure. There are some vehicle makers that do not identify these materials, so the technician will need to evaluate and identify appropriate replacement materials during part replacement -Working With a Previously Replaced Part – working with a previously replaced part that was welded with GMA plug welds -Is usually more difficult than replacing an original part, because plug welds are more difficult to remove than spot welds. The weld metal is usually harder than the surrounding steel, and the welds are difficult to locate because they have been dressed. -May require grinding the edge and working inward to see where the plug welds are located -Is challenging because there is even less space than before for replacement welds -Note: Another way to locate the plug weld sites is to separate the flanges and look for an outline of the plug welds to appear -Replacing the Inner or Outer Rail Half – often, the inner and outer halves of a part are available as separate parts. An example is an inner and outer front lower rail, where: -The inner or outer half can be ordered and used separately when only one half needs replacing -If only the outer half is being replaced, it may not be necessary to remove the engine and transmission -Spot welds are removed from the side that will be replaced -Care is taken to not drill into reinforcements or the other half when removing the spot welds -Note: Replacing only one half may permit straightening otherwise inaccessible damage on the remaining half -Tubular Reinforcements – tubular reinforcements: -Are used in some upper A-pillars, B-pillars, roof rails, and rocker panels -Usually have high-strength tubing with brackets on each end -Are removed by removing welds or bolts in the brackets, and reattached with the same methods -Rail Crush Boxes – crush boxes on rails: -Are sacrificial, separate part extensions of the rail designed to collapse before transferring damage to the rail -May be bolted to the end of the front lower rails and rear rails for easy replacement -May be welded to the bumper reinforcement and replaced as an assembly with the bumper replacement -And may also be called crash boxes or crush caps -Complete Floor Pan – replacement of a complete floor pan: -Is difficult, since most vehicles are built starting with the floor pan -Is highly intrusive, requiring removal of most of the interior -Is installed from under, through the doors, or from the top -May require removal of the front and/or rear frame rails, uniside, roof, or all underbody parts -Requires sealing gaps from exhaust fumes and road spray moisture and chemicals, clearing drain plugs, and replacing floor pan coverings such as sound deadeners and insulating pads -Working with Floor Crossmembers – floor crossmembers: -May be on top or under the floor pan -Are usually separate parts, but may be part of the floor assembly -Are also likely damaged and require replacement is the floor pan is being replaced after a side collision -Separating an Assembly – even if a vehicle maker has a general position on disassembling an assembly, there are exceptions. Also, if the vehicle maker does not say anything, there are considerations for determining whether a part can be disassembled from an assembly -Considerations – considerations for determining whether a part can be disassembled from an assembly include: -Whether the part will be damaged due to layering or another reason that limits access to where it is attached -The type or strength of steels, as there may be special recommendations for UHSS -The presence of reinforcements, plastic inserts, or foam fillers -Whether the joining method can be duplicated -Avoiding Part Damage – this what you need to know to determine if a portion can be removed without damage -Is there a factory seam? If not, it is not even a possibility, as this would be sectioning without a procedure. This is no longer practical -If there are to be no holes, because the part will be attached using spot welding, is there access to the backside of the flanges -Could the unused part be cut away, if necessary, for access to the backside -Is there layering that would pose a problem for part removal without damage -Is the part attached with just spot welds? If there is a laser weld or a GMA weld, it may require too much grinding to separate -Stepped Plug Welds – making stepped plug welds when there are more than two thicknesses: -Is done where a spot welder cannot access both sides of the multiple panel location -Requires plug weld holes to be drilled or punched progressively larger, from the inner to the outermost layer. This helps assure proper penetration between the layers -For example, on a three-layered joint, the first hole on the middle piece should be based on the thickness of the steel, for example, 6 mm (1/4 inch) on 20 gauge material. The hole on the outermost piece is then one size larger – 8mm (3/8 inch) in this example -Note: Check vehicle maker service information for specific hole size recommendations -Dressing Welds – dressing welds gives the repair its finished appearance. When dressing welds: -Make sure it is done in visible areas -Do not grind to the point where the metal is thinner than the adjacent metal -Do not grind into the base metal, as this will weaken the joint -Note: Some vehicle makers may recommend using dye penetrant to check for cracks in the weld after dressing the weld Part Replacement at Factory Seams (2/2) Full Frame Preparation -Service Parts – some vehicle makers make full-frames with a number of serviceable parts. Replacement service parts may include crossmembers, body mounts, brackets, mounting flanges, and some side rails -Crush Boxes and Bumper Mounting Brackets – design features for collision energy management in full-frame construction include: -Bolt-on crush boxes that are designed to be replaced in low-speed front impacts -Bumper mounting brackets, which are often available as separate service parts -Note: These features absorb collision energy, similar to the front portion of a unibody structure -Front/Rear Module Frame Tip and Assemblies – if a damaged part cannot be repaired, the decision must be made for either full or partial replacement. Vehicle maker recommendations and part availability will also affect this decision. Many vehicle makers have front frame kits available for collision repairs. Rear frame kits may also be available. Some of the options include: -Varied partial replacement locations depending on the service parts provided -Frame tips or ends, usually in front of a crossmember. The front-end service part may include a crush zone and body mount -Front or rear assemblies that include not only one, but both front rail ends and a crossmember -Body Mount Replacement Considerations – when determining replacement options for damaged frame mounting areas, verify if the vehicle maker provides a service part. If the part is not available as a separate service part, replacement of a major part of the frame, if not the entire frame, may be the only option. Replacement procedures for frame mounting areas and crossmembers are rare. If there is no replacement procedure, duplicate the original factory attachment method -Accessing Area – depending on the part being replaced, it may require lifting the body slightly for access, or complete removal of the body to complete the procedure. There may be mechanical parts, lines, hoses, wire harnesses, heat shields, or other parts that require removal for access to complete the repair -Frame Thickness – frames are typically thicker than most unibody parts. For example, exterior unibody panels typically measure around 0.8 mm thick. Unibody frame rails and reinforcements can measure up to 1.75 mm thick. Full-frame rails on light-duty pickup trucks and SUVs commonly measure 3-4 mm thick, and can be much thicker on heavy-duty trucks. If not specified by the vehicle maker, it is a good practice to measure the thickness of the part to be welded to ensure the proper welding equipment and settings are used -Beveling Edges of Joints – to prepare the welding area, bevel the joint areas if recommended. Typical bevel angles are between 25 to 45 degrees. A bevel may be recommended for both the existing and replacement part, or just the existing part. A root gap may or may not be recommended. Leaving a root gap and beveling are designed to ensure penetration into the backside or the insert when welding thick steel Frame Welding Considerations -Welding Equipment for Frames – it is important to make sure that you have the correct and necessary equipment and materials capable of welding full-frames -Welding Wire – procedures may specify ER70S-3 electrode wire for frame repairs instead of ER70S-6; both have a tensile strength of 70,000 psi, or 382 megapascals (MPa). The difference is the “3”, which indicates the chemical makeup of the wire. The molten puddle solidifies quicker than ER70S-6, which is good for vertical and overhead welds. ER70S-3 has fewer deoxidizers than ER70S-6, resulting in a bead that is not as smooth as ER70S-6, but this is usually not an issue on frame parts. If the vehicle maker does not specific an electrode wire, either can be used. When welding full frame materials, the electrode wire diameter must be capable of supporting the required current, 0.9 mm (.035 inch) wire is recommended when welding in the 75 to 250 amp range. It is a good practice to verify the welder is equipped with the proper electrode wire for the job. Frame Module Replacement -Frame Tip Replacement – some vehicle makers service frame tips. -Frame Modules – some vehicle makers service replacement front and rear frame modules in addition to the entire frame assembly -Front/Rear Replacement – when replacing a front or rear replacement module, the center section will either overlap or underlap the front and rear sections. Caution must be taken to avoid cutting into the center section frame metal when removing the damaged section and original welds. Grinding away the original weld must be done with care. If initial cuts are made, ensure there is no damage to the center section -Benefit – a benefit to replacing a front or rear module, as opposed to replacing an entire frame assembly, is the original VIN of the frame remains with the vehicle. There are at least four states – Texas, Louisiana, Arizona, and Oregon – that have statutes requiring rebranding of the titles of repaired vehicles in which a major part containing a permanent VIN tag or stamp is replaced. It is also important that the vehicle owner and insurer understand, and agree, which replacement method will be used -Frame Module Replacement Preparation – before replacement of any full-frame part or module, it is important to verify the rest of the frame is dimensionally correct, and any pulling or straightening operations need to be completed before the replacement part is welded in place. -Removal – there are a variety of mechanical and suspension operations required to complete the front module replacement, which may include, but are not limited to, separating the body, and support and/or removing the engine and transmission, suspension, and steering parts, as well as a variety of other items identified in the vehicle service information -Installation – to install the front frame module: -Temporarily clamp the replacement frame module into place and measure to verify it is in the correct position. Tightly clamp the part into position once dimensional accuracy has been verified. -Weld completely around the factory joints -Apply corrosion protection and reassemble all suspension, steering, mechanicals, and body, as required. A wheel alignment will be required. ADAS systems may require calibration depending on vehicle options -Rear Frame Module – following vehicle maker procedures, the replacement of a rear frame module can be done if there is a kink or other damage that cannot be repaired in the rear section. The factory seams is typically located rearward of the cab on a pick up, requiring only the removal of the bed assembly. There will be a variety of other parts that require disconnecting or removal which may include but, is not limited to the rear bumper, trailer hitch, rear axle, fuel tank, suspension, and exhaust. Some parts may remain in position during the module replacement only requiring disconnect and reconnect, as well as support during the module replacement -Removal – to remove the rear frame module: -The welded joint where the frame modules attach may have welded reinforcement brackets that require removal by grinding the welds away -After removal of the reinforcing brackets, grind off the lap welds around the factory seams and remove the rear frame section, or remaining pieces if initial cuts were made -Grind off excess welding material from the center section of the rail that is not being replaced -Note: If the reinforcement brackets are damaged and being replaced, it may be more efficient to cut the rear module off, ensuring you are behind the center section rail overlap area to reduce the weld grinding required to remove the brackets -Installation – to install the rear frame module: -1) Clean all coatings from weld areas, leaving zinc coatings intact -2) If specified by the vehicle maker, apply weld-through primer to all weld surfaces. Clamp the replacement frame module into place and measure to verify it is in the correct position, adjust as required, and verify measurements -3) Remove weld-through primer from the immediate weld area -4) Weld to duplicate the factory joints. The vehicle maker may have a weld diagram of the sequence to complete welds -5) If required, install the reinforcement brackets in the original locations. Inspect for weld defects and cracks with dye penetrant -6) Apply corrosion protection -7) Reassemble other removed parts, and perform a wheel alignment -8) Ensure ADAS calibrations are completed as applicable Performing an Initial Vehicle Inspection (1/2) Vehicle Inspection -Preparing for Initial Customer Contact – when preparing for an initial, vehicle-owner consultation and vehicle inspection, determine if the owner is the insured or claimant. Then, review both the insurance policy and facts-of-loss information provided in the claim file. If the vehicle owner is the insured, reviewing the policy beforehand can be particularly important to understand the policy coverages, limits, exclusions, and any special endorsements. This preparation is necessary to address any questions the vehicle owner may have -Why Perform an Initial Vehicle Inspection? – the initial vehicle inspection is performed to allow the insurance company to make decisions on things like repair versus total loss. In addition, depending on the insurance company and the state in which the claim is located, the company may set reserves to cover the repairs while the vehicle is moved to a repair shop and torn down for further inspection. Reserves are typically set when the carrier initially opens the claim in their system. This inspection may occur at the owner’s residence, place of business, or a drive-in facility if the vehicle is still drivable. If the vehicle is not drivable, the inspection may be performed at a towing facility or repair shop -Insurance and Collision Repair – when reviewing insurance policy information, there are a few common terms you need to understand -Deductibles – auto insurance polies, both collision and comprehensive, may include a deductible. This is the amount that the policyholder is responsible for paying toward an insured loss. The deductible should be verified before contacting the vehicle owner or inspecting the vehicle. The owner may require an explanation of the deductible. Explaining the deductible ensures that owners understand their payment responsibility. Furthermore, it avoids any confusion when the repairs are completed, and the vehicle is ready for delivery. Deductibles are specified in most policies. The insured assumes the loss up to the limit of the deductible. Then, the insurance company pays the amount over the deductible up to the policy limit -Insurance Coverage – collision insurance covers damage to a policyholder’s vehicle due to a collision. Comprehensive polices cover damage to a policyholder’s vehicle that is not related to the collision. The term claimant refers to a vehicle owner making a claim against another’s insurance company. there is no deductible for the claimant -Policy Exclusions and Limits – some insurance polices have items that will not be covered in the event of a loss. These items are typically listed in the exclusions section of the policy. These risks may include: -Deliberate acts -Maintenance issues -Illegal modifications -Customer modifications -Note: Most policies have limits to the amount to which the insurer will be held accountable -Consultation and Inspection Setup – opening the lines of communication with the customer is one of the initial steps in the claims process. It is at this point that you interview the customer and gather any information to be used throughout the repair process. Asking the customer questions about the collision and the vehicle damage often reveals important details that may otherwise be missed. For example, when asked about the collision, the customer might note that the vehicle was in DRIVE, stopped at a light when hit, and that a passenger spilled coffee on the carpet. When discussing damage located away from the point of collision, the customer might indicate that there was previous damage to the hood, roof, left rear door, and left rocker panel molding -Empathy – the initial interview establishes the relationship and can help build trust with customers. When making your initial customer contact, it is important to show empathy. Remember that vehicle owners have gone through a traumatic experience, and their first impression of you could influence their entire claim experience -Initial Customer Consultation – some discussion points for your initial consultation with the vehicle owner include the following: -Review the facts of loss -Review the initial inspection and claims process -Confirm the location of the damage and ask the vehicle owner if there are any other drivability issues -Confirm the vehicle location -If the vehicle is with the owner, confirm the best time for the initial inspection. Ensure that the owner will be available to provide access to the vehicle and keys -If the vehicle is not with the owner, inquire if the owner would like to be present when you inspect the vehicle -Review the policyholder deductible, if applicable -Ask if the vehicle owner has any questions -Obtain Information about the collision – collision information obtained during a customer consultation includes: -The impact source -The speed of the impact -The road conditions at the time of the collision -Brake usage during the collision -The seat belts used. Some vehicle makers recommend replacing seat belts that were in use during the collision -Whether or not the vehicle was drivable or was towed after the collision -Note: knowing what the vehicle hit, or was hit by, aids in evaluating the potential for energy transfer through the vehicle. There is a difference in the amount of energy transferred when hitting another vehicle versus a stationary object, such as a tree -Documenting Vehicle Owner Contact – any time there is communication with the vehicle owner, the event should be documented with the: -Date and time the communication took place -Purpose of the communication -Name of the person who received the communication on the part of the customer -Communication type such as phone, text, or email, including the phone number or email address used -Note: Detailed communication records help to eliminate any confusion about the issue that required the communication. This can reduce or eliminate conflict situations Performing an Initial Vehicle Inspection (2/2) Performing an Initial Inspection -Vehicle Access – gaining access to damaged vehicles can sometimes be a challenge. It is important that you have adequate vehicle access when performing the initial inspection. You need access to all damaged areas and enough distance around the vehicle to obtain the necessary photo documentation. For example, if the vehicle is located against a wall or in a snowbank, request that the vehicle be relocated -Vehicle Information – during the initial inspection, it is important to collect the necessary vehicle information to confirm you are inspecting the correct vehicle. Ensure you have all the trim and options information to allow for accurate parts selection when you prepare the repair estimate.
