When identifying a vehicle, record the: -Vehicle identification number (VIN) -Make and model of the vehicle -Model year and production date, which may be important for parts ordering -Mileage -Body style, such as two-door, four-door, convertible, truck, SUV, or van -Additional Vehicle Information – additional vehicle information should include: -License number -Body color, as well as the vehicle maker’s paint code -Any available trim codes -Drivetrain information – this includes identifying the engine type, size, and the transmission type -Vehicle options – this includes power windows, locks, sunroofs, and cruise control among others. Vehicle options must be correctly identified because they often determine the required procedures or parts -VIN Plates – VIN plates are typically located on the instrument panel or cowl top and are visible through the lower left corner of the windshield. The VIN may also be located on the a-pillar, b-pillar, driver’s door, or driver’s front door jamb -Vehicle Condition – other valuable information that may be obtained during the initial inspection includes: -Any temporary repairs that were done after the collision -Any warning lamps that illuminated since the collision -Identifying any inoperative accessories -Visual cues, such as debris removed from the wheels, suspension, or underbody -Inspecting a Vehicle for Damage – when performing the vehicle inspection, it is important to look for direct, indirect, related, and unrelated damage -Vehicle Damage – direct damage is caused by direct damage with another object. A buckle away from the area of direct impact is considered indirect damage is caused by collision-energy transfer. Initial inspections may also reveal unrelated prior damage. This type of vehicle damage is not in the same area as the other direct and indirect damage -Related Prior Damage – there could also be related prior damage, which is pre-existing damage on a panel that also sustained damage related to the current loss. For example, a vehicle may have been in an accident involving left rear damage a week prior to your inspection. That damage resulted in an estimate to replace the rear bumper cover and repair the left quarter panel. Before the repairs could take place, the same vehicle was in a second accident involving right rear damage. This second set of damage requires replacement of the rear bumper cover and right quarter panel. The rear bumper cover replacement already included on the repair estimate for the first loss would be considered related prior damage for the current claim -Using Visual Cues – When looking at visual clues to identify signs of potential structure damage, note that these should only be used as guides. They should never be used as the only method to determine structural damage. However, visual clues can be used as evidence that structural three-dimensional measuring must be done. In most cases, structural misalignment problems are generally evident, but in some instances, measuring may be the only practical method to determine if structural damage exists -Photo Documentation – when performing an initial vehicle inspection, photograph the vehicle condition and all visible damage to the vehicle -Required Photos – some of the required photos may include: -Four corner photos, showing the license plate on the rear corners -Damage height measurement photos -Close-up photos of the damage -Interior photos -Instrument panel photos, showing radio and GPS options -Driver door panel photos, showing power options -Headliner and sunroof photos -Engine compartment photos -Tread depth measurement photos, showing all tires -Prior damage photos -Visual Documentation – the primary reason for taking photos of a damaged vehicle is to provide visual documentation of the damage. The photo document process also: -Provides a reference for the auto physical damage appraiser (APDA) should they need to review the vehicle after damage has been repaired -Creates a portfolio of the damage to support the written document -May be used for subrogation, litigation, arbitration, or investigative purposes -May be used to determine the possibility of injury severity -When combined with the proper documentation, can reduce or eliminate fraudulent behavior from any of the parties involved -Safety Issues – when performing an initial inspection, look closely to identify any damage to safety and mechanical systems that may make the vehicle unsafe or illegal to drive. The following are examples of safety items that, if damaged, may put the driver or passengers in potentially dangerous situations: -Airbags -Seat belt pre-tensioners -Braking systems -ADAS features such as adaptive cruise control, lane keeping assist, and automatic emergency braking -Note: Furthermore, broken or inoperable headlamps, turn signals, tail lamps, brake lamps, or glass could make the vehicle illegal to drive -Mechanical Issues – examples of mechanical issues include damage to the following items: -Cooling systems -Suspension system -Brake system, including pads, rotors, and lines -Steering system, including the tie rod and tie rod ends -Note: Ignoring these mechanical issues can sometimes create additional damage from driving the vehicle. In some severe instances, the mechanical damage can even be a risk to others on the road -Inspecting Restraints and Malfunction Indicator Lamps – inspect the restraint systems for proper operation and signs of damage. Make sure that recommendations for restraint system operation meet vehicle maker requirements and include all OEM mandatory repairs. Check the instrument panel for illuminated malfunction indicator lamps (MIL). Verify the seat belt is functional and the SRS lamp does not remain lit or blink when the vehicle is running. Remember to consult the vehicle maker’s service requirements to identify mandatory, restraint system replacement items and pre-and post-repair scan requirements -Discussing Drivability – if there is damage that makes the vehicle unsafe or illegal to drive. It is recommended that you go over the problem with the vehicle owner. Explain that driving the vehicle poses a significant safety risk -Customer Drivability Recommendations – when determined that the vehicle is unsafe to driver, recommend that the owner park the vehicle or offer to have it towed to a repair facility. Having the vehicle towed helps reduce liability, as the owner was provided an option other than driving the vehicle. Regardless of which option is chosen, it is important to document this conversation with the vehicle owner. If the owner chooses to continue driving the vehicle, have the owner sign a form that the discussion took place with recommendations to protect the safety of the driver and any passengers Post Repair Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) Testing Purpose -ADAS Post Repair Tests – performing post repair tests will confirm proper system operation. Systems that are not operating properly may not set a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL). Many people are purchasing vehicles because they are equipped with driver assistance features. When a driver knows these systems are on a vehicle, they are also relying on them to function properly and help keep them safe. System Identification -System Verification – prior to test driving the vehicle, it’s imperative that you are familiar with each of the systems the vehicle is equipped with and ensure that the systems are active, An understanding of these systems is also required to properly inspect, diagnose, and repair them. For example, some system tests will require favorable weather or good road conditions. Others require both. Always abide by state an local traffic laws while performing a test drive. When preparing for post repair ADAS testing, the owner’s manual may provide good information about settings and proper operation. -Establishing Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) – it is important to establish standard operating procedures (SOPs) when performing post repair ADAS tests. Some examples include standard testing procedures, test drive routes, and test documentation. In addition, these SOPs should always include referring vehicle maker service procedures. ADAS Testing -Considerations - when performing post repair ADAS test, consider the following -Systems may be activated through an infotainment screen or a button -Vehicles may require the driver to turn the system back on after it has been manually disabled -ADAS icons vary in appearance -Vehicles may need to be driven at or above a specific speed to activate some ADAS -Even after being manually disabled, some systems will be able to reactive themselves if the system senses a need to assist -Cautions – it is important to be safe, cautious, and aware of all situations you put yourself, the vehicle, and other people in while performing post repair ADAS testing. If ADAS is not calibrated correctly, it may not respond properly during post repair testing. - Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) – the adaptive cruise control (ACC) system should -Detect the presence and speed of any forward vehicle -Automatically maintain a set distance from a vehicle ahead -Have braking and accelerating occur without driver intervention -Testing Procedure – testing the adaptive cruise control will require you to test drive the vehicle. It is important to identify a location with light-to-moderate traffic. This test can alaos be done with an assistant in a lead vehicle. When road testing an ACC system, some system checks include proper function of: -Distance settings. Most vehicles have several different distance settings -Cruise Control speed adjust up -Cruise Control speed adjust down -Note: ACC system capabilities may be different from one vehicle to another. Always reference vehicle maker service information to review any available system functionality and testing procedures. - Blind Spot Monitoring – the blind spot monitoring system should -Engage when the turn signal is activated -Illuminate any time a vehicle is in the area that the sensor is monitoring -Gauge the distance of approaching vehicles using radar -Sound an audible alert if the turn signal is turned on or the vehicle starts to drift out of the lane when an object has been detected -Ignore stationary objects -Note: System capabilities vary on different vehicles -Testing Procedure – the blind spot detection system will require a test drive in a location with light-to-moderate traffic. The test can also be performed with an assistant in a follow vehicle. When road testing, some systems check include confirming: -Approaching vehicles from the rear are detected by the blind spot monitoring system -That stationary objects do not trigger a blind spot alert -When testing the system, it is important to test the functionality on both the left and right sides of the vehicle - Lane Departure Warning and Lane Keep Assist – the lane departure warning (LDW) system may provide audio, visual, or steering wheel vibrations to warn the driver if they are drifting out of the lane. The lane keep assist (LKA) should alert the driver with similar audio and visual alerts and also nude the vehicle back into the lane if the driver does not react. When the turn signal is activated, it deactivates the LKA and LDW. -Testing Procedure – lane departure warning and lane keep assist both typically require a test drive on a road with clearly defined center and shoulder liners. Ensure there is no oncoming traffic and there is adequate room along the shoulder. The four tests done with the repaired vehicle on the road are: -Left change with no turn signal -Right change with no turn signal -Left change with turn signal -Right change with turn signal -When performing each of these tests, confirm the appropriate audible and visual alerts function properly. For vehicles with a LKA system, confirm the system nudges the vehicle back into the lane as appropriate. - Cross-Traffic Alert – the cross-traffic alert system should notify the driver of oncoming traffic when backing out of a parking space. It uses an audible alert from the parking aid speaker and a visual alert on the right or left door mirror. The system may also provide a message on the instrumental panel cluster or infotainment screen. -Note: some systems may automatically apply the brakes -Testing Procedure- Cross-traffic alert testing can be performed in a parking lot. The test requires a secondary vehicle to approach while the repaired vehicle is in reverse. When testing this system, verify the system on both the left and right sides of the vehicle is functioning properly. - Backup Stop Assist – the backup stop assist system should: -Sense objects in a vehicle’s path when backing up -Warn using an audible beep and visual indicators depending on the vehicle -Automatically apply the brakes -Note: This test can be performed in a parking lot and requires a stationary object behind the vehicle. When performing this test, make sure: -To use an object that is unlikely to cause any damage to the vehicle if the system fails -The vehicle stops within the designated distance -To depress the brake pedal if the system fails - 360-degree Surround View – the 360 degree surround view -Provides drivers a 360-degree view surrounding their vehicle -Uses multiple cameras in different locations around the vehicle -Feeds visual information from the cameras into the infotainment system, showing a representation of the vehicle -Note: this test can be performed indoors or outdoors and should include inspection to ensure the images are stitched together correctly, in focus, with no overlap or gaps - Rearview Cameras – the rearview cameras should provide a view of the area behind the vehicle when the vehicle is in reverse. The test for this system can be performed indoors or outdoors. After placing the vehicle in reverse, confirm the image is properly focused. It is also important to note that some vehicles may provide a backup grid on the screen to help the driver determine the path of the vehicle while in reverse. When these directional pivot lines are present, it is necessary to ensure the lines turn appropriately as the steering wheel is turned. - Collision Warning/Braking – many emergency warning and braking systems are designed to provide a warning, preload the brakes, and even bring the vehicle to a complete stop. If there is not proper equipment for testing, the tests should not be performed. If testing, first verify that all other ADAS are calibrated and functioning. Place an air wall in an open area and ensure the technician is ready to depress the brake pedal in the event of a system failure. Audible, visual, or haptic feedback may occur with braking within the designated distance. - Park Assist and Active Park – a park assist system should -Provide visual feedback, audio feedback, or a combination of the two to help determine the distance to obstacles. -Correctly use sensors embedded in front, rear, or both bumpers -Note: Active park assist -Is available on some vehicles -Uses cameras and sensors to determine if there is enough room for a vehicle to park between two cars -Allows the vehicle to park itself with some driver assistance -Testing Procedure – perform active park assist and park assist testing in a parking lot. Park assist requires a single stationary object. Active park assist requires two stationary objects spaced to allow for parallel parking. To test park assist, slowly move the vehicle toward the stationary object to confirm you receive the proper alerts. It is important to test this system with the stationary object in front and rear of the vehicle to ensure the sensors on both ends of the vehicle are functioning properly. Active park assist verifies that the vehicle identifies the available parking space, alerts the driver to stop, and tells the driver to put the vehicle in reverse. Then, the driver controls the brake pedal while the system steers the vehicle into the parking space. It is important to be prepared to take control of the steering if either of these systems fail. Also, ensure the stationary objects being used will not cause damage to the vehicle in the event of system failure Documentation and Communication -Deactivation of ADAS – in some cases, the vehicle maker may allow the driver to manually deactivate ADAS features. It’s important to know which systems automatically reactive with the next key cycle and those that may need to be manually reactivated. For example, some vehicles allow the driver to disable the park assist warning system. These systems may not be active the next time the vehicle is driven unless the driver reactivates the system. -Documentation – it is recommended that you document each of the systems tested and attach them to the repair order. I-CAR has developed a checklist that can be used to document the date, time, location, technician, and each system tested. -Customer Consultation – it is a good practice to return vehicles to your customer with all ADAS active. When you review the results of post repair ADAS testing with your customers, advise them that all systems are currently active. Then, allow them the option to manually disable any ADAS they choose. For example, if the customer preference is for the park assist system to not provide audible alerts when parking, remind them that they will need to deactivate the system. Post Repair Process Final Quality -Preparing for Final Quality Control – After all repairs have been completed and the vehicle is out of the production, there are typically a few post repair tasks remaining, which include -Performing a final quality control inspection -Ensuring all final repair documents are complete and accurate -Coordinating a delivery appointment with the vehicle owner -Note: When preparing for the final quality control inspection, print the final repair estimate so it can be compared line by line to the vehicle to ensure all work documented on the final repair estimate has been completed. -Final Quality Control Inspection – in addition to comparing the final estimate to the completed repairs, closely inspect the vehicle, looking at the small details such as buffing compound in jams, overspray, masking materials, or tools left in the vehicle. These small details can stand between you and a completely satisfied customer. The final quality inspection should also include a test drive, listening for wind noises, rattles, drivability issues, and to confirm all systems and accessories are functioning as designed and to ensure no malfunction indicator lamps (MILs) are illuminated. Verify Final Paperwork -Reconcile Invoices – when the vehicle is in the final stages of production, typically final detail/clean-up, the job file is reconciled to ensure all necessary documents are complete, accurate, and in the file. Some examples of items included in the file reconciliation process may include verifying that: -The final estimate is a complete and accurate reflection of the repair work performed. -All sublet and parts invoices are in the file and the prices on the invoices match the final repair estimate. -All supporting documents, such as alignment reports, frame reports, and vehicle maker repair procedures, are in the file. -All electronic communications have been properly uploaded to the insurance company when appropriate. -Note: When processing a claim involving an insurance direct repair program, there may be very specific electronic upload requirements as well as documents requiring a customer signature. -Additional Documentation – additional considerations for items that should be documented and retained in the job file may include: -Pre and Post scan reports -Technical Data Sheets (TDS) for repair materials used (such as panel bond adhesives, seam sealers, etc.) -Documents verifying the materials and procedures used by sublet vendors, such as windshield or rear glass replacement -Note: It is always helpful to fully document all repairs, materials, and procedures used to complete repairs so they are available for future reference in the event of a warranty claim -Preparing the Final Bill – there are many different options available to repair shops for preparing a final repair invoice for the customer. The important thing is that the document used as the final repair bill provides an itemized summary of all repairs completed and the expenses related to each of those repairs. In addition, the final repair bill should include the shop name, address, phone number, and name of the estimator or person assigned as the contact person. -Note: Always reference local and state laws to identify required disclaimers, shop identification information, and any other information that may be legally required to appear on the final repair bill. Vehicle Delivery -Coordinating a Delivery Appointment – another step in the post repair process is scheduling a delivery appointment with the vehicle owner. In some shops, this may not occur until the file has been reconciled and the final quality control inspection has been completed. In other shops, this may be done as soon as the vehicle enters the final detail/clean up department -Check for Rental Car – when preparing to contact the customer to schedule a delivery appointment, it is a good practice to review the job file notes to determine if the customer has a rental car that will need to be returned. This will allow you to update the rental car company of the scheduled return of the rental vehicle, and you should remind the customer to remove any personal belongings and fill the fuel tank before bringing the vehicle back, if required as part of their rental agreement. -Prepare Shop and Insurance Warranties – in preparation for the delivery appointment, it is also a good practice to prepare all warranty and vehicle car instructions that will be provided to the customer. This may include the shop repair warranty, insurance company repair warranty, direction of payment form, etc. -Note: Depending on shop SOP’s or any insurance partner DRP requirements, there may be additional documents to be prepared for the customer or for the customer to sign. -Contact Customer to Coordinate Delivery – when contacting the customer to schedule a delivery appointment, it is a good practice to check the calendar for the staff member responsible for delivering the vehicle to the customer. This ensures the staff member is available to serve the customer upon arrival without keeping them waiting. It is also a good practice to let the customer know the delivery process may take between 20-30 minutes so they can plan accordingly. -Delivery – delivering the vehicle is not simply a matter of handing the customer the keys. It may be your last chance to make a good impression on the customer by providing exceptional customer service, spending the time necessary to review the high-quality work performed on their vehicle, and making sure they are completely satisfied. -Presenting the Repaired Vehicle – when the customer arrives to take delivery of their vehicle, it is a good practice, after greeting the customer, to walk around the vehicle and review the estimate and highlight the completed repairs. This is an opportunity for you to put your repair quality in the spotlight by remind your customer of the repairs that were required and the commitment your shop has to providing high quality repairs. For example, point out any special features of the repair, color matching, panel alignment, etc. that make it appear as though the vehicle has never been damaged. -Review Paperwork – after you have finished your walk around and review of the completed repairs, it is time to review all of the final documents with the customer. For example, review the warranty documents, post repair vehicle care instructions, and any alignment reports or sublet repair documents, such as instructions following a recent windshield installation. -Explain Upcoming CSI – the vehicle delivery process also provides an opportunity for you to explain the CSI survey calls your customer will be receiving and to remind them of your commitment to ensuring they are completely satisfied and your desire to receive a perfect score. It is a good practice to review some of the key questions your customer will be asked and let them know if that, at any time, they feel they would not be willing to give you a perfect score, to please let you know so you can work to address their concerns immediately. It is also a good idea, if an insurance company was involved in the repairs, to let the customer know they may receive more than one survey call, as the insurance company may perform their own CSI survey. -Payment Settlements – the final repair bill may be paid in a number of ways depending on the parties involved in the repair process. For example, if an insurance company that is not part of a DRP relationship is involved, the customer will likely have a check from the insurance company to sign over to the shop to cover the cost of the repairs. If the repair involves an insurance company, and the vehicle owner is insured by the insurance company involved, it is likely there will be a deductible the customer will be responsible for, and it will need to be collected from the customer in addition to the check from the insurance company. -Note: If supplemental damages were found after the initial check was provided to the customer, the customer may need to sign additional documents (such as Direction of Payment (DOP)) so the insurance company can pay the shop directly for the supplemental repairs. Customer Follow-Up -Post Repair Follow-Up – following the vehicle delivery, contact the customer to make sure they are happy with the repair and their experience. This may be your very last chance to make things right, if necessary, before the CSI call. The CS survey company will typically call the customer within 30 days of delivery and ask the customer to rate your shop in several areas. Typically, these include quality and timeliness of communication, time to repair, quality of repair, friendliness, and overall experience. CSI -CSI Reports – CSI survey companies typically provide monthly reports that summarize the customer satisfaction scores based on the customer surveys completed during the designated survey period. In some cases, the CSI survey company may provide individual reports for each survey as they are completed in advance of the monthly summary reports. It is a good practice to closely monitor these individual reports to identify trends that may need to be addressed quickly to correct a customer service issues. -Communication CSI Results – in addition to monitoring individual survey results, it is a good practice to review the monthly summary reports with the entire staff to review customer feedback, identify opportunities for improvement, and celebrate the things that are going well. For instance, if communication is scored low, check to see how often you are talking to the customer and if the information provided during those calls is meaningful. You may find that customers are being contacted on a schedule following shop standard operation procedures, but the information provided during those status updates is not providing a meaningful update to the customers. Priming and Blocking Repaired Areas (1/3) Primers and Their Uses -Primer Coatings – primer coatings include epoxy, self-etching, primer-surfacer, and primer-sealer. Primer coatings must be applied to a cleaned surface. If there is no access for mechanical and chemical cleaning, primers will not adhere to the surface -Epoxy Primer – epoxy primer is a corrosion-inhibiting undercoat applied to a vehicle surface before applying finish coats. Epoxy primer may require an etching base, or can be applied direct to metal. An etching base is a two-step process of applying a metal cleaner and conversion coating, which chemically etches the surface. This process is most often done on restoration projects, rather than in production repair facilities. -Wash and Self-Etching Primers – self-etching primer is a corrosion-inhibiting undercoat containing phosphoric acid. The phosphoric acid etches into the surface to promote adhesion to bare metal, and neutralizes the surface area as it dries. When applied, self-etching primer leaves a thin layer that must be coated with additional primer because self-etching primers do not form a barrier like epoxy primers and would eventually break down. Self-etching primer may be applied in one or two parts, and must be flashed to ensure the acid will not react to additional coatings. Water primers are a form of self-etching primer that are even thinner. Applying water primers too thickly may inhibit the cure. * Self-etching primer is also available in various forms, including aerosol or a pen applicator. -Direct-to-Metal Primers – direct-to-metal primers are a two-part material that can be applied directly to a bare metal surface, and work similar to a self-etching or epoxy primer. Some may be used as a sealer or a surfacer, depending on how the primer is reduced. There is no need to apply a separate primer and sealer because the direct-to-metal primer works as both. The following paint makers have a primer that may either be applied to a bare metal surface or to an E-coated surface: -Axalta -PPG -Sherwin Williams -Akzo Nobel -Glasurit -Adhesion-Promoting Primer – adhesion promoters may be required to be used on some bare plastic parts by some paint makers. They are generally applied to properly prepared plastic parts. They are typically required on polyolefin plastics. -Waterborne Primers – some waterborne primers include primer-surfacers and primer-sealers -Primer-Surfacers – when working with primer-surfacers, surface preparation is key. Make sure the panel surface is properly cleaned and sanded, generally using P180-P320 grit, depending on the product maker’s recommendations and surface type (body filler, refinished panel, or bare steel). Paint makers may recommend a surface prep, such as etch primer, before applying a primer-surfacer. Generally, 1-3 coats of primer-surfacer is acceptable, with a flash time between each coat. Recommended film thickness varies. Some product makers recommend 1.6-1.8 mils, while another recommends 2.0-2.5 mils. -Primer-Sealers – for most primer-sealers, one medium wet coat is acceptable with a film build of 0.5 – 1.5 mils. If a second coat is desired, make sure the first coat has flashed before application. Always reference product maker data sheets for application requirements. -Application – for application of primer-surfacers or primer-sealers, inlet air pressure of 35-45 PSI on conventional spray guns is fairly common, and around 30 PSI for high volume low pressure (HVLP) (around 10 PSI at the air cap). Be sure to refer to the product maker’s technical data sheet for specific recommendations. Drying time for primer-surfacers or primer-sealers depends on the drying method – air dry, forced dry (heat), or infrared -Waterborne Primers Usage Considerations – waterborne primers may be able to be applied to bare steel, or used as a barrier coat. When applying to bare steel, the surface is cleaned with a wax and grease remover. If an etching primer is used, do no apply the waterborne basecoat on top of the etching primer. A primer-sealer should be applied over the top of the etch primer. Depending on the paint system being used, waterborne primers may be tintable. If a waterborne basecoat is being applied, a waterborne primer is not required, as long as the materials being applied are compatible within the specific system. * Use tack clothes that are compatible with the waterborne products you are using. This may be indicated by the product maker or the paint manufacturer -UV Primers – UV primer changes from liquid to solid when exposed to UV light. It takes place at the molecular level. Photo initiators are exposed to a V-A light, which causes them to undergo a chemical reaction, which causes them fragment. These fragments then combine with the resin molecules and begin polymerization and cross-linking reactions. This process turns the resin into a hard, dry coating -Purpose of Sealers – sealer, or primer-sealer, is designed to provide a uniform, even foundation for the basecoat and clearcoat. Sealer can aid with improving color holdout and long-term appearance. Good color holdout prevents primer spots from showing through the basecoat. This can occur because the sealer stops the basecoat from penetrating into the substrate. Additionally, sealer enhances corrosion protection due to the added film thickness and, in general, acts as a cushion. Areas that are prone to chipping should have sealer applied. Some examples of these areas include rocker panels, the front of fenders, and the front of the hood. * Sealer is not designed to cover up surface flaws, such as sandscratches, nor is recommended for use on large areas of bare metal. -When to Use Sealers – sealers are used any time there is a new E-coated part, as the part surface is hard and fully cured, making it more difficult for materials to adhere. Sealers provide a new coating surface that helps with adhesion of the basecoat. Sealers are also used for improved hiding or coverage of the basecoat. Sealers are available in different colors, and may also be tintable to match the basecoat being applied over the sealer. Tintable sealer is typically light gray or transparent, and may have toners added to make a specific color. This also creates a better color match to the OEM sealer color when refinishing a complete panel. * While the sealer is not normally wet when basecoat is applied, it has not fully cured, allowing the basecoat to melt into the sealer to some degree -Urethane Primer-Surfacer/Primer-Sealer – urethane primer-surfacer or primer-sealer helps level out the surface for topcoat application, and is the last step needed before applying topcoats, or may be the final step for some hidden areas that do not need topcoat. Primer surfacer is typically intended to be sanded before topcoat applicat. Additionally, urethane primer-surfacer or primer-sealer: -May be tinted, which sets a color when using translucent basecoats -May be applied direct-to-metal -May be available as an aerosol, and as an ultraviolet (UV) -cured product -* Do not expose eyes or skin to direct UV light. Use UV safety glasses (polycarbonate) available from the lamp manufacturer. UV lamps get very hot and should be kept away from solvent fumes -Featheredging Process – There are several techniques and aspects of the featheredging process that must be taken into consideration. -Sanding the Repair Area – to featheredge the finish, start over the repair area and work outward. Sand to level out the harsh lines around the repair area and other minor damage. Minor damage may include scratches and nicks. The rough edges in an original finish are tapered to prevent the ridges from showing through the new finish -Sanding Body Filler – if body filler is used in a featheredged area, the filler should not contact the painted surface. Sand before or after body filler application. If the finish is featheredged before body filler is applied, featheredging may also be required after the body filler is sanded to contour. When to featheredge during the repair process is a matter of personal preference. -Sanding Coating Layers – sand until about ¼ - 3/8 inch (6-10mm) of each layer of coating is removed and can be seen. The featheredge should look like a bird’s feather with soft, smooth edges. -Working with Sanding Tools – an orbital or dual-action (DA) sander with a flexible backing pad may be used. Place the sander flat against the surface and taper the paint layers by moving the sander in a cross-cutting pattern. Stop frequently to feel for rough edges -Blending a Featheredge – as a featheredge is worked out into the blend area, the abrasive can be increased in steps of up to P100 grit at a time, up to P500 grit. This allows for the previous grit sandscratches to be removed quickly and effectively. When using sanding material above P500 grit, there can be a jump of up to P200 grit at a time, up to P1000 grit. A P500 grit jump between materials can be used with P1000 grit and above. -Visual Inspection of a Featheredge – featheredging is done to ensure a smooth transition from the repair area to the undamaged area. When inspecting the featheredge, look for -Whether the finish is a basecoat, clearcoat, or tri-coat system -The amount and number of finish coats applied -Whether there is a good finish adhesion -Whether there is acceptable film thickness -Coating Soon-To-Be-Enclosed Areas – due to the cleaning requirement for primer coatings, it may be advantageous, even recommended, to apply primer to portions of a panel that will not be accessible after installation. For example, treat the backsides of quarter panels and other areas that may be difficult to access even with wand applicators -Masking for Primers – when masking before applying primers, spray lines should be soft, as opposed to being harsh. Be sure to mask off the jambs from overspray. Additionally, keep the area as small as possible. The entire panel may not need to be primed. When masking for a blend area, use masking techniques designed for blend areas such as reverse masking Priming and Blocking Repaired Areas (2/3) Priming -Mixing Room – the paint mixing room is designed to be self-ventilating and contain any spills that occur. Mixing rooms must also meet local fire codes, be grounded to avoid sparks and explosions, and have explosion-proof wiring -Understanding Mixing Ratios – most products used for refinishing in the automotive collision repair industry require that they be mixed or reduced. Depending on the type of paint system, there can be different mixing ratios used. 3:1 (top). 3:1:1 (middle). 3:1:10-20% (bottom) -3:1 Mixing Ratio – a 3:1 ratio is taking parts of two products and mixing them together. Examples of this are mixing 3 oz. of one primer and 1 oz. of hardener, or 10.5 oz. of one primer and 3.5 oz. of hardener -3:1:1 Mixing Ratio – a 3:1:1 ratio is taking three products and mixing them together. Examples of this are mixing 3 oz. of primer and 1 oz. of hardener and 1 oz. of reducer, or 10.5 oz. of primer and 3.5 oz. of hardener with 3.5 oz. of reducer -a 1:1:10-20% ratio is taking equal parts of two products and mixing them together. Then, by doing a math equation, figure out from the mixed product how much of the mixed product is 10-20%, and then add that 10-20% quantity of the third product. Examples of this are mixing 1 oz. of paint and 1 oz. of reducer and then 0.1 – 0.2 oz. of atomizing agent, or 15 oz. of paint and 15 oz. of reducer and then 1.5-3.0 oz. of an atomizing agent. -Preparing Refinish Materials – mixing ratio cups, mixing sticks, viscosity cups, and strainers are all important tools needed to create a quality refinish. -Mixing Ratio Cups – mixing ratio cups are used for mixing paint, reducer, and hardener. Mixing ratio cups may have graduated scales on the sides of the cup to aid in proper mixtures. Different mixing ratio cups may have parallel sides. The bottom of the mixing ratio cup should be perpendicular to the sides to help ensure proper mixing is attained. Different mixing ratio cups may have a scale for mixing, with a graduated scale for cups that have nonparallel sides -Mixing Sticks – mixing sticks are available by most paint makers. Paint makers that offer mixing sticks will have the mixing sticks needed to support their mixing formula requirements. When using mixing sticks, guidelines must be followed. When using a mixing stick -Place the stick in a container and follow the appropriate scale -Do not set them at an angle -The scales must be followed exactly -Use containers with non-tapered sides -Use a set of mixing sticks made especially for their ratios -Viscosity Cups – viscosity cups are used to gauge how fast a material can pass through a given size orifice. This will then determine the spray gun setup that is needed. There are different types of viscosity cups. Be sure to use the correct cup for the application requirements. Different types of viscosity cups may be able to be interchanged in some applications. Three common viscosity cups used include the Zahn, Ford, and Din cup. -Strainers – strainers should be lint-free, as well as tested for resistance to solvents and water. Soak the strainer for a couple minutes in water and also in solvent. Debris could end up in the paint cut if there is any change to the strainer. Strainers also should have nylon mesh instead of cotton. This is because cotton has lint that can be deposited into the paint cup. -Primer Color Choice – most paint manufacturers offer a tintable option for primer. Tintable primer can be used before applying the color coat to promote color holdout and allow for easier hiding when applying the color coat. This may also speed up the refinish process because a faster application of the color coat can be achieved because less coatings may need to be applied. -Primer Guns – there are currently two types of spray guns used in primer application. High Volume Low Pressure (HVLP), spray guns are one of the more commonly used types of primer guns. This gun uses a high amount of air volume and converts it to low pressure. This helps ensure that more paint is applied to the intended surface, and reduces the chance of overspray. The second type is the reduced pressure (RP) spray gun. This gun applies primer at pressures the same or lower than HVLP guns. -Triggering On and Off – triggering the spray gun on and off at the beginning and end of a spray stroke allows for less use of refinish materials, especially at the edge of panels. -Proper Spray Gun Use – to properly use a spray gun, hold the spray gun the proper distance from the panel. Keep the spray gun level and perpendicular to the panel surface at all times, following the angles and curves with a wrist motion. Move the spray gun in a steady, even motion before pulling the trigger. This should occur before reaching the edge of the refinish area. Finally, release the trigger before stopping movement of the spray gun. -Heeling and Arching – heeling is allowing the spray gun to tilt. Arching or fanning is failure to keep the spray gun parallel to the surface. Both heeling and arching can result in uneven film thickness, orange peel, dry spray, and excessive overspray Heeling on the left. Arching on the right -Travel Speed – the travel speed is the rate that the spray gun is moved along the panel surface. The travel speed of a spray gun can affect finish appearance, and therefore should be steady and even. If the travel speed is too slow, the material may sag and run. This type of finish is unacceptable, and will need to be sanded down and possibly reapplied. If the travel speed is too fast, the material lays down with a rough texture. This type of finish is also unacceptable. -Spray Gun Distance to Panel (setback) – the distance that a spray gun is help relative to the panel surface (also called setback) can have effects similar to that of travel speed. A spray gun that is too close will deposit too much refinish material on the panel, potentially causing runs and sags. These defects will need to be removed. A spray distance that is too far back can have a dry, rough, or poor coverage effect. This can also lead to excessive overspray, causing wasted material and time Top left – 3” – 4” setback. Too close. Top right – 12”-16” setback. Too far. Bottom – 6”-10” setback. Proper distance. -Reverse Priming – reverse priming is the process of applying a larger coat of primer first, followed by progressively smaller applications. Reverse priming is used to ensure a smoother surface, allow for easier sanding, and reduce the amount of overspray edges when spot priming. There are multiple advantages of reverse priming, which include -Less overspray on the edges of each coat (less texture and better adhesion) -Less potential for trapped solvent to cause die back or solvent popping -Edges being left open, allowing solvent to escape, which decreases the dry time -An edge that is easier to feather into the surrounding existing finish Traditional Priming Method on the top. Reverse Priming Method on the bottom. The first coat is the coat closer to the black line. Then second coat, then third coat -Spraying Difficult Areas – difficult areas to spray may include corners and edges of panels. When spraying difficult areas -It may be best to spray these areas first -The pattern size and air pressure may need to be reduced -It may be best to hold the spray gun closer than normal, for example, 1-2 inches (25-50 mm) away from the panel -It may help to aim directly at the center of the problem area -Spray Gun Settings – when adjusting a spray gun, the refinish technician needs to be aware of what adjustments are needed -Fan Width – fan width can be adjusted from a small diameter circle to a large oblong pattern. The fan width adjustment knob is generally on top of the spray gun body. Turn the control knob all the way in to create a small, round pattern with dense coverage. Turn the control knob gradually out to form a vertical, elongated pattern with lighter coverage. If turned too far, the pattern will be too heavy on the ends and too light in the middle -Primer Deposit – the amount of primer deposited can be adjusted to a lighter deposition by turning the fluid control adjustment in, and a heavier deposition by backing the adjustment out. The fluid control adjustment knob is generally located below the fan width adjustment knob on the spray gun body -Air Pressure – air pressure at the gun inlet is often provided by the spray gun maker. The proper inlet air pressure will give a reduced pressure at the air cap with the fan control wide open -Spray Gun Distance and Overlap – spray gun distance when spraying waterborne refinish materials is about the same as spraying solvent-borne refinish materials, 6-8 inches (150-200mm). This may be true for most products, but not for all. Requirements may differ by paint maker. Just like with solvent-borne refinishing materials, most paint makers recommend using a 75%-80% overlap. -Applying Sealer – there are several items that should be taken into consideration in regard to the application of sealer. For example, sealer can be the start of a contour mapping defect if it is not applied correctly. -Wet-On-Wet Application – sealers are typically applied wet-on-wet. Wet-on-wet means the next coating is applied after a flash period and not after the previous coat has fully cured. Most sealers have a window for recoating. If the sealer is not recoated within the window, it may need to be sanded or resealed before applying color. Recoat windows can vary between products. -Using a Slow Reducer – sealer application must be as smooth as possible, or it will show through. Using a slower reducer in the sealer than may be used for other coatings may help the sealer level out -Spray Gun Technique – be sure to use the same gun technique and the same high-quality spray gun as is used when applying basecoat and clearcoat. This is done so the application looks the same and has the same quality. Sometimes there are lesser quality spray guns used when applying undercoats. This should not be done when applying sealer -Minor Defects – minor defects, such as small runs, sags, or debris, may be sanded out. The amount of sanding needed will dictate whether recoating with sealer will be necessary Priming and Blocking Repaired Areas (3/3) Block Sanding -Block Sanding – block sanding is an important step between the last coat of primer and the first coat of sealer or basecoat. Without proper blocking, many imperfections will show up in the final paint applications. The term ‘block sanding’ came from the original way this step was performed, with a piece of sandpaper wrapped around a block of wood. This allowed for a uniform smooth surface. Now there are blocks of all different shapes, sizes, and materials to conform to the varied contours of a vehicle surface. Block sanding is required to identify defects, such as sandscratches and high or low spots in the substrate, so they can be repaired prior to applying basecoat and clearcoat materials. -Sanding Blocks – hand sanding blocks are used by applying even, moderate pressure along the length of the block using the palm and extended fingers. The palm must be flat against a flexible block to avoid applying uneven pressure in the spaces between fingers. Sanding blocks are available in various sizes, shapes, and materials, and may be made of rubber, foam, wood, or fiberglass, and vary in flexibility and function. Sheets of sandpaper may also be folded around the sanding block. -Guide Coat – the guide coat is a contrasting color to the undercoat that identifies high and low spots. When sanded, it is removed from the high spots and remains in the low spots and imperfections. The guide coat may be sprayed on as a thin mist product or a fine powder. A guide coat that is a powder is typically available in a plastic container and applied using a sponge. -Block Sanding Techniques – when block sanding, move from side-to-side, up-and-down, and at angles. A sanding block may be used similar to a body file or cheese grater by holding it parallel to the contour of the panel and moving at a 30 degree angle. Be sure to sand a little and then check the contour of the surface, and remember that refinishing will highlight imperfections, not hide them. Block sanding may either be done with a pneumatic sander or by hand, using a foam or rubber block Preparing a Vehicle for Refinishing (1/3) Removing Pinstripes and Decal Adhesive -Refinishing Safety Considerations – it is important to be aware of all safety considerations when working with solvents and refinish materials, including waterborne products, as they may still include solvents and other hazardous materials. National/local regulations will require employers to provide the proper personal protective equipment (PPE) to protect workers from potential hazards. Recommended PPE for refinish technicians will include the following -Respiratory protection. Fresh air supplied respirators provide the best protection and may be mandated in some states when applying refinish materials -Eye Protection -Chemical-resistant gloves, such as nitrile gloves -A full-body paint suit -*Safety Data Sheets (SDS) are a good source for determining PPE requirement -Taped Pinstripe Removal – there are a few different methods that can be used for removal of tape pinstripes -Heat Gun – one method for removing taped pinstriping is to apply heat with a heat gun or heat lamp to loosen the adhesive. Heat may be required to bring the surface to a recommended temperature or to loosen the adhesive. When using a heat gun, use a circular motion and pull the taped pinstriping away from the panel, while applying tension near the panel -Chemical Remover – another method for removing stripe tape is using a chemical remover, such as a general-purpose adhesive remover or similar material. Test the chemical remover on a small, hidden area, or on damaged panels that been removed, before applying chemical removers to an undamaged panel or visible area -Eraser Disc – an eraser disc can be used to remove the pinstripe and the adhesive residue. Use similar techniques with the eraser disc to remove pinstripes as when removing emblem adhesive. This should be done by applying light pressure, and using horizontal and vertical movements. Residue should be removed from the eraser disc by lightly rubbing the area with general-purpose adhesive remover or isopropyl alcohol. Do not use chemical removers and a heat gun or heat lamp simultaneously -Painted Pinstripe Removal – painted pinstripes can be OEM or aftermarket applications. In areas requiring refinishing, to remove painted pinstripes, use a power sander. Feather out the area to create a smooth area, while keeping the repair area as small as possible. If areas are not feathered out properly, the edges of the pinstripe will show through -Emblems – removing emblems and nameplates from a vehicle can be slightly more complicated than removing pinstripes and decals, particularly if you intend to reuse them. The most common method of removal is to apply heat using a heat gun, induction heater, or heat lamp, followed by a plastic removal tool. Another common method for removal is using plastic fishing line to cut through the adhesive to remove the emblem -Decal and Wrap Removal – some vehicles may also have factory-installed decals, aftermarket vinyl decals, or vehicle wraps that will need to be removed as part of the refinish prep process. -Heat Application – decals can be removed similar to the way taped pinstriping is removed. Such as applying heat with a heat gun or a heat lamp to loosen the adhesive. Be careful not to overheat the area -Eraser Discs and Chemical Remover – decals may also be removed by cleaning with an eraser disc or using a chemical remover, such as general-purpose adhesive remover. Test the chemical remover on a small, hidden area, or on a damaged panel, before applying chemical removers to an undamaged panel or visible area -Wraps – a vehicle wrap is typically used for advertising on company vehicles to cover a large area, or the entire exterior of a vehicle, and are typically removed using a heat gun or heat lamp, similar to removing tape pinstripes or decals. After repairing the vehicle, wraps are typically replaced at the facility where they were originally installed Emblem Adhesive Removal -Emblem Adhesive Removal – the common methods used to remove emblem adhesive are similar to those used for tape stripes and decals. * When applying a general-purpose adhesive remover, wear safety glasses, rubber gloves, and the proper respirator. Apply in a well-ventilated area -Removing Residue – general-purpose adhesive remover is used to loosen and remove adhesive. When using an adhesive remover the surface must be cool, soaking time may be required, and an aerosol can or pump spray bottle should be used on vertical panels. You should scrape the area with a plastic razor blade or squeegee to remove the adhesive residue; additional applications may be needed to remove all of the adhesive residue and you should clean the area with soap and water immediately after adhesive removal. * Take caution when using adhesive removers on or near vinyl or rubber trim pieces and emblem/faceplates with anodized coatings as it can damage the textured finish -Eraser Disc – an eraser disc can be used to remove the adhesive that is left behind after emblem removal. When used correctly, an eraser disc will not damage the painted surface. When using an eraser disc, you should follow the product maker’s speed recommendation. You should use horizontal and vertical movements and apply light pressure. Heavy pressure will not assist in the removal efficiency. Work against the rotation of the eraser disc. Residue from the eraser disc can be removed by lightly rubbing the area with general-purpose adhesive remover or isopropyl alcohol, if needed. If a film remains, rework the area with the eraser disc after it has dried. * Eraser discs should not be used on lacquer coatings or thermoplastic polymers like polyolefin bumper covers -Applying Heat – heat can be applied with a heat gun or heat lamp to soften the adhesive. There are specific safety precautions and additional removal tools are utilized when using a heat gun or heat lamp. Do not overheat the panels as this will damage the paint film. You should monitor heat settings, staying a safe distance from the panel, and using a circular motion during application. You should also wear gloves to avoid contact with the heater. Use a plastic razor blade or squeegee to remove the warmed adhesive, and apply adhesive remover to remove excess adhesive, after the panel has cooled. * Do not use chemical removers and a heat gun or heat lamp simultaneously Cleaning the Affected Area Surface Contaminants – when preparing a vehicle for refinish, the first step should always be cleaning of the affected area. This step is critical to ensure all surface contaminants are removed to keep them from being spready around the refinish area and clogging up your sandpaper. Even clean hands can leave behind oil and salt contaminants. The oil can cause adhesion issues or fisheyes. The salt is a desiccant that attacks moisture, which can lead to blistering. Nitrile gloves can be worn during repair procedures to prevent incidental contact. If contact cannot be avoided, clean the area as soon as possible so it is not forgotten. -Water-Soluble – water-soluble contaminants typically include salt, sugar, dirt, and bugs. To remove water-soluble contaminants, an initial cleaning with a neutral pH soap and water should be done when the vehicle enters the repair facility before any repairs are started. Using hot water decreases the surface tension of the water, which helps the water reach into the pores and crevices. Avoid car wash products that contain wax, to minimize the chance of fisheyes -Non Water-Soluble – non water-soluble contaminants include oil, wax, grease, silicone, petroleum products, and handprints. This can cause coatings to flow around the contaminated area and result in fisheyes, which can cause adhesion issues. Adhesion is adversely affected on both the top and bottom sides of the contaminated area -Cleaning with Solvents- solvent cleaners may be alcohol or petroleum based, and are provided in various strengths. Generally, petroleum-based cleaners cannot be used on plastic parts. Refer to the technical data sheet (TDS) to determine whether or not the cleaners is appropriate for the substrate to be cleaned. Solvent cleaners may have antistatic properties. Wiping panels when cleaning can build up static electricity on the vehicle, which attracts dust and dirt particles. Masking the vehicle with plastic can also cause static electricity. Cleaners with antistatic properties should be used after other cleaning products are used. This is done as a final step to remove any static electricity that has built up during the cleaning process. Do not substitute solvent cleaners with lacquer thinners or reducers. Lacquer thinners or reducers may evaporate too quickly, and will not allow the dirt to be lifted and wiped away. They may also be composed of stronger-solvents, which can cause coatings to reflow or cause other types of adverse reactions. * Nitrile gloves and a respirator should be worn when working with solvents -Substrate Identification – it is crucial to identify the substrate before using any surface cleaner to ensure the appropriate cleaner is used. A substrate, in terms of refinishing, is anything that offers a sound foundation for topcoat application. Substrates may be materials other than just topcoats. The base material can be considered a substrate. Examples of some substrates include -The original finish -E-coat -Areas that have been previously refinished or featheredged -Flexible, rigid, fiber-reinforced, or non fiber-reinforced plastic parts -Aluminum, steel, or galvanized metals -Body filler Surface Cleaners and Tack Cloths -Waterborne Surface Cleaners – when working with waterborne refinishing materials, surface cleaners must also be used to clean contaminants from the panel surface. These cleaners are used on freshly prepared surfaces before the basecoat is applied. They are also typically water-based and must be totally removed before application of the basecoat. Failure to do so may cause finish defects. Additionally, the cleaner should not be allowed to dry before being wiped off the panel. If this occurs, more cleaner should be applied and wiped from the panel surface. Be sure to follow the directions as stated on the TDS -Fisheyes and Adhesion Failure – failure to properly clean the surface before starting the refinish preparation process, as required before each stage of the refinish process, can lead to costly refinishing defects and cycle time delays. One example of such a defect is fisheyes. Fisheyes are small, crater-like openings that appear in refinish material when it is wet. Fisheyes may be caused by silicone, oil, or grease contaminants. For example, oil or grease contaminants may be introduced to the finish by the technician, such as grease reside from lunch or oily skin. Adhesion failure is another example and can be identified by paint film peeling, delaminating, or chipping away. Adhesin failure may be caused by failing to remove contaminants on the surface, such as oil, sanding residue, overspray, water, or solvent cleaner -Proper Cleaning Method – when cleaning the surface to prepare for application of refinish coatings, make sure to use lint-free towels, wipe the surface while it is still wet. Cleaners are designed to lift contaminants off the surface so that they can be wiped off. The contaminant will rest back on the surface if the cleaner is allowed to dry. Wipe in one direction and use the two-towel method. Use on towel for wetting the surface and a separate towel for wiping the surface until it is dry. Ensure that the surface is wiped dry. * Waterborne basecoats are more sensitive to wax and grease remover, so it is vital that it is entirely removed from the surface when using a waterborne paint system -Tack Cloths – tack cloths are designed to remove dust and lint from the vehicle surface before applying refinish coats and tack the surface between color coats. The tack cloth should be unfolded and bunched into a ball before using. Do not apply pressure to the panel when using a tack cloth. Using a tack cloth when it is flat or folded can damage freshly coated surfaces. Tack cloths can be lightly dragged across the surface to help identify dry spray. Because waterborne basecoats are more sensitive to contamination, tack cloths designed for use with waterborne products do not contain varnish. This type of tack cloth is safe for use with solvent-based products as well. * The type of tack cloth shown here does not contain varnish because waterborne basecoats are more sensitive to contamination Sanding and Scuffing The Purpose of Sanding – there are a few reasons for sanding surfaces to be refinished, prior to application of any refinishing products. One reason is to increase the surface area for better adhesion of refinish coatings. Increasing the surface area improves the mechanical bond of refinish coatings. Another reason is to allow solvents to absorb into the surface, which improves the chemical bond. Leveling minor imperfections in the surface is another reason for sanding prior to applying refinishing products Sanded Surface = more surface area Red line is smooth surface Blue wave line is sanded surface Blue line is sanded surface straightened -Sandpaper – there are many levels or grits of sandpaper available, which range from coarse to very fine, and sanding may be done wet or dry. Wet sanding requires sandpaper that is designed for wet sanding, and the surface should be kept constantly wet while sanding. Some product makers require soaking the sandpaper before use. -Sanding Best Practices – when preparing a surface for sanding, there are some best practices that should be followed. Always refer to technical data sheets (TDS) for the proper sandpaper grit selection. Sandpaper grit selection may vary, depending on the sanding method being applied. Generally, you should only increase grit in increments of 100, up to 500 grit. You should increase increments by 100, for sanding materials above 500 grit, and increments of 500 for sanding materials above 1,000 grit -Sanding Issue – poor sanding techniques can lead to refinish defects, such as scratch marks that happen as a result of using a final sanding grit that is too coarse. Not removing sanding dust and residue, or sanding before the coating is completely cured, can cause problems as well. * Dried waterborne basecoats are thinner, so proper surface preparation is even more important -Coarse Scratches and Excessive Primer-Surfacer – finish sanding with a grade of sandpaper that is more coarse than what is recommended by your refinish product maker, and applying excessive coats of primer surfaced to fill these scratches, can result in refinish defects. White = clearcoat Red = basecoat Dark gray = primer/surfacer Tan = body filler Light gray = substrate/panel surface -Temperature Changes – coarse sandscratches in the metal that expand and contract with temperature changes can result in visible scratch marks. This is more likely to occur where refinish materials are thinnest -Body Filler – body filler that is sanded too coarsely can also cause visible scratches. Coarse grit sandpaper is more prone to “plow” through the body filler rather than cutting it. These parts of the body filler may crumble over time and fall into crevices that the primer bridged over. -Shrinking Primer – primer shrinks as it cures and the more primer that is applied, the more it will shrink. This shrinkage can result in visible sandscratches below the surface of the finish. * Excessive coats of primer-surfacer should never be applied to fill coarse scratches in any substrate -Swelling – solvents can soak into body filler. This can cause the sandscratches to swell and appear on the surface. A 2K primer-surfacer or sealer will create a barrier against strong solvents from the clearcoat, but solvents from excessive primer coats can soak into the body filler and cause swelling -Scuffing – scuffing is the form of surface preparation that is least aggressive and used to degloss the surface on a panel being refinished, or to aid in adhesion for new parts that require light abrasion before finishing. Scuffing may be done using a scuff pad, wet or dry sanding with fine grit sandpaper, or a paste-type sanding material using moderate pressure without creating scratches or breaking into the undercoat. Scuffing is sometimes done after application of primer or sealer to remove dirt nibs or small contaminants. Scuffing pads are made of a nylon-type material or a woven plastic abrasive, and are typically color-coded or labeled as coarse to ultra-fine. They may used to prepare areas for blending and are used in areas that a power sander cannot adequately access. Scuffing pads may also be used as a final sand if areas are identified in the prepared surfaces that were missed Preparing a Vehicle for Refinishing (2/3) OEM Finish Preparation -Vehicle Maker Finish and Repaired Panel Preparation – when refinishing a repaired area, the surrounding vehicle maker finish may only need to be cleaned and scuffed or sanded. A vehicle maker finish often provides a sound substrate foundation and generally does not need to be partially or completely removed in preparation for a refinish. Some vehicle maker finishes might have problems that originate from the factory, such as excessive or inadequate film build areas or lack of adhesion. When working on a vehicle maker finish, proper steps need to be taken to ensure a proper repair -Film Thickness Gauge – film thickness gauges are used to measure refinish material film thickness. Depending on the type of film thickness gauge, it may be able to measure film thicknesses on a variety of surfaces. This is because the gauge may be designed to operate on any metal or just steel (ferrous). Depending on the gauge, it may be electronic or magnetic. Film thickness gauges measure film thickness in mils or microns. One mil is 0.025 mm or 1/1,000 of an inch. One micron is 1/1,000 of one milimeter -Original Finish/Refinish – when determining if the finish is original or not, identify how much of the total finish is basecoat/clearcoat. This will help determine if there is body filler used or if the surface was previously refinished. There may instances when the vehicle has never been in a collision, but the film thickness shows that the panel has been refinished. This happens due to damage that occurred at the factory. Even though the vehicle is refinished, it still may be considered an OEM finish. Paint makers may require stripping areas that have unidentifiable refinish materials. Removing surfaces with unidentifiable materials can aid in starting with a good foundation on which topcoats can be applied. Signs that a vehicle has been refinished include overspray, different colors on inner panel areas, a poor color match, tape lines, and a different surface texture. -Maximum Film Thickness – paint makers have limits on the maximum amount of refinish material that should be applied. This thickness represents the total amount of material on the surface after all coatings have been applied. If a vehicle or panel requires being refinished, the technician needs to identify the total build on the surface of the part. Vehicle maker paint film thickness is generally between 4 to 6 mils. If film thickness is 11 mils and an additional 5 mils are required to achieve the proper refinishing, the originally 11 mils of surface thickness needs to be reduced to about 5 or 6 mils before refinishing can begin -Identifying Fillers – fillers can be identified in the refinishing area using a film thickness gauge. If it is determined that the refinish must be removed over the area of filler, special care must be taken to not gouge the surface of the filler -E-Coat Considerations – some parts are shipped with E-coat already applied. Perform a solvent test on parts shipped with an E-coat to verify that the E-coat is sound and not just a shipping coating. Solvent testing will also identify whether or not a substrate will hold up to reducing agents that will be applied to the refinish area -Preparing an E-Coated Surface – you must use soap and water to prepare a topcoat application that has an E-coated surface. You should also clean the area using a wax and grease remover, and by scuffing the surface of the E-coat to aid in adhesion of the topcoats. Blow and tack the surface with clean compressed air and a tack cloth, then apply sealer. Some refinish systems allow a sealer to be applied directly to the E-coat without scuffing or sanding. This will allow for the scuffing and priming step to be skipped -Featheredge Process- to create a featheredge, sand until about 6 to 10 mm of each layer of coating is removed and can be seen. This will level out any harsh lines around the repair area including minor scratches and nicks. The featheredge should look like a bird’s feather, with soft, smooth edges. -Body Filler – begin sanding over the repaired area and work outward. When you should featheredge during the repair process is a matter of personal preference. You should sand the body filler with P180 grit as a starting point. The surface needs to be leveled with a primer-surfacer and then block-sanded smooth. Areas that will not be covered with primer-surfacer should only be scuffed or sanded using P500 grit and sanding material -Sanding Process – use a power sander with a flexible backing pad, place the sander flat against the surface and taper the paint layers by moving the sander in a cross-cutting pattern. Stop frequently to feel for rough edges -Identifying Uncured Finishes – the need to identify uncured finishes is important. An uncured finish will be weak and not resilient to abuse. An uncured finish may also feel soft and pliable, almost rubbery. The finish may chip or scratch easily. * A featheredge over an uncured finish may result in lifting during the sanding process or lifting during the refinish application New Part Preparation -Bare Steel Preparation – when working with bare steel parts, the refinish technician must determine whether or not a part is galvanized, and if a metal cleaner or conversion coating, or a self-etching primer, is required. -Refinishing Products – refinishing products that can be applied to bare metal substrates include -Metal preparation/conversion coatings. These coatings may be used before epoxy primer, but are generally not used with self-etching primer or over body filler -Zinc chromate/phosphate primers, which cover the substrate, but do not etch into the surface -Direct-to-metal or etching primers -Epoxy primers, urethane -2K primers that are easy to sand, provide high build, and have minimal shrinkage. A sealer may not be required -* Waterborne primers should not be applied to bare metal substrates -Prep for Refinishing – preparing a steel part for refinish will typically include cleaning the part and adjacent areas using wax and grease remover. It will also include scuffing or sanding the part with the recommended grit when required for proper adhesion. Be sure not to perforate any galvanized coatings. Some cleaners have an abrasive in them that aids in surface preparation. Clean the part again using wax and grease remover, then apply a corrosion-resistant primer. If using an epoxy primer, a metal conditioner and conversion coating may be required -Aluminum Preparation – the preparation steps for a bare or repaired aluminum part are similar to bare and repaired steel. * Do not use refinish tools and sanding materials on steel and then on aluminum. Cross contamination from steel particles can result in galvanic corrosion in aluminum, which will also cause premature metal damage and paint failure -Working with Aluminum – when working with aluminum, do not use grits coarser than 80. Aluminum scratches very easily and can be difficult to mask due to the softness of aluminum. Clean bare metal surfaces with soap and water. This will remove any water-soluble contaminants. Follow this with the use of wax and grease remover. These two steps will ensure a clean part with no contaminants. Apply a metal cleaner and conversion coating made specifically for aluminum, if required -Preparing Aluminum for Refinishing – when preparing aluminum parts for refinishing, remove the oxide by sanding or abrading the surface. Uncoated aluminum needs to be recleaned. The rate at which aluminum oxidizes varies depending on temperature and humidity levels. After being left uncoated for an extended period of time, the aluminum creates an aluminum oxide coating that needs to be removed. * The oxide layer will begin to reform almost instantly after the part is sanded -Preparation Considerations – refinishing preparation considerations include: Specific expelling or etching primers that may be required by the vehicle maker or refinish product maker. Aluminum panels must be kept free from contact with steel particles when the aluminum is bare. Steel particles that embed into the panel will react with the aluminum and cause surface imperfections Preparing a Vehicle for Refinishing ( 3 /3) Plastics Preparation -Plastic and Composite Refinishing Preparation – when refinishing plastic parts, considerations include -Identifying the type of plastic material use. Categories include rigid vs flexible -Paint maker recommendations for plastic cleaners or other preparation steps -Determining if an adhesion promoter must be applied to the surface before coating applique. Adhesion promoters are typically required on polyolefin plastics -Materials for Plastics – special materials for refinishing plastics may include -Special sealer designed for plastic that may also serve as an adhesion promoter -Adhesion promoters, which are continually being updated -Flex additive, which gives some clearcoats more elasticity. Some clearcoats are quite rigid and would crack if flex additive was not used in the mix -* Some clearcoats should not be applied to plastics -Orientation and Static Electricity – depending upon the application, refinishing a part off of the vehicle may require it to be positioned in the same orientation as it will be on the vehicle. For example, when refinishing a bumper cover that is off of the vehicle, placing the bumper in a horizontal position, as it will be on the vehicle, will aid with color match where the bumper meets the quarter panel. It can also be helpful to ground the vehicle and part being refinished off of the vehicle. Additional antistatic options may include antistatic spray, which is a spray mixture of deionized water and alcohol used in place of wax and grease remover, or an antistatic gun, which blows deionized air on the surface, preventing static buildup -Surface Considerations for Plastics – before starting the refinishing process on a plastic part, there are certain things to consider -Primer – whether or not a part has vehicle maker primer applied to it is one thing you should consider. A primed plastic part will typically not require an adhesion promoter. Check the part by performing a solvent test. You should then wipe a hidden area with reducer. Do not do this on the backside of the part, as the backside is often bare plastic. If color shows up on the cloth, it is likely coming from a shipping coating or inferior primer that must be removed completely before refinishing -Ready for Primer – when the part is ready for primer, it is a good idea to remove the fuzzy plastic residue created by sanding. One way to do this is to block sand the fuzzy plastic with 320 grit. Next, blow off or wipe the area with a dry rag. Vacuum the area and check it again for any pinholes or deep scratches that need to be filled. -Type of Plastic – the type of plastic the part is made of, if not primed, should be considered. Certain types of non-primed plastic may require a specific adhesion promoter. One source for identifying the type of plastic you are working with is the ISO code typically found on the backside of bumper covers -Texture – whether the part is textured or smooth is another consideration. This may affect how the part is prepared for refinishing. The number of coats of material applied may affect the appearance. Applying too many coats of material to a textured part may cause it to appear smoother than it should -Paint Cracking – another consideration is if there is any paint cracking (spider-webbing). This requires the finish to be sanded with a power sander until all the cracking is removed. If this is not done, the cracking will show up again after refinishing -Rigid Plastics – rigid plastic parts may include -Sheet-molded compounds (SMC) -Fiberglass -Polycarbonates -* Before refinishing plastic, the refinish technician should determine if the refinish material will require the use of a flex additive, and understand that the part may contain fibers that can wick cleaners and solvent into the surface and not be released -Flexible Plastics – flexible plastic parts may include -Polyamide -Polyurethane -Thermo-polyurethane -* Some common parts include bumper covers and body trim. Before any work or refinishing of these products can begin, determine if the refinish material will require flex additive, and the specific preparation steps. An interior part may get prepped differently from an exterior part -Cleaning Plastic Parts – there are certain considerations to observe when cleaning plastic parts. -Mold Release Agent – mold release agents must be removed from unprimed, or bare, plastic parts. This mold release agent can cause adhesion issues or peeling if the part is not thoroughly cleaned. Most paint makers only require cleaning and scuffing to remove the mold release agents. The recommendation for pre-baking unprimed bumper covers to remove the mold release agents is typically not required. This is because most replacement bumper cover makers now use water-based mold release agents during the manufacturing process.
