Always follow the surface preparation instructions for the paint system and the substrate being refinished to ensure the paint maker warranty is maintained -Soap and Water – use soap and water, followed by a plastic cleaner -Solvents – only use solvents designed for bare plastic, as these are less likely to be absorbed into the plastic, causing it to swell. It is also important to wipe the plastic dry immediately after application -Primer and Antistatic Cleaner – it may be a good idea to test the cleaning product on the backside of the part or an unseen area to make sure it will not remove the primer. The final step is to wipe the entire panel with an antistatic cleaner. Some paint makers provide a specified product for plastic parts that is both a plastic cleaner and antistatic cleaner -Advanced Driver Assist Systems (ADAS) – if the vehicle is equipped with an advanced driver assist system, such as adaptive cruise control or parallel park assist, the OEM may not allow repairs to or in front of the sensors, as it may impede the sensor’s ability to function. Typically, the only repair that can be done is refinishing, and that can only be done once because excessive film thickness can affect the sensors. * Review manufacturer service information to confirm their position on repairing or refinishing bumper covers on vehicles equipped with any of these systems Processing Vehicle Salvage Determine Salvage Value -It’s Still Worth Something – while it may not be cost-effective to repair a vehicle that has been in a collision, it still retains some salvage value. -Valuation – when a vehicle is damaged beyond repair, auto insurance companies will declare that vehicle is a total loss. Each insurance company has its own standard operating procedure (SOP) to determine the salvage value of a vehicle. Insurance companies will determine a vehicle’s salvage value through a variety of methods, such as: -Salvage bids from local salvage buyers -Online tools that provide an estimated salvage value -Salvage auctions -Obtaining Salvage Quotes – in many cases salvage value is determined by contacting local salvage buyers to obtain quotes or bids. Sending photographs of the salvage vehicle with details about the specific damage to that vehicle is often required. This will provide the potential buyer the information necessary to make an educated decision on their quote or bid. In cases where a salvage vehicle has a higher value, or for specialty vehicles, the salvage buyer may choose to physically inspect the vehicle prior to providing a quote or bid. -Software and Third Party Databases – online vendors are another resource available to help determine salvage value. These vendors use historical salvage sales information to provide an estimated value of the salvage. When using this type of tool, it will most likely be necessary to provide additional information about the vehicle including the: -Year, make, and model information -Vehicle mileage -Vehicle option information, such as engine and trim packages -Location of the vehicle damage -Total value of estimated damages -Salvage Auctions – in many cases, insurance companies will choose to utilize a salvage auction to sell all of their salvage. When it occurs, the vehicle is auctioned off to the highest bidder, establishing the salvage value Complete Salvage Process -Salvage Value Determining Factors – there are some additional factors that can have a significant impact on salvage values -Overall Vehicle Condition – it is important to closely inspect vehicles that are considered a total loss to determine the overall vehicle condition. Pre-existing damage to the vehicle’s body panels and/or the interior, as well as any obvious mechanical damage can have a significant impact on salvage value -Factory Options and Aftermarket Add-Ons – in addition, it is important to pay close attention to identify any aftermarket equipment installed on the vehicle. For example, aftermarket wheels, running boards, drivelines upgrades, or additional factory options, such as entertainment systems and towing packages, can have a significant impact on salvage value. Note: Some insurance companies may utilize a salvage vehicle check-list to help highlight factory options, aftermarket equipment, and areas of the vehicle that are undamaged to help ensure the best possible returns on salvage Determining the Disposition of Salvage -Determine Disposition of Salvage – when a vehicle is considered a total loss, the vehicle owner, depending on prevailing laws, may have more than one option to consider. -Insurance Company Retains Salvage – the first option is to collect a settlement check from the insurance company in exchange for the salvage vehicle. This settlement would reflect the value of the vehicle less any applicable fees, such as a deductible or prior unrelated damage. When exercising this option, the title of the vehicle is transferred to the insurance company so the vehicle can be sold for salvage. -Owner Retains Salvage – the second option is for the owner to retain their vehicle and collect a settlement check from their insurance company for the established market value. This settlement would reflect the value of the vehicle less any applicate fees, such as a deductible, prior unrelated damage, and the established salvage value. When choosing this option, the owner is essentially buying the vehicle back from the insurance company. -State Laws and Guidelines – a state’s laws may not allow a vehicle owner to retain the salvage vehicle, or they may establish buy-back age requirements and/or other specifics about the vehicle’s drivability. If the state does allow the driver to repurchase the vehicle, it may require the insurance company to have the vehicle retitled as a salvage or rebuilt vehicle. Each state has different laws pertaining to a vehicle owner’s repurchasing a vehicle as salvage. The owner will need to consult with their financial advisor or attorney about the specific laws in their state. Recycled Parts Considerations (1/2) Recycled Parts Overview -Recycling Industry – to keep up with the increasing demands placed on collision repair facilities, most recyclers have increased their professional standards to ensure better customer service and provide high quality parts to the collision industry. Many recyclers also have an updated inventory process that allows customers to search online inventories and have improved parts descriptions to ensure repairers understand the part condition before it arrives at the repair facility -Benefits of Using Recycled Parts – there are benefits to using recycled parts. One of these benefits is reducing cycle time. For example, if a new door shell is ordered, all parts must be transferred to the new part. However, if a recycled part is ordered, it may already contain the necessary parts. Another main benefit of using recycled parts is that they are still factory parts, which means the part has the: -Original factory corrosion protection -Proper fit -Proper mounting locations -Note: Note that were may be a liability concerns attached to using recycled safety items such as supplemental restraint system (SRS) parts or brake system parts -Industry Positions on Recycled parts – though the use of recycled parts is quite common in collision repair, there are differing opinions about said usage in the industry. Vehicle makers generally do not recommend the use of recycled parts. One of the primary reasons given is that there is a lack of systems in place to provide quality control. To help put customers at ease, many insurers offer a warranty on sheet metal parts that is good for as long as the customer owns the vehicle. -Industry Positions on Use of OEM Non-Deployed Airbags – there are additional industry positions on recycled parts, specifically on the use of recycled OEM non-deployed airbags. No vehicle maker recommendations their use, not even vehicle makers that typically acknowledge the use of recycled parts. Insurance companies in the United States will not include a recycled airbag on their estimate. Some industry associations, for example the Automotive Service Association (ASA), discourage the use of these types of recycled airbags. The Society for Collision Repair Specialists (SCRS) recommends that repairers use exceptional caution when performing repairs and to only use OEM parts that they know will perform with the same level of quantity as the original part, both upon installation, and for the life of the vehicle. Note: Always follow vehicle maker recommended repair procedures when replacing SRS components -Automotive Recyclers Association (ARA) – the ARA is an international trade association. The ARA has developed several programs, including the Certified Automotive Recycle (CAR) program and the Gold Seal program. The CAR program creates a set of professional standards for participating recycling organizations. The Gold Seal program focuses on customer service concerns, as well as maintaining reputable, quality business practices throughout the automotive recycling industry -Warranties from Recycling Facilities – warranties vary according to each recycling facility. Those that participate in the ARA Gold Seal program are required to offer written guarantees on their parts. The ARA also recommends body parts be warrantied for fit and corrosion protection for at least one rear. Additionally, some recyclers: -May provide a limited lifetime warranty on sheet metal parts -Warranty mechanical parts of sheet metal assemblies such as door parts -May warranty other mechanical parts for 30-90 days -Note: Warranties typically include language about the recycler not being responsible for improper installation, accidental damage, or misuse Making Informed Business Decisions -Terms Used for Recycled Parts – when working with recycled parts and the recycling industry, there are common industry terms used to describe these parts, including: -Used -Like, kind, and quality (LKQ) -Salvage -Note: On the damage report, or repair estimate, it is important to note that remanufactured, rebuilt, or reconditioned parts are not listed as recycled parts -Recycled Part Usage Considerations – there are several important items to consider when making a determination to use recycled parts. For instance, proper documentation of parts is required by the repair facility. Paperwork should include copies of part invoices, as well as the vehicle identification number (VIN) of the source vehicle for each part, in addition to the make, model, and year -Warranties – the use of recycled parts should not void any warranties that would have been maintained had a new part been used. In this instance, warranties from the recycler and insurer may take the place of vehicle maker warranties -Part Condition and Durability – consider the condition of the part when it arrives. It is important to make sure the part has not been comprised by corrosion, or by steps in the removal process, that may have altered the strength of the metal, such as thinning, heating, or bending. This is because the part must be durable in the event of another collision. A part that requires extensive repair that was not previously disclosed may not be worth investing the time in compared to buying the part new -Part Availability – be sure to consider the practically of finding a recycled part, as well as the length of time it will take to be shipped to the collision repair facility. Finding a rare part may take more resources and time than available. If so, it may be necessary to explore alternative options, such as using new or aftermarket parts -OEM Recycled Parts Identification – to help ensure part fit and durability, it is important that the recycled part be a factory original (OEM) part. Check the part for consistent paint film thickness and appearance, as there should be uniform color consistency across the panel. If not, it is likely that the panel has been repaired, replaced, and/or refinished. In addition, check for the absence of VIN labels, or the presence of Replacement Department of Transportation (D-DOT) labels. The R-DOT label is placed on replacement parts, so this is an indication that the part is not factory original -Considerations for Wear Parts – if using recycled parts that are designed to wear over time, they should have equal wear from side to side. In addition, the age and milage should match the vehicle being repaired or the part being replaced. The insurance company may require the part to be replaced with a part that is the same year or newer -Setting Customer Expectations – when deciding to use recycled parts, it may be helpful to compare the cost with aftermarket and reconditioned parts when available. Customer expectations may differ, depending on the condition and age of the vehicle. Some customers may be less willing to use recycled parts on a one-year old vehicle versus a customer with a seven-year old vehicle -Differences and Similarities Between New and Recycled Parts – -It is important that the customer be informed about the similarities and differences between recycled and factory parts when making a repair decision. Some differences the customer should be aware of include that: -Recycled body parts may require minor repair -New body parts may include a factory warranty against corrosion -New mechanical parts may include a factory warranty -Similarities between new and recycled OEM parts include: -Factory corrosion protection -Proper fit -Proper mounting locations -Both parts are factory parts -Other Considerations for Recycled Parts – -There are additional items to consider when deciding to use recycled parts. For instance, there are certain mechanical parts that should not be used for vehicle repairs, which may include: -A/C parts exposed to moisture, such as an accumulator or compressor -Radiator caps -Worn suspension and brake parts -Bushings -Emission items, such as catalytic converters and charcoal canisters, which cannot be sold according to the Clean Air Act -Tie-rod ends -Sheet metal parts should also not be recycled if they: -Exhibit fire damage, cracks at spot welds or fasteners, or the damage is simply unrepairable -Show improper previous repair -Are missing mounting locations -Have pitting caused by corrosion -High-Strength Steels (HSS) – to meet federal crash and corporate average fuel economy (CAFÉ) standards, vehicle makers are using higher strength steels, which are thinner and lighter than mild steels. These HSS steels come in a variety of thicknesses and strengths. When using HSS parts, note that HSS parts, like mild steel parts, may be used for partial and complete replacement. The vehicle maker’s recommendations must be followed with regard to straightening, welding, sectioning, and complete part installation Parts removal and Ordering Considerations -Recycler and Repair Facility Communication – during the parts ordering process, there needs to be good communication. This ensures that the part being delivered by the recycling facility meets the expectations of the repair facility and that there is no confusion on parts description. Communication tools include: -Websites for online ordering at recycler sites, such as Car-Part.com -Instant messaging -Phone calls -Verify the Proper Part – when ordering recycled parts for a repair, they should match the model and model year, depending on the part. The parts may require matching a specific build date. In some instances, there may be changes in vehicle design. Whenever practical, it is the responsibility of the repair facility, before installation, to determine the usability of the recycled parts, and to ensure the parts will not compromise the repair of the vehicle -Vehicle Cutting – proper cut locations are critical for a proper repair and it is the repairer’s responsibility to determine if a specific cut location is required. Foam location may need to be identified on the request form if the repair facility wishes to preserve a foam-filled area. This may require altering the cutting location. When the repair facility is determining the cut location, the vehicle maker part replacement guidelines should be followed. Depending on the vehicle makers recommended cut line, it may be necessary to acquire two parts from a recycler, even though the repair is specific to just one part. For example, when ordering a B-pillar, the standard cut lines may be in the middle of the front and rear door openings but the shop would like the rear cut line moved back to include the dogleg. This may require purchasing the pillar and quarter panel -Parts Assemblies – Included Parts – parts that are included when ordering assemblies depend on the recycler. The decision is made based on the dismantling procedure and availability, as well as what is undamaged on the donor vehicle. For example, doors may include trim, glass, motors, and switches. However, many recyclers may inventory items separately, such as mirrors, if they are in higher demand. Dash panels may include various electronic parts depending on the removal process. Note that states may have laws on how to transfer VIN plates on recycled dash panels. Body structure assemblies may include exterior panels, interior panels, and reinforcements. Suspension parts may include the strut, coil, and brake assembly. Mechanical part inclusions also may vary. For example, gas tanks may include the fuel filter or fuel pump. What is included should be verified with the recycler -Unitized Structure Parts – recycled parts of a unitized structure are generally supplied as part of an assembly and removed from the vehicle following cut location recommendations from the repair facility. Recycled parts that are part of the unitized structure may include, but are not limited to: -Front and rear rails -A-, B-, and C-pillars -The radiator core support -Bumper reinforcements -Quarter panels -The roof panel -Note: Note that outer quarter panels with rolled hem flanges are not candidates for recycled part usage. This is due to the removal process required. Alternate removal methods will result in a work-hardened flange that may fail -Exterior Panels – exterior panels may be supplied as an assembly with all attached parts. However, the exterior panels may also require the collision repair facility to indicate which pars should be included with the panel. Exterior panels that may be used as recycled parts include: -Deck lids -Hoods -Doors -Fenders -Panoramic roofs. These may be bolted-on or bonded-on, making the part easier to remove compared to welded-on roof panels -Recycled Parts Damage Report Considerations – when including recycled parts on a damage report, consider that the parts may require disassembly for installation and refinishing. Some other considerations may include: -Cleaning -Trimming a part to size -Measuring the part to verify it is undamaged -Parts Removal and Quality Control – recycling facilities employ certain parts removal processes to ensure that the parts they supply are of good quality and can be properly installed -Parts Removal and Quality Control Process – the parts removal process is done by the recycling facility, which requires determining what is recycled off of each vehicle and which parts will be scrapped. This process also involves printing out part tags for the person who dismantles the vehicle. The tags are used to determine which parts are removed from the vehicle and inventoried. All parts must then go through a quality control process that requires. -Cleaning -Inspecting the part for damage -Categorizing the part according to the extent of damage -Making sure the part description matches the actual part condition -Communicating any discrepancies to the repair facility -Parts Inspection Process – part of the inspection process is verifying that the part has not been previously repaired and is not an aftermarket part. To check for previous repairs, a recycling facility may: -Check the panel backside -Look for corrosion, different color primer, and/or weld marks -Check paint film thickness on the panel surface -Note: A recycling facility may notify the buyer of previous repairs, whether the panel has been simply refinished or refinished and repaired. If the part is not factory, and has been deemed an aftermarket part, this should also be disclosed to the buyer -Ordering Parts – parts are typically ordered by category type, such as body, electrical/mechanical, and miscellaneous. In some instances, using a recycled assembly is preferable since it eliminates the need for having to order and assemble parts individually. In addition, the repair facility should determine if a partial assembly is available, if that is all that is needed. This may be more cost-friendly than ordering a complete assembly. Note that when ordering parts, reference the vehicle service information to determine if one-time-use fasteners need to be ordered -Part Grades- the following describes the four different ARA part grades: -A Grade – one unit of repair, less than one unit of repair, or ‘clean and undamaged.’ A non-mechanical part that is labeled as A grade may also be listed using the code 000 -B Grade – two units of repair -C Grade – more than two units of repair -X Grade – not enough data to grade the part, essentially an ungraded part -ARA Code and Panel Damage Overview – the ARA Code is a three-character code that serves as an important tool for assessing the condition of a recycled part. The first character, also called position, identifies the damage location. The second position is a letter that refers to the type of damage. There are 15 codes for identifying the various types of damage. The last position identifies the extent of the damage by measuring it in repair units. A repair unit represents damage that does not exceed surface area size similar to a standard credit card. Note: The locator sheet and damage type codes can be downloaded at the ARA website, and is a good resource for understanding the ARA parts grading system -Returning Ordered Parts – in instances where it has been decided not to use the part, the repair facility should send the part back in a similar condition as it was received. Both the repairer and recycler should be in agreement on the condition of any part that is going to be returned. Modifications made toa part may devalue it. Also, if the part is brokered, it is unlikely it can be sent back since it will be different than the original part shipped. If it has been agreed that parts can be removed from an assembly and the remaining portion returned to the recycler, adjacent panels that can be resold should not be damaged during the part removal process Recycled Parts Considerations (2/2) Recycled Parts and Installation Considerations Inspecting Received Parts -Part Inspecting During Delivery – when receiving the part at the collision repair facility, make sure the damage code matches the actual condition of the part. While this is generally done at the recycling facility, it should be done when the part is received by the repair facility as well. When inspecting the part, try to have the delivery driver present. Additionally, be mindful that variances in part descriptions may lead to returns or price negotiations. With regard to cycle time, it may be quicker to repair a part with more damage than anticipated than wait for a replacement part -Inspecting the Part Match – parts may appear the same on the outside, but have a different interior (such as a door). This may require removing the electronics, mechanical parts, and trim pieces from the damaged part and transferring them to the replacement part. Some repairers/insurers may do this as part of their repair policy. When inspecting the part: -It needs to be as good as or better than the part being replaced before it was collision damaged -The repair facility must ensure the recycled part matches the condition and integrity of the original Installing Recycled Panels and Assemblies -Installing Multi-Layered Assemblies – some assemblies, such as rocker panels, pillars, roof rails, and front and rear rails have multi-layered assemblies. This type of construction may make the installation process difficult since: -Inner panels and reinforcements may need to be accessed for joining. -Several access windows may be required on several parts of the assembly -Multi-layered construction requires using offset joints. Multiple offset joints may not be practical -Note: Welded-on parts are generally provided as part of a larger assembly. If only using a portion of the assembly, such as an outer panel, removal of spot welds will be required. This typically results in holes being drilled in the flange area of the service part. Note that this may not be allowed due to the attachment method stated in the vehicle makers repair procedure -Multi-Layered Assembly Determinations – if it has been determined that installing a multi-layered assembly is acceptable, high-strength steel (HSS) reinforcements may not have a sectioning procedure, requiring replacement at factory seams to maintain structural integrity. Sectioning should only be performed when allowed by the vehicle maker. It should also be determined where the sectioning joints can be properly protected from corrosion. In some cases, the outer panels may have to be sacrificed or removed and replaced to access inner panels for joining or sectioning -Multi-Layered Assembly Structure – installing large, welded structural assemblies, such as full-front or full-rear body sections, involves making multiple joints in multiple structural panels and reinforcements. On many vehicles, the panels in the vehicle side structure are made from high- and ultra-high-strength steels that contribute to the structural integrity and occupant safety of the vehicle. Introducing multiple sectioning joints may affect the performance of the vehicle structure during normal operation, as well as during another collision. Most vehicle makers have published warnings against doing full-body sectioning on their vehicles -Bolted-On Unitized Structure Parts and Assemblies – bolted-on unitized structure parts often have design changes from year to year, as well as during model changes. These assemblies also vary in type depending on options and powertrain configurations. When bolt-on recycled parts are being considered for use, it is essential that they are inspected to make sure there are no issues with: -VIN labels -Powertrain and suspension mounting locations and brackets -Indirect damage -Physical condition -Mid-year design changes -Vehicle warranties -Note: Bolted-on unitized structure parts may include engine cradles, crossmembers, and suspension assemblies -Aluminum Structures – aluminum vehicle structures can also be recycled. However, similar to steel, aluminum structures require following specific replacement procedures that are provided by the vehicle maker. How these parts are attached to the vehicle may differ compared to their steel counterparts. Aluminum unitized structural parts may require: -Specific fasteners and welding techniques -Drilling out various types of rivets and replacing with recommended fasteners -Note: When using vehicle makers recommendations to replace aluminum parts, the original attachment is not always duplicated Additional Parts Inspection Criteria -Recycled Glass – recycled glass includes stationary glass such as windshields and side glass, or moveable glass such as door glass. Some encapsulated glass may not be able to be removed without destroying the encapsulation. Whether or not the glass can be reused once removed is indicated in the service information. When ordering a windshield, note that there are various glass configurations and designs based on different vehicle options, for example, advanced driver assist systems (ADAS). When inspecting glass for use, make sure to avoid glass that has: -Visible damage -Delamination -Improper tint or shade -Note: Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify the glass replacement requirements and system calibrations that are required prior to starting glass repairs -Mechanical Parts – commonly recycled mechanical parts include parts related to the following systems: -Air conditioning -Heating/cooling -Suspension -Powertrain -Note: When inspecting recycled mechanical parts, keep in mind that some may need to go through a remanufacturing process -Engines – while specific procedures may vary according to recycling facility standard operating procedures (SOPs), recycled engines are typically cleaned and quality checked, including testing for carbon, oil pressure, and compression -Halfshafts and Driveshafts – -When inspecting halfshafts for use, do not install halfshafts with: -Evidence of heating, welding, or damage -A damaged or misshapened boot -Evidence of CV-joint contamination -On driveshafts, do not install parts with: -Damage, evidence of repair, or corrosion -Any play between the driveshaft and the U-joints -Runout exceeding the vehicle makers specification -Note: Also, be sure to replace U-joints on recycled driveshafts -Exhaust Systems to Avoid – when inspecting exhaust system parts, do not use parts that have: -Excessive corrosion -Flow restrictions -Damaged or missing mounting points -Physical damage -Note: In addition, be sure not to use recycled gaskets or catalytic converters -Inspecting Anti-Lock Brake System (ABS) Parts – when utilizing recycled anti-lock braking system (ABS) parts for installation, remember that parts cannot be flood damaged, and any parts that come in contact with brake fluid need to have been sealed after removal. Additionally, parts taken from the donor vehicle should come from the side away from the area of direct damage. All parts should be tested for proper operation following installation -Suspension Parts – when using recycled suspension parts, compare to the recycled part to an undamaged part to verify that it has the proper dimensions and is the correct part. Inspect for bends or cracks, and use dye penetrant if necessary. Do not install the part if there is evidence of damage, previous repairs, or any signs of heating, welding, or straightening. Also, make sure the suspension part has similar wear to the suspension on the opposite side of the vehicle -Control Modules – when using recycled control modules, it is important to confirm with the recycler that they were tested for proper operation before the vehicle was dismantled. Remember to confirm proper operation of all systems being monitored or controlled by the module being replaced. Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify required service, testing, and calibration procedures. Note that not all system malfunctions will turn on a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL. Therefore, use of a scan tool that is capable of performing the necessary service, testing, and calibration may be required -Wiring Harnesses – depending on the recycling facility, wiring harnesses may not be inventoried. Usually wiring harnesses will stay with the vehicle in the yard and are removed upon request by the customer. Some recycling facilities may include restraint system wiring harnesses. However, if there is damage to the harness, some vehicle makers do not allow repair. Others have specific kits that must be used to make repairs. Some vehicle makers allow only one splice per section of wire. If the recycled wire harness has minor damage, note that wire harness repair options vary according to the vehicle maker and the type of electronic system. Also, note that wire damage on the pigtail of control modules should not be repaired Refinishing Overview Finish Types -Refinishing Overview – one of the final stages of the collision repair process is to refinish the damaged area of the vehicle. The refinishing department is most often identified by the spraybooth and an adjacent prep deck. The refinishing department receives the vehicle from the non-structural or structural repair department, and performs the refinishing operations such as masking, scuffing, sanding, and applying primers and topcoats. The refinish: -Will be the first item the customer inspects when they pick up the vehicle from the collision repair facility -Requires a proper color match and no finish defects -Types of OEM Finishes – there are single-stage, basecoat/clearcoat, tri-coat, and multi-stage finishes, depending on the number of finish layers above the primer coating. Single-stage finishes, which include just a colored finish coat as the final coating layer, are used on utility vehicles where appearance is not an issue. Basecoat/clearcoat (two-stage) finishes, which have a colored basecoat, followed by a clearcoat, are common on passenger vehicles. Tri-coat (three-stage) finishes, which add a translucent midcoat between the basecoat and clearcoat, are found on luxury vehicles. Multi-stage (four-stage) finishes, which add a midcoat and tinted clearcoat between the basecoat and clearcoat, are only found on a select few luxury vehicles -Single-Stage and Basecoat/Clearcoat – -Single-stage refinishing coats are a one-step color coat designed to dry to a glossy finish. There is no clear topcoat applied. This type of finish has limited use. Basecoat material is the color coat in a finish coat application process and is made up of color pigments to match various vehicle colors. Basecoats are: -Not designed to provide a gloss appearance -Followed by an application of clearcoat -Either a solid color, metallic, or mica -Clearcoat is the final protective layer in a two-, three-, or four-stage topcoat system. Clearcoats are used to: -Provide basecoat protection against the sun’s ultraviolet (UV) rays. This protection keeps the basecoat from delaminating, peeling, and turning hazy -Protect against abrasions and damage to the basecoat -Enhance the appearance and color of the basecoat -Tri-Coat and Multi-Stage – tri-coat and multi-stage finishes utilize one or more midcoats between the basecoat and the clearcoat. The mid-coats are typically translucent colors, which allow light to show through, providing depth to the finish. These midcoats may also contain mica, which is the additive used to create pearl coatings. It adds a metallic sheen to the appearance of a finish. Multi-stage finishes consist of a basecoat, two or more tinted midcoats, and clearcoat, providing even more depth than a tri-coat system -Identifying Finish Type – the paint code is a label that identifies the color that was originally applied to the vehicle. Paint code label location varies depending on vehicle make and model. Some locations include: -Inside the glove box -On door pillars -Inside the trunk -Note: Label location information can be found in estimating guides, repair guides, and paint maker chip guides. Paint codes are used by the refinishing technician to mix the proper color formula, for which there can be multiple variants -Terminology – this section covers some of the more common terms used during the refinishing process -Jambing/Edging – jambing, or edging, is refinishing edges such as doorjambs and panel backsides. Jambing may be done before some parts are bolted or welded on the vehicle. It is typically done on areas that cannot be easily accessed or refinished after they are installed -Spot Repair – a spot repair is generally repairing a small area. It begins by making the cosmetic repair, cleaning the surface, priming, and applying topcoats to a small area -Panel Refinishing – panel refinishing or a panel repair is the refinishing of a complete part that is separated by a panel break or feature line. Panel refinishing requires that surrounding panels be either masked or blended into, depending on the difficulty of color match -Featheredge – featheredging is used to gradually taper out the repair area so there is a smooth area with no lines that can show through a finish. A featheredge is made by sanding to taper the repair area. It is so-called because the transition between the different layers is smooth without ridges, like a feather. The featheredge is started over the repair area and worked outward, revealing a small part of each coating layer -Tinting – the color mixed from the paint code does not always match the vehicle color. Tinting is changing the appearance of a color by adding small amounts of different toners to the original formula. The object of tinting is to bring the mixed color closer to matching the vehicle. Tinting is not done with every repair. Tinting should only be done as a last resort -Refinishing Process Overview – there are multiple steps involved in the refinishing process -Masking – masking is the application of paper or plastic material and masking tape to cover areas of the vehicle that will not be refinishing. This protects these areas from overspray. There is also a water-based liquid masking available that is sprayed on the surface and washed off when refinishing operations are complete -Sanding – sanding levels minor imperfections in the surface. Sanding can be done by hand or with a machine. There are many types of sanding grits available, ranging from coarse to very fine, depending on the grading system used. Sanding is done dry or wet. Wet sanding requires sandpaper designed for wet sanding. The surface is kept constantly wet while sanding. Some product makers require soaking the sandpaper before use -Scuffing – scuffing is the least aggressive form of surface preparation. Scuffing is done without creating scratches or breaking into the underlying coat. It is used, for example, to remove the gloss from the finish. Scuffing may be done using a woven plastic scuff pad, fine-grit wet or dry sanding paper, or a paste-type sanding material. Scuffing pads are typically not identified by grit, but will be color-coded or labeled as coarse to ultra-fine -Priming – priming may be done to provide a corrosion-resistant barrier, or to fill minor surface imperfections -Drying, Curing, and Baking – after refinish materials are applied, there are two processes that occur, drying and curing. Drying is the complete evaporation of solvents and liquids from the refinish material. Curing is when a chemical change occurs and the material hardens. Baking is the process of accelerating the drying and curing time in a spraybooth. During the bake cycle, the temperature inside the spraybooth is increased, thus the term “baking” -Blending Overview – blending is often done when applying basecoats because panel-to-panel matches are nearly impossible with many colors. Blending helps achieve the best possible color match by tricking the eye into seeing one color. Any slight visible differences between the original finish and the refinish are disguised because some of the original finish shows through the refinish color -Purpose – blending the basecoat: -Extends the basecoat color into an area or panel adjacent to the repair -Allows some of the original vehicle color to show through the refinish color. The refinish color does not have to be an exact match, and likely will not be. It just has to be a blendable match -Can help disguise slight color differences that would be apparent if panel-painting to an edge -Limitations – blending does have limitations. For example: -Successful blends are very dependent on using good spray gun control and spray technique -Blending will not hide a severe color mismatch. Blending refinish colors that are not blendable may cause mismatch problems, for example, when looking from the front end to the rear end of the vehicle -Blending can never take the place of tinting to correct for an unacceptable color match. It is also true that tinting cannot take the place of blending. There are reasons for doing each process, and one cannot substitute for the other -Preparing Parts for Paint – when preparing to refinish parts or panels, there are some things to consider -Previously Refinished Areas – previously refinished areas may: -Have multiple layers of material built up -Be dry, but not completely cured -Have poor adhesion qualities -Require partial finish removal because of excessive film thickness or improper material application -Have an acceptable film thickness that will allow topcoat application -Note: When refinishing a panel that was previously refinished, it may be helpful to know what paint system was used, if possible -Products Over Bare Steel – when refinishing over bare steel, there may be some additional products that need to be considered include: -Metal preparation/conversion coatings. These coatings may be used before epoxy primer, but are generally not used with self-etching primer or body filler -Zinc chromate/phosphate primers, which cover the substrate, but do not etch into the surface -Direct-to-metal or etching primers -Epoxy primers -Urethane and 2K primers that are easy to sand, provide high build, and have minimal shrinkage. A sealer may not be required if these primers are used -Undercoat Considerations – undercoats, such as primer-surfacers and sealers, may be tinted or may be tintable with single-stage color. Colored undercoats: -Can be used to improve a color match in translucent basecoats. If the undercoat color is not considered during refinishing, it may be difficult to match the vehicle color -Can be used to improve a poor hiding characteristic of some basecoats -Should be used when recommended by the paint or vehicle maker -Note: The vehicle maker may have used a tinted undercoat. Undercoat color can be determined by reading the featheredges -Plastic Refinishing Considerations – when refinishing plastic parts, considerations include: -Identifying the type of plastic material used. Categories include rigid vs. flexible. -Paint maker recommendations for plastic cleaners or other preparation steps -Determining if an adhesion promoter must be applied to the surface before coating application. Adhesion promoters are typically required on polyolefin -Rigid Plastics – rigid plastic parts may include: -Sheet-molded compounds (SMC) -Fiberglass -Polycarbonate -Note: Before refinishing plastic, the refinish technician should determine if the refinish material will require the use of a flex additive -Flexible Plastics – -Flexible plastic parts may include: -Polyamide -Polyurethane -Thermo-polyurethane -Some Common parts include bumper covers and body trim. Before any work or refinishing of these products can begin, determine: -If the refinish material will require flex additive -The preparation steps. An interior part may be prepared differently from an exterior part -Paint Maker Warranties – paint makers generally have warranties addressing the durability of any refinish. However, the refinish must be performed to their standards by a qualified repair facility. The terms of the warranty vary by company, some lasting for as long as the customer owns the vehicle. The warranty covers issues such as: -Peeling or delamination -Cracking or checking -Loss of gloss -Fading or chalking Refinishing -Seam Sealers – seam sealer are applied to seams, such as a pinchweld. -Purpose – the purpose of seam sealers it to: -Reduces the chances of corrosion in a flange and in joints. The purpose is NOT to fill in gaps or poorly fitted joints, but to complete the seal on the joint -Seal out moisture and contaminants from these joining areas -Stop water and air leaks -Prevent fumes from entering the passenger compartment -Reduces wind and road noise -Note: Seam sealers also add to the appearance of the seam -Locations of Use – seam sealers may be used: -On a welded seam where leakage could be an issue -On closure panel openings -Around lamps -In the engine compartment, including on the cowl, around strut towers, and the apron-to-lower rail joint. Any seam sealer will work for underhood applications. Epoxy-, urethane-, and solvent-based seam sealers are all resistant to gasoline, oil, and other fluids and can take underhood temperatures -On floor pans -On quarter panels -Around the fuel filler neck -Mixing of Refinish Color – the refinish color must be mixed using a formula that matches and the paint code. Multiple colored toners are used for custom mixing of thousands of colors in any amount needed to achieve the proper match with the existing finish -Sprayout Panels – making sprayout panels is an important step in determining if a refinish color is going to achieve a blendable color match, as it helps determine when the refinishing technician has a color that is a good match. It also shows how many coats of color are needed to achieve proper coverage. The panel must be made duplicating the actual spraying conditions and technique that will be used on the vehicle -Corrosion Protection – this section discusses anti-corrosion compound material -Anti-Corrosion Compound -anti-corrosion compound material: -Is typically a wax-based coating, though a coating using thin-film technology is not wax-based -Is called several names by product makers, including anti-rust agent, rust preventer, etc. -Is applied as a fine mist with a wand attached to a special spray gun or an aerosol can -Has self-healing capabilities -Location – anti-corrosion compound is typically applied inside non-visible areas, such as inside pillars, rocker panels, and rails. Most anti-corrosion compound has creeping capabilities, which allows it to penetrate pinchweld flanges, such as on rail flanges and the lower rocker panel. Anti-corrosion compound is also applied in the bottom perimeter of door shell cavities, on the backside of panel repairs, and aluminum cavities, not just steel. One vehicle maker, for example, states in their service information that after all straightening or welding work on aluminum parts, the cavities affected must be sealed with a cavity protection agent after being painted. Another vehicle maker, in the service information, states to treat all interior surfaces that have been disturbed by repairs. This includes all box members, cavities, and door interiors -Undercoating Material Overview – undercoating material: -Is a thick tar- oy synthetic rubber-based material -Is applied to wheelhouse and underbody areas, as recommended by the vehicle maker. The bottom of the fuel tank is another area commonly recommended for undercoating application -Protects against abrasion from stone chips and other road debris, and corrosion -May solvent- or water-based. Solvent-based undercoating may have some wax to keep the material flexible in cold temperatures -Can be applied over primer. Some can be applied over bare steel. Check the product maker recommendations -Has varying thicknesses, colors, and consistencies to match to match the OEM and aftermarket appearance -Undercoating Material Characteristics – characteristics of undercoating generally include: -Resistance to road splash -Resistance to oils and solvents -An ability to creep into hard-to-reach areas. Hard-to-reach areas include flanges and under inserts. The material should be sprayed at the correct viscosity to allow it to flow through the spray wand with the proper atomization for thorough coverage -Self-healing. If the material is scratched, some products will flow back into the scratched area -Serving as a sound deadener -Custom Finish Overview – custom painting is the process of applying a unique finish to a vehicle, one that is different from the standard finish that was applied by the vehicle maker. If you encounter a custom finish, it is always a good idea to consult with your paint staff and/or management to determine the best course of action Tools and Equipment -Tools and Equipment – in this section, we will cover the main tools and equipment used during refinishing operations -Spraybooth Functions – a spraybooth provides: -A clean, airtight spray environment -The ability to control the temperatures for spraying and baking -Limited exposure to hazardous chemicals -Fire protection -Color-corrected lighting for color matching -Prep Deck – prep decks may appear similar in appearance to a spraybooth, the difference being that a prep deck typically has curtains and not solid walls. Prep decks may: -Be used as a sanding station -Allow refinish materials to be applied -Be heated -Be used for edging parts -Be regulated by local rules -Note: Local or national fire codes may regulate the types of duties that may be performed inside a prep deck -Mixing Room – mixing rooms contain the materials and equipment needed for refinish operations. The paint mixing room is designed to: -Be self-ventilating -Contain any spills that occur -Meet local fire codes -Be grounded to avoid sparks and explosions -Have explosion-proof wiring -Spray Gun Washer – spray gun washers are used for the cleaning of spray equipment. The spray gun washer on the left is used for waterborne refinish materials, while the one on the right is used for solvent-borne refinish materials -Used Paint, Solvent Containment, and Solvent Recyclers – used material containers are designed for holding small amounts of waste for a short period of time. These containers may also: -Contain a double strainer for collecting material -Hold used material until placing into larger holding drums -Note: When using a used solvent container, it is a safe practice to use a system that has a cover and keeps fumes from escaping. If an uncovered container is used, the fumes can cause a fire and health hazard Reliable Automotive Class in shop 4/6/2023 Stud welding on aluminum and steel -Vehicles have such as Audi. -This is not stud weld puller this is the studs on rear body panel for Audi -99% of dents on aluminum are microcracked -Do attemptive repair -Very hard to cold pull aluminum -need 1mm on the attachment otherwise it wont be correct 233680 140335 0 0 127635 193675 0 0 648335 298450 0 0 287020 272415 0 0 -The screw stops it from turning – just needs to be snug – do not over tighten 595630 287655 0 0 127635 213360 0 0 803275 -259080 0 0 233680 332740 0 0 -Top right of this attachment is the stopper so you can set this on uneven terrain/angle. Restraint System -Airbag Module – is a passive restraint that activates when a vehicle is involved in a collision. -Clock Spring – is a spiral-wound rotary electrical connector, located between the steering wheel and steering column, which allows the steering wheel to turn while maintaining an electrical connection with the airbag, horn, and other devices. -Collapsible Steering Column – An energy absorbing, or collapsing, steering column is designed to collapse during a collision to reduce driver injury. -Upper Column – Steering columns may be designed to collapse in the upper column. Some upper columns have two shafts that are designed to function similarly to a telescope during a collision. This type of design uses a solid shaft that compresses into a hollow shaft to shorten the overall length of the steering column. The shafts may be locked with plastic inserts that shear off upon impact. Upper steering columns may also be designed with steel mesh or accordion-like convolutions that collapse under collision forces. -Lower Steering Sections – The steering column may also be designed to collapse in the lower steering sections. These sections may be linked by offset universal joints to allow the sections to fold during an impact. This keeps the lower steering column from entering the passenger compartment. -Mounting Bracket – The steering column may be designed to collapse at the steering column mounting brackets. Some steering columns are designed to collapse at the bracket that holds the column to the instrument panel frame. This may also be done with plastic inserts that shear upon impact. -Deploying Restraints – Deployment of the restraints may be based on the perceived severity of the collision, deceleration rate, seat belt use, occupant position, and passenger occupant weight. Following deployment, the Restraint Control Module (RCM) may also communicate with the Body Control Module (BCM) to turn on the interior lights and unlock the doors. The Restraint Control Module (RCM) may also initiate a call to 911 through systems like OnStar. -Dual Stage Airbag – The function of a dual stage airbag is to deploy at a reduced force compared to a single stage airbag. It requires two separate igniters and wire harness connectors. This allows the restraint system ECM to deploy whichever inflator is required depending on the crash parameters. Depending on the vehicle maker’s design and programmed crash parameters, dual stage airbags may activate either as a single, reduced-charge deployment, or from different ignitors simultaneously or staggered. Identifying Dual Stage Airbag can be done by referencing vehicle marker service information and looking for two igniter connections on the backside of the airbag module. -Ignitors and Inflators – the Ignitor receives a signal from the Restraint Control Module (RCM) , which then initiates the airbag Inflator -Occupant Classification System (OCS) – is used to determine if the front passenger restraints should be activated. The OCS uses sensors and a control module to provide information directly to the Restraint Control Module (RCM), which controls the Safety Restraint System (SRS). -OCS Weight Sensors – various methods are used to determine front passenger occupant weight for the OCS, including a silicone-filled pressure bladder, a sensor mat, or strain gauges. Always refer to the vehicle maker service information to determine the type of system, mandatory replacement items, and calibration requirements before repairing any OCS. An OCS may also use various other sensors. These include the – Seat belt-use sensor to determine if the seat belt is in use, Seat-position sensor to determine how close the occupant is to the airbag, Status lamp on the instrument panel or rearview mirror to communicate the status of the passenger airbag. -OCS Control Module- An OCS will typically include a separate control module that communicates with the RCM. It may be located under the front passenger seat or under the center console. -Impact and Safing Sensors – Restraint system sensors include front, side, and safing or arming sensors. These sensors monitor vehicle conditions to detect sudden deceleration in a collision. The sensor then sends a signal to the restraint system control module (RCM) to determine if an airbag should be deployed. -Front Impact Sensors- detect front collisions and are located on the front portion of the vehicle. Front Impact Sensor locations may include – On the front lower rails, On the radiator core support, Behind the headlamps, On the front frame rail crossmember. -Side Impact Sensors – located on both sides of the vehicle, are designed to detect side impact, and may be mounted in doors, on B and C pillar, behind or under seats, on the rear wheelhouse. Some vehicles use a pressure-based side impact sensor, located in the doors. These types of sensors are designed to detect a quick increase of pressure inside the door cavity during a side impact. These types of side impacts have a faster response time, but require other impact sensors located in other areas in case the door is not impacted during a collision. -Safing or Arming Sensor – is a secondary sensor that must close with the front or side impact sensors to confirm that a collision is taking place. This is done to prevent an accidental deployment caused by a problem in the front impact sensor circuits. Safing or Arming sensors may be located inside the Restraint Control Module (RCM) or as a separate sensor in the passenger compartment. Some systems use three separate sensors to determine airbag deployment. -Knee Bolsters and Knee Bolster Airbags – The knee bolster is designed into the lower portion of the instrument panel. It may provide an extra cushioned area to protect the knees and prevent sliding under the instrument panel during a collision. -Seat Airbag – require removal of the seat to replace deployed side airbags. -Seat Position Sensor (SPS) – determines the distance between the steering wheel and the driver -Side Airbag – deployment usually requires replacement of – side airbag module, side-impact sensor, any damaged wiring, damaged seat parts on vehicles with a seat mounted side airbag. Some vehicle manufacturers require replacement of the entire seat assembly following a deployment. -System Faults – Most Restraint Control Module (RCM) perform a self-diagnostic test when the ignition key is turned on. If the RCM is not working properly, or if it detects a problem with the restraint system, the airbag warning light will illuminate and remain lit until the problem is corrected. An airbag warning lamp that does not illuminate during the initial bulb check sequence may also indicate a problem. -Restraint Control Module (RCM) – processes crash data to determine which restraints should be deployed. RCM is considered the brains behind the Supplemental Restraint System (SRS). Functions of the RCM include – deploying restraints, checking for system faults, processing information from various input sources, storing information from the collision, storing energy to deploy restraints if battery power is lost during a collision. The mounting location of the RCM may include, but are not limited to – under the lower center position of the instrument panel, under the center console – between the front seats, under a seat, behind a kick panel. -Restraint System Servicing – damaged or deployed restraint systems almost always set a diagnostic trouble code (DTC). However, it is important to remember not all DTC’s will turn on a Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL). Therefore, the first steps when servicing vehicle restraint systems should be performing a system scan. This will allow you to retrieve stored DTC’s, perform system diagnostics, and may help identify damaged system components that may not have visible signs of damage. Restraint Systems Operation (1/2) Seat Belt and Roll Bar Operation Overview -Vehicle Restraint Systems – Vehicles equipped with various mechanical and electronic restraint systems, which protect occupants during a collision. These systems include: -Airbags -Collapsible steering column -Headrests -Roll bars -Seats -Seat belts -Seat belt pre-tensioners -Note: The restraint systems mentioned are considered passive safety features, as they help protect the vehicle occupants from injury once a crash has already occurred. Safety features such as forward collision warning systems, anti-lock brakes, stability control, and lane departure warning systems, are active safety features, as they help to prevent collisions Seat Belts -Seat Belts – seat belts reduce the injuries to vehicle occupants resulting from impact with interior items, keep the occupants positioned correctly, and prevent the occupants from being ejected from the vehicle, in the event of a crash. A vehicle maker may require replacement of all seat belts that were in use during an airbag deployment or collision. Always reference vehicle maker service information to identify service requirements following a collision -Importance of Seat Belts – to understand the important of seat belts in the safety restraint system, consider that an occupant in a vehicle is traveling at the same speed as the vehicle. When the vehicle suddenly stops due to a crash, the occupant continues traveling at the same rate of speed. The seat belt applies an opposing force to hold the occupant in the vehicle and positioned correctly to maximize the effectiveness of airbag systems -Seat Belt Retractors – the seat belt retractors main function is to wind the seat belt into the assembly, allowing it to be pulled out. Seat belt retractors may have other functions, such as emergency locking and automatic locking. Most retractors are designed to be used on only one side of the vehicle and are not interchangeable -Emergency Locking – most retractors have the emergency locking feature, and are referred to as emergency locking retractors (ELR). Many retractors have a pendulum that causes the webbing to lock when the webbing is extracted from the retractor quickly. The webbing may also lock when the speed of the vehicle changes abruptly or when the vehicle is on a steep upgrade or downgrade, cornering hard, or during a roll over -Automatic Locking – retractors that have the automatic locking feature are commonly referred to as automatic locking retractors (ALR). These types of retractors allow occupants and child safety seats to be held tightly in place. Once the seat belt has been fully extended and the tongue is inserted into the buckle, the ALR allows the extra seat belt webbing to retract into the retractor so that it is tight around the car seat or occupant. At this point, a bar locks into a spool with gears, preventing additional seat belt webbing from being released. Some vehicle makers refer to these as child hold-out or cinch mechanisms. Note: To disengage an ALR, unbuckle the seat belt and allow it to retract fully -Load-Limiting Capabilities – some seat belts have load-limiting capabilities that release the seat belt, at a determined rate, when it becomes locked during a collision or sudden stop. These seat belts reduce the force exerted on the occupant by absorbing some of the force created from the momentum of the occupant. Load-limiting seat belts should be inspected for signs of strain or activation. For example, check for signs of strain on the seat belt webbing and proper retractor operation. Some load limiting seat belts use a torsion bar made of relatively soft metal that can bend when this feature is activated -Head Restraints – another supplemental restraint system (SRS) component, in the area of seats and seat belts, is head restraint systems, commonly referred to as headrests. These restraint systems can be manually adjustable, contain reactive mechanical linkages, or be proactive systems that move automatically to aid in protecting occupants when a collision occurs Seat Belt Pre-Tensioner Operation -Seat Belt Pre-Tensioner Description – seat belt pre-tensioners remove seat belt slack to hold the vehicle occupants in the proper position during a collision. Generally, this systems uses restraint system sensors and the restraint control module (RCM) to determine which systems need to be deployed. For example, based on input from restraint system sensors, the RCM may determine the seat belt pre-tensioner needs to deploy, but not deploy the airbags. The RCM may also work with the occupant classification system (OCS) to determine if the front passenger restraint systems should deploy. Note: During deployment, expanding gas may be used to move mechanical parts; as depicted on screen -Types of Seat Belt Pre-Tensioners – although seat belt pre-tensioners have the same purpose, they may function differently and therefore, are specific to vehicle make, model, and position within the passenger compartment. Seat belt pre-tensioners may work by: -Winding the seat belt retractor -Shortening the seat belt buckle assembly with a piston and cable system -Shortening at the seat belt anchoring point -Deploying the seat belt pre-tensioner at the retractor and buckle assembly or anchoring point simultaneously -Note: There are also other less-common deployment methods, such as motorized pre-tensioners. Always refer to vehicle maker service information to determine the type of seat belt pre-tensioner you are working with Roll Bars -Pop-Up Roll Bars – pop-up roll bars are common on convertible vehicles, may be made of aluminum or steel, and deploy using hydraulic cylinders or compressed springs. A pop-up roll bar system may have one bar that extends the width of the vehicle, or two individual bars located on the right and left sides of the vehicle -Pop-Up Roll Bar Activation – pop-up roll bar systems utilize sensors such as yaw rate sensors, lateral accelerometers, and sensor that monitor suspension to chassis position change. These sensors are integrated with the RCM and detect when a rollover is imminent. There are multiple requirements that must occur at the same time in order for roll bar activation to occur. Roll bars will not activate unless all necessary criteria are met such as vehicle speed, deceleration, and roll angle -Stationary Roll Bars – some vehicles use stationary roll bars, which are also designed to limit occupant injury during a vehicle rollover. These roll bars will not pop-up, but are a part of the vehicles profile Restraint Systems Operation (2/2) Airbag Operation Airbags -Airbag Systems Overview – the airbag is a type of vehicle safety device designed to inflate rapidly, and then deflate quickly during a collision -Seat Position Sensor – the seat position sensor (SPS) determines the distance between the steering wheel and the driver -Restraint Control Module (RCM) – the RCM processes pre-crash data to determine which restraints should be deployed -Airbag Modules – airbag modules are typically the most visible part of an airbag system, and mounting locations may include the steering wheel, instrument panel, seats, door panels, or headliner. These airbag modules may include the airbag, inflator module, electrical connectors, and trim covers. Airbag modules are passive restraints that activate when a vehicle is involved in a collision. Unlike seat belts, which only work if the driver or passenger buckle up, airbags are designed to activate automatically. Once activated, the airbag is designed to inflate very quickly, and then deflate almost immediately; the smoke or dust seen during deployment is actually the packing powder/lubricant -Ignitors and Inflators – when the RCM determines it is necessary to deploy an airbag, it sends a signal to the ignitor, which initiates the airbag inflators. An inflator supplies the gas required to inflate an airbag. Conventional inflators use an electrical current to activate an igniter, which starts the burning of sodium pellets, creating nitrogen gas to fill the airbag. Hybrid inflators use an electrical current to energize an initiator, which causes the propellant to ignite, releasing compressed argon gas to fill the airbag. This type of igniter also heats the gas for faster inflation. The repair and service procedures are the same for both systems -Clock Spring – a clock spring is a spiral-wound rotary electrical connector, located between the steering wheel and steering column, which allows the steering wheel to turn while maintaining an electrical connection with the airbag, horn, and other devices. Some vehicles may integrate the clock spring and steering angle sensor into a single part. Always refer to vehicle maker service information for required replacement, service, and calibration information -Direction of Impact – determining thee direction of impact may help to determine the deployment of restraint systems. For example: -A front impact may deploy the front airbags and seat belt pre-tensioners or only the seat belt pre-tensioners -A front/side impact may deploy the front and side airbags or only the seat belt pre-tensioners -A side impact may deploy the side airbags, curtain airbags, and seat belt pre-tensioners -A rear impact may deploy active head restraints and use the seat back hinge to absorb the force from the collision -A rear/side impact may deploy side and curtain airbags -Note: It is important to remember every vehicle is designed differently and may or may not deploy the restraint systems as described -Side Airbags and Curtain Airbags – many vehicles have side airbags to offer extra protection in side impact collisions. A side airbag may be located in the seat backs, in the door trim panels, or along the roof rails from the A-pillar to the rear pillar. Airbags located along the roof rails are typically referred to as curtain airbags -Seat Side Airbags – side airbags may be located on the exterior-facing side of the seat back. They may be e quipped in the front seats only or both front and rear seats. After deployment, the vehicle maker may require replacement of the seat frame or complete seat back assembly, the foam padding, or damaged seat covers. Damaged seat covers should not be repaired. Seat covers that split during airbag deployment should not be re-sewn. Special thread is used that allows the seam to split and the airbag to properly deploy. The vehicle maker may only require replacement of the airbag module if the module has a built-in cover -Side Curtain Airbags – side curtain airbags are located along the roof rails. They typically extend from the A-pillar to the C- or D-pillar. Some larger vehicles may have multiple curtains on both sides to provide protection for both the front and rear occupants -Dual Stage Airbags – many vehicles today are equipped with dual stage airbags. Identify these airbags using vehicle-specific information or by physically examining the airbag -Activation – the function of a dual stage airbag is to deploy at a reduced force compared to a single stage airbag. It requires two separate ignitors and wire harness connectors. This allows the restraint system ECM to deploy whichever inflator is required depending on the crash parameters. Depending on the vehicle makers design and programmed crash parameters dual stage airbags may activate either as a single, reduced-charge deployment, or from different igniters simultaneously or staggered -Personal Safety – dual stage airbags look similar to single stage airbags when viewed from the front. When approaching a vehicle with deployed airbags, treat the airbags as dual stage airbags, with an active charge and still able to deploy. Identifying dual stage airbags can be done by referencing vehicle maker service information and looking for two igniter connectors on the backside of the airbag module. Note: Dual-stage airbags may still have a charge and may deploy. Use extreme caution Impact and Safing Sensors -Impact Sensors- restraint system sensors should include front, side, and safing or arming sensors. These sensors monitor vehicle conditions to detect sudden deceleration in a collision. The sensor then sends a signal to the restraint control module (RCM) to determine if an airbag should be deployed -Front Impact Sensors – front impact sensors detect front collisions and are located on the front portion of the vehicle. Front impact sensor locations may include: -On the front lower rails -On the radiator core support -Behind the headlamps -On the front frame rail crossmember -Side Impact Sensors – side impact sensors, located on both sides of the vehicle, are designed to detect side impact, and may be mounted: -In doors -On B- and C-pillars -Behind or under seats -On the rear wheelhouse -Note: Some vehicles use a pressure-based side impact sensor, located in the doors. These types of sensors are designed to detect a quick increase of pressure inside the door cavity during a side impact. These types of side impact sensors have a faster response time, but require other impact sensors located in other areas in case the door is not impacted during a collision -Safing or Arming Sensor – a safing or arming sensor is a secondary sensor that must close with the front or side impact sensors to confirm that a collision is taking place. This is done to prevent an accidental deployment caused by a problem in the front impact sensor circuits. Safing or arming sensors may be located inside the RCM or as a separate sensor in the passenger compartment. Some systems use three separate sensors to determine airbag deployment -Impact Sensor Operation – impact sensors detect sudden deceleration, or velocity change, at or near the point of impact. These sensors are typically solid state, with no moving parts, and send information to the RCM. The RCM calculates the information from the impact sensor and other sensors and uses that information to determine which, if any, restraint systems should be deployed. To protect passengers from injury from an unnecessary deployment, restraint systems are programmed with specific parameters. Airbags, and other types of restraint systems, may not deploy if the required parameters are not met Restraints Control Module -Restraint Control Module (RCM) – the RCM can be considered the brains behind the SRS. Functions of the RCM may include: -Deploying restraints -Checking for system faults -Processing information from various input sources -Storing information from the collision -Storing energy to deploy restraints if battery power is lost during a collision -Deploying Restraints – deployment of the restraints may be based on the perceived severity of the collision, deceleration rate, seat belt use, occupant position, and passenger occupant weight. Following deployment, the RCM may also communicate with the body control module (BCM) to turn on the interior lights and unlock the doors. The RCM may also initiate a call to 911 through systems like OnStar. Note: The RCM uses capacitors to store enough energy to deploy restraints if the battery power is lost during a collision -Checking for System Faults – most RCMs perform a self-diagnostic test when the ignition key is turned on. If the RCM is not working properly, or if it detects a problem with the restraint system, the airbag warning lamp will illuminate and remain lit until the problem is corrected. An airbag warning lamp that does not illuminate during the initial bulb check sequence may also indicate a problem -Processing Information – the RCM is designed to process information from various input sources. Input sources may include impact sensors, seat belt use sensors, the brake pedal switch, vehicle speed sensors, seat position sensors, and information from other control modules such as the occupant classification system (OCS) for the front passenger seat. Note: The RCM also stores data from the collision. This information includes the number of previous deployments and for which systems -RCM Mounting Locations – RCM mounting locations may include, but are not limited to: -Under the lower center portion of the instrument panel -Under the center console, between the front seats -Under a seat -Behind a kick panel -Note: It is important to refer to vehicle-specific service information to properly locate and identify the RCM Driver SRS -Front Driver Airbag System – the front driver airbag system includes the airbag module and the clock or coil spring. The airbag module contains the airbag inflator, electrical connectors, and trim cover. The clock or coil spring is mounted behind the steering wheel and is designed to provide a continuous electrical connection for the front driver airbag module while the steering wheel is being turned. Other names for the clock or coil spring include supplemental inflatable restraints (SIR) coil, cable reel, spiral cable, sliding contact, or slip ring. -Collapsible Steering Column – an energy absorbing, or collapsible, steering column is designed to collapse during a collision to reduce driver injury -Upper Column – steering columns may be designed to collapse in the upper column. Some upper columns have two shafts that are designed to function similary to a telescope during a collision. This type of design uses a solid shaft that compresses into a hollow shaft to shorten the overall length of the steering column. The shafts may be locked with plastic inserts that shear off upon impact. Upper steering columns may also be designed with a steel mesh or accordion-like convolutions that collapse under collision forces -Lower Steering Sections – the steering column may also be designed to collapse in the lower steering sections. These sections may be linked by offset universal joints to allow the sections to fold during an impact. This keeps the lower steering column from entering the passenger compartment -Mounting Brackets – the steering column may be designed to collapse at the steering column mounting brackets. Some steering columns are designed to collapse at the bracket that holds the column to the instrument panel frame. This may also be done with plastic inserts that shear upon impact. Note: Some columns may use a pyrotechnic charge to deploy the collapsible steering column -Knee Bolster and Knee Bolster Airbags – the knee bolster is designed into the lower portion of the instrument panel. It may provide an extra cushioned area to protect the knees and prevent sliding under the instrument panel during a collision. Vehicles may alternatively be equipped with knee bolster airbags, which inflate, pressing the occupants knees back against the lower part of the seat. This keeps the occupant safely in place. They are designed to deploy with the front airbags and may only be located on the driver side, or on both the driver and passenger sides of the vehicle Passenger SRS -Front Passenger Airbag – passenger airbag modules may be located on the passenger side of the instrument panel, facing the passenger seat. They may also be mounted on top of the instrument panel and use the windshield to deflect the deployment in the proper direction toward the passenger. Some older model vehicles are equipped with a disable switch that allows the driver to turn the passenger airbag off and on. Vehicles equipped with an occupant classification system (OCS) may also disable the passenger airbag when the seat is not occupied or the passenger does not meet the specified weight. -Occupant Classification System – the OCS is used to determine if the front passenger restraints should be activated. The OCS uses many sensors and a control module to provide information directly to the RCM, which controls the SRS -OCS Weight Sensors – various methods are used to determine front passenger occupant weight for the OCS, including a silicone-filled pressure bladder, a sensor mat, or strain gauges. Always refer to the vehicle maker service information to determine the type of system, mandatory replacement items, and calibration requirements before repairing any OCS. An OCS may also use various other sensors. These include the: -Seat belt-use sensor to determine if the seat belt is in use -Seat-position sensor to determine how close the occupant is to the airbag -Status lamp on the instrument panel or rearview mirror to communicate the status of the passenger airbag -OCS Control Module – an OCS will typically include a separate control module that communicates with the RCM. It may be located under the front passenger seat or under the center console -Instrument Panel Frame – passenger airbags are attached to the instrument panel and frame on the passenger side of the vehicle. Instrument panel frames are attached from the A-pillar to A-pillar. It is also used as a mounting point for the instrument panel and steering column. Some makers refer to these frames as instrument panel reinforcements, a cross-car beam, or a crossmember Safety Data Sheets and Labels (1/2) Safety Data Sheets Hazardous Material Regulations -Hazardous Material Regulations – the high cost of medical care for employees led to different types of awareness programs and government mandates to protect employees from hazardous materials and unsafe work practices. In the United States, this program is known as the Right-To-Know Law and is administered by the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OHSA). Note: For additional information on government safety and environmental regulations, visit www.osha.gov/dsg/hazcom/ and ccar-greenlink.org -Facilities Must Be Compliant – it is each facilities responsibility to be compliant with hazardous materials laws. As such, employees must be aware of the hazardous materials stored within the facility. According to OSHA, the number one safety and health violation for collision repair facilities is failure to communicate hazards within the work place to employees.
