Proper hazard communication, such as the sign in the image, is vital to worker health, and safety. -Compliant Checklist – the compliant checklist is: -A compilation of occupational safety and environmental laws -Is not a comprehensive list -Note: An OSHA inspector will use a similar checklist when inspecting a shop Safety Data Sheet (SDS) Layout -SDS Detailed Information – a Safety Data Sheet (SDS) must accompany every hazardous material in a workplace for professional use, and be readily accessible to workers when they are in their work areas. A typical safety data sheet will include hazard information for: -Handling -Storing -Disposing, and -Using material -Note: Employees are allowed to add information on hazards relating to the specific workplace, but cannot reduce the number of sections -SDS Sections – a supplier-provided SDS is divided into 16 sections. All workplace safety data sheets can be combined into a binder for ease of use. -Section 1: Product Identification – this section identifies: -Product identifier or common name -Product maker contact information, and -Recommended use of the material -Note: An SDS must be updated when the emergency telephone number or other product maker identifiers change -Section 2: Hazards Identification – this section identifies: -Hazard classification of the material, for example, flammable liquid -Hazard symbols which are pictorial representations of the particular hazard, such as a flame for a flammable material -Signal word, either warning or danger -Hazard statements, for example, may cause liver and kidney damage or highly flammable liquid and vapor, and -Relevant precautionary statements -Section 3: Composition / Information on Ingredients – this section identifies: -Ingredients contained in the materials -Impurities -Stabilizing additives -Chemical names, and -Concentration percentages -Section 4: First Aid Measures – this section describes: -Care to be given by untrained responders -First aid instructions -Descriptions of important symptoms or effects, and -Recommendations for immediate medical care -Section 5: Firefighting Measures – this section provides instructions for fighting a fire involving the material including: -The appropriate fire extinguisher -Hazards that develop from the material during a fire, and -Special protective equipment, or precautions for firefighters -Using the Proper Extinguisher – extinguishers are designated by class, and section 5 displays the proper use according to class designation -Class A – ordinary combustible materials, such as paper and wood -Extinguishers use water -Class B – flammable liquids, such as gasoline, oil and solvents -Extinguishers use carbon dioxide or dry chemical -Class C – energized electrical equipment, such as batteries and electrical wiring -Extinguishers use carbon dioxide, and -Class D – combustible metals, such as magnesium -Note: A class A-B-C fire is most common in a collision repair facility, and they should have several A-B-C extinguishers throughout the facility -Per letter – -A = for wood, paper, cloth, trash and other ordinary material -B = for gasoline, grease, oil, paint and other flammable liquids -C = for live electrical equipment -D = for combustible metals -Class ABC at the bottom – the 3 blue squares -Fire and Explosion Terms – some terms used when describing fire and explosion materials are: -Flammable materials -Combustible metals -Flashpoint -Auto-ignition temperature -Upper- and lower-flammable limits -Flammable Material – flammable materials can easily catch fire and burn rapidly or explosively -Combustible Materials – combustible materials can burn, but do not catch fire as easily as flammables -Flashpoint – flashpoint is the lower temperature at which a mixture of a liquid’s vapor and oxygen can catch fire near a source of ignition, such as a flame or spark. The lower the flashpoint the greater the hazard. Flammables have a flashpoint below 100 degrees Fahrenheit, and combustibles have a flashpoint at or above 100 degrees Fahrenheit -Auto-Ignition Temperature – this is the lowest temperature a vapor or gas will ignite without a flame or spark being present -Upper- and Lower-Flammable/Explosive Limits – upper- and lower-flammable/explosive limits. These limits are the amount of a material’s vapor in the air that will catch fire with a flame or spark. Above the upper flammable limit, the mixture is too rich to burn. Below the lower flammable limit, the mixture is too lean. These limits also indicate proper ventilation requirements when using a material -SDS Sections continued – now let us continue our look at the sections of SDS. -Section 6 – Accidental Release Measure – this section provides the appropriate response to spills, leaks, or releases, including containment and cleanup practices, and circumstances distinguishing between large and small spills. Section 6 also provides additional information on: -Use of personal precautions -Emergency procedures, including instructions for evacuations, consulting experts, and appropriate protective clothing used during cleanup -Methods and materials used for containment -Cleanup procedures including appropriate techniques for neutralization, decontamination, and cleaning or vacuuming using absorbent materials -Section 7: Handling and Storage – this section provides guidance on safe handling practices including: -Handling incompatible materials -Minimizing the release of materials into the environment -Providing advice on general hygiene practices such as precautions on not eating, drinking, and smoking in work areas -Conditions for safe storage -Section 8: Exposure Controls / Personal Protection – this section indicates: -Exposure limits which are the maximum concentrations of each chemical in the material a worker can be exposed to in a given time -Appropriate engineering controls such as type of exhaust ventilation that can be used to minimize worker exposure -Personal protective measures to prevent illness or injury from exposure to chemicals -Special requirements for protective clothing -Section 8 also uses several acronyms to describe levels of exposure -PEL (Permissible Exposure Limits) are the legal limits for exposure of an employee to a chemical substance or physical agent. Usually expressed in parts per million (PPM) or in milligrams per cubic meter (mg/m3) -TLV (Threshold Limit Value) - is the airborne concentration of a chemical to which all workers may be repeatedly exposed, day after day, over a working lifetime, without adverse health effects -STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit) is the concentration of the material that workers can be exposed to continuously for a short period of time without suffering ill effects -TWA (Time-Weighted Average) – is the concentration for an 8-hour workday and a 40-hour work week to which it is believed that nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed, day after day, for a working lifetime without adverse effect -C (Ceiling) is the concentration of the chemical that should never be exceeded during any exposure -Note: Not all respirators provide protection in all situations -Section 9: Physical and Chemical Properties – section 9, physical and chemical properties, identifies what the material looks and smells like in its natural state, and include: -Properties of material in existing state -Material color -Material smell -Other physical properties, as appropriate -Section 10: Stability and Reactivity – this section describes the reactivity hazards and stability of the material which includes: -Reactivity, which is how reactive the material is and to what other materials -Material Stability, which is how stable the material is under normal ambient temperature, while in storage, and when being handled -Conditions to be avoided -Known or anticipated hazardous decomposition products that could be produced because of use, storage, or heating -Section 11: Toxicological Information – this section identifies toxicological and health effect information (or indicates that the data is not available) which includes: -Likely routes of exposure, be it inhalation, ingestion, skin or eye contact -Effects from short- and long-term exposure -Toxicity levels -Symptoms after the exposure -Whether the material can cause cancer -SDS Sections 12-16 – there are five remaining sections in the SDS. Sections 12-15 are non-mandatory, though there must be placeholders for them. -Section 12: Ecological Information – this section details the environmental impact of the material if released to the environment. Information may include -Data from toxicity tests performed on aquatic and/or terrestrial organisms -Whether there is potential for the chemical to persist and degrade in the environment -Potential for a substance to move from the soil to groundwater -Other adverse effects -Section 13: Disposal Considerations – this section provides instructions on proper disposal practices, recycling, or reclamation of the material or its container, and safe handling practices to minimize exposure. Information may include: -Appropriate disposal containers -Appropriate disposal methods -Description of physical and chemical properties that may affect disposal -Language discouraging sewage disposal -Special precautions for landfills, or incineration activities -Section 14: Transport Information (Non-mandatory) – this section provides guidance on classification information for shipping and transporting of the chemicals contained in the material by: -Road -Air -Rail -Sea -Section 15: Regulatory Information (Non-mandatory) – this section identifies safety, health, and environmental regulations specific for the material, not found anywhere else on the SDS -Section 16: Other Information – this last section indicates the Date SDS was prepared, or the last known revision date Safety Data Sheets and Labels (2/2) Labels Label Indicators -Label Types – there are three types of labels commonly used for identifying hazardous materials. These labels include: -Supplier -Workplace (Such as in the image shown), and -Other means of identification -Note: None of these labels may replace the SDS -Supplier Label Requirements – all hazardous chemicals shipped after June 1, 2015, must be labeled with specific elements including: -Pictograms -Signal words -Hazard statements -Precautionary statements -Labels must be in English -Note: If there is a container without a label, do not handle it until you found out from the repair facility manager what the material is. Once you have determined what the product is, place a label on the container -Supplier Label Information – each container of hazardous material must be labeled, tagged, or marked. -Product Identifier – the product identifier displays: -Product name -Code number or batch number -Note: The manufacturer, importer or distributer decides the appropriate product identifier. The same product identifier must be both on the label and in section 1 of the SDS -Supplier Identification – the supplier identification displays: -Chemical manufacturer, importer or other responsible party -Name -Address -Emergency telephone number -Precautionary Statements – precautionary statements identify the recommended measures to prevent adverse effects from exposure to the hazardous material, including: -Prevention, for example, wear nitrile gloves when using this product -Response, for example, in case of fire use a dry chemical or carbon dioxide extinguisher -Storage, for example, store in a cool well-ventilated place that is locked -Disposal, which will refer to regulations -Hazard Pictograms – hazard pictograms are graphic symbols used to communicate specific information about the hazards of a material. They have a red square frame set at a point with a black hazard symbol on a white background, and are wide to be clearly visible -Label Indicators – -Flame – the flame symbol denotes: -Flammable -Is not used for combustibles -Skull and Crossbones – the skull and crossbones indicates that the material may be toxic or fatal is: -Swallowed -Touched to skin -Inhaled -Gas Cylinder – this symbol means that the substance is compressed, liquefied, or dissolved gas under pressure at 29 pounds per square inch or more -Health Hazard – the health hazard symbol appears on materials with varied levels of health hazards within specific categories such as: -Carcinogen, which is a chemical substance or a mixture of chemical substances which induce cancer or increase its incidence -Reproductive Toxicity, which includes adverse effects on sexual function and fertility in adult males and females, as well as developmental toxicity in offspring -Respiratory Sensitizer, which is a substance that induces hypersensitivity of the airways following inhalation -Target Organ Toxicity that may cause damage to bodily organs -Aspiration Toxicity, which includes severe effects such as chemical pneumonia, varying degrees of pulmonary injury, or death following aspiration -Corrosion – the corrosion symbol denotes chemicals or materials that have corrosive properties whose effects include: -Severe skin burns -Severe or serious eye damage -Corrosion to metals -Exclamation Mark – the exclamation mark appears on materials to indicate less severe toxicity than material identified by the skull and crossbones. It can mean harmful if: -Swallowed -Inhaled -Contacts skin -Note: Effects include irritants to the skin, eye and respiratory tract, an allergic response following skin contact, narcotic effects such as drowsiness, lack of coordination, and dizziness may appear -Exploding Bomb – the exploding bomb pictogram appears on the materials that are explosive or self-reactive, in which heating may cause fire or an explosion without the need for air. Another property this symbol represents is for organic peroxide. Most undiluted organic peroxides can catch fire easily and burn very rapidly and intensely. An example of an organic peroxide in the repair facility is methyl ethyl ketone peroxide. It is used in the manufacture of fiberglass reinforced plastics as a hardening agent -Environment (Non-Mandatory) – the environment pictogram is non-mandatory and appears on materials that indicate levels of aquatic toxicity -Flame Over Circle – the flame over circle symbol appears on chemicals or materials that are oxidizers, which: -Increase the concentration of oxygen -Can cause or contribute to the combustion of liquids and solids -Label Indicators Continued – in addition to pictograms, labels contain other indicators as well. -Signal Word – a signal word indicates the relative level of severity of the hazard, and a potential hazard. There are only two words used as signal words: -Danger, for the more severe hazards -Warning, for the less severe hazards -Note: There is only one signal word on the label no matter how many hazards a material may have -Hazard Statements – hazard statements describe the nature of the hazard(s) of a material, including, where appropriate, the degree of hazard. All of the applicable hazard statements must appear on the label -Supplemental Information – supplemental information is additional information the label producer deems helpful -Personal Protection Symbols – -Personal protection symbols: -Indicate what personal protection is required when working with a material -Can be read in any language -The symbols shown are common in a repair facility and represent: -Eye protection -Face shield -Full-body protection -Gloves -Air-purifying respirator -Supplied-air respirator -Apron -Foot protection Label Types -Inner and Outer Containers – the supplier does not have to label an outer container if the inner container label can be seen and read through the outer container -Workplace Label Requirements – labels created and attached to containers in the workplace are not required to have as much information as a supplier label, and workplace label information can be acquired from the original supplier label or the product SDS. A workplace label must contain: -The product name, as stated on the SDS -Hazardous warning information -Note: The hazard information can be found in section 2 of the SDS -When Workplace Labels are Required – workplace labels are required when: -The supplier label is removed or defaced -Identifying a solution, for example, when a product is mixed or diluted and kept for further use -Transferring to a small container, for example, when contents of one container are transferred to another unlabeled container -Mixing paint, such as when pouring mixed paint into a container that will be stored -Note: Workplace labels are NOT required when material is used in one shift, by one person. Water bottles should also be clearly labeled to ensure water is not confused with other clear liquids -NFPA Label – the National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) has a label system for use by emergency personnel. The codes on the label are shown in different colored boxes according to the hazard represented. The boxes are always in the same position in the diamond. -Fire Hazard is red and is always on top -Instability Hazard is yellow and is always on the right -Health Hazard is blue and is always on the left -Special Hazards is white or blank and is always on the bottom. This designates hazards such as “acidic”, “radioactive”, or “not to be used with water” -NFPA Number Codes – -The NFPA uses a number code, 0-4, to signify the danger level. The higher the number, the higher the danger level - 0 – very low danger -1 – slightly dangerous -2 – moderately dangerous - 3 – seriously dangerous - 4- severely dangerous -In the lower portion of the symbol shown, special hazards are identified. There are currently only two NFPA approved symbols -“OX” which denotes that a material is an oxidizer -“W” with a line through it to denote unusual reactivity with water -HazCom 2012 Number Codes – - HazCom 2012 is OSHA’s number classification system which classifies hazards to determine what label information is required. There are four numbers or categories, 1 through 4 -1 is most severe hazard -4 is least severe hazard -The Hazard category numbers: -Are NOT required to be on labels -ARE required on SDS’s in section 2 -Other Means of Identification – -The last type of labe is “Other means of idenfitication”. This type of label is required for containers where the original label may be hidden or covered. Examples include: -Paint cleaning systems where solvents or residues may damage or cover a label -Large storage containers where labels may be hidden from view -Secondary containers where solvents commonly wash off the label printing -“Other means of identification” labels may be anything that indicates what the product is. These labels must still include basic requirements of a workplace label, and be identified in an employee education program so that everyone is informed about the meanings of any codes, colors, or signs Safety Shield Technologies Nissan presents -Advancements in Safety Technology- Need for: -Monitoring driver, road conditions, and adjacent traffic -Decreasing the response time for braking / emergency situations -Increasing passenger protection in the event of a collision -Nissan responds by creating Safety Shield Technologies. Nissan created Safety Shield technologies to assist drivers while driving by helping avoid collisions and to help protect vehicle occupants when collisions are unavoidable -The Nissan Safety Shield Technologies – monitor, respond, and protect are the three components to Nissan’s Safety Shield Technologies -Collision Repairs and Safety Shield Technologies – -Vehicles must be properly aligned – both structure and suspension -Sensor / camera mounts must be dimensionally correct -Collision Repairs and Safety Shield Technologies (cont’d) – -Body filler can affect sensor operation -Paint thickness can affect sensor operation -Intelligent Cruise Control (ICC) Operation – -ICC sensor reflects radar off vehicle ahead -Determines speed and distance of vehicle ahead -ICC sensor sends information to ADAS control unit -Allows vehicle speed to be reduced or increased -System is turned ON and OFF using switch on steering wheel -Detection range of sensor will not detect all objects -Note: The Intelligent Cruise Control system maintains a set distance from the vehicle ahead -Identifying Vehicles with ICC- -Determine if vehicle is equipped with ICC -2016 Nissan Sentra, Altima, Maxima, Murano, Armada and most 2016 Infiniti models -Look for ON / Off switch on steering wheel -Dash icon (if power available to vehicle) -Note: The activation switch on the steering wheel, which is often shown with a vehicle icon and three lines, indicates the ICC option -ICC System Parts – -Driver Assistance Buzzer -Engine Control Module -Body Control Module -ICC Sensor -ICC Steering Switch -Steering Angle Sensor -ADAS Control Unit -ABS Actuator -ICC System Damage Analysis – -ICC sensor prone to damage -Check for cracks to sensor case or other signs of damage -Repair / replace decisions based on extent of damage and Nissan Recommendations -Plastic repair can hinder sensor performance -Do not paint the sensor -Note: Damage to the front of the vehicle may disable the ICC system -ICC Sensor – the damaged ICC sensor will affect other systems: -Forward Emergency Braking -Distance Control Assist -Predictive Forward Collision Warning -Position Statement for Radar Calibration – The Nissan and Infiniti position statement for the ICC sensor recommends calibrating the sensor any time work is done on or around the sensor -ICC Testing Following Part Replacement – -Perform road tests following ICC service -Look for normal operation in different road conditions -Note: Road tests should be done after working on the ICC system -Predictive Forward Collision Warning Operation – -Uses the ICC sensor to calculate the distance from the vehicle in front of the vehicle ahead and relative speed -If there is an issue with the closing speed, the ADAS control unit sends a signal to the driver assistance buzzer control module to alert the driver -Designated to operate at speeds at or above 3 mph -Identifying Vehicles with PFCW – -Option on the 2016 Nissan Rogue, Altima, Maxima, Murano, and Armada -Option on all 2016 Infiniti vehicles -Look for an indicator on the dash or in the menu system in the instrument cluster -ICC sensor on the front of the vehicle -PFCW Operation Considerations – -Understand when the system is and is not expected to function -Even though there are no mechanical problems, the PFCW system may not operate when there is a narrow vehicle ahead -System may not work in snow or heavy rain, or if the sensor is covered by: -Dirt -Ice -Snow -PFCW may not work driving in a tunnel, driving on a steep downhill slope or sharp curves, or if the distance to the vehicle ahead is too close -Note: Heavy rain can prevent the PFCW system from working properly -Parts that Make up the PFCW – -Display Control Unit -Driver Assistance Buzzer -ICC Sensor -Combination Meter -ADAS Control Unit -ABS Actuator -PFCW Damage Analysis – -System malfunction indicator lamp -High-pitched tone -Forward Emergency Brake (FEB) warning lamp illuminates -PFCW system has a malfunction -ICC sensor is covered or obstructed -Note: The forward emergency brake lamp, which is integral to the PFCW system, will light when the system is off -Identifying Vehicles with LDW / LDP – -Many of the 2016 Infiniti vehicles, and the 2016 Nissan Armada -Look for the ON / OFF control -Look for camera next to rearview mirror -Note: These are examples of what can be used to help identify vehicles with lane departure warning or related systems such as lane keep assist -LDW / LDP System Operation - -When the LDW system is turned ON, the advanced driver assist control module sends an “ON” signal to the chassis control module -Lane camera looks at the lane markers and evaluates the position of the car, sends information to the chassis control module or ADAS depending on vehicle design -Control module determines if a correction should be made and sends info to computers that will make adjustments -Moving vehicle back to center of lane done through braking or steering control depending on vehicle module -Note: the LDW system can be toggled ON and OFF using the information display. -Active Lane Control System Operation- -Helps prevent vehicle wander due to crosswinds or uneven pavement -Helps keep the vehicle in the lane with fewer corrections needed by the driver -Uses many of the same parts as the LDP system -Parts of a LDW System – -Lane Camera Unit -Driver Assistance Buzzer -Body Control Module -Display Control Unit -Combination Meter -ADAS Control Unit -ABS Actuator -LDW/LDP System Damage Analysis – -Check all LDW parts for obvious physical damage -Look for the presence of a malfunction indicator lamp or any trouble codes -Verify that the glass in front of the camera is clean and undamaged and that the vehicle is at the proper ride height -Note that stone chips in the windshield should not be repaired in front of the camera -Windshield and glass position statement -Note: The area in front of the camera should be free of chips, cracks, or debris for proper operation -Lane Departure Camera Calibration- -Required when camera removed or new one installed -Requires printing and sizing targets from service info -Requires attaching targets to a white board -Consult scan tool is used to run calibration -Use of Aftermarket Glass – no bueno -Identifying Vehicles with Blind Spot Warning – -Blind spot warning systems are standard or an option on most INFINITI and Nissan vehicles -Look for the blind spot symbol or indicator light on the door mirrors on door -Note: The indicator lamp on the interior of the door turns on when a vehicle is in a blind spot location -Blind Spot Warning System Operation – -Uses two radar sensors, one on each side of the vehicle, behind each end of the rear bumper -Detection zone is from door mirror to about 10 ft behind rear bumper cover -May steer vehicle back into lane (depending on vehicle option), called blind spot intervention -Will not operate under specific conditions -Designed to operate above 37 mph -Note: This shows the blind spot detection zone, which extends behind the rear bumper and sideways -Parts of a Blind Spot Warning System – -Blind Spot Warning Indicator -BCM -ABS Actuator -Transmission Control Module -Combination Meter -Warning Buzzer -Side Radar -ADAS Control Unit -Blind Spot Warning Damage Analysis- -A damaged radar sensor displays a warning on the information display -Buzzer will sound indicating if sensor is blocked -Look for damage to the left or right side of the rear bumper cover or lower quarter panel area -During repairs, make sure the sensor is mounted correctly and at specification -Make sure that any body repairs do not affect sensor performance -Note: damage to the lower quarter panel or outer bumper cover are potential indicators of radar sensor damage -Rear Cross-Traffic Alert and Parking Sonars – -Rear cross-traffic alert and parking sonars are used in combination to help alert the driver to cross traffic -Rear cross-traffic alert uses the blind spot detection sensor and the four sonar sensors in the rear bumper -Designed to operate at speeds below 5 mph and whenever the vehicle is in reverse -Note: Parking sonars, positioned across the rear bumper, are used to detect objects behind the vehicle -Backup Collision Intervention (BCI)- -System will alert the driver AND brake vehicle to avoid collision -The system is turned ON by selecting the BCI switch -System can be disabled by material covering radar sensor -Never apply two coats of primer to sensor mounting part -If the sonar control module is replaced, it requires configuring to the vehicle using Consult -Note: The BCI provides a rear view of the vehicle when the system is engaged -System Operation for Around View Monitor – -Uses four cameras to create a 360 degree view of vehicle’s surroundings -Can help maneuver in tight spots -Operated on center console -System Parts for Around View Monitor- -Door Mirror RH -Around View Monitor Control Unit -Front Camera -Door Mirror LH -Rear View Camera -AV Control Unit -Around View Monitor Damage Analysis- -Camera mount areas are prone to collision damage, so camera replacement is common -Engage the system to verify it works -Make sure the cameras are properly aligned so the image displays properly -Around View Monitor Camera Calibration – -Calibration required if any camera or camera mounting part has been replaced -Calibration required if around view monitor control unit is removed and installed, or replaced -Calibration is done using a Nissan Consult scan tool -Boundary of each camera image is aligned with lines that have been placed on the shop floor -Identifying Vehicles with Forward Emergency Braking – -Option found on the 2016 Nissan Sentra, Altima, Maxima, Rogue, Murano, and Armada -Option found on the 2016 INFINITI Q50, Q70, QX30, QX60, and QX80 -Look for presence of a camera by the rearview mirror and the ICC sensor -Forward Emergency Braking Operation – -System measures the distance from a vehicle ahead using the ICC sensor in the front bumper -ICC sensor sends data to the ADAS control unit, which determines the possibility of a collision -Using data received, the ADAS control unit does several operations based on the possibility of a collision -The systems can be turned ON or OFF by navigating menus in the information display -Note: the braking system monitors speed two vehicles ahead -Parts of the Forward Emergency Braking System – -Driver Assistance Buzzer -Engine Control Module -ICC Brake Hold Relay -Transmission Control Module -ICC Sensor -Display Control Unit -Combination Meter -ADAS Control Unit -Brake Pedal Position Switch -ABS Actuator -Accelerator Pedal Actuator -Forward Emergency Braking Damage Analysis – -Important to know when the system is malfunction and when certain road or driving conditions can affect system operation -Dirty sensor will cancel system and a beep will sound along with an indicator lamp showing the system is OFF -System cancelled if there is a mechanical issue that prevents system operation -Note: The FEB warning light will turn on to inform the driver that the system is OFF -Forward Emergency Braking Diagnostics and Calibrations- -Note that the FEB system shares parts and diagnostic procedures with the ICC system -As with all Safety Shield Technologies, verify that the system is operation normally -When the intelligent brake assist OFF indicator lamp stays on and SHOULD be off, perform diagnostics -Note: Systems diagnostics are performed using the Consult scan tool -System Overlap- -Lane Camera Unit- -Predictive Forward Collision Warning- -Predictive Forward Collision Warning -Driver Attention Alert -Lane Departure Warning -Active Lane Control -Intelligent Cruise Control (ICC) Sensor- -Forward Emergency Braking -Intelligent Cruise Control -Predictive Forward Collision Warning -ABS Actuator – -Forward Emergency Braking -Blind Spot Warning Intervention System -Predictive Forward Collision Warning -Lane Departure Prevention -Intelligent Cruise Control -Cameras- -Around View -Moving Object Detection -ADAS Control Unit- -Intelligent Cruise Control -Lane Departure Warning -Blind Spot Warning -Predictive Forward Collision Warning -Forward Emergency Braking -Systems that Require Calibration- -Safety Shield Technologies have several parts that must be re-calibrated or initialized if a part of the system is removed or replaced -ICC sensor -ICC control unit -Lane camera -Around view camera -Module summary- students should not be able to identify the operation and parts that make up the: -Intelligent Cruise Control -Predictive Forward Collision Warning System -Lane Departure Warning/Lane Departure Prevention/Active Lane Control -Blind Spot Warning and Rear Cross-Traffic Alert -Rearview Monitor and Around View Monitor -Forward Emergency Braking (Intelligent Brake Assist (IBA)) -Learning Objectives – At the end of this module, students are expected to: -Explain Nissan’s restraint systems -Inspect the airbag system parts for damage following a collision -Determine damage to the occupant classification system -Explain active head restraint operation and verify the system still functions following a collision -Determine if seat belt pre-tensioners deployed and explain replacement procedures -Supplemental Restraint Systems Overview – -Restraint systems provide additional protection inside the high-strength body zone cone construction -Front, seat, knee, and roof-mounted airbags -Occupant classification sensor and seat belt pre-tensioners -Note: Multiple airbag systems are situated throughout the passenger compartment -Restraint Systems Operation- -Nissan advanced airbag systems include dual-stage supplemental front airbags with seat belt sensor and an OCS -The inflation rate of the dual-stage airbags adjust based on crash severity and seat belt usage -Airbags are used with seat belt pre-tensioners to properly position occupant -Tensioner will deploy but the airbag will not depending on collision severity -Note: Multiple airbags located throughout the cabin are deployed by front and side sensors -Restraint Systems Parts- -Driver Airbag Module -Passenger Airbag Module -OCS Control Unit -Crash Sensor -Airbag Diagnosis Sensor Unit -LH Side Curtain Airbag Module -Rear Side Airbag Satellite Sensor LH -Front Side Airbag Module LH -Front Side Airbag Sensor -Front Door Satellite Sensor LH -Common Diagnostics- -SRS Airbag Warning Light Stays On- -Check deployment of airbag module and seat belt pre-tensioner -Check airbag fuse -Check harness connector -Check wiring harness externals -SRS Airbag Warning Light Does Not Turn On- -Check combination meter power supply and ground -Check harness connector -Check wiring harness -Check airbag diagnosis sensor unit -OCS Removal and Installation Considerations – -Disable the restraint system when replacing the OCS -Nissan’s standard is to disconnect and isolate both battery terminals and wait at least 3 minutes -The control unit and seat sensor mat are an integral part of the front passenger seat cushion -Replaced as an assembly -OCS must go through a zero point reset -Process is provided in the vehicle service information -Note: The indicator lamp illuminates when the passenger airbag is turned off -Pop-Up Roll Bars – -Deployment requires replacement of the pop-up roll bar assembly and covers -If NOT deployed, check for visible signs of damage -Also check the harness and connectors for damage -Check for visible signs of damage to the seatback support brace assembly -Active Head Restraint – -Designed to be reused following a collision -Pivot-type system that pushes the head restraint forward when the occupant’s back puts pressure on the seatback assembly -There are no repair recommendations, the seatback must be replaced as an assembly -Note: The head restraint is designed to reset following a rear collision -Seat Belt Pre-Tensioners – -Seat belt pretensioners are activated by signals from the airbag sensor -All seat belt assemblies in use during a collision are replaced unless the collision was minor, belts show no damage, continue to operate properly -Inspect all seat belt assemblies, including retractors and attached hardware after any collision -All seat belt pre-tensioners must be replaced even if the seat belts were not in use during a collision in which the airbags are deployed -No calibration is necessary following replacement of passive restraint system parts, but verify any diagnostic trouble codes that may be present -Latch System – -Nissan’s LATCH, or Lower Anchors and Tethers for Children, system is compatible with child restraint seats -Following a collision where the LATCH system was in use, check the anchoring locations for any damage -Note: The LATCH system is designed to work with child seats -Module Summary – students should now be able to: -Explain Nissan’s restraints systems -Inspect the airbag system parts for damage following a collision -Determine damage to the occupant classification system -Explain active head restraint operation and verify operation -Determine if seat belt pre-tensioners have deployed and explain replacement recommendations -Expenses when scheduling the repair appointment. Bringing the vehicle in on a Tuesday will allow enough time in the work week to complete the repairs without adding two days of weekend rental expenses for our insurance partner or customer. -Temporary Repairs – what happens when your production shop is at capacity, but there are more repairs to be scheduled? Does the vehicle need to be repaired now or can it be made drivable so it can be scheduled in when it will fit into your production schedule? The practice of performing temporary or minor repairs to make the vehicle drivable until you have capacity to bring the vehicle in for repairs may be an uncommon practice in your shop, but it may be a viable option in some instances. Note: When deciding if a vehicle is a good candidate for temporary repairs, always consider all safety and mechanical systems to ensure the vehicle will be safe to drive without doing any additional damage to the vehicle. -Not Appropriate for Temporary Repairs – when doing temporary repairs, all safety and mechanical items should be considered. If an airbag has deployed, the vehicle should not be driven until it has been replaced and the system is working properly. -Appropriate for Temporary Repairs – this situation is more common with weather-related situations, such as a hailstorm. Many vehicles in a small area could have hail damage involving broken glass. The shops in the entire market area will tend to get loaded up quickly. In this case, it may be appropriate to do temporary repairs to allow the customer to continue driving the vehicle until the shop has capacity to get them scheduled in for repairs to correct body panel damage. Work in Process -Scheduling Considerations – when making scheduling decisions, it is not only important to understand your production shop available capacity, but also your available capacity by labor type. -Structural Repairs – in most cases, when a vehicle is in need of heavy structural repairs, the vehicle will be non-drivable and the vehicle will need to be staged for repair when the necessary capacity is available. However, it is not uncommon for a drivable vehicle to require some minor structural repairs, so the WIP and available capacity for the technicians capable of completing this type of repair will need to be factored into your scheduling decision. For example: You are inspecting a vehicle on Wednesday afternoon that will require some minor structural repairs before any other repairs can proceed. Your structural repair technicians are scheduled on other repairs until the end of the day on the following Monday. In this case, there is likely no value in bringing that vehicle in for repairs until your structural technicians have available capacity, even if your body repair technicians do have available capacity. -Express Lane – some repair facilities have established an Express Lane for vehicles with minor damage. These are small “quick” repairs that include small dent repairs only, meaning those confined to one panel. These repairs can be routed so the repair process bypasses others, as a result, these small jobs are not treated as job fillers. These Express Lane repairs may be tracked and scheduled separately from standard production shop repairs. Initial Parts Order -Option Identification Label – some vehicles have a label that identifies all of the options, trims, and accessories that were installed on the vehicle when it was assembled. This label may also have the exterior paint code. The option identification label may be located in the trunk or glove box. In most cases, the repair shop will need dealer assistance to decode the option identification label. A photograph of the label may be useful when ordering parts. -Ordering Parts – there are some key pieces of information that will be necessary to ensure accurate parts orders, such as -Accurate and complete VIN -Vehicle options -Production date -Trim code -Partial Disassembly – in some cases, it may be a good idea to perform a partial disassembly of the vehicle to identify any potential hidden damages and all of the parts that can be ordered prior to the vehicle arriving for repairs. This involves taking the vehicle apart to locate damage, and then putting it back together without repairing it. Partial disassembly may also include recording stages of the disassembly with digital photos, and taking point-to-point measurements. This could be done with a tape measure or electronic measuring system. If electronic measuring is available, a printout of the measurements could be used. Staging and Communication -Updating the Management System – for most shops, the computer management system is the hub of their nonverbal communication process. Making updates to the computer management system benefits the facility as a whole. The management system is typically updated by all employees in one way or another, and these updates can have substantial effect on available capacity, parts ordering, and scheduling. Customers, parts vendors, or rental car companies may be updated by the action you take on the system. -Current and Accurate Information – each department should update the management system for the area for which they are responsible, and monitor the sections of the management system that directly affect them. The production manager would typically be responsible for updating the repair status for each of the vehicles currently in the production shop and in staging, waiting to enter the production shop. The estimator or front office staff would typically be responsible for scheduling customers in for repair and for the information regarding repair labor, parts, and arrival date. Both of these activities have a signification impact on the available capacity showing in your scheduling system. For example: -Inaccurate information from the production side will result in inaccurate WIP and capacity information going into the scheduling system. -Inaccurate information from the estimator could result in an over-scheduling issue due to inaccurate information about incoming repairs. -Conclusion – it is critical to the success of any shop to ensure there is a scheduling system in place based on accurate and current information that is understood by all employees involved in the scheduling process. In addition to the obvious impact that improper scheduling can cause in the production shop, it will typically have a substantial impact on cycle time, which will result in frustration for your insurance partners and reduced CSI scores from your customers. -Sectioning Procedures – sectioning should never make the repair area any stronger or weaker than the factory design. Sleeves and reinforcements should only be used when recommended by the vehicle maker. It is important to note that the procedures provided by the vehicle maker are service specifications, not recommendations. I-CAR’s position is that if vehicle maker procedures or recommendations are not available, DO NOT SECTION. Note: Complete part replacement is recommended when sectioning procedures are not provided -Vehicle Maker Procedures – vehicle maker sectioning procedures may be found on vehicle maker service information websites. -Sectioning and Steel Strength Ratings – structural parts are made from various high-strength steels and have complex shapes to decrease vehicle weight and increase passenger safety. The lightweight materials and designs are used by vehicle makers to get parts to collapse in a specific manner when under collision forces. Adding a sectioning joint to a part that is not approved by vehicle makers may alter how the part will perform in future collisions. -Recommendation Concerns – vehicles are safer due to new designs, and because of this, crash injuries and deaths have steadily declined. If you make a conscious decision to disregard vehicle maker specifications, you need to ask yourself, ‘Am I willing to assume liability should the repair fail during a subsequent collision?’. Automotive engineers develop the repair procedures to ensure crashworthiness. It is very important to follow these procedures to ensure the vehicle parts perform as designed. Sectioning Preparation -Vehicle Preparation for Sectioning – when preparing a vehicle for sectioning, all body dimensions must be restored, even on damaged parts on the vehicle underbody, upperbody, and underhood. This requires: -The vehicle being secured to a bench -Following best practices for straightening, such as only applying heat to parts that will be replaced -Verify the Correct Replacement Part – during the parts receiving process, all parts should be checked when they are received to ensure the correct part was delivered. Even though the part seems to fit the vehicle being repaired, and it looks like the same part, there can be subtle variations due to a mid-year design change. Before cutting the part, the technician should make one last check to: -Ensure the service part is compared to the original by making a visual inspection and taking measurements -Verify that the shapes, mounting locations, and feature lines match the original part -Note: On occasion, the parts may be superseded and have variations in design. If this is encountered, the OEM documentation should be placed in the job file. -Measuring Cut Line – in order to locate the sectioning cut line, note that some vehicle makers stamp die marks on their parts to identify the cut location. Lines are scribed from the die mark around the circumference of the part. Some vehicles may have a mark that looks like a die mark, but note that this is not a cut location. For this reason, always verify the sectioning cut line location with the body repair manual. -Measuring Methods – measurements may be provided from the vehicle maker from a specific reference point, such as the tip of a rail. If a part is collapsed, and the vehicle maker provides measurements from the tip of the part, the service part may be used to help locate the line on the damaged part. This is done by measuring from the tip of the service part to the cut line location. At this point, make a scribe mark around the service part. Measure from a reference point rearward of the scribe mark on the service part. Next, locate the same reference point on the damaged part. Measure to the cut line and scribe a mark around the circumference of the damaged part. -Measuring from Two Points – when measuring, it may be useful to measure two points on the service part – one point ahead of the sectioning cut line and one point behind. Make this measurement on each side of the part. After the part has been cut and fit up to the vehicle, use these measurements to verify the proper position of the part. Note that this is done in addition to using mechanical or electronic measurements. It is one more method that can ensure your part is in the proper position before welding -Transferring Template Measurements – templates may be used to transfer cut lines from the service part to the damaged part and vice versa. Templates are made by the technician. These may be made from masking tape, adhesively backed spark paper, or cardboard. When using items such as masking tape or spark paper, note the tackiness of the product. If it is too sticky, it will be difficult to remove the template without tearing. Templates may be used with other methods to determine cut lines -Marking the Cut Line – a scratch awl should be used to mark the cut line to allow for an accurate measurement. Thick marker lines may cause inaccurate measurements as these lines may be a couple millimeters wide. The side of the cut line that will be cut should also be marked. Masking tape can help provide a straight edge in areas where part contours make it difficult to use a ruler to draw straight lines. If tape is used, mark the side that is the cut side. Note: Recheck measurements against the body repair manual to verify the proper location. Have a fellow technician verify the cut line location using the body repair manual as well to reduce the chance for error. Sectioning Overview (1/4) Sectioning Overview -Part Sectioning Overview – some repairs may require sectioning or a complete part replacement. -Why Parts are Replaced – part replacement, either through sectioning or complete part replacement, is done when parts are damaged beyond repair. The kink vs bend rule may have been used to make this determination. Kinked metal means that the steel has been damaged to the point where structural integrity is lost. However, on higher strength steel, such as ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS), even bends may not be repairable since the part may crack under the pulling forces before the part is straightened. -Damage Analysis – as a best practice, steel above 600 MPa should be checked for micro-cracking after straightening. The location of the damage is one factor that may have been considered during damage analysis. There may not be access to properly straighten a part. Another factor is the shape of the part in the damaged area. It is difficult to straighten a part with several different curves or convoluted areas. Additionally, if the area is a crush zone, straightening is not an option. -Sectioning Defined – sectioning is defined as the process of cutting a portion of a part based on the location of collision damage and vehicle maker recommendations, removing the damaged portion of the part, and installing a portion of the undamaged service part. Some service parts, however, are provided pre-cut based on the sectioning location, so cutting the service part is not necessary. Sectioning is a process that is done away from a factory seam -Benefits of Sectioning – there are several benefits of sectioning a part, such as: -Allowing a smaller area of a part to be replaced -Disturbing fewer original welds and factory seams -Disturbing less of the original corrosion protection -Requiring less disassembly -Preventing a vehicle from becoming a total loss -Note: Overall, sectioning can be a much less invasive repair by keeping the repair to a much smaller area of the vehicle Sectioning Guidelines -General Sectioning Guidelines – years ago, general sectioning guidelines were developed to assist collision repair technicians in choosing the appropriate location in the event the vehicle maker did not provide a location. These guidelines were: -Originally based on the extensive use of mild steel throughout the vehicle structure -Applied when vehicle construction had more simplistic part shapes -Used when there were fewer reinforcements compared to today’s vehicles. -Note: General sectioning guidelines are no longer used -Sectioning and Newer Vehicle Designs – general sectioning guidelines are no longer used for a variety of reasons. Newer vehicles use more inner reinforcements or use reinforcements that cannot be exposed to heat. Increased safety standards have also resulted in vehicle makers making the passenger compartment more intrusion-resistant. Newer designs use a considerable amount of thinner gauge, high-strength steel to lighten the vehicle structure. An increasing amount of tailor-rolled and tailor-welded blanks are used as well, making it difficult to identify crush zones, as there are no distinct lines that show the variations in steel strength. Additionally, current vehicles have more complex shapes used in the vehicle design. Sectioning Overview (2/4) Cutting and Welding Techniques Making Cuts for Sectioning Joints -Making Initial Cuts – to remove the bulk of the panel for sectioning, initial cuts are made into the panel. When making initial cuts: -Cut the parts, both the service and damaged parts, long enough so that they can be trimmed to size -Provide clearance by removing the spot welds and separating the outer panel from the inner panel on the damaged part, making sure not to damage flanges or reinforcements -Use scrap metal to protect the adjacent panels from chisel marks or saw cuts. The term ‘initial cut’ may also be referred to as a ‘rough cut’ -Cut-and-Join – when using the cut-and-join cutting technique, the replacement part must be overlapped with the existing part. Both are then cut at the same time somewhere in the overlap area. The cut-and-join technique is often used when making an open butt joint, but may be used when making a butt joint with backing -Lap-and-Scribe – when using the lap-and-scribe cutting technique: -The service part is overlapped with the existing part -A mark is scribed at the end of the service part -The service part is removed and a cut is made at the scribed location on the damaged existing part -Recommended Cutting Tools – when sectioning parts, the proper cutting tools must be used. This ensures a good clean cut using durable cutting equipment. Cutoff wheels or saw blades are commonly used. It may be helpful to identify the steel strength when selecting a cutting tool. The cutting tool must be able to handle high-strength steel parts. Note that the thickness of the cutting tool used can affect the width of the root gap. For example, a thin cutoff wheel or saw blade will minimize root gap on thin metal. When referencing cutting blades or wheels, the term ‘kerf’ may be used to refer to its thickness -Cutting to Avoid Reinforcements – reinforcements should not be cut when cutting a part for sectioning. Cutting into a reinforcement generally requires the reinforcement to be replaced. Reinforcements may be identified: -In the body repair manual -By panel flanges – panel flanges with three or more visible edges have an interior panel -By spot weld configuration – some spot welds that appear in the middle of a panel are a good indication that a reinforcement is present -Test-Fitting a Replacement Section – test-fitting: -May be done using clamps -May be done using fixtures if using a fixture measuring system -May require that the vehicle rests on its suspension. This varies according to vehicle stiffness. As more HSS and UHSS are used in vehicle construction, the vehicles tend to sag less under the weight of the drivetrain -Requires verification through measurements and adjacent panel fit-up -May require trimming back the service panel and the existing panel. Minor straightening of the panel edges may also be required to achieve proper fit with the adjacent panel -Note: When installing a replacement part, it must be test-fitted to ensure everything is in the proper location before the replacement part is welded in place Welding Sectioning Joints -Proper Root Gap – because an open butt joint has no backing, ensuring that you have the proper root gap is critical. Having the proper root gap can be the difference between having a solid weld, and one that has holes or poor penetration and panel warpage. Having proper root gap is also required when working on a butt joint with backing. If the gap is too narrow or nonexistent, there may be inadequate penetration into the backing. If its too wide, you can have burnthrough or it can be difficult to fill the gap, requiring extra passes -Frame Thickness – frames are typically thicker than most unibody parts. For example, exterior unibody panels typically measure around 0.8mm thick. Unibody frame rails and reinforcements can measure up to 1.75 mm thick. Full-frame rails on light-duty pickup trucks and SUVs commonly measure 3-4 mm thick, and can be much thicker on heavy-duty trucks. If not specified by the vehicle maker, it is a good practice to measure the thickness of the part to be welded to ensure the proper welding equipment and settings are used -Effect of Welding on Joint Area – heat can affect the properties of HSS and UHSS, making it considerably weaker. As material strength increases, the strength of the welded joint area will not be as strong as the undamaged portion of the part. Therefore, the metal adjacent to the weld is weakened and can become an unintended collapse zone -Tack Welding – after the section has been fit-up and appears to be in the proper location, it is tack welded in place. Tack welding allows the technician to: -Make final measurements to be made with the part partially welded in place -Verify the part is in the proper position before final welds are made -Gas Metal Arc Welds for Section Joints – it is important to have proper technique to ensure a quality weld. Proper gas metal arc (GMA) welding requires: -Monitoring the amount of heat being put into a weld. Excessive heat can affect the integrity of the joint, warp panels, or cause burnthrough -Making test welds on similar gauge scrap metal to tune the welder -Visual inspections and destructive tests should be done on test welds -Making stitch or skip welds in alternating locations will control heat in the joint -Making GMA Welds – skip welds are made far from the previous weld to minimize the heat-affect zone. Stitch welds are made adjacent to each other, but a brief pause is used between welds to allow metal to cool down. Similar to fillet welds, making plug welds all in one area can put too much heat into one area of the part. For this reason, alternate locations when making plug welds -GMA Weld Considerations – additional considerations for proper GMA welding include: -Offset sectioning joints – this may be recommended by some vehicle makers to stagger the heat-affect areas -Gaps create corrosion hot spots and unintended collapse zones -Do not use body filler, seam sealer, foam, or undercoating to fill gaps -Ensure that any gaps in the sectioning joint are completely welded closed -Welding Corners – when welding either inside or outside corners on a sectioning joint: -Start about 13 mm before the corner and weld about 13 mm past the corner -Never start a weld at the corner, as the weld might start cold, providing inadequate penetration -Plug Welds for Sectioning Joints – plug welds may be required when making a butt joint with backing to hold the backing in place. Some vehicle makers may recommend using a butt joint with backing where a larger insert is used. When making a butt joint with backing, alternate locations for both plug welds (if used) and fillet welds to control the heat-affect. Start on one side of the joint, then proceed to the other side. Plug weld hole size recommendations vary by vehicle maker. Typically they are between 6-10 mm, with 8 mm being the most common. Hole size often depends on the metal thickness -Using Adhesives – vehicle makers may have procedures that utilize adhesive alone on certain section joints; this is typically on outer body panels when specified. Adhesive may also be used in combination with plug welds, spot welds, or rivets. When used with plug welds, adhesive must be applied away from the weld zone. When used with spot welds, adhesive must be applied over the entire flange for corrosion protection. Using welds, spot or plug, and adhesive to attach a panel is called ‘weld bonding’. Note: Adhesive may be used to make a sectioning joint when recommended by the vehicle maker. Sectioning joints made with adhesive can help with strength and rigidity of the joint -Adhesive Butt Joints with Backing – some vehicle makers recommend a butt joint with backing sectioning joint that uses adhesives only. When making this type of joint, the backing is adhesively bonded in place. The mating surfaces of the backing and the panels being joined are sanded to bare metal before the adhesive is applied -Using Adhesive and Rivets – some vehicle makers recommend sectioning procedures that do not use any welds, but rather adhesives and rivets. Sectioning joints that use adhesive and rivets instead of welds are primarily used on outer body panels and frame rails Sectioning Overview (3/4) Sectioning Joint Types GMA Welded Sectioning Joints -Sectioning Joint Usage – GMA welded sectioning joints include an open butt joint, a butt joint with backing, and an offset joint, as well as a lap joint, which can be flat, flanged, or tapered. Joint usage varies by vehicle maker and is often based on joint location. An open butt joint may be preferred in visible areas, such as a sail panel, while a lap joint may be recommended in hidden areas. Joint usage may also depend on heat exposure. Lap joints may be used on heat-sensitive steel to limit the heat-affect zone. Regardless of the joint used, heat-control techniques should be used to avoid panel warpage and limit the heat-affect zone -Left = Butt Joint with Backing. Middle = Tapered Lap Joint. Right = Offset Joint -Vehicle Maker Recommendations – closely follow vehicle maker recommendations when determining which type of sectioning joint to use. Be sure to use the recommended joint for the part being sectioned. Do not assume that because a butt joint with backing was used on a rear rail, that the same joint can be used on a front rail. Joint types are vehicle-maker specific and are not interchangeable. In other words, do not use recommendations from one vehicle maker on a different vehicle. Always verify the joint type using the body repair manual. Note: The front rail shown here has multiple sectioning locations indicated by the black lines on the rail Making Sectioning Joints -Butt Joint with Backing – a butt joint with backing is made by butting two panels close together and using a backing plate behind the sectioning joint. A root gap between 1-3 metal thicknesses wide is commonly required. This varies according to the vehicle maker, and can even vary depending on the thickness of the metal. Some vehicle makers recommend a root gap that is 1.5 times the metal thickness at the sectioning joint. The joint is welded using a continuous weld bead, using a skip or stitch technique. Backings may either be fabricated or provided with the service part -50 mm sleeve -Backing Considerations – a backing is used to align the service part with the existing part. The sole purpose for some backings is for alignment. A backing also serves to maintain proper fusion and weld penetration while reducing burnthrough. It is important to maintain a consistent root gap – the procedure may have a specific root gap recommendation. Backings may alter the strength of a joint, changing crash characteristics. For this reason, backings should only be used when recommended -Making a Backing – when making a backing, the piece may be cut from a section of the damaged part or leftover service part. Flanges must be removed for the part to fit properly. Using a piece from the removed part ensures similarity in the contour and corrosion protection applied to the part. Another option is to use a multiple piece backing. A multiple piece backing may be used to provide a backing for the entire circumference of the part. This may require trimming the pieces together to fit inside the contours of the part and tack welding them together. -Backing Size – backing sizes can vary from vehicle maker to vehicle maker; however, sometimes it is up to the technician to determine the width of the backing. When determining the size, it may vary according to the cross section of the part being sectioning. For example, a rocker panel would have a larger insert than an A-pillar. The backing size may depend on available space on either side of the joint. In some situations, the reinforcements, holes, and part shape may limit the size of the backing. If the backing width is left to the technician, it is recommended to use the smallest size possible to limit potential corrosion issues -Preparing a Backing – before attaching the backing into the existing part, trim the flanges and grind off any burrs to help with a smooth fit. Apply weld-through primer to the bare metal area on the backing if recommended by the vehicle maker. Drill plug weld holes into the service part and existing part if recommended by the vehicle maker. Some vehicle makers recommend drilling 8 mm plug weld holes in the outer panel to attach the backing. Be sure to follow the vehicle maker recommendations for the hole size, number of plug welds, and distance -Pre-Sleeved Backing – some vehicle makers may service a backing separate from the service part. Some are shipped with the backing already installed. This screen shows a lower front rail sectioning service part that is shipped with the backing already fitted into the end of the part. Once the part is installed, the service section is plug welded in place, followed by a fillet weld along the seam. In some instances, alternative attachment methods, such as rivet bonding, may be specified by the vehicle maker -Flat Lap Joint – a flat lap joint is the most basic type of sectioning joint, where one sheet metal part laps over another. -Overlap Considerations – when making flat lap joints, a 25 mm overlap is commonly recommended. Plug welds may also be applied to the overlap area. Some vehicle makers state that this type of joint is helpful to reduce the heat-affect on reinforcements. Flat lap joints may be used on the pillars, rocker panels, and some rails. Always verify specific recommendations using the vehicle maker body repair manual -Making a Flat Lap Joint – when making a flat lap joint, note that body filler may be required to hide the joint in visible areas. A fillet weld is used at the section joint. With a flat lap joint the overlapped area is thicker, which can cause problems with trim attachment. For example, if there is an overlap joint in the door ring area, the weatherstripping may be difficult to install over the thicker joint area -Flanged Lap Joint – a flanged lap joint is where one sheet metal part is formed into a flange to accept the other sheet metal part. This results in a level outer surface -Applications – when making a flanged lap joint on pillars and rocker panels, an open butt joint or butt joint with backing is made on the pinchweld flange, depending on the part design. Notches may be made on the flange to ensure only one thickness, allowing weatherstripping and trim to fit properly. Note: Vehicle makers may have a combination of flanged lap and open butt joints based on the part design -Creating a Flange – when making a flanged lap joint: -Higher strength steel can make the flanging process more difficult -Manual or pneumatic flanging tools can be used to create the flange -Hand tools, such as a hammer and dolly, can be used to create the flange -Note: The flanging process may also inadvertently straighten a curved panel. For example, when flanging a sail panel, the curve of the sail panel can be straightened by the flanging process. If this happens, the curve must be put back into the panel so the contour is restored -Making a Tapered Lap Joint – when making a tapered lap joint, the ends of one part are to be bent inward at a 45-degree angle or less, depending on the vehicle maker recommendations. Corners are cut out so the ends can be bent downward. The piece with the bent down tabs is inserted into the existing piece. The bent down tabs serve as a backing for the welding process. -Offset Joints – offset joints are used: -For uniside assemblies and some front rail sectioning procedures -With lap joints, open butt joints, or butt joints with backing -When replacing two or more panels of a multi-layer assembly -To stagger the joint line, instead of having a single joint line completely around the circumference of the part. This distributes the joint stress, offsets the heat-affect zone, and helps with part alignment Sectioning Overview (4/4) Sectioning Considerations Part-Specific Sectioning Considerations -Part Specific Considerations – when sectioning, there are many part-specific considerations. -A-, B-, and C-Pillar – A-, B-, and C-Pillars often have UHSS reinforcements with limited sectioning procedures. These parts are often equipped with restraint systems on the vehicle interior side of the part. Restraint system parts must be removed or relocated before any repairs are begun. Pillars often contain materials, such as foam, which must be removed before welding and cutting operations take place. Materials must be reinstalled if removed. -Floor Pan – sectioning the passenger compartment floor is NOT recommended unless there is a procedure from the vehicle maker. Sectioning procedures for the rear trunk floor are more common. Typically a lap joint is made and often plug welded to the rear crossmember. Some procedures require overlap of the new floor over the existing floor. Seam sealers must be replaced. In some instances, even if there was no seam sealer on the trunk floor originally, it may be added to protect the sectioning joint. The rear body panel may need to be removed depending on the vehicle design -Front Rail – Lower/Upper – when sectioning the upper or lower rail on a vehicle -Crush zones are usually avoided when sectioning locations are chosen by the vehicle maker -Adjacent panels may need to be removed, such as the strut tower assembly, apron, or radiator core support -Some of the sectioning locations may require removal of the drivetrain -Radiator Core Support – sectioning is rarely done for repair of the radiator core support. Some procedures do exist, but only on older model vehicles. When sectioning, make sure the part is in the proper position - some vehicles have airbag sensors placed on the radiator core support. If the position of the support is off, or the strength is changed, this could delay or expedite deployment of the front airbags -Rear Body Panel – rear body panels may be made from UHSS; however, this is rare. Most rear body panels are made from a lower-strength steel. Sectioning procedures may be available for the rear body panel -Rear Rail -Considerations for sectioning the rear rail include possible removal of the trunk floor. Removal of the rear crossmember may also be required. The locations for sectioning depend on the vehicle maker recommendations -Rocker Panel – rocker panels: -Have reinforcements made from UHSS, and these are generally not sectioned -May have multiple reinforcements behind the base of the pillars -Have outer panels that typically have multiple sectioning locations -Are generally part of a uniside assembly -May have foam -Uniside – due to the lower strength of steel used for uniside construction, this particular part often has multiple sectioning locations. When sectioning unisides: -Make sure to avoid the reinforcement behind the panel during cutting operations -Weld-bonding is a common attachment method. As mentioned earlier in the course, some sectioning joints use bonding alone -Butt joints and butt joints with backing are often used for better joint appearance Sectioning Joint Considerations -Ultra-High-Strength Steel and Sectioning – not all UHSS parts are off-limits for sectioning. Some vehicle makers provide specific instructions for sectioning their UHSS parts. For example, some vehicle makers provide A- and B-pillar reinforcement sectioning procedures. Regardless of the vehicle maker, always follow the service procedures. Do not section UHSS parts without the vehicle maker’s recommendation -Cutting Outer Panels for Access - in the past, it was common practice to cut out access windows so the backside of a part could be accessed for straightening. For example, a portion of the trunk floor may have been removed to access the backside of the rear rail. When this si done, the area that is cut out is considered a sectioning joint. This is not recommended without a specific procedure. There are some vehicle makers that do allow windows to be cut. Often it is for adjacent part removal rather than straightening. Note: A vehicle maker may recommend an access window be cut when sectioning a part that is located beneath an outer panel -Sectioning Hydroformed Parts – it is often thought that hydroformed parts cannot be sectioned due to the part design. However, this is not necessarily true. Sectioning may sometimes be done. The sectioning decision is based more on steel strength and function than the forming process. Sectioning hydroformed parts still requires that a vehicle maker recommendation exists, but it is important to note that just because a part is hydroformed, does not mean it must be replaced at factory seams -Sectioning in Foam-Filled Areas – foam filler may be installed directly behind a sectioning joint or behind a portion of the part being sectioned. When working with foam filler, make sure it is removed from the weld zone before welding. Most foam is flammable and will burn if ignited from the heat of the welding process -Preserving Foam Fillers – if foam is inside the part, it may be beneficial to preserve the shape and integrity of the original foam material when separating panels or assemblies. In some instances, the foam piece can be installed by bonding it back in place. Foam removed during the sectioning process must be reinstalled. If the foam filler is directly behind the sectioning joint, it is replaced following the welding process. This may be done through an access hole. Foam filler may also be applied after corrosion protection is applied. Some replacement sections may come from the vehicle maker with foam filler installed -Foam Filler Precautions – do not weld near exposed foam filler. Most foam filler is combustible and will begin to melt if exposed to heat higher than about 575 degrees Fahrenheit. When cured foam filler begins to melt, it emits toxic chemicals, including carbon monoxide and cyanide gas. The foam filler will also melt when heat is applied during straightening, or if studs are welded to the part for pulling. Toxic fumes from the metled foam filler will escape from the foam-filled part. The foam filler inside a foam-filled part will not melt when it is cut with a saw. Note: Use respiratory protection when heating foam-filled parts. Other personal protection includes wearing safety glasses, neoprene gloves, and long-sleeved work clothes. Reference the safety data sheet (SDS) for additional information -Dressing Welds – dressing welds gives the repair its finished appearance. When dressing welds: -Make sure it is done in visible areas -Do not grind to the point where the metal is thinner than the adjacent metal -Do not grind into the base metal, as this will weaken the joint -Note: Some vehicle makers may recommend using dye penetrant to check for cracks in the weld after dressing the weld -Corrosion Protection – when completing the sectioning repair, apply corrosion protection to the backside and front side of the sectioning areas. Corrosion protecting the backside is especially important, as heat from the welding process has burned away the corrosion protection. Some vehicle makers may recommend an anti-corrosion compound. If a backing was used, moisture and contaminants can be held between the backing and part, making it particularly prone to corrosion. Anti-corrosion compound is often recommended in these areas, and multiple applications may be recommended Spray Guns -Overview – spray guns are used to apply coatings such as primers, sealers, basecoats, and clearcoats. It does this by atomizing, or breaking down the material into small particles, as it exits the spray gun -Spray Gun Designs – spray guns may be gravity, siphon, or pressure feed -Gravity Feed – gravity feed spray guns operate by the paint cup being located above the spray gun body. Fluid will flow out of a gravity feed spray gun when there is no air pressure and the trigger is pulsed. This is because material moves through the spray gun by gravitational force and not vacuum. -Siphon Feed- a siphon feed spray gun has the paint cup located below the spray gun body. The material is drawn out of the spray gun by vacuum created by the air cap. Atmospheric pressure inside the paint cup forces the material up through the spray gun body and out of the fluid needle -Pressure Feed – pressure feed spray guns, though common at one time in the industry for spraying refinish materials, are now typically used to apply chip-resistant coatings and corrosion protection materials. A pressure feed spray gun operates by pressurizing the cup which allows thicker materials to be atomized -Spray Gun Types – spray guns used for refinishing may be high pressure, or HP, high volume low pressure, or HVLP, reduced pressure, or RP, and low volume pressure, or LVLP. An HP spray gun uses high pressure, low volume air. This type of spray gun may also be referenced to as conventional. HP spray guns are considered old technology. HVLP, LVLP, and RP spray guns are considered high transfer efficiency equipment. -Air Pressure Comparison – HVLP spray guns are similar in construction and adjustments to HP spray guns, but use lower air pressure than HP spray guns. HVLP spray guns generally use about 25-35 psi at the inlet instead of 70-90 psi like an HP spray gun. When the air pressure is measured at the air cap, the pressure is reduced to 10 psi or less. HVLP spray guns also require large amounts of air. Some spray guns may require up to 25 cubic feet per minute, or cfm -HVLP Spray Guns – HVLP spray guns produce less overspray when compared to HP spray guns. HVLP spray guns are held closer to the surface and have better transfer efficiency than HP spray guns. This may also reduce the amount of refinish material that is used. Proper training and practice are necessary for material use to be reduced. Additionally, HVLP spray guns may extend the intervals between spraybooth cleaning. This is a result of better control and less material use. -RP Spray Guns – an RP spray gun generally uses a reduced inlet pressure of 25-32 psi that allows the gun to achieve transfer efficiency above 65%, even though the gun does not have 10 psi or less at the air cap. This type of spray gun is primarily used for high solid clearcoats, as it aids in the atomization and evaporation of solvents and reducers to allow for an improved transfer of material. -LVLP Spray Guns – LVLP spray guns use a lower volume of air to atomize the paint at the cap. LVLP spray guns have a slight reduction in spraying speed with a softer spray and lower overspray levels. They are not designed to spray large areas -Spray Gun Body – the body of the spray gun attaches to the paint cup and houses many working parts. All of the air passages and fluid passages are machined through the body of the spray gun. Most spray gun bodies are made of aluminum. HVLP spray guns have larger air passages through the spray gun body. This allows higher volumes of compressed air -Spray Gun Trigger – the spray gun trigger involves two steps. In the first step, the trigger opens the air valves, filling the chambers with compressed air. The second step pulls the fluid needle off its seat. This allows the refinish material to flow past the needle tip and into the airstream to be atomized. The trigger opens and closes the air valve and fluid valve. When a trigger is pulled part of the way, the air valve is opened. When the trigger is pulled further, the fluid valve is opened because the fluid needle is pulsed off of its seat in the fluid nozzle -Air Valve – the air valve allows the flow of air when the valve is moved away from its seat. The amount of air is controlled by the air adjusting valve and pattern control knob -Paint Cup – the paint cup is mounted to the spray gun body. It may be disposable plastic with a built-in strainer. Refer to the paint maker’s technical data sheet to verify the correct strainer type to use. Paint cups may also be metal, though it may be recommended to use a coated or plastic paint cup with certain materials, such as etching primers and waterborne products, as they may cause damage to uncoated metal paint cups.
