Some refinish materials should not be left in a metal paint cup for extended periods of time -Waterborne Spray Guns – spray guns for waterborne refinish materials have a needle and nozzle typically made of stainless steel. The paint cups are made of plastic or stainless steel. Most plastic paint cups are disposable, reducing the amount of cleanup required. If the spray gun is aluminum, it will have special coatings to resist corrosion. Bare aluminum may corrode over time from repeated exposure to waterborne refinish materials. It is important to follow the spray gun maker’s procedures to avoid premature corrosion. There are special considerations when selecting a spray gun for waterborne refinish materials. Waterborne spray guns must have corrosion-resistant parts. Some older model solvent-borne spray guns may have conversion kits available to be able to spray waterborne refinish materials. -Fluid Needle and Nozzle – the fluid needles and fluid nozzles are matched to the spray gun and the material being sprayed to create a quality finish. They should not be interchanged with other needle and nozzle sizes outside of the manufacturer’s recommendations for the type of spray gun and material being sprayed. Fluid needles and nozzles may be numerically identified. The larger the number, the larger the orifices. Larger numbers and diameter orifices are used to spray thicker materials -Fluid Needle – the fluid needle is opened and closed by the trigger. It is used to control the amount of fluid sent to the air stream. The opening between the nozzle seat and tip of the fluid needle valve determines the amount of fluid that can flow through -Fluid Nozzle – the fluid nozzle works with the fluid needle valve to meter fluid into the air stream. It forms an internal seat for the fluid needle valve to shut off the fluid flow. The fluid nozzle has openings that determine the maximum amount of fluid that can be delivered. The nozzle also provides air flow to the air cap. -Air Caps – air caps can impact the speed and quality of refinish operations -Air Cap Passages – the air cap passages direct air past the fluid needle. These passages are the center hole, auxiliary holes, and side holes. The center hole creates a vacuum to pull refinish material into the air stream. The auxiliary holes are in the middle and help achieve full atomization. The side holes use air pressure to form the spray pattern. This part of the air cap may also be referred to as the air horns -Air Cap Types – the type of air cap may also affect the amount of fluid flow through the spray gun and air pressure required to deliver satisfactory results. The paint maker may recommend a specific air cap for proper application of individual refinish materials. Spray gun air caps are typically coated with a nickel plating on the outside and brass on the inside. In addition to providing corrosion resistance, this special coating helps in cleaning waterborne refinishing materials from the spray gun -Fan Adjustment Knob – the fan adjustment knob is generally located on the top of the spray gun body, though it may also be found on the side of the spray gun. The fan adjustment is used to control the size of the spray pattern. Along with changing the physical spray pattern, the fan adjustment can also affect the atomization of the material being sprayed. -Fan Adjustment – spray guns may produce a spray pattern that is a small diameter circle or a long oblong pattern. To create a small, round pattern with dense coverage, turn in the knob all the way. To form a vertical, elongated pattern with lighter coverage, turn the knob gradually out. If turned too far, the pattern will be too heavy on the ends and too light in the middle. -Fluid Control Adjustment – the fluid control adjustment is used to change the amount of material released from the spray gun by limiting the travel of the fluid needle. The fluid control is located at the rear of a spray gun. Fluid control adjustment needs to be made before the refinish technician begins applying any type of material. An adjustment set too light will cause poor coverage, or hiding, and an adjustment set too heavy may cause runs. Turning the fluid control knob clockwise reduces the distance the fluid needle can open. Turning the knob counterclockwise increases the distance the fluid needle can open. -9 Pins that make up a typical HVLP spray gun -Air Cap -Coupling -Fluid Adjustment Knob -Fluid Control Knob -Fluid Needle -Fluid Nozzle -Pressure Gauge and Valve -Spray Gun Body -Trigger -Spray Gun Setup – before using a spray gun, verify that the correct fluid needle, nozzle, and air cap have been selected for the spray gun and refinish material being used. Make fluid adjustment for the material being sprayed and make sure the compressed air source is clean, dry, and regulated. The air source must provide the proper air volume and pressure according to the spray gun maker specifications. -Inlet Air Pressure – when adjusting air pressure at the inlet of the spray gun, keep in mind that spray gun makers will state a specific inlet air pressure. The proper inlet air pressure will reduce pressure at the air cap with the fan control wide open. The air pressure at the cap for an HVLP spray gun is 10 psi. -Adjusting Inlet Air Pressure – as the fan control is closed, the air cap pressure increases. Air regulators on the wall adjust the air supply to pressures required by the spray gun maker. Begin adjusting the air pressure at the regulator and allow for pressure drops if you are using long air hoses. The pressure gauge at the spray gun should be used to measure inlet air pressure. You can make minor air pressure adjustments at the valve on the gun. -Spray Gun Settings – when adjusting a spray gun, the refinish technician needs to be aware of what adjustments are needed. Adjustment options on the spray gun include fan adjustment and fluid control -Setup Charts – spray gun and paint makers compile a list of information for the refinish technician. This information offers a starting point for the technician when setting up a spray gun. The information provided on the chart helps eliminate the guesswork for what fluid needle, fluid nozzle, and air cap should be used for specific materials being sprayed. Paint makers post the required fluid needle and nozzle size for optimum spray results. Refer to their spray gun specifications for which fluid needle and nozzle will work the best -Testing for Fluid Distribution – when testing for fluid distribution, begin with rotating the air cap so that the air horns are straight up and down. This will produce a horizontal spray pattern. Hold the spray gun 8-10” from the surface and pull the trigger until the refinish material begins to run. The runs will be about the same length if all adjustments are correct. If the sides sag more, the air pressure is too high or the fan adjustment knob is set too wide. If the center sags more, either turn out the fan adjustment knob, turn in the fluid control knob, or increase air pressure -Spraying a Test Pattern – a spray test pattern is used to ensure the gun is applying an even coat to the panel. A spray test pattern should be sprayed before applying any refinish material to the vehicle. That way any problems can be corrected. A spray test pattern should be sprayed on masking paper, not on the spraybooth walls. Hold the spray gun about 8-10” from the surface, pull the trigger, and release it quickly. The spray pattern should be a slender, vertical oval, with even coverage. An improper spray pattern may present an irregular pattern, like a crescent moon, a wise, center pattern, or a constricted, center pattern, similar to a figure eight -Proper Spray Gun Use – to properly use a spray gun, hold the spray gun the recommended distance from the panel while leveling and perpendicular to the panel. Keep the spray gun perpendicular to the surface at all times, following the angles and curves of the vehicle with a wrist motion. Being moving before pulling the trigger. This should occur before nearing a refinish panel. Refinish material application should start just before reaching the edge of the panel. Move the spray gun in a steady, even motion. Release the trigger before stopping movement of the spray gun. Do not fan the spray gun. Fanning is permitted only when spraying a small spot repair area. Refer to the paint maker’s technical data sheet for spray gun setup and application recommendations. -Personal Protection – working with hazardous materials can present health problems if you do not follow important safety precautions -Respirator – respirators are required because refinish materials can irritate the nose, throat, and lungs. OSHA regulations require the use of respiratory protection. For the best protection, a fresh-air respirator is recommended over a cartridge-type respirator. Protective goggles should also be worn is using a half-face respirator. -Paint Suit – a paint suit protects the technician from exposure to refinish materials and overspray. It also provides full body protection because it is worn over other clothing. Long sleeves and pants may also be worn to cover as much skin as possible -Head Sock – a head sock or painter’s cap is used to help prevent hazardous materials from entering the body through the scalp. It should be worn with mask-type respirators -Gloves – wear chemical-resistant gloves, such as nitrile gloves. They protect your hands from exposure to refinish materials -Spray Gun Maintenance – when maintaining a spray gun, lubricate moving parts on a regular basis to help minimize wear and prolong the life of a spray gun. When lubricating a spray gun, use a lubricant that is designed to be used on spray guns, is compatible with refinish materials, and will not contaminate the finish. Do not use conventional oil, such as engine oil, transmission fluid, WD-40, spray or liquid silicone, Liquid Wrench, or air tool oil. These will cause defects in the refinish materials. -Spray Gun Maintenance (cont’d) – it is also important to be able to identify what parts wear out, how often they need replacement, and the availability of parts for the specific spray gun. Know if the spray gun needs to be returned to the manufacturer for repair, or if a rebuild kit is available and what parts are included in the kit. -Spray Gun Cleaning – spray guns should be properly cleaned and maintained to prevent refinish defects. Only use tools that are designed specifically for cleaning the fluid nozzle. For example, do not use paperclips to clean fluid nozzle holes. Doing so can cause damage and affect the spray pattern -Waterborne Cleaning – for waterborne cleaning, clean the spray gun immediately after use. If a spray gun is left to sit with refinishing material still inside, the waterborne material may etch the inner surface, making future cleaning difficult. The spray gun maker may recommend that cleaning solution not enter the air passage. This prevents any etching of the air passage walls. Clean the spray gun with the paint maker’s recommended solution. This helps ensure that the spray gun continues to operate properly and contaminants are not transferred to other refinishing jobs. Once cleaned, use compressed air to dry the inside and outside of the spray gun that has been cleaned with waterborne materials to prevent corrosion issues. -Solvent-Borne Cleaning – for solvent-borne cleaning, do not immerse and soak a spray gun in solvent. Doing so can cause the holes and passages to become blocked with loosened residual refinish materials. This may also strip the needed lubrication. If switching from waterborne to solvent-borne material, or vice versa, make sure the spray gun is properly flushed and dried thoroughly before use. Failure to do so can alter the new refinish material and create sludge in the spray gun. Steel GMA Welding Theory (1/2) Gas Metal Arc Welding Introduction Steel GMA Welding Introductory Basics -Gas Metal Arc Welding Overview – gas metal arc or GMC welding is an electric arc welding process. For mayn years it has been referred to as MIG welding and often mistakenly. MIG welding refers to metal inert gas where an inert gas is used to shield the weld puddle. This is typically associated with welding aluminum. MAG (metal active gas) is where the shielding gas has a reactive component in the welding process, which is typically when welding steel. GMA welding: -Is a consumable electrode, wire feed welding process -Has a welding electrode that is a continuously fed spool of wire -Deposits an electrode as a filler metal into a molten weld puddle to produce a weld bead -Produces heat using a short electric arc between the base metal and a bare wire electrode -Uses an externally supplied shielding gas to protect the molten weld pool from atmospheric contamination that would compromise the quality of the weld -Vehicle Protection – induction of current or voltage spikes can destroy sensitive electronic parts. When welding: -Keep the current path short by placing the work clamp close to the weld site -Disconnect and isolate both battery cables (remove the negative battery cable first) -Keep the welding machine as far away from vehicle computers as possible -Route cables away from computers or sensors -Remove computers or other sensitive electronic parts if welding close than 300 mm or 12 in -Cover glass and other areas of the vehicle with a welding blanket to protect from welding sparks -Personal Safety – for safety purposes there are specific clothing, equipment, and practices recommended to protect technicians when welding. Safety equipment and clothing include the following: -NIOSH-approved welding respirator -Welding helmet -Ear protection -Safety glasses -Leather gloves, sleeves, and welding jacket -Leather boots -No matches or lighters in pockets -Fire extinguisher nearby Electrical Principles of GMA Welding -Basic Electrical Overview – -in order to understand how welding works, it is important to understand some of the basics of electricity. Basic electrical terms include: -Voltage -Current -Resistance -Voltage – voltage is the pressure that is applied to an electrical circuit that forces current to move through a conductor. The more voltage, the greater the current flow -Current – current is the flow of electrons in a circuit. It is the number of electrons that flow past a given point in a circuit in a given period of time. Current may also be called amperage and is measured in amps -Resistance – resistance limits the flow of current. When there is more resistance, less current flows. Resistance is overcome by voltage. Increasing the voltage in a circuit will apply more pressure to the electrons and force a higher flow of current. If resistance in a circuit is increased, and we want current flow to remain the same, then the voltage must be increased to push the current flow through the point of higher resistance -Electricity in GMA Welding – arc welding converts electrical energy to heat or thermal energy. The heat of the arch melts both the base metal and the electrode. The electrical output of the welder through the filler wire is what produces this heat. The amount of heat put into the base metal is a function of the voltage and current outputs of the welder and the travel speed at which the welding gun is moving. During welding, there is enough heat generated to bring metal to a molten state -Current and Wire Speed – wire speed equals current. Increasing wire speed creates less resistance due to the shorter arc length. Increasing the wire speed will put more wire, and thus more current, into the weld puddle. Increased current will increase the heat in the weld puddle. The current capacity of the welder must be sufficient for the thickness of the metal being welded. -Bead Penetration – the current output will affect the penetration. The more current, the deeper the weld bead will penetrate into the base metal -Deposition Rate – the amperage output determines the deposition rate of the electrode wire. Current is controlled by the amount of wire being fed into the weld. Higher amperage settings will deposit more electrode wire than lower amperage settings. Amperage settings will have an effect on the weld bead size. Higher current will result in a larger weld bead size than lower current -Voltage Output – the voltage output remains constant once it is set. An exception to this is synergic machines. The voltage output of the welding machine also controls the length and size of the arc. Changing the length and width of the arc has an effect on the height and width of the weld bead. The voltage must be matched to the amperage setting being used because it controls the melting rate of the electrode wire -Synergic Welders – synergic machines do automatically what a technician must do manually on other machines, which is adjust the current and voltage settings together. Synergic machines use a computer to control the voltage and wire speed of the machine. The technician typically sets the welder based on material thickness. Amperage, wire speed, and voltage are set by the machine and may also be fine tuned individually. -Resistance in GMA Welding – resistance is the gap between the electrode wire and the workpiece. The main variable in the welding circuit that affects resistance is electrode extension or stick-out. Resistance in a welding circuit is also affected by arc length and electrode wire diameter. Shorter arc length equals less resistance and longer arc length equals more resistance -Polarity with GMA Welders – GMA welders are plugged into an alternating current or AC power supply. It is important that the AC power source is adequate for proper machine operation. The welding machine converts this AC power into direct current or DC power that is used to make the weld. GMA welders can be set to either straight or reverse polarity. Switching polarity of a GMA welding machine typically involves reversing the welding gun and work cable connections inside the machine -Reverse Polarity (DC Reverse) – reverse polarity (DC reverse) is when there is a positive electrode and negative workpiece. This is most often used for collision repairs because it provides the best weld, leaves less weld metal to grind off, and has a more stable arc. Reverse polarity is also called direct current electrode positive or DCEP -Straight Polarity (DC Straight) – straight polarity (DC straight) is when there is a negative electrode and positive workpiece. It is recommended when welding with flux-cored wire. Due to the current flowing from the electrode wire to the workpiece, straight polarity directs most of the heat to the workpiece rather than the electrode. Straight polarity is called direct current electrode negative or DCEN GMA Welding Transfer Processes -GMA Welding Transfer Methods – the deposit of the molten filler wire into the weld puddle is called transfer. The molten electrode wire may be transferred to the workpiece using one of three methods. These methods are short-circuit, spray-arc, and globular transfer -Short-Circuit Transfer Method – the short-circuit transfer method is the preferred method for welding on most sheet metal. Compared to the other transfer methods, short-circuit transfer generates less heat. This is because of the very thin electrode wire that is used along with lower current and voltage settings when compared to globular and spray transfer -Short-Circuit Transfer Sequence – there is a continuous process occurring at a rate of between 90 and 230 times per second in the short-circuit transfer mode using 0.6mm or .023 inch wire -1. The wire touches or short-circuits the base metal -2. The wire begins to break off from the heat generated -3. The wire separates, transferring metal to the weld puddle, and an arc is ignited between the end of the electrode wire and base meta -4. The heat of the arc flattens the weld puddle and the end of the electrode wire -5. The wire speed overcomes the arc heat -6. The wire touches the base metal and turns off the arc, starting the process over -Spray-Arc Method – spray-arc transfer: -Sprays a stream of tiny molten droplets across the arc to the base metal -Droplets are usually much smaller than the diameter of the electrode wire -Is achieved by using relatively high voltage and wire speed -Produces a humming or buzzing sound when parameters are correct -Globular Method – globular transfer: -Is when large globs of weld metal carried by gravity transfer from the wire across the arc to the weld pool -Globs or droplets are usually much larger than the wire diameter -Is considered a more unstable transfer with a rough weld bead appearance -Have welding parameters, amperage, voltage, and wire speed that are higher than for short-circuit transfer -Will usually result in splatter GMA Welding Equipment -GMA Welding Equipment – regardless of the type of welding machine used, each has a: -Power source -Spool of electrode wire -Wire feeder -Cylinder of shielding gas -Shielding gas regulator and flow meter -Welding gun and cable -Work clamp and cable -Power Source – GAM welders typically have power sources that operate an incoming voltage of either 220 or 110 volts AC. Actual voltages may range from 220 to 240 and 110 to 120. Every GMA welder has a related amperage output and duty cycle for the power source. The power source must be capable of delivering sufficient amperage and voltage to power the welder -Selecting Amperage – verify if the machine is capable of supplying the necessary amperage for a given thickness of metal. For thinner metal, maximum amperage is not required to produce a quality weld. The thickness of the metal being welded determines how much amperage is required to make a good weld. The formula of one amp required for every 0.025mm or 0.001 in of metal thickness is a good starting point -Duty Cycle – the duty cycle is how many minutes out of ten a welding machine can safely operate continuously at a rated amperage. Duty cycle is listed as a percentage and is typically posted on the welding machine. It can also be found in the operators manual. Duty cycle is not always rated at the maximum output of the welding machine. Operating a welding machine beyond its duty cycle will overheat the machine and produce poor quality welds or shut down -Duty Cycle Example – duty cycle will be different than what is listed if the welding machine is being operated at a different amperage than the rated amperage for the listed duty cycle. For example: -A 130-amp machine rated at 40% duty cycle at 90 amps can operate four out of ten minutes continuously at 90 amps. If the machine is operated at less than 90 amps, the machine will be able to run continuously for more than 4 minutes -If the machine is operated at more than 90 amps, the duty cycle will be less -Electrode Wire Spool – -The electrode is a bare metal wire that is wound on a spool and fed through the welding gun to the contact tip. Each type of electrode wire is identified by an American Welding Society, or AWS, code. A common electrode wire used for collision repair is ER70S-6. Using ER70S-6 as an example: -ER – electrode rod -70 – tensile strength of at least 70,000 psi -S – solid wire -6 – chemical makeup of the wire and its ability to keep oxygen out of the weld -Electrode Wire ER70S-3 – the electrode wire ER70S-3 contains a relatively low percentage of deoxidizers which help keep oxygen out of the weld site. It may produce welds of lower tensile and yield strengths when welding at high current or with straight CO2 shielding gas. ER70s-3 wire is recommended by some vehicle makers for full-frame welding procedures -Electrode Wire ER70S-6 – the electrode wire ER70S-6: -Contains high amounts of silicon and manganese -Produces smooth, well-shaped welds -Works well for overhead and vertical welds -Note: This type of electrode wire is recommended for most collision repairs -Uncommon Wires – there are other wires that are not commonly used in collision repairs. Vehicle makers will usually specify the wire type to use and may require a higher strength. Examples of uncommon wires include: -980 MPa welding wire -Flux-cored wire -980 MPa – Honda and Acura have specific welding wire requirements for 440-980 MPa steel. They require 980 MPa welding wire. The wire can be purchased through the Honda tool and Equipment Program. The electrode wire has a tensile strength of 1,011 MPa -Flux-Cored Wire – Flux-cored electrode wire may be recommended for specific applications by some vehicle makers. Flux-cored wire: -Has an inner core of flux material which acts like shielding gas and is designed to protect the weld from contaminants -Some vehicle makers may specify a shielding gas be used with flux-cored wire -Is only available in diameters of 0.8mm ( .030 in) or larger -Is not recommended for steels thinner than 18 gauge -Produces more spatter than solid wire and forms a slag that must be removed after welding -AWS Code for Flux-Cored Wire – the AWS code for flux-cored wire is different than for solid electrode. Using E71TGS as an example: -E – electrode -7 – tensile strength multiplied by ten-thousand (70,000 psi) -1 – can only be used in flat or horizontal position (1 means wire can be used in all positions) -T – tubular wire -GS – use and performance of the electrode wire -Note: The last digit can be any number between 1 and 11, or the letters ‘G’ or ‘GS’ depending on the recommended shielding gas, polarity, and number of passes required for the finished weld -115-Volt GMA Machines – 115-volt GMA welding machines may: -Be originally set up for flux-cored electrodes -Require switching the polarity of the machine -Require a conversion kit to weld with some electrode wires -Not be capable of supplying the current necessary for full-frames -Electrode Wire Diameter – the electrode wire diameter required for welding is based on the thickness of metal and the amperage required to weld a given thickness -0.6mm or .023 in electrode wire is recommended when welding between 30 and 90 amps -0.8mm or .030 in wire is recommended when welding in the 40 to 145 amp range -0.9mm or .035 in wire is recommended when welding in the 75 to 250 amp range -Note: For example, 18-gauge steel, which is 1.2mm thick, requires 48 amps, so it can be concluded that it should be welded with 0.6mm or .023 in electrode wire -Diameter vs Amperage – when the amperage range overlaps another diameter wire: -Either one can be used -Select a wire diameter where the amperage to be used is closest to the middle of the amperage range for the wire. This will help prevent a change in the type of weld transfer. Note: some vehicle makers may have specific recommendations for wire diameter -Wire Feeder – the wire feeder delivers the electrode wire from the welding machine to the contact tip at a constant speed. This speed determines the current -Burnback Control – welding machines may have an adjustable burnback control. This allows the welding current to stay on for a short time after the wire has stopped feeding, which allows the wire to burn back just enough to be ready for the next act. Too much burnback causes a ball on the wire end and an erratic start. This is separate from the wire feed speed -Shielding Gas – the shielding gas -Protects the weld site from the atmosphere that can cause defects in the weld -Is stored in a high-pressure cylinder that must be fastened securely in an upright position -Is a major factor in managing weld-pool heat -Active and Inert Shielding Gas – there are two types of shielding gases available for welding: active and inert -Active shielding gases protect the weld site and combine with the arc to make a hotter weld. CO2 is a common example of an active shielding gas -Inert shielding gases protect the weld site, but not combine with the arc. Argon and helium are common examples of inert shielding gases -Note: Shielding gas can be a single pure gas or a mixture of two or more gases. Active and inert shielding gases may be mixed with each other -Shielding Gas Choice Options – the type of shielding gas that should be used depends on the metal being welded. -75% argon and 25% CO2: -Is the recommended shielding gas for collision repair on mild and high-strength steels -Provides good weld strength properties, produces a smooth and stable arc, and a small weld puddle that sets up quickly. This is an advantage when welding out of position. -Pure CO2: -Reduces amperage required and provides dep weld penetration, but penetrates too deep on thin-gauge metal -Makes unstable arc which increases spatter -Makes the weld much hotter and it is not commonly recommended -Note: The industry is shifting toward more argon in the shielding gas mix, such as 80/20 and 90/10. Depending on location, these combinations can vary -Regulators and Flow Meters – -Regulators: -Attach to the gas cylinder -Reduce cylinder pressure to working pressure -Must be matched to the type of shielding gas used -Flow Meters: -Measure the flow rate of the shielding gas in cubic feet per hour, or cfh -Should be set between 25 and 30 cfh -Have shielding gas flow rates that may need to be increased when there is a breeze in the area where welding is being done -Note: Welding machines may have gas type and flow rate recommendations in the setup chart supplied with the machine -Welding Gun – A GMA welding gun consists of a gun liner, handle, trigger, neck, contact tip, and a shielding gas nozzle. Some may have a gas diffuser. We will cover the liner, contact tip, and nozzle. -Liner – the welding gun liner guides the electrode wire through the welding gun cable from the welding machine to the contact tip -Contact Tip / Tube – the contact tip: -Is responsible for transferring the current to the electrode wire so that an arc can be struck -Is usually made of copper or a copper alloy -Has the inside diameter stamped on the tip that must match the diameter of the electrode wire being used -Will wear over time and require periodic replacement -Shielding Gas Nozzles – the shielding gas nozzle directs the shielding gas toward the weld area and protects the contact tip. Shielding gas nozzleshave an insulator to prevent short-circuiting the machine to the workpiece. Shielding gas nozzles may be tapered or straight. Tapered nozzles are used for most collision repair welding. Straight nozzles are typically used when higher flow of shielding gas is required. There can be an anti-spatter compound applied to reduce spatter building between the nozzle and contact tip -Work Clamps – although work clamp completes the output electrical circuit for GMA welding, it is not the electrical ground for the welding machine. The ground connection is made through the power cord and goes from the machine to the building ground. The work clamp should be attached as close to the workpiece as possible and must be large enough to carry the current load without overheating Welding Machine Setup and Preparation -Welder Setup and Preparation – all maintenance and setup information can be found in the owners manual for the equipment. Before using a welding machine, learn how to: -1. Connect the welding gun and work cables and load the wire spool into the machine -2. Adjust the wire feeder and drive roll pressure for proper feeding of electrode wire -3. Set the flow rate for the shielding gas -4. Set and become familiar with the voltage and wire speed settings -5. Change the contact tip, insulator, and welding gun liner -Welding Area Surface Preparation – all contaminants must be removed from the weld area before welding. This can include the finish, any undercoating, corrosion, dirt, oil, or grease -Weld-Through Primer – weld-through primer should only be used if the vehicle maker recommends it. Apply it in areas where the galvanized coating has been removed. Weld-through primer: -Is available as sprayable or brush-on products -Protects the bare steel, usually with the use of zinc -Needs to have any excess or overspray removed for adhesion for refinish coatings -Should only be on the mating flanges between two parts -Should be removed from the immediate weld zone before welding -May cause porosity if left in the immediate weld zone -Variables – GMA welding depends on a number of operator variables, all of which must work together to make a good weld. These variables include: -Travel speed -Welding gun angle -Welding gun direction -Electrode stick-out -Joint fit-up -Amperage and voltage setting balance -Travel Speed – the technician controls travel speed. This is how fast the technician moves the welding gun along the joint. The travel speed can affect penetration, bead size, and shape. The correct travel speed to use will depend on a number of factors including the thickness of the material being welded, wire speed, and voltage -Slow Travel Speed – a travel speed that is too slow, with all other parameters being correct, will likely result in burn through on thinner gauge metals. Travel speeds that are too slow may also result in large, flat, and wide weld beads -Fast Travel Speed – a travel speed that is too fast, with all other parameters being correct, will result in poor penetration and inadequate fusion. There is not enough heat input into the metal to completely melt the base metal, and there is a reduction in the amount of weld metal deposited. The metal only melts near the surface, so the weld bead is not sufficiently wetted out and cannot penetrate into the base metal. This results in a tall and narrow weld bead that sits on top of the base metal -Welding Gun Angle – another important factor in a quality weld is the angle that the welding gun is held. The welding gun angle can be looked at in two different planes and broken down into two separate angles: travel angle and work angle -Travel Angle – travel angle is how much the welding gun is pointed either toward or away from the direction of gun travel. The amount of travel angle has an effect on weld bead penetration, shielding gas coverage, and arc stability. It also affects the amount of splatter produced by the weld. Excessive travel angles direct the heat and shielding gas away from the weld puddle, which can cause spatter and poor penetration. As travel angle increases, stick-out also increases. Excessive stick-out leads to a wandering and unstable arc -Work Angle – work angle is the angle that the plane of the welding gun has in relation to the joint being welded. Work angle controls where the electrode wire is aimed at the joint, and will vary according to the joint being welded. Work angle affects weld bead penetration and shape and shielding gas coverage. Incorrect work angles may lead to poor shielding gas coverage, inadequate penetration, and a poorly shaped weld bead with undercut. The electrode wire must be pointed where the weld bead is to be placed -Welding Techniques – when GMA welding steel, the welding gun can either be pushed or pulled along the joint -Pull Technique – the pull technique is when the welding gun is pointed at and dragged away from the weld puddle -It may also be called drag, or backhand -Compared to the push technique, the pull technique increases weld penetration. This is because it directs the heat of the arc at the weld puddle and preheated base metal -The pull technique is typically done using a 70 degree travel angle to the base metal, or 30 degree from perpendicular -Push Technique – the push technique is when the welding gun is pointed and pushed away from the weld -The push technique also uses a 70 degree travel angle to the workpiece -The push technique may be called forehand or lead angle -It produces a flatter, wider weld bead with less penetration than the pull technique because the heat from the arc is directed away from the weld puddle and preheated base metal -Stick-Out – another operator-controlled welding variable is stick-out, or contact tip height. Stick-out is the distance from the end of the contact tip to the end of the electrode wire. -When using 0.6mm (.023 in) electrode wire, stick-out should be between 6 and 13 mm or ¼ and ½ in -Stick-out should be held constant along the entire weld pass -For larger diameter wire, stick-out should be increased -Changes in stick-out change the amperage output of the welding machine. If stick-out is too short, resistance will decrease and amperage will increase. This will cause penetration to increase. If a stick-out is too long, the opposite will happen and penetration will decrease. Due to this, stick-out should be kept consistent for the entire length of the weld -Welding Position – it may be required to perform GMA welds in different positions depending on the location of the part. -Flat – welding in the flat positions is usually the easiest -Horizontal – welding in the horizontal position is usually easier if the welding gun is angled upward, but also depends on the joint -Vertical – welding in the vertical position is easier if the welding gun is angled upward and pulled down on thin-gauge metal -Overhead – overhead welds require lower heat settings than other positions to reduce the amount of spatter falling into the shielding gas nozzle or on the technician -Proper Joint Fit-Up – one variable in ensuring proper penetration is joint fit-up. Proper joint fit-up requires: -The mating flanges of the parts being welded to fit tightly together -Having the right amount of root gap on the joints -Using clamps, screws, or tack welds to hold the parts in the position -Removing screws after welding holes shut -Turning a GMA Welder – -To tune a GMA welding machine -1. Set the machine to the recommended voltage settings and set wire speed at mid-range position -2. Make a practice weld and listen for a steady crackling sound or look for a steady arc -While tuning the machine, be sure to: -Keep the proper stick-out for the wire diameter being used -Move the welding gun across the metal -Test the strength of the sample weld -Use the same metal thickness when practicing -Heat Management Techniques – if too much heat is directed at the workpiece during welding, it may distort and weaken the metal. Heat management is a combination of heat settings used, the time spent making the weld, and the location of the weld in relation to other welds just made. Welding techniques used to manage heat buildup include: -Skip welds -Stitch welds -Alternating plug welds -Stitch Welds – a stitch weld is made by stopping briefly between welds to allow the weld to slightly cool. Manual stitch welding may cause porosity in the weld due to the shielding gas flow stopping each time the trigger is released. Some welding machines are equipped with a stitch weld setting that automatically pulses the arc and leaves the shielding gas flowing -Skip Welds – skip welds are made by making welds in random areas along the joint and then filling in the spaces. Another way to make a skip weld is by starting in the middle of the joint and moving outwards toward the edges, alternating from side-to-side. When welding on heavy-gauge material, skip welds can create problems at the start and stop points. Tune the machine for the hottest possible start in these conditions -Alternating Plug Welds – heat management when making plug welds can include avoiding two or more plug welds next to each other. Randomly alternating the location that is welded will help allow areas to cool slightly before another weld is made and limit the buildup of heat. -Weld Penetration – the ability to recognize causes of weld defects is an important skill to develop. One of the most important characteristics of a quality weld is penetration. Here, we will discuss poor weld penetration and good weld penetration. -Poor Penetration – poor penetration may result in incomplete fusion and lead to failure of the weld. Heat settings that are too low, travel speed that is too fast, excess stick-out, not enough root gap, or an incorrect welding gun angle can all cause penetration problems -Good Penetration – there should be more than just a heat mark on the backside of a weld. If a small ripple can be felt on the backside of the workpiece, it is a sign of good weld penetration. This ripple is called melt-through -Adjusting Penetration – this chart can be used as a quick reference guide when adjusting for penetration and bead height problems. It is similar to a chart published by a welding machine maker. The numbers indicate the order in which the variables should be adjusted. For example, to increase penetration, amperage is the first adjustment that should be made -Burnthrough – if the heat settings are too high, it may melt through the workpiece and create a hole. This is called burnthrough. Burnthrough can also be caused by too slow a travel speed, stick-out that is too short, incorrect welding gun angle, or a root gap that is too wide -Weld Defects – there are a number of common weld defects that can occur because of incorrect welding machine settings or welding technique. These include: -Porosity -Cracks -Distortion -Spatter -Undercut -Overlap -Porosity – porosity, or holes in the weld, is one of the more common types of weld defects. Porosity can be caused by: -A lack of shielding gas -Setting the flow rate too high -A pinched or leaking shielding gas hose, or a plugged gas nozzle -Excessive stick-out -Undercut and Cracks – -Undercut is a groove cut into the base metal on both sides of the weld. Undercut occurs primarily when welding thicker metals, such as those found on full-frame vehicles. It can be caused by: -An unbalanced setting of the wire speed and voltage -Using an incorrect welding gun angle or travel speed -Cracks in welds on steel are not common in the collision repair industry due to thinner gauge steels used in vehicle construction. Cracks to the weld bead itself can be caused by: -A strain on the base metal from distortion -Incorrect heat settings -Voltage and current being set too high -Distortion – distortion is caused by: -An unsteady hand -A worn contact lip or excess stick-out that can cause the electrode to wander as it comes out -Voltage that is set too high causing the electrode to melt off in large drops with thinner areas in between -Electrode wire with internal strain or drive rolls extremely tight -Spatter – spatter is the term for metal particles that are blown away from the arc. A major problem with splatter is that it can cause permanent damage to glass and other adjacent parts. Spatter can result from: -A worn contact tip -The electrode wire being spooled too tight -Improper voltage and current settings -The wrong size or worn drive rolls -The welding gun being angled up or down too much -Dirt or corrosion on the base metal -The shielding gas flow rate set too low -Overlap – overlap is an excess amount of weld metal deposited on the top and either side of the weld bead. It can be caused by excess wire speed, not enough voltage, or too slow of a travel speed. Overlap requires more dressing of the weld before repairs can be continued -Dye Penetrant – weld defects are not always clearly visible. Because of this, there are dye penetrants available that can be used after welding to highlight cracks, skips, or porosity. It is critical to thoroughly remove all dye penetrant residue before continuing repairs GMA Welding Troubleshooting and Maintenance -Electrode Wire Cast and Helix – cast is the curvature of a length of welding wire caused by winding it on the spool. Helix is the pitch of a length of the wire. The straighter the wire, the easier it will come off of the spool and feed through the welding gun liner. There has to be some coil in the wire so that it touches the contact tip to transfer the current. The helix should be small to avoid the wire wandering as it leaves the contact tip -Checking Cast and Helix – checking cast and helix is a troubleshooting step that can be done if the electrode wire is not feeding properly. To check cast and helix: -1. Pull off about 10 ft or 300 cm of electrode wire and cut it with a pair of side-cut pliers -2. Loop the wire by hand, toss it on the floor, and allow it to unravel -3. Measure the cast of the wire. The cast is the diameter of the wire loop as it is lying loose on the floor -4. Measure the helix of the wire, which is the height above the floor -AWS Recommendations – the AWS has specific recommendations for cast and helix. For 0.8 mm (.030 in) or smaller wire, if the cast is at least 300 mm (12 in), and the helix is no more than 25 mm (1 in), the electrode wire is in proper working condition. If the electrode wire does not meet these specifications, do not use it. -Birdnesting – if no wire comes out of the contact tip when the trigger is pulled and the drive rolls keep on feeding the wire, birdnesting will occur. Birdnesting can be caused by too much tension on the drive rolls and the wire hitting a restriction in the gun liner or the contact tip -Troubleshooting – before attempting to fix birdnesting, always turn off the machine. Next, release the pressure on the drive rolls and remove and discard the electrode wire between the drive rolls and contact tip. After clearing it, reload the wire into the welding gun liner. Be sure to correct the problem that caused birdnesting and reduce the drive roll tension to avoid another occurrence -Vehicle Protection – induction of current or voltage spikes can destroy sensitive electronic parts. When welding: -Keep the current path short by placing the work clamp close to the weld site -Disconnect and isolate both battery cables (remove the negative battery cable first) -Keep the welding machine as far away from vehicle computers as possible -Route cables away from computers or sensor -Remove computers or other sensitive electronic parts if welding closer than 300 mm or 12 in -Cover glass and other areas of the vehicle with a welding blanket to protect from welding sparks Steel GMA Welding Theory (2/2) GMA Welds and Techniques Weld Descriptions -Weld Descriptions – here we will provide an overview of the four different GMA welds -Plug Weld – plug welds join two or more lapped pieces together with holes punched or drilled in the top piece, or top pieces. If more than two pieces are being joined together, both top pieces should have holes with the hole in the top piece slightly larger than the hole in the middle piece. Vehicle makers may have recommendations for plug weld hole size and location. If they do not, holes should be 8 mm or 5/16 in if the flange width will support it -Fillet Weld – the fillet weld is a triangular shaped weld bead that is deposited at the joint formed by two overlapping pieces. Fillet welds are commonly done on lap joints during collision repairs -Butt Joint with Backing Weld – the butt joint with backing weld joins two pieces and a backing piece at the joint location. The backing piece is typically the same thickness as the other two pieces. To allow penetration into the backing, a root gap, usually 2 to 3 thicknesses of the workpieces, is required. If the root gap is not wide enough, penetration into the backing will decrease and more heat will be required -Open Butt Joint Weld – the open butt joint weld joins two pieces nearly butted against one another. A small root gap between the pieces is required to allow for penetration of the weld and to minimize distortion of the pieces when they are welded. The root gap is usually around one metal thickness. Too large of a root gap, on thin gauge metal, will make welding the joint difficult Weld Techniques -Plug Weld Techniques – to make a plug weld, aim the electrode at the bottom piece, through the hole in the top piece, and circle around the hole making sure that all edges are fused with the bottom piece. The electrode wire should be at a 90 degree angle to the bottom piece to avoid trapping gas pockets in the weld. Plug welds may be easier to make if the heat settings are turned up slightly and a faster travel speed is used. There are two different ways to make plug welds -1. Start at the edge of the joint and move around the piece either clockwise or counterclockwise -2. Start in the center of the bottom coupon and move out towards the edges -Fillet Weld Technique – when making a fillet weld, the electrode wire is aimed at the joint, slightly off center toward the lower piece, using a 45 degree work angle. When welding two different thicknesses of metal with a fillet weld, the work angle should be adjusted so that the electrode wire is angled more toward the thicker piece. The welding gun may be either pushed or pulled at a 70 degree travel angle to the workpiece. However, making vertical fillet welds on a thin gauge material is typically easiest using the pull technique -Butt Joint with Backing Weld Technique – to make a butt joint with backing weld, the electrode wire is either pushed or pulled at a 70 degree travel angle to the workpiece while keeping the welding gun at a 90 degree work angle -Open Butt Joint Technique – to make an open butt joint weld, the technique is similar to the butt joint with backing weld Test Welds -Test Welds – before performing welds on a vehicle, make test welds on similar materials that would use the same surface preparation. After making test welds, visually inspect and destructively test them -Plug Weld – to visually inspect a plug weld, check the front side of the weld for evidence of cracks, porosity, skips, voids, and undercut. None should exist. The plug weld must also completely fill the hole and have a bead height of no more than 3 mm or 1/8 in. on the backside, there must be no burnthrough. Look for evidence of penetration, such as a raised ripple, or melt-through. The height of this melt-through should be no more than 1.5 mm or 1/16 in -Fillet Weld – inspect the front side of the weld for porosity, skips, and voids. These defects are acceptable if they are individually not more than 3mm or 1/8 in long for a 38 mm or 1 ½ in long weld. The weld should completely fill the joint, have no cracks or undercut, and have a bead height of 3 mm or 1/8 in or less. The backside of the fillet weld should also be visually inspected. There must not be any burnthrough that creates a hole and there should be melt-through or a ripple as evidence of penetration. The melt-through should have a width between 0 and 5 mm or 3 and 3/16 in, and a height no greater than 1.5mm or 1/16 in -Butt Joint with Backing Weld – to visually inspect the butt joint with backing, inspect the front side of the weld for porosity, skips, and voids. These defects are acceptable if they are individually not more than 3 mm or 1/8 in long for a 38 mm or 1 ½ in long weld. The weld should completely fill the joint, have no cracks or undercut, and should have a bead height of 3 mm or 1/8 in or less. On the backside there must be no burnthrough that creates a hole. Look for evidence of penetration, such as melt-through or a ripple on the backing piece. The melt-piece should have width close to the width of the weld on the front side and a height no greater than 1.5 mm or 1/16 in. the line left by the joint should not visible anywhere along the weld on the backside -Open Butt Joint Weld – to visually inspect the open butt joint weld, inspect the front side of the weld for porosity, skips, and voids. These defects are acceptable if they are individually not more than 3 mm or 1/8 in long for a 38 mm or 1 ½ in long weld. The weld should completely fill the joint, have no cracks or undercut, and should have a bead height of 3 mm or 1/8 in or less. On the backside there should be no burnthrough that creates a hole. Look for evidence of penetration, such as melt-through forming a weld bead on the backside, leaving no evidence of the original panel edges. The melt-through height should be no greater then 1.5 mm or 1/16 in Making the Weld -Making the Weld – each weld has specific steps required to make the weld on the vehicle. Here, we will list the steps for making each weld -Plug Weld – to make a plug weld on a vehicle -1. Clean the mating surfaces thoroughly. Avoid removing any zinc coating -2. Drill or punch holes in the top piece following the vehicle maker’s recommendation for the number, size, and location of the holes -3. Apply weld-through primer, if specified by the vehicle maker, to any mating surfaces that do not have a zinc coating -4. Tightly clamp the pieces together -5. Remove the excess weld-through primer from the lower piece, through the hole -6. Connect the work clamp as close to the weld site as possible -7. Make the plug welds using the settings that were determined from the test welds -8. Dress the welds. Make sure not to reduce the thickness of the surrounding metal or remove any of the galvanized coating -Fillet Weld – to make a fillet weld on a vehicle -1. Clean the mating surfaces thoroughly. Avoid removing any zinc coating -2. Apply weld-through primer to all mating surfaces, if specified by the vehicle maker, that do not have a zinc coating -3. Clamp the joint tightly together, and remove any exposed weld-through primer from the immediate weld zone -4. Attach the work clamp to a bare metal area of the vehicle, as close to the welding site as possible -5. Make the welds on the vehicle using the settings and techniques that were used to make successful test welds -6. Dress the welds, if necessary. If the welds are dressed, do not reduce the thickness of the surround metal or remove any galvanized coatings. Remove excess weld-through primer on the exterior surfaces before applying additional coatings -Butt Joint with Backing Weld – to make a butt joint with backing weld on a vehicle: -1. Clean the mating surfaces thoroughly. Avoid removing any zinc coating -2. Apply a weld-through primer, if specified by the vehicle maker, to all mating surfaces that do not have a zinc coating -3. Clamp the joint tightly together, leaving the proper root gap. Some procedures may call for the backing piece to be plug welded to one or both of the top pieces. 4. Remove any excess weld-through primer from the backing piece in the immediate weld zone -5. Attach the work clamp to a bare metal area of the vehicle, as close to the welding site as possible -6. Make the welds on the vehicle using the settings and techniques that were used to make successful test welds -7. Dress the welds, if necessary. If the welds are dressed, do not reduce the thickness of the surrounding metal or remove any zinc coatings. Remove excess weld-through primer on the exterior surfaces before applying additional coatings -Open Butt Joint Weld – to make an open butt joint weld on a vehicle: -1. Clean the mating surfaces thoroughly. Avoid removing any zinc coating -2. Clamp the joint tightly together, leaving the proper root gap -3. Attach the work clamp to a bare metal area of the vehicle, as close to the welding site as possible -4. Make the welds on the vehicle using the settings and techniques that were used to make successful test welds. The weld may be made continuously or by a series of short stitch welds. The open butt joint weld requires a welding technique that limits the amount of heat input into the weld to avoid burnthrough -5. Dress the welds, if necessary. If the welds are dressed, do not reduce the thickness of the surrounding metal or remove any galvanized coatings Steering and Suspension Operation (1/3) Steering System Operation Parallelogram Steering Systems -Steering Systems Description – types of steering systems used on vehicles include rack and pinion steering systems and parallelogram. Rack and pinion systems are the most commonly used on cars and light trucks, while parallelogram steering systems are typically found on heavy duty trucks (typically ¾ ton or greater) -Left is Rack and Pinion. Right is Parallelogram -Parallelogram Steering System Parts – a parallelogram steering system is typically used on heavy duty full-frame vehicles. Parts of a parallelogram steering system include the: -Steering gear box -Pitman arm -Idler arm -Center link -Tie rods -Steering shaft -Note: The steering gear box receives input from the steering column shaft. The rotation of the steering wheel is converted to the lateral movement of the steering linkage -Blue = Idler arm -Yellow = Tie rod -Red = Center link -Purple = Pitman arm -Green = Steering Gearbox -Orange = Steering shaft -Steering Gear Box – the worm gear, which receives direction from the shaft, has spiral grooves that move a ball nut back and forth. The teeth on the ball nut mesh with the sector shaft gear teeth and move the sector shaft when the worm gear is rotated. The sector shaft is part of the steering gear assembly. It is connected directly to the pitman arm and is also known as the pitman shaft. Movement of the sector shaft transfers the steering wheel motion to the horizontal steering system - Pitman arm on left. Sector shaft on top right. Worm gear on bottom right -Parallel Steering Linkage – a parallel steering linkage is composed of tie rods, an idler arm, a center link, and a pitman arm -Pitman Arm – the pitman arm connects the steering gear to the center link. It supports one side of the center link and moves the steering linkage to the right or left. The pitman arm positions the center link at the proper height -Center Link – The center link, also called the relay line, or drag link, transfer the movement of the pitman arm to the tie rods. The center link is positioned at the proper height for the tie rods to minimize toe change with suspension travel. A tapered-end-type link has the studs from the pitman arm and idler arm fit into the holes in the ends of the center link and is considered a non-wear-type link. Some center links have ball studs on the ends that fit into holes in the pitman and idler arms. The ball sockets on the center link may wear. This is considered a wear-type link -Idler Arm – the idler arm is connected to the center link with a ball stud. It keeps the center link parallel to the frame mountings. The idler arm support is bolted to the frame on the opposite end of the center link from the pitman arm and moves parallel to and in unison with the pitman arm -Tie Rod – the tie rod assembly connects the center link to the steering arms. The steering arms are connected to the steering knuckles. Steering arms may be part of the steering knuckle or bolted to the knuckle. The tie rod assembly consists of an inner and outer tie rod connected by an adjuster sleeve or tube. Each tie rod adjuster sleeve has left-hand and right-hand threads that attach to the tie rod end and tie rod. Sleeve rotation allows for adjustment to change the tie rod assembly length to set toe -Steering Damper – the steering damper, also called the stabilizer, helps absorb road shock and reduces the shock reaching the steering wheel. A steering damper is similar to a shock absorber. It is connected from one of the steering links to the chassis or frame. It is usually found on larger full-frame or off-road vehicles. When a front wheel strikes a road bumper, the shock is transferred from the front wheel to the steering linkage, steering gear, and steering wheel. The steering damper reduces the effect on the steering wheel. -Parallelogram Operation – a parallelogram steering system operates by using a steering gear box to transfer the steering movement from the steering shaft to the pitman arm. The pitman arm transfer steering motion to the center link. The center link then moves the tie rods, which are mounted to the steering knuckles. An idler arm is connected to the center link on the opposite end from the pitman arm. Rack and Pinion Steering Systems -Rack and Pinion Steering Parts – rack and pinion steering systems are lighter weight, have fewer external parts, and take up less room than a parallelogram steering system. They take the place of the: -Idler Arm -Pitman Arm -Center link -Steering gear box -Note: they are also most common on unibody vehicles. However, there are some full-frame vehicles that use rack and pinion steering. -Rack and Pinion Steering Gear – a common rack and pinion assembly includes the steering pinion gear and the steering rack. The pinion gear is connected to the steering shaft and has grooves that fit into teeth on the steering rack. It moves the rack right or left based on the rotation of the steering shaft and pinion gear. The steering rack is a rod with teeth on one side. It meshes with grooves on the pinion gear and moves laterally based on pinion gear rotation. The steering rack also has tie rods on each end that connect to the steering knuckle -Other Rack and Pinion Steering Parts – other rack and pinion parts include tie rods (both inner and outer), bushings, mounting brackets, bellows boots, and boot clamps -Tie Rods – tie rods connect to the end of the steering rack with an internally or externally threaded inner toe rod. Tie rods also have a threaded end for installation onto the tie rod end. The function and appearance is very similar to the tie rods used on a parallelogram steering system -Rack and Pinion Steering System Operation – on a rack and pinion steering system, the rack moves horizontally on bushings inside of a housing. The rack is moved by the pinion gear that fits into the teeth of the rack. The pinion gear is connected to the steering column shaft, which turns with the steering wheel. When the steering wheel is turned, the pinion rotation moves the rack laterally. The rack moves the inner and outer tie rods. The outer tie rod ends are connected to the steering arm, which are usually part of the knuckle. Steering Column Systems -Steering Column – steering columns are designed to receive input from the steering wheel and transfer the steering action from the column to the rack and pinion or parallelogram steering gear. Steering columns and shafts are also designed to collapse upon impact with the driver or if there is any rearward movement of the steering gear. The column may also house a variety of switches, controls, and the tilt/telescopic features if equipped. Note: Some steering columns may have pyrotechnic charges to promote collapse of the column in certain collision situations. -Steering Column Parts – the steering wheel is attached to the top portion of the steering column and houses the driver airbag module. It may also include controls for the cruise control, radio, and other systems. The clock spring, providing electrical connections for the airbag and other steering wheel mounted controls, is behind the steering wheel, and may also contain the steering angle sensor. Some models may be equipped with a theft deterrent steering column lock system. Steering column parts include but are not limited to: -The steering wheel -The steering column jacket -Steering shafts -Universal joints (U-joints) -Brackets -Upper Steering Column Parts – upper steering column parts typically include the: -Steering column housing, shaft, and bearings -Column position controls -Turn signal switch/lighting controls -Windshield wiper/washer switch -Clock spring -Steering angle sensor -Other controls -Ignition lock cylinder -Note: Lock cylinders may have tamper-resistant fasteners with heads that twist off during installation. A slot may have to be cut into the bolt head to remove these fasteners Steering Power Assist Systems -Power Steering Purpose – power steering systems reduce the effort that is required by the driver to turn the steering wheel. Power steering systems may be: -Conventional-type power steering systems, which use hydraulic fluid to provide steering assist -Electronic-type power steering systems, which use an electric motor that is mounted to the rack assembly or column to provide steering assist -Power Steering Parts – parts of a conventional power steering system include the: -Power steering hydraulic pump -Power steering hoses -Power steering belt -Power steering fluid reservoir -Spool valve -Torsion bar -Steering rack -Pinion gear -Hydraulic Power Steering Parts – parts of a typical hydraulic power steering system include the power steering pump, fluid reservoir, fluid lines and hoses, and fluid cooler. Power steering pumps may be either belt-driven or driven by electric motors. Electrically driven hydraulic power steering may be called electro-hydraulic power steering. Power steering coolers are typically hard lines that route the fluid in a back-and-forth pattern. The coolers are typically located in an area where there is good airflow, such as in front of the radiator or lower front crossmember -Electronically-Controlled Steering – types of electronically-controlled steering include: -Variable-assist hydraulic power steering -Variable gear ratio steering -Electro-hydraulic power steering -Electric-assist power steering -Electric steer or steer-by-wire -Variable-Assist Hydraulic Power Steering – at low speeds, variable-assist power steering systems provide more steering assist to create minimal steering effort for easy turning. Electronically controlled variable-assist hydraulic power steering system parts include the parts of a typical hydraulic power steering system. These systems may have a fluid control valve or solenoid. Depending on system design, the fluid control valve can be located at the power steering pump or the steering gear. The system may have additional sensors and parts, and may be controlled by the powertrain control module, or another module. -Variable Gear Ratio Steering – variable gear ratio steering (VGRS) is not common, but may be available on some typically high-end sport or luxury vehicles. VGRS is able to vary the steering wheel turn angle to actual wheel steer response rate through the use of a special mechanism in the steering shaft or gear - Left is Low Ratio (Quick) /Vehicle speed low. Right is High Ratio (slow) /Vehicle speed high -Electro-Hydraulic Power Steering – the main parts of an electro-hydraulic power steering system include the electric motor and hydraulic pump assembly. These are typically one-piece integral units. Depending on the vehicle, these may be located in the front of the vehicle, vulnerable to collision damage. Parts include the electronic control module and inpurt sensors. The control module may be disintegrated into the pump and motor assembly. Typical input sensors include vehicle speed and electric motor temperature. Main parts also include a conventional power steering gear assembly. -Electric-Assist Power Steering – parts of an electric-assist power steering system include an electric motor mounted to either the steering column or steering gear. These systems use an electric motor instead of hydraulics to provide steering assist. Electric-assist power steering is typically a variable-assist design and uses an electronic control module to control the amount of steering assist provided -Electric Steer or Steer-by-Wire – electric steer or steer-by-wire systems use an electric motor, instead of a mechanical shaft, to turn the wheels. Electric steer is typically used for the rear steering of four-wheel steering systems. With steer-by-wire, there is no mechanical connection between the steering column and steering gear. A variety of designs were used on early versions of rear or four-wheel steering systems for sports and performance vehicles in the late 1980s and early 1990s. some systems were totally mechanical, with a steering shaft controlling a rear steering gear, while others used hydraulics to turn the rear wheels, with or without electronic control Steering and Suspension Operation (2/3) Suspension System Operation Front Suspension System -Front Suspension System Description – vehicle suspension systems are designed to support the vehicle, keep the tires in contact with the road surface, provide directional control of the vehicle, maintain alignment angles, and provide ride quality. Suspension systems found on vehicles include: -MacPherson strut -Modified strut -Short-long arm (SLA) -Double wishbone -Multi-link -Solid axle -Twin I-beam -Note: The Twin I-beam suspension was popular on Ford trucks for many years and utilized two separate front axle beams -Top left – MacPherson Strut -Top right – Double Wishbone -Bottom left – Multi-Link -Bottom right – Short-Long Arm (SLA) -MacPherson Strut – a strut style suspension utilizes a strut with a coil spring to support the weight of the vehicle and maintain wheel position. A strut assembly consists of a strut hosing with a strut rod, upper mount/bearing, and spring. Additional parts include the steering knuckle, hub and bearing assembly, lower control arm with ball joint, and stabilizer bar and links (if equipped). The strut mount attaches to the top of the strut rod and holds the coil spring in position. The strut mount also secures the strut assembly to the vehicle. Note: the strut also acts as a ride damper or shock absorber. -MacPherson Strut, continued – the coil spring mounts between the strut and strut mount. The steering knuckle is typically a cast aluminum or cast steel part. The steering knuckle attaches to both the strut and the lower control arm, and sometimes the steering linkage. The steering knuckle also attaches to the hub, bearing, or spindle. The lower control arm attaches to the engine cradle or vehicle frame. The lower control arm positions the wheel at the bottom. The lower ball joint connects the lower control arm and steering knuckle. The ball joint allows movement when steering left and right, along with movement when the wheel moves up and down -Modified Strut – a modified strut suspension system is similar to a MacPherson strut system in design. This type of strut has the coil spring located between the lower control arm and frame or crossmember, instead of on the strut housing. The spring, lower control arm, and ball joint support the vehicles weight. The lower control arm and upper strut mount maintain wheel position -Short-Long Arm – parts of an SLA suspension include the: -Steering knuckle -Hub and bearing, or spindles and bearings -Upper and lower control arms -Ball joints -Coil springs (or torsion bars on some vehicles) -Shock absorbers -Stabilizer bar and links (if equipped) – this may be referred to as a “sway bar” in some service information. -Note: the steering knuckle may also be referred to as the spindle assembly -Double Wishbone – a double wishbone front suspension is a modified SLA design. This suspension features upper and lower control arms (wishbones) to position the wheel, the steering knuckle is taller than on a typical SLA suspension. This suspension uses a fixed position strut with coil spring that typically has a damper fork on the bottom to support the vehicle’s weight. The damper fork connects the strut to the lower control arm and allows the axle shaft to pass through its center. Double wishbone suspensions may have a radius arm attached to the lower control arm for positioning the lower ball joint at the correct position. Note: the strut assembly does not control wheel position on the double wishbone suspension -Multi-Link – a multi-link suspension is similar in design and function to a double-wishbone suspension, but instead of using a single upper and lower control arm, this system typically uses two upper and lower control arms. Each arm has a ball joint in the end where it connects to the steering knuckle. Multi-link suspensions use a fixed position strut or coil-over shock for suspension control. Only one of the lower arms is weight bearing, or has the strut attached to it. Note: Multi-link suspension may use three or more control links/arms to control wheel position -Solid Axle – parts of a typical solid-axle front suspension include the axle housing, axle shafts, springs, shock absorbers, control arms, hub and bearing assemblies; stabilizer bar and links (if equipped), and steering knuckles. The steering knuckles are typically attached to the axle housing with ball joints, but may use bushed kingpins, or spindle bolts that provide the pivot points for the front wheels to turn. Note: Heavy trucks typically use a solid front axle beam Rear Suspension System -Rear Suspension System Description – rear suspension systems may either be a solid axle design or an independent design. Solid axles may be drive axles on rear-wheel drive and four-wheel drive vehicles or trailing axles on front-wheel drive vehicles. An independent rear suspension is used to improve the ride and handling of a vehicle. Independent rear suspensions may be found on front- and rear-wheel drive vehicles as well as four-wheel drive vehicles. They may be either a strut or an SLA design -Solid Drive Axle – parts of a typical solid drive axle rear suspension include the axle housing, ring and pinion gears and differential, axle shafts, bearings, springs, shock absorbers, and stabilizer bar and links, if equipped. The wheel bearings are typically located in the end of the axle housing tube. Axles with leaf springs usually have U bolts securing the axle to the springs, bushings in the front and rear spring eyes, a mounting bracket in the front, and rear mounting shackles. Axles with coil springs use trailing or control arms with bushings at the mounting points, and usually have a panhard rod/track bar to keep the axle centered -Solid Trailing Axle – parts of a typical solid trailing axle rear suspension include the axle tube (or beam), springs, shock absorbers, trailing arms on coil spring designs, hub and bearing assemblies or spindles and bearings, and stabilizer bar and links, if equipped. Springs may be either coil or leaf design, with coil springs being the most common. Trailing arms have bushings where they connect to the axle housing and chassis. Some designs may have the trailing arms built into the axle housing -Independent Strut Style – parts of a typical strut-style independent rear suspension system include control arms, knuckles, strut assemblies – which include the coil springs, stabilizer bar and links (if equipped), tie rods and tie rod ends, and radius rods or trailing arms. These systems have adjustable links for adjusting the rear toe. Tie rods may be adjustable or solid rods that adjust with cams where they attach to the chassis. Radius rods or trailing arms may be used for vehicles that have a single bar lower control arm. The radius rod or trailing arm is used to maintain the wheel position for the proper wheelbase. The independent strut rear suspension is typically found on front-wheel drive vehicles -Independent SLA Style – parts of a typical SLA-style independent rear suspension system include the upper and lower control arms, ball joints, knuckles, springs, shock absorbers, stabilizer bar and links (if equipped), and tie rod and tie rod ends. Control arms have bushings where they are attached to the chassis. Springs may be either coil or leaf, with coil being the most commonly used. Steering and Suspension Operation (3/3) Ride Control Ride Height Control Parts and Description -Electronically Controlled Suspension Identification – the presence of electronically controlled suspension systems is typically not identifiable by emblems or other highly visible labeling on the vehicle. -Part identification – perform a thorough visual inspection of the suspension system. Look for wiring connected to suspension parts, such as shock absorbers or struts. Look for the presence of air or hydraulic fluid lines to the shock absorbers, struts, or springs. Check for the presence of a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL) for an electronic suspension system when the ignition is first turned ON. Look for switches on the dash or console for manual control of electronic suspension systems. Systems may have an On/Off switch, a switch for sport or comfort damper settings, or a switch for control of vehicle height. Visually identify parts that would indicate the presence of specific electronic or electronic/hydraulic systems -Option Labels – the vehicle option label provides useful information for system identification. Many repair shops and dealership parts departments can decode the option label to determine if an electronically controlled suspension system is present on the vehicle. A VIN-drive parts ordering system may also be used to determine if the vehicle is equipped with an electronically controlled suspension systems -Computer-Controlled Ride Height – computer-controlled ride height systems are one type of electronically-controlled suspension system. These systems are sometimes called level-ride or automatic load leveling systems. Computer-controlled ride height systems may be on the rear of the vehicle only, or the system may be on all four corners of the vehicle. Computer-controlled ride height systems may be air systems. The ride height may be adjusted using air shock absorbers or the vehicle may be equipped with air springs. These systems may also be designed to use a hydraulic system that controls the ride height adjustment -Left- Control Module. Right – Electronically-Controlled Ride Height System -Air Shock Absorber Height Control Systems – the simplest type of height control system is the air shock absorber system, which only controls the height at the rear of the vehicle. These systems typically include rear air shock absorbers which do not act as springs for the vehicle, but rather are used to assist the springs in holding the vehicle level with heavy loads. -Parts – height control systems include an air compressor with a dryer to remove moisture from the air. These systems may use air lines from the compressor to the shock absorbers. An exhaust valve allows air to be removed from the shock absorbers. The exhaust valve is typically built into the air compressor. These systems include height sensors that monitor the ride height of the vehicle. Some rear-only air shock absorber systems have a combination height sensor and control module assembly -Operation – height control systems that use a rear air shock absorber control the ride height by either adding or removing air from the shock absorber air chamber. These systems are controlled by the rear height sensor and control module. The height sensor determines rear ride height and either instructs the air compressor to run or the exhaust valve to open. Typically, these systems have delayed activation to avoid having the compressor run in response to temporary changes in ride height -Hydraulic Height Control Systems – hydraulic height control systems have hydro-pneumatic cylinders in place of the conventional shock absorbers or struts. These systems use the hydro-pneumatic cylinders to control both the ride height and suspension damping force. Hydro-pneumatic cylinders work in series with the vehicle springs. The hydraulic cylinders are used to work with the conventional springs and provide additional and adjustable spring rate and ride height to the vehicle. -Parts – the basic parts of a hydraulic height control system typically include a hydraulic fluid pump, fluid pressure accumulator, control valves or actuators, hydraulic cylinders, input sensors, a control module, switches, and other basic electrical circuit parts -Operation – hydraulic height control systems have accumulators that store hydraulic fluid in one chamber, separated from high pressure gas in another chamber by a rubber bladder. The system may have a front and rear accumulator or separate accumulators for each corner of the suspension. Control valves control the flow of hydraulic fluid between the accumulator and hydraulic cylinder. When the control module calls for increased ride height at a corner, the control valve opens a valve that allows fluid to flow into the hydraulic cylinder and lift the vehicle.
