Hydraulic fluid is added and removed from the hydraulic cylinders at each corner of the vehicle to either raise or lower ride height Ride Damper Control Parts and Operation -Computer-Controlled Variable Shock Absorber Damping – another type of electronically controlled suspension is computer-controlled variable shock absorber damping. Variable shock absorber damping may be controlled mechanically by varying the orifice size for the shock absorber fluid. Damping may also be controlled magnetically by varying the viscosity of the shock absorber fluid -Semi-Active Suspensions – semi-active suspension systems typically consist of computer-controlled variable dampers that respond to up-and-down body motion inputs to read the road surface. These systems react to the first bounce or suspension deflection to control the second bounce or deflection. They are not active, but rather reactive, semi-active systems do not directly control suspension deflection; they indirectly control it by altering the firmness of the shock absorber damping -Damper Controls – control of variable rate dampers is handled by a control module, which sends an output signal to a solenoid valve located inside the shock absorber body, or in a damping control unit located outside the shock absorber body. -Internal Valve Damper Control – the operation of computer-controlled suspension dampers may be electrically controlled by a solenoid or stepper motor. These use internal actuators or valves located in the damper body or piston to control the flow of the fluid in the shock absorber. Valves are opened or closed to vary the opening size and resistance to fluid flow inside the shock absorber. More resistance to flow produces a firmer suspension damping force and less resistance produces a softer damping force. -External Valve Damper Control – the solenoid or stepper motor may be located in external damping valves on some systems. External damping units are used to allow for more valves than would fit internally in the shock absorber body. The additional valves allow a greater degree of tuning flexibility due to the increase in the number of damping force settings -Magnetic Damper Control - magnetically-controlled variable damper systems have constant shock absorber valving with a variable viscosity fluid. These systems are less complicated and have fewer parts than valve-based variable dampers. The damping force firmness is controlled by varying the viscosity of the fluid inside the shock absorber, instead of varying the opening size that the fluid is forced through -Magnetic Damper Fluid – magnetic damper systems use a special fluid to control the damping force firmness. The fluid has special randomly suspended magnetic particles. In the presence of a magnetic field, the particles align themselves with the magnetic field lines and each other. This raises the viscosity of the fluid. The fluid has an unlimited range of viscosity depending on the strength of the magnetic field in which it is placed. It can go from a fluid-like state when no magnetic field is present, to an almost plastic-like state in the presence of a strong magnetic field. The magnetic field is produced by an electromagnet located on the piston of the damper -Top is off. Bottom is on with magnetic field lines going thru -Combination System – vehicles with electronically controlled suspensions may have combination systems. Combination systems have computer control of both ride height and shock absorber damping. These may have air springs and separate computer-controlled variable dampers. Combination systems may use computer-controlled dampers to adjust both the ride height and handling characteristics of the vehicle. Dampers in these systems may be hydraulic struts with air bladders built in for springs or may be totally hydraulic for both suspension damping and ride height -Active Stabilizer Bars – conventional stabilizer bars (anti sway bar/roll bar) help reduce body roll when cornering by connecting the left and right suspension together with a torsion bar and end links that transfer movement of the suspension on one side to the other, resisting body roll; this can result in a harsh ride condition. Active stabilizer bars allow for high levels of body roll control and suspension handling while using softer springs and dampers for increased ride comfort. Note: Off road vehicles may use a special stabilizer bar with an actuator that can decouple the two ends, allowing for greater suspension travel -Parts – the basic parts of an active stabilizer bar system include a hydraulic fluid pump and reservoir, control module, control valves, high pressure hydraulic fluid lines, lateral acceleration sensors, hydraulic motors or actuators, and stabilizer bars. Each axle may have a stabilizer bar that is made of two parts or it may have a solid bar that is connected to the suspension on one side with a variable hydraulic linear actuator -Operation – when a vehicle that is equipped with active stabilizer bars goes around a corner or curve, the lateral acceleration or yaw rate sensors detect the body roll and sends a signal to the control module. The control module acts on the valve blocks to supply pressurized hydraulic fluid to the correct size of the oscillating motor or hydraulic actuator. The oscillating motor, or actuator, then twists the stabilizer bar halves in opposite directions to counteract the roll of the vehicle body over the suspension Structural Damage Analysis – Structures (1/3) Vehicle Structures Types of Vehicle Construction -Types of Frames – common vehicle structures include the unibody structure, space frame, and body-over-frame -Unibody – a unibody structure has a single structure built by attaching multiple parts of sheet metal. Attachment methods include spot welds, laser welds, rivets, adhesives, or a combination of methods. The unibody frame has exterior welded-on body panels, such as roofs and quarter panels, included in the overall structure. The drivetrain, steering, and suspension parts are mounted directly to the body structure. A unibody structure depends on correct structural alignment for proper steering and suspension alignment. This frame type may be used for cars, sport-utility vehicles, and trucks -Space Frame – space frame vehicles are similar to unibody vehicles, but have a lighter-weight design. Space frame vehicles use lightweight metal or a metal and composite combination, so they are less dependent on the outer body panels for strength. Note: Space frames are similar to the inner structures commonly found in race cars -Body-Over-Frame – body-over-frame vehicles, which are often referred to as full-frame vehicles, have a frame independent of the unitized structure. Two main types of frames include perimeter and ladder designs. Ladder frames are more common on recreational vehicles (RVs), busses, and commercial trucks. Body-over-frame vehicles have a separate unitized structure bolted to the frame. It uses the same energy management characteristics that are used for unibody vehicles. These vehicles have the engine, drivetrain, and steering and suspension parts bolted to the frame -Modern Full-Frame Characteristics – modern pickup truck and SUV frame designs vary, depending on the vehicle. Features of modern frame designs include: -A combination of characteristics from both ladder-type and perimeter-type frame designs -Both open C-channel and box-type rails -Hydroformed rails. Box-type and tubular hydroformed rails are flangeless, one-piece structures formed from a metal tube. -Welded or riveted mounting brackets -Crossmembers that are welded or riveted to the frame -Collapse zones -Torque boxes -Note: Many pickup trucks and SUVs have modern frames with a lower center of gravity for better handling and a more car-like ride -Top is Open C-Channel. Bottom is Box-Type Frame -Aluminum Unibody Frame – there are many advantages to using aluminum vs. steel, with weight savings being a primary advantage. Vehicle makers may reduce the weight of a vehicle structure by approximately 30% when using an aluminum frame. The three aluminum body design concepts used today include: extrusion-intensive frame structures, space frames, and unibody structures. Aluminum structures will typically include extruded parts in several locations that have limited repairability due to restricted access to the inside of the part. Some common areas for these extruded parts include: -Rocker panels -Roof rails -Pillars -Frame rails -Tie bars The Collision -Basic Vehicle Design – to determine how the vehicle should be repaired, it is helpful to understand how the vehicle is constructed. Each vehicle is designed with three distinct sections: Front, Rear, Center. Each section has a specific function in the event of a collision. The front and rear structures are designed to collapse up to a specific point and transfer energy to the opposite end of the vehicle. This controlled deceleration of the vehicle helps absorb collision energy, as well as re-route the collision energy around the center section -Passenger Compartment Design (Center Section) – unlike the front and rear sections of the vehicle, the center section or passenger compartment: -Is not designed to collapse -Has collision energy transferred around it -Remains intact and provides a safe space for the vehicle occupants -Keeps occupants inside the vehicle during the collision -Collision Energy – collision energy starts at the point of impact on the vehicle. The absorption and transfer of collision energy is done using several techniques. Crush zones which are specific designs in the structural components used to collapse or bend in a controlled manner absorb the collision energy. Different strengths of steel, may also be used to either absorb or transfer the collision energy, as well as different thicknesses of material, which may vary within a part depending on design. Finally, reinforcements transfer collision energy to other areas of the vehicle -Crush Zones – crush zones are designed into the vehicle to absorb collision energy. Crush zones may be holes, dimples, or slots stamped into a part. These zones are made by using tailor-welded blanks, tailor-rolled blanks, and/or reinforcements. Crush zones use different types of steel strength and thicknesses. These zones may have formed ribs or stamped areas across the width of the part – these may also be called convolutions. The crush zone kick-up or offset areas bend during a collision and redirect collision energy. A kick-up or offset may be designed to direct the engine and transmission under the passenger compartment, rather than into it, during a collision -Importance of Proper Repair – during the damage analysis process, the estimator/appraiser must determine the appropriate repair methods to ensure the collision characteristics are restored to pre-accident integrity. This will make sure the structure reacts as designed by the vehicle maker in a subsequent collision. Using specific materials and part design, automotive engineers have designed the vehicle to collapse in a predictable manner to protect those inside the passenger compartment. Changing a part design or repairing parts that should not be repaired can affect the original design, which can alter the collision energy management of the vehicle. Note: It is important to note that the repairer and/or insurer may be liable for any personal injury or property damage caused by improper repairs Analyzing Vehicle Damage -Direct Damage – when analyzing vehicle damage, it is helpful to determine the area of direct damage. Direct damage is the point of impact where the vehicle has the most obvious damage. It is here where damage analysis generally begins. Direct damage may also be referred to as primary damage or initial impact -Mass Inertia Damage – mass inertia damage is caused by unrestrained object in the vehicle, or parts of the vehicle itself, that continue momentum after the vehicle has been stopped by a collision. This can damage both the interior and exterior of the vehicle -Examples of Mass Inertia Damage – types of damage that can be caused by mass inertia include a damaged pickup bed or trunk from unrestrained items, a bent motor mount, and a cracked composite radiator core support from a rear impact. Other examples include a collapsed steering column from the driver impacting the steering wheel, damage to seat frames, center consoles, door trim panels, and cracked windshields -Indirect Damage – indirect damage is essentially the same as mass inertia damage. Indirect damage: -Is caused by the force of the collision as it moves through the vehicle -Occurs away from the point of impact -Can be found anywhere on the vehicle, which makes it difficult to locate -May not be evident without close inspection -May be identified by accurate measuring -Can include mechanical parts, such as wheel bearing damage caused by an impact to a wheel -May also include bent engine mount brackets, which are considered a type of indirect damage -Indirect Damage on Late Model Vehicles – indirect damage today looks different from indirect damage on older model vehicles. Stronger steels transfer more collision energy around the passenger compartment. There may be no visible panel gap misalignment, but still be indirect damage away from the direct impact area. Measuring may be the only way to verify indirect damage due to collision energy transfer. A buckle in the roof used to be a very common indicator of structural damage, but this is no longer the case. The solid structure of the center section often prevents this from happening; however, collision energy may still have radiated to the opposite end of the vehicle. New body designs change the way a vehicle is inspected -Secondary Damage – secondary damage is damage that resulted away from the initial impact during a second impact with another object. Secondary damage includes both direct and indirect damage. Depending on the collision, there may be multiple locations of damage that are secondary damage. For example, a vehicle may hit a barrier, spin, and hit another barrier or oncoming vehicle. This can present unique challenges when trying to determine the point of initial impact, since there may be several areas that show direct damage. Customer interviews can be helpful in determining how a collision occurred -Impact Angles and Damage Analysis – understanding the impact angles and how energy travels through the damaged vehicle is an essential part of damage analysis -What are Impact Angles? – when analyzing damage to a vehicle, it is important to understand the impact angle. These angles include: -Straight-on front -Straight-on rear -Offset front or rear, which is just a portion of the front or rear of the vehicle -Rollover -Side -Note: Another type of impact angle is when an object directly hits the unibody. This is not as evident, as most of the damage will be underneath the vehicle -Damage Analysis Considerations – determining the direction of the impact is helpful for a thorough analysis. Understanding how energy traveled through the vehicle structure helps the appraiser/estimator identify likely secondary or indirect damage. It is necessary to look for where the damage was initiated, then try to follow the path the energy likely traveled through the vehicle -Examining Impact Angles – examining the impact angle of vehicles is key to understanding how energy traveled through the vehicle: each impact angle can affect how damage is transferred into the vehicle structure; for example, a side impact, due to the very strong side structure, may result in the front and rear swaying to the side of the collision, giving the vehicle a banana shape; with late model vehicles, a banana shape generally requires severe impact forces due to the reinforcement of the center section; another example is when a vehicle has an offset front-end collision – this type of damage may cause the front end to sway toward one side of the vehicle; depending on the speed of the collision, the energy -Types of Damage Conditions – collision impacts result in specific damage conditions to the vehicle structure. The extent or number of conditions created in the vehicle structure depends on the angle and severity of the collision. Types of damage conditions include diamond, twist, sag, mash, and side sway. -Diamond – the diamond condition is often created by an offset collision impact where one side of the vehicle is moved forward or rearward of the opposite side. It causes the center section to be out of square, taking on a diamond-like appearance. Diamond is commonly associated with full-frame vehicles, but it is starting to be found on unibody vehicles as increased use of reinforcements and ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS) become standard in vehicle construction. For example, diamond may occur from a direct impact to the rocker panel. If there is a diamond in a unibody vehicle, it is generally accompanied by a dent or wrinkle in the floor pan. -Twist – a twist occurs when the center section of a vehicle is out of level. Either one or both ends are out of level with each other. A twist is a height problem, where a portion of the vehicle center section is higher or lower than the opposite side. A twist condition may not be evident in the upper body structure; however, the vehicle may appear as though there is suspension damage. On full-frame vehicles, twists can occur in the frame and/or the unitized structure. A twist can occur from colliding with a curb or ditch at high speed, becoming airborne at one point, or being involved in a rear impact. Note: It is important for technicians to repair the twist condition before it is anchored for pulling. Not making this repair ahead of time can lock the twist condition into the vehicle -Grey is undamaged. Tan is damaged -Sag – sag is a condition where the front or rear sections of the vehicle or the cowl have height measurements that are out of specification. It is generally the result of a straight-on collision. Sag is indicated by body panels having inconsistent panel gaps from top to bottom. This happens when the cowl drops down in a front-end collision. Due to the stronger center sections, this condition is not as common as it was on older models -Grey undamaged. Tan damaged -Mash – mash is also called collapse or short rail, and is a condition where any section or frame member is shorter than factory specification. Mash is common on front and rear rails from front and rear impacts. Damage may be more visible in the front and rear section of the vehicle compared to the center section. For example, collapse of the rails may lead to distortions or buckles in panels, such as fenders, hoods, and quarter panels -Side Sway – sway, or side sway, occurs when collision energy forces a part of the vehicle to one side or the other. This condition is a width misalignment and generally occurs to the front or rear section of the vehicle. Side sway may be evident by wide gaps on one side of the vehicle and narrow gaps on the opposite side. Direct impact would generally take place on the side of the vehicle. When analyzing damage, an indication of side sway is evidence of buckling on the inside of the driver side rail and buckling on the outside of the passenger side rail -Additional Unitized Structure Visual Damage Indicators – additional visual damage indicators are used to check the unitized structure -Wheel Position – check the position of the wheel in the wheelhouse. The most obvious assumption is that there is suspension damage; however, if this is not visible, it is likely that there is damage to the suspension mounting areas that are part of the vehicle structure -Cracked Seam Sealer or Paint – check for cracked seam sealers or paint. This may require lifting the carpeting or raising the vehicle to view the floor pan or trunk floor. Cracked seam sealer may be an indication of inner structure damage -Closure Panel Operation – check for improper closure panel operation. If the door, hood, or deck lid binds, drops, or stickers when opening, it may be an indication of larger structural damage -Cracked Windshield – a cracked windshield is an indicator that there is stress in the upper body structure that caused the glass to crack -Buckled Exterior Panels – while dented panels are often a result of a direct impact, structure misalignment will also cause panels to buckle -Using Visual Cues – when looking at visual cues regarding structural damage, note that these should only be used as guides. They should never be used as the only method to determine structural damage. However, they can be used as evidence that structure three-dimensional measuring must be done. In most instances, measuring may be the only practical method to determine if structural damage exists. The visual indicators simply show that the vehicle should be measured. Most estimators and auto physical damage appraisers use visual cues to identify if measuring is required. It is rare to do field measurements. Most structural misalignment problems are generally evidence. However, there are situations when damage is very subtle, and only measuring will identify a structural problem Structural Damage Analysis – Structures (2/3) Measuring for Damage Analysis Point-to-Point Measurements -Types of Measurements – types of measurements used for vehicle repair include point-to-point and three-dimensional. Point-to-point measurements are used for damage analysis only, and are not a substitute for three-dimensional measuring. Three-dimensional measuring is required for any type of structural repair and can be used for damage analysis as well -Measuring for Damage Analysis – measuring for damage analysis helps: -Locate indirect and hidden damage -Determine a repair plan to restore vehicle safety, performance, and appearance -Identify suspension damage -Note: The estimate, or damage report, is generally not considered complete until a complete and thorough damage analysis is done that includes measuring the vehicle structure -Measurements within Normal Tolerance – when making measurements or looking at measurement results, compare the measurements with the visual damage. Determine if the measurements match the visible damages and direction of impact. Try to determine how far the damage radiates into the vehicle. In other words, measure until you find measurements within normal tolerance -Point-to-Point Measuring – point-to-point measurements can be taken with a tape measure or tram gauge. Tape measures must have a metric scale, since most dimension sheets list dimensions in metric measurements. When making the measurements, make sure the tape measure lays flat between the points being measured. Bends or twists will give inaccurate readings. A tram gauge is a telescoping measuring device with adjustable pointers on each end. It is used for making point-to-point measurements. Some tram gauges may require a tape measure to measure between the pointers. This is used for tram gauges that do not have a built-in measuring device. Tram gauges are available in different sizes and may be digital or equipped with a built-in tape measure. Most tram gauges have metric measurements printed on the bar and pointers -Making Point-to-Point Measurements – point-to-point measurements for holes of the same size are made either edge-to-edge or center-to-center. If measuring from fasteners, typically measurements will be made center-to-center, measuring to the center of each bolt head -Measuring for Structural Damage – common point-to-point measurements used to perform quick checks for structural damage include: -Lower ball joint to frame -Underhood crosscheck -Underbody crosscheck -Door and deck lid openings -Note: Point-to-point measurements can also be comparative measurements from one side of the vehicle to the other. Point-to-point measurements should not be a substitute for three-dimensional measurements Three-Dimensional Measuring -Three-Dimensional Measuring Equipment – there are several types of three-dimensional measuring systems used to measure a vehicle and return it to proper dimensions. Each system uses a specific method for providing measurement readings to the technician. Some of these systems include: -Universal mechanical or laser -Fixture measuring systems – this is not commonly used for damage analysis, as it requires the vehicle to be mounted to a bench -Computerized measuring systems -Three-Dimensional Planes – measuring systems must read all three dimensions to verify a specific point on a vehicle. These dimensions include height, width, and length. Three-dimensional measuring equipment may be installed in the estimating bay to assist in writing accurate damage reports. This type of equipment generally does not require anchoring the vehicle in place. Measuring systems use datum, centerline, and zero planes as vehicle reference points -Datum Plane (Height) – a datum plane is an imaginary surface or plane positioned beneath the vehicle that is parallel to the vehicle underbody. It is used by measuring systems as a starting point from which height is measured to various reference points on the vehicle. The datum plane distance from the bottom of the vehicle varies according to the maker of the measuring system or vehicle makers dimension guide -Centerline Plane (Width) – centerline plane is: -An imaginary plane that cuts the vehicle in half from the floor to the roof lengthwise, front to rear -Used by measuring systems as a starting point for width measurements -Used to establish a width position for vehicle reference points -Zero Plane (Length) – zero plane is used by measuring systems as a starting point from which length is measured. This is an imaginary plane that extends across the width of the vehicle. The zero plane is projected at different locations depending on the maker of the dimension guide. The zero plane should not be in an area that has been damaged. If the dimension guide shows the zero plane in an area that has been damaged, such as the front torque box area, the zero plane can be moved to the rear torque box area. The listed measurements in the dimension guide, however, must be adjusted accordingly -Three-Dimensional Measuring Points – points used for measuring the vehicle are control and reference points. These points: -May be the same points used during vehicle assembly -Are used for damage analysis -Include bolts, slots, holes, formed and pierced parts, and divots -Generally require starting with three known good points to set up the measuring system -Three-Dimensional Suspension Measurements – similar to the point-to-point measurements taken from the lower pivot point, three-dimensional measurements are made from the vehicle centerline to verify proper positioning of the suspension. They can help determine if the lower rails, strut towers, suspension parts, or engine cradles are out of alignment -Three-Dimensional Suspension Measurements, continued – one suspension measurement that is used is called steering axis inclination (SAI). These points can be verified by measuring the distance from the centerline to the upper pivot point, at the top of the strut tower, and the lower pivot point at the lower ball joint. When the upper and lower pivot points are at specification, the SAI angle will be correct. The purpose of the SAI is for directional control of the vehicle. If the angle is different from side to side, the vehicle may pull. SAI is measured with true vertical. An engine cradle that has moved or been damaged may cause an incorrect SAI -Three-Dimensional Suspension Measurements, continued – when making three-dimensional measurements, technicians start with the center section of the vehicle. This part of the vehicle is essentially the foundation of measuring, and provides a reference for all other measurements. There are instances when the center section is damaged; however, only three good points are needed to establish a good foundation -Dimension Resources – specific points used to make three-dimensional measurements are provided by: -Measuring equipment makers -Vehicle makers -Note: Estimating guides and vehicle makers also provide quick point-to-point measurements. Measurements, whether three-dimensional or point-to-point, are almost exclusively provided in millimeters -Vehicle Repair Tolerance – during vehicle production, most vehicles are built within a certain tolerance. Tolerance: -Is the range that a measurement can vary by and still be acceptable, so the vehicle panels fit and operate properly, and the suspension allows the vehicle to handle properly. This may be as low as zero, or no tolerance, for some vehicle makers -May include variations in dimensions between vehicles of the same make. This is called vehicle build tolerance -Vehicle Repair Tolerance, continued – due to this tolerance, not all vehicle measurements will be identical and may vary by a millimeter or two, during collision repairs, the vehicle must be returned to proper specification. For collision repair purposes, tolerance depends on what is being measured. For example, areas with very low or no tolerance may include non-adjustable door hinges, uniside construction, nonadjustable suspension mounting locations, and airbag sensor mounting locations. Items with more tolerance may include rail measurements. However, in most cases, tolerance will not exceed +/- 3mm -Asymmetrical vs Symmetrical Measurements – most points on a vehicle are in the same location on the left and right side, or symmetrical. Asymmetrical vehicles have: -Structural variances from the left to the right -Reference points in different locations -Note: For example, some vehicles may have strut tower bolts in different positions. Since the strut tower colts are reference points, the measurements may be different even though there is no structure variation between the left and right side of the vehicle -Reading Computerized Measuring Sheets – most three-dimensional measuring systems offer the option of printing out the measuring results. On these particular systems, a computer is required to measure and record. The measuring readouts vary by maker. With most systems, improper measurements may be highlighted in a different color. Some colors may vary, depending on whether the measurement is too long or short, or too high or too low. The measuring print outs note the specific measuring locations and provide the profile and overhead view of the vehicle. The computerized printouts provide complete measurements and an overall picture of the damage. They may also have a negative sign to indicate a short or narrow measurement Structural Damage Analysis – Structures (3/3) General Repair Considerations Part Repairability -Work Hardening – metal is work-hardened each time it is formed, bent, stretched, or compressed. As a part becomes more work-hardened, it becomes more brittle, making it prone to cracking. A metal part is work-hardened, initially when it is formed at the manufacturing plant. However, it is work-hardened further when it is deformed during a collision and straightened during repair -Work Hardening, continued – work hardening changes the original characteristics of the part. This can be an issue if the par tis not repaired properly. A work-hardened part may not reach properly if it is involved in another collision, possibly allowing more intrusion into the passenger compartment. Although the effects of work hardening can be reduced with the use of heat, applications of heat are primarily limited to mild steel -Bent or Kinked Parts – there is a common statement in the collision industry that if a part is bent, it can be straightened, if it is kinked, it must be replaced. -Bent – a bend is a smooth, continuous change in shape, and generally can be repaired without altering the state or shape -Kinked Parts – a kink is defined as a sharp bend with a small radius over a short distance. A part is considered kinked if after straightening, there is a permanent area of deformation that will not return to its original shape without the use of excessive heat. The kinked area has experienced changes in the metal, and may have visible cracks or tears. These changes create permanent damage that cannot be repaired, regardless of the type of repair performed -Stage and Shape – when making a part repair decision, the most important factors to consider are that the metal state and shape must be restored. In other words, both strength and appearance must match the pre-accident integrity of the part. Metal has a molecular grain structure. This structure gives metal its strength and cannot be seen with the naked eye. When metal is damaged, its grain structure is rearranged. This grain structure damage can: -Weaken the part -Cause the part to react differently if involved in another collision -Occur when an area is kinked -Note: The grain structure in kinked areas will likely be irreversibly affected. When an area is kinked, it is likely to have microscopic cracking of the grain structure. Aluminum is especially vulnerable to this, and will often crack during straightening. Some high-strength steel (HSS) and ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS) are typically not repairable regardless of whether the part is kinked or bent. UHSS is almost always replaced if damage, dur to permanent changes in the steel Heating and Sectioning Considerations -Heat issues – HSS and UHSS are altered by the application of heat. Heat makes HSS and UHSS softer, more malleable, or weaker, essentially losing high tensile strength. Heating a part to straighten it is generally not recommended, unless the part is a mild steel part where limited heat application will not affect the integrity of the steel -Vehicle Maker Heat Limit Recommendations – to determine if heat can be applied for stress relieving, refer to the body repair or service manual. Some vehicle makers have specific guidelines for heating steel during repairs. These may include not allowing heat to be used for repairs, such as straightening kinks. The vehicle maker may only allow cold straightening. Some vehicle makers state that HSS should generally not be heated. If heat is allowed, it is generally to be used only as a last resort. This may include using heat when straightening the vehicle back to proper dimensions. If the material cannot be identified, it should be treated as a type of HSS and not be heated -Damage Repair Recommendations – determining how a part needs to be repaired depends on recommendations from the vehicle maker. These recommendations can be found at -Vehicle maker collision repair websites, typically for a normal fee -ALLDATA, which is a subscription website -OEM1Stop, which is a subscription website -I-CAR Repairability Technical Support (RTS) side -Note: Damage removal is generally done by straightening, removing the entire part at factory seams, removing a portion of the part at factory seams, or by sectioning the part -Complete Part Replacement – replacing a complete part at factory seams is common on UHSS parts, though some vehicle makers offer partial replacement of their UHSS parts. Replacement may be done because reinforcements make partial replacement difficulty. Replacements may require additional undamaged part removal. In some instances, complete part removal may be extensive and removal may require disturbing factory seams that create additional corrosion hotspots -Partial Replacement – a portion of a part can be replaced at factory seams, or a new joint can be created away from factory seams. This is called “sectioning”. Many parts of a vehicle are not one piece, but composed of multiple pieces that are joined together. Replacing a portion of a part at factory seams is sometimes recommended. Sectioning does this at an area of the part away from factory seams, creating a new joint. Either of these partial replacement methods requires a recommendation from the vehicle maker, with either a procedure or available partial part. There may also be a recommendation to partially replace a part at factory seams at one location, and section in another location -Partial Replacement, continued – the advantages of partial replacement include that the repair affects a smaller area, there are fewer OEM welds disturbed, less original corrosion protection disturbed, and potentially less disassembly. Laser-welded joints are not considered a good factory seam for partial replacement. Most vehicle makers do not recommend this joint -Determining Partial Replacement Options – when considering whether partial replacement is a viable repair option, determine if a procedure exists to make this type of repair, or if there is a partial part available, such as a portion of an assembly. Also, determine if the partial replacement option removes all of the damage, which joints are recommended, and how corrosion protection will be applied after the joint is complete. -Cutting Access Windows – some repair procedures from vehicle makers have been developed that require cutting access windows into a part to access the opposite side of a part. This is generally done to allow access to a reinforcement for sectioning or to a part so that it can be removed for complete replacement. For example, on some vehicles, access windows in the outer, upper B-pillar are required for removing the reinforcement. A similar method I sused to access the rocker panel reinforcement. Cutting any type of access window is technically considered a sectioning joint, and can be more intrusive since a window has two or more cuts compared to just one in a traditional sectioning joint. Re-welding all these cuts create a larger heat-affect zone, which can be damaging to higher strength steel. It also requires a larger area to be cosmetically finished. For these reasons, this procedure is done only when recommended by the vehicle maker -Other Damage Analysis Inspection items – items to consider when inspecting collision-damaged parts include: -Corrosion that may affect the repair process or indicate previous damage -Previous repairs that may affect how repairs will be made to new damage -Previous repairs are often indicated by plug welds, flange variations, or a thicker finish that requires identification using a film-thickness gauge -If a part was previously repaired, the same repair may not be possible. For example, instead of sectioning in the same location, complete part replacement may be the only option -Other Damage analysis Inspections Items, continued – items to consider when inspecting collision-damaged parts also include damage to the vehicle finish and damage to the suspension or suspension mounting locations. Make sure that the steering wheel is in the straight-ahead position, check the position of the front wheels Steering System Overview (1/3) -Steering System Parts Inspection – it is important for structural and non-structural technicians to identify any damage to steering system parts so the vehicle is returned to the customer in a safe condition. In a visual inspection, inspect for: -Misalignment of the wheel positions in relation to each other -Misalignment of the wheel positions when the steering wheel is centered -Any bent, twisted, or broken parts, parts that have shifted on their mounting locations, and bushing that have moved or are distorted -Damage to system-related wires, connectors, sensors, lines, hoses, and belts -Damage to the vehicle structure at any steering system part mounting location -*Unrelated or wear/service related concerns should be documented as well -Steering Column – steering columns transfer the steering action from the steering wheel to the rack and pinion or parallelogram steering gear. Steering columns are designed to collapse upon impact with the driver and typically contain accessory controls, such as the lighting and windshield wiper controls. Steering column parts include, but are not limited to: -The steering wheel -The steering column jacket -Steering shafts -Universal joints (U-joints_ -Brackets -Steering Wheel Damage Analysis and Inspection – common collision-related damage to the steering wheel includes a cracked or bent steering wheel. Steering wheel damage can also result from airbag deployment. When inspecting the steering wheel, check for the following: -Loose mountings -Steering wheel wobble or runout, which can be an indication of a bent steering shaft -Noise while turning the steering wheel -Abnormal steering effort -A damaged airbag module or cover -Damaged wiring or connectors -Scratches or cuts in the steering wheel -Damage to controls and switches on the steering wheel -Steering Column Shaft – steering column shafts transfer the action of the steering wheel to the steering gear. The steering shaft may be composed of the lower, intermediate, and upper steering shafts -Steering Column Jacket – houses the steering shaft and related parts. Attaches to the vehicle body. May be one or two pieces. * two-piece steering column jackets typically telescope into each other upon impact with the driver. -Universal Joints – U-joints allow the steering shafts to rotate at angles. They may be covered with a plastic shroud or rubber boot. A flexible coupling may be used at the steering gear input. U-joints may be used in two or more locations on the upper and lower steering column. -Upper Steering Column Parts – upper steering column parts may include: -the steering column shaft - lighting controls -the windshield wiper/washer switch -the ignition lock cylinder. -*Some lock cylinders have tamper-resistant fasteners. The tamper-resistant fasteners typically have heads that twist off during installation. To remove the fasteners, a slot may have to be cut into the bolt head. -Additional Steering Column Parts – additional steering column parts include -Springs, seals, bearings, and mounting brackets -The upper and lower shroud or trim cover -* Another important steering column part is the clock spring. The clock spring is located below the steering wheel and provides a continuous electrical connection between the driver airbag system wiring and the airbag module. It may also provide an electrical connection for the horn and other steering wheel mounted controls. Most vehicles will have a steering angle sensor as well. - clock spring is in the center, where the yellow wires are coming out -Steering Column Safety Features – steering columns have a built-in safety feature that absorbs the driver impact by collapsing at a specific point. By collapsing upon impact, injury to the driver may be reduced. Collapsing the steering column also prevents the steering wheel and column from being pushed up toward the driver during a frontal collision. Steering column collapse is managed in two parts: upper and lower. Some steering systems control column collapse by linking the lower section to two or more offset universal joints. The universal joints allow the section to fold upon impact. The U-joints themselves do not collapse. Impact force on the bottom arrow, collapse on the top arrow -Steering Column Capsules – some systems have capsules on the steering column bracket assembly. These are designed to break away during an airbag deployment or driver impact on the steering wheel, allowing the shaft to collapse. During inspection, the capsules must be securely seated in the bracket slots and checked for looseness when pushed or pulled by hand. the yellow area. That clip thing is the steering column capsule -Column Ignition Lock System – some vehicles have a safety feature in the steering column that prevents the steering wheel from being locked when the transmission is in gear and the vehicle may be moving. The steering column electric park lock contains a spring-loaded pin that mechanically prevents the ignition key cylinder from being turned to the LOCK position when the transmission is not in the PARK position. If vehicle power is lost or the transmission is not in the PARK position, the driver will not be able to turn the ignition key to the LOCK position and will not be able to remove the ignition key from the column. -Tilt Steering Columns – tilt steering columns tilt the steering wheel up or down according to the driver’s preference, and have the other end of the upper shaft connected to the lower or intermediate shaft using a U-joint. Some systems tilt at a joint just behind the steering wheel. Others tilt at the U-joint just before the cowl. When the steering wheel tilt lever is released, the spring-loaded locking mechanism is released. The locking mechanism has teeth that fit into a splined collar on the steering column jacket. This type of design is common for vehicles with steering columns that tilt just behind the steering wheel -Power Tilt Steering Column – power tilt steering column systems may have a power tilt mechanism with a memory function built into the tilt operation. If equipped with a memory function, the computer uses a position sensor to determine the location of the column. The column may also have a telescoping feature. -Rack and Pinion Steering System – rack and pinion steering systems are the most common type of steering system for passenger vehicles and trucks up to ½ ton. Rack and pinion systems are lighter weight, have fewer external parts, and take up less room than a parallelogram steering system. A rack and pinion assembly consists of the housing, steering pinion gear, and the steering rack. Other rack and pinion parts include -Tie rods (both inner and outer) -Bushings -Mounting brackets -Bellows boots -Rack and Pinion Inspection – there are several things to check for when you are inspecting a rack and pinion steering system -Rack and Pinion Assembly Inspection – when inspecting the rack and pinion assembly, check for: -Worn or damaged mounting locations and bushing -Visible damage to the housing assembly -Pinion shaft end-play -Damaged tie rod end seals -Damage to lines and hoses -Damaged sensors, connectors, and wiring -Other Assembly Inspection Points – when inspecting the rack and pinion assembly, you should also check for -Wheels not pointing straight ahead when the steering wheel is centered -Steering wheel free-play exceeding ¼ inch -Corrosion or fluid leaks on the assembly -Rack and Pinion Mounting Locations – the rack and pinion assembly may be -Mounted to the front crossmember -Attached to the cowl behind the engine -Attached to the engine cradle -* The steering rack assembly should be level to the vehicle body structure -Pinion Gear – the pinion gear is connected to the steering shaft and has grooves that fit into teeth on the steering rack. It moves the rack right or left based on the rotation of the steering shaft and pinion gear -Steering Rack – the steering rack is a rod with teeth on one side. It meshes with grooves on the pinion gear and moves laterally based on pinion gear rotation. The steering rack also has tie rods on each end that connect to the steering knuckle. -Tie Rods – tie rods connect to the end of the steering rack with an internally or externally threaded inner tie rod. Tie rods also have a threaded end for installation onto the tie rod end. The function and appearance are very similar to the tie rods used on a parallelogram steering system. Tie rod ends are held in place with a lock nut. They have a ball stud that allows lateral and vertical movement and may have a grease zerk. -Ball Sockets – ball sockets are used to connect movable steering parts. For example, ball sockets are used to connect the outer tie rod end with the steering knuckle, or the inner tie rod end with the steering rack. They are used at both ends of a tie rod assembly and allow a swivel motion between parts. Typically, the movable tie rod joint on the outside is called a ball stud, while the tie rod end on the inside is called a ball socket. Ball sockets should not have free play. As this is an indicator of wear or damage -Bellow Boots – bellows boots are a flexible rubber covering over the inner tie rod-to-rack connection. Bellows boots protect the inner ball stud from dirt, dust, water, and various other contaminants. They also retain the lubrication at the joint. If the bellows boots are filled with power steering fluid, that is an indicator the seals on the rack are bad. Rack and Pinion Steering System Operation – on a rack and pinion steering system, the rack slides horizontally on bushings inside of a housing. The rack is moved by the pinion gear that fits into the teeth of the rack. The pinion gear is connected to the steering column shaft, which turns with the steering wheel. When the steering wheel is turned, the pinion rotation moves the rack laterally. The rack moves the inner and outer tie rods. The outer tie rod ends are connected to the steering arms, typically part of the knuckle, which turns the wheels. -Parallelogram System Inspection – when checking the steering system for damage, inspect for the following -Bent, twisted, or broken parts -Noises -Binding -Uneven turning effort -Looseness -Hard steering -Fluid leakage -* Damaged parts should be replaced, not repaired. Never straighten bent steering parts. Straightening may cause safety and part performance issues -Parallelogram Steering System Parts – a parallelogram steering system is most common on heavy duty full-frame vehicles. Parts of a parallelogram steering system include the -Gear box (green) -Sector shaft (orange) -Pitman arm (purple) -Idler arm assembly (blue) -Center link (red) -Tie rods and tie rod ends (yellow) -Steering Gear Box – the steering gear box receives input from the steering column shaft. The rotation of the steering wheel is converted to the lateral movement of the steering linkage. The worm gear, which receives direction from the shaft, has spiral grooves that move a ball nut back and forth. The teeth on the ball nut mesh with the sector shaft gear teeth and move the sector shaft when the worm gear is rotated. Most late model vehicles use gear boxes with recirculating balls. The recirculating balls, which basically function like ball bearings, assist the movement between the ball nut rack and the sector gear. -Pitman Arm – the pitman arm connects the steering gear to the center link. It supports one side of the center link and moves the center link to the right or left. The pitman arm positions the center link at the proper height Center link in the center. Pitman arm on the right -Wear-Type Pitman Arms – a wear-type pitman arm has ball sockets and studs at the outer end. They have studs that fit into the center link opening, as well as a ball and socket that is subject to wear. They may require replacement if the ball stud is loose in the pitman arm. Some pitman arms are equipped with a grease zerk, requiring periodic maintenance. Follow the vehicle maker’s recommendation for the specific type of grease wear type -Non-Wear-Type Pitman Arms – non-wear-type pitman arms have a tapered opening in the outer end. They also have a ball stud in the center link that fits into this tapered opening. Typically, non-wear pitman arms only require replacement if the arm is damaged or bent in a collision. non wear type -Sector Shaft – the sector shaft is part of the steering gear assembly. It is connected directly to the pitman arm and is also known as the pitman shaft. Movement of the sector shaft transfers the steering wheel motion to the horizontal steering system. This sector shaft is twisted at the splines where the pitman arm attaches. circled part is the splines -Idler Arm – the idler arm is connected to the center link with a ball stud. It keeps the center link parallel to the frame mountings. The idler arm support is bolted to the frame on the opposite end of the center link from the pitman arm. Types of idler arms include a -Constant torque-type -Taper-type -Threaded-type -Bushing-type -Center Link – the center link connects the steering gear to the tie rods. It connects to the pitman arm and idler arm to form a parallelogram. The center link provides the proper height for the tie rods, minimizing toe change. A tapered-end-type link has the studs from the pitman arm and idler arm fit into the holes in the ends of the center link. This type of link is considered a non-wear-type link. Some center links have ball studs on the ends that fit into holes in the pitman and idler arms. The ball sockets on the center link may wear. This is considered a wear-type link. Blue is the idler arm, Red is the center link, Purple is the pitman arm -Drag Link System – not all parallelogram systems have idler arms. Some systems use a drag link. The drag link socket is attached to the -Adjusting Tube -Steering Knuckle -* The adjusting tube is positioned between the inner tie rod and drag link socket. This type of systems is typically used only on 2- or 4- wheel drive vehicles with solid axle suspension -Tie Rod Assembly – the tie rod assembly connects the center link to the steering arms. The steering arms are connected to the steering knuckles and may be bolted on to or be part of the knuckle/spindle assembly. A tie rod assembly consists of an inner and outer tie rod end connected by an adjuster sleeve or tube. Each tie rod adjuster sleeve has left-hand and right-hand threads that attach to the tie rod end and tie rod. Sleeve rotation changes the tie rod length and provides toe adjustment. Clamps are used to tighten the tie rod sleeves. Some tie rod assemblies use an inner and outer tie rod that is adjusted by threading the outer tie rod onto the threads of the inner tie rod. The outer tie rod is secured in place with a locking nut. -Steering Damper – the steering damper, also called the stabilizer, helps absorb road shock and prevents the shock from reaching the steering wheel. A steering damper is similar to a shock absorber. It is connected from one of the steering links to the chassis or frame. It is not used on all vehicles. It is most commonly found on larger full-frame or off road vehicles. When a front wheel strikes a road bump, the shock is transferred from the front wheel to the steering linkage, steering gear, and steering wheel. The steering damper reduces the effect on the steering wheel. the gold part -Parallelogram Operation – a parallelogram steering system operates by using a steering a gear box to transfer the steering movement from the steering shaft to the pitman arm. The pitman arm transfer steering motion to the center link. The center link then moves the tie rods, which are mounted to the steering knuckles. An idler arm is connected to the center link on the opposite end from the pitman arm. (picture is when the steering wheel moves to the right, the wheels and all the other parts move to the right as well.) -Power Steering Parts – parts of a conventional hydraulic power steering system include -The power steering hydraulic pump -Power steering hoses and lines -Power steering belt -Power steering fluid reservoir -Spool valve -Torsion bar -Steering rack -Pinion gear -Power Steering Purpose – power steering systems reduce the effect that is required by the driver to turn the steering wheel. Power steering systems may be -Conventional-type power steering systems, which use hydraulic fluid to provide steering assist -Electronic-type power steering systems, which use an electric motor that is mounted to the rack assembly or column to provide steering assist. -What to look for – when performing damage analysis inspections on a power steering system, it is important to look for signs of collision damage. Damaged parts are typically replaced -Power Steering Belt and Pulley Inspection -When inspecting a power steering belt, check for -Cracks or fraying to the webbing or cords -Proper belt tension (belts that slip will not provide the required pressure for steering assist) -Proper belt alignment (improperly aligned belts will cause premature wear of the belt) -When inspecting a pump pulley, check for -Bends or cracks to the pump pulley -A nick in the pulley (nicks can cause premature wear of the belt) -Power Steering Hose and Line Inspection – inspect power steering hoses and lines for -Leaks -Loose or poor connections -Cracks, bends, kinks, swelling, chafing, or other deterioration -Maintenance vs Collision Damage – hoses with a soft and spongy feel may be an indication an improper fluid has been used. If items are found to be in need of repair but appear to be maintenance items vs collision-related damage, these items need to be fully documented and reviewed with the customer. If the customer chooses not to repair these items, your conversation with the customer should be summarized on the final documents and signed by the vehicle owner, to confirm the customer is aware of the necessary repairs. -Hydraulic Power Steering Parts – parts of a typical hydraulic power steering system include the power steering pump, fluid reservoir, fluid lines and hoses, and fluid cooler. Power steering pumps may be either belt-driven or driven by electric motors. Electrically driven hydraulic power steering may be called electro-hydraulic power steering. Power steering coolers are typically hard lines that route the fluid in a back-and-forth pattern. The coolers are typically located in an area where there is good airflow, such as in front of the radiator or lower front crossmember left top Red is the pump, yellow is the reservoir, the hose is the black tube, the line is the silver tube -Electronically Controlled Steering – types of electronically controlled steering include -Variable-assist hydraulic power steering -Variable gear ratio steering -Electro-hydraulic power steering -Electric-assist power steering -Electric steer or steer-by-wire -Variable-Assist Hydraulic Power Steering – at low speeds, variable-assist power steering systems provide more steering assist to create minimal steering effort for easy turning. Electronically controlled variable-assist hydraulic power steering system parts include the parts of a typical hydraulic power steering system. These systems may have a fluid control valve or solenoid. Depending on system design, the fluid control valve can be located at the power steering pump or the steering gear. The system may have additional sensors and parts, and may be controlled by the powertrain control module, or another module -Electro-Hydraulic Power Steering – the main parts of an electro-hydraulic power steering system include the electric motor and hydraulic pump assembly. These are typically one-piece integral units. Depending on the vehicle, these may be located in the front of the vehicle, vulnerable to collision damage. Parts include the electronic control module and input sensors. The control module may be integrated into the pump and motor assembly. Typical input sensors include vehicle speed and electric motor temperature. Main parts also include a conventional power steering gear assembly. -Electric-Assist Power Steering – parts of an electric-assist power steering system include an electric motor mounted to either the steering column or steering gear. These systems use an electric motor instead of hydraulics to provide steering assist. Electric-assist power steering is typically a variable-assist design and uses an electronic control module to control the amount of steering assist provided. -Electric Steer or Steer-by-Wire – electric steer or steer-by-wire systems use an electric motor, instead of a mechanical shaft, to turn the wheels. Electric steer is typically used for the rear steering of four-wheel steering systems. With steer-by-wire, there is no mechanical connection between the steering column and steering gear. A variety of designs were used on early versions of rear four-wheel steering systems for sports and performance vehicles in the late 1980s and early 1990s. Some systems were totally mechanical, with a steering shaft controlling a rear steering gear, while others used hydraulics to turn the rear wheels, with or without electronic control Suspension System Overview (2/3) Front Suspension System -Visual Inspection Overview – During vehicle disassembly and repairs, it is important to identify any damage to suspension system parts so the vehicle is returned to the customer in a safe condition. Though a non-structural technician will not typically complete suspension-related work on a vehicle, they should have some familiarity with the parts, operation, and knowledge of inspecting basic damage. A visual inspection includes -Conducting a walk around to observe the positions of the wheels in relation to each other and the wheel openings -Looking for ride height differences from side to side, or front to back -Looking for any bent, twisted, or broken parts -Assessing parts that have shifted on their mounting locations -Checking bushings that have moved or are distorted, bent ball joints, and damaged or distorted dust/grease boots, which may indicate a bent ball joint stud. -*Inspect system-related cables, connectors, sensors, lines, and hoses for damage, as well as damage to the vehicle structure, where the suspension is mounted. If there are any unrelated or service/wear concerns, document them -Abnormal Noises – when moving the vehicle into the shop, listen for any abnormal noises. Clunking or rattling noises may indicate loose fasteners or bushings where steering or suspension parts attach to the chassis. If a bushing is loose in a part, it can cause unwanted movement where it attaches to the vehicle. Other causes of clunks include loose ball sockets on tie rods and ball joints, and part interference during suspension deflection. Clicking noises may indicate interference problems or problems with parts that have rotational movement. Rumbling or squealing sounds are typically caused by bearings or power steering problems. If there is damage to a tire or wheel, the corresponding wheel bearing may also be damaged. * Test equipment is available that utilizes clip-on microphones to allow listening to wheel bearings and other parts suspected of making noise -Front Suspension System Description – vehicle suspension systems are designed to support the vehicle, keep the tires in contact with the road surface, provide directional control of the vehicle, maintain alignment angles, and provide ride quality. Suspension systems found on vehicles include -MacPherson strut – a strut style suspension utilizes a strut with a coil spring to support the weight of the vehicle and maintain wheel position. A strut assembly consists of a strut housing with a strut rod, upper mount/bearing, and spring. Additional parts include the steering knuckle, hub and bearing assembly, lower control arm with ball joint, and stabilizer bar and links (if equipped). The strut mount attaches to the top of the strut rod and holds the coil spring in position. The strut mount also secures the strut assembly to the vehicle. * The strut also acts as a ride damper or shock absorber -Continued – the coil spring mounts between the strut and strut mount. The steering knuckle is typically a cast aluminum or cast steel part. The steering knuckle attaches to both the strut and the lower control arm, and sometimes the steering linkage. The steering knuckle also attaches to the hub, bearing, or spindle. The lower control arm attaches to the engine cradle or vehicle frame. The lower control arm positions the wheel at the bottom. The lower ball joint connects the lower control arm and steering knuckle. The ball joint allows movement when steering left and right, along with movement when the wheel moves up and down. Photo includes (from top to bottom): Upper strut mount, strut rod, coil spring, strut housing, steering knuckle, hub and bearing assembly, lower ball joint, lower control arm -Modified strut – a modified strut suspension system is similar to a MacPherson strut system in design. This type of strut has the coil spring located between the lower control arm and frame or crossmember, instead of on the strut housing. The spring, lower control arm, and ball joint support the vehicle’s weight. The lower control arm and upper strut mount maintain wheel position Photo includes (front right to left) – ball joint, coil spring, lower control arm, crossmember -Short-long arm (SLA) – parts of an SLA suspension include the: Steering knuckle, Hub and bearing or spindles and bearings, Upper and lower control arms, Ball joints, Coil springs (or torsion bars on some vehicles), Shock absorbers, Stabilizer bar and links (if equipped) – this may be referred to as a “sway bar” in some service information. * The steering knuckle may also be referred to as the spindle assembly Photo includes (from top to bottom): Upper ball joint, Steering knuckle, Spindle, Lower ball joint, Stabilizer links, Shock absorber, Coil spring, Stabilizer bar -Double wishbone – a double wishbone front suspension is a modified SLA design. Tis suspension features upper and lower control arms (wishbones) to position the wheel. The steering knuckle is taller than on a typical SLA suspension. This suspension uses a fixed position strut with coil spring that typically has a damper fork on the bottom to support the vehicle’s weight. The damper fork connects the strut to the lower control arm and allows the axle shaft to pass through its center. Double wishbone suspensions may have a radius arm attached to the lower control arm for positioning the lower ball joint at the correct position. * The strut assembly does not control wheel position on the double wishbone suspension Photo includes (from top to bottom): Upper control arm, Upper ball joint, Knuckle, Hub and bearing, Lower ball joint, Lower control arm, Damper fork, Strut assembly -Multi-link – a multi-link suspension is similar in design and function to double wishbone suspension, but instead of using a single upper and lower control arm, this system typically uses two upper and lower control arms. Each arm has a ball joint in the end where it connects to the steering knuckle. Multi-link suspensions use a fixed position strut or coil-over shock for suspension control. Only one of the lower arms is weight bearing, or has the strut attached to it. * Multi-link suspensions may use three or more control links/arms to control wheel position Photo includes (from top to bottom): Upper control arms(links), Coil-over shock, Steering knuckle, Hub and bearing assembly, Lower control arms (links) -Solid axle – parts of a typical solid axle front suspension include the axle housing, axle shafts, springs, shock absorbers, control arms, hub and bearing assemblies, stabilizer bar and links (if equipped), and steering knuckles. The steering knuckles are typically attached to the axle housing with ball joints, but may use bushed kingpins, or spindle bolts that provide the pivot points for the front wheels to turn. * Heavy trucks typically use a solid front axle beam Photo includes (from right to left): Hub assembly, Ball joint, Shock absorber, Spring, Control arm, Differential, Axle housing, Steering knuckle -Twin I-beam -* The twin I-beam suspension was popular on Ford trucks for many years and utilized two separate front axle beams Top left – MacPherson Strut Top right – Double Wishbone Bottom left – Multi-Link Bottom right – Short-Long Arm (SLA) Rear Suspension -Rear Suspension System Description – rear suspension systems may either be a solid axle design or an independent design. Solid axles may be drive axles on rear-wheel drive and four-wheel drive vehicles, or they may be trailing axles on front-wheel drive vehicles. An independent rear suspension is used to improve the ride and handling of a vehicle. Independent rear suspensions may be found on front- and rear-wheel drive vehicles, as well as four-wheel drive vehicles. They may be either a strut or an SLA design -Solid Drive Axle – parts of a typical solid drive axle rear suspension system include the axle housing, ring and pinion gears and differential, axle shafts, bearings, springs, shock absorbers, and stabilizer bar and links, if equipped. The wheel bearings are typically located in the end of the axle housing tube. Axles with leaf springs usually have U-bolts securing the axle to the springs, bushings in the front and rear spring eyes, a mounting bracket in the front, and rear mounting shackles. Axles with coil springs use trailing or control arms with bushings at the mounting points, and usually have a panhard rod/track bar to keep the axle centered Photo includes (from bottom to top): Axle shaft, leaf spring, Axle housing, Shock absorber -Solid Trailing Axle – parts of a typical solid trailing axle rear suspension system include the axle tube (or beam), springs, shock absorbers, trailing arms on coil spring designs, hub and bearing assemblies or spindles and bearings, and stabilizer bar and links, if equipped. Springs may either be coil or leaf design, with coil springs being the most common. Trailing arms have bushings where they connect to the axle housing and chassis. Some designs may have the trailing arms built into the axle housing. Photo includes (from left to right): Hub and bearing assembly, Trailing arm, Shock absorber, Track bar, Axle tube, Coil spring -Independent Strut Style – parts of a typical strut-style independent rear suspension system include control arms, knuckles, strut assemblies – which include the coil springs, stabilizer bar and links (if equipped), tie rods and tie rod ends, and radius rods or trailing arms. These systems have adjustable links for adjusting the rear toe. Tie rods may be adjustable or solid rods that adjust with cams where they attach to the chassis. Radius rods or trailing arms may be used for vehicles that have a single bar lower control arm. The radius rod or trailing arm is used to maintain the wheel position for the proper wheelbase. The independent strut rear suspension is typically found on front-wheel drive vehicles Photo includes (from left to right): Knuckles, Lower control arms, Strut assemblies -Independent SLA Style – parts of a typical SLA-style independent rear suspension system include the upper and lower control arms, ball joints, knuckles, springs, shock absorbers, stabilizer bar and links (if equipped), and tie rod and tie rod ends. Control arms have bushings where they are attached to the chassis. Springs may be either coil or leaf, with coil being the most commonly used. Photo includes (from top to bottom): Upper control arm, Tie rod, Lower control arm Suspension System Overview ( 3/3) Ride Height Control Parts and Operation -Ride Height Difference – when inspecting for ride height different, begin by looking at the tires and wheels. After an inspection of the tires and wheels indicate that there is no damage or that they are properly inflated, the next visual inspection criteria would be a ride height difference. This vehicle has a visible ride height difference, or is out of level, between the left and right front Ride height difference -Measuring Ride Height – ride height measurements can either be compared to a listed specification or to the opposite side of the vehicle. Ways of measuring ride height include measuring from the ground up to specified points on the chassis. This should be done on a level surface with the front wheels pointed straight ahead. Ride height can also be measured between suspension parts and reference points on the chassis. This method is used to identify a weak or damaged spring as the cause of the ride height problem, but will not identify which end of the vehicle has the bad spring. Ensure that the tires are all the same size and at the correct air pressure, and that there are no excessive loads in the vehicle, before measuring the ride height. * Having a location that is pre-determined to be level, for making measurements that need to be done from a level surface, can help to reduce damage analysis cycle time -Causes of Ride Height Problems – causes of ride height problems include -Structural damage to the vehicle -Weak or damaged springs, which will typically cause both ends of the side of the vehicle with the bad spring to have an incorrect ride height -A bent shock absorber or strut rod, which may limit suspension travel -* Ride height differences may be caused by uneven or excessive loads in the vehicle. Ensure that there are no excessive loads in the vehicle when checking ride height. Additionally, if equipped, custom aftermarket suspension parts and tires may result in measurements different than the original specifications for the vehicle, such as the ride height. * If a bent shock absorber or strut rod causes the suspension to become frozen, in eight the fully extended or fully retracted position, the ride height will not be correct -Electronically Controlled Suspension Identification – the presence of electronically controlled suspension systems is typically not identifiable by emblems or other highly visible labeling on the vehicle. *Caution! Identify disabling procedures for ride height control systems before jacking or lifting the vehicle to avoid system damage -Part Identification – perform a thorough visual inspection of the suspension system. Look for wiring connected to suspension parts, such as shock absorbers or struts. Look for the presence of air or hydraulic fluid lines to the shock absorbers, struts, or springs. Check for the presence of a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL) for an electronic suspension system when the ignition is first turned ON. A pre-scan may reveal damage-related codes. Look for switches on the dash or console for manual control of electronic suspension systems. Systems may have an On/Off switch, a switch for sport or comfort damper settings, or a switch for control of vehicle height. Visually identify parts that would indicate the presence of specific electronic or electronic/hydraulic systems -Option Labels – the vehicle option label provides useful information for system identification. Many repair shops and dealership parts departments can decode the option label to determine if an electronically controlled suspension system is present on the vehicle. A VIN-driven parts ordering system may also be used to determine if the vehicle is equipped with an electronically controlled suspension system -Computer-Controlled Ride Height – computer-controlled ride height systems are one type of electronically controlled suspension system. These systems are sometimes called level-ride or automatic load leveling systems. Computer-controlled ride height systems may be on the rear of the vehicle only, or the system may be on all four corners of the vehicle. Computer-controlled ride height systems may be air systems. The ride height may be adjusted using air shock absorbers, or the vehicle may be equipped with air springs. These systems may also be designed to use a hydraulic system that controls the ride height adjustment -Air Shock Absorber Height Control Systems – the simplest type of height control is the air shock absorber system, which only controls the height at the rear of the vehicle. These systems typically include rear air shock absorbers that do not act as springs for the vehicle, but rather, are used to assist the springs in holding the vehicle level with heavy loads. -Parts – height control systems include an air compressor with a dryer to remove moisture from the air. These systems may use air lines from the compressor to the shock absorbers. An exhaust valve allows air to be removed from the shock absorbers. The exhaust valve is typically built into the air compressor. These systems include height sensors that monitor the ride height of the vehicle. Some rear-only air shock absorber systems have a combination height sensor and control module assembly -Operation – height control systems that use a rear air shock absorber control the ride height be either adding or removing air from the shock absorber air chamber. These systems are controlled by the rear height sensor and control module. The height sensor determines rear ride height and either instructs the air compressor to run or the exhaust valve to open. Typically, these systems have delayed activation to avoid having the compressor run in response to temporary changes in ride height -Hydraulic Height Control Systems – hydraulic height control systems have hydro-pneumatic cylinders in place of the conventional shock absorbers or struts. These systems use the hydro-pneumatic cylinders to control both the ride height and suspension damping force. Hydro-pneumatic cylinders work in series with the vehicle springs. The hydraulic cylinders are used to work with the conventional springs and provide additional and adjustable spring rate and ride height to the vehicle. -Parts – the basic parts of a hydraulic height control system typically include a hydraulic fluid pump, fluid pressure accumulator, control valves or actuators, hydraulic cylinders, input sensors, a control module, switches, and other basic electrical circuit parts. Hydraulic systems may have high pressure fluids, which may cause injury – do not loosen lines or hose connections unless following service information cautions and procedures -Operation – hydraulic height control systems have accumulators that store hydraulic fluid in one chamber, separated from high pressure gas in another chamber by a rubber bladder. The system may have a front and rear accumulator or separate accumulators for each corner of the suspension. Control valves control the flow of hydraulic fluid between the accumulator and hydraulic cylinder. When the control module calls for increased ride height at a corner, the control valve opens a valve that allows fluid to flow into the hydraulic cylinder and lift the vehicle. Hydraulic fluid is added and removed from the hydraulic cylinders at each corner of the vehicle to either raise or lower ride height. Ride Damper Control Parts and Operation -Computer-Controlled Variable Shock Absorber Damping – another type of electronically controlled suspension is computer-controlled variable shock absorber damping. Variable shock absorber damping may be controlled mechanically by varying the orifice size for the shock absorber fluid. Damping may also be controlled magnetically by varying the viscosity of the shock absorber fluid -Semi-Active Suspensions – semi-active suspension systems typically consist of computer-controlled variable dampers that respond to up-and-down body motion inputs to read the road surface. These systems react to the first bounce or suspension deflection to control the second bounce or deflection. They are not active, but rather reactive. Semi-active systems do not directly control suspension deflection; they indirectly control it by altering the firmness of the shock absorber damping -Damper Controls – control of variable rate dampers is handled by a control module, which sends an output signal to a solenoid valve located inside the shock absorber body, or in a damping control unit located outside the shock absorber body. Another type of variable rate damper uses an electromagnetic coil to produce a magnetic field that changes the viscosity of a special hydraulic fluid -Internal Valve Damper Control – the operation of computer-controlled suspension dampers may be electronically controlled by a solenoid or stepper motor. These use internal actuators or valves located inside the damper body or piston to control the fluid in the shock absorber. Valves are opened or closed to vary the opening size and resistance to fluid flow inside the shock absorber. More resistance to flow produces a firmer suspension damping force, and less resistance produces softer damping force -External Valve Damper Control – the solenoid or stepper motor may be located in external damping valves on some systems.
