External damping units are used to allow for more valves than would fit internally in the shock absorber body. The additional valves allow a greater degree of tuning flexibility due to the increase in the number of damping force settings -Magnetic Damper Control – magnetically controlled variable damper systems have constant shock absorber valving with a variable viscosity fluid. These systems are less complicated and have fewer parts than valve-based variable dampers. The damping force firmness is controlled by varying the viscosity of the fluid inside the shock absorber, instead of varying the opening size that the fluid is forced through -Magnetic Damper Fluid – magnetic damper systems use a special fluid to control the damping force firmness. The fluid has special randomly suspended magnetic particles. In the presence of a magnetic field, the particles align themselves with the magnetic field lines and each other. This raises the viscosity of the fluid. The fluid has an unlimited range of viscosity depending on the strength of the magnetic field in which it is placed. It can go from a fluid-like state when no magnetic field is present, to an almost plastic-like state in the presence of a strong magnetic field. The magnetic field is produced by an electromagnet located on the piston of the damper. Off ontop. On on bottom -Combination System – vehicles with electronically controlled suspensions may have combination systems. Combination systems have computer control of both ride height and shock absorber damping. These may have air springs and separate computer-controlled variable dampers. Combination systems may use computer-controlled dampers to adjust both the ride height and handling characteristics of the vehicle. Dampers in these systems may be hydraulic struts with air bladders built in for springs, or they may be totally hydraulic for both suspension damping and ride height -Active Stabilizer Bars – conventional stabilizer bars (anti say bar/roll bar) help reduce body roll when cornering by connecting the left and right suspension together with a torsion bar and end links that transfer movement of the suspension on one side to the other, resisting body roll; this can result in a harsh ride condition. Active stabilizer bars allow for high levels of body roll control and suspension handling, while using softer springs and dampers for increased ride comfort. These stabilizer bars also have improved straight line suspension comfort over conventional stabilizer bars because they do not stiffen the basic suspension during a one-sided suspension compression. *Off-road vehicles may use a special stabilizer bar with an actuator that can decouple the two ends, allowing for greater suspension travel -Parts – the basic parts of an active stabilizer bar system include a hydraulic fluid pump and reservoir, control module, control valves, high pressure hydraulic fluid lines, lateral acceleration sensors, hydraulic motors or actuators, and stabilizer bars. Each axle may have a stabilizer bar that is made of two parts, or it may have a solid bar that is connected to the suspension on one side with a variable hydraulic linear actuator -Operation – when a vehicle that is equipped with active stabilizer bars goes around a corner or curve, the lateral acceleration or yaw rate sensors detect the body roll and send a signal to the control module. The control module acts on the valve blocks to supply pressurized hydraulic fluid to the correct side of the oscillating motor or hydraulic actuator. The oscillating motor, or actuator, then twists the stabilizer bar halves in opposite directions to counteract the roll of the vehicle body over the suspension The Best or Nothing: The Mercedes-Benz Story (X0039E-US.SSB) X0039E-US.SSB – Pre-Test In the Nice Speedweek race, Emil Jellinick entered a Daimler vehicle under what name -&gt; Monsieur Mercedes Which dealership department consults with customers to help them choose the best Mercedes-Benz vehicle to suit their needs? The vehicle is removed from the dealer’s line of credit -When a lease ends, Mercedes-Benz Financial Services determines what happens with the vehicle. -A lease vehicle can be disposed of through three primary channels: -Customer – can purchase the vehicle at residual value -Dealer – can purchase the vehicle for their Pre-Owned fleet -Auction – Vehicle can be entered into auction -Mercedes-Benz Financial Services -Helps support the ownership experience for both retail and lease customers -Develops, maintains, and provides resources for vehicle repair and maintenance -Service – Technicians use – STAR TekInfo – official MBUSA site that hosts technical information for service and repair on Mercedes-Benz vehicles -MBUSA – provides dealers with direct communication with specialists -Vehicle Master Inquiry System – helps dealers maintain ownership and service records -Mercedes-Benz Academy – provides technical, product, and skills training -Three orders -Stock Order – within 48 hours from your facing PDC, up to 5 days from non-facing PDCs -Daily Stock Order (DSO) – next day from facing PDC if the order is placed before 1pm, up to 5 days from non-facing PDC -Vehicle Off-Road (VOR) – urgent parts received within 24 hours -MBUSA has 5 parts distribution centers -California -Texas -Illinois -Florida -New Jersey -CAC (customer assistance center) Contact Info: 1-800-FOR-MERCEDES (800-367-6372) -Fax: 1-201-476-6212 -Email: Mercedes_benz@mailca.custhelp.com X0039E-US.SSB – Lesson 6 Driven to Delight Customers are the VIPs -Driven to Delight – Delivering World-Class Customer Experience the Mercedes-Benz Way -LEAD = Listen, Empathize, Add value, Delight -Show Empathy -This shows MB way of delivering exceptional service -Delight – Share Knowledge, Add a touch of luxury -&gt; My customer -Every customer, every time, everywhere, no exceptions -List: - www.mercedes-benz.com/en/Mercedes-benz/classic/museum -MBUSA Website: mbusa.com -Daimler Website: www.daimler.com -MBUSA Youtube Channel: www.youtube.com/user/mbusa -Twitter: @MercedesBenz -Instagram: @mbusa @mercedesbenz -Facebook: www.facebook.com/MercedesBenz -LinkedIn: www.linkedin.com/company/mercedes-benz-usa -iBooks on LL: learn.mbusa.com Understanding Cycle Time as an Appraiser (1/2) Cycle Time Overview -Cycle Time Definition – what is cycle time? It is the time from the beginning to the end of a process. The definition of cycle time varies slightly depending on perspective. For example, for the: -Customer, or vehicle owner, it is the time from when the vehicle keys are handed to a repair facility to when the keys are returned after a satisfactory vehicle repair -Repair facility, it may be a measurement of time from when the vehicle arrived to when the vehicle is delivered to the customer -Insurer, it is the time from the first notice of loss until the last payment of the claim. Insurers will also monitor repair facility cycle time by looking at keys-to-keys or the length of time for a rental car -Cycle Time Definition – what is cycle time? It is the time from the beginning to the end of a process. The definition of cycle time varies slightly depending on perspective. For example, for the: -Customer, or vehicle owner, it is the time from when the vehicle keys are handed to a repair facility to when the keys are returned after a satisfactory vehicle repair -Repair facility, it may be a measurement of time from when the vehicle arrived to when the vehicle is delivered to the customer -Insurer, it is the time from the first notice of loss until the last payment of the claim. Insurers will also monitor repair facility cycle time by looking at keys-to-keys time or the length of time for a rental car -Who Affects Cycle Time? – everyone affects cycle time including: -Those involved in the processing of the claim -The call center claim rep -The auto physical damage appraiser (APDA) -Repair facilities -Sublet vendors -The customer -Benefits of Reducing Cycle Time – the benefits of reducing cycle time include improved customer satisfaction index (CSI), reduced rental car time, an increase in productivity, and an increase in the quality of work. CSI is the measure of how the vehicle owner feels about the entire repair experience. Vehicles that are returned faster to the customer can add to the positive repair experience -Production Efficiency – one goal is to develop an efficient production process so the vehicles flow through the repair process without any interruption. This can lead to increased capacity which will help other vehicles in early stages of the claims process move forward more quickly. Work that is done more efficiently has a direct effect on the quality of the work done. When there is less of a rush to get the vehicle ready for delivery, fewer things get missed. Additionally, when there is less stress, everyone involved in the claims process has a positive attitude, and it shows in the quality of work output and CSI results -Principles of Reducing Cycle Time – the principles of reducing cycle time include doing tasks that allow work to be done more continuously on each vehicle, not necessarily being more productive on each vehicle. Working more continuously on each vehicle means less time spent in each repair bay as the vehicle keeps moving to the next stage of repair. This is commonly referred to as touch time. Other principles include breaking down the barriers that slow workflow and improving efficiency in every phase of the repair -Methods of Reducing Cycle Time – methods an APDA could implement to improve repair efficiency include: -Coordinating with the repair shop to inspect the vehicle the same day it is scheduled to be fully disassembled and ready for an initial or supplement inspection -Inspecting all damaged bolt-on parts and structural, electrical, and mechanical damages to ensure all damage is identified. This will reduce the likelihood of additional supplements -Reviewing damage in detail with the repair shop to build an itemized blueprint of the repair plan. Set expectations for communication regarding questionable damage or future supplemental damage that is found -What Reducing Cycle Time does not mean – reducing cycle time does not mean cutting corners, sacrificing quality for faster times, or doing an incomplete or improper repair. The goal is having a complete and safe repair, while at the same time improving the claim cycle -Measuring Cycle Time – in order to improve cycle time, record a baseline measurement of at least 30 days of data. For example, record the days from when the claim is assigned to an APDA to the date the customer is contacted to schedule the repair inspection. Also, record when the claim is assigned to an APDA to the date the initial inspection is completed. Can cycle time be broken down even further? -Breaking Down Cycle Time – within the claims process, there are several areas, or sub-cycles, we can focus on. For example, cycle time could be from when the estimate is complete to the start of the repair or from the date of the supplement request to supplement completion. Tracking sub-cycle time will help to identify opportunities for improvement. If you only look at the initial call to final payment, you lose opportunities for efficiency. Any improvement in sub-cycle time will improve overall cycle time -Chart Cycle Time – Identify Improvement Opportunities – vendors of claim management systems and insurance companies use various computer programs to measure and calculate cycle times. Use a simple chart to record cycle time. Include rows and columns to note milestones. Include when the APDA was notified of vehicle disassembly, a supplement request was made, and when the vehicle was re-inspected. Also, record when the vehicle was re-inspected to the date the supplement was completed and provided to the repair facility. Note when the vehicle was dropped off at the repair facility to the date of delivery. Another sub-cycle is from when the claim was assigned to the APDA to the date final payment was issued and the claim was closed. From this information, average the totals, with the final column indicating the total cycle time. This allows you to evaluate the numbers and determine opportunities for improvement -Inaccurate or Missing Information – inaccurate or missing information can affect the claims process. For example, inaccurate or missing information can lead to supplements, lost productivity, and increased claims processing expense. Examples of inaccurate or missing information may include the: -Customer contact information -Customer address -Vehicle information -Facts of loss -Damage location -Repair facility location -Lean Processing – one of the steps of eliminating waste is to work lean. Lean processing in a one-phrase definition is to get the right tools and materials to the right place at the right time while minimizing waste. For example, reviewing all assigned vehicle inspections and mapping out the most efficient route for completing the inspections would be an example of minimizing waste. Implementing lean processes also requires being open to change. Lean processing is about working efficiently, being organized, and always looking for opportunities for improvement. Lean processing principle include a commitment to continuously improving work quality and taking a proactive, rather than a reactive approach. It is more productive to anticipate problems before they happen and implement a preventive action, rather than dealing with them as they occur -Applied to an APDA – what are some lean processes that can be applied to an APDA? Some examples include creating files for each of the standard required documents and having them available when inspecting a vehicle. Create SOPs for a “toolbox”, such as cameras, forms, a tape measure, and tread depth gauges. Review all scheduled appointments to ensure the most efficient route mapping. This will help to ensure the right tools are at the right place at the right time while minimizing waste -SOPs – many insurers and collision repair facilities have developed SOPs to provide written, and in some cases, visual guidance to ensure processes are performed exactly the same every time -Examples of SOPs for an APDA – what are some SOPs an APDA could implement? -Have contact with vehicle owners in the same day to coordinate inspection appointments -Thoroughly review the vehicle to identify all damage visible at the time of inspection -Review the damage and repair plan in detail, and communicate expectations for supplemental damages if the vehicle is at a repair facility -Complete the estimate while on-site at the vehicle. This will allow you to compare the appraisal to the damaged vehicle to verify that nothing was overlooked, while having easy access in the event questions arise -Follow guidelines for digital photos -Complete supplement inspections and provide copies to the repair facility within 24 hours of request Understanding Cycle Time as an Appraiser (2/2) Cycle Time Management before Repairs -Cycle Time Bandits – cycle time bandits are practices that steal time and make process time less efficient. Cycle time bandits specific to estimating include an incomplete damage report and hidden damage discovered after the repair process has begun. This can result in supplements and repairs that have not been authorized, thus causing delays. These do not have insurance authorization, so the repair may have to wait. How do you reduce cycle bandits? Blueprinting -Repair Blueprinting – blueprinting provides an entire repair plan picture before repairs are started. All needed parts are determined down to the clips, moldings, and one-time use fasteners. This helps to ensure all the parts are in-house before work is begun, determine any sublet work, and find and hidden damage. Many repair facilities have implemented repair blueprinting. Blueprinting is often credited as being the number one efficiency improvement step. This allows the vehicle to enter the repair stream and continue without stopping -Vehicle Disassembly – blueprinting should include disassembly sufficient to expose 100% of the related damage. This means disassembling the vehicle until all of the damaged parts and related panels are removed. Disassembly exposes all damage by removing all related panels necessary to obtain access. This will also allow all damaged parts requiring replacement to be identified and ordered. Disassembly will determine which clips or fasteners must be ordered. Because some clips or fasteners will break during removal, identifying these up front will allow them to be ordered and be on hand during the reassembly process -Repair vs Replace Decisions – disassembly of the vehicle to the point of exposing all related damage also allows for all repair vs replace decisions to be made. Have vehicle maker repair procedures available at the time of disassembly. This will also help to identify any tools, equipment, or repair materials required -Completing the Teardown – note that disassembly of the vehicle to expose 100% of the related damage should not be comprised based on job size. Teardown at the beginning may feel like wasted time or inefficient use of time, but it allows an efficient repair process. Benefits include that all parts are ordered, and there are no surprises during the repair, resulting in better cycle time, reduced rental and claims processing exposure, and improved CSI results. The APDA should communicate with the shop expectation that they need digital documentation of the teardown stages. Record stages of the disassembly with digital photos and measurements. This can be done with a tape measure or an electronic measuring system. If electronic measuring is available, include a printout of the measurements -Technician Participation – why is it important for the technician to be available when writing an estimate? Technician input helps determine what can be repaired and what must be replaced. This input also helps with the parts ordering, such as what can be reused and what needs to be purchased. Writing an estimate without technician input can lead to installing a part even when it is not needed. In a time when severity is driving up the number of totals, it becomes more critical to understand what can be repaired and what absolutely must be replaced. A technician may be able to work on dents during the blueprinting process to verify repairability. Writing an estimate without technician participation can lead to a part being discarded without photo documentation -What is a Supplement? – what is a supplement? A supplement is identifying additional parts or labor necessary to complete the repair. As an industry, supplements have become too commonplace and accepted as just part of doing business. Don’t let this be your culture. The repair processes are more efficient if more time is taken at the beginning than filing a supplement in the middle or end of the repair. There is a saying “slow down to go faster”. Going slower on the front end during blueprinting means faster repairs toward the end of the process. Avoid the temptation and pressure to hurry through the estimating and blueprinting process. One of the reasons for disassembling the vehicle to expose 100% of the related damage is to avoid supplements which result in repair delays -What does a Supplement Cost? – actual supplement costs vary according to what needs to be repaired. But it is important to look beyond just part and labor cost. Supplement costs include decreased CSI. Customers will feel frustrated when the vehicle is not delivered on the date promised. Unhappy customers could translate to lost business. This could also result in additional expenses for added services to improve customer relations. Supplement costs also include longer keys-to-keys. This can result in increased rental or claims processing time. For the APDA, supplement costs can include lost time for new claim inspections and additional travel expenses. What can be done to help avoid supplement costs? Follow proper blueprinting processes, including disassembly sufficient to expose all related damage Understanding High Voltage Safety (1/2) Electricity Basics -Voltage – voltage is the amount of pressure that is available to push the current through a conducting loop or circuit. This is the electrical potential difference, or the difference in electrical potential between two places in a circuit. Higher voltage will push larger amounts of current through a circuit. If there is no voltage, there can be no current flow. Voltage is measured in volts. The symbol for voltage is V or sometimes an E (electromotive force) when voltage is used in mathematical equations. -Voltage = Pressure -Measured in Volts -Symbol = V or E -Current – current is the rate of flow of electricity, the quantity of electrons moving past a fixed point in one second. It is measured in amperes or amps. The symbol for current is A or I in equations. There are two types, direct current (DC) and alternating current (AC) -Current = Rate of Flow of Electrons -Measured in Ampere or Amps -Symbol = A or I -Direct Current (DC) – with DC circuits, current flows in only one direction. Typically, DC power comes from batteries that have negative and positive terminals. Current is the flow of electrons from the negative side of a battery through the circuit to the positive side of the battery. On most low voltage systems, less than 60 volts DC, the negative battery terminal is connected to the vehicle chassis. This is called grounding. High voltage systems, 60 volts or higher, are isolated from the vehicle body and chassis on both the positive and negative side of the system -Direct Current (DC): -Flow of electrons in single direction -Example – automotive battery provides DC -Alternating Current (AC) – alternating current switches direction in a circuit, alternating back and forth many times per second. AC is commonly supplied by utility companies to residential and commercial locations. Alternating current is also used in electric and hybrid vehicle drive motors and related circuits -Alternating Current (AC): -Flow of electrons switch direction backward and forward -Resistance – electrical resistance restricts the flow of electric current. Within an electrical circuit, a motor or other load are the main source of resistance. Components called resistors may also be added to a circuit to restrict the flow of electricity. Resistance is measured in ohms and the resistance symbol is Ω or R when u sed in equations -Resistance = Restriction to Flow -Measured in Ohms -Symbol = Ω or R -Ohm’s Law – in most situations, you will not need to use Ohm’s Law to perform your job. The Ohm’s Law Formula does not provide a great visual of the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance. An understanding of this relationship is very important when working around high voltage (HV) systems. With Ohm’s Law, if we know two of the circuit values, we can solve for the third value. For example: if we know what the voltage and resistance are in the circuit, we can solve for the current in the circuit. Voltage divided by resistance equals current. Here are two examples of the Ohm’s Law relationship: -If the voltage in a circuit increases and resistance stays constant, the amount of current will decrease -If the voltage in a circuit stays constant and resistance decreases, the amount of current will increase -To find the voltage – V = I x R -To find the current – I = V ÷ R -To find the resistance – R = V ÷ I -Ohm’s Law – -Voltage – using Ohm’s Law, lets solve for the voltage in this circuit. We know that the circuit has 0.3 amps of current. We also see that there are 40 ohms of resistance in the circuit. Ohm’s Law states that current multiplied by resistance equals voltage. For example, in this circuit 0.3 amps X 40 ohms = 12 volts. -Current – lets solve for the current in this low voltage circuit. We know that the circuit has a 12-volt power supply. We also know that there are 60 ohms of resistance in the circuit. Ohm’s Law states voltage divided by resistance equals current. For example, in this circuit 12 volts / 60 ohms = 0.2 amps -Resistance – finally, lets solve for the resistance in this circuit. We know that the circuit has 3 amps of current. We also know that there is a 12-volt battery in the circuit. Ohm’s Law states that voltage divided by current equals resistance. For example, in this circuit 12 volts / 3 amps = 4 ohms -Power Source – a basic electrical circuit contains a power source, load, and path with wires connecting them all together. The power source provides voltage for a circuit. Batteries, electrical generators, and alternators are common sources of power. When a power source is connected to a complete circuit, voltage pushes electrical current. -Circuit Path – wires provide the path for current to flow. Circuits are insulated wire that connect different system parts together in a circuit. They use connectors at the attachment points on the parts to link them together. A wiring harness is a group of wires routed around the vehicle. Electricity flows through some materials more easily than others. There materials are either conductors or insulators. Insulators are materials that restrict or stop current flow. Glass, plastic, and some types of rubber are insulators. Wood is not considered a good conductor, but it will conduct electricity. Wet wood conducts better than dry wood. Pure water is not a good conductor, but most water is not pure. Water typically contains impurities; these can act as charged particles called ions which allowed current to flow. -Loads – loads consume the electrical energy in a circuit and perform work. Typical loads may include: -Lights -Heating elements -Displays -Sneakers -Motors -Solenoids -Note that batteries may be considered a load in a circuit when they are being charged -Switches, Relays, and Circuit Protections – switches provide a way to open or close the circuit path so the load can be turned on or off. A relay is an electromagnetic switch that is operated by a relatively small electric current that can turn on or off another electric circuit. So, a relay is an electric-controlled switch. Circuit protections are designed to open the circuit path if excessive current is flowing. This provides protection for other parts in the circuit. -Relay Use in HV System – as mentioned, a relay can be used to turn on or off another separate circuit, such as a much larger electric current system like a high voltage battery. In this case, relays are used to provide electrical isolation of the HV system from the low voltage circuit. The low voltage circuits are grounded to the chassis. The high voltage circuit is not grounded to the chassis. It uses a floating system that will be discussed in detail later in this module. The relay or contactor provides the on and off switching of the high voltage system providing the electrical isolation required. -Your Body: Conductor or Insulator? – our bodies are not insulators, they are conductors. It is true that they are not as good of a conductor as a metal wire, but your body will conduct current if you become part of a circuit. We are conductors because our body is mostly made of water. If your body becomes part of an electrical circuit, you may be injured or killed if enough current is flowing through your body. Our brains, nerves, and muscles run on small electrical signals. Large amounts of electricity can cause harmful interruptions to those electrical signals. -Electrical conductors allow the energy to go through it – sea water, gold, silver -Electrical insulators don’t allow the energy to go through it – glass, rubber, plastic -You have skin in the game – our skin has higher resistance than your inner tissue. Dry skin tends to have high resistance, but this does not make your skin an insulator. Dry skin might increase your resistance at the contact points, but at higher voltage levels, this can break down quickly with devastating results. If you skin is damp or wet, its resistance will be lower which lowers your outer body resistance. Wet skin significantly lowers your resistance allowing even more current to flow. Ohm’s Law might not be at the top of your thoughts when working on or around vehicles, but its results can kill you if your skin accidently contacts high voltage -Electric Shock – electric shock occurs when a person comes into contact with an electrical power source, and current flows through the body or some portion of the body. Electrical shock may result in no injury at all or may result in severe injury or even death. If the voltage is low, current is very small. Electricity may not be felt at all. As voltage increases, current increases. Depending on your body resistance and the current path through your body, there will be a point that you will feel the effects of being shocked. A slight shock can be a disturbing tingling sensation. The typical response is to release an object when lightly shocked. -High Voltage and Ohm’s Law – so why did we discuss Ohm’s law earlier in this module? Ohm’s Law applies to you if you ever become part of the circuit! Here is why. Ohm’s Law states that for a given voltage, current will flow inversely proportional to the circuit resistance. This means less resistance in a circuit will equal more current. It also holds true that for a given resistance, more voltage will push more current through the circuit. When you get shocked, your body becomes a conductor in the circuit. Your body has some amount of resistance, but it is not an insulator. With low voltage circuits, a small amount of current will flow, and you may not feel much of a shock. At higher voltage levels, more current will flow, and this may cause a severe shock -Electric Shock – Electrocution – large amounts of electricity flowing through our bodies can result in severe injury or death. A significant electrical shock can cause very severe injuries to the heart, brain, nervous system, and burns to tissue. The voltage levels present in the HV systems of electric vehicles is at a high enough level that it can cause severe injury or death by electrocution. -Shocking Examples – a rule to live by: current will flow through all available paths. Do not become a path. Some examples include: -Touching both wires in a powered circuit -Placing your body in parallel with the power source or load with power applied -Placing your body across the only open in an otherwise complete circuit -A technician’s metal tool accidentally comes in contact with an energized part of the circuit, and your body is in contact with the other side of power -Arc Flash Hazard – electrocution isn’t the only hazard when working around high voltage batteries. Today’s high voltage batteries can present another element of danger to the body called arc flash. An arc flash is an electrical explosion. The high temperatures cause rapid heating of the surrounding air and extreme pressures, resulting in an arc blast. The high voltage propulsion batteries store a large amount of energy and present an arc blast and arc flash hazard is shorted. Disconnecting connectors or parts while power is applied (under load) also presents an arc flash hazard. Because of the force of the arc blast explosion, burns, concussions, hearing loss, shrapnel injuries, and eye injury are all common injury. -Risk Management – protecting yourself from electrical shock is a matter of ensuring there are no open, accessible, high voltage sources and no possible paths for current to flow through any part of your body. What not to wear when working around high voltage systems are items that are good conductors. Remove these conductive items before working near a high voltage system. What to wear when working around high voltage (or if there is any doubt about high voltage system integrity) are items that are good insulators and provide protection in case of accidental contact with high voltage or a short circuit. Following the vehicle service procedures and precautions is also a major factor in risk management. -What not to wear – watches, rings, necklaces -What to wear – electrically insulated gloves and boots, face shield to protect against arc flash sparks, flame-resistance clothing to reduce danger from sparks -Lineman gloves – knowing when it is necessary to wear lineman’s gloves is critical. Insulating gloves are the first line of defense, providing protection against shock and electrocution if contact is made with an exposed high voltage circuit or part. Some vehicle markers state that you need to wear your lineman’s gloves anytime you are working around high voltage parts or if you are unsure of the presence of high voltage. These parts may include but are not limited to: -High voltage battery -System main relay -Service plug receptacle and fuse -Orange-colored cables -Inverter assembly -Electric drive motors -Lineman’s Gloves Ratings/Layering – the voltage in some electric vehicles may exceed 800 volts. To provide protection it is recommended to use glove ratings that are class 0. This rating is recommended for EV/HEV work. Class 0 gloves have the following ratings -Maximum AC use voltage of 1,000 volts -AC/proof tested to 5,000 volts -Maximum DC use voltage of 1,500 volts -DC/proof tested to 20,000 volts -Color label – Red -When using lineman’s gloves, put them on in the following order: -1) Cotton liners t absorb moisture on hands -2) High-voltage gloves -3) Leather outer gloves to help protect the HV gloves from cuts or puncture -The leather protectors should not be used for any other purpose. A sliver of metal lodged in the leather glove could damage the linemen’s gloves -Inspection Requirements – safety regulations require that the technician who will use the insulated rubber gloves inspect the gloves for damage before each use. Also inspect them following any incident that may have caused damage. If the inspection finds any of the following, the gloves should be discarded: -Hole, tear, puncture, crack, or cut -Embedded foreign object -Dry rot cracks -Texture changes caused by swelling, softening, hardening, or becoming sticky or inelastic -The gloves should be tested every six months by an official testing facility. Once the gloves are removed from the plastic bag they were shipped in, they start to deteriorate. So, even if they are used only once, they will require retesting in six months -High Voltage PPE – vehicle makers may identify specific high voltage personal protective equipment (PPE) that may include -Outer gear -Arc flash protective face shield -Arc-rated hood -Class 0 lineman’s glove kit -Storage bags -Conclusion – understanding electrical principles and personal protective measures are a couple of the ingredients in working with high voltage systems safely. Complacency may get you hurt or worse. You don’t have to fear working on high voltage systems, you must respect high voltage and take proper precautions. In the next module, we will look at the electric vehicle safety methods and examine a number of different ways that a technician can greatly reduce the risk of electrical shock by following policies and procedures. Understanding High Voltage Safety (2/2) Electrical Vehicle Safety -Is this vehicle safe to work on? – when preparing to inspect or work on any vehicle with HV systems, this should always be your first question. Is this vehicle safe for me to work on? A vehicle that has been in a collision may have unknown damage to high voltage system parts causing a normally safe system to become unsafe. Whenever there is any concern regarding the HV system integrity, it is best to approach the vehicle assuming there are unknown hazards until proven otherwise. Determining the condition and integrity of a vehicle’s HV system should be performed by a technician with the knowledge and training for the specific vehicle. More information on HV system inspections and service is covered in other courses -Safety First – you don’t need to be afraid of electricity, but you do need to respect it by managing the risk of an electric shock. Vehicle high voltage systems are designed with high voltage power management, containment, and safety features. This helps ensure the occupants are safe from shock hazards under normal operating conditions and in most collision scenarios. Many of these features also provide some measure of protection for service technicians conducting routine maintenance unrelated to HV systems. So, as you prepare to work on or around an electric vehicle, follow general safety precautions -Preventing High Voltage Direct Contact – no one intentionally wants to get shocked or electrocuted. It’s always a mistake that causes an electrical shock or worse. High voltage systems have safety systems built into the vehicle. Vehicle makers have specific procedures for working around these systems, that if followed, the HV systems will be powered down and made safe to perform collision and other repairs. Keep in mind, the HV battery will always store high voltage. When you walk up to the vehicle, do you know that the HV system has not been compromised? Is it shut down properly? Is there zero risk? Until you are certain, you need to use the appropriate PPE to avoid high voltage direct contact. PPE is that final safety layer of protection against electrical hazards and accidental direct contact -The I-Car Electric Vehicle – the vehicle, systems, and parts shown are not of a specific vehicle make or model, but rather a generic representation for the purposes of training. These systems and parts are what is typically found in production vehicles with the HV electric systems. Production vehicles have many similar parts and features and have additional modules and parts with different names that make up the HV system. The following screens will explain the main electrical parts and functions of a vehicle’s HV system. It’s important to have an understanding of how the parts and systems function together along with the safety features designed into HV electric vehicles. It’s always required that you have the appropriate training and service information for the vehicle on which you are working. -Low Voltage Electric System – electric vehicles have an on-board low voltage system that is separate or electrically isolated from the HV system. The low voltage system is used for many of the electrical and electronic systems and features that are found on most non-electric vehicles -Low Voltage Electrical System Schematic- the electronic control modules that manage, monitor, and command the HV systems are usually powered by the low voltage system for safety purposes. Low voltage electrical systems are connected to the chassis/body of the vehicle in most vehicles creating a common ground for electrical current in the low voltage systems. The low voltage system has its own unique power distribution path known as a 12 volt buss. The positive or hot side of the low voltage system circuits is isolated and insulated from the chassis and routed to the intended circuits and systems, so it does not short-to-ground or create an unintentional path to another circuit or system. This is accomplished with insulating materials in the wiring, connectors, and part design -High Voltage Isolation Schematic – the HV electrical system must keep the high voltage contained and isolated from the vehicle chassis, other circuits, and unintended destinations and deliver the energy to the HV system parts as managed by the control modules. The HV system has its own unique power distribution path known as an HV buss. HV systems require a high degree of isolation and insulation due to the increased shock/electrocution and arc potential. The current from the HV system should not be going to unintended locations. High voltage does not drip like a fuel leak. There is no smell. It just waits patiently for you to make a mistake and zap, it shocks you. -High Voltage Battery – the HV battery is used to power the electric motors and other HV parts of an electric vehicle. It is made up of a large number of battery cells or packs to provide the energy required to power the vehicle -HV System Internal Schematic – within the HV battery system, there will be a variety of sensors, contactors, and other parts to support its health and function. The HV batteries themselves are insulated from the case and vehicle chassis. The HV battery outer case is electrically connected to the vehicle chassis. The low voltage system supplies the power for the battery management system to monitor and manage the high voltage battery contractors. Contractors are specially designed HV-rated relays that are turned on and off by the low voltage battery management system. When these contractors are open, there is no high voltage coming out of the battery -Contactors Safety Considerations – HV contactors have been known to malfunction in a closed state. It is not an intended design function, or common, but it has happened. When this happens, the high voltage is available to all parts connected to the HV battery buss. HV switching with contactors (HV relays) can experience arcing if opened under load. The power management system strategies will typically open and close the main contactors under low/no load conditions. This may not always be the case. If one of the control modules detects certain system faults, it may open the HV contactors -Electric Drive Motor – electric drive motors convert electrical energy into mechanical energy used to drive the vehicle’s wheels. Electric motors generate near-instant torque or turning force. This means they produce extremely fast acceleration compared to internal combustion engines -Electric Drive Motor Schematic – for improved safety, the electrical windings in the drive motor are insulated from the motor outer case. The case is electrically connected to the vehicle chassis -Electric Vehicle Controller – control modules monitor and manage the HV system integrity, operation, and performance. They communicate over the on-board network to manage the flow of energy within the HV system. Vehicle acceleration, regenerative braking, battery state of charge, thermal management, and many other functions are managed by these control modules. This could be thought of similar to the control modules in non-electric vehicles that manage engine, transmission, and other system operations -Electric Vehicle Controller Schematic – on-board control modules work together to integrate the different system operations. Each of the major system may have their separate control modules that interact with the EV control module. These control modules receive their power from the low voltage system via the 12-volt buss -High Voltage Isolation Monitor Schematic – there are built-in, self-test functions that monitor HV system integrity to detect high voltage leakage, loss of high voltage isolation to the chassis, and other possible faults of the HV system. When an issue is detected by these monitors, it may illuminate warning messages, set DTCs, limit vehicle operations, or disable the HV system completely. The specifics depend on system design and the nature or severity of the fault. -Safety Loop Circuits – HV systems typically include a low-current safety interlock (integrity) loop circuit. These safety loop circuits tie in with HV connectors, harnesses, and other system parts. If a fault is detected withing the safety loop circuit, the main contractors are opened removing power from the HV buss. If the contractors are already open, they are not allowed to close. This is usually a series circuit of linked connection points. If any connection opens up, it is detected by the monitoring control module. -Safety Disconnects – HV battery safety disconnects are designed to disable the HV battery prior to performing electric vehicle service. These are manually disconnected by a technician. The safety disconnects are plastic that do not conduct electricity. However, there are conductors in the disconnect. What if there is a small crack in the plastic and a small amount of moisture is present? Do you want to find out with your bare hand? Is that a risk you want to take? Electric vehicles may also have a special pyrotechnic safety disconnect. This is designed to open the HV circuit under certain conditions such as an airbag deployment. -Safety Disconnect Schematics – the HV battery safety disconnects is usually placed in series with the HV battery cells at the mid-point in the cell arrangement. When removed, no current can flow. It may also tie into the low current interlock loop safety system. When the safety disconnect is removed, there may be residual high voltage. This residual high voltage may remain in the system parts until it bleeds off via internal components designed for this purpose -Floating System Design – the high voltage system is considered a floating design. This means both the positive and the negative side of the power source and all interconnected parts of the HV systems are isolated from the chassis and body of the vehicle. The HV system parts are attached to the chassis, but the high voltage is well insulated within the case, wiring, connectors, and system parts -Electrical Bonding Schematic – electrical bonding is the practice of connecting all exposed metal items not designed to carry electricity to a common ground, although not ground for the HV system. This is done in an effort to mitigate electric shock, should there be an internal insulation failure in any HV module or part. The cases and housings of the HV parts are connected to the vehicle chassis ground. There may also be redundant ground (bonding) straps. This is an important part of HV system safety design. Vehicle makers have to conform to certain standards for HV isolation and safety. Robust engineering has gone into modern EVs to ensure these requirements are met. In the following screens, lets look at a few scenarios where HV system faults could potentially lead to electrical safety faults. -Electrical Bonding Scenario 1 – in this scenario the high voltage has a short to the chassis. With the floating HV system, if only one of the high voltage lines accidently connected with the chassis, there would only be a shock hazard if someone touched an exposed point on the other HV line. The HV monitor system should detect this fault -Electrical Bonding Scenario 2 – in this scenario, if both HV lines shorted to the chassis, the master fuse would open. This is due to the electrical bonding to the chassis of the vehicle, providing a direct short of the high voltage buss. Electrical bonding provides an uninterrupted path through the chassis, causing excessive current flow which will blow the fuse, disabling the high voltage circuit. The HV safety systems should prevent an electrical shock hazard as long as electrical bonding integrity is intact -Electrical Bonding Scenario 3 – if a fault occurred within module 1 and the electrical bonding to the chassis was not intact, the module case would then be at the potential of the high voltage line to which it was connected. If someone touches the module, there should not be a shock hazard, like a bird on a wire. In this scenario, there is another fault shorting the HV to the chassis as shown in module 2. The case of module 2 becomes the potential of the HV buss. With module 1 having a fault and the person touching module 1 and any metal part on the vehicle, the consequences could be deadly. While this is not likely to happen, is it a risk you are willing to take. -HV-Trained Service Technicians – vehicle makers have established polices and procedures that dictate only trained electrical HV service technicians can perform specific tasks. These tasks may include: -Performing inspections -Determining HV system integrity -Disabling HV systems -Servicing HV systems -Procedures typically require the use of visual safety indicators, and other measures are in place. The HV-trained service technician documents that the vehicle is safe for the collision repair technicians to work on the vehicle. Understanding Vehicle Communications Networks (1/2) Module 1 – Understanding Vehicle Communication Networks -Overview – a vehicle communication network manages information transfer between systems and modules to provide the functionality of different electronic systems on modern vehicles. Understanding the vehicle communication network is an important first step for improving the technicians diagnostic skill set -Early Electrical Systems – vehicle electrical systems have evolved throughout the history of the automobile. Early electrical systems were very basic and may have a generator, battery, fused links, wires, switches, relays, and load devices such as light bulbs or motors. In a lighting circuit, when the switch was turned to the ON position, the contacts on the circuit closed and current flowered to the lights connected to that switch -The Introduction of Electronic Control Modules – eventually electronic modules started to appear in vehicles, controlling the engine and eventually the transmission, climate control, anti-lock brakes (ABS), supplemental restraint systems (SRS), lighting, and more. Electronic control modules may be referred to as an electronic control unit (ECU) or simply as a vehicle computer -Electronic Control – with electronic control, when the driver turns the light switch to the ON position, the switch is an input device to a control module. The module then carries out the request to activate the lights, usually by energizing a relay that completes the circuit, providing power to the lights. Electronic control modules are computers dedicated to a function or system. Electronic control modules monitor sensors, switches, and data, possibly from other modules. They perform the required function based on the programming of the module. -Powertrain Control Module Example – think of each module like a building with an entrance door and an exit door. The entrance door provides inputs, and the exit door provides the requested or programmed outputs. For example, the powertrain control module (PCM) monitors different inputs, processes the information, and controls outputs to obtain desired engine performance -Multiple Inputs – on a vehicle equipped with an internal combustion engine, the PCM monitors various input sensors to control the engine management system. Commonly used input sensors monitor: -Oxygen -Engine coolant temperature -Mass airflow -Manifold absolute pressure -Throttle position -PCM Processing – the PCM monitors the input sensors and can make changes to the outputs based on changing conditions. It contains an electronically erasable, programmable, read-only memory (EEPROM). The EEPROM contains operating parameter information, as well as programming, to control engine operation. The PCM also communicates with other systems and performs diagnostic testing on itself and the various system sensors and parts. -Outputs – the PCM controls various output devices to obtain proper operation of the engine management system. The various outputs can include: -Ignition coils -Fuel injectors -Fuel pump relays -Variable valve timing -Throttle control -Other powertrain parameters -Control Module Communication – -There are a variety of sensors and devices that provide input to computers, including: -Variable resistors. These may be air and fluid temperatures, pressure, or position -Voltage or frequency generating sensors -Switches -Acceleration sensors -Rotary optical position sensors -Yaw rate sensors -Others depending on the equipped vehicle systems and options -The computer relies on the accuracy of the information provided by the input devices to control the output devices. For example, if the coolant temperature sensor is not providing accurate information, the engine may overheat due to the computers failure to activate the radiator cooling fan -Module Power, Ground, and Reference Voltage – for reliable operation, computers require a stable supply of voltage and good ground connections. Some computers have an internal voltage regulator that provides a precise reference voltage to its sensors that give input information. The battery and charging system must function as designed for any computerized system to operate properly and have a consistent and accurate reference voltage. Check supply voltage, grounds, and verify reference voltage is within specifications -Networked Control Modules – with the increased number and complexity of vehicle systems, the amount of wiring needed increases. This impacts the weight of the vehicle. Modern networking reduces the amount of wiring necessary as well as the number of isolated systems. By reducing the number of individually isolated systems, diagnostic communications are streamlined. Computer modules and the systems they support are all connected so they can talk to each other and share data -Control Module Priority- one challenge for electronic system engineers was to devise a way to manage communication traffic among all the connected computer modules. They also had to program the network so that information or operation of certain systems and modules had a higher priority. Communication protocols and specifications were set up for network signal levels, validation, and arbitration methods. These communication standards simplified the process for modules to communicate and understand message priorities across the network -CAN Bus Communication System – networking, or connecting modules together, is accomplished using two wires twisted together to reduce outside electromagnetic interference. Serial data is created. This data contains an electronic message formatted with identification and validation information at the beginning, embedded data in the middle, and followed by an end of message flag. The two-wire network sends data so other modules that need this information can receive it. This controller area network is often referred to as the CAN bus. Just like a city bus delivers passengers, the CAN bus delivers data from one place to another. A typical CAN communication system may incorporate a low-speed and high-speed bus network -Multiplexing – -Multiplexing refers to multiple computers and modules sharing a communication bus. Modern vehicles typically have multiple networks and related buses that focus on different systems of a vehicle. Examples of different CANs may include: -Powertrain -Convenience systems -Infotainment -Safety -Some vehicles may have a media-oriented systems transport (MOST) bus that supports data rates needed for multimedia operations, such as audio, video, voice, and data signals. Another communications bus that can support higher data speeds is FlexRay -Local Interconnect Network – a local interconnect network (LIN) may be used in a hierarchy of network configurations, where LIN-based networks serve as subnetworks to a primary CAN. Working along with the CAN, the LIN-based networks may then reduce the load on the CAN. The LIN is a lower-speed protocol used to provide local communications within a functionality group. For example, the network that communicates occupant classification system (OCS) module data to the restraints control module (RCM) may use a LIN -Gateway Modules – networks may have certain modules in charge as domain masters. These domain masters oversee the operations within a particular area, such as powertrain, infotainment, safety, and advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS). Other modules connected to the domain master are referred to as nodes. Gateway modules may be used to manage communications and the diagnostic interface. Regardless of the system, network communication relies on the fundamental electrical principles of voltage, resistance, and current. If there is damage to the CAN harness or connectors, communication may be limited to the electronic control units that remain connected and functioning on the network -The Network Information Highway – on newer vehicles there will be more than one CAN bus. The network wiring harness is made up of two wires that represent the CAN high and CAN low signals. The wires are twisted to minimize interference from other electronic signals. To reduce noise and minimize signal ringing or echoes on the CAN bus, there are two resistors that may be referred to as termination resistors. They are most commonly 120 ohms and placed across CAN high and CAN low at the ends of the bus. Two 120-ohn resistors are in parallel, and the total network resistance is 60 ohms. Knowing this value is useful when troubleshooting the bus. These resistors are often inside a module. -The Network Sensors – along the span of the CAN bus are various sensor, system modules, and controllers. Sensors provide input for the controller modules. System modules consist of input, output, or power modules that are controller by controller modules. Two node lines connect the ECMs to the CAN bus harness -Types of CAN Bus Networks -Low-Speed – a low speed CAN bus communication network communicates on an average of 33.33 kilobits per second (kbps) -Mid-Speed – a mid-speed CAN bus communication network is a dual-wire network that communicates at approximately 95.2 kbps. This network is commonly used for entertainment systems, such as the radio, and transmits large quantities of data in a short period of time -High-Speed – a high speed CAN bus communication network is a dual-wire network that communicates at approximately 500 kbps. This network is used for real-time data for safety, powertrain, and chassis systems -Flexible Data Rate – CAN flexible data-rate (FD) is used on some vehicle networks to meet the needs of increased data transfer rates. The CAN FD can transfer data up to five times faster. Using CAN FD, the ECUs can switch to different data rates to send larger or smaller messages. With CAN FD you can use the 11-bit identifier like Classical CAN, or the 29-bit identifier of the FD extended frame format (FEFF). Classical CAN was limiting activities with its 1 megabit per second (Mbps) transfer rate. Faster communication is needed for ECU flashing, ADAS, electric vehicle (EV), infotainment, and head-up display (HUD) applications. CAN FD can support rates higher than 8 mbps as well as payloads up to 64 bits of data as compared to 8 bits in CAN -Private CAN – some vehicle systems have a very high demand for certain modules or sensors. When a system needs to have fast, uninterrupted communication with a module or sensor, the vehicle may have a private CAN. This allows some modules to have real-time updates to deliver information more accurately either to the driver or to other parts of the vehicle to take action if the driver does not. This can commonly be found on ADAS and may also be used on other systems -Interface Bus – the interface bus is the two-wire twisted pair that connects all the control modules when there is more than one data bus. The interface bus has two 120-ohm resistors and a total resistance of 60 ohms. If you encounter a situation where multiple controller modules are not communication, then there is likely damage to the interface bus -Parallel CAN Buses – if there are only two CAN buses, they can run in parallel with the use of two control modules. The network diagram displayed shows both high-speed (HS-CAN) and mid-speed (MS-CAN) buses Understanding Vehicle Communications Networks (2/2) Module 2 – What’s in a Code -System Codes – DTCs are used to notify technicians about system faults. Each DTC corresponds to a fault detected in the vehicle. When a current or history fault is detected, a corresponding trouble code is activated. A vehicle stores the trouble code in its memory when it detects a part or system that is operating outside of acceptable limits -Different Code Types – active codes identify current problems with the vehicle. Pending codes occur when a potential issue has been identified in the system and may require several occurrences before it goes active. Codes may be stored in a vehicle computer as history codes. A history code is a DTC from a fault that was detected but is currently functioning a fault. History codes may be retained by the computer for access. These codes may show a failure history of a part of a vehicle system and may indicate issues relevant to the current repair -Diagnostic Trouble Codes Overview – even though DTC standardization has improved, frequently technicians must research codes to verify their meaning. While some codes have common formats and are shared among several vehicle makers, many other codes are specific to the vehicle maker. Never assume a code from one vehicle is the same on another vehicle -Diagnostic Trouble Codes Explained- -On-board diagnostic-II (OBD-II) mandates standardized DTCs. All DTCs consist of one letter and a four-digit number. The letter signifies the system that contains the fault: -B-body -C-chassis -P-powertrain -U-communications -The number associations are: -First digit – generic or manufacturer-specific DTC -Second digit – vehicle system setting the fault -Third and fourth digits – system part or section containing the fault -Diagnostic Trouble Code Example – for example, a P0300 code is a powertrain system fault that indicates an engine misfire that is not related to only one cylinder. If the last digit was a number other than 0, it would correspond to the cylinder number that is misfiring. If the first digit was a 1, this would be an OEM code -Where to Find Code Identifiers – service information from the vehicle maker is the most reliable resource for identifying DTCs. Internet search engines can also be useful for finding code identifiers and their descriptions. Many OEM-specific codes have more than six digits. These additional digits may identify specific failure types or other information the vehicle maker may include -Freeze Frame Data – freeze frame data that captures relevant data at the time the code was set may be available. Additional information may identify key cycles since the code set, mileage, or time and date information. Module replacement may be required due to the way some collision-related fault codes are stored and then retained in the computer memory. They cannot be erased. This is usually associated with SRS control modules -Network Communication – vehicle network communications are standardized to allow electronic devices to readily communicate with one another. Unlike some office or computer networks, standards in vehicle design ensure communication remains continuous, even if parts of the network fail. Vehicle diagnostic connectors are also standardized so that scan tools can support many vehicle brands. Security measures are being implemented to address activities or attacks and may require specific interface tooling to access the network or protect some functions of the vehicle -Common Standards – in terms of automotive networks, there are two key organizations that assist with the development of common standards. Engineers in the automotive industry design networks using standards from both the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE). OEM data sheets commonly list these ISO and SAE codes. However, they have no relevance for the technician – these are simply protocols or standards used by engineers who develop the broadcast messages sent over the data bus. -Vehicle Diagnostic Connector – a standardized data link connector (DLC) is required on all OBD-II vehicles. The scan tool uses this port to allow an interface for viewing codes, live data, programming, parameters, and more. This connector contains 16 terminals and must be located under the dash on the driver side of the passenger compartment. The CAN bus always uses pins 6 and 14. When inspecting a vehicle, check to ensure this connector is in proper location and in good condition. Some communication bus protocols may use other pins on the DLC -Reviewing the Scan Report -when possible, pre-scan a collision-damaged vehicle using a scan tool. This initial check can often provide a variety of DTCs on the scan report. While this list can be very intimidating, technicians are able to quickly evaluate faults by reviewing the initial DTCs. Then they can prioritize among the most common areas of concern. Communication networks often produce redundant codes. In other words, one or more of the codes listed on a scan report may point to the same diagnostic concern. A careful visual inspection of collision-damaged areas of the vehicle may lead to a common root cause -Interpreting Multiple Codes – damage to electrical wiring can cause multiple codes. For example, if a CAN HI wire is cut, multiple DTCs may be set. When performing a scan, it may appear that the fuel level sensor (Circuit A) has a fault, the fuel level sensor (Circuit B) has a fault, and there is a problem with multiple vehicle systems. It is unlikely that these circuits and modules are all faulty, so the next step is to determine what these codes have in common. In this example, the technician researched the problem. Through careful visual inspection, the common factor was damage to the CAN harness. If a broken wire is located, reference the vehicle-maker repair procedures to make the repair -Understand the Systems – if there is a fault detected in one system, it is possible that there will be additional codes set in other systems. Due to multiple DTCs being set, even if there is a single problem, a technician may think there is more than one issue. If there are multiple modules reporting U codes for no communication or invalid data, look to see if they are indicating a common module in which the problem may reside. That system may be the root cause Using Vehicle Maker Repair Procedures (1/2) Module 1 – Repair Information Availability -Introduction to OEM Repair Procedures – with all the new technology built into todays vehicles, collision damage estimating and repair have become more challenging. Because it is important to provide complete, accurate, and safe repairs, estimators and repair technicians need to know how to access and use the appropriate OEM service information. Knowing how to locate and effectively use service information makes the damage estimating and repair processes as efficient as possible -Importance of Using OEM Repair Procedures – vehicle service information sources provide information such as diagnostic and repair procedures necessary to achieve complete and accurate repairs. When analyzing damage, the appropriate service information helps to determine the extent of damage. This helps identify if the vehicle can be safely and economically repaired -Repair Information – repair information includes details for damage analysis, repairs, and parts replacement. The information provides recommended removal and attachment methods and locations: -This may also include: -Mechanical repairs or parts replacement -Electrical repairs or parts replacement -Wiring diagrams -Parts and systems diagnostics -Repair information provides: -Personal safety precautions and recommended safety equipment -Vehicle protection guidelines -Considerations for refinishing -Handling and storage of hazardous materials -Determine Requirements – access service information at the time of the estimate -Before blueprinting -Before removing parts -Before beginning repairs -Throughout the blueprinting and repair processes -Determine which service information is required to complete a repair. Be aware that OEMs may provide position statements for certain repair procedures. However, position statements do not take the place of service information for making the repairs. Vehicles are constructed from various types of materials, including steel, aluminum, and plastic. Determine the type of material used for a specific part by accessing the appropriate service information. The service information may also provide the various attachment methods used for the original construction and repairs of these vehicle -Vehicle-Specific Service Information – access the service information for the make, model, and year of the vehicle. There may be slight design variations for different model years even though body styles are not different. Always refer to the service information for the year of the vehicle in question. The service information provides the specific procedure and parts required and helps develop a repair plan -Access Appropriate Sources – determine whether accessing the information electronically or using printed material would be more convenient. Some vehicle makers may also offer CD-ROMs. In situations where there is no computer access in the work area, print the service information to have a copy at the workstation. Some electronic service information may also be downloadable for access at a later time. Printed copies or downloaded files of electronic service information can be cataloged for future use or reference. File it with the repair order. before referencing any previously printed material, downloaded material, or CD-ROMs, verify that it is still current -Web-Based Sources – there are various online sources available for accessing vehicle service information. Access to some vehicle maker websites may require using a specific operating system and web browser. For example, some websites work with the Windows operating system only and require the Internet Explorer web browser. Some websites are available free of charge and some are fee-based. Fee-based websites may offer several subscription options. The subscriptions are for a set time period, such as 24 or 72 hours, 31 days, or one year. Subscription rates vary depending on the vehicle maker -Navigating a Website – navigation for each vehicle maker website varies. Some vehicle makers provide tutorials to show how to effectively navigate their website. There may be options for selecting service information in different languages. Along with English, information may be available in other languages, such as French or Spanish. A vehicle maker service and repair website may be a separate site from the collision repair website. For example, Fiat Chrysler Automobiles (FCA) has a website that offers vehicle-specific collision repair information. This free website provides technical collision repair information for a number of late model FCA vehicles. FCA also has a more comprehensive fee-based website that include all the mechanical and body repair information for each make and model vehicle -Technical Service Bulletins – vehicle makers issue technical service bulletins (TSBs) to provide new or updated service information. A TSB is issued when there are multiple occurrences of a problem on a vehicle make or a model. TSBs are not recalls. TSBs may be called by a different name, such as collision repair information bulletin or service advisory. Access TSBs on the vehicle makers service information website. Some TSBs are also published in vehicle maker newsletters, such as Ford’s On Target, and Toyota’s Collision Pros -Special Publications – special publications are available. Some subjects include plastic repair, weld bonding, and paint materials. Emergency response guidelines may provide service information regarding restraint systems and hybrid vehicle systems; however, they do not replace service information. These publications help make rescue situations safer for emergency response personnel. Download these guides from the vehicle maker website. A vehicle owners manual can be another source of technical service information. Depending on the vehicle maker, owners manuals provide information such as: -System functions -Parts locations with illustrations -Fluid capacities -Replacement bulb sizes -Other Websites – there are other websites that may be helpful for finding collision repair information. Two examples are: -ALLDATA -Mitchell -The ALLDATA and Mitchell websites provide detailed diagnostics and repair information sourced from vehicle makers. A subscription is required to gain access to the repair information. With ALLDATA, although the information is copyrighted by the vehicle maker, they have an agreement to distribute it. ALLDATA information contains the actual OEM articles and diagrams with no editorial or content changes. ALLDATA changes the format only into one standardized layout or consistent navigating. For information on how to use ALLDATA, check out the I-CAR Collision Repair News article: Step-By-Step: Accessing ALLDATA Collision Information -OEM Service Information Websites – another information source is the National Automotive Service Task Force (NASTF) website. This website provides information and links to OEM repair websites, including a subscription price list -I-CAR Website and RTS Portal – the I-CAR website is a good source for accessing information and training opportunities that support vehicle maker service procedures. The I-CAR website include a path to I-CAR Repairability Technical Support (RTS) which is found under the Technical Knowledge tab. The RTS Portal is subscription-based, or access is free when a shop or individual participates at a certain level of I-CAR training. With access to the I-CAR RTS Portal, technicians, appraisers, owners, and shop managers gain a research department for the information that is hard to find or needs clarification. -RTS Ask I-CAR and Linking Pin Activity – through the Ask I-CAR feature, the Technical Support team takes questions about specific collision repair information. While the Technical Support team cannot send out vehicle maker repair procedures, due to copyright laws, they can verify if the information exists. In response to industry feedback, the RTS website include a video demo with most OEM service information links showing how to navigate that website. RTS subscribers also gain a path to the vehicle makers through the OEM Linking Pin Activity. That means that if the Technical Support team cant find the information requested, they will contact the vehicle maker directly -I-CAR Training – I-CAR training courses available for purchase are a valuable reference to vehicle maker service procedures. I-CAR training courses are designed for use in conjunction with vehicle maker service information. Proper training is helpful for understanding service information. Depending on the course, I-CAR training is available: -Online -Line in class (welding, training and certification and hands-on skills development) -Virtual Test 1-All of these are true about accessing an OEM repair procedure EXCEPT it: is started after the blueprinting process 2-Before using previously printed service information-verify that the information is current 3-A vehicle makers position statement can be used in place of service information for making certain repairs – false 4-Vehicle makers issue technical service bulletins to provide new service information-true 5-All of these statements are true about requesting information from the I-CAR RTS Technical Support team EXCEPT – the RTS team will verify if the requested information exists – X wrong Using Vehicle Maker Repair Procedures (2/2) Module 2 -Using an OEM Repair Procedure -Repairs – vehicle makers provide the necessary information required for a specific repair. Repair information may include items such as the recommended removal and installation steps and attachment methods. Service information may also include information for repairing a particular type of material such as steel, aluminum, or plastic. GM and FCA are examples of vehicle makers that provide recommended procedures for repairing plastic parts -Refinishing – OEM repair websites may provide refinishing information. Accessing the information may help determine approved refinish materials for a repair. These materials may be included in the repair information or available as a separate manual. Paint code label locations are also identified -Parts Replacement – part replacement information can be found in various sources. Some parts catalogs are available in printed manuals and some vehicle maker service information websites provide online parts catalogs. Service information may show illustrations of replacement parts along with part numbers for ordering specific parts. Some parts allow replacement of the inner or outer structure and others require replacement of the complete assembly. Accessing the service information. -Service Information Terms – depending on the vehicle maker, terms used to identify service procedures, parts, products, or equipment can vary. It is important for estimators and technicians to be aware that differences in terms exist. Understanding terms used in specific service information sources can help with locating and using the information. Strut tower assemblies, which include the apron panel, may also be called wheelhouse panels. Rocker panels are also called side sill panels or simply sills. If available, refer to the specific vehicle maker service information glossary for definitions of acronyms and terms -Personal Safety and Vehicle Protection – service information sources typically include cautions, warnings, and notices, in various locations. These are presented to reduce the risk of personal injury or further damage to the vehicle. It is important to read and understand all personal safety and vehicle protection information -Special Tools and Materials – service information may provide information about special tools, products, and equipment that will be required to complete a repair. Instructions for using tools and materials may also be provided. This information may show or list specific locations of material types. Repair procedure pages may include links to material usage charts. These charts may include material types and strengths, or the repairability of certain parts -Measurement Information – service information may include point-to-point measurements such as underbody, under hood, and side aperture opening dimensions. Frame measuring systems provide three-dimensional frame and body measurements for most vehicles. Estimating and measuring systems can also identify point-to-point measurements -Sectioning Information – for recommended sectioning procedures, refer to the vehicle-specific service information. Sectioning locations may be indicated with dotted lines in a general area of a part or a specific location. Some procedures include measurements -Fastener Information – most vehicle maker service information sources provide torque specifications for installing parts. Torque specification units provided in the service information can vary. Specifications may be provided in metric and standard units. A vehicle maker may designate some fasteners one-time-use, and this is included in the service information -Parts Information – use service information to determine how parts are serviced and types of replacement parts. Some service information sources give exploded view illustrations that can help establish if a part is available separately or if it is only available as an assembly. Individual or diagram may be linked to more detailed information sources -Recommended Steps – vehicle maker service information typically provides the specified steps to follow for body, collision, or mechanical repairs. Photos and illustrations are used to help identify parts or locations applicable to the procedure. Icons identify specific tools, products, or procedures that apply. Service information may have links or threads to new pages or screens that provide more detailed information or additional steps for a procedure. Follow all links or threads to ensure access to the complete information -Repair Procedure Information – some repair procedures may only be available with the replacement parts.
