Additionally, be aware that collision repair information may or may not be included in the body repair information section found in service manuals. Body repair focuses on removal and installation of bolt-on parts such as trim, exterior panels, bumpers, and restraints. Collision repair information typically shows how to repair, section, or straighten outer body panels and the body structure. This information may be in a separate section -Special Product Requirements – service information may indicate when special products are required for a repair along with special instructions for applications. These include products such as: -Seam sealers -Adhesives -Paint materials -Foams -Corrosion Protection -Generally, following the product makers instructions. Service information identifies original applications and locations -Special Tool Requirements – service information indicates when specific tools, or equipment are required to properly repair certain parts or systems. These may include: -Electronic parts alignment -Calibration tools and equipment -Frame repair equipment -Riveting tools -Systems such as a blind spot detection or other advanced driver assistance systems may require calibration when damaged parts or attachments are repaired or replaced. Some of these calibration procedures require the use of special tools. OEMs require using vehicle-specific scan tools -Flowcharts – performing some procedures may include following a flowchart. Flowcharts are written for mechanical problems. Flowcharts contain questions and statements designed to guide the technician through a systematic troubleshooting sequence. These include specific tasks such as electrical tests or yes and no questions to make decisions based on information gained. Reading through the complete flowchart before proceeding helps avoid unnecessary steps in the process -Electronic Service Information – electronic service information format varies. Most provide a drop-down content list or menu for navigating to specific information. Navigating vehicle maker websites can typically be done by selecting the vehicle make, model, and year. Then select the desired category of information or a specific procedure. The service information where specific procedures are displayed may link to related information. Other websites may use keywords for content searches, symbols and icons, a help option, and using the vehicle identification number (VIN). Most vehicle maker service information websites will offer a help option that can be used to get tips for navigating the site. Depending on the website, there may be an e-mail contact field that can be used to send questions or comments for specific assistance needs -Symbols and Icons – it is important to understand what the symbols and icons used in the service information represent. For example, some symbols may be used for the weld method or type of weld required Test 1-service information may show illustrations with part numbers for ordering replacement parts – true 2-vehicle makers use the same standard terms to identify service procedures, parts, products, or equipment – false 3-Torque specifications may be provided in metric and which of these units – universal – X wrong 4-vehicle maker service information typically provides the specified steps to follow our body, collision, or mechanical repairs – true 5-a flowchart may include yes and no questions to guide through a systematic troubleshooting sequence-true Vehicle Check-In Process Vehicle intake -Receiving the Vehicle – the vehicle check-in process is one of the first steps in the repair process. Vehicles may arrive at a repair shop by: -Tow-in -Insurance/DRP assignment -Walk-in -Note: Regardless of how the vehicle arrives at the repair shop, the customer’s first impression could influence their feelings about the entire claim experience. When making initial customer contact, either over the phone for tow-in or face-to-face for drivable DRP and walk-in customers, it is important to show empathy and remember that the customer has gone through a traumatic experience. Ensure the vehicle keys have been collected, including any keys for theft-deterrent devices. -Tow-In – when a vehicle arrives as a tow-in, it will be necessary to contact the vehicle owner to: -Obtain any missing customer ID information -Obtain details about how the damage occurred -Obtain insurance company (payer) information -Explain the blueprinting process -Obtain the necessary repair authorization -Set customer expectations -Note: In some cases, towed-in vehicles have been pre-arranged by the insurance company or vehicle owner, and towing expenses may have been pre-paid. However, in many cases, towing expenses are not pre-paid, or the shop has made towing arrangements, and a towing invoice will need to be paid. In some cases, the repair facility may have billing arrangements with a preferred towing vendor that allows for towing invoices to be signed and then processed like other payables. -Insurance/DRP Assignment – a vehicle may arrive at the repair shop as a result of a Direct Repair Program (DRP) assignment. This means the customer has been in contact with their insurance company and chosen a specific repair shop to inspect and provide an estimate of vehicle damages or to drop the vehicle off for repairs. In either case, it will be necessary to obtain or confirm all customer contact information, insurance company contact information, vehicle identification number, and facts of loss from the customer. Even if the customer was in for an estimate before dropping their vehicle off for repairs, it is a good practice to review the customer and insurance company information with them. This ensures nothing has changed since the original estimate was written. Repairs should be scheduled depending on the available production shop capacity and insurance partner guidelines. -Walk-In – walk-in customers are those customers that arrive at the repair shop without a scheduled estimate or repair appointment. In these cases, the customer interaction will be very similar to a DRP customer, who arrives without a written estimate. It will be necessary to obtain or confirm all customer and insurance company contact information, vehicle identification information, and facts of loss from the customer. It may also be necessary to schedule the customer to return for their estimate or repairs based on estimator and repair shop capacity. -Initial Vehicle Inspection and Walk-Around – the vehicle walk-around is used to establish a relationship and build trust with your customer. It is also used to gather facts about the collision, get an overview of primary damages, and look for signs of secondary damages. -Reviewing the Facts of Loss – while completing your walk-around with the customer, it is important to obtain information about the facts of the loss to gain an understanding of impact the vehicle sustained during the collision. During the walk-around, ask the customer about: -The impact source -The speed of the impact -The road conditions at the time of the collision -Brake usage during the condition -Unusual noises such as clunking, rubbing, or rattling -Unusual mechanical issues such as vibrations, pulling, or drivability issues -Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) that do not appear to be functioning properly such as park assist, lane keep assist, or head up display -Note: Understanding the details of the collision aids in evaluating the potential for energy transfer through the vehicle. There will be a difference in the amount of energy transferred when hitting another vehicle vs. a stationary object such as a tree -Confirming Vehicle Damage – another part of the vehicle walk-around with the customer includes the identification of any unrelated, prior damage to the vehicle. This includes prior collision damage, parking lot dings, etc. This is an important piece of the walk-around process, as identifying these types of damage prior to starting repairs will help to ensure all parties are on the same page with regard to repairs to be completed. This also provides an opportunity to sell the customer your services for any additional unrelated damages. In many cases, it is more cost effective for the customer to complete these unrelated damage repairs while completing the claim-related repairs due to overlapping operations. -Vehicle Information – during the initial inspection, ensure that the necessary vehicle information is collected. This will provide confirmation that the correct vehicle is being inspected. Documentation of the vehicle’s trim and options will allow for accurate parts selection when preparing the repair estimate. When identifying a vehicle, record the: -Vehicle identification number (VIN) -Year/make/model/production date of the vehicle -Current mileage -Body style/color/trim codes -Vehicle options -License number -Drivetrain information – this includes identifying the engine type and size, and the transmission type. -Conduct Customer Consultation – after the vehicle walk-around is completed, the details of the initial inspection and conversation with the customer should be documented and reviewed. This helps ensure the customer understands what was discussed and is in agreement with all the information that was covered. -Related vs. Unrelated Damage – during your customer consultation following the walk-around, it is important to review all of the unrelated damages to ensure there is no confusion about which damages are being repaired. Also, document the vehicle condition at the time of check-in. this ensures there is no confusion or frustration for the customer when the vehicle is ready for delivery. Note: For tow-in vehicles, the vehicle inspection and walk around can be done without the customer. However, the customer should be contacted following your walk-around to conduct your customer consultation and discuss the results. -Payment Responsibilities – it is also important to review the claim details and confirm the parties responsible for payment. For example, if the vehicle owner is the insured party, there will likely be a deductible the customer will be responsible for, along with payment for any unrelated damages upsold to the customer. Having this discussion with the customer prior to starting any repairs helps to eliminate the possibility of misunderstanding and frustration after repairs have been completed -Communication Preferences – during the conversation with the customer, it is always a good idea to review the facilities status update process with the customer. This may include confirming the customers preference for frequency of status updates, and confirming the preferred method of contact such as phone, text, email, etc. Note: At this time, it is also a good practice to encourage the removal of personal items from the vehicle -Reviewing the Next Steps – before concluding the consultation with the customer, review the next step in the repair process, which is the blueprinting process. Inform the customer that they should expect a call when blueprinting is complete to discuss the damages identified, necessary repairs, and target completion date. Review the blueprinting process with the customer so they understand that this is the first stage of the production shop repair process. In this stage, the vehicle is completely disassembled in the area(s) of damage, including any necessary trim removal for the refinish process. Disassembly helps ensure all of the collision damage is identified, resulting in a more efficient repair process. -Coordinate Customer Rental – speak with the vehicle owner to determine if a rental vehicle will be required during the repair process. If a rental is required, determine who will be responsible for payment and coordinate with a preferred vendor for a rental vehicle to be available for the customer. -Customer Comfort Items – the customer may be required to spend time waiting for their scheduled appointment or waiting for a rental car. It is a good business practice to have a comfortable waiting area. Some best practices for customer waiting areas include: -Being clean and professional -Having adequate seating -Having a clean restroom that is separate from the restroom used by employees -Having basic refreshments such as coffee, water, etc. -Documenting Customer Contact – it is important to note any time there is any communication with the vehicle owner, including notes from your walk-around, customer consultation, and any other points of contact or communication throughout the entire repair process. Communication notes should include: -Date and time the communication took place -Purpose of the communication -Name of the person who received the communication on the part of the customer. -Communication type (phone, text, email) including the phone number or email address used. -Note: Detailed communication records help to eliminate any confusion about what was discussed, when it was discussed, and actions agreed upon. Inspecting the Vehicle -Completing the Vehicle Intake Process – when finalizing the vehicle intake process, there are a few steps that should be taken to prepare the vehicle for the repair process -Inspect for Biohazards – it is important to inspect all vehicles for biohazards before getting into the vehicle or starting the damage analysis/blueprinting process. Inspect the vehicle for common biohazards including bool, needles, and mold. Follow the proper safety procedures for handling any biohazard. -Determine Drivability – before attempting to drive a vehicle that has been checked-in for repairs, look closely to identify any damages to safety and mechanical systems. Damage to these systems may make the vehicle unsafe to drive in the parking lot or into the shop, Some examples of items that, if damaged, could make the vehicle unsafe to drive includes: -Deployed airbags -Brake or steering system damage, which could result in loss of control -Cut or exposed wires, which could result in a short -Note: If damages are identified that could make the vehicle unsafe to drive, it is recommended the vehicle be pushed or towed into the repair shop. -Photo Documentation – before moving the vehicle into the blueprint department and beginning any disassembly, it is important to ensure all necessary pre-repair photos have been obtained. Some of the required photos may include: -Four corner photos, showing the license plate on the rear corners -Damage height measurement photos -Closeup photos of the damages -Interior photos, instrument panel photos, showing radio/GPS options -Driver door panel photos, showing power options -Headliner/sunroof photos -Engine compartment photos -Tread depth measurement photos, showing all tires -Prior damage photos -In-Shop Vehicle Identification – before staging the vehicle for the blueprinting process, it is a good practice to mark the vehicle using the shop standard operating procedures (SOPs) for vehicle identification. While in the shop, vehicles may be identified by key and mirror tags, on-vehicle mapping, or colored hats, among others. -Key/Mirror Tags and On-Vehicle Mapping – key/mirror tags and on-vehicle mapping are the most common forms of vehicle identification used in most collision repair shops. This requires simply attaching a tag to the vehicle keys and rearview mirror with a number that corresponds with the work order number. Then, mark the designated glass with additional identifying marks based on the shop SOPs. -Colored Hats – hats are another form of identification used in some shops. These hats are typically color-coded and used to provide a simple visual indicator for many different things. For example, the colored hats may include: -A stage in the repair process -A vehicle is waiting for parts -A vehicle is ready for production -A vehicle is a quick hit or fast lane repair -The insurance partner -The production team -Repair Authorization and Direction of Payment – remember to obtain any necessary signatures from the vehicle owner authorizing the repair shop to complete repairs. In some cases, shops may require a direction of payment form to be signed. This form authorizes the insurance company to pay the shop directly for services rendered. Always refer to your shop SOPs to identify procedures regarding obtaining repair authorization and direction of payment. Vehicle Construction Material Types (1/2) Materials in Vehicle Design -Vehicles are Different – vehicles are built differently now than in the past. Regulations require vehicle construction that provides improved occupant protection and vehicle performance. Vehicle design has changed to meet both National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) crash standards and Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE) standards. New design technologies used in the construction have to be strong, yet lighter, to provide the required improvements. To achieve better occupant protection, construction materials need to be stronger. For example, stronger side panel assemblies are necessary to improve side impact protection and roof crush resistance. To achieve rollover safety requirements, higher strength materials are being used in the A- and B-pillars, and roof rails. Vehicles are built for performance, which includes better fuel economy and lower emissions. Use of stronger steel provides increased passenger protection and better fuel economy. To achieve better fuel economy, vehicles have to be lighter, which requires using lighter-weight construction materials -Roof Crush Resistance – roof crush resistance requirements are from Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard (FMVSS) 216. This standard establishes strength requirements for the passenger compartment roof to reduce deaths and injuries due to the crushing of the roof into the occupant compartment in rollovers. Previous requirements called for a roof crush resistance of 1.5 times the vehicle weight. Current requirements call for a roof crush resistance of 3.0 times the vehicle weight. Another testing organization, the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety (IIHS), requires a roof crush resistance of 4.0 times the vehicle weight to achieve a “Good” rating. The IIHS rating is only a consumer advisory, not a regulation -Crash Protection Rating – the NHTSA New Car Assessment Program (NCAP) provides a motor vehicle crash test rating system to consumers using a star rating system, from 1 to 5 stars, with 5 being the highest, or best rating. This system also provides a measure of the relative safety potential of vehicles in collisions. Vehicle crash ratings are a product of the construction materials and design of the vehicle. Vehicle repairs should maintain the original crash rating. Ask yourself how many stars are you willing to comprise? In other words, will a 5-star rated vehicle provide 5-star collision protection following repairs? -Collision Energy Management – the use of high strength steel or aluminum may affect the distribution of collision energy and the spread of the resulting damage. This is achieved in part by absorbing and/or transferring the collision energy. Parts may be designed to -Absorb collision energy through the use of collapse or crush zones -Transfer collision energy through the type of forming process used, and the use of higher strength materials, such as hot-stamped boron-alloyed steel parts, and/or reinforced parts Why is Identification Important -importance of Material Identification – because various types of construction materials have different repair considerations, it is more important than ever to identify the material being repaired. Identification may be necessary to determine. -Appropriate removal and attachment tools, equipment, and/or techniques that may be required. -The appropriate repair or replace decisions. Different repair practices are required when repairing higher strength steel or aluminum panels compared to carbon fiber panels -Limits of repairability -Vehicle Construction Materials – this screen shows examples of vehicle construction materials having characteristics designed to provide higher strength, while also providing reduced weight. These materials include: -Higher-strength steel -Aluminum -Magnesium -Carbon fiber AHSS – top left Aluminum – top right Magnesium – bottom left Carbon fiber – bottom right -Material Applications – various types of materials may be used anywhere on a vehicle -Common Applications – the type of material may differ for the same part on different vehicles or models. In this example, there are four radiator core supports provided from different vehicle makers and, depending on the vehicle, the support may be made from high-strength steel, aluminum, magnesium or carbon fiber. Another example of a part that may be made from most of these advanced materials is an engine cradle, which is commonly constructed from steel, aluminum or magnesium. It is becoming more common for vehicle makers to use a variety of materials on any one structure. The specific material used is selected to best fit the design requirements of a specific application. Because of this, it is important to know how to identify the material used on the part being inspected Steel – Jeep Grand Cherokee – top left Magnesium – Ford F-150 – top right Aluminum – Mercedes Benz E Class – bottom left Carbon Fiber – Nissan GT-R – bottom right Material Identification Methods -How are Materials Identified – it is important to use the current vehicle service information for complete and accurate material identification. -Service Information – the type of material used for a specific part may be identified in the service information, which may include OEM or independent service information sources. Depending on the source, the material may be identified as a general or specific type of material. For example, a steel part may be identified as a zinc-coated steel part; a mild, high-strength steel part; or, categorized by type and strength, such as dual-phase (DP) 980. * Service information may have design variations for different model years, even if the body style has not significantly changed. Always refer to the appropriate service information for the model year and build date of the vehicle in question -OEM Procedures – accessing repair procedures should be part of the repair planning process. OEM procedures are developed with vehicle design and specific construction materials taken into consideration. OEM procedures can be accessed from the vehicle maker’s service information websites. Most OEM service information is subscription-based. Links to OEM service information can be accessed from the I-CAR website -Internet Resources – this is an example of an online illustration that calls out the specific material types, and lists applicable repair procedures -Other Repair Information Sources – other service information sources may also be used to access repair procedures. These include sources such as: -ALLDATA provides OEM repair information that addresses diagnostics, repair, and estimating information -Mitchell provides OEM collision repair information. -* ALLDATA and Mitchell are subscription- and/or fee-based information sources -Estimating Databases and Guides – estimating databases or guides may be used for material identification. Materials may be identified in the parts listing. In this example, Mitchell Collision Estimating identifies the vehicle’s front pillar as high-strength steel. Estimating guides should be used along with service information to verify part identification -Other Information Resources – other sources that may be used for material identification include organizations involved with the development of a material. These sources provide general -American Iron and Steel Institute – the American Iron and Steel Institute website www.steel.org contains technical information regarding the use of higher-strength steels for automotive applications -Aluminum Association – the Aluminum Association website www.aluminum.org has listings of vehicles that have used and may currently be using aluminum for exterior body panels and structures -Automotive Composites Alliance – the Automotive Composites Alliance website www.autocomposites.org has information regarding the use and characteristics of carbon fiber parts for automotive application -International Magnesium Association – the International Magnesium Association website www.intlmag.org provides information regarding the use of magnesium for automotive applications -Physical identification Methods – a magnet can be used for an easy and quick identification method for metal parts. Magnets are not attracted to a non-ferrous part, such as aluminum. They are attracted to a ferrous part, such as steel. If hardness testing equipment is available, hardness testing may be another method that can be used for material identification. Hardness testing equipment may: -Be used for identification of various types and strengths of steel -Require a small piece be removed from the part -Using Part Construction for Identification – knowing the type of part construction may help with material identification. For example, castings found in vehicle structures are typically either aluminum or magnesium. Other parts, such as some drivetrain and suspension parts, may be made from cast iron. Some examples of aluminum or magnesium parts that may be a casting include radiator core supports, strut towers, and pillars. Stamped parts are made using aluminum or steel. Stampings are used for exterior body and structural parts. Extrusions are made using aluminum. Extrusions may be made using the hydroforming process. Extrusions are generally used for structural applications, such as lower rails and rocker panel reinforcements Casting – top left Extrusion – bottom left Stamping – right -Using Attachments for Aluminum Identification – service information may provide the specific attachments used for the original construction of newer vehicles. Knowing the specific attachment may help with identification of the material. For example, specific attachments used for aluminum parts include: -Bolts that use isolators, such as corrosion-resistant coatings or nylon washers. Isolating strips that are made from rubberized sealant or have a bi-metallic design are used between aluminum exterior body panels and a steel inner structure. It is important to not confuse corrosion-resistant coatings on bolts with thread locking compound -Rivets such as self-piercing rivets (SPRs) -Clinches to connect outer skins to inner panels and reinforcements -* Because some types of attachments may be used for steel as well as aluminum parts, using attachments as a means of identification should be used along with other methods. If the panels are spot welded, the panels are likely to be steel Spot Welds and Rivets – top left Self Piercing Rivets and Bolts – top right Self Piercing Rivets – bottom left Clinches – bottom right Vehicle Construction Material Types (2/2) Material Types Types of Steel -Types and Strengths of Steel – damage analysis considerations and strengths of steels include -Vehicle makers may have different names for the same type of steel. For example, high-tension steel, extra-high-strength steel, etc. are all names for steels that may be similar in strength. Therefore, it is important to pay attention to actual tensile strength and/or yield strength, typically listed in megapascals (MPa) rather than a vehicle maker’s steel trade name -One megapascal is one million pascals and is equal to about 145 psi. Therefore, a part that is rated at 800 MPa is equal to about 116,000 psi Top to Bottom Hydroformed Aluminum &gt;800 MPa – Ultra-High-Strength Steel &gt;400MPa&lt;800MPa – Extra-High-Strength Steel &gt;180MPa&lt;400MPa – High-Strength Steel &lt;180MPa – Mild Steel -Steel Strengths and Common Part Locations Chart – this chart shows strengths and locations of some steel parts. The diagonal arrow in the middle of the chart is for reference only to show the direction and flow for reading the chart. A UHSS part is typically more brittle than an HSS part, which in turn, is typically more brittle than a mild steel part. * too much information to include. Chart does not show any words so I would have to recreate chart -Steel Part Forming and Strength – the forming, or manufacturing process, of steel parts may contribute to the strength of the part. Some part forming and manufacturing processes include hot-stamping, tailor-rolled blanks, and tailor-welded blanks Tailor-Welded Blank and a Laser Weld -Hot-Stamping – the hot-stamping process starts with a boron-alloyed steel part, which is heated and then pressed into shape using a cooled die (die-quenching). The die cools the stamped pillar at different rates along the length of the part, making some parts of the inner pillar stronger than other parts of the same pillar. This process is used instead of tailor-welding or tailor-rolling. When making repair decisions, consider that these parts contribute to the crash safety rating. An incorrect repair will reduce the safety performance of the structure -Tailor-Rolled Blanks – another design being used in vehicle structures is tailor-rolled blanks. Tailor-rolled blanks: -Are often made from higher-strength steel, and/or hot-stamping, and may have limited repairability -Have different thicknesses of the same strength steel within the same part -Have no seam at the transition. Because seams are not produced, identifying tailor-rolled parts may be difficult. During the manufacturing process, the roller increases pressure in areas of the sheet steel where thinner metal is desired, and decreases pressure for areas where thicker metal is desired From top to bottom 1.0 mm 1.05-1.85 mm 1.9 mm 1.85-1.65 mm 1.6 mm 1.65-1.75 (Transition Zone) 1.8 mm 1.0 mm Mild steel -Tailor-Welded Blanks – some parts are made using tailor-welded blank technology. A tailor-welded blank is a panel that has dissimilar thicknesses or metal alloys. The purpose of using a tailor-welded blank is to provide strength and/or stiffness in areas of a one-piece structure without using reinforcements, thus reducing the weight of the part. The tailor-welded blank is designed for collision energy management. The varying thicknesses and strengths of the part can be designed to collapse in a predictable manner. One example is a tailor-welded front rail that has a collapse zone at the front of the rail assembly. These assemblies may not have conventional crush initiators, convolutions, or holes that are commonly used to identify crush zones. Tailor-welded blanks are laser-welded together to form one continuous piece. The laser welds cannot be duplicated during the repair process. During damage analysis, consider that the laser weld is typically not a sectioning location. Never section a tailor-welded blank at the laser weld unless called for in a vehicle maker procedure -Steel Usage – the percent of higher strength steel used in vehicles increases with each new model year. Today, coated boron-alloyed steel is found in pillar, roof, and floor reinforcements. Gray – Ultra-High Strength Die-Quenched Green – Ultra-High-Strength Yellow – High-Strength Orange – Regular Grade -High-Strength Steel – applications for high-strength steel include -Unitized structure parts -Some exterior body panels -* More exterior body panels may be constructed from higher strength steel in the future to provide another area where weight reduction can apply. Weight reduction can be achieved by reducing the thickness of the panel. Repairs may be limited on exterior panels made from high strength steel because these parts are harder, thinner, more sensitive to heat, and more prone to cracking during straightening -Ultra-High-Strength Steel – UHSS may have a tensile strength of 780 MPa and above. These steel grades are suitable for forming structural and safety-related automotive components. UHSS can be formed into complex shapes such as door impact beams, bumper reinforcements, seating, and structural body parts -Boron-Alloyed Steel – some boron-alloyed steel receives a special coating during the manufacturing process. Boron-alloyed steel: -May not be galvanized, since the coating burns off during the hot-stamping process. The use of heat during galvanizing also changes the structure of the steel. -May be coated during manufacturing with an aluminum/silicone coating applied at a low temperature to the sheet blank. This provides corrosion-protection properties similar to zinc coating. Like a zinc coating, the aluminum/silicon coating is a sacrificial coating that is more active than steel, so it corrodes to protect the steel -* There is a type of boron-alloyed steel called Usibor used in the pillars of various vehicles. Usibor is an ultra-high-strength, hot-worked steel. Other applications for Usibor steel include front and rear bumper reinforcements, door intrusion beams, A- and B-pillar reinforcements, and roof and floor reinforcements -Aluminum Usage – the aluminum content in some vehicles is increasing with each new model year. Examples of aluminum parts on current vehicles include the outer panels, structures, frames and wheels. -Aluminum Considerations – the presence of aluminum parts requires the appropriate tools, procedures, materials, and/or training for repairs. Although vehicle maker recommendations vary, aluminum castings are not repaired. Aluminum extrusions have very limited, or no repair options. The most repairable types of aluminum parts are stampings. Identifying aluminum parts is an important step to determine the correct repair plan. A magnet can be used for easy and quick identification. Depending on the extent of damage, equipment and techniques are available to allow for the repair of aluminum exterior body panels -Aluminum Alloys – pure aluminum is very soft and ductile. Aluminum alloys are pure aluminum mixed with other elements. The adding of other elements can affect the strength, formability, weldability, and corrosion resistance. Heat treating refers to heating and cooling operations performed to change the properties or structure of a metal. Aluminum alloys are classified as heat-treatable or non-heat treatable. Series 2000, 6000, and 7000 are heat-treatable, while series 1000, 3000, 4000, and 5000 are not heat-treatable. The most common allows used for automotive applications are 5000 and 6000 series Picture: Red – Heat treated – -2000 series -6000 series -7000 series Blue – Non-Heat treated – -1000 series -3000 series -4000 series -5000 series Magnesium -Magnesium Characteristics – parts made from magnesium are increasingly being used with newer vehicle construction. Magnesium is a lightweight material that is both lighter and stronger than some steel. Magnesium has good noise and vibration dampening characteristics. In addition, magnesium is a non-magnetic metal. Parts made from magnesium are typically made from castings -Magnesium Applications – as a construction material, magnesium has a wide variety of automotive applications -Vehicle Construction Examples – some applications where magnesium parts are used in newer vehicle construction include -Radiator core supports -Front engine and suspension crossmembers -Roof frame and mounting brackets -Inner liftgates -Interior Applications – additional applications where magnesium parts are used in vehicle construction include: -Instrument panel frames -Steering wheels -Seat frames -Inner door panels -Magnesium Precautions – when working with or around magnesium, it is important to be aware that: -Magnesium parts and dust can ignite -While you should exercise caution, the likelihood of a fire is minimal. With magnesium parts, the heat dissipates quickly. For a part to ignite, the entire part must exceed 883 degrees F -In the event of a fire, a person trained on using a class D fire extinguisher can extinguish the fire. If this type of extinguisher is not available, the fire can be smothered with dirt or sand. Applying water may spread the fire -* For optimum safety, always consider the heat exposure when cutting, welding, or grinding on or near magnesium parts. Although magnesium can be welded, no procedures currently exist for automotive applications Carbon Fiber -What is Carbon Fiber – carbon fiber-reinforced composite, more commonly called carbon fiber, is being used more on newer vehicles. -Carbon fiber parts are a combination of a polymer and carbon fibers. The carbon fibers are the reinforcing substance. Carbon fibers are made from carbon atoms, which are joined together to form long fiber strands. These fibers are very strong and lightweight. -Carbon fiber parts are reinforced through the use of woven carbon fiber matting, or chopped carbon fibers. A part made from chopped carbon fiber is heavier than a part made from woven carbon fiber matting. Carbon fiber matting is manufactured in various weave patterns -*Note: There are aftermarket treatments that will give a part the cosmetic appearance of being carbon fiber when they are not actually carbon fiber parts. For example, vinyl decals, paint, and hydro-dipping Vehicle Protection During the Repair Process (1/2) Initial Inspection -Protecting the Vehicle – from the initial vehicle inspection to the final delivery and all steps in between, it is important to avoid causing additional damage to vehicles we work on and to surrounding vehicles. Careful inspection of the vehicle and our surroundings is critical. Think through each inspection, disassembly, and repair operation, considering what can go wrong and what safeguards will help to avoid further vehicle damage -Vehicle Check-In – during the vehicle check-in, the initial safety inspection is performed and the vehicle is prepared for the repair process -Inventory and Store Personal Belongings – it is important to protect the vehicle owner’s personal belongings. When the vehicle is drivable, it is a good practice to instruct the owner to remove any personal belongings prior to dropping the vehicle off for repairs. For tow-in or non-drivable vehicles, check the vehicle for any personal belongings that will need to be inventoried and stored for protection -Crash Wrap Exposed Areas – crash wrap any exposed areas. It is important to seal any normally protected areas, such as those exposed due to collision-related damages. Exposed areas may include electrical components, window openings, door gaps, and hood/trunk openings -Protect the Interior – protect the interior of the vehicle against production-shop-related damages by installing floor mats, seat covers, steering wheel covers, and keeping the windows rolled up during the repair process -Check for Alternative Fuel Systems – determining the presence of an alternative fuel system during the initial check-in is a good best practice to help reduce safety concerns for the vehicle, as well as shop staff. Some examples of alternative fuel systems are hybrid, electric, propane, and compressed natural gas. Note the presence of any alternative fuel systems using the shop standard operating procedure SOP) for vehicle markings -Emergency Response Guides – emergency response guides (ERGs) are: -Free online publications that explain how to approach an electric vehicle or hybrid electric vehicle -Written by the vehicle makers for emergency responders to safely extricate someone from an electric vehicle, put out a fire, and handle other emergency situations -Effective for the collision industry in that they include identification information and part locations and descriptions, among other information. Hybrid vehicle disabling procedures are included, but the information may not include instructions for pulling the service disconnect, which is recommended before a vehicle is towed or doing collision repairs -Panel Gaps – note any areas with gaps that have closed up and could contact adjacent panels. Highlight these areas with appropriate vehicle markings to alert all shop employees. These areas often include the door contacting -Towing and Moving the Vehicle – closely inspect the vehicle prior to moving the vehicle within the shop and/or parking lot. Check the brakes before starting the vehicle. Identify any broken/damaged suspension and/or driveline components. Check for possible electrical issues that could cause a safety concern when restoring power to the vehicle or placing the key in the RUN position. Use a car dolly, or similar equipment, when suspension damage prevents the vehicle from rolling away -Moving the Vehicle Safely – do not push electric or hybrid vehicles with the wheels on the ground. Moving the vehicle with the drive wheels on the ground may generate electrical energy and charge the condenser in the inverter. There is also a risk of starting a fire if damage to high-voltage cables create a short to the vehicle’s chassis Vehicle Protection During the Repair Process (2/2) Production Shop Safely -Teardown – precautions before doing a teardown on collision damaged vehicles include: -Disable restraint systems to prevent accidental deployment -Use caution when applying penetrating oil near the vehicle finish. Some penetrating oils contain solvents that may damage the finish -Protect the vehicle finish against sharp objects. Clean up broken glass inside the vehicle to prevent additional damage during the teardown process -Protect any exposed system components, including airbag sensors, ABS sensor, MAP sensors, electrical connectors, and damaged or cut wires -Identify vehicle maker repair procedures and requirements -Identify proper lifting points -Fluids, Hoses, and Lines – -prepare to capture any leaking fluids or drained fluids as part of the teardown process. Captured fluids may include: -Antifreeze -Transmission fluid -Power steering fluid -Fuel -Brake fluid -Brake fluid may damage the finish and can be conductive if it absorbs enough moisture, and can cause fire if it gets into electrical connectors. Identify and cap any open connection points for the: -Air conditioning system -Power steering system -Fuel system -Brake system -Cooling system -Electrical connections -Note: Use caution when working with brake and fuel system parts. Refer to vehicle maker recommendations for depressurizing systems before starting any work. Verify self-test systems are disabled on brake systems -One-Time Use Fasteners – technicians must identify one-time use fasteners and torque specifications as noted in the vehicle maker repair procedures. One-time use fasteners may also be referred to as single-use fasteners -Electrolyte Spills – electrolyte spills are highly corrosive to: -Body panels -Frame rails -Pinch welds -Exterior finish -Electrical components -Mechanical components -Belts -Hoses -Note: Most high-voltage batteries have a gel-type electrolyte, which does not spill. However, it is possible that battery damage can result in some electrolyte leakage. Electrolyte from a high-voltage battery is highly alkaline or caustic. Lead-acid batteries have electrolyte that is highly acidic -Safety Equipment – safety equipment required for cleaning up battery electrolyte spills include: -Chemical resistant gloves (acid or alkaline) -Litmus paper for checking the acidic or alkaline content of the suspected spill -Neoprene rubber boots or shoe covers, as well as coveralls, suits, or aprons -Alkali- and acid-resistant face shields or goggles. Wear eye protection to protect against accidental splashing of the electrolyte -Neutralizing Spills – electrolyte spills require neutralizing with a solution. The solution used depends on whether it is an alkaline or acidic spill. For an alkaline spill, such as from a hybrid vehicle battery, neutralize with boric acid and water solution. Neutralize any spilled acid. For an acidic spill, such as from a 12-volt battery, neutralize with baking soda or ammonia -Alternative Fuel Vehicles – alternative fuel vehicles must be handled carefully to avoid possible hazards -Electric and Hybrid Electric Vehicles – use caution and follow vehicle-maker recommendations regarding service on high-voltage batteries. Some vehicle makers suggest only factory-trained technicians may perform high-voltage battery removal and installation. Some vehicle makers may offer extended warranty parameters, such as 10 years and 100,000 miles. Damaging a battery during removal may void this warranty -Compressed Natural Gas Vehicles – most compressed natural gas (CNG) fueled vehicles are conversions. CNG vehicles made by a vehicle maker are generally identifiable by the nameplate. CNG conversions are identified by: -A label inside the fuel-filler door, and there may be a decal -A fuel-filler valve instead of, or in addition to, a gasoline filler neck -Stainless steel fuel lines, which may have a sheathing cover -Note – CNG is very dense when compressed but lighter than air when released so there is more risk of fire at the ceiling level than at the floor level -Liquid Propane Gas Vehicles – most liquid propane gas (LPG) vehicles are conversion with no official emblem or nameplate for identification. These vehicles are identifiable by the: -Fuel-filler valve, instead of or in addition to, a gasoline filler neck -Torpedo- or doughnut-shaped fuel tanks either in the cargo area, underside, or on the truck bed -Copper fuel lines, which may be covered by a sheathing -Check label inside the fuel door -Liquid Propane Gas Considerations – propane is a liquid state under pressure, but immediately releases as a vapor when a pressure-relief valve opens. A mild temperature difference, such as moving the tank from a cold to a hot area, is enough to open the pressure-relief valve. Propane tanks are painted white or a light reflective color-dark-colored tanks can absorb enough heat from the sun to open the pressure-relief valve. Propane vapor is heavier than air, so the vapors will hover near the floor if released. Propane is highly flammable. Mixtures in the air as low as 2% will easily ignite and explode. Released vapors can form a flammable layer against the floor -Electronic Key – electronic keys are popular on many vehicles, not just HEVs and EVs. Depending on the work being performed, it is important to make sure the vehicle is powered off before attempting repairs. To ensure the vehicle does not power up or start unexpectedly, creating an unsafe condition or damage to the vehicle or its surroundings, store keys the required minimum distance from the vehicle. Features such as remote start and phone apps, where the vehicle owner can start the vehicle or operate parts of the vehicle, also needs to be considered and deactivated. Disconnecting the 12-volt battery typically works, keeping in mind some vehicles may have auxiliary batteries that may keep some systems powered. -Part Storage – store undamaged parts safely. Organize removed parts to avoid loss or damage. Use parts carts and part bins whenever possible. Do not store removed parts inside the vehicle. Bag interior parts to protect them from damage and repair debris. Store glass safely in a glass rack. When working with or handling trim panels, headliners, seats, carpet, or other items that may stain easily, be sure your hands, gloves, and clothes are clean to avoid leaving stains -Heat Considerations – heat should only be used following vehicle maker guidelines. If heat is applied to parts and the vehicle maker does not have allowances for heat or the recommended limits are exceeded, the affected parts must be replaced. When heating a part: -Slowly heat the area around the damage (50-75mm from the center) to prevent a sudden variation in the metal temperature. -Keep tension applied to the steel, and allow the steel to cool naturally. Do not quench with water -Use temperature indicators to monitor the amount of heat applied to a part -When using non-contact thermometers, take into consideration that shiny surfaces, like bare metal, may have a low emissivity and not provide an accurate reading -Safety when Using Heat – when using heat, the working area must be clear of flammable and combustible items. Fire prevention equipment and first aid supplies, which includes treatment for burns, should be readily available. Fire prevention equipment may include a fire extinguisher, bucket of water, and a fire blanket -Protecting the Finish – protect the vehicle finish against sharp objects, such as clamps, chains, and other pulling hardware. This can be done with fender covers or a welding blanket. Verify there is no broken glass inside the vehicle that could cause additional damage during repairs -Anchoring – anchor the vehicle properly to avoid additional damage to the vehicle. Clean the anchoring points before clamping. Make sure that wires, brake and fuel lines, and hoses will not be damaged when attaching clamps. Do not anchor to mechanical or suspension parts. Check and re-apply coatings to anchoring and clamp attachment areas to avoid possible corrosion -Working with Vehicle Fluids – vehicle fluid warnings and safety precautions should be considered when working with air conditioning oils, brake fluids, and power steering fluid -Air Conditioning Oils – use caution when handling air conditioning oils, as they can cause damage to the vehicle finish, plastic parts, engine drive belts, and coolant hoses. Only use oil from a sealed container. Some oils will absorb moisture if left open and can cause damage when introduced into an A/C system -Brake Fluids – brake fluid spills may damage the finish and can be conductive if it absorbs enough moisture. Spills can cause problems with electronic systems and may lead to a fire if the fluid comes into contact with electrical connectors -Power Steering Fluids – do not mix power steering fluid types. This may result in seal deterioration or leaks -General Safety Guidelines – follow these general guidelines for vehicle safety: -Ensure vehicle fluids, such as coolant and oil, are filled before starting the vehicle. Before adding new coolant, review the vehicle maker’s specifications for the correct coolant type and for the ratio of coolant necessary to provide adequate freeze protection -Do not attempt to start or leave the vehicle running without the battery connected. -Do not use a jumper box in place of the battery or disconnect the battery while the vehicle is running -Do not connect, or disconnect, modules or sensors while the battery is connected, ignition is ON, or the vehicle is running, unless instructed to do so by the vehicle maker -General Safety Guidelines (cont’d) – follow these additional general guidelines for vehicle safety: -Always use caution when working on fuel or brake system parts. Refer to the vehicle maker’s recommendations for disabling self-test systems and depressurizing systems before starting any work -Verify the brake system is working properly after completing repairs and prior to attempting to drive the vehicle -General Safety Tasks – observe the following guidelines for safe repair of mechanical components: -Do not use an impact wrench to install a wheel bearing – torque all fasteners to correct specifications -Do not use shock absorbers that have longer travel than specified by the vehicle maker. If a suspension is allowed to travel more than designed by the vehicle maker, part damage may occur -To avoid damage to the vehicle, use caution to identify proper lifting points before raising the vehicle off the ground -Do not allow the suspension or axle to hang unsupported, as this may tear or cause other damage to the brake lines -Fasteners that are heated should be replaced -Sanding – protect all interior surfaces to minimize sanding dust and debris from getting into the vehicle. Do not apply body filler to the windshield or rear glass pinchweld areas. Protect exterior trim and moldings on adjacent panels when sanding -Corrosion Protection – apply corrosion protection materials to all repaired areas. Use appropriate corrosion protection when joining dissimilar materials to prevent galvanic corrosion. Remove any shavings or grinding particles with a magnet or vacuum. If unattended, these materials may rattle, make noise, and lead to corrosion issues. Use caution when working with carbon fiber, as exposed carbon fiber can cause galvanic corrosion if it comes in contact with metal. Carbon fiber dust is conductive and can also cause electrical shorts -Repair Product Considerations – use caution when working with: -Adhesives -Fillers -Foams -Seam sealers -Corrosion protection materials -Other products -Note: Maintain clean gloves to minimize the chances of touching tools, panels, or the interior and transferring adhesive. Remove or protect the vehicle interior and adjacent areas from materials that may drip or cause overspray. Wear multiple layers of nitrile gloves before starting the procedure. This will allow for easy replacement of contaminated gloves -Checking the Repair Zone – when welding, heating, cutting, grinding, drilling, pulling, or stress-relieving, identify what is behind any outer panels under repair to avoid damage or starting a fire. Check for: -Foam -Seam sealer -Corrosion protection -Undercoating -Wire harnesses -Sound deadening materials -Interior trim/carpet -Adhesive-bonded panels -Brake lines -Fuel tanks and lines -Note: When cutting, grinding, drilling, or welding, pay attention to where the sparks, chips, and debris are going. Sparks can embed in glass, and steel particles can cause rust rings on surfaces. Spark paper and welding blankets can help protect against this. Curtains between work stalls can help protect adjacent vehicles. Examples of items to check for also include bedliners, trim panels, control modules, adjacent panels, glass, and surrounding vehicles. When applying corrosion protection or other flammable products, ensure adequate time is allowed for the flammable gases to dissipate before welding, using a heat gun, or using other ignition sources in the vicinity. -General Welding Considerations – prior to welding on a vehicle: -Surrounding the welding location with welding blankets -Disconnect and isolate both battery cables -Disable the passive restraint system -Identify all material types in the repair zone -Identify interior trim panels that may need to be removed to provide access to the panel backside and avoid damage from weld-on dent removal tools or other repair processes -Remove electronic equipment within 12” of the repair area – this includes control modules and sensors -Keep the current path short by placing work clamps close to the weld site -Route cables away from wiring harnesses and sensitive electronic parts that may be damaged due to the welder’s magnetic fields -Alternative Fuel Vehicles – when welding on an electric or hybrid electric vehicle, refer to vehicle maker requirements. Some vehicle makers require technicians to disable the high-voltage system is welding within 12” of the high-voltage battery. Disabling the battery may involve removing the service disconnect. Vehicle makers may require removal of the high-voltage battery to avoid heat damage related to cutting or welding. Always use caution when welding on alternative fuel vehicles. Remember that LPG and CNG are both flammable and explosive. Note: Before welding, cover the high-voltage battery with a welding blanket to protect it from sparks -Heating Aluminum – use caution when applying heat to aluminum parts, as they do not change color before reaching their melting point -Heating High- and Ultra-High-Strength Steel – heating considerations for high-strength steel (HSS) and ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS) include: -Do not straighten UHSS parts unless instructed to do so in the OEM documentation -Straighten HSS parts cold unless OEM documentation recommends heating -Observe the vehicle maker’s limits for both the maximum temperature and the length of heating time when heating parts -Note: Using heat on parts that are to be replaced during initial straightening may help to reduce collateral damage to return adjacent parts. This allows the entire structure to return to proper dimensions without having to apply excessive force to remove damage to HSS and UHSS replacement parts -Refinishing – vehicle safety precautions apply when performing refinishing tasks, including prepping, painting, wet sanding, and buffing the vehicle -Prep – mask and/or cover all areas outside the repair zone to minimize overspray issues. Protect exterior trim and adjacent panels when sanding. Ensure trim on any adjacent panels, that were not blended/painted, are properly covered/masked to prevent damage -Heating/Baking – identify hybrid or propane-fueled vehicles that may require special attention. Use caution when heating/baking vehicles with high-voltage batteries and refer to vehicle maker recommendations for temperature and maximum allowable time for bake cycle exposure -Wet Sand/Buff – cover trim and glass on panels being wet-sanded and buffed to protect from damage during the sanding process or contact from buffer and/or buffing materials Windows and Advanced Driver Assist Systems (ADAS) (1/2) Introduction -Advanced Driver Assistance Systems – advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) are a collection of safety systems that warn the driver of a potential collision or lane deviation. Some systems actively control the vehicle to prevent a collision, such as providing braking or steering assistance. Other types of ADAS automatically control common functions and assist the driver in emergency situations. Some ADAS cameras and sensors attach to, or are located near, the windshield -Purpose of this course – this course covers ADAS parts that relate to the windshield, and systems that have sensors or cameras attached to the windshield. These typically require removal whenever the windshield is removed. Some parts require special calibration procedures to be done after installation. A few examples are – Lane Departure warning, Collision mitigation cameras, rain sensors. -There are many types of ADAS, but this course does not cover systems that use cameras or sensors located in other areas of the vehicle. For example, this course does not cover – blind spot detection, parking assist, rearview cameras. Parts for these systems are typically located on the bumpers, door mirrors, or the rear body. -Windshield and ADAS Part Damage – windshields and ADAS parts can be damaged on the road by projectiles, such as rocks or other types of road debris. They can also be damaged as a result of a collision. Windshield replacement or repairs are commonly done by glass repair or replacement service providers. The vehicle owner may directly contact a glass service provider if only the windshield work to these glass service providers. This work may be for repairable windshield damage, windshield replacement, or R&amp;I during collision repairs. -Windshield and ADAS Part (continued) – ADAS parts associated with the windshield can also be damaged. A pre-scan using diagnostics software may identify damaged or non-functioning ADAS parts. Diagnostic software can also be called a scan tool. This may require special diagnostic software and may have to be done by a dealership. Direct damage to the windshield in areas of ADAS parts is an indication that those parts may require replacement. Inspection of ADAS parts may require removal of the parts to get a good look at them. Inspect these parts for cracks to the module, lens, or sensor. Also look for debris in the lens. The vehicle maker may require a camera to be replaced if debris is found in the lens - Lane Departure Warning/Lane Keep assist – the lane departure warning (LDW) and lane keep assist (LKA) systems use the same type of camera. This is often a multi-purpose object detection camera, which may work with the other types of ADAS. Most lane keep assist cameras are located behind the windshield while some are located on the grille. For LDW or LKA, the camera monitors lane markings on the road. An LDW system only gives warnings if the vehicle is drifting out of the lane. Warnings may be audio, visual, or a vibration of the seat or steering wheel. An LKA system may provide an audible and/or visual alert. A light steering assistance feature may help to keep the vehicle in the lane which typically, can be turned off. - Collision Mitigation System – collision mitigation systems (CMS) may use the object detection camera located behind the windshield, a radar unit located behind the grille, or both. Depending on the system, these are used to detect vehicles, objects, or pedestrians on the road ahead. A CMS may provide a warning if approaching a vehicle or object too closely. It may also provide automatic braking or braking assist if a collision is imminent. This is done to avoid collisions and reduce the effect of a low-speed impact. - Pre-Collision Systems – a pre-collision system may work in conjunction with a CMS. Both initiate the restraint systems if they detect an imminent collision. For example, a pre-collision system may adjust the seats, activate the seat belt tensioners, raise the head restraints, and close the windows to protect the occupants. - Automatic High-Beam Assist – automatic high beam assist is a feature that automatically controls the high beams. It turns off the high beams when it detects oncoming traffic, or when it detects a slower moving vehicle ahead. The system turns the high beams back on when the vehicle has passed. High beams are also turned on or off depending on the vehicle speed. These types of systems use either a separate camera sensor attached to the interior rearview mirror, or work with a multi-purpose object detection camera. - Night Vision Systems – night vision systems use a special camera located behind the windshield or in the grille. They provide an enhanced video display of objects in the dark. These types of systems use one of two types of night vision technologies. Far-infrared technology uses thermal imaging to detect living objects, such as pedestrians and animals. Near-infrared systems use high-powered infrared lighting, which is invisible to the human eye. This type of light is similar to household remote controls. - Head-Up Display – the head-up display (HUD) projects images onto the windshield or a transparent display. The projected image often appears holographic with the use of a special type of windshield. HUD increases the driver’s ability to pay attention to the road ahead. Information projected may include: -The speedometer -The tachometer -Navigation -Safety alerts - Automatic Windshield Wipers – an automatic windshield wiper system uses a rain sensor. The rain sensor may also be called a moisture sensor. These are commonly attached directly to the windshield. The sensor detects the amount of moisture on the windshield by sending an infrared ray through the windshield. The wet windshield deflects the infrared ray, sending a signal to the wiper motor module to start the wipers. The wiper switch may have to be turned on or adjusted to the automatic setting to work -Windshields and ADAS Concerns – before removing, replacing, or repairing the windshield, verify whether or not the vehicle is equipped with ADAS. If it is, determine part removal and installation requirements. Refer to the vehicle specific instructions for windshield removal to determine what parts require removal. Order the glass specific to equipped ADAS parts. Do not repair glass in front of cameras or sensors. Some vehicle markers state that the windshield will require replacement if damage is in front of the camera or sensor. Other vehicle markers may provide a diagram of the windshield. The diagram may extend beyond the camera or sensor locations where repairs are not allowed. Subaru provides a diagram for vehicles equipped with Eyesight cameras where glass repairs are prohibited. -ADAS Calibrations – ADAS are becoming much more common with new models. They are no longer limited to luxury vehicles. Some systems may become standard on all vehicle markers. Most cameras require some sort of calibration, if not multiple calibrations, for each system on the vehicle. Calibrations may include driving the vehicle, an intensive camera aiming procedure, or both. These types of calibrations usually require the use of a scan tool. Special tools, such are target kits, may be required for calibration aiming procedures. An ADAS may be considered a safety system. If parts are not properly installed and calibrated, this may result in a liability issue. Windows and Advanced Driver Assist Systems (ADAS) (2/2) -Identification – when inspecting a damaged windshield, look for cameras and sensors. Safety systems associated with the windshield can be found a few different ways. Perform a visual inspection for sensors, cameras, buttons, and switches. Look for MILs, or malfunction indicator lamps, on the dash and page through menus located either on the dash, or the infotainment menu. The OEM Calibration Requirements Search tool on the I-CAR RTS website helps identify which options are available on different models. RTS also provides a helpful article on damage analysis for ADAS identification. Finally, you can identify equipped ADAS using a scan tool, either an OEM-specific tool, software, or an aftermarket tool. -Vehicle-Specific Service Information – look up the windshield removal instructions in the vehicle-specific service information. These instructions will typically identify ADAS parts that require removal when removing the windshield. Refer to any provided links for removing procedures to ensure proper removal. Vehicle-specific service information will also identify when calibration is required and provide instructions. Links to OEM service information websites are available in the I-CAR Repairability Technical Support, or RTS Portal. This website also provides instructions on accessing and navigating each OEM website. -OEM Glass Replacement – the vehicle maker may specify OEM replacement glass. This ensures the replacement glass has the same light refraction properties or ADAS mounting brackets as the original glass. -Glass Replacement Considerations – the black border, or frit, must match the locations of cameras and sensors. The wrong frit design may block the view and cause issues. Do not dispose of the damaged glass until you verify the replacement part includes all the necessary brackets. A bracket may have to be transferred to the new glass. Order the replacement windshield according to the ADAS options on the vehicle. A windshield may have different part numbers. -HUD Glass Replacement Considerations – on vehicles equipped with a HUD, cover the projector during the repair. If the windshield requires replacement, be sure to order the glass specific to the HUD. The HUD on some vehicles must be calibrated whenever the windshield is removed and installed. Some HUDs do not project onto the windshield. In these cases, a transparent display pops up from the dash panel. It is still important to avoid damage by ensuring they are covered during the windshield removal. -HUD Glass – the image on the right represents a windshield that is specially designed for the reflection of a HUD. Notice that the image is clear and may even appear holographic. -Non-HUD Glass – the image on the right represents a windshield that is not designed for reflecting a HUD. If the correct windshield is not ordered, the image will not display properly. The image may reflect off both the inside and outside surfaces of the glass, which appears as a double image. The image may also appear crooked and blurry. -Cameras – besides the object detection camera which can be a single or a double system, there are also night vision cameras. Cameras may be attached to a bracket on the windshield, rearview mirror assembly, or the upper console. The vehicle-specific windshield removal procedure will identify all the parts that require removal, including any cameras or sensors. At least one vehicle maker, Nissan/Infiniti, does not require the camera to be removed if the windshield will be removed and reinstalled. -Camera Removal – follow the instructions in the vehicle-specific service information to prevent damage to the camera or electrical system during removal. Disconnect and isolate the negative battery cable on certain vehicles. Other vehicles may only need to be turned off for a few seconds before removal. The camera units are commonly located behind trim panels around the rearview mirror. Remove these to access the camera unit and electrical connector. Disconnect the electrical connectors before removing the camera unit. This can only be done after removing the camera on certain vehicles. -Camera Removal Concerns – the camera removal procedure will help identify the location of the release tabs and a sequence of pulling directions for removing the camera unit. Cameras that are attached to the upper console sometimes require removing mechanical fasteners. Avoid touching the camera lens or allowing the lens to get dirty during removal. Cover the lens during repairs. Vehicle makers require the camera to be replaced if the lens get dirty because it is too small to clean properly. -Rain Sensors – rain sensors typically attach to a bracket on the windshield near the rearview mirror. Some rain sensor units include an ambient light sensor. The ambient light sensor detects the level of ambient light and turns the headlamps on or off accordingly. Some rain sensor units include a solar sensor to detect the location of sunlight and automatically control the air conditioning system to the driver or passenger side. Rain sensors typically have a gel pad or layer that contacts the surface of the glass. This gel pad may also be called silicone or film. -Rain Sensor Removal – the removal procedure may require the battery to be disconnected before removing the rain sensor. Remove trim covers and the rearview mirror or other parts as specified in the procedure. Disconnect the electrical connector before removing the sensor unit. Always use care when removing the sensor to prevent damaging the gel pad. Some vehicle makers require the entire sensor be replaced if the gel pad is damaged during removal. At least on vehicle maker specifies to unlock the rain sensor and wait 30 minutes for the gel pad to release from the windshield. -Rain Sensor Precautions – do not touch the surface of the gel pad and keep it clean during storage. The vehicle maker may recommend leaving the rain sensor attached to the original glass until it can be installed on the replacement windshield. It may be beneficial to first verify it can be removed without damage. This will help prevent delays if a new gel pad must be ordered. Reattach it to the original windshield to help keep it clean. Some rain sensors cannot be reused if they are removed. -Cameras/Sensors for Auto High-Beam – high beam assist systems may use a separate photo sensor built into the rearview mirror assembly. Follow the procedure for removing the rearview mirror equipped with a high beam assist sensor. Other systems use the multi-purpose object detection camera to automatically control the high beams. Calibration for automatic high beam systems is typically not required. However, there is a calibration procedure for the high beam assist system on some makes and models. -Night Vision Cameras – some Lexus and Mercedes-Benz vehicles have a night vision camera mounted to the rearview mirror assembly. A Lexus equipped with night view assist will have the night vision camera lens separate from the two-object detection cameras but are included in the same assembly. Both the object detection cameras and the night vision system require separate calibration procedures when reinstalled. A night vision camera on an equipped Mercedes is a separate part. Calibration of the night vision camera is required when reinstalled. BMW and Audi also have night vision systems, but the night vision cameras on those vehicles are not mounted on the windshield. -ADAS Installation Instructions – before installing a replacement windshield, verify that it has the brackets and frit design for the ADAS parts. ADAS parts are typically installed or reinstalled in the reverse order of removal. Install the part, connect the electrical connector, and replace the trim covers. It may be required to connect the electrical connector before reattaching the negative battery terminal or turning on the vehicle. Verify the windshield is clean in the camera or sensor locations before installation. This may be specified in the procedure but is a general good practice. Clean these areas of the windshield with a lint-free cloth, isopropyl alcohol, or a specified product. Glass cleaners can contaminate camera lenses preventing them from functioning properly. -Rain Sensor Installation – before installing the rain sensor onto the windshield, remove any gel pad remnants from the glass surface. Thoroughly clean the windshield in the sensor area. Gel pad remnants on the original glass indicate that the pad is damaged. Some vehicle makers specify that the gel pad be replaced only if damaged. Others require replacement anytime the sensor is removed. Some gel pads are not supplied and require the entire rain sensor replacement if the gal pad is damaged. Press the rain sensor firmly and evenly onto the windshield to prevent air pockets as these will adversely affect the automatic windshield operation. It may be indicated to connect the rain sensor before connecting the battery for recognition. You may also need to reset the rain sensor. -Determine Calibration Requirements – when determining calibration requirements for ADAS parts, the I-CAR RTS OEM Calibration Search tool can provide the ADAS options and OEM name. RTS also includes requirements and special tools here. From there, you can look up the service information referring to the vehicle’s specific make, model, and year of the vehicle. Advanced systems change throughout the years. Different cameras or sensors may be used, or they may be programmed to work with multiple systems. For example, an object detection camera on a late model vehicle may detect vehicles and pedestrians, and work with the collision mitigation system (CMS). An older model of the same vehicle may only detect lane markings on the road. Refer to the windshield or ADAS part removal and installation procedure to determine calibration requirements. -Most ADAS Cameras Require Calibration – expect most removed cameras to require calibration, especially if a new glass is installed. Don’t rely on a lit MIL or a pre-scan DTC to determine if calibration is required. Multiple calibrations may be required for different ADAS parts, or if the ADAS part works with more than one system. -Not Only Vehicle-Specific – ADAS calibration requirements are not only vehicle specific, but also depend on other factors. The model year is important when determining calibration requirements as well as the chosen options package. Object detection cameras are capable of many operations. They will only operate according to the chosen options package. For example, one vehicle might use a separate sensor for high-beam assist. A different model year with the same options package may use the object detection camera for high-beam assist. The CMS may also use the object detection camera. This may require multiple calibrations if the camera is removed and installed. -Static Camera Calibration – a static camera calibration procedure is done while the vehicle is parked. Use a specially designed target for the procedure. The aiming target and other special tools may be exclusive to dealership service facilities. Some vehicle makers provide digital copies of target for print. These must be printed to the correct size before using it for the calibration procedure. Place the targets precisely to ensure an accurate calibration. This may involve many measuring steps. Static calibration may require a large amount of space. This can be done indoors with minimal background light. The procedure may be done using a scan tool, display menu, or both. -Static Camera Calibration Considerations – to ensure an accurate calibration, the vehicle structure must be restored to proper specifications. A wheel alignment may also be required. Follow all of the steps in the vehicle-specific calibration procedure. This may include ensuring the vehicle is on a level surface, the vehicle is empty, and the tires are the correct size and inflated properly. Some vehicle makers may require the calibration procedure be done only on approved wheel alignment equipment. Static camera calibration procedures often require many steps and require the use of diagnostic tools. -Dynamic (Drive) Camera Calibration – some calibration procedures require the vehicle to be driven, known as a dynamic calibration. During this drive, the scan tool is connected. Follow the instructions in the service information and the scan tool. A dynamic camera calibration procedure typically requires consistent driving conditions for a specified amount of time. Conditions may include maintaining a minimum driving speed on a road with lane markings and fair weather. Avoid rain, fog, or snow, which may block the camera’s view and interfere with aiming. Honda requires both static and dynamic aiming procedures to calibrate the object detection camera on some models. The dynamic aiming procedure must be completed within 30 minutes after the static aiming procedure. -Communication and Documentation – with ADAS becoming more common, collision repair facilities, glass installers, and damage appraisers must be aware of any extra steps involved with windshield replacement or removal. Most object detection cameras require a calibration procedure to ensure they are working properly before returning the vehicle to its owner. The vehicle owner expects all systems to function properly after repairs. The calibration procedure may have to be sublet to a dealership if the calibration tools are only available to them. Include the documentation of all calibration procedures in the repair order. Working Safely Around Hazardous Materials (1/2) Hazardous Materials Overview Hazardous Material Exposure -Acute and Chronic Exposure – over exposure to hazardous materials can be acute, chronic or both acute and chronic -Acute – acute health effects either appear immediately or appear within a few hours of exposure to a hazardous material. Some signs of an acute exposure include: -Burns -Eye irritation -Runny nose -Sore throat or cough -Skin rash or redness -Shortness of breath -Fatigue or dizziness -Nausea, chills, or other flu-like symptoms -Chronic – chronic health effects result from repeated long-term exposure to a hazardous material. Some signs of a chronic exposure include: -Extra sensitivity -Nerve damage -Lower lung capacity -Asthma symptoms -Olfactory or Sensory Fatigue – olfactory or sensory fatigue is a phenomenon that occurs when you are exposed to a chemical for a period of time. You will become unable to smell the chemical or hazardous material after a period of time, even though it is present in the area and over the allowable limit for exposure -Containing a Hazardous Spill – there are certain hazardous materials in the work environment that may end up getting spilled. Certain steps should be taken to contain a hazardous material spill. -Evacuate – you should evacuate the area when necessary. For example, it is necessary to evacuate when noxious odors are present or when it is obvious that the spill cannot be contained to one area -Fire Department – it may be necessary to alert the fire department when a hazardous spill cannot be contained -Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) – put on the proper personal protective equipment or clothing -Absorbent Material – contain the spill by spreading absorbent material on the liquid.
