If necessary, create a berm with the absorbent material to prevent the hazardous liquid from spreading. You may also use an absorbent “snake” to create a portable berm -Sweep Up – once contained and absorbed, you will need to sweep up the absorbent material. Treat the absorbent material as a hazardous waste material by disposing of it in a hazardous waste container. If there is a visible film left on the floor after cleanup, use shop towels to clean it up. Dispose of the towels as if they are a hazardous waste -Routes of Entry to the Body – there are multiple routes for hazardous materials to enter your body. -Breathing – breathing is the most common method of entry for hazardous materials to the body. This is due to how easily and quickly contaminated air can be accidently inhaled -Swallowing – swallowing does not refer to eating or drinking the hazardous materials. It refers to engaging in unsanitary practices such as not washing your hands before eating, drinking, or smoking in an area where hazardous materials are present or stored. Touching your hands to your face when sneezing or coughing also enables exposure -Absorption – absorption through the skin or eyes are also common routes of entry. Skin is porous, like a sponge, which includes the top of the head where chemicals are likely to land. Common ways that hazardous materials are absorbed into the skin or eyes is by picking up a solvent-soaked cloth, washing hands with gasoline or other solvents, and rubbing your eyes -Injection – injection of hazardous materials into your skin includes entering your body through a rash, a cut, or split in your skin. The end of your finger near the fingernail is especially prone to splitting. Materials can be forced through the skin by air tools or cleaning equipment Hazardous Material Categories -Hazardous Materials in a Collision Repair Facility – hazardous materials that may be found in a collision repair facility are grouped into the following categories- -Isocyanates -Bloodborne pathogens -Reactives and flammables -Corrosives -Toxins -Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) -Irritants -Carcinogens -Allergens -Isocyanates – isocyanates are used to cure certain urethane materials, and are sever respiratory, skin, and eye irritants -Catalysts and Hardeners – isocyanates are a common ingredient in many catalysts or hardeners. Catalyzed finishing products include many of the most common refinishing materials. Many adhesives and body fillers are also catalyzed with isocyanate-containing hardeners. Isocyanate vapors are released when mixing, pouring, and applying these products. They are also released when welding a panel with a catalyzed finish residue -Sensitivity – isocyanates can also lead to your skin or lungs becoming extremely sensitive to exposure. Once sensitized, even a vary small exposure can trigger an adverse reaction. If your lungs become sensitized, you can be subject to severe asthma attacks. Symptoms include shortness of breath, coughing, wheezing, and tightness in the chest. Evidence indicates that isocyanate sensitization can also be caused by skin exposure. Sensitized skin reactions may include a rash, itching, hives, and swelling of the arms and legs -Exposure Time – sensitized skin reactions could be immediate or delayed for hours, and sensitization can become permanent. Any worker sensitize to isocyanates should not work with these products. Isocyanates have no taste or smell, making it impossible to determine if a worker is being overexposed. A NIOSH-approved, supplied-air, full-face, or hood respirator that isolates a refinishing technician from the workplace air is the best protection when applying products containing isocyanates -PPE – isocyanates are also released when a product is opened and mixed. Butyl rubber gloves are recommended to prevent skin exposure. Painters goggles are also recommended to prevent or reduce eye exposure. If an isocyanate-containing finish splashes on bare skin, wash the exposed area with soap and water for 15 minutes -Bloodborne Pathogens – bloodborne pathogens include HIV and hepatitis B viruses that may be present in blood stains. Detailing technicians may be at the highest risk for exposure. -Centers for Disease Control (CDC) – according to the Centers for Disease Control (CDC), HIV is detectable by tissue-culture techniques 1-3 days after drying, but the rate of inactivation is rapid. The hepatitis B virus can live at least one week, and possibly up to 30 days in a blood stain outside the body. Following any chance of exposure to bloodborne pathogens, employees are instructed to see a physician. Those at high risk of exposure to bloodborne pathogens should receive further training on avoiding exposure and the required steps to take in the event of exposure -OSHA – bloodborne pathogen training should satisfy the OSHA requirement for standard 29 CFR 1910. 1030. For workplaces exposed to bloodborne pathogens, the OSHA Bloodborne Pathogens standard establishes specific requirements for labeling, containment, and disposal of waste material contaminated with blood. The standard also requires that employers develop and implement a written exposure control plan that includes provisions for hepatitis B vaccinations. This plan must be reviewed and updated annually. More information on bloodborne pathogens can be found on the OSHA standard 29 CR 1910. 1030 OSHA website -Reactives and Flammables – reactives can become unstable or undergo rapid or violent chemical reactions when heated or mixed with other materials. Flammables have a low flash point, and must be kept away from heat sources and open flames. Some common examples include battery acid, anti-corrosion compounds, petroleum-based solvents, glazing putty, adhesion promoters and adhesives, fuels, and most refinish materials -Corrosives – corrosives are either highly acidic or alkaline. Wearing neoprene gloves, a rubber apron, goggles, and a face shield will provide proper personal protection. Some common examples of corrosives include battery (sulfuric) acid (besides being reactive, it is also a corrosive), chemical paint strippers, full-strength degreasers and sodium hydroxide -Neuro or Nerve Toxins – neuro or nerve toxins destroy nerve endings and may cause central nervous system depression. The effects of this are similar to drinking too many alcoholic beverages. Neuro or nerve toxins include toluene, xylene, and methyl-ethyl ketone. These chemicals are present in many solvents, adhesives, and finishes. Read the SDS or product label to see if any of these chemicals are present in the material you are working with. Some common examples include weld-through primers, glazing putty, rubbing compound, some solvents and adhesives, urethanes, clearcoats, buffing compounds, and polishes -Blood Toxins – blood toxins destroy or deform red blood cells, which reduces the amount of oxygen carried through the bloodstream. This can lead to difficulty breathing and unconsciousness. Some common examples include some aerosol cleaners, finish removers, some adhesive cleaners, acrylic enamels, and reducers -Liver Toxins – liver toxins usually enter the body when breathing vapors while pouring, mixing, or applying products. Effects on the body are determined by the length and intensity of exposure to the chemical. These toxins are contained in toluene, xylene, and butyl acetate. Look at the SDS or material label for these chemicals. Some common examples include glass adhesives and seam sealers, brake cleaner and parts cleaning fluid, topcoat additives, reducers, and hardeners -Reproductive Toxins – reproductive toxins may cause birth defects or sterility. Some common examples include gasoline, brake fluid, and any material containing lead, such as solder -Irritants – irritants include skin, eye, and lung irritants. Skin and eye irritants can cause itching, burning, and rashes. Lung irritants include: -Upper respiratory, which affect the nose and throat and are normally not life-threatening -Middle respiratory, which affect the trachea and bronchial parts of the respiratory system; symptoms include coughing and wheezing and are normally not life-threatening -Lower respiratory, which travel deep into the lungs and can be life-threatening -Irritant Materials – some materials contain irritant materials that cause lower respiratory issues. These materials are made up of highly volatile thinners, such as acetone and 1-1-1 trichloroethane. These thinners are highly mobile and can easily penetrate human tissue. The working ingredients in these products are solids. The thinners are added to make other products that are easier to use. Some examples include: -Rubberized undercoatings, weld-through primers, adhesives, seam sealers, welding fumes, and glass adhesives -Glazing putties -Brake fluid, motor oil, gear oil, automatic transmission fluid, and power steering fluid -Carcinogens – certain materials contain known carcinogens (cancer-causing agents). This is a chronic effect of exposure to a chemical. These chemicals are listed by different agencies, such as OSHA, the United States National Toxicology Program (NTP), and the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC). Some common examples include diesel fuel, engine oil, transmission fluid, asbestos (found in some brake pads and shoes), refrigerants, plastic filler dust, rubbing compound, body filler, chromates in some etching primers, chromium in some epoxy primers, and crystalline silica in some primer-surfaces -Allergens – allergens may cause people to react to certain chemicals. Normally, the reaction is not when that person is first exposed to a chemical. The reaction starts with the second or further exposures. It is difficult to know which materials will cause an allergic reaction because each person will have a different reaction to different materials. Some common examples include cleaners, adhesives, resins, hardeners, topcoat additives, polishes, and some dressings Working Safely Around Hazardous Materials (2/2) Personal Protective Equipment Protective Clothing and Eye Protection -Hand, Foot, and Body Protection – hazardous material regulations require employers to provide the proper personal protection where required. Selecting the appropriate protection can be confusing. More information on protective equipment is available from: -SDS information -Product makers -Product and equipment suppliers -Paint distributors and jobbers -Makers of PPE -Note: Equipment and material suppliers may be willing to work with repair facilities to help find equipment that workers are comfortable with and will actually use. The correct choice of protective clothing may be difficult. No one material is resistant to all chemicals, and no glove is completely impenetrable. Proper foot protection throughout the collision repair facility generally includes steel-toed leather shoes or boots with solvent-resistant soles -Hand Protection – types of hand protection used in a collision repair facility include disposable light-duty nitrile gloves for limited tasks where dexterity is required, and medium-duty nitrile gloves, which should be used when working with chemicals, solvents, and adhesives. Medium-duty nitrile gloves can be used multiple times. Neoprene gloves are designed for use with corrosive acids and alkalies. A neoprene glove is also designed for repeat use. Leather or cotton gloves help to protect against cuts and abrasions, and can be used multiple times. Leather gloves also protect against burns when welding -Eye and Face Protection – eye and face protection must be certified by United States standards -American National Standards Institute (ANSI) – United States standards are published by the American National Standards Institute (ANSI). The ANSI symbol or standard number “Z87”must be printed on the eye or face protection equipment. More information on standards and selection of personal protective equipment can be found at the following website: www.ansi.org -Equipment – proper eye protection should be used where it is required. Proper eye and face protection also includes locating eye wash stations where they may be required, such as near the mixing room and detailing bays. Eye or face protection includes safety glasses or goggles. Goggles are recommended whenever working with refinish materials or solvents to prevent liquids splashing into your eyes. You should use a full-face shield when grinding or sanding. You should also wear a welding helmet when welding. Safety glasses should also be worn under the welding helmet -Welding Protection – the welding process involves many safety hazards, including ultraviolet ray exposure. When you are welding it, it is important to wear gloves, safety glasses, a respirator, a welding jacket, shoes, and a welding helmet. To prevent exposure, the following precautions should be taken. -Wear clothing that covers all parts of the body and a dark enough helmet lens shade. -Protect against flash burn to the eyes and skin. Say “cover” before striking an arc to protect those around you who may be in the area -Note: The American Welding Society (AWS) provides a lens shade range for various welding currents. As a starting point, a welding lens shade of 9-10 can be used for low power plasma cutting. A lens shade of 10-11 is recommended for GMA welding. Exposure to ultraviolet rays can also be caused by improper equipment maintenance. Some examples include cracks or scratches in the filter plate, damaged or missing lens gaskets, tears in welding gloves or other protective clothing. Repair or replace damaged equipment, as required Hearing Protection -Decibel Levels – exposure to high levels of noise causes hearing loss and may also cause other harmful health effects. Sound is measured in decibels (dB) -Allowable Sound Levels – the extent of damage depends primarily on the intensity of the noise and duration of the exposure. -Hearing Protection – depending on the location, hearing protection is required when noise exposures being to exceed 85 dB, averaged over 8 working hours, or an 8-hour time-weighted average (TWA). Hearing protection is recommended, but not required, when noise exposures are at 85 dB TWA. If over 90 dB, the average is applied over a shorter time period. The chart shows how the allowable duration decreases as the average noise level increases. Information on dB levels for specific equipment is provided by equipment makers. Having to raise your voice to be heard is a good indication that hearing protection is required. At 85 dB, it is necessary to raise your voice to be heard from 1 foot. You need to shout to be heard from 2 feet -OHSA – if the noise level in a workplace is over 85 dB TWA, OSHA requires establishment of a Hearing Conservation Program in the workplace. OSHA requires this program to include: -The distribution of hearing protection and instruction on how to use it -Identification of tasks that generate loud noise and those areas marked off as requiring hearing protection -Annual hearing tests, or audiograms, for those individuals that work in areas where noise exposures are at or above an 8-hour TWA of 85 dB -Ear Plugs and Ear Muffs – proper hearing protection includes ear plugs and ear muffs. Hearing protection is rated. There will be a noise reduction rating (NRR) for each type of hearing protection. The NRR is a single number description of the noise reduction capability of a hearing protection. The more protection, the higher the number. Makers of hearing protection provide NRR levels Respiratory Protection -Eliminating the Source – the first defense against breathing in contaminants in a collision repair facility is eliminating or minimizing the contaminants at the source. This includes using: -Vacuum sanding systems -Welding fume extractors -Vacuum-recovery media blasting -Downdraft spraybooths -Ventilated paint mixing benches or rooms -Paint mixing room fume extractors -HVLP spray guns -Note: Spray coatings should always be applied inside an approved spraybooth, and never in an open repair facility. Some areas have regulations on spraying materials -Move to Clean Air – in a case where a technician is overcome by fumes: -1) First move the technician outside or to an area of the repair facility that has clean air -2) You should then seek medical help by taking the technician to a medical facility or having a paramedic come to the repair facility -National Insitute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) – the second line of defense against breathing contaminants is a respirator. Any respirator for use in the workplace in North America must be approved by the National Insititue for Occupational Safety and Heatlh (NIOSH). NIOSH is responsible for testing and approving respirators. Respirators approved by NIOSH undergo a testing and certification process. The use of non-approved respirators is a violation of OSHA regulations and could result in citations and fines. Respirators must be used as designed to maintain approved ratings. Note: No modiciations or repairs that alter the respirator should be made -Air-Purifying Respirators – respirators can generally be divided into two types, air purifying or supplied air. Air-purifying respirators perform the following functions: -Filter the contaminated air through filter layers or absorbent cartridgesl and are full- or half-faced -Protect againts either vapors, particulates, or both -Usually have interchangable cartridges and pre-filters, so one respirator can be used to help protect against different respiratory hazards -Are considered negative-pressure, because the user must inhale to pull in the filtered air. This puts more pressure outside the mask than there is inside the mask, making it especially important that there are no that there are no leaks, since contaminated air enters through the path of least resistance. A battery-powered, air-purifying respirator is considered a positive-pressure, air-purifying respirator -May be disposable, for one-time use -Using cartridges which may have an expiration date. These dates must be calculated using air sample information and the manufacturer’s data. The cartridges are changed on or before the expiration date. -Note: Assistance on selecting the proper air-purfying respirator or cartridge for a given contaminant is available from makers of respirator equipment, chemical makers, suppliers, and jobbers. Nort, MSA, and 3M have an outline respirator change-out program that is available free of charge -Choosing the Proper Air-Purifying Respirator – when choosing the proper air-purifying respirator, it may be necessary to distinguish between particulates and vapors -Particulates – particulates are solid or liquid particles suspended in the air and include: -Fumes, which are the solid particulates given off by welding -Dusts, which are solid particulates from sanding or grinding -Dust particulates, which are larger than fume particulates -Mists, which are the liquid particulates when spraying -Dust/Mist Masks – layers of filtered material are required to filter out particulates. Dust/mist masks are used only for large particulates that can be seen, such as dust. They do not protect against hazardous contaminants because they are generally loose-fitting, and it is difficult to achieve a proper face seal -Vapors – vapors are created by the evaporation of liquids. Organic vapors are formed when a solvent or paint evaporates. Vapors require an absorbent material, which is usually activated carbon contained in a cartridge. The carbon in a filter is used to absorb vapors. Most vapor-cartridge respirators include a pre-filter for protection against both vapors and particulates. Paint spray, for example, is a combination of particulates and vapors -Color Codes – cartridge-type filters are color-coded to indicate the type of material each is designed to filter. These color codes have been set by NIOSH. For example, cartridges with a black color code are designed to protect against organic vapors. Purple or pink color codes are highyl efficient for protection against particulates. These filters used to be referred to as high-efficiency particular air (HEPA) filters. This designation is no longer used, though the filter still meets the filtration criteria for a HEPA filter. A yellow color code is a combination filter designed to protect against acid gases and organic vapors -Particulate Filter Classifications – NIOSH has developed a classification system for particulate filters -Particulate filter Efficency -the NIOSH system identifies a series of three particulate filters and rates them based on filtration efficiency. The three particulate filters include: -100-rated filters (99.97% efficiency), this filter offers similar protection to HEPA filters -99-rated filters (99% efficiency) -95-rated filters (95% efficiency) -Particulate Filter Categories – each are divided into three categories: -N for non-oil resistant -R for resistant to oil -P for oil-proof -Note: In some paint and body applications, a 95% efficient particulate filter is adequate. Oil-based products are generally not used, so a non-oil resistant (N) filter or oil-resistant ® filter is adequate. Currently, there are no specific guidelines for the selection of the appropriate efficiency rating -Particulate Filter Use – HEPA or 100-rated filters are used when working with asbestos, when welding toxis metals such as galvanized steel, and when buffing with products that contain silica. N95 filters can be used for general welding. A 100 filter can be used for general welding, but breathing resistance is higher. There is also a potential for exposure to hazardous vapors during some welding operations, which would require an air-purifying respirator that absorbers vapors. For welding, a supplied-air welding helmet would be the best protection. The hazards are reduced by removing coatings from surfaces that are to be welded -Choosing a Respirator – choosing the type of respirator to wear depends on the material being used. The first step is to check the SDS. The SDS might not be available for a particular material. For example, it may be necessary to sand or grind the finish off a part that has an unknown coating. In that case, use common sense and good judgement. You cannot be over-protected. A HEPA or 100-type cartridge respirator may be the first choice if there are unknown particulate fumes -Storing Air-Purifying Respirators – air-purifying respirators with cartridges must be stored away from any contaminated area and areas with high humidity and temperature extremes. The best storage is the airtight plastic bag that comes with most respirators. This is done primarily to prevent moisture contamination. Moisture will shorten the life of a charcoal-filled vapor cartridge. Note: Moisture will shorten the life of a charcoal-filled vapor cartridge -Supplied-Air Respirators – supplied-air respirators have an outside source of fresh, breathable air. Supplied-air respirators are primarily used when finishing in a spraybooth, but they are also required in environments that are oxygen-deficient, considered immediately threatening to your life or health, or contain unknown contaminants or unknown levels of contaminants. Areas that contain airborne concentrations of contaminants above the maximum use concentrations of air-purifying respirators also require supplied-air respirators. Areas that contain contaminants with poor warning properties, such as isocyanates, do as well. An exception to this is if there is a formal schedule in place to change air-purifying respirator cartridges -Grade D Specifications – there are currently nine grades of air classified by the United States Compressed Gas Association. The three highest grades are for use in medical facilities. A supplied-air respirator system must supply Grade D air, which is the fourth highest. Grade D air currently has requirements that call for oxygen content of 19.5%-23.5%. other requirements include: -Condensed oil content of less than 5 mg/m3, CO (carbon monoxide) of less than 10 ppm -The CO must always be monitored on a supplied air breathing system -CO2 (carbon dioxide) of less than 1000 ppm -Air Sources – air can be supplied by a compressor or an air pump -Compressor as a Source – one source of air for a respirator is an air compressor fitted with approved filters. When using an oil-lubricated compressor to supply Grade D air, the CO level must be monitored. This can be done using a high-temperature or carbon monoxide alarm, or both. If only a high-temperature alarm is used, the air supply must be monitored at enough interveals to prevent CO in the breathing air from exceeding 10 ppm. Proper maintenance of the compressor is also required. In the United States, the air must be sampled and analzyed before it reaches the end user. A multiple-stage filter is generally used to remove oil, water, hydrocarbons, and odors. A concern that an overheated compressor could burn oil and generate CO is the reason for the monitoring equipment. Another source of CO in an air line is the location of the compressor intake -Air Pump as a Source – using an air pump as the air source generally consists of an oil-free, electric, rotary-vane motor, filters, and a hose connection. The air pump intake must be located in a clean air environment, usually outside waya from any exhaust vents. This is necessary because an air pump does not purify the air of solvents, thinners, and other hazardous chemicals found in a repair facility environment. An air pump only moves air from one source to another. Advantages to using an air pump include portability (it can be placed anywhere electricity is available), it uses its own air (there is no worry about using up the repair facility’s compressor capacity to supply breathing air), and low maintenance -Protection from Isocyanates – full-face, supplied-air respirators or hoods are recommended when applying finishes that contain isocyanates. -Protect Information – information regarding protection from isocyanates is indicated in government information, product maker lierature, and SDS information. But there is an alternative, if certain conditions are met. As an alternative to formal change-out schedules, painters can use respiratory cartridges equipped with end-of-service-life indicators that provide users a visual sign, such as a color change, indicating when the filter should be changed. ESLls have not yet been approved to detect the type of isocyanates used in refinishing materials. Therefore, a calculated change-out schedule should be used. Still another alternative is a disposable air-purifying cartridge respirator for one-time use. The reason for these extra precautions is because isocyanates do not have good warning properties. An organic vapor cartridge could be used to capacity without warning. Since isocyanates can enter the body through the eyes, hoods or full-face shields are recommended for protection. Safety glasses or prescription eyewear do no offer enough protection for refinishers. If a half-face, supplied-air respirator is all that is available, wear goggles -OSHA – the revised OSHA respiratory protection standard allows the use of an air-purifying respirator when applying an isocyanate coating, but only if the following two requirements are met: -1) The employer ensures that the spraybooth is functioning properly and is properly maintained -2) The employer implements a formal respiratory cartridge change-out schedule to ensure that painters change organic vapor cartridges before the cartridges become saturated with contaminants -Respirator Seal – half-face air-purifying respirators must have a proper seal. This requires male respirator wearers to be clean shaven, at least where the respirator seals against the face. An employee should not be allowed to do a task that requires respiratory protection if he refuses to shave to allow a tight respirator seal until a full-face respirator is made available. An employee must also not be allowed to do a task that requires respiratory protection if he or she refuses to wear a respirator. Regulations regarding how to handle an employee with facial hair are NIOSH recommendations that OSHA has adopted -Medical Approved – certain information is needed for a physican to approve a respirator for a technician. -Medical Approval – the physician or other licensed healthcare provider must be provided the following information -Type and weight of the respirator to be used by the employee -Duration and frequency of respirator use -Expected physical work effort -Additional protective clothing and equipment to be worn -Temperature and humidity extremes that may be present -Copy of the written respirator program -Note: If a questionnaire is used, the employer may have to provide a follow-up medical examination, based on the questionnaire responses to the employee’s history of medical conditions. The follow-up examination must include any medical tests, consultations, or procedures the physician or healthcare provider considers necessary. The employee must be provided the opportunity to discuss the questionnaire or examination with the physican or other licensed healthcare provider. If the medical examination shows that the employees helth would be at risk by using a negative pressure respirator, the employer is required to provide a powered air-purifying respirator or supplied-air respirator -Additional Medical Evaluations – additional medical evaluations are required if: -The employee reports medical signs or symptoms related to the ability to use a respirator -The physician, licensed healthcare provider, supervisor, or respirator program administrator informs the employer that the employee neds to be re-evaluated -Observations during fit-testing or other respirator program evaluation shows a need for re-evaluation -A change occurs in the workplace conditions or employees responsibility that will change the respirator use -Respirator Fit-Testing – -Respirators with a face seal must be properly fit-tested before using the respirator on the job. OSHA states fit-testing must be done: -Annually -Whenever a different respirator facepiece is used -If there is a change in the employees physical condition which could affect respirator fit. Examples include dental work, significant weight change, or facial scars -Fit-testing not only determines whether the respirator face seal is adequate, it also: -Provides an opportunity to check the the respirator fit is comfortable -Allows the employee to choose another, more comfortable respirator if desired -Reinforces respirator training, such as how to put on and adjust the respirator -Partially fulfills some of the annual respirator training requirements -Respirator Program – United States regulatinos require employers to develop and implement a written respiratory protection program. -Program Written Documentation – United States regulations require employers to develop and implement a written respiratory protection program. The program must be updated as often as necessary to reflect the changes in workplace conditions that affect respirator use. Items that must be put into writing include: -Worksite-specific procedures that require a respirator -The criteria for selecting a respirator for use in the workplace -Medical evaulations or questionnaire results of employees required to use respirators -Fit-testing procedures for air-purifying respirators with a face seal -Procedures for proper use of respirators in routine and emergency situations -Other Written Requirements – other written requirements for a respirator program include procedures and schedules for cleaning, disinfecting, storing, inspecting, repairing, discarding, and otherwise maintaining respirators. They also include an air-purifying cartridge change-out schedule, procedures on how the quality, quantity, and flow of breathing air is being maintained for supplied-air respirators. They should include records of training employees in the respiratory hazards to which they are potentially exposed during routine and emergency situations. They should include records of training of employees in the proper use of respirators, including putting on respirator program for effectiveness. For areas of the repair facility where respirator use is not required, employers still must have a situation where a written program is not required is where a dust mask is the only type of mask used. Respirators, training, and medical evaluations must be provided by the employer at no cost to the employee. A program administrator must be designed to administer or oversee the respiratory protection program, and conduct the required evaluations of the program’s effectiveness. The administrator must be qualified by the proper training or experience Workplace Safety Repair Facility Safety Work and Tool Safety -Facility Safety Equipment – todays collision repair facility has many different types of safety equipment available to the staff. For this equipment to be effective, its important that all employees have a clear understanding of where this equipment is located and how to use the equipment. Such equipment includes eye wash stations, fire extinguishers, and emergency shutoff switches. In addition, knowing where emergency exits are located is also important -Safety in the Repair Facility – solids, liquids, and gases can all be dangerous if handled unsafely. Some examples of equipment or situations that can cause injury include: -Exposed fuel tanks or containers -Compressed natural gas (CNG) -Handling or transporting glass using suction cups -An exposed or operating engine -Working with batteries -Welding -Safe Work Practices with Fuel – -Working Around Fuel – whenever working with or around fuel, refer to those safe work practices and: -DO keep fuel and fuel tanks away from sparks and other potential sources of ignition -DO have a proper fire extinguisher available -DO NOT turn ON ignition or crank an engine with a fuel line disconnected -DO NOT prime engine with fuel while cranking the engine -DO relieve fuel pressure before performing any engine repairs -DO work in a well-ventilated area -DO NOT use as a cleaning agent -DO identify air conditioning and fuel rail access ports before attaching test equipment -DO post and comply with “No Smoking” signs in the area -Fuel Containers – when working around fuel containers: -Store fuel only in UL-approved containers -DO NOT fill containers completely with liquid fuel; leave about 1 inch for expansion -If filled containers must be transported, make sure they are secured to prevent tipping -DO NOT store a partially filled container for long periods of time -Never leave containers open after using the container -Use a ground strap to limit the chance of static electricity causing a fire -Note: Gasoline MUST only be stored in an approved red container -Alternative Fuels – when working near compressed fuel vehicles, such as CNG or liquefied petroleum gas (LPG): -Turn off the gas supply at the tank, and run the engine until it stops. Then, disconnect the battery before welding, grinding, or cutting on a vehicle -Remove LPG tanks before moving the vehicle into a spraybooth -Release CNG before moving the vehicle into a spraybooth. Empty the cylinder by opening the manual release valve before bringing the vehicle inside the repair facility -Wear proper hearing and eye protection, as the releasing pressure sound could cause hearing damage and flying objects could cause an eye injury -Note: When servicing a CNG-powered vehicle, do not operate infrared heaters (IR) that are suspended from the ceiling. Open any ceiling vents -Engine Compartment – when working around an engine compartment – -Wear proper eye and skin protection -Avoid long hair, loose clothing, and jewelry, these can easily get caught in moving parts -Avoid touching hot engine parts -Avoid breathing exhaust fumes -Vent exhaust fumes outside of the repair facility -Keep hands away from fan blades and other moving parts, as an electric engine cooling fan may start at any time -Remove the ignition key to prevent accidental starting of the engine -Engage the parking brake and use wheel chocks to prevent the vehicle from moving when working under the hood or underneath the vehicle -Use safety props when working under hoods and deck lids -Automotive Batteries – when working around automotive batteries: -Wear proper eye and skin protection -DO NOT smoke, or have open flames or sparks near a battery -DO NOT remove vent caps from maintenance-free batteries -DO NOT charge or jump-start a frozen battery. If charging, ensure that the battery charger is turned off before the clamps are connected or disconnected -Read the markings on the battery to determine the polarity of the terminals -Ensure that all electrical loads are off before disconnecting or reconnecting battery terminals, and always disconnect and isolate the negative (ground) cable first, and reconnect the ground cable last -DO NOT over charge or exceed charge rate of the battery, verify in service information -Parts Handling and Storage – for proper parts handling and storage: -Wear appropriate PPE when handling parts -Store parts in a safe area, not inside the vehicle -It’s not advised to store heavy parts high up -Care will need to be taken when moving dangerous parts such as glass or sharp objects -Note: Never carry glass under your arm or over your head. Hold the glass with your palms outward so that it can only fall away from you -Welding Hazards – the welding process involves many safety hazards -Welding Fumes – by breathing welding fumes, symptoms similar to a 24-hour flu may occur within 4-8 hours of exposure. Methods of protecting from welding fumes include: -Wearing a respirator, such as a supplied-air welding helmet, or by having a source of fresh air to breathe -Using a fume extractor, such as the one shown in the image -Having a fan, or other source, for circulating the air -Welding Electrical Shock – to avoid electrical shock from a welding machine -Configure the welding machines according to the equipment makers instructions, and ensure proper grounding connections are used -Keep cables, wires, and connections in good condition -Do not place the welding machine in a wet place, or stand in a wet place when welding -Do not use the welding machine as a table for beverages Storage Regulations -Storage Regulations – proper storage for hazardous materials include: -A neat and orderly cabinet with doors that can be kept closed -Keeping away from sunlight or extreme conditions -a well-ventilated area -Proper labels or signs -Meeting federal and local requirements -Keeping away from fuse panels -Container Requirements – proper containers for hazardous materials must: -Be in good condition -Be sealed or covered when not in use -Contain proper labeling -Have a secondary curb or tray, if required -Grounding Requirements – some containers must be grounded to eliminate the chance of sparks. This includes; -Cabinets with flammable materials -Containers used when transferring flammable materials -Storage drums -Paint mixing tables -Flammables and Combustibles – materials that display the flammable pictogram must adhere to specific regulations. This includes having: -Explosion-proof electrical equipment -“NO SMOKING” signs posted -Proper venting on tanks and the room -Fire extinguishers nearby Repair Facility Maintenance and Emergency Handling -Maintenance and Emergencies – poor maintenance can result in equipment breakdown and accidents. Emergencies are best handled when there is a plan in place to handle spills, fires, evacuations, etc. Every work place is required to have these plans in writing -Areas of Possible Emergencies – emergencies at a collision repair facility can take place anywhere and when using any tool. Some of the more probable areas include: -Hydraulic lines used on straightening equipment -Solvent Recyclers -Lifts -Paint mixing room -Compressor unit or lines/hoses -Spraybooth -Office -Hydraulics – safety considerations for hydraulic power systems include: -Clearly identifying pressure lines; these lines must be protected from damage -Working with pressures regulated within specified limits -Emergency stops being fully visible and operational -Making safety cables or chains available -Having an inspection and maintenance schedule for pulling clamps, chains, etc. -Solvent Recyclers – solvent recycling systems heat and distill used solvents -Solvent Recycler – an equipment makers recommendations for inspecting and maintaining this equipment may require: -An annual oil change -An annual gasket replacement -Dust removal from the condenser coils -Note: Do not use a spray gun cleaner as a recycler for leftover paint materials -Solvent Recycler Sludge – depending on the materials being recycled, and local and regional codes, the leftover solvent-recycler sludge may be: -Collected in a closed drum and removed by a waste hauler -Subject to a one-time Toxic Characteristic Leaching Procedure (TCLP) test to determine if it must be treated as hazardous waste -Dried and disposed of as non-hazardous waste -Areas of Possible Emergencies – -Vehicle Lifts – safety requirements for vehicle lifts include identifying load capacity, which must not be exceeded, having all safety guards in place, limit stops working properly, hand and foot controls being operational, operators being properly trained in the use of equipment, and scheduling periodic equipment inspections. If the lift operates erratically, it should not be used -Mixing Room – a paint mixing room should provide a safe, clean, well-lit area for a refinisher to store and mix refinishing materials. The mixing room: -Should be located near the spraybooth -Must be constructed to meet building, fire, and electrical codes -Should have sufficient ventilation, or fume extractors installed -Spraybooth – spraybooths must have proper airflow to quickly remove vapors, fumes, and paint overspray. Spraybooth maintenance will help ensure proper operation. Maintenance may include; changing filters according to the spraybooth makers recommendations, checking the spraybooth pressure daily, cleaning air hoses frequently, preferably daily, cleaning and adjusting spraybooth burners to give the proper flame at maximum efficiency, and keeping the spraybooth clean of any dust, dirt, and debris. A spraybooth is not an emission control device. The spraybooth filters are designed to capture solid particulates only. Compliance with rules for VOCs may require the exhaust air to be treated with equipment installed outside the spraybooth. Another method of reducing VOC emissions is using waterborne or high-solids refinishing materials. Maintaining a water-wash spraybooth filter system requires checking the pump motors, spray nozzles, and filter tanks regularly, and treating the water with proper chemicals to prevent algae growth or other problems -Floors – some floor safety requirements include; keeping the surface clean, providing a non-slip surface, where required, and using floor drains that must include grates. Another requirement may include being connected to a holding tank, grease trap, or sump which pumps to a holding tank. Note: Frame rack surfaces are especially slippery if dust is present. Also, oil on most surfaces can be slippery -Compressor – regularly checking and maintaining the shop compressor is required for proper operation. Air compressors can start at any time when the power is on. Ensure equipment is powered off before conducting maintenance to avoid injury. This process includes following the equipment makers recommendations for draining fluid from the air tank, receivers, dryers, and filters. It also includes checking and filling the oil reservoir, cleaning the air strainers, and cleaning or blowing dust off the fins of cylinders, heads, intercoolers, aftercoolers, and any other parts that collect dust or dirt. Other compressor cleaning and maintenance steps include checking the oil filter/separator in the air line, checking the tightness of the drive belts, checking for air leaks on the compressor and piping system, and checking the automatic tank drain, and listening for unusual noises -Written Emergency Plan – United States regulations require every workplace to have a written emergency plan, appropriate to the hazards in the workplace. The plan must include: -Emergency conditions that may arise within the workplace and from nearby workplaces -An evacuation plan -Spill cleanup procedures -Assigned rescue and medical duties -Training procedures -Written Evacuation Plan – -Steps in the evacuation part of the emergency plan should have written procedures to: -Notify workers of the nature and location of the emergency -Evacuate workers safely -Check and confirm the safe evacuation of all workers -Part of the written evacuation plan should also include: -Getting the applicable Safety Data Sheet(s) (SDS) for the hazardous materials involved -Notifying the fire department or other emergency responders -Notifying adjacent workplaces or residents that may be affected -Note: Practice meeting in a safe location outside the facility at a central point. Assign one or more workers the responsibility for ensuring that everyone is present at the meeting place following an emergency -Hazardous Material Spill Safety Procedures – there are specific procedures to follow depending on the type of hazardous material spill -Non-Containable Spill – if the spill cannot be contained: -Report the spill immediately to the local fire department or other authorities -Evacuate all personnel from the area -Have the SDS available for cleanup personnel -Dry Hazardous Spill – if a dry hazardous spill occurs: -Use a HEPA-filtered vacuum to collect fine dust, such as dust from fiberglass and asbestos -If a vacuum is not available, lightly wet the dust with water and sweep it up -Do not use compressed air to blow the dust -Materials used to clean up the spill must be disposed of as hazardous waste Workplace Education -Hazardous Materials Education Responsibilities – three groups share in the responsibility of providing hazardous materials education: -Makers or suppliers -Employers -Employees -Supplied Responsibilities – education on hazardous materials begins with the supplier. The supplier is required to: -Classify and label supplied materials -Provide a current SDS for every hazardous materials used in a workplace -Provide, at the request of an inspector, the source for any data about the toxicity of a material -Employer Responsibilities – employers are required to have procedures established for the safe use, handling, storage, and disposal of hazardous materials. This includes personal protective equipment. An employer is also required to enforce safe work practices for use, handling, storage, and disposal of chemicals. Employers are also required to provide: -Labels for all unmarked containers of hazardous materials -An SDS for every material -A Right-To-Know training program -Employee Responsibilities – to get the most out of a hazardous materials program, employees should: -Work with the employer to follow the hazardous materials program -Ensure all required labels are on the appropriate containers -Follow directions on supplier and workplace labels, and do not remove, deface, or change labels -Follow procedures for safe use, storage, handling, and disposal of hazardous materials -Know the location of, how to use, and review the SDS -Know emergency procedures involving a hazardous material -Inform the employer if a material lacks hazard information -Wear the required personal protective equipment -Work with the employer on developing a safety program -Posting Requirements – employers are required to provide signage in the workplace regarding: -Emergency telephone numbers -Exit signs -Signs prohibiting eating, smoking, or drinking, where appropriate -Safety and health posters -Hazardous Materials Inventory List – -The employer is also responsible for taking inventory of all hazardous materials and keeping an inventory list. There are two types of lists that can be developed. -A complete list of all materials used in the workplace -A list of materials used in specific work areas -Either list is acceptable and must include -The material name -Where the chemical is used -Quantity of hazardous material -Who made the list and the date of creation or revision -The area of the facility the list refers to -Inventories can be done by: -Counting materials -Referring to purchase invoices -Looking at the SDS sheets -Written Hazardous Communication Program – each workplace is responsible for starting and maintaining a written hazardous communication program for all employees who work with hazardous materials. The program must include: -A list of the hazards of the materials in the work area -Methods used to inform employees of the hazards of non-routine tasks, such as changing brake fluid or recharging an air conditioning system -Methods for labeling using an “other means of identification” system, where this is used -Methods used by the employer to ensure the proper SDS copies are accessible in the work areas -Hazardous Material Training – employers are required to provide training on hazardous materials. This training must include: -Methods that be used to detect the presence or release of a hazardous materials in the work area -The physical and health hazards of the materials in the work area -Measures employees can take to protect themselves from the hazards -The information included in the hazardous communication program developed by the program Writing the Estimate How to Create an Accurate Estimate Preliminary Inspection -Course Overview – using information from the damage analysis courses and Basic Estimate Writing, this course looks at the physical process of reviewing a damaged vehicle and writing N estimate. The major steps in writing an estimate are to: -Obtain preliminary information such as vehicle, customer, and insurance information, and ask the customer about the collision -Review the damage -Record details about the repair in terms of labor operations, labor times, part types, refinishing operations, and miscellaneous repair expenses -Review the estimate with the vehicle owner and obtain repair authorization -Information Gathering – before the vehicle inspection, review the loss details in the claim file or assignment. When possible, it is a good idea to consult with the owner as well. A customer consultation can provide valuable information regarding the loss such as: -Details about the accident such as speed, direction of impact, road conditions, and which seatbelts were in use -Observations since the accident such as noises, pulling, drivability, malfunction indicator lamps (MILs), and accessories that no longer work -Temporary repairs that were done after the collision -Pre-existing damage -Note: Some of this information can be valuable when looking for signs of secondary damage resulting from energy transfer -Where to Start – it is important to have a consistent routine for writing estimates. This includes establishing a sequence for capturing vehicle identification information and a standard approach to inspecting a vehicle. An example inspection procedure may include starting with identifying the vehicle. This includes recording the vehicle identification number (VIN), license number, production date, and paint and trim codes. Next, complete an initial vehicle walkaround. Begin your damage inspection starting with the primary area of damage and thoroughly inspecting one part group at a time. The sequence in which you choose to complete your estimate is not as important as establishing a routine that you follow for every vehicle inspection. This will help to ensure all vehicle inspections result in a complete, thorough, and accurate estimate of repairs -Vehicle Walkaround – starting with a vehicle walkaround allows you to consider information obtained earlier regarding the facts of loss as well as the observations provided from the vehicle owner. Your initial walkaround should include looking for structural damage indicators and identifying direct and indirect damage. Also look for signs of previous repairs and pre-existing damage. When pre-existing damage is identified, complete a fully itemized estimate for these repairs using the pre-existing damage function within your estimating system. This allows you to document the damage without including it in the claim-related damage estimate -Approaching a Damaged Electric or Other Alternative Fuel Vehicle – when approaching an electric vehicle or other alternative fuel vehicle, be aware that the collision may have comprised alternative fuel safety systems and present a potential hazard. When working with an electric vehicle, always assume that the high-voltage (HV) circuit is energized even though steps have been taken to disable the system. After a collision, anything is possible. Do not wear metallic objects or jewelry. Do not have metal objects, such as mechanical pencils or tools, in your pockets. A metallic object may fall from a pocket onto an HV part or connection and cause a short circuit. Make sure your skin is not wet and there is no moisture present on or around the vehicle -Damage Inspection – if the estimate is being written at a collision repair facility, the next step is full disassembly and blueprinting of the vehicle. However, for a field estimate, it may be necessary to complete an estimate of visible damage followed by a supplemental inspection after the vehicle has been disassembled. When performing your damage inspection, remember to work with one part group/system at a time, carefully inspecting all parts of that group/system before moving on to the next. Take all required photos such as four corners, VIN, and license plate before beginning the disassembly process. Then, take photos to document the damage identified during the disassembly and blueprinting process -Thorough Damage Inspection – using the blueprinting process provides a thorough and complete damage inspection. Some examples of tasks that should be performed during the blueprinting process include: -Disassembling and inspecting of all damaged areas -Verifying gaps prior to opening doors, hoods, deck lids, and hatches. Opening these closure panels that have no clearance can cause additional damage -Trying all electrical systems (lights, horn, radio, door switches, and climate control) -Documenting MILs illuminated on the instrument panel -Raising the vehicle for inspection if underbody damage is suspected -Performing point-to-point measurements to verify structural or suspension damage -Reviewing the pre-health scan to identify any trouble codes and necessary repairs -Reviewing vehicle maker repair procedures to identify mandatory items such as one-time use fasteners and calibration or initialization requirements -How Previous Repairs Affect the Estimate – when performing vehicle inspections, it is important to look for signs of previous repairs that could have an impact on repair decisions for the current damages. For example, if previous repairs have been performed on a panel that is also part of the current damage, it is possible the combination of prior repairs and current damage will make replacing the panel the best decision. Previous repairs are often indicated by plug welds, flange variations, or a thicker finish that requires identification using a film-thickness gauge. You should also look for corrosion that may complicate the repair, and/or refinish process, making panel replacement the best decisions -Repair vs Replace – when evaluating a damaged panel, the decision to repair or replace is often a complex one. There are several items that need to be considered, such as panel repairability and cost-effective repairs -Repairability – for example, with regard to repairability, a panel may look repairable and could actually be repaired. But due to the location and type of damage, the long-term durability of the repair is a concern. Therefore, the panel should be replaced -Cost-Effective – an example of a cost-effective repair decision may be a panel that is repairable, but the expense of the panel makes replacing the panel the best choice. One consideration may be that a part is on backorder and not available. After factoring in the additional expense for a rental vehicle and the inconvenience of customers being without their vehicle, the more cost-effective decision may be to repair the panel. -Additional Input – when completing your damage inspection and making repair and replace decisions, there is often damage that requires additional input. For example, a panel may appear repairable, but the location of the damage may make the repair difficult, or a vehicle with a custom finish may require additional repair time or materials to duplicate the finish. In these cases, it is good practice to consult with a repair technician or production manager to discuss alternatives that ensure you select the best option for a complete, safe, and quality repair -Sublet Repairs – when writing for sublet repairs, it is important to contact the sublet vendor to confirm their availability. Finding a sublet vendor that can fit seamlessly into the repair schedule helps ensure cycle time is not disrupted. When contacting the vendor, provide the details of the repairs to be sublet so accurate pricing can obtained. This will ensure the estimate is written as accurate as possible -Glass Repairs – when replacing, consider that not all glass is the same for each model of vehicle. You must check the vehicle options. For example, vehicles equipped with a head-up display (HUD) may require a special type of windshield. Installing the wrong type of windshield may result in a double or distorted HUD image. Other common information required when ordering glass replacement or service include (if equipped): -Acoustic glass -Advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) such as rain detection, lane departure warning sensors, or cameras -Heated glass or heated wipers -Window tint, which may require you to identify between aftermarket and factory window tinting -The frit, which is the black band around the perimeter of the stationary glass Estimate Preparation Considerations -General Considerations – when performing damage inspections, there are some additional items to keep in mind including: Overhaul, Sectioning, OEM service requirements -Overhaul – when repairs require replacement of multiple parts of an assembly, the estimating database may offer an overhaul labor operation. There are efficiencies gained by replacing multiple parts of an assembly at the same time rather than individual times for each part of the assembly. Commonly overhauled assemblies include: Bumper assemblies, suspension systems, steering columns. -Sectioning – when evaluating part replacement, partial replacement at factory seams or sectioning may be an option. This is generally a less intrusive repair and may require less disassembly of the vehicle. Always refer to vehicle maker service information to determine if there is a sectioning procedure, or if the part is supplied as a partial assembly. If the vehicle maker does not provide a procedure, the part should not be sectioned. -OEM Service Requirements – when preparing an estimate, it is important to reference vehicle maker service information to identify: -Types of construction material and locations to confirm repairability -Required supplemental restraint system (SRS) part inspection and replacement following a restraint system deployment -One-time use fasteners -Required SRS and ADAS calibrations -Note: Remember that late model vehicles are equipped with many integrated SRS and ADAS that may have very specific service requirements. Identifying these requirements during the estimating process helps ensure a complete and accurate repair plan -Front-End Damage Inspection Considerations – when preparing an estimate on a vehicle with front-end collision damage, inspection items often include ADAS, lighting, the colling system, the A/C system, and steering and suspension. In some cases, your estimating system may provide a reminder of these considerations -ADAS – many late model vehicles have radar sensors and cameras mounted in the grille and bumper area. These require inspection and calibration even if the repair only involves R&amp;I of system parts. For example, if collision repairs require R&amp;I of the windshield, a calibration of the forward-facing camera system may be required after the windshield is reinstalled -Lighting – collision repairs might include remove and install, called R&amp;I, or remove and replace, called R&amp;R. with front lamps, there are some items that should be added to the estimate. Include aiming of the headlamps following installation and calibration. Also, include initialization requirements for vehicles equipped with ADAS such as adaptive headlamps, high beam assist, and night vision -Cooling – when parts of a cooling system must be replaced, such as a radiator or damaged hose, the system may need to be drained, refilled, and bled. Often the estimating software will automatically add these operations to the estimate, but be sure to verify it is an included operation. This should be done each time a cooling part is R&amp;R’d or R&amp;I’d. sometimes a system must be topped off with coolant after it is refilled, even if the coolant is captured and recycled. You may need to add this to the estimate. If there is no coolant in the system, be sure coolant has been added to the estimate. Note that not all vehicles use the same coolant/antifreeze. In some instances, special coolant may be required by the vehicle maker. It will be necessary to reference OEM service information to identify the correct product to be used -A/C Considerations – if there is damage to an A/C system and the system remains charged, you must include evacuation and recharging the system on the estimate. If the damage to the A/C system caused refrigerant to escape, it will be necessary to re-charge the system with refrigerant and compressor oil as appropriate. Also add this to the estimate. It is also important to remember that A/C systems that have been open may require replacement of the receiver dryer or accumulator as a result of moisture absorber -Steering and Suspension – when replacing steering and suspension components, such as struts, inner and outer tie rods, steering rack, or steering knuckle, it will be necessary to perform a wheel alignment. Some estimating systems may prompt you to add a wheel alignment to your estimate, but in some cases it may require manual selection of a wheel alignment. Note that in many cases, the steering angle sensor (SAS) will require calibration when performing a wheel alignment -Doors and Mirrors – considerations when estimating damage to doors and mirrors include: -Door mirrors may include side view cameras used for several ADAS systems -Doors may contain SRS parts, such as impact sensors and airbags -Note: It is important to remember some ADAS or SRS may require calibration following R&amp;I or R&amp;R of system-related parts. Note that it may be necessary to R&amp;I all interior door trim to access exterior trim parts such as mirrors, handles, lock cylinders, and belt moldings. Add these considerations to the estimate, as needed -Welded Panel Repairs – when replacing a weld-on panel there may be additional repairs required that are not included in base labor allowances. For example, when replacing a rear body panel, it may be necessary to repair “weld zone” damages resulting from normal cutting, welding, and grinding procedures. It is important to reference procedure page (P-page) information for the estimating system you are using to identify included and non-included labor operations -Panel Repair and Corrosion Protection – if a welding process was used, either for panel replacement or panel straightening there will be heat damage to the corrosion protection on the backside of the panel which must be restored to ensure the durability of the repair. Restoration of this corrosion protection is often overlooked but must be addressed with rust preventative products such as cavity wax or other corrosion preventative products -Including Finishes on the Estimate – identifying the finish type is required when preparing an estimate. Begin by determining the paint code, which will indicate if the color is a basecoat/clear coat, multi-stage, or other type of finish. The estimating system will make adjustments to the way the refinish times are calculated based on this information. Once you know the paint code, you can determine additional refinish operations that may be required. Some examples of these may be color-matching or correcting surface conditions such as chips, scratches, or corrosion. It is also important to identify any trim that requires R&amp;I for the refinish process on your estimate -Materials – when writing an estimate, note that the repair and refinish process requires many different materials including: -Surface cleaners, sanding products, tape, and paint -Noise, vibration, and harshness (NVH materials such as foam or sound-deadening material -Seam sealers -Chip-resistant coatings -Note: Charges for refinish materials are typically an automatic calculation done by the estimating system based on the refinish labor and “paint and material” rate. This is established in your estimating system profile. Items such as NVH materials, seam sealers, and chip-resistant coatings are typically not included in the refinish materials calculation. If appropriate, you may need to add them from the parts code table or as manual entries -Hazardous Waste Disposal – another expense typically added to a damage estimate is a hazardous waste disposal or Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) charge. Waste fluids such as paints, solvents, thinner, antifreeze, and oil will typically require hazardous waste disposal. A hazardous waste disposal charge may be a pre-determined figure or calculated when creating the estimate Writing the Estimate -Knowing your Estimating System – when all damage inspections and research have been completed, its time to start the estimate-writing process. As you create the estimate, remember that each time you make an entry, the database is masking additional decisions in the background. This is based on many factors including vehicle identification information, P-page information, and the operations selected. It is also important to have a strong understanding of how your estimating system is designed – “Inside-Out” or “Outside-In”. for example, when replacing a hinge pillar using an inside-out system, the estimating database labor allowance will likely include R&amp;I of the door and hinges. However, when using an outside-in system, the labor allowance for replacing the hinge pillar typically will not include R&amp;I of the door and hinges. This logic may not apply in all cases. So, it is critical to have a strong understanding of the P-pages for the system you are using -Using Line Notes – line notes are used to provide explanations for limiting repairs to a specific area of a panel, prior damage descriptions, any special requests made by the customer, explanations for why automatic entries have been modified, or providing parts vendor information when alternative parts are used. For example, a vehicle maker might provide a sectioning procedure for replacing a quarter panel, but the information provider only lists a full uniside replacement in the database. You, as the estimator, may elect to manually override the labor time to reflect the sectioning operation. In this case, you should enter a line note explaining the change. Be clear so that everyone understands how the repairs are to be completed and the reason for overriding the database-provided labor time. Note: This is not only a courtesy to those reading your estimate. It may also be legal responsibility. Failure to add the repair notes could result in fines or a fraud investigation in some states. -Choosing Replacement Parts – the estimating process also includes making decisions regarding replacement part type. While the primary factor when making these decisions is repair quality, additional factors may include: -Availability -Customer preference -Insurance partner estimating guidelines -State laws -Note: it is important to remember state and local laws take precedence over any shop or insurance company estimating guidelines -Reviewing the Completed Estimate – after you complete the estimate, it is a good practice to compare the completed estimate to the damaged vehicle and parts. This ensures that you capture all damage and include all necessary repair operations in the estimate. It is also good practice to review the completed estimate with the vehicle owner. If appropriate, you may also review the estimate with the insurance company, so all involved parties have a clear understanding of the repair plan -Repair Authorization – before repairs begin, it is necessary to have the vehicle owner sign a repair authorization. This typically gives the shop authorization to begin repairs and reflects an agreement between the parties. This document may also include language about payment and authorizing the insurance company to pay the shop directly for services provided. For drivable vehicles, repair authorization is typically obtained while the vehicle owner is on-site, dropping off the vehicle for repairs. For non-drivable vehicles, waiting for the customer to come into the repair facility and sign the repair authorization form may take time. Some repair facilities have started using electronic signatures such as text messages to obtain repair authorization. This allows the repair process to begin sooner and eliminates waiting for the customer to come to the repair shop to provide authorization for the repair 2022 EQS: Electrifying Luxury LITE X0002E-US.SBA Pre Test Where is the windshield washer fluid flap located?
